> 6. **Service Life Prediction** – use parabolic oxidation law or finite-element thermal analysis.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **highest-yield reference** for any PEMUT412 Module 1 numerical. **Memorize the units, the sign conventions, and the physical meaning of each symbol.**

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Variables & Units | Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Fourier Thermal Resistance (TBC) | $R_{th} = \dfrac{t_c}{k_c}$ | $t_c$ = coating thickness [m], $k_c$ = thermal conductivity [W/m·K] | Computes how much temperature drop a coating produces across its thickness. |
| 2 | Temperature Drop Across TBC | $\Delta T = \dfrac{q \cdot t_c}{k_c}$ | $q$ = heat flux [W/m²] | Predicts substrate temperature under service heat flux. |
| 3 | Thermal Shock Resistance Parameter | $R_{TS} = \dfrac{k \cdot \sigma_f (1-\nu)}{E \cdot \alpha}$ | $\sigma_f$ = flexural strength [Pa], $\nu$ = Poisson's ratio, $E$ = Young's modulus [Pa], $\alpha$ = CTE [1/K] | Predicts cracking under rapid heating/cooling cycles. |
| 4 | Stoney Equation (Residual Stress) | $\sigma_f = \dfrac{E_s \cdot t_s^2}{6 \cdot R \cdot t_f}$ | $E_s$ = substrate modulus, $t_s$ = substrate thickness, $R$ = radius of curvature [m], $t_f$ = film thickness [m] | Determines residual stress from wafer curvature. |
| 5 | CTE-Mismatch Stress | $\sigma_{th} = \dfrac{E_f (\alpha_s - \alpha_c) \Delta T}{1 - \nu_f}$ | $\alpha_s$, $\alpha_c$ = CTE of substrate and coating, $\Delta T$ = temperature change | Critical for predicting spallation. |
| 6 | Parabolic Oxidation Law | $x^2 = 2 k_p t$ | $x$ = oxide scale thickness, $k_p$ = parabolic rate constant [m²/s] | Predicts lifetime under oxidizing atmospheres. |
| 7 | Archard's Wear Equation | $V = \dfrac{K \cdot W \cdot L}{H}$ | $V$ = worn volume, $W$ = normal load, $L$ = sliding distance, $H$ = hardness, $K$ = wear coefficient | Estimates wear life of tribological coatings. |
| 8 | Adhesion Work (Young–Dupré) | $W_{ad} = \gamma_{sv} + \gamma_{cv} - \gamma_{sc}$ | $\gamma$ = interfacial energies [J/m²] | Thermodynamic adhesion metric. |
| 9 | Critical Load (Scratch Test) | $L_c = \dfrac{\pi \cdot r^2 \cdot H_s}{2}$ | $r$ = scratch track radius, $H_s$ = substrate hardness | Empirical adhesion measurement. |
| 10 | Thermal Expansion (1D) | $\dfrac{\Delta L}{L_0} = \alpha \cdot \Delta T$ | $\alpha$ = coefficient of thermal expansion [1/K] | Base relation for all CTE-mismatch problems. |

> [!WARNING]
> **PITFALL #1:** In the Stoney equation, students often confuse $t_s$ (substrate thickness) with $t_f$ (film thickness). On a KTU answer sheet, swapping these will lead to **3–5 mark deductions** because the resulting stress value will be off by orders of magnitude.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace:** YSZ TBCs on turbine blades permit **200–300 °C** of additional inlet temperature, improving jet engine efficiency by **~1.5% per 25 °C**.
- **Biomedical:** Hydroxyapatite (HA) coatings on Ti-6Al-4V hip implants enable bone in-growth.
- **Cutting Tools:** TiAlN coatings on carbide drills increase tool life by **3× to 10×**.
- **Electronics:** SiO₂ and Si₃N₄ coatings serve as diffusion barriers and dielectric layers in CMOS devices.

## 2.5 The Three Principal Failure Modes

Any KTU 14-mark question is likely to ask the student to **identify and explain** failure modes. Master these three:

| Failure Mode | Trigger | Microstructural Signature |
|---|---|---|
| **Spallation** | CTE mismatch → tensile stress at interface | Buckling, delamination craters |
| **Cracking (Through-thickness)** | Thermal cycling → compressive stress | Vertical cracks, mud-cracking |
| **Oxidation-Induced Failure** | High T + O₂ → TGO growth | α-Al₂O₃ layer at bond-coat interface |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 – Temperature Drop Across a Thermal Barrier Coating (TBC)

This is the **single most asked derivation** in KTU PEMUT412 Module 1, frequently appearing in Part B as a 7-mark sub-question.

### Step 1: State Fourier's Law of Heat Conduction

Fourier's law in 1D steady-state heat conduction states that the heat flux $q$ through a material is proportional to the negative temperature gradient.

$$q = -k \frac{dT}{dx}$$

### Step 2: Apply Boundary Conditions

For a single ceramic coating layer of thickness $t_c$ with a hot gas temperature $T_g$ on the top surface and a bond-coat temperature $T_{bc}$ at the interface:

- At $x = 0$: $T(0) = T_g$ (gas-side surface)
- At $x = t_c$: $T(t_c) = T_{bc}$ (bond-coat interface)

### Step 3: Integrate the Differential Equation

Since $q$ is constant through the layer at steady state, we can integrate:

$$\int_{T_g}^{T_{bc}} dT = -\frac{q}{k_c} \int_{0}^{t_c} dx$$

This yields:

$$T_{bc} - T_g = -\frac{q \cdot t_c}{k_c}$$

### Step 4: Solve for the Temperature Drop

$$\Delta T = T_g - T_{bc} = \frac{q \cdot t_c}{k_c}$$

### Step 5: Physical Interpretation

- A **thicker coating** ($t_c \uparrow$) → larger $\Delta T$ → cooler substrate.
- A **lower thermal conductivity** ($k_c \downarrow$) → larger $\Delta T$ → better insulation.
- A **higher heat flux** ($q \uparrow$) → larger $\Delta T$ → coating must be designed thicker.

### Worked Numerical Example (Typical KTU Style)

> **Problem:** A YSZ TBC of thickness $t_c = 300 \;\mu\text{m}$ and thermal conductivity $k_c = 1.0 \;\text{W/m·K}$ is subjected to a heat flux $q = 5 \times 10^5 \;\text{W/m}^2$. Calculate the temperature drop across the coating.

**Given:**
- $t_c = 300 \times 10^{-6} \;\text{m} = 3 \times 10^{-4} \;\text{m}$
- $k_c = 1.0 \;\text{W/m·K}$
- $q = 5 \times 10^5 \;\text{W/m}^2$

**Solution:**

$$\Delta T = \frac{q \cdot t_c}{k_c} = \frac{(5 \times 10^5) \cdot (3 \times 10^{-4})}{1.0}$$

$$\Delta T = \frac{150}{1.0} = 150 \;\text{K}$$

**Therefore, the temperature drop across the coating is 150 °C.** If the gas temperature is 1300 °C, the bond-coat sees approximately **1150 °C**, which is the metallurgical limit for a typical NiCoCrAlY bond coat.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 – CTE-Mismatch Induced Thermal Stress

This derivation is **critical for explaining spallation failure**.

### Step 1: Define Free Strain Mismatch

When two bonded materials are heated through a temperature change $\Delta T$, the difference in their free thermal expansions produces a strain:

$$\varepsilon_{mismatch} = (\alpha_s - \alpha_c) \cdot \Delta T$$

Where:
- $\alpha_s$ = CTE of substrate
- $\alpha_c$ = CTE of coating

### Step 2: Apply Biaxial Stress–Strain Relation

For a coating constrained by the substrate (biaxial in-plane stress state), the stress in the coating is:

$$\sigma_{th} = \frac{E_f}{1 - \nu_f} \cdot \varepsilon_{mismatch}$$

### Step 3: Substitute the Mismatch Strain

$$\sigma_{th} = \frac{E_f (\alpha_s - \alpha_c) \Delta T}{1 - \nu_f}$$

### Step 4: Sign Convention Analysis

- If $\alpha_s > \alpha_c$: substrate tries to expand more → coating experiences **compressive** stress.
- If $\alpha_s < \alpha_c$: substrate tries to expand less → coating experiences **tensile** stress (spallation risk).

### Numerical Worked Example

> **Problem:** A TiN coating ($E_f = 450 \;\text{GPa}$, $\nu_f = 0.25$, $\alpha_c = 9.4 \times 10^{-6} \;\text{K}^{-1}$) is deposited on a steel substrate ($\alpha_s = 12 \times 10^{-6} \;\text{K}^{-1}$) and cooled from $500 \;\text{°C}$ to $25 \;\text{°C}$. Calculate the thermal stress in the coating.

**Given:**
- $E_f = 450 \times 10^9 \;\text{Pa}$
- $\nu_f = 0.25$
- $\alpha_s = 12 \times 10^{-6} \;\text{K}^{-1}$
- $\alpha_c = 9.4 \times 10^{-6} \;\text{K}^{-1}$
- $\Delta T = 25 - 500 = -475 \;\text{K}$ (negative because cooling)

**Step 1: Compute Mismatch Strain**

$$\varepsilon_{mismatch} = (\alpha_s - \alpha_c) \cdot \Delta T = (12 - 9.4) \times 10^{-6} \cdot (-475)$$

$$\varepsilon_{mismatch} = 2.6 \times 10^{-6} \cdot (-475) = -1.235 \times 10^{-3}$$

**Step 2: Compute Stress**

$$\sigma_{th} = \frac{450 \times 10^9 \cdot (-1.235 \times 10^{-3})}{1 - 0.25}$$

$$\sigma_{th} = \frac{-5.558 \times 10^8}{0.75}$$

$$\sigma_{th} = -7.41 \times 10^8 \;\text{Pa} = -741 \;\text{MPa}$$

**Interpretation:** The **negative sign** indicates **compressive stress** in the coating, which is favorable for adhesion (suppresses cracking). However, if $\alpha_c > \alpha_s$, the sign would flip and the coating would be under **tensile stress**, promoting spallation.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 – Stoney Equation for Thin Film Stress

### Step 1: Consider a Substrate–Film Bilayer

A thin ceramic film of thickness $t_f$ is bonded to a substrate of thickness $t_s$. The bilayer bends with a radius of curvature $R$ due to internal stress $\sigma_f$ in the film.

### Step 2: Force Balance

The stress in the film must be balanced by a uniform stress in the substrate. For pure bending (substrate is much thicker than film):

$$\sigma_f \cdot t_f = \frac{E_s \cdot t_s^2}{6R}$$

### Step 3: Solve for Film Stress

$$\sigma_f = \frac{E_s \cdot t_s^2}{6 \cdot R \cdot t_f}$$

### Step 4: Validity Conditions

- $t_f \ll t_s$ (thin film limit)
- Elastic deformation only
- No plastic deformation at the interface

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Stoney's equation appears in KTU papers:** It is the **only way to experimentally measure residual stress in a coating** without destroying it. The radius of curvature $R$ is measured by laser profilometry, and the rest of the inputs are known. Therefore, students can expect a **2-mark sub-question** asking to compute residual stress from measured curvature data.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix – Coating Selection Decision Tree

> [!NOTE]
> **The following matrix is the analytical backbone for any "compare and select" type KTU question (typically 7 marks).**

| Service Demand | Recommended Coating | Deposition Method | Justification |
|---|---|---|---|
| Thermal insulation (T > 1200 °C) | 8% YSZ | Atmospheric Plasma Spray (APS) or EB-PVD | Lowest $k$ (~1 W/m·K) |
| Oxidation resistance | α-Al₂O₃ | CVD or Pack Cementation | Self-healing alumina scale |
| Wear resistance (cutting) | TiAlN, CrAlN | Cathodic Arc PVD | HV > 30 GPa, oxidation resistant |
| Biomedical | Hydroxyapatite (HA) | Plasma Spray | Bioactive, bonds to bone |
| Electrical insulation | SiO₂, Al₂O₃ | PECVD | High dielectric strength |
| Optical (anti-reflection) | MgF₂, SiO₂ | E-beam evaporation | Controlled refractive index |
| Corrosion resistance | Cr₂O₃ | Electrodeposition | Dense, low porosity |

---

## 3.5 Bond Coat vs Top Coat – The TBC Architecture

A modern aerospace TBC has **three functional layers**:

1. **Substrate** – Single-crystal Ni-based superalloy (PWA 1484, CMSX-4)
2. **Bond Coat (BC)** – 50–150 µm NiCoCrAlY or Pt-aluminide. Function: adhesion, oxidation resistance, CTE gradient.
3. **Thermally Grown Oxide (TGO)** – 0.5–2 µm α-Al₂O₃. Forms in service.
4. **Top Coat (TC)** – 100–500 µm YSZ. Function: thermal insulation.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic for the layer order:** **"Superalloy → Bond → Oxide-grows → Topcoat"** or **"S-B-O-T"**.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram 1 – Classification Tree of Ceramic Coating Deposition Routes

This Mermaid diagram maps the **complete taxonomy of deposition techniques** as required by the KTU 2024 scheme. The use of purely alphanumeric node identifiers and clean uppercase labels ensures successful compilation.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A1[CERAMIC COATING DEPOSITION ROUTES] --> B1[GAS PHASE PROCESSES]
    A1 --> B2[THERMAL SPRAY PROCESSES]
    A1 --> B3[WET CHEMICAL PROCESSES]

    B1 --> C1[PHYSICAL VAPOR DEPOSITION]
    B1 --> C2[CHEMICAL VAPOR DEPOSITION]

    C1 --> D1[SPUTTERING]
    C1 --> D2[EVAPORATION]
    C1 --> D3[CATHODIC ARC]
    C1 --> D4[ION BEAM DEPOSITION]

    C2 --> E1[THERMAL CVD]
    C2 --> E2[PLASMA ENHANCED CVD]
    C2 --> E3[LOW PRESSURE CVD]

    B2 --> F1[ATMOSPHERIC PLASMA SPRAY]
    B2 --> F2[HIGH VELOCITY OXY FUEL]
    B2 --> F3[DETONATION GUN SPRAY]
    B2 --> F4[COLD SPRAY]

    B3 --> G1[SOL GEL PROCESS]
    B3 --> G2[ELECTRODEPOSITION]
    B3 --> G3[SPIN COATING]
    B3 --> G4[DIP COATING]
```

---

## 4.2 Diagram 2 – Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Thermal Barrier Coating System

```mermaid
graph LR
    Input1[HIGH TEMP GAS FLOW 1300C] --> Layer1[TOP COAT YSZ 300UM]
    Layer1 --> Layer2[THERMALLY GROWN OXIDE TGO 1UM]
    Layer2 --> Layer3[BOND COAT NICOCRALY 100UM]
    Layer3 --> Output1[SUBSTRATE NICKEL SUPERALLOY 800C]
    
    Layer1 -. REDUCES HEAT FLUX .-> Layer2
    Layer2 -. OXIDATION BARRIER .-> Layer3
    Layer3 -. CTE GRADIENT ADHESION .-> Output1
```

---

## 4.3 Diagram 3 – Process Flow for Sol-Gel Ceramic Coating

This Mermaid flowchart captures the wet-chemical sol-gel route, which is a **high-frequency topic** for 3-mark short-answer questions.

```mermaid
graph TD
    P1[START METAL ALKOXIDE PRECURSOR] --> P2[HYDROLYSIS REACTION]
    P2 --> P3[CONDENSATION REACTION]
    P3 --> P4[FORMATION OF SOL COLLOIDAL SUSPENSION]
    P4 --> P5[AGING GELATION NETWORK GROWTH]
    P5 --> P6[DRYING SOLVENT EVAPORATION]
    P6 --> P7[CALCINATION 400 TO 800 C]
    P7 --> P8[FINAL CRYSTALLINE CERAMIC COATING]
```

---

## 4.4 Diagram 4 – Failure Mechanism Map for Ceramic Coatings

```mermaid
graph TD
    FM[COATING FAILURE] --> FM1[SPALLATION]
    FM --> FM2[CRACKING]
    FM --> FM3[EROSION]

    FM1 --> FM1A[CTE MISMATCH STRESS]
    FM1 --> FM1B[BOND COAT OXIDATION]
    FM1 --> FM1C[INTERFACE CONTAMINATION]

    FM2 --> FM2A[THERMAL SHOCK]
    FM2 --> FM2B[MECHANICAL FATIGUE]

    FM3 --> FM3A[PARTICLE IMPACT]
    FM3 --> FM3B[CAVITATION DAMAGE]
```

---

## 4.5 Diagram 5 – Schematic Cross-Section of an APS-Deposited YSZ Coating

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical-Schematics Adaptation Note:** Because Mermaid cannot accurately render the **microstructural splat morphology** of plasma-sprayed coatings (splat boundaries, porosity network, microcracks), the following block diagram represents the **functional architecture** of each splat, which is what a KTU examiner expects a student to describe in a 7-mark question.

```mermaid
graph TB
    Layer1A[GAS SIDE SURFACE 1300 C] --> Layer1B[OUTER YSZ SPLAT LAYER HIGH POROSITY]
    Layer1B --> Layer1C[MIDDLE YSZ SPLAT LAYER MEDIUM POROSITY]
    Layer1C --> Layer1D[INNER YSZ SPLAT LAYER LOW POROSITY]
    Layer1D --> Layer1E[BOND COAT NICOCRALY]
    Layer1E --> Layer1F[SUBSTRATE SUPERALLOY]
    
    Layer1B -. MICROCRACKS .-> Layer1C
    Layer1C -. HORIZONTAL DELAMINATIONS .-> Layer1D
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Valuation Convention:** Part A questions test **Recall and Understanding (Bloom Levels L1–L2)**. Answers must be **crisp, 3–5 sentences**, with a clear definition plus one supporting fact.

---

### Part A – Question 1 **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Q: Define a ceramic coating. List any four commonly used ceramic coating materials with one application for each.**

**Model Answer:**

> A **ceramic coating** is an engineered thin layer of inorganic, non-metallic material applied to a substrate to enhance surface properties such as wear resistance, thermal insulation, or corrosion protection, without altering bulk characteristics.
>
> **Four common ceramic coating materials with applications:**
> 1. **Alumina (Al₂O₃)** – used as a wear-resistant coating on cutting tools and as a tribological coating in diesel engines.
> 2. **Yttria-Stabilized Zirconia (YSZ)** – applied as a thermal barrier coating on nickel-based superalloy turbine blades in jet engines.
> 3. **Titanium Nitride (TiN)** – deposited on carbide drills and inserts to extend tool life in machining operations.
> 4. **Chromium Carbide (Cr₃C₂)** – used as a hard-facing coating in high-temperature sliding wear environments up to 900 °C.

---

### Part A – Question 2 **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**Q: What is a thermal barrier coating? Why is yttria-stabilized zirconia (YSZ) preferred as the top coat material?**

**Model Answer:**

> A **Thermal Barrier Coating (TBC)** is a multi-layer ceramic coating system, typically consisting of a metallic bond coat and a ceramic top coat, applied to hot-section components (e.g., gas turbine blades) to insulate the underlying superalloy substrate from extreme service temperatures.
>
> **Reasons YSZ is preferred:**
> 1. **Low thermal conductivity** (~1.0 W/m·K) due to phonon scattering at the Y³⁺ dopant sites.
> 2. **High coefficient of thermal expansion** (~11 × 10⁻⁶ /K) close to that of nickel superalloys, minimizing CTE-mismatch stress.
> 3. **Phase stability** of the non-transformable tetragonal phase (t′) up to 1200 °C, avoiding the destructive tetragonal-to-monoclinic transformation.
> 4. **Chemical inertness** in combustion environments.

---

## 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Valuation Convention (ESE):** Part B questions carry 14 marks and must offer internal choice. Each sub-question is typically 7 marks, demanding **Apply and Analyze (Bloom Levels L3–L4)** with full mathematical or descriptive rigor. Examiners allocate approximately **5–6 minutes per 7-mark sub-question**.

---

### PART B – QUESTION A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**

**Q: (a)** Explain in detail the various methods of deposition of ceramic coatings. Discuss the relative merits of Physical Vapor Deposition (PVD) and Chemical Vapor Deposition (CVD) techniques. **(7 Marks)**

**Q: (b)** A thermal barrier coating made of 8% yttria-stabilized zirconia (YSZ) is to be designed for a turbine blade. The heat flux at the gas–coating interface is $q = 4 \times 10^5 \;\text{W/m}^2$. The thermal conductivity of YSZ is $k_c = 1.2 \;\text{W/m·K}$. If the maximum allowable substrate temperature is $T_s = 950 \;\text{°C}$ and the gas temperature is $T_g = 1250 \;\text{°C}$, calculate the required minimum coating thickness. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question A(a)

**Methods of Deposition of Ceramic Coatings:**

The deposition methods can be classified into three major families:

**1. Physical Vapor Deposition (PVD):**
PVD involves the vaporization of a solid target material followed by transport and condensation onto the substrate. Common variants include:
- **Sputtering:** Argon ions bombard a target, ejecting atoms that deposit on the substrate. Used for TiN, CrN coatings.
- **Evaporation (Thermal/E-beam):** Source material is heated to vaporize and condense on substrate. Used for optical coatings.
- **Cathodic Arc PVD:** High ionization, dense coatings with high adhesion. Used for TiAlN on cutting tools.

**2. Chemical Vapor Deposition (CVD):**
CVD relies on chemical reactions of gaseous precursors on a heated substrate to form the coating.
- **Thermal CVD:** Used for SiC, Si₃N₄.
- **Plasma-Enhanced CVD (PECVD):** Lower temperatures, used in semiconductor industry.
- **Metal-Organic CVD (MOCVD):** Used for oxide coatings like YSZ.

**3. Thermal Spray Processes:**
- **Atmospheric Plasma Spray (APS):** Molten powder injected into plasma jet, deposited as splats. Used for TBC top coats.
- **High Velocity Oxy-Fuel (HVOF):** High-velocity gas jet for dense coatings.
- **Cold Spray:** Solid-state deposition, minimal oxidation.

**4. Wet Chemical Methods:**
- **Sol-Gel:** Metal alkoxides hydrolyzed and condensed to form ceramic films.
- **Electrodeposition:** For oxide films.

**Comparison of PVD vs CVD:**

| Parameter | PVD | CVD |
|---|---|---|
| Operating Temperature | Lower (200–500 °C) | Higher (800–1100 °C) |
| Coating Density | High | High |
| Adhesion | Good | Excellent (often diffusion-bonded) |
| Conformal Coverage | Limited on complex shapes | Excellent (gas fills cavities) |
| Deposition Rate | Lower | Higher |
| Process Complexity | Moderate | High (toxic gases, byproducts) |
| Typical Coatings | TiN, TiAlN, CrN | SiC, Si₃N₄, diamond, YSZ |

---

#### Model Solution for Question A(b)

**Given Data:**
- $q = 4 \times 10^5 \;\text{W/m}^2$
- $k_c = 1.2 \;\text{W/m·K}$
- $T_g = 1250 \;\text{°C}$
- $T_s = 950 \;\text{°C}$

**Required:** Minimum coating thickness $t_c$

**Step 1: Calculate the Required Temperature Drop**

The temperature drop across the coating must at least equal the difference between gas temperature and substrate temperature:

$$\Delta T = T_g - T_s = 1250 - 950 = 300 \;\text{°C}$$

**Step 2: Apply Fourier's Law**

Rearranging $\Delta T = \dfrac{q \cdot t_c}{k_c}$:

$$t_c = \frac{\Delta T \cdot k_c}{q}$$

**Step 3: Substitute Values**

$$t_c = \frac{300 \times 1.2}{4 \times 10^5}$$

$$t_c = \frac{360}{4 \times 10^5}$$

$$t_c = 9 \times 10^{-4} \;\text{m}$$

$$t_c = 900 \;\mu\text{m} = 0.9 \;\text{mm}$$

**Conclusion:** The minimum coating thickness required is **900 µm (0.9 mm)** to keep the substrate temperature below 950 °C.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall #2:** Many students mistakenly compute $\Delta T = T_g - T_s$ in Kelvin without converting. Fortunately, a temperature *difference* in Celsius equals that in Kelvin, so this is safe — but if the problem gave absolute temperatures in different scales, **failure to convert would cost 1 mark**. Always state your units explicitly.

---

### PART B – QUESTION B (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – May 2023]**

**Q: (a)** Describe the structure of a thermal barrier coating (TBC) system used in aero-engine turbine blades. Discuss the role of each layer and the major failure mechanisms associated with TBCs. **(7 Marks)**

**Q: (b)** A TiC coating ($E_f = 450 \;\text{GPa}$, $\nu_f = 0.20$, $\alpha_c = 7.4 \times 10^{-6} \;\text{K}^{-1}$) is deposited on a stainless-steel substrate ($\alpha_s = 17 \times 10^{-6} \;\text{K}^{-1}$, $E_s = 200 \;\text{GPa}$). The system is cooled from a deposition temperature of $T_d = 600 \;\text{°C}$ to room temperature $T_r = 25 \;\text{°C}$. Calculate the thermal stress developed in the coating. If the coating thickness is $t_f = 5 \;\mu\text{m}$ and substrate thickness $t_s = 1 \;\text{mm}$, and the substrate curvature radius after cooling is measured to be $R = 2.5 \;\text{m}$, calculate the residual stress in the film using the Stoney formula. Comment on the difference. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question B(a)

**Structure of a TBC System:**

A modern TBC system consists of four distinct layers, each with a specific function:

**1. Substrate (Base Material):**
- Typically a single-crystal Ni-based superalloy such as CMSX-4 or PWA 1484.
- Provides the bulk mechanical strength at elevated temperatures.
- Has a CTE of ~12–16 × 10⁻⁶/K.

**2. Bond Coat (Metallic Layer, 50–150 µm):**
- Composed of NiCoCrAlY (MCrAlY type) or a Pt-aluminide diffusion coating.
- **Roles:**
  - Enhances adhesion between ceramic top coat and metallic substrate.
  - Provides oxidation resistance by forming a protective α-Al₂O₃ scale.
  - Reduces CTE mismatch between substrate and ceramic top coat.
  - Accommodates differential thermal expansion.

**3. Thermally Grown Oxide (TGO, 0.5–2 µm):**
- Forms naturally in service by oxidation of Al in the bond coat.
- Composed primarily of α-Al₂O₃.
- Acts as the true oxidation barrier.
- **Critical:** TGO growth is the primary driver of TBC spallation.

**4. Top Coat (Ceramic, 100–500 µm):**
- 8% Yttria-Stabilized Zirconia (YSZ) deposited by APS or EB-PVD.
- **Roles:** Provides thermal insulation (low k), reduces metal surface temperature by 100–300 °C.

**Major Failure Mechanisms:**

| Mechanism | Description |
|---|---|
| **Spallation at TGO/Top-Coat Interface** | Growth of TGO creates in-plane compressive stress → buckling → delamination. |
| **Bond Coat Oxidation** | Depletion of Al in bond coat → formation of less-protective spinels. |
| **Sintering of Top Coat** | At high T, pores close, $k$ increases, coating loses insulating function. |
| **CMAS Attack** | Calcium-Magnesium-Alumino-Silicates from ingested air melt and infiltrate the coating. |
| **Foreign Object Damage (FOD)** | Mechanical impact by ingested particles. |

---

#### Model Solution for Question B(b)

**Given Data:**
- $E_f = 450 \;\text{GPa} = 450 \times 10^9 \;\text{Pa}$
- $\nu_f = 0.20$
- $\alpha_c = 7.4 \times 10^{-6} \;\text{K}^{-1}$
- $\alpha_s = 17 \times 10^{-6} \;\text{K}^{-1}$
- $T_d = 600 \;\text{°C}$, $T_r = 25 \;\text{°C}$
- $\Delta T = T_r - T_d = 25 - 600 = -575 \;\text{K}$
- $t_f = 5 \;\mu\text{m} = 5 \times 10^{-6} \;\text{m}$
- $t_s = 1 \;\text{mm} = 1 \times 10^{-3} \;\text{m}$
- $R = 2.5 \;\text{m}$

**Part (i): CTE-Mismatch Thermal Stress**

**Step 1: Compute Mismatch Strain**

$$\varepsilon_{mismatch} = (\alpha_s - \alpha_c) \cdot \Delta T = (17 - 7.4) \times 10^{-6} \cdot (-575)$$

$$\varepsilon_{mismatch} = 9.6 \times 10^{-6} \cdot (-575) = -5.52 \times 10^{-3}$$

**Step 2: Apply Biaxial Stress Equation**

$$\sigma_{th} = \frac{E_f \cdot \varepsilon_{mismatch}}{1 - \nu_f} = \frac{450 \times 10^9 \cdot (-5.52 \times 10^{-3})}{1 - 0.20}$$

$$\sigma_{th} = \frac{-2.484 \times 10^9}{0.80}$$

$$\sigma_{th} = -3.105 \times 10^9 \;\text{Pa}$$

$$\boxed{\sigma_{th} = -3.105 \;\text{GPa (compressive)}}$$

**Part (ii): Stoney Formula Residual Stress**

**Step 1: Apply Stoney's Equation**

$$\sigma_{Stoney} = \frac{E_s \cdot t_s^2}{6 \cdot R \cdot t_f}$$

**Step 2: Substitute Values**

$$\sigma_{Stoney} = \frac{200 \times 10^9 \cdot (1 \times 10^{-3})^2}{6 \cdot 2.5 \cdot (5 \times 10^{-6})}$$

$$\sigma_{Stoney} = \frac{200 \times 10^9 \cdot 1 \times 10^{-6}}{75 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$\sigma_{Stoney} = \frac{2 \times 10^5}{75 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$\sigma_{Stoney} = 2.667 \times 10^9 \;\text{Pa}$$

$$\boxed{\sigma_{Stoney} = 2.667 \;\text{GPa (tensile)}}$$

**Comparison and Comment:**

- The CTE-mismatch method predicts a **compressive stress of −3.105 GPa**.
- The Stoney measurement indicates a **tensile stress of +2.667 GPa**.
- The **sign reversal** suggests that the biaxial CTE-mismatch model alone is insufficient. In reality, factors such as **intrinsic growth stress (from deposition), grain boundary stress, and phase transformation stresses** contribute additional components.
- The discrepancy highlights that **real coating stress is the superposition of thermal mismatch stress and intrinsic (growth) stress**.
- The **order-of-magnitude agreement** (~GPa range) is typical for hard ceramic coatings and confirms that mechanical equilibrium is satisfied.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall #3:** In the Stoney equation, students routinely:
> 1. **Forget to square** $t_s$ (the substrate thickness).
> 2. **Swap film and substrate thicknesses**, leading to a stress value off by a factor of 200 (since $t_s/t_f = 200$ in this problem).
> 3. **Drop the factor of 6**, which is a **3-mark deduction** because it is fundamental to the derivation.
> 4. **Forget to convert µm and mm into metres**, producing dimensional nonsense.
>
> Always write down each substitution explicitly with units. **Part marks in KTU valuation are awarded for correct substitution, even if the final number is wrong.**

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Ceramic Coatings (Module 1, PEMUT412)**

**Core Definitions:**
- Ceramic coatings are thin engineered layers that modify **surface** properties only.
- TBCs are the most commercially important subclass, with **YSZ top coat** and **NiCoCrAlY bond coat**.
- HA coatings are the **biomedical** benchmark for bone in-growth.

**Key Materials to Memorize:**
- **Al₂O₃** – wear & corrosion resistance.
- **YSZ** – thermal insulation.
- **TiN, TiAlN, CrN** – tribological coatings on cutting tools.
- **SiC, Si₃N₄** – CVD-derived hard ceramics.
- **HA** – biomedical use.

**Deposition Methods:**
- PVD family → sputtering, evaporation, cathodic arc.
- CVD family → thermal, PECVD, LPCVD, MOCVD.
- Thermal spray → APS, HVOF, cold spray, detonation gun.
- Wet chemical → sol-gel, electrodeposition.

**High-Yield Numerical Equations (with units):**
- $\Delta T = \dfrac{q \cdot t_c}{k_c}$
- $\sigma_{th} = \dfrac{E_f (\alpha_s - \alpha_c) \Delta T}{1 - \nu_f}$
- Stoney: $\sigma_f = \dfrac{E_s t_s^2}{6 R t_f}$
- Archard wear: $V = \dfrac{K W L}{H}$
- Parabolic oxidation: $x^2 = 2 k_p t$

**Critical Sign Conventions:**
- Negative CTE-mismatch stress → compressive → **safe**.
- Positive CTE-mismatch stress → tensile → **spallation risk**.

**Key Numbers to Memorize:**
- YSZ thermal conductivity ≈ **1.0 W/m·K**.
- Coating thickness range = **0.1 µm to 500 µm**.
- TBC substrate temperature reduction = **100–300 °C**.
- Hardness of TiAlN ≈ **30 GPa**.
- Typical Vickers hardness of Cr₂O₃ ≈ **15–20 GPa**.

**Failure Mechanisms (frequently asked for 3 marks):**
1. Spallation (TGO-driven).
2. Through-thickness cracking (thermal shock).
3. CMAS infiltration.
4. Sintering of top coat at high T.
5. Foreign Object Damage (FOD).

**Mnemonic for the TBC Layer Order:** **"Substrate → Bond → Oxide (TGO) → Top"** or **"S-B-O-T"**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Glass
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT412 - COMPOSITE MATERIALS AND CERAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Glass

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

**Glass** is defined as an **amorphous, inorganic, non-metallic solid material** typically produced by the rapid quenching of a melt, possessing a random (non-crystalline) atomic arrangement that lacks long-range periodicity but exhibits short-range order over a few atomic distances.

Mathematically, a glass is characterized by a continuous random network (CRN) of polyhedra (commonly SiO₄ tetrahedra in silicate glasses) in which no two polyhedra share regular geometric relationships over macroscopic distances.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Expectation):**
> *"A glass is a non-crystalline solid obtained by quenching a melt rapidly enough to avoid crystallization. It exhibits a glass transition temperature (T_g) below which it behaves as a rigid solid and above which as a supercooled liquid."*

The **glass transition temperature (T_g)** is a critical thermodynamic parameter, typically lying between **500 K and 1500 K** depending on composition, and represents a second-order transition where properties like specific volume, entropy, and enthalpy change slope continuously without latent heat.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **classroom of students during a lecture** — if they are seated in neat rows, that represents a **crystal** (ordered arrangement). Now imagine the same students during a **lunch break**, scattered in small clusters of friends (each cluster has its own internal arrangement), but there is no overall repeating pattern across the whole cafeteria. This scattered arrangement is exactly how a **glass** is structured at the atomic level.

- Each "cluster of friends" = a **structural unit** (e.g., SiO₄ tetrahedron)
- The whole cafeteria scatter = **random network**
- The moment students stop running around and settle into this arrangement = the **glass transition temperature (T_g)**

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Glass is Neither a True Liquid nor a True Solid:**
> Glass does not flow visibly at room temperature (viscosity > 10¹² Pa·s), nor does it have a sharp melting point. It is **thermodynamically metastable** and kinetically trapped in a disordered state. This is why very old windowpanes are not measurably thicker at the bottom — a common misconception.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Standard glass transition range for silicates:** T_g ≈ **700 K – 900 K** (≈ 427 °C – 627 °C)
- **Softening point (log η = 7.6 dPa·s):** typically **1000 K – 1300 K**
- **Working point (log η = 4.0 dPa·s):** **1200 K – 1500 K**
- **Refractive index (n_D) of soda-lime glass:** **1.51 – 1.52**
- **Density of typical silicate glass:** **2.4 – 2.8 g/cm³**
- **Young's Modulus:** **60 – 90 GPa**
- **Poisson's Ratio:** **0.20 – 0.25**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight — Module 1 Focus:**
> Under KTU 2024 Module 1, the topic of **Glass** emphasizes: (i) Zachariasen's rules of glass formation, (ii) Network formers, modifiers, and intermediates, (iii) The structural theory of glass, and (iv) Properties and classification.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Zachariasen's Rules of Glass Formation (1932)

William H. Zachariasen proposed **four empirical rules** that an oxide (M_xO_y) must satisfy to be a viable **glass former**:

1. **Rule 1 — Coordination:** An oxygen atom must not be linked to more than **two cations**.
2. **Rule 2 — Cation Coordination:** The coordination number of the cation (M) must be **small (typically 3 or 4)**.
3. **Rule 3 — Oxygen Sharing:** Cation-oxygen polyhedra must share **corners (vertices)** only, not edges or faces.
4. **Rule 4 — Network Continuity:** At least **three corners** of each polyhedron must be shared to build a 3D random network.

| Oxide Example | Cation Coordination | Glass Former? | Reason |
|---------------|---------------------|---------------|--------|
| SiO₂ | 4 | **Yes** | All four rules satisfied |
| B₂O₃ | 3 | **Yes** | Triangular BO₃ units |
| GeO₂ | 4 | **Yes** | Similar to SiO₂ |
| Na₂O | 6 | **No** | Modifier, high coordination |
| CaO | 6 | **No** | Modifier, high coordination |
| Al₂O₃ | 6 (octahedral) | **Intermediate** | Acts as former or modifier |

### 2.2 Structural Roles in Oxide Glass

The ionic constituents of glass are classified into three structural categories:

#### A. Network Formers (Glass Formers)
These cations form the backbone of the random network via strong covalent M–O bonds.
- **Examples:** Si⁴⁺, B³⁺, P⁵⁺, Ge⁴⁺
- Form polyhedra: SiO₄ tetrahedra, BO₃ triangles, PO₄ tetrahedra

#### B. Network Modifiers
These are cations that **break** the network by introducing non-bridging oxygens (NBO).
- **Examples:** Na⁺, K⁺, Ca²⁺, Mg²⁺, Pb²⁺
- Mechanism: Na₂O breaks Si–O–Si bonds to form Si–O⁻Na⁺ and Si–O–Si, reducing viscosity and T_g.

#### C. Network Intermediates (Conditioners)
These cations can act as either formers or modifiers depending on composition.
- **Examples:** Al³⁺, Zn²⁺, Zr⁴⁺, Ti⁴⁺
- In silicate glass with sufficient Na₂O, Al³⁺ acts as a former (forming AlO₄⁻ tetrahedra), but in pure silica, it acts as a modifier.

### 2.3 Random Network Theory (Warren's Model)

B.E. Warren (1930s) experimentally confirmed using X-ray diffraction that **silica glass (SiO₂)** consists of a 3D random network of SiO₄ tetrahedra linked at corners.

- Every **Si** atom is bonded to **4 oxygen atoms** (tetrahedral coordination)
- Every **O atom** is bonded to **2 Si atoms** (bridging oxygen, BO)
- The **Si–O–Si bond angle** varies between **120° and 180°** (mean ≈ 144°)
- The **Si–O bond length** is **0.162 nm** with **< 1% variation**

> [!NOTE]
> **Bridging Oxygen (BO) vs Non-Bridging Oxygen (NBO):**
> A bridging oxygen links two network polyhedra (Si–O–Si). When a modifier like Na₂O breaks a Si–O–Si bond, an oxygen becomes bonded to only one Si — this is a **non-bridging oxygen (NBO)**. The fraction of NBO governs viscosity, T_g, and chemical durability.

### 2.4 Glass Transition — Thermodynamic Perspective

At the **melting point (T_m)**, the crystal–liquid transition is **first-order** (with latent heat). Below T_m, the supercooled liquid has a higher entropy, enthalpy, and specific volume than the crystal.

On **rapid cooling**, the supercooled liquid avoids crystallization at T_m. Instead, its specific volume follows the **supercooled liquid line** until molecular mobility drops sharply at **T_g**, after which it follows the **glass line** (essentially a frozen liquid).

- Above T_g: **supercooled liquid** (viscous, flowable)
- Below T_g: **glassy solid** (rigid, brittle)

The **glass transition** is a **kinetically controlled phenomenon** — its temperature depends on cooling rate:
- Higher cooling rate → **higher apparent T_g**
- Lower cooling rate → **lower apparent T_g** (more time for structural relaxation)

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical for Numerical Problems — Memorize the following table.**

| # | Formula / Relation | Symbol Meaning | Units | Application |
|---|-------------------|----------------|-------|-------------|
| 1 | $\eta = A \exp\left(\dfrac{E}{RT}\right)$ | η = viscosity, A = pre-exponential, E = activation energy, R = 8.314 J/mol·K | Pa·s | High-temp viscosity (Arrhenius) |
| 2 | $\log \eta = \log A + \dfrac{E}{2.303\,RT}$ | Linear form for plotting | log(Pa·s) | Experimental data fitting |
| 3 | $\alpha = \dfrac{\Delta L}{L_0 \, \Delta T}$ | Linear thermal expansion coefficient | K⁻¹ | Thermal stress calculations |
| 4 | $\sigma_{\text{th}} = \dfrac{E \, \alpha \, \Delta T}{1-\nu}$ | Thermal stress developed under constraint | Pa | Toughened glass, thermal shock |
| 5 | $\Delta T_{\text{crit}} = \dfrac{\sigma_f (1-\nu)}{E \alpha}$ | Critical temperature difference before fracture | K | Thermal shock resistance |
| 6 | $n = \dfrac{\sin i}{\sin r}$ | Refractive index (Snell's Law) | dimensionless | Optical glass design |
| 7 | $\text{NBO fraction} = \dfrac{2[N_a]}{[N_s] + 2[N_a]}$ | N_a = moles of Na₂O, N_s = moles of SiO₂ | dimensionless | Predicting structure |
| 8 | $R = \dfrac{M}{V_m}$ | Molar refractivity, M = molecular weight, V_m = molar volume | cm³/mol | Lorentz-Lorenz equation |
| 9 | $\dfrac{n^2 - 1}{n^2 + 2} \cdot \dfrac{M}{\rho} = R$ | Lorentz-Lorenz equation | cm³/mol | Computing refractive index |
| 10 | $T_g \approx \dfrac{2}{3}\,T_m$ (Kauzmann's rule) | T_g = glass transition, T_m = melting point | K | Quick estimation of T_g |

### 2.6 Engineering Utility of Glass

- **Soda-lime silicate glass:** Windowpanes, bottles, containers (low cost, T_g ≈ 820 K)
- **Borosilicate glass (Pyrex):** Labware, cookware (low α = 3.3×10⁻⁶ K⁻¹, high thermal shock resistance)
- **Lead glass (crystal):** Decorative, optical (high n = 1.7, high density)
- **Aluminosilicate glass:** Smartphone screens, fiber optics reinforcement
- **Fused silica (SiO₂):** UV optics, semiconductor crucibles (T_g ≈ 1473 K, ultra-low α)

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Arrhenius Viscosity–Temperature Relation

The viscosity of a glass-forming melt above T_g is well-described by an Arrhenius-type equation because viscous flow involves the cooperative breaking and reformation of bonds.

#### Step 1 — Activation Energy Concept

Consider the rate of bond-breaking events per unit volume per unit time. From statistical mechanics, the rate k of an activated process is:

$$k = A \exp\left(-\frac{E}{RT}\right)$$

Since viscosity is inversely proportional to the rate of flow (a slower bond rearrangement implies higher viscosity), we postulate:

$$\eta \propto \frac{1}{k}$$

Therefore:

$$\eta = C \cdot \frac{1}{A \exp(-E/RT)} = \frac{C}{A} \exp\left(\frac{E}{RT}\right)$$

Letting $A' = C/A$:

$$\eta = A' \exp\left(\frac{E}{RT}\right)$$

This is **Equation (1)** — the fundamental Arrhenius viscosity equation.

#### Step 2 — Linearization (for Experimental Plotting)

Taking the logarithm (base 10) of both sides:

$$\log \eta = \log A' + \frac{E}{2.303\,RT}$$

Let $T$ vary while $A'$ and $E$ remain constant for a given composition. Then $\log \eta$ vs $1/T$ is a **straight line** with:

$$\text{Slope} = \frac{E}{2.303\,R}$$

$$\text{Intercept} = \log A'$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Application:** Glass manufacturers plot $\log \eta$ vs $1/T$ to identify the working point, softening point, and annealing point — all critical for processing.

#### Step 3 — Vogel–Fulcher–Tammann (VFT) Modification

For temperatures near T_g, the simple Arrhenius form fails. The empirical **VFT equation** introduces a divergence temperature T_0 (often ≈ T_g − 50 K):

$$\log \eta = A + \frac{B}{T - T_0}$$

This is **three-parameter** (A, B, T_0) and is widely used for accurate description over wide T ranges.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Thermal Stress in a Constrained Glass Plate

When a glass plate is suddenly cooled from T_1 to T_2 (such that ΔT = T_1 − T_2), the surface contracts faster than the interior, generating **tensile stress at the surface**. If this exceeds the tensile strength σ_f, fracture occurs.

#### Step 1 — Free Thermal Strain

If unrestrained, the surface would contract by:

$$\epsilon_{\text{th}} = \alpha \, \Delta T$$

where α = linear coefficient of thermal expansion.

#### Step 2 — Constrained Strain

In a fully constrained plate (no shrinkage allowed), the mechanical strain induced is:

$$\epsilon_{\text{mech}} = -\alpha \, \Delta T$$

(The negative sign indicates that the constraint produces an opposing mechanical tensile strain.)

#### Step 3 — Stress–Strain Relation (Hooke's Law, Biaxial)

For a thin plate constrained in two dimensions, the biaxial elastic stress is:

$$\sigma = \frac{E}{1-\nu} \cdot \epsilon_{\text{mech}}$$

Substituting ε_mech:

$$\sigma = \frac{E}{1-\nu} \cdot \alpha \, \Delta T$$

This is the **maximum tensile stress** at the surface.

#### Step 4 — Critical Temperature Difference

Setting σ equal to the tensile strength σ_f and solving for ΔT:

$$\Delta T_{\text{crit}} = \frac{\sigma_f (1-\nu)}{E \alpha}$$

**Interpretation:**

- High σ_f → high thermal shock resistance (e.g., **tempered glass**: σ_f ≈ 700 MPa)
- High E → low thermal shock resistance
- High α → low thermal shock resistance
- Therefore, **low α is the key** → this is why **borosilicate glass** (α = 3.3×10⁻⁶ K⁻¹) is used in labware, while **fused silica** (α = 0.5×10⁻⁶ K⁻¹) is used in space telescopes.

#### Step 5 — Numerical Worked Example

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Example (Borosilicate Glass):**
> Given: E = 64 GPa, ν = 0.20, α = 3.3×10⁻⁶ K⁻¹, σ_f = 30 MPa.
> Compute: ΔT_crit

$$\Delta T_{\text{crit}} = \frac{(30 \times 10^{6})(1-0.20)}{(64 \times 10^{9})(3.3 \times 10^{-6})}$$

$$\Delta T_{\text{crit}} = \frac{24 \times 10^{6}}{2.112 \times 10^{5}}$$

$$\Delta T_{\text{crit}} \approx 113.6\ \text{K}$$

So a borosilicate plate can withstand a sudden ΔT ≈ 113 K before cracking. For comparison, soda-lime glass (α = 9×10⁻⁶ K⁻¹) gives ΔT_crit ≈ 42 K.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Lorentz–Lorenz Equation (Refractive Index from Composition)

The refractive index n of a glass is related to its density ρ and molecular polarizability through the **Lorentz–Lorenz equation**:

$$\frac{n^2 - 1}{n^2 + 2} \cdot \frac{M}{\rho} = R$$

where M is molar mass, ρ is density, and R is **molar refractivity** (cm³/mol).

#### Step 1 — Polarizability Basis

For a single atom/molecule, the Clausius–Mossotti relation gives:

$$\frac{\epsilon_r - 1}{\epsilon_r + 2} = \frac{N \alpha}{3 \epsilon_0}$$

where ε_r = dielectric constant, N = number density, α = polarizability, ε_0 = vacuum permittivity.

#### Step 2 — Substitution of Refractive Index

Since $n^2 \approx \epsilon_r$ at optical frequencies (neglecting absorption):

$$\frac{n^2 - 1}{n^2 + 2} = \frac{N \alpha}{3 \epsilon_0}$$

#### Step 3 — Multiply by Molar Volume

Multiplying both sides by V_m = M/ρ = 1/N:

$$R = \frac{n^2 - 1}{n^2 + 2} \cdot \frac{M}{\rho}$$

The LHS R is the **molar refractivity**, which is **additive** — for a glass of oxide components:

$$R = \sum_i x_i R_i$$

where x_i is the mole fraction and R_i is the refractivity of oxide i (tabulated in handbooks).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Application:** Optical glass designers use the Lorentz–Lorenz additivity to *design* the refractive index and dispersion of a glass from its oxide composition before any melting is done. This is foundational for designing **camera lenses, fiber-optic cores, and laser windows**.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Glass Property Table

| Property | Fused Silica | Soda-Lime | Borosilicate (Pyrex) | Lead Glass |
|----------|--------------|-----------|----------------------|------------|
| SiO₂ (wt%) | 99.8 | 72 | 81 | 56 |
| T_g (K) | 1473 | 820 | 830 | 720 |
| α × 10⁻⁶ (K⁻¹) | 0.5 | 9.0 | 3.3 | 8.5 |
| n_D | 1.458 | 1.512 | 1.474 | 1.700 |
| Density (g/cm³) | 2.20 | 2.50 | 2.23 | 3.05 |
| Thermal Shock ΔT (K) | ~1000 | ~40 | ~115 | ~40 |
| Cost | Very High | Low | Moderate | High |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Diagram 1: Random Network Model of Silica Glass (Warren–Zachariasen)

The following mermaid diagram illustrates the **2D schematic of a continuous random network (CRN)** showing network formers (Si), bridging oxygens (BO), and the disruption caused by a network modifier (Na⁺).

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeA["SiO4 Tetrahedron 1"] --- nodeB["Bridging Oxygen BO"]
    nodeB --- nodeC["SiO4 Tetrahedron 2"]
    nodeC --- nodeD["Bridging Oxygen BO"]
    nodeD --- nodeE["SiO4 Tetrahedron 3"]
    nodeE --- nodeF["Bridging Oxygen BO"]
    nodeF --- nodeG["SiO4 Tetrahedron 4"]
    nodeG --- nodeH["Non Bridging Oxygen NBO"]
    nodeH --- nodeI["Network Modifier Na plus"]
    nodeI --- nodeJ["Network Modifier Na plus"]
    nodeC --- nodeK["Bridging Oxygen BO"]
    nodeK --- nodeL["SiO4 Tetrahedron 5"]
    nodeL --- nodeM["Bridging Oxygen BO"]
    nodeM --- nodeE
    nodeG --- nodeN["Bridging Oxygen BO"]
    nodeN --- nodeO["SiO4 Tetrahedron 6"]
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- Each `SiO4 Tetrahedron` node represents a Si⁴⁺ center bonded to 4 oxygens
- `Bridging Oxygen` nodes link two tetrahedra (corner-sharing)
- The `Non Bridging Oxygen` is one where a Na⁺ modifier has broken the network — this oxygen bonds to only **one** Si
- `Network Modifier` nodes (Na⁺) sit in the interstitial voids, electrostatically balancing the negatively charged NBO

---

### 4.2 Diagram 2: Functional Block Architecture of Glass Formation Process

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    rawMat["Raw Materials: Silica Sand, Soda Ash, Limestone, Cullet"]
    batchMix["Batch Mixing and Weighing"]
    furnace["Melting Furnace 1500 to 1600 C"]
    refine["Refining and Homogenization"]
    forming["Forming: Float, Blow, Draw, Press"]
    anneal["Annealing Lehr T near Tg"]
    inspect["Quality Inspection"]
    product["Finished Glass Product"]

    rawMat --> batchMix
    batchMix --> furnace
    furnace --> refine
    refine --> forming
    forming --> anneal
    anneal --> inspect
    inspect --> product

    cullet1["Recycled Cullet Glass"]
    cullet1 --> batchMix
    control["Temperature and Viscosity Control Loop"]
    furnace -.-> control
    control -.-> forming
```

**Process Explanation:**

1. **Raw materials:** High-purity silica (SiO₂), sodium carbonate (Na₂CO₃), calcium carbonate (CaCO₃), and recycled cullet
2. **Batch mixing:** Homogenized proportions fed to furnace
3. **Melting (1500–1600 °C):** Endothermic reactions form molten glass
4. **Refining:** Bubbles removed by fining agents (e.g., Na₂SO₄)
5. **Forming:** Float process (sheets), blow (bottles), draw (fibers)
6. **Annealing:** Controlled cooling near T_g relieves residual stress — critical for flat glass

---

### 4.3 Diagram 3: Classification Hierarchy of Glasses

```mermaid
graph TD
    glass["Glasses"]
    oxide["Oxide Glasses"]
    nonoxide["Non Oxide Glasses"]
    metallic["Metallic Glasses"]

    glass --> oxide
    glass --> nonoxide
    glass --> metallic

    oxide --> sil["Silicate SiO2 based"]
    oxide --> bor["Borate B2O3 based"]
    oxide --> phos["Phosphate P2O5 based"]
    oxide --> ger["Germanate GeO2 based"]

    sil --> soda["Soda Lime"]
    sil --> boro["Borosilicate Pyrex"]
    sil --> alum["Aluminosilicate"]
    sil --> lead["Lead Crystal"]

    nonoxide --> chalco["Chalcogenide S Se Te"]
    nonoxide --> halo["Halide based Fluoride Glass"]

    metallic --> fe["Fe based"]
    metallic --> zr["Zr based"]
    metallic --> pd["Pd based"]
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- The glass universe branches into three primary families: oxide, non-oxide, and metallic
- Within oxide glasses, **silicate glasses** dominate (>90% of commercial production)
- **Non-oxide glasses** (chalcogenides) are used in IR optics and phase-change memory
- **Metallic glasses** are emerging for high-strength transformer cores and sporting goods

---

### 4.4 Diagram 4: Volume vs Temperature Curve — Glass Transition

```mermaid
graph LR
    A["High T Liquid"] -->|Cool at Slow Rate| B["Crystal at Tm Sharp Transition"]
    A -->|Cool at Fast Rate| C["Supercooled Liquid"]
    C -->|Cool further| D["Glass Transition at Tg Slope Changes"]
    D --> E["Rigid Glass"]

    style B fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0066cc
    style D fill:#ffe1e1,stroke:#cc0000
    style E fill:#f0f0f0,stroke:#333333
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** This is the qualitative V–T plot. At slow cooling, crystallization occurs sharply at T_m (first-order). At fast cooling, the supercooled liquid line continues until T_g, where the slope changes — this is the **glass transition**, a kinetic, not thermodynamic, event.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define glass. List Zachariasen's rules for glass formation.**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Glass is an amorphous, inorganic, non-metallic solid material typically obtained by rapid quenching of a melt to prevent crystallization. It lacks long-range atomic order but exhibits short-range order over a few atomic distances.

**Zachariasen's Four Rules:**

1. An oxygen atom must not be linked to more than **two cations**.
2. The coordination number of the cation (M) must be **small** (typically 3 or 4).
3. Cation–oxygen polyhedra must share **corners only**, not edges or faces.
4. At least **three corners** of each polyhedron must be shared to form a continuous 3D network.

*(3 marks: 1 for definition + 2 for the four rules)*

---

#### **Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Distinguish between network formers, modifiers, and intermediates with examples.**

**Model Answer:**

| Category | Role | Coordination | Examples |
|----------|------|--------------|----------|
| **Network Former** | Builds the backbone of the random network via strong covalent M–O bonds | 3 or 4 | SiO₂, B₂O₃, P₂O₅, GeO₂ |
| **Network Modifier** | Breaks Si–O–Si bonds, introduces non-bridging oxygens (NBO), reduces T_g and viscosity | 6 or higher | Na₂O, K₂O, CaO, MgO, PbO |
| **Network Intermediate** | Can act as former or modifier depending on glass composition | 4–6 | Al₂O₃, ZnO, ZrO₂, TiO₂ |

*(3 marks: 1 mark per category with examples)*

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Internal Choice Rule (KTU 2024 Scheme):** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

---

### **Question A [14 Marks] [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**(a)** Explain the **Random Network Theory** of glass structure as proposed by Zachariasen and experimentally verified by Warren. Discuss the role of network modifiers in disrupting the network. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A soda-lime glass has the composition: 72 wt% SiO₂, 15 wt% Na₂O, 9 wt% CaO, 3 wt% MgO, 1 wt% Al₂O₃. Calculate the **number of non-bridging oxygens per silicon atom (NBO/Si)**. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to Question A(a):**

**Random Network Theory (RNT):**

According to Zachariasen (1932), the structure of an oxide glass (e.g., SiO₂) consists of a **3D continuous random network (CRN)** of cation–oxygen polyhedra. The key postulates are:

1. Each cation M is surrounded by a fixed number of oxygen atoms forming a polyhedron (e.g., **SiO₄ tetrahedron** in silicate glass).
2. Each oxygen is bonded to **at most two cations**.
3. The polyhedra are linked at **corners**, not edges or faces, allowing angular flexibility.
4. The network is **random** — bond angles and torsion angles vary over a range (e.g., Si–O–Si angle varies from 120° to 180°).

**Warren's Experimental Confirmation (1930s):**

B.E. Warren used **X-ray diffraction** on silica glass and observed:
- A sharp first peak at d ≈ 0.162 nm corresponding to **Si–O bond length**
- A second broader peak at d ≈ 0.32 nm corresponding to **O–O distance in tetrahedra**
- Absence of sharp Bragg peaks (rules out crystalline order)
- A radial distribution function showing **short-range order** (tetrahedral geometry preserved) but **no long-range periodicity**

This confirmed Zachariasen's CRN model and earned the framework the name **Warren–Zachariasen Random Network Model**.

**Role of Network Modifiers (Na₂O, CaO, MgO):**

When a modifier oxide like Na₂O is added to silica glass, the following reaction occurs at high temperature:

$$\equiv \text{Si}-\text{O}-\text{Si}\equiv + \text{Na}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \equiv \text{Si}-\text{O}^{-}\text{Na}^{+} + \text{Na}^{+}\text{O}^{-}-\text{Si}\equiv$$

**Effects:**
- Each Na₂O molecule **breaks one Si–O–Si bridge**, creating **two non-bridging oxygens (NBOs)**
- Na⁺ ions reside in the interstitial voids, electrostatically balancing the negatively charged NBOs
- The network becomes **less polymerized**
- Properties change:
  - **T_g decreases** (e.g., pure SiO₂ T_g = 1473 K → soda-lime T_g ≈ 820 K)
  - **Viscosity decreases** (easier to melt and form)
  - **Thermal expansion α increases** (weaker network)
  - **Chemical durability decreases** (more vulnerable to water attack)

Thus, modifiers are deliberately added to **control processability** while compromising on thermal and chemical performance.

---

#### **Model Solution to Question A(b):**

**Given composition (by weight %):**

| Oxide | wt% | M (g/mol) | Moles (mol) |
|-------|-----|-----------|-------------|
| SiO₂ | 72 | 60.08 | 72 / 60.08 = **1.1986** |
| Na₂O | 15 | 61.98 | 15 / 61.98 = **0.2420** |
| CaO | 9 | 56.08 | 9 / 56.08 = **0.1605** |
| MgO | 3 | 40.30 | 3 / 40.30 = **0.0744** |
| Al₂O₃ | 1 | 101.96 | 1 / 101.96 = **0.0098** |

**Step 1 — Number of Network Formers (Si, Al):**

Each mole of SiO₂ contributes **1 Si atom**.
Each mole of Al₂O₃ contributes **2 Al atoms**.

$$N_{\text{Si}} = 1.1986\ \text{mol}$$
$$N_{\text{Al}} = 2 \times 0.0098 = 0.0196\ \text{mol}$$

Total network former moles (treating Al as former, valid when Na/Al ratio > 1):

$$N_F = 1.1986 + 0.0196 = 1.2182\ \text{mol}$$

**Step 2 — Number of NBO generated by modifiers:**

Each mole of Na₂O contributes **2 NBOs** (since it has 1 O per Na, and breaks 1 Si–O–Si bridge, creating 2 NBOs effectively per Na₂O via dissociation):

Wait — more accurately, each Na₂O contributes **2 Na⁺ ions**, each balancing one NBO⁻.

So:

$$N_{\text{NBO from Na}} = 2 \times N_{\text{Na}_2\text{O}} = 2 \times 0.2420 = 0.4840$$

Each mole of CaO contributes **1 NBO** (Ca²⁺ balances 2 NBOs, but 1 CaO = 1 Ca²⁺ = balances 2 NBO⁻):

$$N_{\text{NBO from Ca}} = 2 \times 0.1605 = 0.3210$$

Each mole of MgO contributes similarly:

$$N_{\text{NBO from Mg}} = 2 \times 0.0744 = 0.1488$$

**Total NBOs:**

$$N_{\text{NBO}} = 0.4840 + 0.3210 + 0.1488 = 0.9538\ \text{mol}$$

**Step 3 — Compute NBO per Si atom (treating only Si as network former for NBO/Si definition):**

$$\frac{N_{\text{NBO}}}{N_{\text{Si}}} = \frac{0.9538}{1.1986} \approx 0.796$$

$$\boxed{\dfrac{N_{\text{NBO}}}{N_{\text{Si}}} \approx 0.80}$$

**Interpretation:** Approximately 0.80 non-bridging oxygens exist per silicon atom, indicating a moderately depolymerized network — consistent with a workable soda-lime glass having moderate viscosity at melting temperatures.

---

### **Question B [14 Marks] [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a)** With the help of a **schematic V–T (volume vs temperature) diagram**, explain the **glass transition phenomenon**. Discuss why the glass transition is a **kinetic** and not a **thermodynamic** transition. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A borosilicate glass plate is suddenly quenched from 250 °C to 25 °C. Given:
- Young's Modulus E = 64 GPa
- Poisson's Ratio ν = 0.20
- Linear thermal expansion coefficient α = 3.3 × 10⁻⁶ K⁻¹
- Tensile strength σ_f = 30 MPa

Calculate the **thermal stress developed** and determine whether the plate will **fracture**. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to Question B(a):**

**Schematic V–T Diagram:**

```
Volume V
   |
   |  *  (Crystal)
   |   \
   |    \
   |     *  (Supercooled liquid)
   |      \
   |       \  <-- slope = liquid expansion
   |        \
   |         * T_g (kink point)
   |          \
   |           \  <-- slope = solid (glass) expansion
   |            \
   |             * (Glass)
   |_________________________ Temperature T
            T_m       T_g
```

**Description of the Diagram:**

- **Path 1 (Slow cooling from melt):** Volume decreases linearly with T until **T_m** (melting point), where it drops **discontinuously** (latent heat release, first-order transition) to the crystalline state.
- **Path 2 (Fast cooling):** Volume follows the **supercooled liquid line** below T_m. At **T_g**, the slope changes discontinuously (but no latent heat) to a shallower slope matching the crystalline expansion rate. Below T_g, the material is a **rigid glass**.

**Why T_g is Kinetic, Not Thermodynamic:**

1. **No latent heat:** Unlike T_m, there is **no enthalpy discontinuity** at T_g — only a change in slope. This rules out a first-order thermodynamic transition.

2. **No unique temperature:** T_g depends on the **cooling rate**. Faster cooling shifts T_g to a higher temperature; slower cooling shifts T_g lower. This rate-dependence is a hallmark of kinetic processes.

3. **Relaxation times:** A glass is formed when the **structural relaxation time τ** of the supercooled liquid becomes comparable to the **experimental timescale**. Above T_g, τ is short (≪ cooling time) → equilibrium is maintained → liquid behavior. Below T_g, τ is enormous (≫ cooling time) → structure is frozen → glass behavior.

4. **Kauzmann paradox:** If the supercooled liquid could be cooled infinitely slowly below T_g, its entropy would drop below that of the crystal — a thermodynamic impossibility (Kauzmann, 1948). Nature avoids this by **kinetically trapping** the system as a glass at T_g. This confirms T_g is kinetic.

**Conclusion:** Glass transition is a **second-order kinetic transition** governed by molecular mobility and experimental timescale, not a true equilibrium phase change.

---

#### **Model Solution to Question B(b):**

**Given:**

- ΔT = T_initial − T_final = 250 − 25 = **225 K**
- E = 64 × 10⁹ Pa
- ν = 0.20
- α = 3.3 × 10⁻⁶ K⁻¹
- σ_f = 30 × 10⁶ Pa

**Step 1 — Compute Thermal Stress:**

$$\sigma_{\text{th}} = \frac{E \, \alpha \, \Delta T}{1 - \nu}$$

Substituting:

$$\sigma_{\text{th}} = \frac{(64 \times 10^{9})(3.3 \times 10^{-6})(225)}{1 - 0.20}$$

Computing the numerator:

$$\text{Numerator} = 64 \times 3.3 \times 225 = 47{,}520$$

$$64 \times 3.3 = 211.2$$
$$211.2 \times 225 = 47{,}520$$

With units: 47,520 × 10³ = **4.752 × 10⁷ Pa**

Denominator:

$$1 - 0.20 = 0.80$$

Therefore:

$$\sigma_{\text{th}} = \frac{4.752 \times 10^{7}}{0.80} = 5.94 \times 10^{7}\ \text{Pa}$$

$$\boxed{\sigma_{\text{th}} \approx 59.4\ \text{MPa}}$$

**Step 2 — Compare with Tensile Strength:**

- Developed stress: σ_th = **59.4 MPa**
- Allowable strength: σ_f = **30 MPa**

Since **σ_th (59.4 MPa) > σ_f (30 MPa)**, the plate **WILL FRACTURE**.

**Step 3 — Compute Critical ΔT for Survival:**

$$\Delta T_{\text{crit}} = \frac{\sigma_f (1-\nu)}{E \, \alpha} = \frac{(30 \times 10^{6})(0.80)}{(64 \times 10^{9})(3.3 \times 10^{-6})}$$

$$\Delta T_{\text{crit}} = \frac{24 \times 10^{6}}{2.112 \times 10^{5}} \approx 113.6\ \text{K}$$

Since actual ΔT = **225 K** ≫ ΔT_crit = **113.6 K**, fracture is inevitable.

**Step 4 — Engineering Recommendations:**

- Use **tempered borosilicate** (σ_f can be raised to 200 MPa via ion exchange)
- Switch to **fused silica** (α = 0.5 × 10⁻⁶ K⁻¹ → ΔT_crit ≈ 750 K)
- Apply **controlled cooling** (annealing) to reduce thermal gradient

---

### ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Commonly Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Confusing "Glass Former" with "Modifier":** Writing Na₂O as a glass former is the most common error. Na₂O is a **modifier**, period.
>
> 2. **Mixing up Bridging Oxygen (BO) and Non-Bridging Oxygen (NBO):** A bridging oxygen is bonded to **two** network-forming cations; an NBO is bonded to **one** network former and balanced by a modifier cation. Diagrams must clearly distinguish both.
>
> 3. **Calculating NBO/Si incorrectly:** Forgetting that each Na₂O contributes **2 NBOs** (not 1). Many students write NBO = moles of Na₂O, which is **wrong** by a factor of 2.
>
> 4. **Treating T_g as a Thermodynamic Transition:** T_g is **kinetic** and depends on cooling rate. Writing "T_g is a first-order transition" will cost full marks.
>
> 5. **Formula Errors in Thermal Stress:** The denominator must be (1 − ν), not ν. A frequent slip.
>
> 6. **Skipping Units:** KTU examiners **penalize** missing units in numerical answers. Always write Pa, MPa, GPa, K⁻¹ explicitly.
>
> 7. **Not Drawing Diagrams:** For 7-mark sub-parts, a **labelled diagram** is **mandatory** to score full marks. A textual-only answer loses at least 2 marks.

---

### 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Glass (Module 1)**
>
> ✅ **Definition:** Glass = amorphous inorganic solid with no long-range order, formed by quenching a melt; metastable, lacks sharp melting point.
>
> ✅ **Zachariasen's Rules (4 rules):** (1) O bonded to ≤ 2 cations; (2) Small cation CN (3 or 4); (3) Corner-sharing polyhedra; (4) ≥ 3 corners shared.
>
> ✅ **Three Structural Roles:** Former (Si, B, P, Ge) — Modifier (Na, K, Ca, Mg) — Intermediate (Al, Zn, Ti).
>
> ✅ **Random Network Theory (Warren):** SiO₄ tetrahedra linked at corners; Si–O–Si angle varies 120°–180°; Si–O bond = 0.162 nm.
>
> ✅ **Glass Transition (T_g):** Kinetic, second-order; cooling-rate dependent; no latent heat; relates to molecular relaxation time τ.
>
> ✅ **Kauzmann's Rule:** T_g ≈ (2/3) × T_m (in Kelvin).
>
> ✅ **Bridging Oxygen (BO):** Bonds 2 network formers; Non-Bridging Oxygen (NBO): bonds 1 former + 1 modifier.
>
> ✅ **Arrhenius Viscosity:** $\eta = A \exp(E/RT)$ — valid for T > T_g; VFT equation for full range.
>
> ✅ **Thermal Stress:** $\sigma_{\text{th}} = \dfrac{E \alpha \Delta T}{1-\nu}$; **Critical ΔT:** $\Delta T_{\text{crit}} = \dfrac{\sigma_f(1-\nu)}{E\alpha}$.
>
> ✅ **Low α = High Thermal Shock Resistance:** Fused silica (α ≈ 0.5×10⁻⁶ K⁻¹) ≫ Borosilicate ≫ Soda-lime.
>
> ✅ **Types of Glass:** Silicate (90% market), Borate, Phosphate, Germanate, Chalcogenide, Halide, Metallic.
>
> ✅ **Lorentz–Lorenz Equation:** $\dfrac{n^2 - 1}{n^2 + 2} \cdot \dfrac{M}{\rho} = R$ — used for optical glass design.
>
> ✅ **Key Industrial Glasses to Remember:** Soda-lime (windows), Borosilicate/Pyrex (labware), Fused silica (optics), Aluminosilicate (smartphones), Lead crystal (decorative).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Refractories
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEMUT412 - COMPOSITE MATERIALS AND CERAMICS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Refractories # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology) A **Refractory** is a special class of inorganic, non-metallic, polycrystalline ceramic material that is chemically and physically stable at **elevated temperatures (typically above 1500 °C)**, possesses a high melting point, and resists the corrosive action of molten metals, slags, gases, and chemicals even under simultaneous mechanical load, thermal shock, and chemical attack. > [!NOTE] > **KTU Board Definition (High-Weight):** A refractory is *“an inorganic, non-metallic material that can withstand high temperatures and corrosive action of gases, liquids, and slags without undergoing significant deformation or chemical change.”* ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Engineering Intuition Think of a **refractory** as the **"heat-shield armor"** of an industrial furnace — the same way a firefighter's suit protects the body from fire, a refractory brick lines the inside walls of a furnace and protects the outer steel shell from the **brutal temperatures of molten steel (~1650 °C)**, aggressive slag chemistry, and abrasive wear. **Real-world analogy:** Imagine pouring boiling water (100 °C) into a **paper cup** vs. a **ceramic mug**. The paper cup fails instantly, but the ceramic mug holds because of its low thermal conductivity, high heat capacity, and chemical stability. Refractories perform this same role — but at **temperatures 15× higher** than boiling water. ## 1.3 The "3R" Property Triad of Refractories Every KTU-board question on refractories eventually traces back to these three core attributes: | Property | Symbol | What it measures | |----------|--------|------------------| | **Refractoriness** | — | Ability to withstand high temperature without melting/deforming | | **RUL (Refractoriness Under Load)** | $T_{0.5}, T_{1}, T_{2}$ | Ability to bear load at high temperature | | **Resistance to Thermal Shock** | $R_{ts}$ | Ability to survive rapid temperature change | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Syllabus Highlight (PEMUT412 / M1):** Refractoriness is *NOT* the melting point. It is the **temperature at which the refractory begins to deform (softens) under its own weight** — this distinction is frequently asked as a 3-mark question. ## 1.4 Standard Metrics & Units Used Throughout KTU Board Exams - **Pyrometric Cone Equivalent (PCE):** Dimensionless cone number (e.g., Cone 30, Cone 35) - **Refractoriness Under Load (RUL):** Temperature in **°C** (typically 1500–1700 °C for high-duty refractories) - **Porosity (Apparent):** Expressed as **% volume** (range 5–30 %) - **Thermal Conductivity:** $k$ in **W/(m·K)** - **Cold Crushing Strength (CCS):** in **N/mm² or MPa** - **Bulk Density (BD):** in **g/cm³ or kg/m³** - **Coefficient of Thermal Expansion (CTE):** $\alpha$ in **/°C or /K** # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Engineering Demands on a Refractory (Why so special?) A refractory inside a steel-making furnace must simultaneously survive **five hostile conditions**. Each one rules out a family of materials: 1. **High Temperature** (up to 1800 °C) → Rules out all organic polymers and most metals. 2. **Mechanical Load** (weight of furnace charge) → Rules out glassy materials. 3. **Thermal Cycling** (heating/cooling cycles) → Rules out brittle materials like silica glass. 4. **Chemical Attack** (molten slag, fluxes, gases) → Rules out single-oxide ceramics without proper bonding. 5. **Abrasion & Erosion** (flowing molten metal, dust) → Rules out soft, porous materials. > [!TIP] > The **rule of thumb** taught in KTU coaching: *“A refractory fails by the WEAKEST of these five properties — not the strongest.”* This is why a refractory with 95 % alumina can still fail if it has high porosity and poor thermal-shock resistance. ## 2.2 Detailed Properties of Refractories ### 2.2.1 Physical Properties - **Bulk Density (BD):** Mass per unit total volume (including pores). Higher BD = denser brick = better load-bearing and abrasion resistance. - **Apparent Porosity (AP):** Ratio of open pore volume to total volume, expressed as a percentage. $$AP \,(\%) = \frac{W_{sat} - W_{dry}}{W_{sat} - W_{susp}} \times 100$$ - **True Porosity:** Includes closed pores; always ≥ apparent porosity. - **Cold Crushing Strength (CCS):** Measured at **room temperature**; high CCS does NOT guarantee high-temperature strength (hence RUL exists). - **Permeability:** Measure of how easily gases pass through the brick; controlled by pore-size distribution. ### 2.2.2 Thermal Properties - **Refractoriness (PCE):** Measured using **Seger Cones** — pyramid-shaped samples that soften at a known heating rate. - **Refractoriness Under Load (RUL):** Measured by heating a cylindrical specimen under a fixed compressive load (typically **0.2 N/mm² = 2 kg/cm²**) and recording the temperature at which it deforms by 0.5 %, 1 %, or 2 %. $$T_{0.5}, \; T_{1}, \; T_{2} \quad \text{(in °C)}$$ - **Thermal Conductivity ($k$):** Low $k$ → insulating refractory (e.g., fireclay). High $k$ → conductive refractory (e.g., SiC, graphite). - **Thermal Expansion (CTE, $\alpha$):** Low $\alpha$ → better thermal-shock resistance. - **Thermal Shock Resistance ($R_{ts}$):** Empirical metric; high $R_{ts}$ = can survive quenching from 1000 °C into water without cracking. ### 2.2.3 Chemical Properties - **Chemical Composition:** Expressed in terms of **RO, R₂O₃, RO₂** groups. - **RO** = basic oxides (CaO, MgO) - **R₂O₃** = neutral/amphoteric oxides (Al₂O₃, Cr₂O₃) - **RO₂** = acidic oxides (SiO₂, ZrO₂, TiO₂) - **Resistance to Slag Attack:** A basic refractory (MgO) is attacked by acidic slag (SiO₂-rich) but resists basic slag (CaO-rich). The opposite is true for acidic refractories. - **Resistance to CO Attack:** Important for blast-furnace linings — iron oxide impurities catalyze the reaction $2CO \rightarrow CO_2 + C$ (carbon deposition), which shatters bricks. ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | # | Formula / Relation | Meaning | Unit | |---|-------------------|---------|------| | 1 | $AP = \dfrac{W_{sat} - W_{dry}}{W_{sat} - W_{susp}} \times 100$ | Apparent porosity | % | | 2 | $BD = \dfrac{W_{dry}}{V_{total}}$ | Bulk density | g/cm³ | | 3 | $T_{0.5}, T_{1}, T_{2}$ | RUL at 0.5 %, 1 %, 2 % deformation | °C | | 4 | $R_{ts} = \dfrac{k \cdot S}{\alpha \cdot E}$ | Thermal-shock resistance parameter (Hasselman) | — | | 5 | $q = \dfrac{k}{\rho \cdot c_p}$ | Thermal diffusivity | m²/s | | 6 | $\Delta L = L_0 \cdot \alpha \cdot \Delta T$ | Linear thermal expansion | m | | 7 | $CCS = \dfrac{F_{max}}{A_0}$ | Cold crushing strength | N/mm² | | 8 | $PCE \leftrightarrow$ Cone number | Empirically linked to softening T | Cone no. | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Hasselman's Thermal-Shock Parameter (Eq. 4):** $R_{ts}$ is *higher* when $k$ and $S$ (strength) are high, and *lower* when $\alpha$ and $E$ (elastic modulus) are high. This is why porous fireclay bricks (low $E$, low $k$, low $\alpha$) have **better thermal-shock resistance** than dense magnesia bricks. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility | Industry | Application of Refractory | Working Temperature | |----------|---------------------------|---------------------| | Iron & Steel (BOF, EAF, ladle) | Ladle lining, tundish, blast-furnace hearth | 1500–1750 °C | | Cement (rotary kiln) | Burning zone, transition zone | 1400–1600 °C | | Glass (tank furnace) | Tank blocks, regenerator | 1500–1600 °C | | Petrochemical (FCC) | Reactor lining | 600–800 °C | | Power (CFBC boiler) | Cyclone, loop seal | 850–950 °C | | Non-ferrous (Cu/Ni smelter) | Converter, anode furnace | 1200–1350 °C | The **largest consumer** of refractories globally is the **iron & steel industry (~60 %)**, followed by cement (~15 %) and glass (~8 %). # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Numerical Analysis ## 3.1 Worked Problem 1 — Apparent Porosity (3-Mark KTU-Style) **Problem:** A refractory brick was tested in the laboratory. The dry weight $W_{dry}$ = **2850 g**, the weight when fully saturated with water $W_{sat}$ = **3010 g**, and the weight when suspended in water $W_{susp}$ = **1740 g**. Compute the apparent porosity of the brick. ### Step 1 — Identify the correct formula From the KTU Formula Sheet (Section 2.3, Eq. 1): $$AP \,(\%) = \frac{W_{sat} - W_{dry}}{W_{sat} - W_{susp}} \times 100$$ ### Step 2 — Substitute the given numerical values - $W_{sat} - W_{dry} = 3010 - 2850 = 160 \text{ g}$ *(this represents the volume of open pores filled with water)* - $W_{sat} - W_{susp} = 3010 - 1740 = 1270 \text{ g}$ *(this represents the total volume of the brick, by Archimedes' principle)* ### Step 3 — Perform the ratio calculation $$AP = \frac{160}{1270} \times 100$$ $$AP = 0.12598 \times 100 = 12.60 \, \%$$ ### Step 4 — Engineering Interpretation > [!NOTE] > A porosity of **12.6 %** places this brick in the **medium-duty fireclay** category. For a high-duty blast-furnace hearth brick, the desired porosity is **< 10 %**. For an insulating refractory, porosity of **60–80 %** is acceptable because trapped air provides thermal insulation. --- ## 3.2 Worked Problem 2 — RUL & Refractoriness Distinction (Common KTU Trap) **Problem (KTU 2022 Pattern):** A refractory brick has a PCE value of Cone 33, an RUL ($T_{1}$) of 1620 °C, and a melting point of 1780 °C. Explain why the refractoriness of the brick is reported as **1620 °C** and not 1780 °C. ### Step 1 — Recall definitions - **Melting point** = the temperature at which the *solid* becomes a *fully liquid* phase. - **Refractoriness** = the temperature at which the *bricks begin to deform under load* — i.e., where the **liquid phase is sufficient to allow viscous flow** under the applied 0.2 N/mm² load. - Refractories are **multi-phase, polycrystalline ceramics** with a glassy/oxide matrix. The first liquid forms at the **eutectic temperature**, which is far below the pure-oxide melting point. ### Step 2 — Why is $T_1 < T_{melt}$? At $T_1$ = 1620 °C, the brick contains enough liquid phase to deform by **1 %** under the 0.2 N/mm² load, even though the bulk solid has not melted. Above 1780 °C, the brick is a fully flowing liquid and is no longer useful as a structural lining. ### Step 3 — Engineering conclusion > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rule of Valuation:** "Refractoriness ≠ Melting point." Always quote **RUL** as the service temperature, with a safety factor of **~150–200 °C below $T_1$**. In this example, the **safe service temperature is ~1430–1450 °C**. --- ## 3.3 Worked Problem 3 — Bulk Density and True Density Relationship **Problem:** A refractory brick has a true density $\rho_{true} = 3.85 \text{ g/cm}^3$ and an apparent porosity of 18 %. Calculate its bulk density. ### Step 1 — Recall relation The relationship between bulk density, true density, and porosity is: $$BD = \rho_{true} \times (1 - P_{true})$$ where $P_{true}$ is the *true* (total) porosity. For an open-pore brick, $P_{true} \approx AP$ when closed porosity is negligible. ### Step 2 — Substitute $$BD = 3.85 \times (1 - 0.18)$$ $$BD = 3.85 \times 0.82$$ $$BD = 3.157 \text{ g/cm}^3$$ ### Step 3 — Compare with the "rule of thumb" reference table | Brick type | Typical BD (g/cm³) | Typical AP (%) | |-----------|-------------------|----------------| | Fireclay (low duty) | 1.8 – 2.0 | 20 – 30 | | Fireclay (high duty) | 2.2 – 2.4 | 10 – 18 | | Magnesia | 2.8 – 3.0 | 15 – 20 | | Chrome-magnesia | 3.0 – 3.2 | 15 – 22 | | **Calculated brick** | **3.157** | **18** | The calculated brick most closely matches a **chrome-magnesia** composition. --- ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Property Trade-off This is the most frequently tested concept in KTU 14-mark questions — understanding **why** a property is chosen for a given application: | Property | Increase Effect on Refractory | Trade-off (the catch) | |----------|-------------------------------|------------------------| | ↑ Alumina (Al₂O₃) content | ↑ Refractoriness, ↑ Hardness, ↑ Slag resistance | ↑ Cost, ↓ Thermal-shock resistance | | ↑ Porosity | ↑ Insulation, ↑ Thermal-shock resistance | ↓ Strength (CCS), ↑ Permeability | | ↑ Bulk density | ↑ Strength, ↑ Erosion resistance | ↑ Thermal conductivity, ↑ Weight | | ↑ Magnesia (MgO) content | ↑ Basic-slag resistance | ↓ Hydration resistance (reacts with H₂O) | | ↑ SiO₂ content | ↑ Acid-slag resistance | ↓ Refractoriness (low melting) | | ↑ Chrome (Cr₂O₃) | ↑ Neutrality, ↑ Strength | Toxicity (Cr⁶⁺), environmental issue | | ↑ ZrO₂ content | ↑ Thermal-shock resistance | ↑ Cost, phase transformation at 1170 °C | > [!TIP] > **KTU Examiner's Tip:** The "best" refractory is **NOT** the one with the highest refractoriness. It is the one that **balances the trade-off matrix** for the *specific* service environment (slag chemistry, temperature, thermal cycling). # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Refractory Lining System The following block diagram shows the **layered architecture** of a typical industrial furnace lining, mapping how refractories of different grades work together as a **functional system**: ```mermaid flowchart TB A["Hot Face — Molten Metal / Slag Zone
Temperature 1500 to 1750 C
Highest Refractoriness Required"]:::hot B["Working Lining — Primary Refractory
High Alumina or Magnesia Brick
PCE 35 to 38 RUL above 1650 C"]:::work C["Safety Lining — Backup Layer
Fireclay or High Alumina
Protects Shell in Case of Wear"]:::safe D["Insulating Layer — Low k Brick
Ceramic Fibre or Insulating Castable
k 0.2 to 0.5 W per mK"]:::ins E["Outer Steel Shell
Provides Mechanical Support
No Direct Contact with Heat"]:::shell F["Cooling System — Water or Air
Removes Residual Heat
Maintains Shell below 200 C"]:::cool A --> B B --> C C --> D D --> E E --> F classDef hot fill:#ff6b6b,stroke:#7a0000,color:#ffffff classDef work fill:#ffa94d,stroke:#7a3d00,color:#000000 classDef safe fill:#ffd43b,stroke:#7a6500,color:#000000 classDef ins fill:#69db7c,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#000000 classDef shell fill:#4dabf7,stroke:#0d47a1,color:#000000 classDef cool fill:#b197fc,stroke:#311b92,color:#ffffff ``` **Engineering interpretation of the diagram:** - The **hot face** receives the maximum thermal and chemical assault; it uses the most expensive, highest-grade refractory (e.g., magnesia-carbon in BOF, or fused-cast AZS in glass tanks). - The **insulating layer** keeps the outer steel shell cool, saving fuel and protecting personnel. - Each layer's job is **different**, so each layer uses a **different refractory type** — this is why a single furnace may contain **3–5 different refractory grades**. --- ## 4.2 Classification Flowchart — Refractories by Chemistry ```mermaid flowchart LR R["REFRACTORIES"]:::root --> AC["Acidic"]:::acid R --> BA["Basic"]:::basic R --> NE["Neutral"]:::neutral AC --> AC1["Silica Brick - 95 percent SiO2"] AC --> AC2["Semi-Silica Brick"] AC --> AC3["Zircon - ZrSiO4"] BA --> BA1["Magnesia - 85 percent MgO"] BA --> BA2["Dolomite - CaO and MgO"] BA --> BA3["Chrome-Magnesia"] NE --> NE1["Fireclay - 25 to 45 percent Al2O3"] NE --> NE2["High Alumina - 45 to 95 percent Al2O3"] NE --> NE3["Chromite - Cr2O3"] NE --> NE4["Carbon - C"] NE --> NE5["Silicon Carbide - SiC"] classDef root fill:#2b2d42,stroke:#000000,color:#ffffff classDef acid fill:#e63946,stroke:#7a0000,color:#ffffff classDef basic fill:#1d3557,stroke:#000080,color:#ffffff classDef neutral fill:#2a9d8f,stroke:#005f4f,color:#ffffff ``` **Memory mnemonic for KTU exams:** **"SAN"** → **S**ilica = **A**cidic, m**A**gnesia = **B**asic, others = **N**eutral. --- ## 4.3 Test Method — RUL Apparatus Schematic ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph TP["RUL Test Rig"] T1["Furnace Chamber with Controlled Heating"]:::rig T2["Load Applied: 0.2 N per mm squared"]:::rig T3["Specimen: Cylinder 50 mm dia x 50 mm height"]:::rig T4["Displacement Transducer Records Deformation"]:::rig T5["Plot: Temperature vs Deformation percent"]:::rig end T1 --> T2 --> T3 --> T4 --> T5 classDef rig fill:#fefae0,stroke:#7a6500,color:#000000 ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1.
