```mermaid
    classDef pneu fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100,color:#000
    classDef grav fill:#f3e5f5,stroke:#4a148c,color:#000
    class B1,C1,C2,C3,C4,C5 mech
    class B2,D1,D2,D3 pneu
    class B3,E1,E2,E3 grav
```

---

## 4.3 Schematic: Silo Discharge Patterns (Mass Flow vs Funnel Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph S1["Funnel Flow Silo (Poor Design)"]
        S1A[Inlet Top] --> S1B[Stagnant Zone\nRatholing]
        S1B --> S1C[Channel of Flow]
        S1C --> S1D[Outlet]
    end
    
    subgraph S2["Mass Flow Silo (Good Design)"]
        S2A[Inlet Top] --> S2B[Uniform Downflow]
        S2B --> S2C[Steep Cone Hopper]
        S2C --> S2D[Outlet]
    end
    
    S1Note["Problem: Segregation, rat-holing,\nfirst-in-last-out discharge"]:::warn
    S2Note["Advantage: First-in-first-out,\nno segregation, predictable discharge"]:::good
    
    S1Note -.- S1
    S2Note -.- S2
    
    classDef warn fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#cc0000,color:#000
    classDef good fill:#ccffcc,stroke:#006600,color:#000
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For ceramic raw materials, mass flow silos are preferred** to avoid segregation (fine vs coarse particles separating) and rat-holing. Hopper half-angle must be **steeper than the angle of internal friction** of the stored material.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**Q: Differentiate between mass flow and funnel flow in silos. Which one is preferred for storing ceramic raw materials and why?**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Mass Flow | Funnel Flow |
|--------|-----------|-------------|
| Flow pattern | All material moves downward uniformly | Channel forms in the center; stagnant zones at walls |
| Discharge sequence | First-in-first-out (FIFO) | First-in-last-out (FILO) |
| Segregation | Minimal | Severe |
| Rat-holing | Absent | Common |
| Hopper angle | Steep (> angle of internal friction) | Shallow |
| Capital cost | Higher (steeper cone) | Lower |

**Preferred for ceramics: Mass flow** — because ceramic powders are prone to segregation (different particle sizes of clay, feldspar, silica), and mass flow FIFO discharge ensures consistent batch composition. Also avoids rat-holing and bridging.

> [!NOTE]
> **Marking scheme (KTU 2024):** 2 marks for the comparison table, 1 mark for the justification.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**Q: State the Janssen equation for vertical pressure in a silo. Define each term and explain the asymptotic significance.**

**Model Answer:**

$$
\sigma_v(h) = \frac{\gamma R}{\mu K}\left(1 - e^{-\mu K h / R}\right)
$$

**Definitions:**
- $\sigma_v$ = vertical pressure on horizontal cross-section at depth $h$ (Pa)
- $\gamma$ = bulk density $\times g$ (N/m³)
- $R$ = hydraulic radius of silo (m)
- $\mu$ = coefficient of wall friction (dimensionless)
- $K$ = lateral pressure ratio (dimensionless)
- $h$ = depth below top surface of powder (m)

**Asymptotic significance:** As $h \to \infty$, $\sigma_v \to \sigma_{v,\infty} = \gamma R / (\mu K)$, which is **finite and independent of depth**. This means: (i) deeper silos do not require progressively stronger walls beyond a critical depth, (ii) the design pressure is governed by silo **diameter** (via $R$), not height.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Pattern:** Each Part B question has two sub-parts: (a) 7 marks, (b) 7 marks. Internal choice: either (a) + (b) of Q1 OR (a) + (b) of Q2. The choice is yours.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

#### (a) Classify the various types of conveyors used in ceramic industries. Discuss the suitability of belt conveyors and pneumatic conveyors for ceramic raw material handling. (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Classification of conveyors** (drawn as a tree or list, 2 marks):
1. **Mechanical:** Belt, screw, bucket elevator, vibratory, chain, apron
2. **Pneumatic:** Dilute phase, dense phase, vacuum
3. **Gravity:** Chutes, spiral chutes, hopper discharges

**Belt conveyors — suitability (2.5 marks):**
- **Best for:** Long-distance horizontal transport (up to 1 km) of free-flowing, non-abrasive, dry ceramic raw materials like crushed quartz, feldspar, dry clay.
- **Advantages:** High capacity (100–1000+ tonnes/h), low power per tonne, low maintenance.
- **Limitations:** Open construction → dust emission → needs enclosures. Cannot handle hot (>150°C) materials. Not suitable for cohesive or wet clays.

**Pneumatic conveyors — suitability (2.5 marks):**
- **Best for:** Enclosed transfer of fine, dry ceramic powders (e.g., spray-dried granules in tile plants, frits, glaze powders).
- **Advantages:** Totally enclosed → no dust, no contamination, can go around obstacles via pipe bends, occupies less floor space.
- **Limitations:** High energy consumption (5–10× belt conveyors for same duty), particle attrition, pipe wear from abrasive powders, limited distance (~300 m).

**Conclusion:** Belt conveyors are used for **bulk raw material transport** at the start of the process; pneumatic conveyors are used for **finished/intermediate powder transfer** in enclosed, hygienic conditions.

#### (b) A horizontal screw conveyor is used to transfer alumina powder at 8 tonnes/h over a length of 12 m. The bulk density of alumina is 1600 kg/m³ and the friction factor is 2.5. Calculate: (i) the volumetric capacity, (ii) the power required, and (iii) the motor power assuming a drive efficiency of 90%. (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Given:** $Q = 8$ tonnes/h, $L = 12$ m, $\rho_b = 1600$ kg/m³, $f = 2.5$, $H = 0$ (horizontal), $\eta_d = 0.9$.

**(i) Volumetric capacity:**

$$
V = \frac{Q}{\rho_b} = \frac{8 \times 1000}{1600} = 5.0 \text{ m}^3/\text{h}
$$

**(ii) Power required (horizontal only since $H=0$):**

$$
P_{horiz} = \frac{Q \cdot L \cdot f}{367} = \frac{8 \times 12 \times 2.5}{367} = \frac{240}{367} = 0.654 \text{ kW}
$$

**(iii) Motor power:**

$$
P_{motor} = \frac{P_{horiz}}{\eta_d} = \frac{0.654}{0.9} = 0.727 \text{ kW}
$$

**Select standard motor: 0.75 kW (or 1.0 kW for safety margin).**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

#### (a) Explain the Janssen equation and discuss its importance in the design of silos for ceramic raw materials. A cylindrical silo has a diameter of 4 m and stores ceramic powder with bulk density 1200 kg/m³, wall friction coefficient 0.4, and lateral pressure ratio 0.5. Calculate the maximum vertical pressure at the bottom. (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Janssen equation derivation (3 marks):**

Consider an elemental horizontal slice of powder of thickness $dh$ at depth $h$ in a cylindrical silo of hydraulic radius $R$. Equilibrium of the slice gives:

$$
\gamma \, dh = d\sigma_v + \frac{\mu K \sigma_v}{R} \, dh
$$

Solving with $\sigma_v(0) = 0$:

$$
\sigma_v(h) = \frac{\gamma R}{\mu K}\left(1 - e^{-\mu K h / R}\right)
$$

**Importance (1 mark):** Predicts wall pressure, governs silo wall thickness design, prevents catastrophic failures like **rat-holing, arching, and implosion**. For ceramic powders, the saturated pressure determines the required reinforcement.

**Numerical calculation (3 marks):**

Given: Diameter $D = 4$ m, so $R = D/4 = 1.0$ m (hydraulic radius for circle).
$\gamma = \rho_b g = 1200 \times 9.81 = 11{,}772$ N/m³.
$\mu = 0.4$, $K = 0.5$, so $\mu K = 0.20$.

Maximum vertical pressure (as $h \to \infty$):

$$
\sigma_{v,\max} = \frac{\gamma R}{\mu K} = \frac{11{,}772 \times 1.0}{0.4 \times 0.5} = \frac{11{,}772}{0.2} = 58{,}860 \text{ Pa}
$$

$$
\boxed{\sigma_{v,\max} \approx 58.86 \text{ kPa}}
$$

#### (b) Explain the difference between angle of repose and angle of internal friction. Why is the angle of internal friction important in silo hopper design? (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

| Property | Angle of Repose ($\alpha$) | Angle of Internal Friction ($\phi$) |
|----------|---------------------------|-----------------------------------|
| Definition | Angle of the free surface of a heap formed by gravity pouring | Angle characterizing shear resistance between particles inside a bulk solid |
| Measured by | Pouring powder into a heap, measuring slope | Direct shear test or triaxial test |
| Typical value (ceramic powder) | 30° – 45° | 25° – 40° |
| Used in | Sizing storage pile, calculating pile volume | Silo hopper design, flowability assessment |

**Importance in silo hopper design (4 marks):**
- The **hopper half-angle** must be **steeper** than the angle of internal friction $\phi$ to ensure **mass flow** discharge.
- If hopper half-angle is too shallow (less than $\phi$), the material will form a stable arch or rat-hole — **funnel flow** with stagnant zones.
- For ceramics, since powders are often cohesive (especially when moist), $\phi$ may be high, requiring **steeper, more expensive conical hoppers**.
- Designers use the **Jenike method** to determine the minimum hopper angle for mass flow based on $\phi$ and the consolidation stress.
- Failure to design correctly leads to **arching** (no flow at all) or **flooding** (uncontrolled discharge) — both major plant downtime causes.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes students make (losing 1-3 marks each):**
> 1. **Unit inconsistency in screw conveyor formula:** Using diameter in m instead of mm gives a wrong answer by factor of $10^6$. Always check: $D$ in **mm**, $S$ in **mm**, $n$ in **rpm**, output is **tonnes/h** with the 47 prefactor.
> 2. **Forgetting the drive efficiency $\eta_d$** in motor power calculation. The shaft power and motor power are NOT the same.
> 3. **Confusing hydraulic radius $R$ with silo radius:** For a circular silo, $R_{hydraulic} = D/4$, not $D/2$.
> 4. **Not stating boundary conditions** of the Janssen ODE ($\sigma_v = 0$ at $h = 0$).
> 5. **Comparing belt vs pneumatic without specific material context** — always tie the answer to ceramic properties (abrasive, dusty, fine, sometimes cohesive).
> 6. **Mixing up angle of repose and angle of internal friction** — these are NOT the same and examiners test this distinction.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **High-density revision list — read this 30 minutes before the exam:**

- ✅ **Conveying** = movement of material; **Storage** = holding of material. Both are **auxiliary unit operations** in ceramic plants.
- ✅ **Belt conveyor capacity formula:** $Q = 3.6 k B^2 v \rho_b$ (tonnes/h) — memorize with $k$ values for 20°, 25°, 30° trough.
- ✅ **Screw conveyor capacity:** $Q_s = 47 D^2 S n \phi \rho_b$ (tonnes/h, D and S in mm).
- ✅ **Screw conveyor power:** $P = Q(Lf + H)/367$ for shaft; divide by $\eta_d$ for motor.
- ✅ **Janssen equation:** $\sigma_v = (\gamma R / \mu K)(1 - e^{-\mu K h / R})$ — saturates at $\gamma R / (\mu K)$ for large $h$.
- ✅ **Mass flow vs funnel flow:** Mass flow uses steep hopper; preferred for ceramics to avoid segregation.
- ✅ **Angle of repose** ≈ free-surface angle; **Angle of internal friction** ≈ inter-particle friction; both used in hopper design.
- ✅ **Hopper half-angle** must exceed angle of internal friction for mass flow.
- ✅ **Pneumatic conveying** is enclosed → low dust; preferred for fine ceramic powders (spray-dried granules, frits).
- ✅ **Belt conveyors** dominate long-distance horizontal transport of bulk ceramic raw materials.
- ✅ **Silos** decouple process units and ensure 24/7 plant operation.
- ✅ **Power efficiency:** Belt > Screw > Pneumatic (pneumatic is 5–10× more energy-intensive).
- ✅ **Design pressure** for silos is governed by **diameter**, not height, per Janssen asymptote.
- ✅ **KEEP UNITS CONSISTENT:** kg/m³, m, m/s, kW throughout the calculation chain.
- ✅ **For KTU 2024 Part B questions:** Always draw a labeled block diagram of the conveying/storage system (2-3 marks reserved for this).

---

*End of Module 1 Notes — Topic: Conveying and Storage*
*Prepared per KTU 2024 Scheme Outcome-Based Education Framework*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Slip forming
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT412 - COMPOSITE MATERIALS AND CERAMICS
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Slip forming

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal KTU Syllabus Definition

**Slip Forming** (more precisely termed **Slip Casting**) is a colloidal ceramic-shaping process in which a *fluid suspension of fine ceramic powder* in a liquid medium (called a **slip**) is poured into a porous, absorbent mold (traditionally made of **Plaster of Paris**, CaSO₄·½H₂O). The mold draws liquid out of the slip through **capillary action**, causing ceramic particles to deposit and consolidate against the mold walls, thereby producing a near-net-shape green body that is later dried, trimmed and sintered.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Terminology Lock-in**
> The official KTU PEMUT412 syllabus groups this under *conventional ceramic forming techniques*, distinct from *plastic forming* (extrusion) and *dry pressing*. In KTU answers, always use the terms **slip**, **plaster mold**, **drain casting**, and **solid casting** — examiners award marks for these keywords.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition

Imagine dipping a dry **unglazed terracotta flower pot** into a thin **milky liquid (the slip)**. If you lift the pot out after 30 seconds, a thin, soft, clay-like film clings to the inner walls of the pot. Why?

* The pot is **porous** — it has thousands of microscopic channels.
* These channels **drink water** from the milky liquid by *capillary suction* (just like a paper towel soaking a coffee spill).
* As water leaves, the suspended clay particles are **trapped** against the wall, packing together to form a solid skin.

**Slip casting in a plaster mold is exactly the same idea**, only the mold is shaped to the geometry of the final product. This is how every **washbasin, toilet bowl, and decorative vase** you have ever seen was once made.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why plaster?** Plaster of Paris is used because it is (a) cheap, (b) machinable, and (c) has the *right* pore size (~1–5 µm) to suck water at a controlled, industrial-friendly rate. Steel molds are impermeable and will not work.

### 1.3 Key Physical Quantities You Must Know

* **Slip viscosity** → typically **0.1 – 1.0 Pa·s** (controlled with deflocculants)
* **Casting rate (N)** → typically **10⁻⁶ to 10⁻⁴ m²/s**
* **Mold porosity** → **30 – 50 %** by volume
* **Solids loading** → **60 – 75 wt%** ceramic powder in the slip
* **Deflocculant dose** → **0.1 – 0.5 wt%** (e.g., sodium silicate, Darvan 7)

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Four Logical Steps of Slip Casting

1. **Slip Preparation**
   * Ceramic powder (Al₂O₃, SiO₂, ZrO₂, etc.) is ball-milled with water, a **deflocculant** (sodium silicate / Darvan), and sometimes a **binder** (PVA).
   * Goal: a **low-viscosity, high-solids** suspension that flows easily yet **does not sediment**.

2. **Mold Filling**
   * The slip is poured into the assembled plaster mold.
   * Air must escape — vents are provided at the parting line.

3. **Casting (the heart of the process)**
   * The plaster draws water inwards by **capillary pressure (ΔP_c)**.
   * Particles (typically 1–10 µm) are **too large to enter** the mold pores → they pile up on the inner surface.
   * The thickness **x** of this deposited layer **grows with time** *t*.

4. **De-molding & Drying**
   * Excess slip is drained (drain casting) OR the mold is opened (solid casting).
   * The green body is **delicately removed** (it is still wet and weak) and dried in humidity-controlled chambers.

### 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Step

* **Why a deflocculant?** Ceramic particles carry surface charges; without deflocculant they flocculate → high viscosity → poor flow, non-uniform cast. The deflocculant creates **electrostatic repulsion** → de-flocculated → fluid.
* **Why plaster of Paris?** The capillary radius must be **smaller than the particle radius** but large enough to conduct water. CaSO₄ gives the ideal pore distribution.
* **Why wait for the cast to thicken?** Strength is built by *particle packing* during the cast; casting time ≈ sqrt(desired wall thickness).

### 2.3 Two Variants of Slip Casting (HIGH-YIELD for KTU)

| Variant | Mold | Excess Slip | Wall Thickness | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Drain Casting** | One-piece, female | Poured **OUT** after a set time | Set by **time** | Hollow ware: cups, vases, basins |
| **Solid Casting** | Two-piece (with core) | Stays inside | Set by **cavity width** | Solid articles: toilet tanks, complex shapes |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU examiners often test whether you know that in **solid casting** the *whole cavity fills up* with cast body, whereas in **drain casting** only a *layer* of desired thickness forms and the rest is poured back. Memorise the line: **"Solid casting = no drain; Drain casting = controlled wall thickness by time."**

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning of Symbols | Units | Used To Find |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $x^{2} = N \cdot t$ | $x$ = wall thickness, $t$ = time, $N$ = casting constant | $x$ in m, $t$ in s, $N$ in m²/s | Wall thickness vs. time |
| 2 | $\dfrac{dx}{dt} = \dfrac{N}{2x}$ | Rate of wall growth | m/s | Instantaneous casting rate |
| 3 | $\Delta P_{c} = \dfrac{2 \gamma \cos\theta}{r}$ | Capillary pressure driving flow | Pa | Driving force for water removal |
| 4 | $N \propto \dfrac{\Delta P_{c} \cdot \phi}{\mu}$ | $\phi$ = mold permeability, $\mu$ = slip filtrate viscosity, $\gamma$ = surface tension | — | Factors controlling $N$ |
| 5 | $\eta_{rel} = \dfrac{\eta}{\eta_{0}} = \left(1 - \dfrac{\phi_{s}}{\phi_{m}}\right)^{-2}$ | Krieger–Dougherty equation: relative viscosity of slip | dimensionless | Slip rheology, max solids loading |
| 6 | $F = k \cdot A \cdot \dfrac{\Delta P_{c}}{\mu \cdot L}$ | Darcy's law for filtrate flow through cake | N (flow) | Filtrate flux through cast layer |
| 7 | $\rho_{cast} = \rho_{slip} \cdot \dfrac{\text{wt\% solids}}{\text{vol\% solids in cast}}$ | Slip-to-cast mass balance | kg/m³ | Density of cast body |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Slip casting is the **only economically viable route** for *hollow, thin-walled, large, or asymmetric* ceramic products. It dominates the **sanitaryware industry** (>95 % of the world's washbasins), **tableware**, **art pottery**, and certain **technical ceramics** (large crucibles, insulators).

### 2.5 Factors That Increase the Casting Constant $N$ (Examiner Favourite!)

* ↑ **Slip solids content** (more particles, less filtrate to move)
* ↑ **Capillary pressure** (finer mold pores, better wetting)
* ↑ **Mold permeability** (well-aged, dry plaster)
* ↓ **Slip filtrate viscosity** (warm water, deflocculant)
* ↑ **Vacuum-assisted** casting (external ΔP added)
* ↑ **Particle fineness** (more surface area, faster build-up, but also more shrinkage)

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: The Fundamental Slip-Casting Equation $x^{2} = N \cdot t$

This is the **most important derivation** for KTU 2024 — it has appeared in almost every model paper for this module.

**Starting physical picture:**
Consider a single wall of thickness $x$ being deposited. Water is being pulled through **two** resistances in series:
1. The **cake** (deposited cast layer) of thickness $x$.
2. The **plaster mold** of thickness $L_{m}$.

By **Darcy's Law**, the volumetric flow rate of filtrate $Q$ per unit area is:

$$Q = \dfrac{\Delta P}{\mu \left( \dfrac{x}{k_{c}} + \dfrac{L_{m}}{k_{m}} \right)}$$

where:
* $\Delta P$ = total pressure driving filtration (capillary + hydrostatic)
* $\mu$ = filtrate (water) viscosity
* $k_{c}$ = permeability of the cast cake
* $k_{m}$ = permeability of the mold
* $L_{m}$ = mold thickness

**Step 1 — Assume mold resistance is small initially**
Early in casting, the cake is thin. To a first approximation, we treat the mold resistance as a *constant* and lump it into an effective term. The standard assumption (Adcock-Davis) is that the **capillary pressure in the mold is the limiting factor** and cake resistance grows linearly with $x$:

$$\dfrac{dx}{dt} = \dfrac{K}{x}$$

where $K$ is a constant capturing $\Delta P$, $\mu$, $k_{c}$, and $k_{m}$.

**Step 2 — Separate variables**

$$x \, dx = K \, dt$$

**Step 3 — Integrate from $0$ to $x$, and $0$ to $t$**

$$\int_{0}^{x} x' \, dx' = K \int_{0}^{t} dt'$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the integrals**

$$\left[ \dfrac{(x')^{2}}{2} \right]_{0}^{x} = K \cdot t$$

$$\dfrac{x^{2}}{2} = K \cdot t$$

**Step 5 — Define the casting constant $N = 2K$**

$$x^{2} = N \cdot t$$

$$\boxed{x = \sqrt{N \cdot t}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Mark-Carrying Conclusion:** The wall thickness is **proportional to the square root of time**, NOT linear with time. Doubling the time only increases the wall by a factor of $\sqrt{2} \approx 1.414$. This is why slip casting is **slow for thick walls** — a 10 mm wall needs 4× the time of a 5 mm wall.

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Casting Constant in Terms of Process Parameters

Starting from Darcy flow and the assumption that **capillary pressure** is the only driving force, with the mold resistance lumped into a single term $L_{e}/k_{m}$:

$$N = \dfrac{2 \cdot k_{c} \cdot \Delta P_{c}}{\mu \cdot \rho_{s} \cdot \left( 1 - \epsilon \right)}$$

where:
* $\Delta P_{c} = \dfrac{2 \gamma \cos\theta}{r_{mold}}$ is the **capillary suction pressure**
* $\rho_{s}$ = solids density in the cast
* $\epsilon$ = porosity of the cast (typical $0.35 - 0.45$)
* $r_{mold}$ = effective pore radius of the plaster

**Step-by-step expansion:**

Step 1 — Substituting the Young–Laplace form of $\Delta P_{c}$:

$$N = \dfrac{2 \cdot k_{c}}{\mu \cdot \rho_{s} \cdot (1 - \epsilon)} \cdot \dfrac{2 \gamma \cos\theta}{r_{mold}}$$

Step 2 — Combine constants:

$$N = \dfrac{4 \, k_{c} \, \gamma \cos\theta}{\mu \, \rho_{s} \, (1 - \epsilon) \, r_{mold}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering meaning:** $N$ increases with *finer mold pores* (smaller $r_{mold}$), *better wetting* (smaller contact angle $\theta$), and *higher cake permeability* $k_{c}$ (coarser particles). It decreases with *higher filtrate viscosity* $\mu$.

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Krieger–Dougherty Slip Viscosity (Conceptual + Working Form)

For a **deflocculated** slip, the relative viscosity follows:

$$\eta_{rel} = \left( 1 - \dfrac{\phi}{\phi_{m}} \right)^{- [\eta] \phi_{m}}$$

where:
* $\phi$ = solids volume fraction in slip
* $\phi_{m}$ = maximum packing fraction (~ 0.6 for spheres)
* $[\eta]$ = intrinsic viscosity (= 2.5 for hard spheres)

For monodisperse hard spheres, $[\eta]\phi_{m} = 2$, giving the popular **Mooney equation**:

$$\eta_{rel} = \exp\left( \dfrac{2.5 \, \phi}{1 - \phi / \phi_{m}} \right)$$

**Worked numerical example (typical KTU 12-mark style):**

A slip has $\phi = 0.45$ and $\phi_{m} = 0.60$. Find $\eta_{rel}$ and hence the *maximum solids loading* if $\eta_{rel}$ must stay below **100**.

**Solution:**

Step 1 — Plug in:

$$\eta_{rel} = \exp\left( \dfrac{2.5 \times 0.45}{1 - 0.45/0.60} \right)$$

Step 2 — Denominator:

$$1 - \dfrac{0.45}{0.60} = 1 - 0.75 = 0.25$$

Step 3 — Numerator × division:

$$\dfrac{2.5 \times 0.45}{0.25} = \dfrac{1.125}{0.25} = 4.5$$

Step 4 — Exponentiate:

$$\eta_{rel} = e^{4.5} \approx 90.0$$

Step 5 — Verdict: $\eta_{rel} = 90 < 100$ → **the slip is workable**.

This kind of calculation is *exactly* what a 14-mark KTU sub-question on slip rheology looks like.

### 3.4 Quick Analytical Matrix — How Each Process Variable Affects the Cast

| Process Variable | Effect on $N$ (Casting Rate) | Effect on Cast Quality | KTU Key-Word |
|---|---|---|---|
| Finer plaster pores ($r_{mold}\downarrow$) | **Increases** | Sharper surface, faster | *capillary pressure* |
| Higher solids in slip | Increases (mild) | Lower shrinkage, denser | *solids loading* |
| Higher slip viscosity | **Decreases** | Thinner, weaker body | *rheology* |
| Finer ceramic particles | Increases (mild) | Smoother, more shrinkage | *sinterability* |
| Warmer slip | Increases (μ↓) | Less air bubbles | *temperature* |
| Vacuum-assisted | **Strongly increases** | Faster, less defects | *pressure casting* |
| Old, wet mold | **Decreases** | Soft cast, slow | *mold conditioning* |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Process Block Diagram — Slip Casting Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Ceramic Powder] --> B[Ball Milling with Water and Deflocculant]
    B --> C[Slip Quality Check - Viscosity and Density]
    C --> D{Quality OK?}
    D -- No --> B
    D -- Yes --> E[Pour Slip into Plaster Mold]
    E --> F[Capillary Suction Draws Water Out]
    F --> G[Ceramic Particles Deposit as Cast Layer]
    G --> H{Casting Type?}
    H -- Drain Casting --> I[Drain Excess Slip After Time t]
    H -- Solid Casting --> J[Let Slip Fill Entire Cavity]
    I --> K[Trim and De-mold Green Body]
    J --> K
    K --> L[Controlled Humidity Drying]
    L --> M[Green Body Ready for Sintering]
    M --> N[Final Sintered Ceramic Component]
```

### 4.2 Drain Casting vs Solid Casting — Block Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph DC[Drain Casting - Hollow Ware]
        A1[Slip Poured] --> A2[Wall Builds Up to x]
        A2 --> A3[Excess Slip Poured Out]
        A3 --> A4[Hollow Green Body]
    end
    subgraph SC[Solid Casting - Solid Articles]
        B1[Slip Poured Between Mold and Core] --> B2[Slip Solidifies Against All Surfaces]
        B2 --> B3[Mold Opened and Core Removed]
        B3 --> B4[Solid Green Body]
    end
```

### 4.3 Micro-Mechanism — How a Cast Layer Forms

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    P1[Slip with Suspended Ceramic Particles] --> P2[Mold Pore - Capillary Radius r]
    P2 --> P3[Capillary Pressure pulls water in]
    P3 --> P4[Particles too large to enter pores]
    P4 --> P5[Particles Packed on Inner Wall as Cake]
    P5 --> P6[Cast Layer of Thickness x grows with t]
    P6 --> P7[Water Exits to Outside of Mold]
```

### 4.4 Casting Curve — Wall Thickness vs. Time (Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    T0[t = 0] -->|Rapid Initial Build-up| T1[t = t1, x = x1]
    T1 -->|Slower Growth| T2[t = t2 = 4 t1, x = 2 x1]
    T2 -->|Even Slower| T3[t = t3 = 9 t1, x = 3 x1]
    T3 -->|Process Stopped at Target x| T4[De-mold]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the curve:** The **square-root** behaviour is the *single most important visual* to remember. To go from 3 mm to 6 mm wall thickness, you need **four times** the casting time. This is why industrial casters use *pressure casting* to break out of this slow square-root law.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]**
> *Define slip casting. List the two main variants and give one industrial product made by each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Slip casting is a ceramic forming process in which a fluid suspension of fine ceramic powder (slip) is poured into a porous plaster mold. The mold absorbs water by capillary action, leaving a consolidated ceramic layer on its inner walls, which is later de-molded, dried and sintered.

**Two variants (1 mark each):**
1. **Drain casting** — excess slip is drained after the desired wall thickness is built. Used for **hollow ware** (e.g., washbasin, cup, vase).
2. **Solid casting** — the slip fills the entire cavity and solidifies. Used for **solid articles** (e.g., toilet tank, insulator body).

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Retest]**
> *What is the role of a deflocculant in slip preparation? Name two common deflocculants used in ceramic processing.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Role (2 marks):** A deflocculant is added to the slip to **disaggregate flocculated** ceramic particles by creating **electrostatic repulsion** (or steric repulsion) on their surfaces. This **lowers the slip viscosity** at a given solids loading, allowing *higher solids content* with the *same flowability*, which in turn **reduces casting shrinkage** and gives a denser green body.

**Examples (½ mark each):**
1. **Sodium silicate** (water glass, Na₂SiO₃)
2. **Sodium carbonate** (Na₂CO₃, soda ash)
3. **Darvan 7** (sodium polyacrylate – organic deflocculant, optional)

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

> **Q.A [KTU University Exam – 2024 Scheme, Model Paper 2]**
>
> **(a) [7 marks]** Derive the fundamental slip-casting equation $x^{2} = N t$ starting from Darcy's law, clearly stating all assumptions. Explain how the casting constant $N$ depends on capillary pressure, mold permeability and filtrate viscosity.
>
> **(b) [7 marks]** Compare **drain casting** and **solid casting** with neat sketches. Discuss the effect of slip solids loading, particle size and mold moisture on the casting rate and final cast quality.

### Model Solution — Q.A

**Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks)**

**Step 1 (1 mark) — Physical setup:**
Water flows from the slip, through the growing cast layer of thickness $x$ and through the plaster mold of thickness $L_{m}$, driven by capillary pressure $\Delta P_{c}$.

**Step 2 (2 marks) — Apply Darcy's law in series:**

$$J = \dfrac{dV}{A \, dt} = \dfrac{\Delta P_{c}}{\mu \left( \dfrac{x}{k_{c}} + \dfrac{L_{m}}{k_{m}} \right)}$$

**Step 3 (1 mark) — For early-stage casting, assume $L_{m}/k_{m}$ dominates and is constant**, and the cake resistance grows linearly with $x$. This reduces to:

$$\dfrac{dx}{dt} = \dfrac{K}{x}$$

**Step 4 (2 marks) — Integrate:**

$$x \, dx = K \, dt \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \int_{0}^{x} x' \, dx' = K \int_{0}^{t} dt' \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \dfrac{x^{2}}{2} = K t$$

**Step 5 (1 mark) — Define $N = 2K$:**

$$\boxed{x^{2} = N \cdot t}$$

**Dependence of $N$ (within the 7-mark allocation, mention verbally):**
$N$ is **directly proportional** to $\Delta P_{c}$ (i.e., to $\gamma \cos\theta / r_{mold}$) and inversely proportional to filtrate viscosity $\mu$ and to mold/cake resistances. Finer mold pores and better wetting increase $N$; a viscous slip lowers $N$.

---

**Part (b) — Drain vs Solid Casting (7 marks)**

| Aspect (1 mark each) | Drain Casting | Solid Casting |
|---|---|---|
| **Mold** | One-piece female | Two-piece with core |
| **Excess slip** | Drained out after time $t$ | Remains, solidifies completely |
| **Wall thickness** | Controlled by **time** $x = \sqrt{Nt}$ | Controlled by **cavity width** |
| **Cycle time** | Shorter | Longer |
| **Product example** | Washbasin, vase | Toilet cistern, insulator |
| **Material utilisation** | Excess slip reused | Some scrap with the core |
| **Geometry** | Hollow, open shapes | Solid, complex, closed shapes |

**Effect of process variables (1.5 marks each, written in 3 short paragraphs):**
* **Solids loading ↑** → Higher casting rate (less water to remove), lower shrinkage, denser body.
* **Particle size ↓** → Smoother surface, but *more* shrinkage and slower flow; finer pores in the cake resist flow.
* **Mold moisture ↑** → Lower capillary suction → *slower* casting → softer, weaker green body; molds must be conditioned to a controlled humidity before each cycle.

---

> **Q.B [KTU University Exam – 2024 Scheme, Model Paper 3 — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE]**
>
> **(a) [7 marks]** With the help of a labelled schematic, describe the **slip casting process** for manufacturing a ceramic washbasin. Discuss the role of (i) plaster of Paris as a mold material, and (ii) deflocculants in slip preparation.
>
> **(b) [7 marks]** A ceramic slip with **45 vol% solids** ($\phi_{m} = 0.60$, $[\eta] = 2.5$) is to be used for slip casting. The maximum allowable relative viscosity is **100**. Verify whether the slip is workable. If the solids loading is increased to **52 vol%**, will the slip still be workable? Comment on the practical implications.

### Model Solution — Q.B

**Part (a) — Process Description (7 marks)**

**Schematic description (3 marks):**
* A two-part plaster mold is clamped together, leaving a cavity shaped like a washbasin.
* The slip (suspension of vitreous china in water + deflocculant) is poured through a **feed cup** at the top of the mold.
* Plaster pores draw water inward; particles build a uniform cast layer on the cavity wall.
* After the **target wall thickness** is reached (say 8–10 mm), excess slip is **drained** out and recycled.
* After a few minutes of *drainage time*, the mold is opened; the **green basin** is carefully lifted out, dried, glazed and sintered at ~1200 °C.

**Role of plaster of Paris (2 marks):**
* It is **porous** (30–50 % porosity) — the pore radius (~1–5 µm) is **smaller than ceramic particles** (1–10 µm) but large enough to allow water to pass.
* Generates the **capillary suction pressure** ($\Delta P_{c} = 2\gamma \cos\theta / r$) that drives the process.
* It is **cheap, machinable, and reusable** for 50–100 cycles before it must be discarded.

**Role of deflocculants (2 marks):**
* Provides **electrostatic repulsion** between ceramic particles → breaks up flocs.
* Allows a slip with **high solids content** to remain **low in viscosity** → better flow, less shrinkage, denser cast.

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Check (7 marks)**

**Step 1 (1 mark) — Given:** $\phi = 0.45$, $\phi_{m} = 0.60$, $[\eta]\phi_{m} = 2.5 \times 0.60 = 1.5$.

**Step 2 (1 mark) — Apply Krieger–Dougherty:**

$$\eta_{rel} = \left( 1 - \dfrac{0.45}{0.60} \right)^{-1.5}$$

**Step 3 (1 mark) — Simplify:**

$$1 - \dfrac{0.45}{0.60} = 1 - 0.75 = 0.25$$

$$\eta_{rel} = (0.25)^{-1.5} = \left( \dfrac{1}{0.25} \right)^{1.5} = (4)^{1.5}$$

**Step 4 (1 mark) — Evaluate:**

$$(4)^{1.5} = 4 \times \sqrt{4} = 4 \times 2 = 8$$

**Verdict (1 mark):** $\eta_{rel} = 8 \ll 100$ → the slip is **very comfortably workable** (in fact, viscosity is low; one could push solids higher).

**Step 5 (1.5 marks) — Repeat for $\phi = 0.52$:**

$$1 - \dfrac{0.52}{0.60} = 1 - 0.8667 = 0.1333$$

$$\eta_{rel} = (0.1333)^{-1.5} = (7.5)^{1.5} = 7.5 \times \sqrt{7.5} \approx 7.5 \times 2.739 \approx 20.55$$

**Verdict (1 mark):** $\eta_{rel} \approx 20.6 < 100$ → still **workable**, and now with **higher solids** → less shrinkage → better cast quality.

**Practical implication (1 mark):** Increasing solids from 45 to 52 vol% **doubles the cast density** of the green body, reduces **drying shrinkage** from ~3 % to ~1.5 %, lowers cracking tendency, but **slightly** raises viscosity. This is the *classical industrial trade-off* in slip rheology.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls (3-Mark and 14-Mark)**
> 1. **Confusing "drain casting" with "solid casting"** in definitions — students often swap them. Remember: **drain = drain out excess; solid = solid throughout**.
> 2. **Writing $x = Nt$ instead of $x = \sqrt{Nt}$** — this is a **derivation-mark killer**. The square root is the *whole point* of the derivation.
> 3. **Forgetting to define $N$** at the end of the derivation — you *must* state that $N$ is the **casting constant** with units m²/s. Examiners deduct ½ to 1 mark for missing the unit.
> 4. **Naming "plaster of Paris" without explaining why** — it is not enough to name the mold material; you must state that it is *porous* and *generates capillary suction*.
> 5. **In numerical problems, forgetting to convert units** — e.g., using thickness in mm without converting to m before applying $x^{2} = Nt$. KTU evaluators check the *unit consistency* explicitly.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

* **Slip = ceramic powder + water + deflocculant (± binder)**. It is a *colloidal suspension*, not a solution.
* **Slip casting = capillary-driven colloidal forming** in a **porous plaster mold**.
* **Two variants:** **Drain casting** (hollow ware, wall set by *time*) and **Solid casting** (solid articles, wall set by *cavity*).
* **The king equation:** $\boxed{x^{2} = N \cdot t}$ — wall thickness grows as the **square root of time**.
* **Casting constant $N$** ∝ $\Delta P_{c}$ / $\mu$ ∝ $\gamma \cos\theta / (r_{mold} \cdot \mu)$.
* **Faster casting if:** finer plaster pores, warmer slip, higher solids, vacuum-assist, lower filtrate viscosity.
* **Deflocculants** (sodium silicate, Darvan 7) **break up flocs** and **lower viscosity** → enable high-solids, low-shrinkage slips.
* **Plaster of Paris** is the industry standard mold material because its pore size (~1–5 µm) is **smaller than ceramic particles** but large enough to conduct water.
* **Darcy's law** is the underlying transport equation; the **Krieger–Dougherty equation** governs slip rheology.
* **Square-root kinetics** make thick walls *very* time-consuming — this is why **pressure casting** was invented industrially.
* **Drying shrinkage** is a function of slip solids loading: more solids → less water → less shrinkage → fewer cracks.
* **KTU keywords to embed in every answer:** *capillary pressure, plaster mold, deflocculant, casting constant, drain casting, solid casting, green body, sintering*.
* **Typical numerical form (guaranteed in 14-mark questions):** "Verify whether a slip is workable using the Krieger–Dougherty equation at a given $\phi$ and $\phi_{m}$."

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Plastic forming
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEMUT412 - COMPOSITE MATERIALS AND CERAMICS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Plastic forming # SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Plastic Forming (Plastic Forming of Ceramics):** > A class of ceramic shaping (forming) processes in which a **plasticized ceramic mass** — a mixture of ceramic powder, water (or other solvent), and organic binders/plasticizers — is deformed under an applied stress into a desired geometry, where the deformation is **irreversible and time-dependent (visco-plastic)**, but the body retains its shape after the forming stress is removed, prior to drying and sintering. In KTU 2024 scheme terminology for **PEMUT412**, plastic forming sits within the broader umbrella of **ceramic forming/shaping processes** and is contrasted with: - **Dry pressing** (powder compaction under uniaxial/isostatic pressure in a die) - **Slip casting** (suspension consolidation in a porous mould) - **Casting of melts** (glass, fused cast refractories) The unique feature of plastic forming is that the ceramic body is in a **leather-hard, plastic, dough-like consistency**, allowing continuous or semi-continuous shaping operations such as extrusion, injection molding, jiggering, and roll forming. --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Think of Play-Doh or a baker's dough:** > When you mix flour with just enough water, it becomes a sticky, pliable dough that you can squeeze through a nozzle (extrusion), press into a mould (jiggering), or roll into sheets (calendering). If you use too little water it crumbles; too much water and it sags. The exact same principle governs plastic forming of ceramics — we add just the right amount of **water + organic binder** to ceramic powder so it behaves like a baker's dough, then shape it before firing it permanently into a hard ceramic. A more engineering-oriented analogy: a **toothpaste tube**. You squeeze (apply pressure) on a Bingham plastic (toothpaste) through a die (nozzle), and it emerges with a fixed cross-section, holding its shape until dried. Ceramic extrusion works identically — except the "toothpaste" is alumina or clay, and after drying/sintering it becomes a hard engineering ceramic. --- ## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics The following constants and metrics are routinely used in plastic-forming analysis: - **Bingham yield stress (τ_y)**: typically **0.5 kPa – 100 kPa** for clay-water systems - **Plastic viscosity (η_p)**: typically **10 Pa·s – 10⁴ Pa·s** depending on solids loading - **Capillary number (Ca)**: dimensionless, typically **10⁻³ – 10¹** - **Die swell ratio (B)**: typically **1.0 – 1.4** - **Solids loading (φ)**: volume fraction of ceramic powder, typically **0.50 – 0.65** for extrusion - **Bingham number (Bi)**: dimensionless ratio of yield stress to viscous stress - **Extrusion pressure (P_ext)**: typically **0.5 – 10 MPa** for soft-mud extrusion - **Standard forming moisture content**: **15 – 25 wt%** for clay-based bodies --- ## 1.4 Classification of Plastic Forming Processes | S.No | Process | Geometry Suitability | Typical Material | |------|---------|----------------------|------------------| | 1 | **Stiff-mud extrusion** | Long, uniform cross-section (bricks, tiles, pipes) | Clay, alumina | | 2 | **Soft-mud extrusion** | Complex profiles, thin walls | Porcelain, stoneware | | 3 | **Injection molding** | Net-shape complex 3-D parts | Si₃N₄, Al₂O₃, ZrO₂ | | 4 | **Jiggering** | Axially symmetric shallow ware (plates) | Porcelain, bone china | | 5 | **Jollying** | Axially symmetric hollow ware (cups, bowls) | Porcelain | | 6 | **Roll forming / Calendering** | Thin sheets, tapes | Ferrites, PZT, substrate tapes | | 7 | **Deep drawing** | Cups, cones from plastic blanks | Refractory sheets | | 8 | **Hand throwing / Wheel-throwing** | Custom shapes | Artware, traditional pottery | > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Highlight — Module 1 Focus:** > Module 1 of PEMUT412 typically emphasizes the **principles, mechanics, rheology, and process parameters** of plastic forming rather than individual art-ware techniques. The high-yield topics for examination are: **extrusion (mechanics + die design), injection molding (binder systems + defects), jiggering/jollying, and the rheology of plastic ceramic masses.** # SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Why is Plastic Forming Required? Plastic forming becomes necessary because of two fundamental engineering needs: 1. **Geometry that dry-pressing cannot produce** — Long continuous profiles (tubes, rods, honeycombs), thin-walled shapes, and complex 3-D nets that cannot be uniaxially pressed. 2. **Mechanical integrity of the green body** — Plastic forming produces a body with very **uniform density** (because the plastic mass is fully consolidated before being forced through the die) and good inter-particle bonding, reducing lamination defects. ## 2.2 The Plastic Ceramic Mass — Composition Logic A plastic ceramic mass has four essential constituents: | Component | Function | Typical Examples | Typical Amount | |-----------|----------|------------------|----------------| | **Ceramic powder (solids)** | Structural skeleton | Clay, Al₂O₃, SiC, ZrO₂ | 60 – 85 wt% | | **Water (or non-aqueous solvent)** | Primary plasticizer | Water, ethanol, toluene | 10 – 30 wt% | | **Organic binder** | Green strength, cohesion | PVA, CMC, methyl cellulose, PEG | 1 – 8 wt% | | **Lubricant / Plasticizer** | Reduce friction, improve flow | Stearic acid, glycerine, wax | 0.5 – 3 wt% | | **Deflocculant / Dispersant** (optional) | Stabilize suspension before forming | Sodium silicate, Darvan | < 1 wt% | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why organic binders?** After drying, ceramic particles alone have very low green strength (often < 1 MPa). Organic binders form a polymer bridge between particles that gives the green body enough strength to be handled, machined, and transported to the kiln without cracking. ## 2.3 Rheology of Plastic Ceramic Masses A plastic ceramic mass exhibits **visco-plastic** flow — meaning it behaves as a solid below a critical stress (the **yield stress τ_y**) and as a viscous fluid above it. ### 2.3.1 Bingham Plastic Model The simplest and most widely used rheological model for plastic ceramic masses is the **Bingham plastic**: $$ \tau = \tau_y + \eta_p \dot{\gamma} $$ Where: - **τ** = shear stress applied (Pa) - **τ_y** = yield stress (Pa) — must be exceeded before flow begins - **η_p** = plastic viscosity (Pa·s) - **γ̇** = shear rate (s⁻¹) This is the **single most important equation** in plastic forming of ceramics. ### 2.3.2 Herschel–Bulkley Model (More General) When the mass is shear-thinning (viscosity decreases with shear rate), the **Herschel–Bulkley** model is used: $$ \tau = \tau_y + K \dot{\gamma}^n $$ Where: - **K** = consistency index (Pa·sⁿ) - **n** = flow behaviour index (dimensionless) - **n < 1** → shear-thinning (pseudoplastic) - **n = 1** → reduces to Bingham - **n > 1** → shear-thickening (dilatant) ### 2.3.3 Capillary Rheometry Equation For flow through a die of radius **R** and length **L**, the **wall shear stress** is: $$ \tau_w = \dfrac{\Delta P \cdot R}{2L} $$ And the **apparent wall shear rate** for a Newtonian fluid is: $$ \dot{\gamma}_w^{apparent} = \dfrac{4Q}{\pi R^3} $$ Where **Q** = volumetric flow rate (m³/s). For a Bingham plastic, the **true wall shear rate** is corrected using the **Rabinowitsch-Mooney equation**: $$ \dot{\gamma}_w^{true} = \dot{\gamma}_w^{apparent} \left[\dfrac{3}{4} + \dfrac{1}{4}\dfrac{d \ln \dot{\gamma}_w^{apparent}}{d \ln \tau_w}\right] $$ --- ## 2.4 Extrusion Mechanics — The Core Process ### 2.4.1 Auger / Piston Extruder Pressure For an **auger extruder**, the extrusion pressure is approximately: $$ P_{ext} = \dfrac{8 \eta_p L_v Q}{\pi D_v^4} $$ For a **piston extruder**, the pressure drop in the barrel (Poiseuille flow) is: $$ P_{ext} = \dfrac{8 \eta_p L Q}{\pi R^4} $$ Where: - **L_v** = axial length of the auger chamber - **D_v** = barrel diameter - **L** = barrel length ### 2.4.2 Die Pressure Drop For flow through a die of length **L_d** and radius **R_d**, including both **Poiseuille (viscous)** and **Bagley (end-correction)** terms: $$ P_{die} = \dfrac{2 L_d}{R_d} \tau_y + \dfrac{8 \eta_p L_d Q}{\pi R_d^4} $$ > [!NOTE] > The **first term** represents the **yield stress contribution** (pressure needed to initiate flow at the die wall) and the **second term** is the **viscous flow contribution** (pressure needed to overcome viscous resistance inside the die). ### 2.4.3 Total Extrusion Pressure $$ P_{total} = P_{barrel} + P_{die} + P_{auger} $$ --- ## 2.5 Die Swell (Extrudate Swell) When a visco-plastic mass exits the die, the cross-section of the extrudate is **larger** than the die cross-section. This is called **die swell** and is quantified by the **swell ratio B**: $$ B = \dfrac{D_{extrudate}}{D_{die}} $$ For a Bingham plastic, an approximate relationship: $$ B \approx 1 + \dfrac{1}{2}\left(\dfrac{\tau_y}{\tau_w}\right)^2 $$ Higher yield stress → higher die swell. --- ## 2.6 Bingham Number — The Key Dimensionless Group The **Bingham number** compares yield stress to viscous stress: $$ \text{Bi} = \dfrac{\tau_y \cdot L}{\eta_p \cdot V} $$ Where **V** = average velocity and **L** = characteristic length. - **Bi >> 1** → flow dominated by yield stress (plug flow dominates) - **Bi << 1** → flow dominated by viscosity (parabolic Poiseuille profile) > [!IMPORTANT] > In **ceramic extrusion, Bi is typically large (Bi > 10)**. This means most of the material in the die moves as a **plug** with no internal shear, except in a thin layer near the die wall called the **lubrication layer**. --- ## 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula | Meaning / Use | Typical Units | |---|---------|---------------|----------------| | 1 | $\tau = \tau_y + \eta_p \dot{\gamma}$ | Bingham plastic rheology | Pa | | 2 | $\tau = \tau_y + K \dot{\gamma}^n$ | Herschel–Bulkley (shear-thinning) | Pa | | 3 | $\tau_w = \dfrac{\Delta P R}{2L}$ | Wall shear stress in die/capillary | Pa | | 4 | $\dot{\gamma}_w = \dfrac{4Q}{\pi R^3}$ | Apparent wall shear rate | s⁻¹ | | 5 | $P_{die} = \dfrac{2L_d}{R_d}\tau_y + \dfrac{8\eta_p L_d Q}{\pi R_d^4}$ | Die pressure drop (Bingham) | Pa | | 6 | $P_{barrel} = \dfrac{8\eta_p L Q}{\pi R^4}$ | Piston barrel pressure (Poiseuille) | Pa | | 7 | $B = 1 + \dfrac{1}{2}\left(\dfrac{\tau_y}{\tau_w}\right)^2$ | Die swell ratio | dimensionless | | 8 | $\text{Bi} = \dfrac{\tau_y L}{\eta_p V}$ | Bingham number | dimensionless | | 9 | $\dot{\gamma} = \dfrac{\partial v}{\partial y}$ | Definition of shear rate | s⁻¹ | | 10 | $\eta = \dfrac{\tau}{\dot{\gamma}}$ | Apparent viscosity | Pa·s | --- ## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility Plastic forming is the **workhorse** of the traditional ceramics industry. Engineering applications include: - **Building bricks and roof tiles** (extruded clay, world production > 10⁹ bricks/year) - **Porcelain insulators** for high-voltage transmission (jiggered/jollied) - **Ceramic tubes and pipes** for chemical/thermal service (extruded) - **Catalyst support honeycombs** for automotive exhaust (extruded cordierite) - **Technical ceramics** (Si₃N turbine rotors, ZrO₂ biomedical implants) via **ceramic injection molding (CIM)** - **Thin ceramic tapes** for MLCCs and substrates via **doctor-blade tape casting** (a sister process to roll forming) # SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Bingham Plastic Velocity Profile in a Circular Die This is the **most important derivation** for plastic forming — it determines the velocity of the ceramic mass as it flows through a die. ### Step 1 — Setup the Problem We consider a Bingham plastic flowing through a cylindrical die of radius **R** under an axial pressure gradient **dP/dz**. The cylindrical coordinate system is chosen with z-axis along the die axis. The steady-state **force balance** on a cylindrical shell of radius **r** and length **dz** gives: $$ \dfrac{dP}{dz} \cdot \pi r^2 = -\tau(r) \cdot 2\pi r $$ ### Step 2 — Solve for the Shear Stress Distribution Dividing both sides by $2\pi r$: $$ \tau(r) = -\dfrac{r}{2} \dfrac{dP}{dz} $$ This tells us that the **shear stress varies linearly from zero at the centre (r = 0) to a maximum at the wall (r = R)**. ### Step 3 — Apply the Bingham Constitutive Equation Substituting the Bingham equation $\tau = \tau_y + \eta_p \dot{\gamma}$: $$ -\dfrac{r}{2}\dfrac{dP}{dz} = \tau_y + \eta_p \dfrac{dv_z}{dr} $$ ### Step 4 — Determine the Plug-Flow Radius The **plug-flow radius** $r_p$ is defined as the radius at which the shear stress exactly equals the yield stress — i.e. below this radius, the material flows as a rigid plug (no shear). Setting $\tau(r_p) = \tau_y$: $$ -\dfrac{r_p}{2}\dfrac{dP}{dz} = \tau_y $$ $$ r_p = -\dfrac{2\tau_y}{dP/dz} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Key insight:** Since $dP/dz$ is negative (pressure drops along the die), $r_p$ is **positive** as expected. ### Step 5 — Solve for the Velocity Profile in the Shear Region ($r_p \le r \le R$) Rearranging the constitutive equation: $$ \eta_p \dfrac{dv_z}{dr} = -\dfrac{r}{2}\dfrac{dP}{dz} - \tau_y $$ Integrating with respect to **r**: $$ v_z(r) = -\dfrac{1}{4\eta_p}\dfrac{dP}{dz} \left(R^2 - r^2\right) - \dfrac{\tau_y}{\eta_p}(R - r) + v_z(R) $$ Applying the **no-slip boundary condition** $v_z(R) = 0$ at the die wall: $$ v_z(r) = \dfrac{1}{4\eta_p}\left(-\dfrac{dP}{dz}\right)\left(R^2 - r^2\right) - \dfrac{\tau_y}{\eta_p}(R - r) $$ ### Step 6 — Solve for the Velocity Profile in the Plug Region ($0 \le r \le r_p$) In the plug region, the material moves as a rigid body with velocity equal to $v_z(r_p)$: $$ v_{plug} = v_z(r_p) = \dfrac{1}{4\eta_p}\left(-\dfrac{dP}{dz}\right)\left(R^2 - r_p^2\right) - \dfrac{\tau_y}{\eta_p}(R - r_p) $$ ### Step 7 — Calculate the Volumetric Flow Rate $$ Q = \int_0^R 2\pi r \, v_z(r) \, dr $$ Splitting into plug and shear regions: $$ Q = \int_0^{r_p} 2\pi r \, v_{plug} \, dr + \int_{r_p}^{R} 2\pi r \, v_z(r) \, dr $$ After integration (shown in detail below): $$ Q = \dfrac{\pi R^4}{8\eta_p}\left(-\dfrac{dP}{dz}\right)\left[1 - \dfrac{4}{3}\dfrac{r_p}{R} + \dfrac{1}{3}\left(\dfrac{r_p}{R}\right)^4\right] $$ This is the **fundamental Bingham flow equation** in a circular duct. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Rabinowitsch Correction for Bingham Plastic ### Step 1 — Start from the Capillary Rheometry Data In a capillary rheometer, we measure **Q** vs **ΔP** for a die of radius **R**. The **apparent wall shear rate** (assuming Newtonian behaviour) is: $$ \dot{\gamma}_{app} = \dfrac{4Q}{\pi R^3} $$ The **wall shear stress** is: $$ \tau_w = \dfrac{\Delta P \cdot R}{2L} $$ ### Step 2 — Compute the Logarithmic Derivative Define the slope on a log–log plot: $$ n' = \dfrac{d \ln \tau_w}{d \ln \dot{\gamma}_{app}} $$ ### Step 3 — Apply the Rabinowitsch–Mooney Generalization The **true wall shear rate** for a non-Newtonian fluid is: $$ \dot{\gamma}_{true} = \dot{\gamma}_{app} \left[\dfrac{3n' + 1}{4n'}\right] $$ > [!NOTE] > For a **Newtonian fluid** ($n' = 1$): $\dot{\gamma}_{true} = \dot{\gamma}_{app}$ ✓ > For a **shear-thinning fluid** ($n' < 1$): $\dot{\gamma}_{true} > \dot{\gamma}_{app}$ ✓ > For a **Bingham plastic** ($n' = 1$ in the shear region, but with yield stress), a further **Hagenbach–Couette correction** is required for kinetic energy and end effects. --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Pressure Drop Across an Extrusion Die (Worked Numerical Example) **Problem:** A clay-based ceramic mass (Bingham plastic) with $\tau_y = 5 \text{ kPa}$ and $\eta_p = 200 \text{ Pa·s}$ is extruded through a cylindrical die of length $L_d = 50 \text{ mm}$ and radius $R_d = 10 \text{ mm}$. The volumetric flow rate is $Q = 2 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$. Calculate the die pressure drop. ### Step 1 — Identify the Given Quantities $$ \tau_y = 5000 \text{ Pa}, \quad \eta_p = 200 \text{ Pa·s}, \quad L_d = 0.05 \text{ m}, \quad R_d = 0.01 \text{ m}, \quad Q = 2 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^3/\text{s} $$ ### Step 2 — Apply the Die Pressure Equation $$ P_{die} = \dfrac{2L_d}{R_d}\tau_y + \dfrac{8\eta_p L_d Q}{\pi R_d^4} $$ ### Step 3 — Compute the Yield-Stress Term $$ \dfrac{2L_d}{R_d}\tau_y = \dfrac{2 \times 0.05}{0.01} \times 5000 = 10 \times 5000 = 50{,}000 \text{ Pa} = 50 \text{ kPa} $$ ### Step 4 — Compute the Viscous Term $$ \dfrac{8\eta_p L_d Q}{\pi R_d^4} = \dfrac{8 \times 200 \times 0.05 \times 2 \times 10^{-5}}{\pi \times (0.01)^4} $$ Numerator: $8 \times 200 \times 0.05 \times 2 \times 10^{-5} = 1.6 \times 10^{-2}$ Denominator: $\pi \times 10^{-8} = 3.1416 \times 10^{-8}$ $$ \dfrac{1.6 \times 10^{-2}}{3.1416 \times 10^{-8}} = 5.09 \times 10^{5} \text{ Pa} \approx 509 \text{ kPa} $$ ### Step 5 — Sum the Two Terms $$ P_{die} = 50 \text{ kPa} + 509 \text{ kPa} = 559 \text{ kPa} \approx 0.56 \text{ MPa} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Observation:** The viscous term dominates (about 10× the yield term) at this flow rate. For **slow extrusion or small dies**, the yield term dominates. This trade-off is critical in **die design**. --- ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Process Selection | Selection Criterion | Extrusion | Injection Molding | Jiggering | Roll Forming | |----------------------|-----------|-------------------|-----------|--------------| | **Geometry** | Continuous profile | Complex 3-D net shape | Axially symmetric | Sheet/tape | | **Production volume** | Very high | Medium-high | High | High | | **Binder content** | 15 – 25 wt% water | 30 – 50 wt% polymer | 20 – 30 wt% water | 20 – 40 wt% | | **Pressure required** | 0.5 – 10 MPa | 10 – 100 MPa | Hand pressure | Roll pressure | | **Typical wall thickness** | > 3 mm | > 1 mm | > 2 mm | 0.1 – 5 mm | | **Tolerance** | ±1 – 3% | ±0.3 – 0.5% | ±1 – 2% | ±1 – 5% | | **Surface finish** | Moderate | Excellent | Good | Excellent | | **Green strength** | Moderate | High | Low | Moderate | --- ## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Common Defects in Plastic Forming | Defect | Cause | Cure | |--------|-------|------| | **Lamination / cracking** | Air entrapment, poor plastic mass | De-air under vacuum, increase aging | | **Die swell mismatch** | High yield stress, elastic recovery | Increase plasticizer, reduce flow rate | | **Surface tearing (shark-skin)** | Wall slip, too high shear rate | Lubricate die, reduce velocity | | **Density gradients** | Friction at die wall | Taper die entrance, optimize L/R | | **Sticking to die** | Insufficient lubricant | Add stearic acid / glycerine | | **Sagging after forming** | Too much plasticizer | Reduce water content, add binder | | **Springback** | Elastic recovery of organic binder | Control drying rate, use lower elastic binders | # SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Plastic Forming Process Map ```mermaid flowchart TD A["Ceramic Powder

List any four processes that fall under this category. **Model Answer (3 marks):** > **Plastic forming** is a ceramic shaping process in which a ceramic mass — consisting of ceramic powder, water (or solvent), and organic binders/plasticizers — is deformed under an applied stress into a desired shape while the mass is in a **plastic, dough-like state**, retaining its shape after the stress is removed and prior to drying and sintering. *(1.5 marks)* The four major plastic-forming processes are: **(1 mark)** 1. Extrusion (stiff-mud / soft-mud) 2. Injection molding 3. Jiggering and Jollying 4. Roll forming / Calendering > [!NOTE] > **Alternative acceptable answer:** Hand throwing, deep drawing, and die pressing of plastic clay are also valid processes. --- ### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Paper PEMUT412] **Q2.** State the **Bingham plastic model** and explain the significance of the yield stress $\tau_y$ in ceramic extrusion. **Model Answer (3 marks):** The **Bingham plastic constitutive equation** is: $$ \tau = \tau_y + \eta_p \dot{\gamma} $$ *(1 mark)* Where $\tau$ is shear stress, $\tau_y$ is **yield stress**, $\eta_p$ is **plastic viscosity**, and $\dot{\gamma}$ is shear rate. *(0.5 marks)* **Significance of $\tau_y$:** The yield stress is the **minimum stress that must be applied** before the plastic ceramic mass will flow. Below $\tau_y$, the mass behaves as a rigid solid; above $\tau_y$, it flows like a viscous fluid. *(1 mark)* In ceramic extrusion, $\tau_y$ determines: - The **minimum extrusion pressure** needed to initiate flow. - The **plug-flow region** in the die (where shear stress is less than $\tau_y$). - The **die swell ratio** (higher $\tau_y$ → larger die swell). - The **green-body sag resistance** after forming. *(0.5 marks)* --- ## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice) > [!IMPORTANT] > KTU 2024 ESE pattern requires **internal choice** within a module. Both questions below are fully solved 14-mark options. --- ### Question A (14 Marks) — **Plastic Forming: Classification, Rheology, and Extrusion Mechanics** **`[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Adapted PEMUT412]`** #### Part (a) — 7 Marks **Q(a).** Classify the various **plastic forming processes** of ceramics with one example application for each. Discuss the role of **organic binders and plasticizers** in plastic forming. **Model Solution:** **Classification of plastic forming processes (5 marks, 1 mark each for any 5):** 1. **Extrusion** — Used for bricks, tiles, pipes, honeycomb catalyst substrates (e.g. cordierite). 2. **Injection molding (CIM)** — Used for complex 3-D net-shape parts such as Si₃N₄ turbocharger rotors, ZrO₂ dental implants. 3. **Jiggering** — Used for flat, axially symmetric ware like porcelain plates. 4. **Jollying** — Used for hollow, axially symmetric ware like porcelain cups and bowls. 5. **Roll forming / Calendering** — Used for thin ceramic sheets and tapes, e.g. ferrites and PZT substrates. 6. **Deep drawing** — Used for refractory cups, cones, and crucibles. 7. **Hand throwing** — Used for artware and custom pottery. **Role of binders and plasticizers (2 marks):** Organic binders (such as PVA, CMC, methyl cellulose) provide **green strength** by forming polymer bridges between ceramic particles after drying. They give the green body enough mechanical integrity to be handled, machined, and transported to the kiln without cracking. Without binders, dry ceramic powder compacts are extremely fragile. Plasticizers (such as water, glycerine, PEG, stearic acid) **reduce inter-particle friction**, improve the deformability of the mass, lower the yield stress, and improve die-wall lubrication. They enable continuous shaping without cracking or tearing. #### Part (b) — 7 Marks **Q(b).** A clay-based ceramic paste exhibits **Bingham plastic** behaviour with $\tau_y = 8 \text{ kPa}$ and $\eta_p = 150 \text{ Pa·s}$. It is extruded through a cylindrical die of radius $R = 8 \text{ mm}$ and length $L = 40 \text{ mm}$ at a volumetric flow rate of $Q = 1.5 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$. Calculate the **die pressure drop** and the **plug-flow radius**. **Model Solution:** **Given:** $\tau_y = 8000 \text{ Pa}$, $\eta_p = 150 \text{ Pa·s}$, $R = 0.008 \text{ m}$, $L = 0.04 \text{ m}$, $Q = 1.5 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$. **Step 1 — Die pressure equation:** $$ P_{die} = \dfrac{2L}{R}\tau_y + \dfrac{8 \eta_p L Q}{\pi R^4} $$ **Step 2 — Yield term:** $$ P_y = \dfrac{2 \times 0.04}{0.008} \times 8000 = 10 \times 8000 = 80{,}000 \text{ Pa} = 80 \text{ kPa} $$ **Step 3 — Viscous term:** Numerator = $8 \times 150 \times 0.04 \times 1.5 \times 10^{-5} = 7.2 \times 10^{-4}$ Denominator = $\pi \times (0.008)^4 = \pi \times 4.096 \times 10^{-9} = 1.287 \times 10^{-8}$ $$ P_\eta = \dfrac{7.2 \times 10^{-4}}{1.287 \times 10^{-8}} = 55{,}950 \text{ Pa} \approx 56 \text{ kPa} $$ **Step 4 — Total die pressure:** $$ P_{die} = 80 + 56 = 136 \text{ kPa} \approx 0.136 \text{ MPa} $$ **Step 5 — Plug-flow radius:** The pressure gradient in the die: $$ -\dfrac{dP}{dz} = \dfrac{P_{die}}{L} = \dfrac{136{,}000}{0.04} = 3.4 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa/m} $$ Plug-flow radius: $$ r_p = \dfrac{2\tau_y}{P_{die}/L} = \dfrac{2 \times 8000}{3.4 \times 10^6} = 4.7 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m} = 4.7 \text{ mm} $$ **Conclusion:** - **Die pressure drop = 136 kPa ≈ 0.136 MPa** - **Plug-flow radius ≈ 4.7 mm** (about 59% of die radius) > [!WARNING] > **Examiner Pitfall (PEMUT412 valuation):** > 1. **Unit mismatch** is the #1 mistake. Convert mm → m before plugging into the formula. Students who write `R = 8` (in mm) directly will get an answer off by a factor of **10⁶**. > 2. **Forgetting the yield term** in the die pressure equation — full marks only if **both terms** are computed. > 3. **Plug-flow radius** must be expressed in mm or m, not as a unitless fraction. Examiners specifically check for dimensional consistency. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — **Injection Molding and Jiggering/Jollying** **`[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Adapted PEMUT412]`** #### Part (a) — 7 Marks **Q(a).** Describe the **ceramic injection molding (CIM)** process. Discuss the role of the **binder system** and explain any four **defects** that can occur in CIM parts. **Model Solution:** **CIM Process (3 marks):** Ceramic Injection Molding is a plastic-forming process used to produce **complex, near-net-shape ceramic parts** by injecting a heated ceramic-binder mixture (called the **feedstock**) into a closed mould. The steps are: 1. **Feedstock preparation** — Ceramic powder (~80 – 85 vol%) is mixed with a thermoplastic binder (~15 – 20 vol%) at elevated temperature (~150 – 200 °C) in a sigma-blade mixer or twin-screw extruder to form a homogeneous, injection-moldable compound. 2. **Injection molding** — The feedstock is heated above the binder melting point and injected under high pressure (~10 – 100 MPa) into a closed mould using a conventional injection-molding machine. 3. **Cooling and ejection** — The mould is cooled below the binder solidification temperature, and the **green part** (called the "brown part") is ejected. 4. **Binder burnout** — The organic binder is slowly removed by thermal debinding in a controlled-atmosphere furnace (typically 200 – 600 °C) — this is the **slowest and most critical step**. 5. **Sintering** — The brown part is sintered at 1200 – 1800 °C to achieve full density. **Binder system role (2 marks):** The binder system must: - Provide **low viscosity** at the molding temperature for easy mold filling. - Give **high green strength** after cooling for demolding. - Be **removable without defects** during debinding (no slumping, no cracking). - **Wet** the ceramic powder surface for good adhesion. Typical multi-component binder systems include **polyethylene (PE) + paraffin wax + stearic acid** (surfactant) — each component has a specific function. **Defects in CIM (2 marks — 0.5 each):** 1. **Weld lines** — Formed when two flow fronts meet but do not fuse properly, leaving a weak seam. 2. **Powder-binder separation** — Occurs when binder is too fluid; powder concentrates in some regions causing density gradients. 3. **Slumping during debinding** — The binder melts before it has fully escaped, causing the part to lose shape. 4. **Voids / Blisters** — Trapped air or rapid binder decomposition creates internal voids and surface blisters. #### Part (b) — 7 Marks **Q(b).** Describe the **jiggering and jollying** processes used in traditional ceramic ware production. Compare them in terms of **(i) geometry produced, (ii) water content of the plastic mass, (iii) tool rotation, and (iv) production rate.** **Model Solution:** **Jiggering (3.5 marks):** Jiggering is a plastic forming process used to produce **flat or shallow, axially symmetric ceramic ware** such as **porcelain plates, saucers, and tiles**. - A **plastic clay bat** (moisture 20 – 25 wt%) is placed on a **rotating plaster mold** (the "jigger mold"). - A **heated metal profile tool** (the "jigger arm") descends onto the rotating clay and presses it against the rotating mold, forming the upper surface of the ware. - Excess clay is trimmed off by the tool edge. - The formed piece is then dried and fired. **Jollying (3.5 marks):** Jollying is the complement of jiggering and is used to produce **hollow, axially symmetric ware** such as **cups, bowls, and vases**. - A **plastic clay ball** is placed inside a rotating hollow plaster mold. - A **heated metal profile tool (the "jolly arm")** is inserted into the mold cavity and presses the clay outward against the mold wall. - The tool shapes the **inner surface** of the ware while the mold shapes the **outer surface**. - Excess clay is trimmed; the piece is then dried and fired. **Comparison Table (built into the answer — 0 marks additional but examiners reward clarity):** | Aspect | Jiggering | Jollying | |--------|-----------|----------| | (i) **Geometry** | Flat, shallow ware (plates) | Hollow ware (cups, bowls) | | (ii) **Water content** | 20 – 25 wt% | 20 – 25 wt% | | (iii) **Tool rotation** | Tool is stationary; mould rotates | Tool is stationary; mould rotates | | (iv) **Production rate** | 6 – 12 pieces/min (semi-auto) | 4 – 10 pieces/min (semi-auto) | > [!WARNING] > **Examiner Pitfall (PEMUT412 valuation):** > 1. Students often **confuse jiggering and jollying** — remember: **jiggering = flat plates** (tool on top); **jollying = hollow ware** (tool inside). > 2. Both processes use a **plaster mould** that absorbs water from the clay — this is why **water content is critical**. Too little water → cracks; too much → sagging. > 3. Examiners specifically look for the keyword **"axially symmetric"** — half the marks are lost if the student describes them only as "plate making" or "cup making" without this general principle. --- ## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Plastic Forming (PEMUT412 Module 1)** ### ✅ Core Definitions - **Plastic forming = shaping a plastic, dough-like ceramic mass** (powder + water + binder + lubricant). - **Bingham plastic** = visco-plastic material with a **yield stress** that must be exceeded before flow begins. ### ✅ Essential Equations (Memorize!) - Bingham model: $\tau = \tau_y + \eta_p \dot{\gamma}$ - Wall shear stress: $\tau_w = \dfrac{\Delta P R}{2L}$ - Die pressure: $P_{die} = \dfrac{2L}{R}\tau_y + \dfrac{8\eta_p L Q}{\pi R^4}$ - Plug-flow radius: $r_p = \dfrac{2\tau_y}{P/L}$ - Die swell: $B = 1 + \dfrac{1}{2}(\tau_y/\tau_w)^2$ - Bingham number: $\text{Bi} = \dfrac{\tau_y L}{\eta_p V}$ ### ✅ Four Major Processes 1. **Extrusion** — long continuous profiles (bricks, tiles, pipes, honeycombs) 2. **Injection molding (CIM)** — complex 3-D net-shape parts (technical ceramics) 3. **Jiggering & Jollying** — axially symmetric ware (plates / cups) 4. **Roll forming** — sheets, tapes, thin substrates ### ✅ High-Yield Numerical Facts - Typical extrusion pressure: **0.5 – 10 MPa** - Typical solids loading: **50 – 65 vol%** - Typical binder content (CIM): **15 – 20 vol%** of feedstock - Die pressure has **two terms**: yield term + viscous term - Plug flow dominates when **Bingham number Bi >> 1** ### ✅ Critical Distinctions (Likely 2-Mark Questions) - **Plastic forming vs. Dry pressing** — wet/dough vs. dry powder; continuous vs. discrete shapes. - **Plastic forming vs. Slip casting** — plastic mass vs. fluid suspension; no mould absorption vs. capillary absorption. - **Jiggering vs. Jollying** — flat ware vs. hollow ware. - **Stiff-mud vs. soft-mud extrusion** — moisture < 12% vs. > 18%. ### ✅ Top Defects to Remember 1. **Lamination** (air entrapment) → vacuum de-airing 2. **Die swell mismatch** → optimize flow rate, plasticizer content 3. **Shark-skin tearing** → wall lubrication, lower shear rate 4. **Slumping during debinding (CIM)** → slow heating rate 5. **Weld lines (CIM)** → gate redesign ### ✅ Real-World Applications (For 2-Mark "Give Examples") - **Extrusion**: bricks, roof tiles, ceramic pipes, cordierite honeycomb catalyst substrates. - **Injection molding**: Si₃N₄ turbine rotors, ZrO₂ dental crowns, Al₂O₃ insulators. - **Jiggering/Jollying**: porcelain tableware (cups, plates). - **Roll forming**: ferrite sheets, PZT tapes, ceramic substrates for MLCCs.

topics.title: Dry forming
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT412 - COMPOSITE MATERIALS AND CERAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Dry forming

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

**Dry forming** is a powder-processing route in ceramic manufacturing in which a *granulated, low-moisture ceramic powder* (typically containing less than **4 wt%** binder/lubricant) is consolidated into a shaped "green" body by the application of *mechanical pressure* in a rigid die or flexible mould, **without** the presence of a liquid suspension medium.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight**
> Dry forming is the **most economical** shaping technique for ceramics, accounting for the bulk of production in *structural clay products, technical ceramics, electronic ceramics,* and *refractories*. It sits alongside *plastic forming* (extrusion) and *wet forming* (slip casting, tape casting) as one of the three classical forming families.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Pharmaceutical Tablet Press"

Imagine pushing a spoonful of damp medicinal powder into a small metal cylinder and pressing it with a punch — what comes out is a hard, geometrically perfect tablet. Now replace the medicine powder with **alumina (Al₂O₃)**, the cylinder with a **tungsten carbide die**, and the punch with a **hydraulic ram capable of 200 MPa** — and you have **uniaxial dry pressing**, the workhorse of ceramic shaping.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Two Pillars of Dry Forming Success:**
> 1. **Flowability** — the powder must flow *like dry sand* into every corner of the die cavity.
> 2. **Compressibility** — the powder must *pack densely and uniformly* under pressure without trapping air or cracking.
>
> A powder that is *too fine and cohesive* flows poorly; a powder that is *too coarse* compacts poorly and leaves porous green bodies. Hence, **granulation** (spray-drying into 100–300 µm spherical agglomerates) is the secret of industrial dry pressing.

### 1.3 Standard Performance Metrics (KTU-Representative Values)

| Metric | Typical Industrial Value |
|---|---|
| Compaction Pressure | **30 – 200 MPa** (uniaxial) ; **100 – 400 MPa** (isostatic) |
| Green Density Achieved | **55 – 65 %** of theoretical |
| Powder Moisture | **< 4 wt%** |
| Granule Size | **80 – 300 µm** (post-spray drying) |
| Production Rate | **500 – 5000 parts/hour** (automatic press) |

> [!TIP]
> For a KTU board question worth full marks, **always quote a numeric range** when describing a process parameter. Examiners award additional credit for quantified, industry-grade answers.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Sub-Families of Dry Forming

#### (A) Uniaxial (Die) Pressing
- Powder is compacted in a **rigid metal die** between two moving punches.
- Best for parts of **simple geometry** (discs, bricks, tiles, bushings) with height-to-diameter ratio **H/D ≤ 1**.
- Pressure transmission is **unidirectional** → leads to non-uniform density.

#### (B) Cold Isostatic Pressing (CIP)
- Powder is sealed in a *flexible elastomeric mould* (urethane, rubber) and immersed in a pressurised fluid (typically **oil or water-glycol**) at **100 – 400 MPa**.
- Pressure is **omnidirectional** → near-isotropic green density.
- Used for large or complex parts (crucibles, spark-plug insulators, turbine preforms).

#### (C) Hot Isostatic Pressing (HIP)
- A **simultaneous combination** of high pressure (**100 – 200 MPa** of inert gas, usually argon) and elevated temperature (**1200 – 2000 °C**).
- Performs **compaction + sintering simultaneously** → near-theoretical density (**> 99.5 % TD**).
- Used for critical aerospace, biomedical, and optical ceramics.

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Operating Sequence — Uniaxial Pressing

1. **Powder Conditioning:** Granulated powder is fed from a hopper into the die cavity by *gravity flow* aided by **vibratory feeders**.
2. **Fill & Pre-compaction:** The lower punch retracts to set the *fill depth*; a scraper (or feed shoe) levels the powder.
3. **Compaction Stage:** The upper punch descends; pressure rises through the **re-arrangement → elastic deformation → plastic deformation → fragmentation → cold welding** sequence.
4. **Hold & Stress Release:** Pressure is held briefly to allow *stress relaxation* in the compact.
5. **Ejection:** The lower punch pushes the green body out of the die; *side-wall friction* during ejection is the most common source of defects.
6. **Recovery:** The part is inspected for cracks, weighed, and conveyed to sintering.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Friction is the great enemy of dry pressing.** Three friction forces act simultaneously:
> - **Die-wall friction** ($F_{dw}$) — between powder and rigid die surface.
> - **Particle-particle friction** ($F_{pp}$) — internal Coulomb friction.
> - **Punch-powder friction** ($F_{ppu}$) — between powder and punch faces.
> These losses consume up to **80–90 %** of the applied ram force in tall compacts, which is why industrial presses **lubricate die walls with stearic acid or zinc stearate**.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\rho_{green} = \dfrac{m_{compact}}{V_{compact}}$ | Green (unfired) density | g/cm³ |
| 2 | $D = \dfrac{\rho_{green}}{\rho_{theoretical}}$ | Relative (fractional) density | dimensionless |
| 3 | $P(z) = P_{0}\,e^{\, -\, 2\,\mu_{w}\,K\,z\,/\,D_{die}}$ | Janssen pressure decay along die height (axial pressure) | MPa |
| 4 | $\ln\!\left(\dfrac{1}{1-D}\right) = K\,P + A$ | **Heckel equation** — linear in pressure for plastic deformation | dimensionless |
| 5 | $\dfrac{C}{\left(1 - C\right)} = \dfrac{P}{a} + \dfrac{b}{a}$ | **Kawakita equation** — relates compaction $C$ to pressure $P$ | dimensionless |
| 6 | $\sigma_{axial} = \sigma_{radial}\,K_{0}$ | Stress state with lateral stress coefficient $K_{0}$ (Jaky: $K_0 = 1 - \sin\phi$) | MPa |
| 7 | $P_{eject} = \mu_{w}\,\sigma_{radial}\,A_{wall}$ | Ejection force due to die-wall friction | N |
| 8 | $\Delta V/V = \rho_{0}/\rho_{f} - 1$ | Volumetric strain during compaction | dimensionless |
| 9 | $P_{CIP} = \dfrac{P_{hydro}}{3}\!\left(1 + 2\,\dfrac{R_{int}}{R_{ext}}\right)$ | Pressure-transmission factor for thin-walled isostatic bags | MPa |
| 10 | $\eta = \dfrac{P_{ram}\,A_{ram}}{P_{compact}\,A_{compact}}$ | Mechanical advantage / pressure-multiplication factor | dimensionless |

> **Notation Conventions Used in the Sheet**
> $\mu_{w}$ = die-wall friction coefficient; $K$ = lateral-stress ratio; $D_{die}$ = die diameter; $\phi$ = internal angle of friction; $C$ = degree of volume reduction; $a, b$ = Kawakita material constants; $P_0$ = applied top-punch pressure; $z$ = depth from top punch.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Dry-formed parts form the backbone of:
- **Alumina substrates** for hybrid integrated circuits.
- **Silicon nitride** and **zirconia** rolling-element bearings.
- **PZT piezo-ceramics** for fuel injectors and ultrasonic transducers.
- **Silicon carbide** wear tiles in mining and armour.
- **Refractory bricks** for blast furnaces and glass tanks.

In every one of these cases, dry forming was chosen because it is **fast, dimensionally precise, and easily automated** — the three virtues demanded by mass production.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Heckel Equation (Logarithmic Reciprocal-Density Form)

The **Heckel equation** is the single most important compaction relation in ceramic dry forming. It models the densification of a powder bed as a first-order kinetic process in pressure.

**Starting Physical Statement:**
For a powder undergoing plastic deformation, the rate of change of pore volume with respect to pressure is proportional to the *current* pore volume.

$$\dfrac{d\,(\text{pore volume})}{dP} \;=\; -\,k\,\times\,(\text{current pore volume})$$

**Step 1 — Express pore volume in terms of relative density D:**
The relative density is $D = \rho_{green}/\rho_{th}$, so the pore volume fraction is $(1 - D)$. Substituting:

$$\dfrac{d(1 - D)}{dP} \;=\; -\,k\,(1 - D)$$

**Step 2 — Rearrange the differential equation:**
Move $(1 - D)$ to the left-hand side:

$$\dfrac{d(1 - D)}{(1 - D)} \;=\; -\,k\,dP$$

**Step 3 — Integrate both sides from the initial state ($D = 0$ at $P = 0$) to a general state ($D$ at $P$):**
Performing the integration on the left from $(1 - 0) = 1$ to $(1 - D)$, and on the right from $0$ to $P$:

$$\ln(1 - D)\,\Big|_{1}^{\,1 - D} \;=\; -\,k\,P\,\Big|_{0}^{\,P}$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the limits:**
On the left, $\ln(1 - D) - \ln(1) = \ln(1 - D) - 0 = \ln(1 - D)$. On the right, we obtain $-kP$.

$$\ln(1 - D) \;=\; -\,kP$$

**Step 5 — Multiply by $-1$ to obtain the conventional Heckel form:**
Flipping the sign and introducing a constant $A$ that accounts for initial particle rearrangement (which is *not* a plastic process and is not captured by the simple integration above):

$$\ln\!\left(\dfrac{1}{1 - D}\right) \;=\; kP \;+\; A$$

**Step 6 — Replace $k$ with the conventional symbol $K$:**
The slope $K$ is the **Heckel constant** — a direct measure of the powder's *plastic deformability*. *Soft, ductile* powders (e.g., NaCl, ductile metals) give large $K$; *hard, brittle* ceramics (e.g., SiC, B₄C) give small $K$.

$$\boxed{\;\ln\!\left(\dfrac{1}{1 - D}\right) \;=\; K\,P \;+\; A\;}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Interpretation of the Intercept $A$:**
> $A = \ln\!\left(\dfrac{1}{1 - D_0}\right)$, where $D_0$ is the *tap density* (the density obtained by merely settling the powder). Hence $A$ encodes the **efficiency of initial rearrangement** before any plastic flow begins.

### 3.2 Derivation of Axial-Pressure Decay (Janssen's Equation) in a Die
