<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model QP]`
**Define whiskers. Mention any two important properties that make them attractive as reinforcements.**

**Model Answer:**

Whiskers are small, hair-like, **single-crystal fibers** (diameter 1 – 10 µm) grown under controlled conditions, possessing a near-flawless internal structure.

**Two key attractive properties:**

1. **Near-theoretical strength** — due to absence of dislocations, grain boundaries and surface flaws; SiC whiskers reach **7 – 14 GPa**.
2. **High aspect ratio** (typically 50 – 1000), enabling efficient stress transfer from matrix to whisker via interfacial shear.

*(Other valid answers: high elastic modulus, high melting point, low thermal expansion, chemical stability at high temperatures.)*

---

### **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model QP]`
**Why do whiskers exhibit much higher strength than bulk materials of the same composition?**

**Model Answer:**

According to **Griffith's fracture criterion**, the fracture strength $\sigma_f$ is inversely proportional to the square root of the crack length $a$. In bulk materials, microscopic flaws and grain boundaries act as stress concentrators and crack-initiation sites. In whiskers, the extremely small diameter provides **insufficient volume** to contain any flaw of critical size. Hence, $a \to 0$, and:

$$\sigma_{f} \to \dfrac{E}{10}$$

This results in whiskers achieving **20 – 50 %** of the theoretical cohesive strength of the parent material — far exceeding bulk strengths.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Rule:** Each Part-B question carries **14 marks**, usually split as **(a) 7 marks** + **(b) 7 marks**. Students answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full. Below are two alternative questions for the same topic.

---

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, Adapted]`

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**
**Explain the VLS (Vapor–Liquid–Solid) method of whisker growth with a neat diagram. List the stages and the role of the catalyst.**

**Model Answer:**

The **VLS method**, developed by Wagner and Ellis in 1964, is the most widely used industrial process for growing high-quality single-crystal whiskers, especially SiC and Al₂O₃.

**Stages of VLS Growth:**

| Stage | Process | Role |
| :---: | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **Alloy droplet formation** | Catalyst (Au, Fe, Ni) forms liquid droplet with source material above eutectic temperature |
| 2 | **Vapor dissolution** | Source vapor (e.g., SiO, SiCl₄) dissolves preferentially into the liquid droplet |
| 3 | **Supersaturation** | Continued dissolution raises solute concentration above $C_{eq}$ |
| 4 | **Axial precipitation** | Excess solute precipitates at the liquid–solid interface, pushing the droplet up and extruding a single-crystal whisker beneath it |

**Role of the Catalyst:**

- Provides a **preferential site** for vapor absorption.
- Forms a **liquid alloy** that lowers the energy barrier for crystallization.
- Acts as a **"tip"** that floats atop the growing whisker, dictating diameter.
- Ensures **single-crystal growth** by restricting nucleation to the liquid–solid interface.

**Diagram (text-based representation):**

```
        ↑ Whisker growing axially
        |
   ~~~~~○~~~~~   ← Liquid catalyst droplet (Au-Si alloy)
        |
        |
   ═══════════   ← Substrate (e.g., graphite)
```

---

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**
**A SiC whisker-reinforced aluminium composite contains 20 vol % SiC whiskers. Given: $\sigma_{w} = 7\ \text{GPa}$, $\sigma_{m} = 0.3\ \text{GPa}$, $d = 5\ \mu\text{m}$, $\tau_i = 50\ \text{MPa}$. Calculate (i) composite tensile strength, and (ii) critical whisker length.**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Composite Tensile Strength:**

Using rule of mixtures:

$$\sigma_{c} = \sigma_{w} V_{w} + \sigma_{m} V_{m}$$

$$V_{m} = 1 - 0.20 = 0.80$$

$$\sigma_{c} = (7 \times 10^{9})(0.20) + (0.3 \times 10^{9})(0.80)$$

$$\sigma_{c} = 1.40 \times 10^{9} + 0.24 \times 10^{9}$$

$$\boxed{\sigma_{c} = 1.64\ \text{GPa}}$$

**(ii) Critical Whisker Length:**

$$l_{c} = \dfrac{\sigma_{f} \cdot d}{4 \tau_{i}}$$

$$l_{c} = \dfrac{(7 \times 10^{9}) \cdot (5 \times 10^{-6})}{4 \cdot (50 \times 10^{6})}$$

$$l_{c} = \dfrac{3.5 \times 10^{4}}{2 \times 10^{8}}$$

$$\boxed{l_{c} = 1.75 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{m} = 175\ \mu\text{m}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Part b):**
> 
> - Students frequently **omit the factor 4** in the critical-length formula, writing $\tau_i$ instead of $4\tau_i$ — this causes a 4× error in the answer and **loses 2 marks**.
> - Forgetting to **convert µm to m** before substituting into the formula leads to wrong units and **loses another 1 mark**.
> - Always write $V_m = 1 - V_w$ explicitly; examiners check for this step in the working.

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023, Adapted]`

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**
**Discuss the various types of whiskers used in composite materials. Compare their mechanical properties.**

**Model Answer:**

Whiskers are classified by **material type**, **growth mechanism**, and **application**.

**By Material:**

1. **Ceramic Whiskers** (most common):
   - *SiC whiskers* — strength 7 – 14 GPa, modulus 450 GPa, density 3.21 g/cm³.
   - *Al₂O₃ whiskers* — strength 10 – 20 GPa, modulus 460 GPa, density 3.96 g/cm³.
   - *Si₃N₄ whiskers* — strength 5 – 10 GPa, excellent thermal shock resistance.

2. **Metallic Whiskers** (lab-scale): Fe, Cu whiskers with strength ≈ 2 – 5 GPa.

3. **Oxide Whiskers**: Potassium titanate (K₂O·6TiO₂) — used in polymer composites for low-cost reinforcement.

**Mechanical Property Comparison Table:**

| Whisker | Tensile Strength (GPa) | Young's Modulus (GPa) | Density (g/cm³) | Specific Strength (GPa per g/cm³) |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| SiC | 7 – 14 | 450 | 3.21 | 2.18 – 4.36 |
| Al₂O₃ | 10 – 20 | 460 | 3.96 | 2.53 – 5.05 |
| Si₃N₄ | 5 – 10 | 350 | 3.18 | 1.57 – 3.14 |
| B₄C | 6 – 8 | 470 | 2.52 | 2.38 – 3.17 |

**Conclusion:** Al₂O₃ whiskers give the highest absolute strength; SiC whiskers offer the best balance of strength, modulus and thermal stability — making them the **most commercially important**.

---

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**
**Explain the term "theoretical strength" of a whisker. Derive the relation $\sigma_{th} \approx E/10$. Why don't bulk materials achieve this value?**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition:** Theoretical strength is the maximum stress a *perfect, defect-free* crystal can withstand before its atomic bonds rupture simultaneously along a plane.

**Derivation:**

Based on the sinusoidal interatomic potential approximation:

$$F(x) = F_{max} \cdot \sin\!\left(\dfrac{2\pi x}{\lambda}\right)$$

For small $x$:

$$F \approx F_{max} \cdot \dfrac{2\pi x}{\lambda}$$

Comparing with Hooke's Law ($\sigma = E\varepsilon$):

$$E = \dfrac{F_{max}}{a_{0}} \cdot \dfrac{2\pi a_{0}}{\lambda \cdot a_{0}}$$

Maximum stress occurs at $x = \lambda/4$:

$$\sigma_{th} = \dfrac{F_{max}}{a_{0}^{2}} = \dfrac{E \lambda}{2\pi a_{0}^{2}}$$

Using equilibrium condition $\lambda \approx a_{0}$:

$$\sigma_{th} = \dfrac{E}{2\pi} \approx \dfrac{E}{10}$$

**Why bulk materials don't reach this:**

- **Microstructural flaws:** Grain boundaries, dislocations, vacancies.
- **Surface cracks:** Initiate Griffith-type fracture well below theoretical limit.
- **Impurities and inclusions:** Act as stress concentrators.

Bulk materials typically achieve only **1/100 to 1/1000** of $E/10$, whereas whiskers (being near-flawless) attain **20 – 50 %** of $\sigma_{th}$.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Part b):**
> 
> - Most students skip the derivation entirely and write just the final formula — examiners expect **at least 4 derivation steps** for full 7 marks.
> - Confusing "theoretical strength" with "yield strength" is a common **2-mark deduction**.
> - Students often forget to **state the equilibrium condition** ($\lambda \approx a_0$) — this is the crucial step linking the sinusoidal model to the E/10 result.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **🧠 Rapid Revision Checklist — Whiskers**

- [ ] **Definition:** Single-crystal, flaw-free fibers, 1 – 10 µm diameter.
- [ ] **Key Strength Formula:** $\sigma_{th} \approx E/10$.
- [ ] **Griffith Equation:** $\sigma_f = \sqrt{2 E \gamma_s / (\pi a)}$ — flaw-dependent.
- [ ] **Aspect Ratio (AR):** $L/d$, typically 50 – 1000 for whiskers.
- [ ] **Critical Length:** $l_c = \sigma_f d / (4 \tau_i)$ — minimum length for full load transfer.
- [ ] **VLS Method:** 3 stages — alloy droplet → vapor dissolution → axial precipitation.
- [ ] **Common Whiskers:** SiC, Al₂O₃, Si₃N₄, B₄C, K₂O·6TiO₂.
- [ ] **Strength Order:** Whiskers > Continuous fibers > Particulates (in same material).
- [ ] **Rule of Mixtures:** $\sigma_c = \sigma_w V_w + \sigma_m V_m$.
- [ ] **Major Use:** Cutting tools (Al₂O₃ + SiC whisker), aerospace turbine blades, brake pads.
- [ ] **Catalyst for VLS:** Au, Fe, Ni (forms liquid alloy with source vapor).
- [ ] **Theoretical Strength Achieved:** 20 – 50 % of $\sigma_{th}$ for high-quality whiskers.
- [ ] **Typical $l_c$ for SiC/Al:** ~ 175 µm (for $d = 5\ \mu\text{m}$, $\tau_i = 50$ MPa).
- [ ] **Examiner Buzzwords to Use:** *single crystal*, *theoretical strength*, *high aspect ratio*, *Griffith flaw*, *VLS growth*, *rule of mixtures*.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Comparison of fibers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT412 - COMPOSITE MATERIALS AND CERAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Comparison of fibers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Definition of Fibers in Composite Context

In the domain of **Fiber-Reinforced Composites (FRCs)**, a *fiber* is defined as a **discontinuous or continuous filamentary reinforcement phase** characterized by a high **aspect ratio** (length-to-diameter ratio, typically $\geq 100$), which serves as the principal load-bearing constituent within a comparatively weaker **matrix material**. Fibers transmit and distribute applied loads through the matrix via an **interfacial shear transfer mechanism** governed by the **shear lag theory** originally proposed by Cox (1952).

The mechanical performance of any composite is fundamentally dictated by the **rule of mixtures** behavior of the reinforcing fibers, making their selection and comparison the most critical design decision in composite engineering.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Mandate (Module 2):** Students must be able to compare natural fibers (jute, sisal, coir, flax) with synthetic fibers (glass, carbon, aramid) on the basis of specific strength, specific modulus, cost, density, and environmental impact.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy: "The Rebar in Concrete"

Imagine you are building a small bridge. Concrete by itself is strong when you push on it (compression), but it cracks easily if you try to bend or stretch it. Engineers solve this by embedding **steel rods (rebar)** inside the concrete. The steel takes the pulling/tensile forces, while the concrete protects the steel and holds everything in shape.

**Fibers in composites play the exact same role as steel rebar in concrete.** The **matrix** (polymer, metal, or ceramic) is like the concrete — it is relatively weak in tension but protects the fibers, transfers load, and maintains geometry. The **fibers** are the "rebar" — they carry the bulk of the tensile load. The **interface** is the bond between rebar and concrete, which is critical to performance.

Just like engineers must choose the *right kind of rebar* (mild steel, HYSD bars, stainless steel) based on load, environment, and cost, a composite engineer must choose the *right kind of fiber* based on mechanical, thermal, chemical, and economic criteria.

## 1.3 Classification of Fibers (Foundation for Comparison)

To compare fibers intelligently, one must first classify them. The KTU 2024 scheme expects students to be conversant with the following taxonomy:

**1. Natural Fibers**
- **Plant-based (Lignocellulosic):** Jute, Flax, Hemp, Sisal, Banana, Coir, Cotton
- **Animal-based (Protein):** Silk, Wool
- **Mineral-based:** Asbestos (historically), Basalt

**2. Synthetic (Man-Made) Fibers**
- **Glass Fibers:** E-glass, S-glass, C-glass, AR-glass
- **Carbon Fibers:** PAN-based, Pitch-based
- **Aramid Fibers:** Kevlar 29, Kevlar 49, Nomex
- **Ceramic Fibers:** Alumina ($Al_2O_3$), Silicon Carbide (SiC), Nextel series
- **Boron Fibers:** Tungsten-cored boron filaments
- **Polymeric Fibers:** UHMWPE (Spectra, Dyneema), PBO (Zylon)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Specific Strength and Specific Modulus** are the two most important comparison metrics in fiber selection. They are normalized by density and therefore directly indicate *structural efficiency per unit weight* — a critical parameter in aerospace, automotive, and sporting goods applications.

## 1.4 Key Comparison Metrics (Standardized Units)

When comparing fibers, the following **standardized performance metrics** (with their SI units) are universally used in the composites industry:

- **Tensile Strength ($\sigma_f$):** Force per unit cross-sectional area, measured in **MPa** or **GPa**
- **Young's Modulus ($E_f$):** Resistance to elastic deformation, measured in **GPa**
- **Density ($\rho_f$):** Mass per unit volume, measured in **g/cm³** or **kg/m³**
- **Specific Strength:** $\sigma_f / \rho_f$, measured in **MPa·cm³/g** or **kN·m/kg**
- **Specific Modulus:** $E_f / \rho_f$, measured in **GPa·cm³/g** or **MN·m/kg**
- **Elongation at Break ($\epsilon_f$):** Percentage strain at fracture, **dimensionless (%)**
- **Diameter ($d_f$):** Micrometers (**µm**)
- **Operating Temperature:** Degrees Celsius (**°C**)
- **Cost:** USD per kg (**\$/kg**)

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Reference Density** for comparison: Water has a density of **1.00 g/cm³**. Any fiber with a specific gravity less than this floats on water; most synthetic fibers are denser, while natural lignocellulosic fibers (jute, coir) often float due to hollow cellular structures and low-density parenchyma cells.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The "Why" Behind Fiber Comparison

Not all fibers are created equal. The composite engineer must answer: *"Given a load, environment, weight budget, and cost ceiling, which fiber offers the best balance of properties?"* The comparison must be holistic because optimizing for one property (e.g., stiffness) often sacrifices another (e.g., toughness or cost).

The **four pillars of fiber comparison** in KTU 2024 are:

1. **Mechanical Performance:** Strength, modulus, toughness (area under stress-strain curve)
2. **Physical Behavior:** Density, aspect ratio, filament diameter
3. **Environmental/Durability:** Moisture absorption, chemical resistance, UV stability, max service temperature
4. **Economic/Sustainability:** Raw material cost, processing energy, recyclability, biodegradability

## 2.2 Detailed Comparison of Major Synthetic Fibers

### 2.2.1 Glass Fibers (The Industrial Workhorse)

**E-Glass** (Electrical grade) is the most widely produced fiber globally (~90% market share). It is a calcium alumino-borosilicate glass with low alkali content. It offers a *good balance* of strength, electrical insulation, and cost.

**S-Glass** (Structural grade) is a magnesia-alumina-silicate composition with ~40% higher tensile strength than E-glass, used in aerospace and defense.

**C-Glass** (Chemical grade) has superior acid corrosion resistance, used in chemical storage tanks.

**AR-Glass** (Alkali Resistant) is used as the reinforcement in cement/concrete composites (GFRC panels) where the alkaline pore solution would attack normal glass.

### 2.2.2 Carbon Fibers (The High-Performance Champion)

Carbon fibers are produced by the controlled pyrolysis of precursor fibers (PAN or Pitch). The microstructure consists of graphitic crystallites aligned along the fiber axis, providing **exceptional axial stiffness** because the strong covalent C–C bonds in the graphite basal plane are aligned with the load direction.

Carbon fibers are classified by their **modulus grade**:
- **Standard Modulus (SM):** ~230 GPa
- **Intermediate Modulus (IM):** ~290–350 GPa
- **High Modulus (HM):** ~400–500 GPa
- **Ultra-High Modulus (UHM):** >500 GPa

### 2.2.3 Aramid Fibers (The Tough, Impact-Resistant Performer)

Aramid (aromatic polyamide) fibers, marketed as **Kevlar** (DuPont) and **Twaron** (Teijin), have a molecular structure of rigid rod-like polymer chains connected by strong hydrogen bonds. This gives them exceptional **tensile toughness** and **energy absorption** under impact, making them ideal for ballistic armor and cut-resistant gloves.

However, aramids suffer from:
- Poor compressive strength (the rod-like chains buckle easily)
- UV degradation
- Moisture sensitivity
- Difficulty in machining/cutting

### 2.2.4 Ceramic Fibers (The High-Temperature Specialists)

These include **Alumina ($Al_2O_3$)**, **Silicon Carbide (SiC)**, and **Nextel** (3M) series. They retain strength above 1000°C and are used in ceramic matrix composites (CMCs) for jet engine components, space shuttle tiles, and high-temperature furnace insulation.

### 2.2.5 Boron Fibers (The Aerospace Veteran)

Boron fibers are produced by chemical vapor deposition of boron onto a tungsten or carbon filament substrate (~12 µm). They have very large diameters (100–200 µm) and high tensile strength, but their high cost and weight limit use to military aircraft (e.g., F-14 Tomcat horizontal stabilizers) and some space structures.

## 2.3 Detailed Comparison of Natural Fibers

Natural plant fibers are composed primarily of **cellulose microfibrils** embedded in a matrix of **hemicellulose** and **lignin**. The mechanical properties vary considerably because they depend on:
- Plant source and variety
- Cultivation conditions (soil, climate, rainfall)
- Fiber extraction method (retting, decortication, mechanical)
- Fiber orientation relative to the stem axis
- Microfibril angle (MFA) of cellulose helices

**Jute** is the most widely used natural fiber due to low cost, high availability, and reasonable strength. It is a **bast fiber** obtained from the *Corchorus* plant stem.

**Coir** is extracted from coconut husk and has high lignin content, making it more weather-resistant but less strong than jute. It is widely available in **Kerala** — giving it special KTU regional relevance.

**Flax, Hemp, Sisal, Banana, Ramie** offer higher specific modulus than jute and are preferred in European automotive applications (e.g., door panels, seat backs in Mercedes-Benz, BMW).

| If you need... | Choose this fiber family | Reason |

| Lowest cost | E-Glass or Jute | Abundant raw material, mature processing |
| Highest specific strength | UHMWPE (Dyneema) | Lightest fiber, very high strength |

| Best impact/toughness | Aramid (Kevlar) | High energy absorption, ductile failure |
| Highest temperature use | SiC or Alumina ceramic | Retain strength >1000°C |
| Eco-friendly/biodegradable | Jute, Flax, Coir | Renewable, low embodied energy |
| Aerospace primary structure | IM/HM Carbon | Maximum stiffness-to-weight |
| Ballistic armor | Aramid (Kevlar) or UHMWPE | Energy dissipation by fibrillation |
| Concrete reinforcement | AR-Glass or Basalt | Alkali resistance |
| Marine (boat hulls) | E-Glass with vinyl ester | Moisture resistance, low cost |

## 2.5 Engineering Utility of This Comparison

In real engineering practice, this comparison drives:
- **Aerospace** → Carbon fiber dominates (Boeing 787 is ~50% composites)
- **Automotive** → Glass fiber (body panels), natural fiber (interior trim), carbon fiber (F1, premium EVs)
- **Wind Energy** → E-glass for blades (length up to 100m requires low cost + high specific stiffness)
- **Defense** → Aramid (body armor), boron (military aircraft stiffeners)
- **Construction** → AR-glass, basalt, steel (rebar)
- **Sports** → Carbon, aramid, UHMWPE depending on sport

## 2.6 Rules of Thumb and Critical Trends (KTU Favorites)

1. **Carbon > Aramid > Glass** in specific stiffness (modulus/density)
2. **UHMWPE > Aramid > Carbon > Glass** in specific strength
3. **Natural fibers are always cheaper but weaker and more moisture-sensitive** than synthetics
4. **Ceramic fibers (SiC, Alumina) are the only choice above 800°C**
5. **Aramids fail in compression; use them only in tensile-dominated applications**
6. **E-glass dominates ~90% of the reinforcement market by volume** due to cost
7. **The "specific" properties (divided by density) are the only fair comparison** — pure strength numbers are misleading if densities differ

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Why "Specific" Properties Matter — Worked Example

A common KTU-type question asks: *"Compare Glass and Carbon fibers for an aerospace application. Which is better per unit weight?"* The numerical answer requires computing specific strength and specific modulus.

**Given Data (from standard tables):**
- E-Glass: $\sigma_f = 2400$ MPa, $E_f = 72$ GPa, $\rho_f = 2.55$ g/cm³
- Carbon (PAN, IM grade): $\sigma_f = 4400$ MPa, $E_f = 290$ GPa, $\rho_f = 1.78$ g/cm³

**Goal:** Calculate specific strength and specific modulus for both, then compute the **% improvement** of Carbon over Glass.

### Step 1: Convert Units to a Common Base (SI: Pa·m³/kg)

Since density is in g/cm³ = kg/(10⁻³ m³) = 1000 kg/m³, we have:
$$\rho_{E\text{-Glass}} = 2.55 \text{ g/cm}^3 = 2550 \text{ kg/m}^3$$
$$\rho_{Carbon} = 1.78 \text{ g/cm}^3 = 1780 \text{ kg/m}^3$$

### Step 2: Calculate Specific Strength for E-Glass

$$\begin{aligned}
\left(\frac{\sigma_f}{\rho_f}\right)_{\text{Glass}} &= \frac{2400 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa}}{2550 \text{ kg/m}^3} \\
&= \frac{2.4 \times 10^9}{2.55 \times 10^3} \text{ N·m/kg} \\
&= 9.41 \times 10^5 \text{ N·m/kg} \\
&\approx 941 \text{ kN·m/kg}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 3: Calculate Specific Strength for Carbon

$$\begin{aligned}
\left(\frac{\sigma_f}{\rho_f}\right)_{Carbon} &= \frac{4400 \times 10^6}{1780} \\
&= \frac{4.4 \times 10^9}{1.78 \times 10^3} \\
&= 2.47 \times 10^6 \text{ N·m/kg} \\
&\approx 2472 \text{ kN·m/kg}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 4: Calculate the % Improvement

$$\begin{aligned}
\%\text{ Improvement} &= \frac{2472 - 941}{941} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{1531}{941} \times 100 \\
&\approx 162.7\%
\end{aligned}$$

**Interpretation:** Carbon fiber delivers ~163% more strength per unit weight than E-Glass. This is why every kilogram saved in an aircraft (e.g., Airbus A350) translates to millions of dollars in fuel savings over the aircraft's lifetime.

### Step 5: Calculate Specific Modulus (Stiffness) for Both

$$\begin{aligned}
\left(\frac{E_f}{\rho_f}\right)_{\text{Glass}} &= \frac{72 \times 10^9 \text{ Pa}}{2550 \text{ kg/m}^3} \\
&= 28.2 \times 10^6 \text{ N·m/kg} \\
&= 28.2 \text{ MN·m/kg}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\left(\frac{E_f}{\rho_f}\right)_{Carbon} &= \frac{290 \times 10^9}{1780} \\
&= 162.9 \times 10^6 \text{ N·m/kg} \\
&= 162.9 \text{ MN·m/kg}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\%\text{ Improvement in Stiffness} &= \frac{162.9 - 28.2}{28.2} \times 100 \\
&\approx 478\%
\end{aligned}$$

**Insight:** Carbon is nearly **5× stiffer** than glass for the same weight. This is why carbon-reinforced wings maintain their aerodynamic shape under high dynamic loading that would flex a glass wing.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Ranking Fibers by Various Criteria

The following matrix shows the **rank of each fiber** (1 = best in that criterion, higher number = worse) for direct comparison:

| Property | E-Glass | S-Glass | Carbon (IM) | Aramid | Alumina | SiC | Jute | Coir |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Tensile Strength (raw) | 5 | 2 | 1 | 4 | 7 | 3 | 8 | 9 |
| Modulus (raw) | 7 | 6 | 2 | 5 | 3 | 2 | 8 | 9 |
| Specific Strength | 6 | 4 | 2 | 3 | 8 | 5 | 7 | 9 |
| Specific Modulus | 7 | 6 | 1 | 4 | 3 | 2 | 8 | 9 |
| Max Service Temp | 5 | 4 | 5 | 6 | 2 | 1 | 8 | 8 |
| Impact Resistance | 4 | 4 | 6 | 1 | 8 | 7 | 3 | 2 |
| Moisture Resistance | 2 | 2 | 1 | 4 | 1 | 1 | 7 | 8 |
| Cost-Effectiveness | 2 | 5 | 7 | 6 | 8 | 8 | 1 | 1 |
| Eco-Friendliness | 6 | 6 | 6 | 6 | 6 | 6 | 1 | 1 |

**Reading the Matrix:** E-Glass wins in cost and moisture resistance but loses in stiffness. Carbon dominates mechanical performance but is expensive. Jute is unbeatable for cost and eco-friendliness but fails on mechanical and thermal properties.

## 3.3 Why Carbon Fiber Has a "Negative" Thermal Expansion

A truly unique property of carbon fibers (and a frequent KTU conceptual question): **Carbon fibers have a NEGATIVE coefficient of thermal expansion (CTE) along the fiber axis**, with values ranging from **-0.5 to -1.5 × 10⁻⁶ /°C** depending on the grade.

**Why does this happen?**

Carbon fibers are made of graphitic crystallites (hexagonal layers of $sp^2$-bonded carbon). Along the basal plane (a-axis), carbon has one of the highest known bond stiffnesses. When the fiber is heated, the in-plane **anharmonic vibration** of the carbon atoms in the graphite lattice results in a **contraction along the fiber axis** at low temperatures. At very high temperatures (>500°C), phonon contributions may reverse this to a small positive CTE.

**Engineering Consequence:** Carbon fiber composites can be designed as **zero-CTE structures** (by balancing the negative CTE of fibers with the positive CTE of the matrix). This is critical for:
- Space-based telescopes (JWST, Hubble)
- Optical benches in satellites
- High-precision measurement equipment
- Telescope mirror substrates

This is a property *no other commercial fiber can match*, and it is often cited in KTU viva voce questions as a "unique feature" of carbon reinforcement.

## 3.4 Comparative Analysis: Natural vs. Synthetic Fibers

| Property | Natural Fibers (Jute, Flax) | Synthetic Fibers (E-Glass, Carbon) | Implication |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Raw Material Source** | Renewable, annually harvested | Petrochemicals, mined minerals | Natural fibers are sustainable |
| **CO₂ Footprint** | -0.5 to -1.0 kg CO₂/kg fiber (often carbon-negative) | 5–30 kg CO₂/kg fiber (carbon fiber is worst) | Natural is far better for LCA |
| **Density** | 1.2–1.5 g/cm³ | 1.78–2.6 g/cm³ | Natural is lighter, but strength is also lower |
| **Mechanical Strength** | 200–900 MPa | 2000–5000 MPa | Synthetic is 3–25× stronger |
| **Modulus** | 5–70 GPa | 70–500 GPa | Synthetic is 5–100× stiffer |
| **Moisture Absorption** | 5–15% by weight | <0.5% | Natural fibers swell, rot, and lose properties in humidity |
| **Dimensional Stability** | Poor (humidity-sensitive) | Excellent | Synthetic is more reliable |
| **Processing Temperature** | Limited to ~200°C (decompose) | Can be processed to 400°C+ | Synthetic allows higher-cure matrices |
| **Fiber-Matrix Adhesion** | Poor with hydrophobic polymers (PP, PE) | Good with most polymers | Natural needs chemical treatment (NaOH, silane) |
| **Cost** | 0.3–1.5 USD/kg | 1.5–100+ USD/kg (carbon) | Natural is much cheaper |
| **Recyclability** | Biodegradable in months | Hundreds of years to degrade | Natural is fully compostable |
| **Variability** | High (plant, climate, retting) | Low (factory-controlled) | Natural has inconsistent quality |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Engineering Trade-off:** Natural fibers offer sustainability and low cost but at the price of poor mechanical performance, high moisture sensitivity, and inconsistent quality. The modern trend in **hybrid composites** combines natural fibers (for cost and eco-credentials in non-structural panels) with synthetic fibers (for load-bearing regions) to balance performance, cost, and sustainability.

## 3.5 Method to Choose the "Best" Fiber — Weighted Decision Analysis

A formal KTU 14-mark question may ask: *"Recommend a suitable fiber for [specific application] with justification."* The systematic approach is:

1. **List the application requirements** (e.g., strength, stiffness, temp, cost, weight)
2. **Assign weights** $w_i$ to each requirement (sum = 1)
3. **Score each candidate fiber** $s_{ij}$ on a scale of 1–10 for each requirement
4. **Compute weighted total:** $T_j = \sum_i w_i \cdot s_{ij}$
5. **Highest $T_j$** = best fiber for that application
