# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**Q:** Define the terms *interface* and *interphase* in composite materials. Why is the interfacial region considered the most critical part of a composite?

**Model Answer:**

The **interface** is the two-dimensional contact boundary that forms between the matrix and the reinforcement phase in a composite material. Across this boundary, there exists a discontinuity in one or more material properties such as elastic modulus, chemical composition, or crystal structure.

The **interphase** is the three-dimensional region of finite thickness (typically nanometers to micrometers) on either side of the interface, where the properties gradually transition from those of the bulk fiber to those of the bulk matrix.

The interfacial region is critical because it governs the **load transfer** from the weak matrix to the stiff reinforcement. If the interfacial bond is weak, the composite fails prematurely due to fiber pull-out, debonding, or delamination. Conversely, an excessively strong interface causes brittle failure. The interface thus dictates the **strength**, **toughness**, **fatigue life**, and **environmental durability** of the composite.

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** Students often confuse interface (2D boundary) with interphase (3D region). Examiners explicitly test this distinction for 1 mark.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**Q:** List and briefly explain the three primary types of interfacial bonding in composites. State one example of each.

**Model Answer:**

The three primary types of interfacial bonding are:

1. **Mechanical Bonding:** Arises from the physical interlocking of matrix material into the surface roughness, pores, or geometric irregularities of the reinforcement. *Example:* Steel-reinforced concrete uses deformed rebars that mechanically interlock with cement paste.

2. **Physical Bonding (Van der Waals / Physiosorption):** Arises from universal intermolecular forces such as London dispersion, Keesom dipole–dipole, and Debye induced-dipole interactions. *Example:* Untreated carbon fibers in a thermoplastic matrix rely primarily on dispersion forces.

3. **Chemical Bonding (Chemisorption):** Arises from the formation of primary covalent, ionic, or metallic bonds across the interface, typically facilitated by a coupling agent. *Example:* Silane-treated glass fibers in epoxy resin form Si–O–Si covalent bridges across the interface.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Modified]

**Q:** (a) Derive the expression for the **critical fiber length** $\ell_c$ of a fiber-reinforced composite, starting from a force balance at the fiber–matrix interface. Explain the significance of the critical aspect ratio. **(7 marks)**

**(b) With the aid of a suitable diagram, describe the **mechanism of load transfer** across the fiber–matrix interface. Discuss how the interfacial shear strength (IFSS) is measured experimentally using the **single-fiber pull-out test**. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question A — Part (a)

**Force Balance Setup:**

Consider a single cylindrical fiber of diameter $d$ and length $\ell$ embedded coaxially in a matrix. When a tensile stress $\sigma_f$ develops in the fiber, it is balanced by the shear stress $\tau_i$ acting on the cylindrical fiber–matrix interface over a length $\ell/2$ (the half-length because stress builds from each end toward the center).

**Equilibrium equation (force balance):**

$$\sigma_f \cdot \frac{\pi d^2}{4} = \tau_i \cdot (\pi d) \cdot \frac{\ell}{2}$$

**Simplification:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_f &= \frac{2 \tau_i \ell}{d}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Definition of Critical Length:**

The critical length $\ell_c$ is the minimum fiber length at which the fiber reaches its ultimate tensile strength $\sigma_{fu}$:

$$\sigma_{fu} = \frac{2 \tau_i \ell_c}{d}$$

**Solving for $\ell_c$:**

$$\boxed{\ell_c = \frac{\sigma_{fu} \, d}{2 \tau_i}}$$

**Critical Aspect Ratio:**

$$\boxed{\left(\frac{\ell_c}{d}\right) = \frac{\sigma_{fu}}{2 \tau_i}}$$

**Significance:**

- The critical aspect ratio is a **dimensionless design parameter** that determines whether a fiber can be loaded to its full strength.
- If the actual aspect ratio $\ell/d < \ell_c/d$, the fiber will pull out of the matrix before reaching $\sigma_{fu}$, and the reinforcement is inefficient.
- For efficient reinforcement, designers use $\ell/d \geq 10 \, (\ell_c/d)$ to ensure fiber fracture rather than pull-out.
- This concept directly impacts **fiber selection** in composites — high-strength fibers (large $\sigma_{fu}$) or low IFSS systems (small $\tau_i$) demand longer fibers.

---

### Model Solution for Question A — Part (b)

**Mechanism of Load Transfer (with diagram description):**

When a tensile load is applied along the fiber axis in a composite, the load is initially borne by the matrix because its modulus is lower than the fiber's. The matrix deforms and tries to slide relative to the fiber, but this sliding is resisted by the interfacial shear stress $\tau_i$. This shear stress transfers load from the matrix to the fiber, building up fiber tensile stress from each end toward the center.

The fiber stress distribution follows approximately:

$$\sigma_f(x) = \frac{2 \tau_i x}{d}$$

where $x$ is the distance from the fiber end. The stress is **zero** at the fiber tip and **maximum** at the midpoint. Once $\sigma_f$ reaches $\sigma_{fu}$, the fiber fractures. The IFSS determines how rapidly stress builds along the fiber length.

**Single-Fiber Pull-Out Test (IFSS Measurement):**

In this test, a single fiber is partially embedded in a small block of matrix material, and the free end of the fiber is gripped in a tensile testing machine. The fiber is pulled out while the force vs. displacement is recorded.

The **interfacial shear strength** is calculated as:

$$\tau_i = \frac{F_{max}}{\pi d \, \ell_e}$$

where:
- $F_{max}$ = maximum force recorded during pull-out
- $d$ = fiber diameter
- $\ell_e$ = embedded fiber length

A plot of $\tau_i$ versus $\ell_e$ shows that for very short embedded lengths, $\tau_i$ is artificially high (because the entire interface must fail simultaneously). The **plateau value** of $\tau_i$ at moderate $\ell_e$ (typically $50\text{–}100\,\ell_c$) represents the true interfacial shear strength.

---

### Question B [Alternative for Internal Choice, KTU 2024 Scheme]

**Q:** (a) Define **wettability**. Derive the **Young–Dupré equation** from first principles and explain its significance in the design of fiber-reinforced composites. **(7 marks)**

**(b) Discuss the role of **coupling agents** in polymer matrix composites. With a chemical equation, show how a **silane coupling agent** bonds a glass fiber to an epoxy matrix. Compare the IFSS values of untreated and silane-treated glass fiber–epoxy systems. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question B — Part (a)

**Definition of Wettability:**

Wettability is the ability of a liquid (such as molten matrix resin or solution) to spread on and adhere to a solid surface (the reinforcement). It is quantified by the **contact angle** $\theta$, measured through the liquid at the three-phase (solid–liquid–vapor) contact line.

**Derivation of the Young–Dupré Equation:**

**Step 1 — Dupré Equation (Energy Definition):**

The work of adhesion $W_{ad}$ is the reversible energy required per unit area to separate the solid–liquid interface into two independent solid–vapor and liquid–vapor surfaces:

$$W_{ad} = \gamma_{SV} + \gamma_{LV} - \gamma_{SL}$$

**Step 2 — Young's Equation (Mechanical Force Balance):**

At equilibrium, the horizontal components of surface tension at the triple line must balance:

$$\gamma_{SV} = \gamma_{SL} + \gamma_{LV}\cos\theta$$

Rearranging:

$$\gamma_{SL} = \gamma_{SV} - \gamma_{LV}\cos\theta$$

**Step 3 — Substitution:**

Substituting into the Dupré equation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{ad} &= \gamma_{SV} + \gamma_{LV} - (\gamma_{SV} - \gamma_{LV}\cos\theta) \\
W_{ad} &= \gamma_{LV}(1 + \cos\theta)
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the **Young–Dupré equation**.

**Significance in Composite Design:**

1. **Lower contact angle → higher work of adhesion → stronger interface.**
2. The equation allows designers to predict interface strength from easily measured contact angle and liquid surface tension.
3. For molten thermoplastics on fibers, $\theta < 90°$ is required; ideally $\theta \rightarrow 0°$.
4. Surface treatments (plasma, sizing, chemical etching) are designed to reduce $\theta$ by modifying $\gamma_{SV}$.
5. For metal matrix composites, the molten metal must wet the ceramic reinforcement; otherwise, the composite contains voids and weak interfaces.

---

### Model Solution for Question B — Part (b)

**Role of Coupling Agents:**

Coupling agents are **bifunctional molecules** that bridge the reinforcement and the matrix by chemically bonding to both phases. They serve three primary functions:

1. **Promote chemical bonding** between otherwise non-reactive phases.
2. **Improve wettability** by reducing the contact angle of the molten resin on the fiber surface.
3. **Form a flexible interphase** that can absorb stress and prevent crack propagation, thereby improving toughness.

**Mechanism of Silane Coupling on Glass Fiber–Epoxy:**

A typical silane coupling agent is **3-aminopropyltriethoxysilane (APTES)** with structure $(C_2H_5O)_3-Si-(CH_2)_3-NH_2$.

**Step 1 — Hydrolysis of Ethoxy Groups:**

The silane first hydrolyzes in the presence of moisture to form silanol (Si–OH) groups:

$$(C_2H_5O)_3Si-(CH_2)_3-NH_2 + 3H_2O \rightarrow (HO)_3Si-(CH_2)_3-NH_2 + 3C_2H_5OH$$

**Step 2 — Condensation with Glass Surface:**

The silanol groups react with the hydroxyl groups on the glass fiber surface to form covalent Si–O–Si bonds:

$$Si-Fiber-OH + HO-Si-(CH_2)_3-NH_2 \rightarrow Si-Fiber-O-Si-(CH_2)_3-NH_2 + H_2O$$

**Step 3 — Reaction with Epoxy Matrix:**

The terminal amine group ($-NH_2$) reacts with the epoxy ring to form a covalent C–N bond:

$$-NH_2 + \underset{\text{epoxy}}{CH_2-CH(O)-CH_2-} \rightarrow -NH-CH_2-CH(OH)-CH_2-$$

**Comparison of IFSS Values:**

| System | IFSS (MPa) | Failure Mode |
|---|---|---|
| Untreated Glass Fiber / Epoxy | $15\text{–}25$ | Adhesive failure at interface |
| Silane-Treated Glass Fiber / Epoxy | $50\text{–}80$ | Cohesive failure in matrix |
| Improvement Factor | $\sim 2.5\times$ to $4\times$ | Shift from adhesive to cohesive failure |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. Students often write the wrong direction for the silane condensation — water is released, not consumed.
> 2. Many students forget the **hydrolysis step** that must precede condensation; APTES cannot directly condense with the fiber.
> 3. The amine-epoxy reaction is a **ring-opening nucleophilic addition**, not a simple substitution.
> 4. Confusing **adhesive failure** (interface separates) with **cohesive failure** (matrix breaks) costs marks in IFSS comparison questions.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] **Interface** is a 2D boundary; **Interphase** is a 3D region of finite thickness — never use these terms interchangeably.
- [x] The **three primary bonding types** are mechanical, physical (van der Waals), and chemical (chemisorption); electrostatic and interdiffusion are secondary but important.
- [x] **Wettability criterion:** Contact angle $\theta < 90°$ for adequate bonding; $\theta \rightarrow 0°$ is ideal.
- [x] **Young–Dupré Equation:** $W_{ad} = \gamma_{LV}(1+\cos\theta)$ — derived by substituting Young's equation into the Dupré energy balance.
- [x] **Critical Fiber Length:** $\ell_c = \dfrac{\sigma_{fu}\, d}{2\tau_i}$ — factor of 2 in denominator is mandatory.
- [x] **Critical Aspect Ratio:** $\dfrac{\ell_c}{d} = \dfrac{\sigma_{fu}}{2\tau_i}$ — design rule: actual $\ell/d \geq 10 \cdot (\ell_c/d)$.
- [x] **Coupling agents** (e.g., APTES silanes) form covalent bridges between reinforcement and matrix via a three-step mechanism: hydrolysis → condensation → matrix reaction.
- [x] **IFSS testing:** Single-fiber pull-out gives $\tau_i = F_{max}/(\pi d \ell_e)$; plateau value at moderate $\ell_e$ is the true IFSS.
- [x] **Engineering Trade-off:** Too-strong interface → brittle failure; too-weak → inefficient load transfer. Optimal interface balances strength and toughness.
- [x] **Hamaker constant** $H \sim 10^{-19}\text{–}10^{-20} \ \mathrm{J}$ characterizes van der Waals adhesion across interfaces.
- [x] **Real-world use cases:** Silane-treated glass/epoxy in wind turbines; plasma-treated carbon/epoxy in aerospace; deformed rebars in concrete; silane SAMs in microelectronics.
- [x] **Memorize the four-force Young equation:** $\gamma_{SV} = \gamma_{SL} + \gamma_{LV}\cos\theta$ — a guaranteed question in every KTU board exam.
- [x] **Coupling agent mechanism has three steps:** (1) hydrolysis of alkoxy groups, (2) condensation with fiber surface, (3) covalent bonding to matrix.
- [x] **Hamaker force** scales as $F \propto r^{-3}$ for sphere-flat geometry — short-range but important at nanoscale interfaces.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Thermoset
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUTUT412 - COMPOSITE MATERIALS AND CERAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Thermoset

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **thermoset** (or thermosetting plastic / thermosetting polymer) is a polymeric material that undergoes an irreversible, chemically cross-linked curing reaction upon the application of heat, pressure, or a chemical catalyst (hardener), forming a permanent three-dimensional (3D) covalently bonded network structure. Once cured, a thermoset cannot be remelted, reshaped, or reprocessed by the application of heat — a property fundamentally arising from the **permanent cross-links** that form between the polymer chains.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Standard):**
> *"A thermoset is a polymer resin that, once cured by a chemical reaction (polymerization and/or cross-linking) into a rigid, infusible solid, cannot be re-melted or re-formed. The cross-linked 3D network imparts high mechanical strength, thermal stability, and chemical resistance, making thermosets the dominant matrix material in fibre-reinforced polymer (FRP) composites."*

### 1.2 Key Terminology for KTU Exams

| Term | Meaning | KTU Importance |
|---|---|---|
| **Monomer** | Small reactive molecule that forms the building block | High |
| **Oligomer** | Short chain of repeating units (pre-polymer) | High |
| **Cross-link** | A covalent bond linking two or more polymer chains | Critical |
| **Cure** | The chemical process of converting a liquid resin to a rigid solid | Critical |
| **Degree of Cure (α)** | Fraction of reactive groups that have reacted (0 to 1) | High |
| **Gel Point** | Stage at which the first infinite molecular network forms | High |
| **Vitrification** | Transition from rubbery to glassy state during cure | Medium |
| **Exotherm** | Heat released during the exothermic cure reaction | Medium |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Boiled Egg" Model

Imagine a **thermoplastic** as a bowl of **spaghetti** — long, slippery noodles sliding past each other. You can heat it, reshape it, and cool it down as many times as you wish.

Now imagine a **thermoset** as a **bowl of boiled rice** that has been left to set, or more vividly, a **3D mesh of cured egg white**. When you heat the liquid egg (albumen) it permanently solidifies into a rubbery, then rigid, network. Even if you re-heat it, it will *burn or char* — it will never flow back into a liquid. The proteins in the egg white have formed **permanent cross-links** (disulfide bridges, hydrophobic interactions) that lock every chain in place.

> [!NOTE]
> **Memory Hook for Exams:** *"Thermoplastics = Spaghetti (reversible). Thermosets = Boiled Egg (irreversible)."*

### 1.4 Why Thermosets Dominate Composite Matrices

In fibre-reinforced polymer composites (FRPs), the matrix must:
1. **Wet** the fibres efficiently (low initial viscosity).
2. **Transfer load** to the high-strength fibres via shear at the interface.
3. **Resist** elevated temperatures, moisture, and solvents.
4. **Maintain dimensional stability** under sustained loads.

Thermosets fulfil all four because:
- They start as **low-viscosity liquids** (ideal for fibre impregnation).
- They form a **rigid 3D network** (excellent load transfer).
- Cross-linking gives **superior thermal and chemical resistance** compared to thermoplastics.
- The network prevents **creep** under sustained loading.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Metric for Reference:** A fully cured epoxy thermoset has a **cross-link density** of approximately $\rho_x \approx 1 \times 10^{-3}$ to $1 \times 10^{-2}$ mol/cm³, yielding a glass transition temperature ($T_g$) in the range of **80 °C to 250 °C** depending on the hardener system.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Chemistry of Cross-Linking — A Step-by-Step Logic Flow

The transformation of a liquid thermoset resin into a rigid 3D network occurs in **three kinetic stages**:

1. **Stage I — Molecular Growth (Pre-Gel):**
   - Linear or branched prepolymer chains form.
   - The system remains soluble and fusible (analogous to a viscous syrup).
   - Molecular weight increases steadily, but no infinite network yet exists.

2. **Stage II — Gelation (Network Onset):**
   - At the **gel point**, the first molecule of infinite molecular weight appears (the "gel molecule").
   - Beyond this point, the system becomes insoluble and infusible.
   - Viscosity rises **catastrophically** (effectively approaching infinity on the macroscopic scale).

3. **Stage III — Vitrification & Final Cure (Post-Gel):**
   - The cross-link density continues to rise.
   - The glass transition temperature $T_g$ of the network climbs past the cure temperature $T_{cure}$.
   - The reaction becomes **diffusion-controlled** and slows dramatically.
   - A fully cured thermoset is obtained when $T_g$ reaches its maximum equilibrium value $T_{g,\infty}$.

### 2.2 The "Why" Behind the 3D Network

> [!NOTE]
> **The Core Question Examined:** *Why does cross-linking make a polymer strong and thermally stable?*

**The Why:**
- In a linear thermoplastic, chains are held together only by weak **van der Waals** forces and chain entanglements. Heat provides enough energy to overcome these weak forces → flow.
- In a thermoset, **covalent bonds** tie every chain to its neighbours in a 3D web. To melt a thermoset, you would have to **break primary chemical bonds** (∼ 350 kJ/mol for C–C), which requires temperatures so high that the polymer **degrades (chars)** before it flows.

**The How:**
- Cross-linking is initiated by heat, catalysts, or hardeners that open reactive sites (epoxide rings, C=C double bonds, etc.).
- These reactive sites form **covalent bridges** (C–C, C–O–C, etc.) between chains.
- Network architecture: **highly cross-linked** (e.g., phenolic) → rigid, brittle, high $T_g$; **lightly cross-linked** (e.g., elastomeric polyurethane) → flexible, rubbery.

### 2.3 Major Families of Thermosetting Resins (KTU High-Yield Classification)

| Resin Family | Reactive Group | Curing Mechanism | Typical $T_g$ (°C) | Principal Composite Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Epoxy** | Epoxide (oxirane) ring | Addition cure with amine / anhydride hardener | 80 – 250 | Aerospace, wind blades |
| **Polyester (Unsaturated)** | C=C in styrene + polyester | Free-radical addition (peroxide initiator) | 70 – 110 | Marine, automotive |
| **Vinyl Ester** | Epoxy backbone + acrylate C=C | Free-radical addition | 100 – 150 | Chemical tanks, pipes |
| **Phenolic** | Methylol groups (–CH₂OH) | Condensation (releases water) | 200 – 300 | Fire-resistant panels, brakes |
| **Polyurethane** | Isocyanate (–N=C=O) | Addition with polyol | – 40 to +90 | Foams, elastomers |
| **Bismaleimide (BMI)** | Maleimide C=C | Thermal addition (high $T$) | 230 – 300 | High-temp aerospace |
| **Cyanate Ester** | –O–C≡N | Thermal cyclotrimerization | 250 – 350 | Radomes, electronics |

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All equations below are exam-critical. Memorize the symbol, the meaning, and the unit.**

**Symbol Glossary:**

$$\begin{aligned}
T_g &: \text{Glass transition temperature (K or °C)} \\
E &: \text{Young's modulus of cured thermoset (GPa)} \\
\rho_x &: \text{Cross-link density (mol/m³ or mol/cm³)} \\
M_c &: \text{Molecular weight between cross-links (g/mol)} \\
\alpha &: \text{Degree of cure (dimensionless, 0 → 1)} \\
T_{g,\infty} &: \text{Maximum achievable glass transition (K)} \\
T_{g,0} &: \text{Glass transition of uncured resin (K)} \\
\nu_e &: \text{Number of elastically active network chains per unit volume} \\
R &: \text{Universal gas constant} = 8.314 \; \text{J/(mol·K)} \\
\lambda &: \text{Wavelength of infrared spectroscopy peak (for FTIR cure monitoring)}
\end{aligned}$$

| # | Formula | Engineering Meaning | KTU Marks Frequency |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $T_g = T_{g,0} + \dfrac{\lambda \cdot \alpha}{1 - (1-\lambda)\alpha}$ | **DiBenedetto equation** — relates $T_g$ to degree of cure $\alpha$ | ★★★★★ |
| 2 | $E = 3 \nu_e R T$ | **Rubber elasticity theory** — modulus of lightly cross-linked thermoset | ★★★★ |
| 3 | $\rho_x = \dfrac{1}{M_c}$ | Definition of cross-link density via $M_c$ | ★★★★★ |
| 4 | $\nu_e = \dfrac{\rho}{M_c}$ | Network chain density (per m³) | ★★★★ |
| 5 | $\dfrac{d\alpha}{dt} = k(T)(1-\alpha)^n$ | Cure kinetics — nth order rate equation | ★★★★ |
| 6 | $k(T) = A \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{E_a}{RT}\right)$ | Arrhenius temperature dependence of rate | ★★★★★ |
| 7 | $\alpha_{gel} = \dfrac{2}{f}$ | Flory-Stockmayer gel point (functionality $f$) | ★★★★ |
| 8 | $\tau = \tau_0 \exp\!\left(\dfrac{U}{RT}\right)$ | Creep/relaxation time (WLF context) | ★★★ |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Marking Tip:** In Part A (3-mark) questions, examiners *love* asking: *"Define cross-link density and write its relationship with molecular weight between cross-links."* The expected answer is exactly Equation 3: $\rho_x = \dfrac{1}{M_c}$.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Thermoset-based composites are foundational in:
- **Aerospace:** Boeing 787 Dreamliner's composite airframe uses **BMI and epoxy** matrices for service up to 180 °C.
- **Wind energy:** Vestas and GE blade root joints use **epoxy** for fatigue resistance.
- **Automotive:** Sheet moulding compound (SMC) with **polyester/glass** reduces car body weight by 30 %.
- **Marine:** Hulls of mine-countermeasure vessels use **vinyl ester** for osmosis resistance.
- **Electronics:** **Cyanate ester** PCBs maintain $D_k$ (dielectric constant) stable up to 200 °C.
- **Fire safety:** **Phenolic** matrices in train interiors release minimal smoke and toxic gases (low heat release rate < 65 kW/m² per EN 45545).

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation I — Cross-Link Density from Swelling Data (Flory-Rehner Equation)

A fully cured thermoset is **insoluble** but will **swell** when immersed in a good solvent. By measuring the equilibrium swelling ratio, we can determine the cross-link density $M_c$ using the **Flory-Rehner** equation.

**Starting Assumptions (Stated Explicitly):**
- The network is isotropic and ideal (no chain entanglements treated as cross-links).
- The polymer is in the rubbery state (i.e., $T > T_g$).
- The Flory χ-parameter characterizes polymer–solvent interaction.

**Step-by-Step Derivation:**

The total free energy change on mixing an already cross-linked network with a solvent is the sum of (i) the **free energy of mixing** $\Delta G_m$ and (ii) the **elastic free energy of the network** $\Delta G_{el}$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\Delta G_{total} = \Delta G_m + \Delta G_{el}
\end{aligned}$$

The **Flory-Huggins** expression for $\Delta G_m$ per unit volume:

$$\begin{aligned}
\Delta G_m = RT \left[ n_1 \ln \phi_1 + n_2 \ln \phi_2 + \chi n_1 \phi_2 \right]
\end{aligned}$$

where $\phi_1$ is the solvent volume fraction, $\phi_2$ is the polymer volume fraction, and $\chi$ is the Flory interaction parameter.

The **elastic free energy** stored in a deformed network (Gaussian chain statistics, affine deformation):

$$\begin{aligned}
\Delta G_{el} = \dfrac{1}{2} \nu_e k_B T \left( \lambda_x^2 + \lambda_y^2 + \lambda_z^2 - 3 \right)
\end{aligned}$$

For isotropic swelling, the linear extension ratio at equilibrium is $\lambda = \phi_2^{-1/3}$.

At equilibrium, the chemical potential of the solvent inside the gel equals that of the pure solvent outside. Setting $\partial \Delta G_{total} / \partial n_1 = 0$ and substituting the isotropic $\lambda$, after several algebraic manipulations (assumed standard for the Flory–Rehner derivation), the equilibrium condition reduces to:

$$\begin{aligned}
\ln(1 - \phi_2) + \phi_2 + \chi \phi_2^2 = -\dfrac{\rho V_1}{M_c} \left( \phi_2^{1/3} - \dfrac{\phi_2}{2} \right)
\end{aligned}$$

Solving explicitly for $M_c$ (the molecular weight between cross-links), we obtain the canonical **Flory-Rehner equation**:

$$\begin{aligned}
\boxed{\;\dfrac{1}{M_c} = \dfrac{\dfrac{\ln(1-\phi_2) + \phi_2 + \chi \phi_2^2}{V_1 \left(\phi_2^{1/3} - \dfrac{\phi_2}{2}\right)} = \rho_x\;}
\end{aligned}$$

where:
- $M_c$ = molecular weight between cross-links (g/mol)
- $\phi_2$ = polymer volume fraction at equilibrium swelling
- $\chi$ = Flory–Huggins polymer-solvent interaction parameter
- $V_1$ = molar volume of the solvent (cm³/mol)
- $\rho_x$ = cross-link density (mol/g)

**Conversion Logic (Textual Explanation):**
The left-hand side of the boxed equation represents the **mixing contribution** — it captures the thermodynamic drive of the solvent to dissolve the polymer. The right-hand side captures the **elastic resistance** of the network chains to being stretched. At equilibrium, these two forces balance. The measurement of $\phi_2$ (via weight gain) directly yields $M_c$, and hence $\rho_x$.

---

### 3.2 Derivation II — Modulus from Cross-Link Density (Rubber Elasticity Theory)

For a **lightly cross-linked** thermoset above its $T_g$ (rubbery state), the **Gaussian network theory** gives the shear modulus as:

$$\begin{aligned}
G = \nu_e R T
\end{aligned}$$

where $\nu_e$ is the number of elastically active network chains per unit volume (mol/m³).

The relationship between $\nu_e$ and the cross-link density $\rho_x$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\nu_e = \dfrac{\rho}{M_c} = \rho \cdot \rho_x
\end{aligned}$$

where $\rho$ is the polymer mass density (g/m³ or kg/m³).

**Deriving Young's Modulus $E$:**

For an isotropic, incompressible rubber, classical elasticity theory gives:

$$\begin{aligned}
E = 2G(1 + \nu_{Poisson})
\end{aligned}$$

For an incompressible material, Poisson's ratio $\nu_{Poisson} = 0.5$, so $(1 + \nu_{Poisson}) = 1.5$:

$$\begin{aligned}
E = 2G \cdot 1.5 = 3G
\end{aligned}$$

Substituting $G = \nu_e R T$:

$$\begin{aligned}
E = 3 \nu_e R T
\end{aligned}$$

Substituting $\nu_e = \rho / M_c$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\boxed{\;E = 3 \rho \cdot \dfrac{R T}{M_c} = 3 \rho \rho_x R T\;}
\end{aligned}$$

**Conversion Logic:**
- A higher cross-link density (smaller $M_c$) → stiffer network.
- A higher temperature $T$ (still above $T_g$) → more thermal agitation of chains → **stiffer rubber** (entropic elasticity).
- This counterintuitive result (rubber gets stiffer when hot) is a hallmark of entropic elasticity and is heavily tested in KTU viva voce.

---

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 3]**
**Define a thermosetting polymer. Give two examples of thermoset resins used as matrix materials in fibre-reinforced composites.**

**Model Answer:**

A **thermosetting polymer (thermoset)** is a polymeric material that undergoes an irreversible chemical reaction (curing) to form a permanent three-dimensional cross-linked network. Once cured, it cannot be remelted or reprocessed by the application of heat.

**Two examples:**
1. **Epoxy resin** — used in aerospace composites (carbon-fibre reinforced plastics, CFRP) for high mechanical strength and low shrinkage.
2. **Unsaturated polyester resin** — used in glass-fibre reinforced plastics (GRP) for marine hulls and automotive body panels due to low cost and easy processing.

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Insight:** Both examples must be resins actually used in composites. Naming "Bakelite" alone is insufficient — examiners expect a composite-application link.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 3]**
**What is meant by "cross-link density" of a thermoset? How is it related to the molecular weight between cross-links?**

**Model Answer:**

**Cross-link density ($\rho_x$)** is defined as the number of cross-links (or equivalently, the moles of elastically active network chains) per unit mass (or per unit volume) of the cured thermoset.

**Mathematical relationship:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\rho_x = \dfrac{1}{M_c}
\end{aligned}$$

where $M_c$ is the **molecular weight between cross-links**, expressed in g/mol. A higher $\rho_x$ (smaller $M_c$) means a tighter, more rigid network with a higher glass transition temperature $T_g$, greater brittleness, and improved chemical resistance.

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall Callout:** Students frequently confuse $\rho_x$ (mol/g) with $\nu_e$ (mol/m³). The conversion is $\nu_e = \rho \cdot \rho_x$, where $\rho$ is the density of the polymer in g/m³. Examiners deduct 1 mark for this distinction error.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Module 3]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the **three stages of curing** of a thermosetting resin. Discuss the molecular changes that occur in each stage.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Derive the **relationship between Young's modulus and cross-link density** for a lightly cross-linked rubbery thermoset. State clearly the assumptions made.

---

### Model Solution to Question A

#### (a) Three Stages of Curing

The curing of a thermosetting resin progresses through **three distinct kinetic stages** as the degree of cure $\alpha$ increases from 0 to 1:

**Stage 1 — Molecular Growth (Linear/Branched Pre-Gel):**
- Reactive groups (epoxy, amine, C=C, etc.) begin to react.
- Linear or branched prepolymer chains form; molecular weight increases steadily.
- The system remains **soluble, fusible, and processable** (low viscosity).
- Analogy: thin syrup that can still be poured.

**Stage 2 — Gelation (Network Onset):**
- At a critical conversion $\alpha_{gel}$, the first molecule of **infinite molecular weight** appears.
- The resin sets into a **rubbery, elastic gel** that is insoluble in solvents.
- Viscosity rises **catastrophically** because the entire mass is now one connected network.
- The Flory–Stockmayer criterion gives $\alpha_{gel} = 2/f$ for a system of functionality $f$.

**Stage 3 — Vitrification and Final Cure:**
- After gelation, cross-linking continues, raising the network density.
- The glass transition temperature $T_g$ climbs (per the DiBenedetto equation) and may exceed the cure temperature $T_{cure}$.
- The reaction becomes **diffusion-controlled**; the resin vitrifies into a glassy rigid solid.
- The reaction is essentially complete when $T_g \to T_{g,\infty}$.

**Schematic representation:**

```
Liquid Resin     ->    Gel Point      ->    Rubbery Gel    ->    Glassy Solid
(Stage 1, pre-gel)        (gelation)    (Stage 2)         (Stage 3, vitrification)
α = 0 to αgel         α = αgel          α = αgel to αcrit   α > αcrit
```

#### (b) Derivation of Young's Modulus vs. Cross-Link Density

**Assumptions Stated Explicitly:**
1. The thermoset is **lightly cross-linked** (Gaussian network).
2. The test temperature $T$ is **above $T_g$** (rubbery regime).
3. The material is **incompressible** (Poisson's ratio $\nu = 0.5$).
4. Affine network deformation: junctions move affinely with macroscopic strain.
5. Each chain behaves as a Gaussian coil (no excluded volume).

**Derivation Steps:**

**Step 1 — Start with the shear modulus of a Gaussian network:**

$$\begin{aligned}
G = \nu_e R T
\end{aligned}$$

where $\nu_e$ = number of network chains per unit volume (mol/m³), $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K), $T$ = absolute temperature.

**Step 2 — Relate $\nu_e$ to the cross-link density $\rho_x$ (mol/g):**

$$\begin{aligned}
\nu_e = \rho \cdot \rho_x = \dfrac{\rho}{M_c}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the classical elasticity relation for isotropic material:**

$$\begin{aligned}
E = 2G(1 + \nu)
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Use incompressibility ($\nu = 0.5$):**

$$\begin{aligned}
E = 2G(1 + 0.5) = 3G
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute $G$ and $\nu_e$:**

$$\begin{aligned}
E = 3 \nu_e R T = 3 \left(\dfrac{\rho}{M_c}\right) R T = 3 \rho \rho_x R T
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\boxed{\;E = 3 \rho \rho_x R T\;}
\end{aligned}$$

**Engineering Interpretation:** A thermoset with a higher cross-link density (tighter network, smaller $M_c$) is **stiffer** in its rubbery state. Increasing temperature $T$ increases $E$ — this is the **entropic elasticity** phenomenon, opposite to metals, and a classic KTU viva question.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2023, Module 3]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Compare the **structure, properties, and applications** of three major thermoset resins (epoxy, polyester, and phenolic) used as composite matrices.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the **DiBenedetto equation** relating glass transition temperature $T_g$ to the degree of cure $\alpha$. Sketch the typical $T_g$ vs. $\alpha$ curve and identify the gel point and vitrification point on it.

---

### Model Solution to Question B

#### (a) Comparison of Epoxy, Polyester, and Phenolic Resins

| Property | Epoxy | Unsaturated Polyester | Phenolic |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Chemistry** | Epoxide ring + amine hardener | C=C in polyester backbone, dissolved in styrene | Phenol + formaldehyde (condensation) |
| **Cure type** | Addition (no by-products) | Free-radical addition | Condensation (releases water) |
| **Cure shrinkage** | Very low (0.5 – 2 %) | High (4 – 8 %) | Moderate (1 – 2 %) |
| **Tensile strength (MPa)** | 60 – 90 | 40 – 70 | 30 – 50 |
| **$T_g$ (°C)** | 120 – 180 | 80 – 110 | 250 – 300 |
| **Chemical resistance** | Excellent | Good (poor to alkalis) | Excellent |
| **Fibre wetting** | Excellent | Good | Moderate |
| **Cost** | High | Low | Moderate |
| **Toxicity** | Hardener amines toxic | Styrene emission concerns | Formaldehyde emission |
| **Applications** | Aerospace CFRP, wind blades, electronics | Marine GRP, automotive SMC, bathtubs | Brake linings, ablative nose cones, fire-resistant panels |
| **KTU exam frequency** | ★★★★★ | ★★★★★ | ★★★★ |

#### (b) The DiBenedetto Equation

The **DiBenedetto equation** is the standard thermodynamic model for how the glass transition temperature $T_g$ of a thermosetting network evolves with the degree of cure $\alpha$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\boxed{\;\dfrac{T_g - T_{g,0}}{T_{g,\infty} - T_{g,0}} = \dfrac{\lambda \alpha}{1 - (1-\lambda)\alpha}\;}
\end{aligned}$$

where:
- $T_{g,0}$ = glass transition of the fully uncured resin (α = 0)
- $T_{g,\infty}$ = glass transition of the fully cured network (α = 1)
- $\lambda$ = a material constant (typically 0.3 – 0.6) representing the ratio of segmental mobilities in the cured vs. uncured states
- $\alpha$ = degree of cure (0 to 1)

**Key Behaviour:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\alpha = 0 \;&\Longrightarrow\; T_g = T_{g,0} \quad \text{(liquid resin)} \\
\alpha = 1 \;&\Longrightarrow\; T_g = T_{g,\infty} \quad \text{(fully cured network)} \\
0 < \alpha < 1 \;&\Longrightarrow\; T_g \text{ rises non-linearly with } \alpha
\end{aligned}$$

**Sketch of the Curve:**

```
Tg
 |
 |                                          . . . . . . Tg_infinity (asymptote)
 |                                    . . .
 |                                . .
 |                            . .           <-- VITRIFICATION (Tg = Tcure)
 |                        . .
 |                   . .          <-- GEL POINT
 |              . .
 |          . .    <-- STAGE 1: Molecular growth
 |      . .
 |   . .
 |  .
 |__________________________________________________ alpha
 0          αgel         0.5           0.9         1.0
              ^                       ^
              |                       |
         GEL POINT              VITRIFICATION
   (first infinite          (Tg crosses Tcure,
   network forms)          reaction becomes
                            diffusion-controlled)
```

**Engineering Significance:**
- The DiBenedetto equation tells the **mould designer** when the part can be demoulded (typically when $T_g$ exceeds the demoulding temperature by ∼ 20 °C).
- It explains why **post-cure** at elevated temperature is needed to push $T_g$ to $T_{g,\infty}$ — without it, the part may be under-cured and dimensionally unstable.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks Lost on DiBenedetto Questions:**
> 1. Forgetting to define the constant $\lambda$ — examiners allocate 1 mark for this.
> 2. Drawing the $T_g$ vs. $\alpha$ curve as linear (it is **not** — it is sigmoidal / hyperbolic).
> 3. Confusing $T_{g,0}$ (uncured) with the cure temperature $T_{cure}$ — they are unrelated quantities.
> 4. Failing to label the gel point and vitrification point on the sketch — 1 mark is reserved for this.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Thermoset Module**

**🔹 Core Concepts (Must-Know for Part A):**
- Thermoset = permanently cross-linked polymer; **cannot be remelted**.
- Cross-link density $\rho_x = 1 / M_c$ (units: mol/g).
- Three stages of cure: **molecular growth → gelation → vitrification**.
- Gel point: $\alpha_{gel} = 2 / f$ (Flory-Stockmayer).
- $T_g$ rises with $\alpha$ per DiBenedetto equation.
- 4 major thermoset families: **epoxy, polyester, vinyl ester, phenolic**.

**🔹 Numerical Knobs (Must-Know for Part B):**
- Flory-Rehner equation: relates $\rho_x$ to swelling ratio $\phi_2$ and $\chi$.
- Rubber elasticity: $E = 3 \rho \rho_x R T$ (valid only in rubbery state, i.e., $T > T_g$).
- Cure kinetics: $d\alpha/dt = A \exp(-E_a/RT)(1-\alpha)^n$ — use Arrhenius for temperature dependence.
- Higher functionality $f$ → earlier gelation (lower $\alpha_{gel}$).

**🔹 Common Pitfalls (Where Marks Are Lost):**
- Calling a thermoset "stronger" than a thermoplastic without specifying matrix/reinforcement.
- Confusing $\rho_x$ (mol/g) with $\nu_e$ (mol/m³).
- Writing DiBenedetto as a linear relationship.
- Forgetting that thermosets **decompose**, not melt, on heating.
- Ignoring the role of the hardener in defining the final $T_g$ of epoxy.

**🔹 Examiner's One-Liner Hot Phrases:**
- *"Once cured, a thermoset cannot be reprocessed — this is a consequence of the permanent 3D covalent network."*
- *"Cross-link density controls stiffness, $T_g$, and chemical resistance simultaneously."*
- *"The gel point is the in-situ rheological signature of network formation."*

**🔹 Industrial Relevance Tag (For Higher-Marks Answers):**
- Always mention at least one **real composite application** (e.g., wind turbine blade, Boeing 787 fuselage, marine hull).
- Use quantitative $T_g$ ranges wherever possible (e.g., "epoxy cured with TETA: $T_g \approx 120$ °C").

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Thermoplastic and Elastomeric polymers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT412 - COMPOSITE MATERIALS AND CERAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Thermoplastic and Elastomeric polymers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 3: THERMOPLASTIC AND ELASTOMERIC POLYMERS

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is a Polymer?

A **polymer** is a large macromolecule composed of many repeating structural subunits called **monomers**, joined together by covalent **chemical bonds** through a process known as **polymerization**. The term originates from the Greek words *poly* (many) and *meros* (parts). In the context of **composite materials**, polymers serve as the **matrix phase** that binds reinforcement (fibers/particles) together, transferring loads and protecting them from environmental degradation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** A polymer is a high molecular weight compound formed by the linkage of a large number of smaller molecules (monomers). The repeating unit is called a **mer**, and the number of such units is the **degree of polymerization (n)**.

### 1.2 Classification of Polymers (KTU Framework)

For the KTU 2024 scheme, polymers are classified along two key axes:

**A. Based on Thermal Response (Behavior on Heating):**

| Category | Behavior on Heating | Chain Architecture | Examples |
|----------|---------------------|--------------------|----------|
| **Thermoplastics** | Soften, melt, flow — processable repeatedly | Linear / Branched chains, *weak secondary bonds* | PE, PP, PVC, PS, Nylon |
| **Thermosetting plastics** | Irreversibly harden, char on heating | 3D **cross-linked** network, *strong covalent bonds* | Epoxy, Phenolic, Melamine |
| **Elastomers** | Flexible, rubbery, large reversible deformation | Lightly cross-linked chains, *coiled/spiral* | Natural rubber, Neoprene, Silicone |

**B. Based on Mechanical Response (Deformation Behavior):**

- **Plastics:** Rigid, low elastic strain (<1–5 %)
- **Elastomers:** Highly elastic, large reversible strain (100–1000 %)
- **Fibers:** High tensile strength, low elongation
- **Foams:** Cellular, low density

### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy (Concept Visualization)

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The "Spaghetti Bowl" Picture**
>
> Imagine a bowl of cooked spaghetti. Each noodle is a **polymer chain**.
> - **Thermoplastic** = Loose, dry noodles — they slide past each other easily. Reheat the bowl and the noodles slip and slide. Cool it and they stiffen again. You can reheat and re-shape them any number of times.
> - **Elastomer** = Noodles tied loosely with a few rubber bands — they can stretch, but the rubber bands pull them back. The cross-links act as those rubber bands.
> - **Thermoset** = Noodles glued together at every contact point into a 3D mesh — once set, you can never reshape it by heating; it will just char.

### 1.4 Formal Definition of Thermoplastic Polymers

**Thermoplastic polymers** are linear or lightly branched chain polymers held together by **weak intermolecular secondary forces** (van der Waals, dipole–dipole, hydrogen bonding). On heating, these secondary forces weaken, allowing chains to slide past one another, producing a viscous flow. On cooling, they re-solidify without any permanent chemical change. Hence, they are **reprocessable and recyclable**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (verbatim use in exams):** *"Thermoplastics are polymers that soften on heating and harden on cooling in a reversible physical process, with no chemical change taking place. They consist of linear or branched chains bonded by secondary forces."*

### 1.5 Formal Definition of Elastomeric Polymers

**Elastomers** (elastic + polymer) are amorphous polymers that exhibit **large, fully reversible elastic deformation** (typically 100–1000 % strain) at room temperature, governed by **entropy-driven recoiling of coiled chains** between sparse chemical cross-links. The cross-links (e.g., sulfur vulcanization) prevent permanent viscous flow while allowing chain segments to uncoil under load.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (verbatim use in exams):** *"Elastomers are lightly cross-linked polymers having long, flexible, coiled chain segments which can undergo large elastic deformation. They are amorphous above their glass transition temperature (Tg), and the cross-links prevent chain slippage, ensuring elastic recovery."*

### 1.6 Key Physical Metrics to Remember

- **Degree of Polymerization (n):** Number of repeat units in a chain
- **Molecular Weight (M):** $M = n \times M_0$ where $M_0$ is the mer weight
- **Glass Transition Temperature (Tg):** Temperature below which polymer becomes rigid and glassy
- **Melting Temperature (Tm):** Applicable only to *semicrystalline* polymers
- **Young's Modulus (E):** Stiffness indicator
- **Cross-link Density:** Number of cross-links per unit volume (critical for elastomers)

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Structural Anatomy of Thermoplastics

### 2.1.1 Chain Architecture

A thermoplastic consists of:
1. **Backbone:** A long chain of covalently bonded carbon (or C–O, C–N) atoms
2. **Pendant groups:** Side groups (e.g., –CH₃ in PP, –Cl in PVC, –C₆H₅ in PS) that influence stiffness, polarity, and crystallinity
3. **Chain ends:** Terminal groups that limit molecular weight
4. **Inter-chain forces:** Weak secondary bonds that consolidate the bulk

### 2.1.2 Polymerization Mechanisms (KTU Module 3 Foundation)

**Step 1 — Initiation:** An initiator (free radical, cation, anion) opens the first monomer's π-bond.

**Step 2 — Propagation:** The active chain end repeatedly attacks fresh monomers, growing the chain.

**Step 3 — Termination:** Two active chains combine (coupling) or one abstracts a hydrogen (disproportionation), ending growth.

The result is a mixture of chains of different lengths — described by the **molecular weight distribution** (polydispersity index, PDI = Mw/Mn).

### 2.1.3 Crystallinity in Thermoplastics

Thermoplastics are never 100 % crystalline. They are **semicrystalline**, containing:
- **Crystalline regions** (ordered lamellae) — provide strength and density
- **Amorphous regions** (random coils) — provide toughness and transparency

| Polymer | % Crystallinity | Behavior |
|---------|----------------|----------|
| HDPE | 70–80 % | Tough, opaque |
| LDPE | 40–55 % | Soft, flexible |
| Nylon 6,6 | 35–45 % | Strong, fibers |
| PS (atactic) | 0 % | Glassy, transparent |

### 2.2 Structural Anatomy of Elastomers

### 2.2.1 The "Coiled Spring" Model

An elastomer chain in its **unstressed state** is a randomly coiled, high-entropy configuration. When stretched:
- The chain uncoils, **decreasing entropy**
- The cross-links pull it back when released, **increasing entropy**
- This **entropy elasticity** is the fundamental origin of rubbery behavior

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Elastomers are NOT Entropy-Spring + Viscosity:** Unlike a true viscous fluid, the sparse cross-links prevent chain slippage. Unlike a true solid, the weak secondary forces allow segmental motion. The result is *entropy-elastic* behavior governed by thermodynamics, not bond-stretching (which is *energy-elastic*, as in metals).

### 2.2.2 Vulcanization

Vulcanization is the process of forming **sulfur cross-links** between polyisoprene (natural rubber) chains. Invented by **Charles Goodyear (1839)**, it converts sticky, temperature-sensitive raw rubber into a useful, durable elastomer.

**Sulfur cross-link formation (simplified):**

$$
\text{2 R–SH} + \text{S} \xrightarrow{\text{heat, accelerator}} \text{R–S–S–R} + \text{H}_2\text{S}
$$

- **Light cross-linking** (1–2 % S) → soft, extensible rubber
- **Heavy cross-linking** (30–40 % S) → hard rubber (ebonite)

### 2.3 Glass Transition Temperature (Tg) — The Master Variable

Tg separates the **glassy** state (brittle, rigid, E ≈ 3 GPa) from the **rubbery** state (flexible, E ≈ 3 MPa) in amorphous polymers.

| Polymer Type | Tg (°C) | Tm (°C) | Behavior at 25 °C |
|--------------|---------|---------|---------------------|
| HDPE | –120 | 137 | Rubbery/Plastic |
| Natural rubber | –73 | 36 (isotactic) | Elastomer |
| PVC | +80 | 212 | Rigid plastic |
| Polystyrene | +100 | (amorphous) | Glassy |
| Nylon 6,6 | +57 | 265 | Tough plastic |
| Silicone rubber | –127 | – | Elastomer |

**Rule of Thumb (KTU High-Yield):** For an elastomer to be rubbery at room temperature (≈ 25 °C), its **Tg must be well below 25 °C** (typically < –40 °C).

### 2.4 Mechanical Behavior — Stress-Strain Philosophy

### 2.4.1 Thermoplastic Response

- **Below Tg:** Glassy, brittle fracture at low strain (1–2 %)
- **Above Tg (semicrystalline):** Yielding, cold drawing, necking, ductile failure
- **At Tm:** Liquid flow

### 2.4.2 Elastomer Response

A typical elastomer stress-strain curve shows:
1. **Low-modulus region** (initial uncoiling)
2. **Strain-induced crystallization** (sudden stiffening at high strain — e.g., natural rubber)
3. **No yield point** — purely elastic up to fracture

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table is examinable verbatim in Part A 3-mark and Part B derivations.**

| S.No. | Formula / Relation | Meaning | Units |
|:-----:|--------------------|---------|-------|
| 1 | $M = n \cdot M_0$ | Polymer molecular weight | g/mol |
| 2 | $\text{PDI} = \dfrac{M_w}{M_n}$ | Polydispersity Index | dimensionless |
| 3 | $T_g(K) = \dfrac{T_g(°C) + 273}{}$ | Unit conversion | K |
| 4 | $\sigma = E \cdot \varepsilon$ | Hooke's Law (small strain) | Pa |
| 5 | $\tau = \eta \cdot \dot{\gamma}$ | Newton's Law of viscosity | Pa·s |
| 6 | $f = \dfrac{E'}{E}$ | Loss factor (damping) | dimensionless |
| 7 | $n = \dfrac{N_A \cdot m}{M_0}$ | Number of mers in a sample | dimensionless |
| 8 | $\rho_c = \dfrac{v_{cross}}{V_{total}}$ | Cross-link density | mol/m³ |
| 9 | $T_g \approx \dfrac{2}{3} T_m$ (K) | Beaman-Boyer rule (approx.) | K |
| 10 | $\eta_0 = A \cdot e^{E_a / RT}$ | Arrhenius viscosity of melt | Pa·s |

**Note on equation 9:** The Beaman-Boyer empirical rule is a quick KT paper-pen test relation valid for symmetric, flexible-chain polymers.

### 2.6 Why This Matters in Engineering

- **Thermoplastics** dominate the **matrix phase in fiber-reinforced composites** (e.g., polypropylene + glass fiber in automotive dashboards) because they can be melt-processed.
- **Elastomers** are used as **toughening agents** in rigid polymer matrices to absorb impact energy (e.g., rubber-toughened polystyrene, HIPS).
- **Tg** dictates the **upper service temperature** of a polymer composite.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Molecular Weight from Degree of Polymerization

**Problem Setup:** A polyethylene sample has 5,000 repeat units. Given the molecular weight of ethylene monomer (CH₂=CH₂) is 28 g/mol, calculate the polymer molecular weight.

**Step 1 — Identify the parameters:**

$$
M_0 (\text{ethylene}) = 2(12) + 4(1) = 28 \text{ g/mol}
$$

$$
n = 5000 \text{ (degree of polymerization)}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the master relation $M = n \cdot M_0$:**

$$
M = 5000 \times 28 \text{ g/mol}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute:**

$$
M = 140{,}000 \text{ g/mol} = 1.4 \times 10^5 \text{ g/mol}
$$

**Result:** $M = \mathbf{1.4 \times 10^5 \text{ g/mol}}$, which classifies the polymer as a medium-to-high molecular weight polyethylene (typical for structural applications).

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Number of Mers in a Sample

**Problem Setup:** 100 g of PVC (repeat unit = –CH₂–CHCl–, $M_0 = 62.5$ g/mol). Find the number of mers (n).

**Step 1 — Apply the relation $n = \dfrac{N_A \cdot m}{M_0}$:**

$$
n = \dfrac{(6.022 \times 10^{23}) \times 100}{62.5}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the denominator and numerator:**

$$
\text{Numerator} = 6.022 \times 10^{25}
$$

$$
\text{Denominator} = 62.5
$$

**Step 3 — Final evaluation:**

$$
n = \dfrac{6.022 \times 10^{25}}{6.25 \times 10^{1}} = 9.635 \times 10^{23} \text{ mers}
$$

**Result:** $n \approx \mathbf{9.6 \times 10^{23} \text{ mers}}$ — illustrating the enormous molecular scale of a polymer sample.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Glass Transition Temperature — Free Volume Concept

**Theory:** As a polymer is cooled from the melt, the free volume (microvoids) shrinks. At Tg, the free volume reaches a critical value $f_g \approx 0.025$, below which segmental motion is frozen.

**Step 1 — Free volume at any temperature:**

$$
f(T) = f_g + \alpha_f (T - T_g)
$$

where $\alpha_f$ is the thermal expansion coefficient of free volume.

**Step 2 — Setting T = Tg:**

$$
f(T_g) = f_g = 0.025
$$

**Step 3 — For a polymer heated from Tg to T, the fractional free volume change is:**

$$
\Delta f = \alpha_f (T - T_g)
$$

**Step 4 — Practical implication for elastomer design:**

To keep an elastomer flexible at service temperature $T_{svc} = 298$ K, we need $T_g \ll 298$ K. The difference $(T_{svc} - T_g)$ sets the available free volume for chain mobility.

**Example Check (Natural Rubber):** $T_g = –73 °C = 200$ K, $T_{svc} = 298$ K.

$$
\Delta f \propto (298 - 200) = 98 \text{ K} \text{ of free-volume "budget"}
$$

This large free-volume budget is why natural rubber is so flexible at room temperature.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Cross-link Density from Swelling (Elastomer Analysis)

**Problem Setup:** An elastomer sample of mass 1 g is immersed in a solvent. The swollen mass is 4 g. The polymer-solvent interaction parameter $\chi = 0.4$, polymer density $\rho_p = 1.1$ g/cm³, solvent molar volume $V_1 = 80$ cm³/mol. Find the cross-link density $\rho_c$.

**Step 1 — Flory-Rehner equation (equilibrium swelling):**

$$
\rho_c = \dfrac{-\left[ \ln(1 - v_{2s}) + v_{2s} + \chi v_{2s}^2 \right]}{V_1 \left[ v_{2s}^{1/3} - \dfrac{v_{2s}}{2} \right]}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute volume fraction of polymer in swollen gel, $v_{2s}$:**

$$
m_{\text{swollen}} = 4 \text{ g}, \quad m_{\text{polymer}} = 1 \text{ g}
$$

Assuming volumes are additive (1 g solvent ≈ 1 cm³):

$$
v_{2s} = \dfrac{1/1.1}{(1/1.1) + 3/1.0} = \dfrac{0.909}{0.909 + 3.0} = 0.233
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute into the numerator:**

$$
\ln(1 - 0.233) = \ln(0.767) = -0.265
$$

$$
\text{Numerator} = -\left[-0.265 + 0.233 + 0.4 \times (0.233)^2\right]
$$

$$
= -\left[-0.265 + 0.233 + 0.0217\right] = -\left[-0.0103\right] = 0.0103
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute into the denominator:**

$$
v_{2s}^{1/3} = (0.233)^{1/3} = 0.616
$$

$$
v_{2s}/2 = 0.116
$$

$$
\text{Denominator} = 80 \times (0.616 - 0.116) = 80 \times 0.500 = 40 \text{ cm}^3/\text{mol}
$$

**Step 5 — Final cross-link density:**

$$
\rho_c = \dfrac{0.0103}{40} = 2.58 \times 10^{-4} \text{ mol/cm}^3
$$

**Result:** $\rho_c \approx \mathbf{2.6 \times 10^{-4} \text{ mol/cm}^3}$ — a typical lightly cross-linked elastomer.

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5: Rubber Elasticity — Stress in a Cross-linked Elastomer

**Theory (Gaussian chain statistics):** The elastic stress in a rubbery network is **entropic**, not energetic.

**Step 1 — Entropic force on a single Gaussian chain:**

The end-to-end distance distribution is Gaussian. The entropic free energy is:

$$
F_{\text{chain}} = -T \cdot S = kT \left( \dfrac{3r^2}{2N b^2} \right) + \text{const}
$$

where $N$ is the number of segments, $b$ is the segment length, $r$ is the end-to-end distance.

**Step 2 — Force to stretch the chain (one dimension):**

$$
f = \dfrac{\partial F}{\partial r} = \dfrac{3kT \cdot r}{N b^2}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert to macroscopic network stress (per unit area, A):**

The number of network chains per unit volume is $\nu$ (related to cross-link density $\rho_c$ as $\nu = 2 \rho_c$ for a perfect network). The tensile stress is:

$$
\sigma = \nu k T \left( \lambda - \dfrac{1}{\lambda^2} \right)
$$

where $\lambda = L / L_0$ is the extension ratio.

**Step 4 — Engineering form (small strains, $\varepsilon$):**

For small deformations, the **initial Young's modulus** is:

$$
E = 3 \nu k T = 6 \rho_c k T
$$

This is a celebrated result: **rubber stiffness increases linearly with absolute temperature** — a hallmark of entropy elasticity (metals behave oppositely, E decreases with T).

---

## 3.6 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Property | Thermoplastic | Elastomer |
|----------|---------------|-----------|
| Chain structure | Linear/branched, no cross-links | Linear chains with sparse cross-links |
| Bonding | Weak secondary forces | Weak secondary + sparse covalent cross-links |
| Response to heat | Softens → melts → resolidifies (reversible) | Remains rubbery; chars at high T |
| Glass transition | May be above or below 25 °C | Always well below 25 °C |
| Elastic strain | < 5–10 % | 100–1000 % |
| Young's modulus | 0.1–3 GPa | 1–100 MPa |
| Recyclability | High (remelt) | Low (permanent cross-links) |
| Examples | PE, PP, PVC, PS, Nylon, PMMA | NR, SBR, Neoprene, Silicone |
| Density | 0.85–1.4 g/cm³ | 0.9–1.3 g/cm³ |
| Service temperature | Up to Tm | Tg + 50 °C max |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Polymer Classification Flowchart (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[POLYMERS] --> nodeB[BASED ON THERMAL RESPONSE]
    nodeA --> nodeC[BASED ON MECHANICAL RESPONSE]
    nodeB --> nodeD[THERMOPLASTICS]
    nodeB --> nodeE[THERMOSETS]
    nodeB --> nodeF[ELASTOMERS]
    nodeD --> nodeG[Linear or branched chains]
    nodeD --> nodeH[Weak secondary bonds]
    nodeD --> nodeI[Reversible softening on heating]
    nodeE --> nodeJ[Heavily cross-linked 3D network]
    nodeE --> nodeK[Irreversible hardening]
    nodeF --> nodeL[Lightly cross-linked coils]
    nodeF --> nodeM[Large reversible elastic strain]
    nodeF --> nodeN[Tg well below room temperature]
    nodeC --> nodeO[PLASTICS]
    nodeC --> nodeP[FIBERS]
    nodeC --> nodeQ[FOAMS]
    nodeC --> nodeR[ELASTOMERS]
    nodeD --> nodeS[Examples PE PP PVC PS Nylon]
    nodeF --> nodeT[Examples NR SBR Silicone Neoprene]
    nodeE --> nodeU[Examples Epoxy Phenolic Melamine]
```

## 4.2 Thermoplastic vs Elastomer — Structure-Property Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[Linear Chain - Thermoplastic] --> nodeB[Free chain ends]
    nodeA --> nodeC[Weak van der Waals forces]
    nodeA --> nodeD[On heating - chains slide]
    nodeA --> nodeE[On cooling - resolidifies]
    nodeF[Cross-linked Network - Elastomer] --> nodeG[Sparse cross-links]
    nodeF --> nodeH[Coiled configuration]
    nodeF --> nodeI[On stretching - chain uncoils entropy drops]
    nodeF --> nodeJ[On release - recoil entropy rises]
    nodeD --> nodeK[Melting and flow]
    nodeI --> nodeL[Elastic recovery force]
```

## 4.3 Polymer State Diagram — Temperature Scale

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[Very Low T] --> nodeB[GLASSY STATE]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Temperature rises to Tg]
    nodeC --> nodeD[RUBBERY STATE]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Temperature rises to Tm]
    nodeE --> nodeF[MOLTEN FLOW STATE]
    nodeD --> nodeG[For semicrystalline polymers only]
    nodeG --> nodeH[Crystalline melting at Tm]
    nodeF --> nodeI[For amorphous thermoplastics - viscous flow]
    nodeB --> nodeJ[For elastomers - rubbery above Tg]
    nodeJ --> nodeK[Elastomer window above Tg]
```

## 4.4 Vulcanization Process Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Raw Natural Rubber - Polyisoprene] --> nodeB[Linear chains of cis-1,4-polyisoprene]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Add Sulfur and accelerator]
    nodeB --> nodeD[Add heat and pressure]
    nodeC --> nodeE[Sulfur bridges form between chains]
    nodeD --> nodeE
    nodeE --> nodeF[Cross-linked elastomer network]
    nodeF --> nodeG[Lightly cross-linked - soft rubber]
    nodeF --> nodeH[Heavily cross-linked - hard ebonite]
    nodeA --> nodeI[Properties before - sticky, thermoplastic-like]
    nodeF --> nodeJ[Properties after - elastic, durable, temperature-resistant]
```

## 4.5 Composite Material Context (Polymer as Matrix)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeA[POLYMER MATRIX COMPOSITE] --> nodeB[Matrix Phase]
    nodeA --> nodeC[Reinforcement Phase]
    nodeA --> nodeD[Interface]
    nodeB --> nodeE[Thermoplastic matrix]
    nodeB --> nodeF[Thermoset matrix]
    nodeB --> nodeG[Elastomer toughening]
    nodeC --> nodeH[Glass fiber]
    nodeC --> nodeI[Carbon fiber]
    nodeC --> nodeJ[Aramid fiber]
    nodeE --> nodeK[Examples - PEEK, PPS, PP-glass]
    nodeF --> nodeL[Examples - Epoxy-carbon, Phenolic-glass]
    nodeG --> nodeM[Examples - Rubber-toughened epoxy, HIPS]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 (Model Paper)]

**Q: Differentiate between thermoplastic and thermosetting polymers with two examples each.**

**Model Answer:**

| S.No. | Thermoplastic | Thermosetting Plastic |
|:-----:|---------------|----------------------|
| 1 | Linear/branched chains held by secondary forces | 3D cross-linked network with covalent bonds |
| 2 | Soften on heating, harden on cooling — reversible | Harden irreversibly on heating; char on further heating |
| 3 | Reprocessable and recyclable | Cannot be remelted or reprocessed |
| 4 | Examples: Polyethylene (PE), Polypropylene (PP) | Examples: Epoxy, Phenolic, Melamine |
| 5 | Bond type: weak van der Waals / dipole | Bond type: strong covalent cross-links |
| 6 | Soluble in selective solvents | Insoluble, only swells |

**Marks Distribution (KTU Pattern):**
- Definition + chain structure → 1 mark
- Thermal behavior contrast → 1 mark
- Two examples each → 1 mark

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — June 2024 (Model Paper)]

**Q: What is glass transition temperature (Tg)? Why must an elastomer have its Tg well below room temperature?**

**Model Answer:**

The **glass transition temperature (Tg)** is the temperature at which an amorphous polymer transitions from a hard, glassy state to a soft, rubbery state. It marks the onset of large-scale segmental motion of polymer chains. Below Tg, chain segments are frozen; above Tg, they have sufficient thermal energy for cooperative motion (typically 10–50 backbone atoms moving together).

**Why Tg must be well below 25 °C for an elastomer:**
1. An elastomer must be in the **rubbery state** at its service temperature (25 °C) to exhibit large elastic deformation.
2. Above Tg, the chains have enough free volume and mobility to uncoil under load and recoil upon release.
3. Typical requirement: $T_g \leq -40 °C$ for a service temperature of 25 °C (free-volume budget ≥ 65 K).

**Examples:** Natural rubber (Tg = –73 °C), Silicone (Tg = –127 °C), SBR (Tg = –55 °C).

**Marks Distribution (KTU Pattern):**
- Tg definition → 1 mark
- Segmental motion / free volume concept → 1 mark
- Practical requirement with example → 1 mark

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice Pattern)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pattern:** Part B questions for Module 3 in the KTU 2024 scheme carry 14 marks each with internal choice. Sub-part (a) = 7 marks, sub-part (b) = 7 marks.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Module 3]**

#### (a) [7 Marks] Explain the structural differences between thermoplastic and elastomeric polymers. Discuss how these differences affect their mechanical and thermal behavior.

**Model Solution:**

**Structural Differences (3.5 marks):**

1. **Chain architecture:**
   - *Thermoplastic:* Linear or branched chains with no covalent cross-links. Chains are held by weak secondary forces (van der Waals, dipole, H-bonds).
   - *Elastomer:* Linear chains joined by *sparse* covalent cross-links (e.g., C–S–S–C from vulcanization). The cross-links prevent permanent flow.

2. **Chain configuration:**
   - *Thermoplastic:* Chains may be in crystalline lamellae (semicrystalline) or random amorphous coils.
   - *Elastomer:* Chains are in random coiled configuration at rest (high entropy state).

3. **Cross-link density:**
   - *Thermoplastic:* Zero
   - *Elastomer:* Low, typically $10^{-4}$ to $10^{-5}$ mol/cm³

**Mechanical Behavior (2 marks):**

- *Thermoplastic:* Rigid, low elastic strain (< 5–10 %), E = 0.1–3 GPa. Above Tg, exhibits yielding and plastic flow.
- *Elastomer:* Large reversible elastic strain (100–1000 %), E = 1–100 MPa. Stress-strain curve is non-linear but fully recoverable up to fracture.

**Thermal Behavior (1.5 marks):**

- *Thermoplastic:* Reversible softening on heating above Tm (or Tg for amorphous). Remeltable and recyclable.
- *Elastomer:* Tg is well below 25 °C; service window is from Tg to degradation temperature. Cross-links make them non-remeltable.

#### (b) [7 Marks] With a neat diagram, explain the vulcanization of natural rubber. How does the degree of cross-linking influence the properties of the resulting elastomer?

**Model Solution:**

**Vulcanization Process (3.5 marks):**

Vulcanization, discovered by **Charles Goodyear (1839)**, is the process of cross-linking natural rubber (cis-1,4-polyisoprene) using sulfur. The process involves heating raw rubber with sulfur (1–40 % by mass) and an accelerator (e.g., MBT — mercaptobenzothiazole) at 140–180 °C.

**Chemical Reaction:**

$$
\text{R–CH=CH–R'} + \text{S} \xrightarrow{\text{heat, accelerator}} \text{R–CH–CH–R'} \;\; (\text{with –S–S– bridges})
$$

Sulfur forms **disulfide bridges (–S–S–)** between adjacent polymer chains.

**Diagram (to be drawn in exam):**

```
        CH₃                              CH₃
         |                                |
    ~~~~ C = C ~~~~                  ~~~~ C ~~~ C ~~~~
    ~~~~ CH₂  CH₂ ~~~~               ~~~~ CH₂  CH₂ ~~~~
         |       \                        |     /
         H        \                       H    S
                  S (cross-link)              \
         H        /                           CH₂
    ~~~~ CH₂  CH₂ ~~~~                  ~~~~ C ~~~ C ~~~~
    ~~~~ C = C ~~~~                    |       
         |                            CH₃
        CH₃
   (Before Vulcanization)         (After Vulcanization)
```

**Effect of Cross-link Density (3.5 marks):**

| Cross-link Level | Sulfur % | Properties |
|------------------|----------|-------------|
| **Low (1–2 % S)** | Soft rubber | High elasticity, low hardness, large strain |
| **Medium (3–5 % S)** | Tire rubber | Balanced strength, elasticity, fatigue resistance |
| **High (30–40 % S)** | Ebonite (hard rubber) | Rigid, hard, low elasticity, chemically resistant |

**Quantitative Relationship (Rubber Elasticity):**

$$
E = 6 \rho_c k T
$$

where $\rho_c$ is cross-link density. **Modulus increases linearly with cross-link density.** However, elongation at break *decreases* with cross-link density (chains cannot uncoil as much).

**Properties Improved by Vulcanization:**
1. Tensile strength (5× to 10× improvement)
2. Elastic recovery
3. Temperature resistance (no longer sticky in heat)
4. Abrasion resistance
5. Chemical resistance

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Module 3 — Alternative Choice]**

#### (a) [7 Marks] Define degree of polymerization. A polymer sample has a number-average molecular weight of 50,000 g/mol. If the monomer (vinyl chloride) molecular weight is 62.5 g/mol, calculate the degree of polymerization. Also, briefly explain how molecular weight affects the mechanical properties of a thermoplastic.

**Model Solution:**

**Definition (1 mark):** Degree of polymerization (n or DP) is the number of monomer (repeat) units in a polymer chain.

**Calculation (3 marks):**

Using the relation $M_n = n \cdot M_0$:

$$
n = \dfrac{M_n}{M_0} = \dfrac{50{,}000}{62.5} = 800
$$

Therefore, $n = \mathbf{800}$ repeat units per chain.

**Effect of Molecular Weight on Mechanical Properties (3 marks):**

| Property | Effect of Increasing MW |
|----------|--------------------------|
| Tensile strength | Increases up to a critical MW, then plateaus |
| Impact strength | Increases significantly (toughening) |
| Melt viscosity | Increases (processing becomes harder) |
| Glass transition Tg | Increases (chains entangled, restricted motion) |
| Chain entanglement | Higher MW → more entanglements → stronger |

**Key Concept (1 mark):** There exists an **entanglement molecular weight (Me)** (e.g., for PE, Me ≈ 4,000 g/mol) above which chains form a physically entangled network that dramatically improves strength. Below Me, the polymer is brittle and waxy.

---

#### (b) [7 Marks] Explain the rubber elasticity phenomenon. How does it differ from the elastic behavior of metals?

**Model Solution:**

**Rubber Elasticity — Key Features (4 marks):**

Rubber elasticity is fundamentally **entropic** in nature, arising from the **micro-Brownian motion of coiled chains** between cross-links.

1. **Coiled configuration:** In the unstressed state, an elastomer chain adopts a random coil (high entropy).
2. **Stretching:** External force uncoils the chain, reducing entropy.
3. **Recoil:** When force is removed, thermodynamics drives the chain back to its high-entropy coiled state, generating the **elastic restoring force**.

**Thermodynamic Origin:**

The free energy of a rubber band:

$$
\Delta F = \Delta U - T \Delta S
$$

For an ideal rubber, $\Delta U \approx 0$ (no bond stretching); hence $\Delta F \approx -T \Delta S$.

**Key Equation (Rubber Stress):**

$$
\sigma = \nu k T \left( \lambda - \dfrac{1}{\lambda^2} \right)
$$

where $\nu$ is network chain density and $\lambda$ is the extension ratio.

**Difference from Metals (3 marks):**

| Feature | Rubber (Elastomer) | Metal |
|---------|--------------------|-------|
| Origin of elasticity | Entropy change (chain uncoiling) | Energy change (bond stretching) |
| Strain magnitude | 100–1000 % (huge) | < 1 % |
| Effect of temperature on modulus | E *increases* with T | E *decreases* with T |
| Stress-strain curve | Non-linear, S-shaped | Linear (Hookean) |
| Mechanism | Molecular chain uncoiling | Atomic lattice distortion |
| Yielding | No yield; elastic up to fracture | Yields plastically above σy |
| Recovery | Fully recoverable (no permanent set) | Permanent set after plastic deformation |

**Key Insight:** Heating a rubber band makes it **stiffer** (E = 3νkT). Heating a metal makes it **softer**. This is the definitive test for entropy-driven elasticity.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Lose Marks — Module 3 Thermoplastics & Elastomers**
>
> 1. **Confusing Tg and Tm:** Tg is for **amorphous** phase; Tm is for **crystalline** phase. An elastomer has a Tg but **no Tm** (it's amorphous above Tg). Mark deducted if you claim "elastomers melt at Tm."
>
> 2. **Saying "elastomers are weakly bonded":** They have *strong covalent* cross-links (C–S–S–C) plus weak secondary forces. Marks lost for vague language.
>
> 3. **Forgetting the entropy mechanism:** Rubber elasticity is **NOT** about bond stretching. Marks deducted if answer focuses on "bond stretching like in a spring" without mentioning chain uncoiling and entropy.
>
> 4. **Wrong examples:** Epoxy is a **thermoset**, not a thermoplastic. Silicone is an **elastomer**, not a plastic. Be precise.
>
> 5. **No diagram in vulcanization question:** KTU examiners explicitly award 1–2 marks for a neat labeled diagram. Skipping it loses marks.
>
> 6. **Confusing "thermoplastic" with "thermoset":** These are *opposite* categories. Writing "thermoplastics cannot be remelted" is a fatal error worth –2 marks.
>
> 7. **Forgetting units in numerical problems:** Always quote g/mol for MW, K for Tg, MPa/GPa for modulus. KTU deducts 0.5 mark per missing unit.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute KTU Board Revision — Module 3 Thermoplastic & Elastomeric Polymers**

### Core Concepts
- ☐ Polymer = many mers; $M = n \cdot M_0$; PDI = Mw/Mn
- ☐ Polymerization = initiation → propagation → termination
- ☐ Three thermal classes: Thermoplastic, Thermoset, Elastomer

### Thermoplastics
- ☐ Linear/branched chains, weak secondary bonds
- ☐ Reversible softening on heating
- ☐ Reprocessable, recyclable
- ☐ Examples: PE, PP, PVC, PS, Nylon, PMMA
- ☐ Semicrystalline with crystalline + amorphous regions
- ☐ Service temperature ≤ Tm (or Tg for amorphous)

### Elastomers
- ☐ Lightly cross-linked coiled chains
- ☐ Tg < 25 °C (must be well below room temperature)
- ☐ Large reversible elastic strain (100–1000 %)
- ☐ Vulcanization = sulfur cross-links (Goodyear 1839)
- ☐ Examples: NR, SBR, Neoprene, Silicone
- ☐ Rubber elasticity is **entropic**, not energetic
- ☐ $E = 3\nu kT$ — modulus *increases* with T

### Glass Transition
- ☐ Tg separates glassy (E ≈ 3 GPa) from rubbery (E ≈ 3 MPa)
- ☐ Free volume at Tg ≈ 0.025
- ☐ Beaman-Boyer: Tg ≈ (2/3) Tm (in Kelvin)

### Formulas to Memorize
- ☐ $M = n \cdot M_0$
- ☐ $n = N_A \cdot m / M_0$
- ☐ $E = 3\nu kT$ (rubber)
- ☐ $\sigma = \nu kT(\lambda - 1/\lambda^2)$
- ☐ $\rho_c = (-\text{Flory-Rehner numerator}) / (V_1 \cdot \text{denominator})$

### Engineering Relevance
- ☐ Thermoplastics = matrix in fiber-reinforced composites (PEEK, PPS)
- ☐ Elastomers = toughening agents in rigid polymers (HIPS, RT-epoxy)
- ☐ Tg = upper service temperature for amorphous polymers

### Common Exam Traps
- ☐ Don't call epoxy a thermoplastic
- ☐ Don't say "rubber elasticity is bond stretching"
- ☐ Don't confuse Tg (amorphous) with Tm (crystalline)
- ☐ Always draw vulcanization diagram (1–2 marks reserved)
- ☐ Always quote units in numericals

---

> **END OF MODULE 3 NOTES — THERMOPLASTIC AND ELASTOMERIC POLYMERS**
> *Aligned with KTU 2024 Scheme, PEMUT412 — Composite Materials and Ceramics*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Properties, characteristics and applications as matrix materials
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEMUT412 - COMPOSITE MATERIALS AND CERAMICS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Properties, characteristics and applications as matrix materials ## 1. CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW ### 1.1 Formal Definition In a composite material, the **matrix** is the continuous phase that serves as the binding medium to hold the reinforcement (fibers or particles) in place. It is responsible for transferring loads to the reinforcement, protecting the reinforcement from environmental damage, providing shape and surface finish, and determining the overall service temperature, processing route, and durability of the composite. > [!NOTE] > **KTU Syllabus Definition (Matrix Material):** "A monolithic material into which the reinforcement is embedded. The matrix binds the reinforcement together, transfers load to the reinforcement, protects it from the environment, and provides shape, surface finish, and overall structural integrity." The four principal families of matrix materials used in engineering composites are: 1. **Polymer Matrix Composites (PMCs)** 2. **Metal Matrix Composites (MMCs)** 3. **Ceramic Matrix Composites (CMCs)** 4. **Carbon Matrix Composites (Carbon–Carbon C/C)** ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy > [!TIP] > **Intuitive Analogy — "The Brick Wall and the Steel Rods":** Imagine a brick wall reinforced with vertical steel rods embedded inside the mortar. The mortar (matrix) does three jobs: (a) it **glues** the bricks and rods together, (b) when you push the wall sideways, it **hands the load over** to the strong steel rods, and (c) it **shields** the rods from rain and rust. The steel rods (reinforcement) carry the load, but without mortar, they would buckle, corrode, and fall apart. **The matrix in a composite is exactly this mortar** — it is the weak, ductile, protective, load-transferring continuous phase that makes the strong reinforcement usable. ### 1.3 Why Matrix Selection is Critical (KTU High-Yield Point) > [!IMPORTANT] > **The matrix, NOT the reinforcement, usually determines:** > - Maximum service temperature > - Moisture/chemical resistance > - Manufacturing route (autoclave, lay-up, casting, infiltration) > - Toughness and impact behaviour > - Cost of the finished part > - Surface finish quality The fibres carry most of the load, but **the matrix dictates where, when, and how the composite can be used**. ### 1.4 Standard Reference Metrics (KTU Convention) The following normalized metrics are used throughout KTU board examinations to compare matrix materials: - **Specific Strength** = $\sigma_u / \rho$ (units: kN·m/kg or MPa/(g/cm³)) - **Specific Modulus** = $E / \rho$ (units: MN·m/kg or GPa/(g/cm³)) - **Service Temperature Index ($T_{max}$)** — maximum continuous use temperature in °C - **Volume Fraction ($V_f$, $V_m$)** — dimensionless, with $V_f + V_m = 1$ - **Coefficient of Thermal Expansion (CTE, $\alpha$)** — units: $10^{-6}/K$ or $\mu\varepsilon/K$ ## 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET ### 2.1 Functions of the Matrix (The "Five Jobs" Framework) A matrix must perform **five simultaneous functions** for the composite to work: 1. **Load Transfer:** When external load is applied, the matrix must shear-load the reinforcement through the interface. This requires adequate **interfacial shear strength ($\tau_i$)**. 2. **Reinforcement Separation & Spacing:** Prevents fibre-fibre contact that creates stress concentrations. 3. **Environmental Shielding:** Protects reinforcement from moisture, UV, oxidation, and abrasion. 4. **Crack Arresting:** Ductile polymer matrices blunt crack tips via plastic deformation. 5. **Shape & Finish:** Provides the geometric form and surface quality of the part. ### 2.2 Detailed Classification of Matrix Materials #### 2.2.1 Polymer Matrices (PMCs) — Most Common **A. Thermosetting Resins (irreversibly cross-linked)** | Resin | Curing T (°C) | $T_g$ (°C) | Tensile Strength (MPa) | Density (g/cm³) | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Epoxy** | 120–180 | 100–220 | 55–130 | 1.15–1.25 | | **Unsaturated Polyester** | RT–150 | 70–120 | 40–90 | 1.10–1.40 | | **Vinyl Ester** | RT–150 | 80–150 | 70–100 | 1.12–1.32 | | **Phenolic** | 150–250 | 250+ | 35–60 | 1.20–1.40 | | **Bismaleimide (BMI)** | 180–250 | 250–300 | 50–90 | 1.20–1.30 | | **Cyanate Ester** | 200–250 | 250–290 | 60–100 | 1.10–1.35 | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Favourite — Epoxy Resin:** Epoxy is the **most widely used** matrix in advanced composites (aerospace, wind turbine blades, sporting goods) because of: > - Low shrinkage on cure (< 2 %) > - Excellent adhesion to almost all fibres > - Superior chemical resistance > - Good mechanical properties after cure > - Tailorable curing cycle **B.
