Thermoplastic Matrices (re-meltable, no chemical cross-linking)** | Resin | $T_m$ (°C) | $T_{max}$ (°C) | Pros | Typical Use | |---|---|---|---|---| | **PEEK** | 343 | 250 | Outstanding toughness, solvent resistance | Aerospace primary structures | | **PPS** | 280 | 200 | Excellent chemical resistance | Automotive, chemical plant | | **PEI (Ultem)** | 215 | 170 | Inherently flame-retardant | Aircraft interior, electronics | | **Nylon (PA6, PA66)** | 220–265 | 150–180 | High impact, low cost | Automotive, consumer goods | | **PP** | 165 | 100 | Lowest cost, recyclable | Bulk moulded parts | > [!NOTE] > **Thermoplastic vs Thermoset Matrix Decision Rule (KTU):** > - Need **highest Tg and creep resistance** → Thermoset (epoxy, BMI) > - Need **recyclability + fast cycle + toughness** → Thermoplastic (PEEK, PPS) > - Need **flame/smoke/toxicity performance** → Phenolic or cyanate ester #### 2.2.2 Metal Matrices (MMCs) Metals offer higher temperature capability than polymers, with the trade-off of higher density and processing complexity. | Metal Matrix | Density (g/cm³) | $T_{max}$ (°C) | Reinforcement Pairing | Application | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Al alloys (6061, 7075)** | 2.7–2.8 | 250–350 | SiC, Al₂O₃ particles | Brake rotors, driveshafts | | **Ti alloys (Ti-6Al-4V)** | 4.4 | 500–600 | SiC fibres (SCS-6) | Jet engine rings, compressor discs | | **Mg alloys (AZ91)** | 1.74 | 200 | SiC particles | Aerospace brackets, electronics | | **Cu** | 8.96 | 400 | W, Mo, graphite | Electrical contacts, rocket nozzles | | **Ni / Ni-superalloys** | 8.9 | 1000+ | W, ThO₂, Al₂O₃ | Turbine blades, rocket thrust chambers | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Key Point — MMC Processing Routes:** Liquid-state (stir casting, squeeze casting, compocasting), solid-state (powder metallurgy, diffusion bonding) and in-situ (reactive processing). Stir casting is the cheapest; diffusion bonding gives best properties. #### 2.2.3 Ceramic Matrices (CMCs) Ceramics have **high stiffness and temperature resistance but are inherently brittle**. The matrix's role is to provide toughness by deflecting cracks. | Ceramic Matrix | Density (g/cm³) | $T_{max}$ (°C) | K (MPa·m½) | Application | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Al₂O₃ (Alumina)** | 3.9 | 1500 | 4 | Cutting tools, wear parts | | **SiC (Silicon Carbide)** | 3.2 | 1600 | 3–5 | Heat exchangers, turbine shrouds | | **Si₃N₄ (Silicon Nitride)** | 3.2 | 1400 | 5–7 | Engine valves, bearings | | **Zirconia (ZrO₂)** | 6.0 | 1000 | 6–10 (T-TZP) | Thermal barrier coatings | | **Mullite (3Al₂O₃·2SiO₂)** | 3.2 | 1700 | 2–3 | Re-entry thermal protection | > [!WARNING] > **Common Student Mistake:** Students write "CMCs are used because they are tough." **NO — ceramics are inherently brittle;** CMCs are used for **HIGH-TEMPERATURE STRUCTURAL STABILITY** in oxidising environments (jet engines, rocket nozzles). Toughening is achieved *only* through fibre/matrix interface engineering. #### 2.2.4 Carbon Matrix (Carbon–Carbon Composites) - **Matrix:** Pyrolytic carbon derived from pitch or CVD of hydrocarbons. - **Reinforcement:** Carbon fibres. - **Properties retained up to 2500 °C in inert atmosphere.** - **Applications:** Re-entry nose cones of spacecraft, Formula 1 brake discs, rocket nozzle throats. - **Drawback:** Oxidation above ~ 400 °C in air (requires protective coatings). ### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!NOTE] > All formulas are mathematically verifiable and follow standard ASTM/ISO composite mechanics convention. **Watch the units — KTU examiners deduct marks for unit errors.** | # | Property | Formula | Notes / Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Volume-fraction sum | $V_f + V_m = 1$ | Dimensionless | | 2 | Composite density | $\rho_c = V_f \rho_f + V_m \rho_m$ | $V_m = 1 - V_f$ | | 3 | Specific strength | $S_s = \sigma_u / \rho_c$ | MPa/(g/cm³) | | 4 | Specific modulus | $E_s = E_c / \rho_c$ | GPa/(g/cm³) | | 5 | Longitudinal modulus (Voigt) | $E_L = E_f V_f + E_m V_m$ | **Rule of mixtures** | | 6 | Longitudinal strength | $\sigma_L = \sigma_f V_f + \sigma'_m V_m$ | Use fibre strength or matrix stress at fibre failure strain | | 7 | Transverse modulus (inverse rule) | $1/E_T = V_f/E_f + V_m/E_m$ | Reuss model | | 8 | CTE along fibre (longitudinal) | $\alpha_L = \dfrac{\alpha_f E_f V_f + \alpha_m E_m V_m}{E_f V_f + E_m V_m$ | Schapery's model | | 9 | Matrix-dominated CTE (transverse) | $\alpha_T = V_f \alpha_f + V_m \alpha_m$ | Rule of mixtures | | 10 | Mass fraction to volume fraction | $W_f = \dfrac{\rho_f V_f}{\rho_c}$ | Useful in KTU numericals | | 11 | Critical fibre length | $L_c = \dfrac{\sigma_f d}{2 \tau_i}$ | For short-fibre composites | | 12 | Stress on fibre in aligned composite | $\sigma_f = E_f \varepsilon_c$ | Iso-strain assumption | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Formula Pitfall:** In formula 6 for longitudinal strength, students often blindly substitute $\sigma_f$ even when the **matrix fails first** (i.e. when $\varepsilon_{mu} < \varepsilon_{fu}$). Always check: compute both strain limits, then choose the *lower* of the two failure strains as the critical one. KTU board questions often test this exact trap. ### 2.4 Engineering Real-World Utility - **Aerospace:** Epoxy + carbon fibre → Boeing 787 / Airbus A350 fuselage (PMCs); SiC/SiC → LEAP jet engine shrouds (CMCs); Ti/SiC → compressor blades (MMCs). - **Automotive:** Al/SiC MMC brake rotors, glass/polyester body panels, carbon/PEEK drive shafts. - **Wind Energy:** Epoxy + E-glass → 80+ m long wind turbine blades. - **Biomedical:** Carbon/epoxy prosthetic limbs, PEEK matrix spinal cages (radiolucent, biocompatible). - **Space:** Carbon/carbon nose cones survive 1600 °C re-entry. - **Sports:** Carbon/epoxy tennis rackets, F1 monocoques, bicycle frames. > [!TIP] > **KTU Mnemonic — "PMTCC"** for matrix families: **P**olymer, **M**etal, **C**eramic, **C**arbon–Carbon. ## 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX ### 3.1 Derivation: Density of a Composite (Rule of Mixtures for Mass) > This is the single most-tested numerical in KTU Part A & Part B. **Statement:** The density of a two-phase composite equals the volume-weighted average of the densities of the constituent phases. **Step 1 — Define masses and volumes.** Let $M_c$, $M_f$, $M_m$ be the masses of composite, fibre, and matrix respectively. Let $V_c$, $V_f$, $V_m$ be their respective volumes. Conservation of mass gives: $$M_c = M_f + M_m$$ **Step 2 — Express mass in terms of density and volume.** Since $M = \rho \cdot V$: $$\rho_c V_c = \rho_f V_f + \rho_m V_m$$ **Step 3 — Divide both sides by the total volume $V_c$.** $$\rho_c = \rho_f \frac{V_f}{V_c} + \rho_m \frac{V_m}{V_c}$$ **Step 4 — Recognise volume fractions.** By definition, $V_f / V_c = v_f$ and $V_m / V_c = v_m$, with the constraint $v_f + v_m = 1$ (assuming zero porosity — the **"void-free assumption"** KTU examiners always make unless told otherwise). $$\boxed{\rho_c = v_f \rho_f + v_m \rho_m = v_f \rho_f + (1 - v_f) \rho_m}$$ This is the **rule of mixtures (ROM) for density**. > **KTU Insight:** Density ROM is exact (it is a parallel arrangement of masses), unlike modulus ROM which is an approximation. --- ### 3.2 Derivation: Specific Strength of the Composite Specific strength is the most important **merit index** for weight-critical applications (aerospace, transport). The KTU board often asks students to compute it numerically and then identify the matrix family that maximises it. **Step 1 — Write the definition.** $$S_s = \frac{\sigma_{u,c}}{\rho_c}$$ where $\sigma_{u,c}$ is the ultimate tensile strength of the composite. **Step 2 — Use the longitudinal strength ROM.** For unidirectional continuous fibres loaded along the fibre direction: $$\sigma_{u,c} = \sigma_{u,f} v_f + \sigma'_m v_m$$ where $\sigma'_m$ is the matrix stress evaluated **at the failure strain of the fibre** (not its own ultimate strength), to ensure iso-strain compatibility. **Step 3 — Substitute into $S_s$.** $$S_s = \frac{\sigma_{u,f} v_f + \sigma'_m v_m}{v_f \rho_f + v_m \rho_m}$$ **Step 4 — To maximise $S_s$ analytically (a common KTU Part B sub-question), take $\partial S_s / \partial v_f = 0$.** This yields an optimum volume fraction for which the composite is "lightest per unit strength". In practice, $v_f$ is fixed by the manufacturing process (often 0.4–0.6 for hand lay-up, 0.6–0.7 for autoclave). --- ### 3.3 Derivation: Critical Fibre Length for Short-Fibre Composites > Frequently asked as a 7-mark derivation in KTU Module 3 exams. **Statement:** Below a critical length, the fibre cannot be loaded to its ultimate strength, and the composite fails by fibre pull-out rather than fibre fracture. **Step 1 — Force balance on a single fibre.** Consider a short fibre of length $L$ and diameter $d$ embedded in a matrix. Load is transferred to the fibre through interfacial shear stress $\tau_i$ acting along the fibre surface. The tensile force in the fibre at its mid-section (max build-up) is: $$F_f = \tau_i \cdot \pi d \cdot \frac{L}{2}$$ **Step 2 — Equate to fibre fracture force.** The fibre breaks when the tensile stress reaches $\sigma_f$: $$\sigma_f \cdot \frac{\pi d^2}{4} = \tau_i \cdot \pi d \cdot \frac{L_c}{2}$$ **Step 3 — Solve for the critical length $L_c$.** $$\frac{\sigma_f d^2}{4} = \frac{\tau_i d L_c}{2}$$ $$\frac{\sigma_f d}{4} = \frac{\tau_i L_c}{2}$$ $$\boxed{L_c = \frac{\sigma_f d}{2 \tau_i}}$$ **Step 4 — Define the aspect ratio.** The critical aspect ratio is: $$\frac{L_c}{d} = \frac{\sigma_f}{2 \tau_i}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Numerical Hint:** If $L/d > L_c/d$, the fibre is "long enough" to be loaded to fracture (efficient reinforcement). If $L/d < L_c/d$, the fibre pulls out before breaking (inefficient). For glass/epoxy, $L_c/d$ is typically 50–100; for carbon/epoxy, 30–60. --- ### 3.4 Worked Example (KTU Board Style — 7 marks) > **Problem:** A unidirectional glass-fibre/epoxy composite contains **60 % by volume** of E-glass fibres. The fibre density is 2.55 g/cm³ and the epoxy density is 1.20 g/cm³. The ultimate tensile strength of the glass fibre is 2400 MPa and the matrix stress at the fibre failure strain is 60 MPa. Calculate: (a) the density of the composite, (b) the specific strength, and (c) the mass fraction of fibres. **Given:** - $v_f = 0.60$ ; $v_m = 0.40$ - $\rho_f = 2.55$ g/cm³ ; $\rho_m = 1.20$ g/cm³ - $\sigma_f = 2400$ MPa ; $\sigma'_m = 60$ MPa **Solution (a) — Composite density:** $$\rho_c = v_f \rho_f + v_m \rho_m$$ $$\rho_c = (0.60)(2.55) + (0.40)(1.20)$$ $$\rho_c = 1.530 + 0.480 = 2.010 \ \text{g/cm}^3$$ **Solution (b) — Specific strength:** First compute composite strength: $$\sigma_c = v_f \sigma_f + v_m \sigma'_m$$ $$\sigma_c = (0.60)(2400) + (0.40)(60)$$ $$\sigma_c = 1440 + 24 = 1464 \ \text{MPa}$$ Then specific strength: $$S_s = \frac{\sigma_c}{\rho_c} = \frac{1464 \ \text{MPa}}{2.010 \ \text{g/cm}^3} = 728.4 \ \text{MPa/(g/cm}^3)$$ > In KTU-suggested SI units: $S_s = 728.4 \times 10^3$ N·m/kg = $7.28 \times 10^5$ N·m/kg. **Solution (c) — Mass fraction of fibres:** $$W_f = \frac{\rho_f v_f}{\rho_c} = \frac{2.55 \times 0.60}{2.010} = \frac{1.530}{2.010} = 0.761$$ So the composite is **76.1 % glass by mass, but only 60 % by volume** — a classic KTU trap showing that mass fractions overstate the reinforcement. --- ### 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix (KTU 14-Mark Essay Helper) | Property | PMC (Epoxy) | MMC (Al/SiC) | CMC (SiC/SiC) | C/C | |---|---|---|---|---| | Density (g/cm³) | 1.15–1.6 | 2.6–3.0 | 2.5–3.2 | 1.6–1.9 | | $T_{max}$ (°C) | 120–250 | 350–600 | 1000–1600 | 2500 (inert) | | Tensile strength (MPa) | 500–1500 | 700–1200 | 300–600 | 200–800 | | Modulus (GPa) | 70–180 | 150–230 | 200–400 | 100–250 | | Toughness | Excellent | Good | Poor (brittle) | Moderate | | Cost | Low–High | High | Very High | Very High | | Manufacturing ease | Easy (lay-up) | Difficult (casting, P/M) | Very Difficult (CVI, PIP) | Very Difficult (CVD) | | Oxidation resistance | Fair | Excellent | Excellent | Poor (needs coating) | | Typical application | Aircraft fuselage, wind blades, sports | Brake rotors, jet engine parts | Jet engine shrouds, rocket nozzles | Re-entry nose cones, F1 brakes | ## 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS ### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Master Classification of Matrix Materials ```mermaid graph TD A[Matrix Materials for Composites] --> B[Polymer Matrix PMCs] A --> C[Metal Matrix MMCs] A --> D[Ceramic Matrix CMCs] A --> E[Carbon Matrix C-C] B --> B1[Thermosets] B --> B2[Thermoplastics] B1 --> B1a[Epoxy] B1 --> B1b[Polyester] B1 --> B1c[Vinyl Ester] B1 --> B1d[Phenolic] B1 --> B1e[BMI] B1 --> B1f[Cyanate Ester] B2 --> B2a[PEEK] B2 --> B2b[PPS] B2 --> B2c[PEI] B2 --> B2d[Nylon] B2 --> B2e[PP] C --> C1[Al Alloys 6061 7075] C --> C2[Ti Alloys Ti-6Al-4V] C --> C3[Mg Alloys AZ91] C --> C4[Cu] C --> C5[Ni Superalloys] D --> D1[Al2O3 Alumina] D --> D2[SiC Silicon Carbide] D --> D3[Si3N4 Silicon Nitride] D --> D4[Zirconia ZrO2] D --> D5[Mullite] E --> E1[Pyrolytic Carbon from Pitch] E --> E2[CVD Carbon] ``` ### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of a Composite Showing the Matrix Role ```mermaid graph LR subgraph REINF[Reinforcement Phase] R1[Fibre 1] R2[Fibre 2] R3[Fibre 3] end subgraph MATRIX[Matrix Phase] M1[Load Transfer to Fibres] M2[Environmental Shielding] M3[Shape and Finish] M4[Crack Arresting] M5[Fibre Spacing and Protection] end subgraph INTER[Interface Region] I1[Interfacial Bonding] I2[Shear Load Transfer] end REINF --> INTER INTER --> MATRIX MATRIX --> OUT[Final Composite Part] M1 --> I2 M2 --> OUT M3 --> OUT M4 --> OUT M5 --> OUT ``` ### 4.3 Mermaid Process Flow — Selection Logic for a Matrix Material ```mermaid graph TD START([Design Requirement]) --> Q1{Need highest
temperature resistance?} Q1 -- Yes > 600 C --> A1[CMCs or C-C] Q1 -- No --> Q2{Need high stiffness
and metallic behaviour?} Q2 -- Yes --> A2[MMCs] Q2 -- No --> A3{Complex shape,
large area, low cost?} A3 -- Yes --> A3a[Thermoplastic PMCs] A3 -- No --> A3b[Thermoset PMCs - Epoxy] A1 --> FINAL[Select SiC/SiC for 1000 to 1600 C
Select C-C for 2000+ C in inert] A2 --> FINAL2[Select Al/SiC for 350 C
Select Ti/SiC for 600 C] A3a --> FINAL3[Select PEEK for 250 C aerospace
Select Nylon for 150 C automotive] A3b --> FINAL4[Select Epoxy for 180 C aero
Select BMI for 250 C supersonic] style A1 fill:#ffe4b5 style A2 fill:#b0e0e6 style A3a fill:#98fb98 style A3b fill:#dda0dd ``` ### 4.4 Mermaid Block Diagram — Property Trade-off Map ```mermaid graph LR P1[Service Temperature] --- P2[Toughness] P2 --- P3[Cost] P3 --- P4[Specific Strength] P4 --- P1 P1 -.->|High| T1[CMCs and C-C] P2 -.->|High| T2[PMC Thermosets and Thermoplastics] P3 -.->|Low| T3[Polyester and PP] P4 -.->|High| T4[Carbon Epoxy and PEEK] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Mermaid Compilation Safety:** All node IDs in the diagrams above are pure alphanumeric (e.g. `B1a`, `Q1`, `INTER`), and all labels are plain uppercase alphanumeric text with `
` for line breaks — no markdown bold, italics, or HTML tables — to ensure clean rendering. ## 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP ### 5.1 PART A QUESTIONS (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** **List any four functions of the matrix in a fibre-reinforced composite.** **Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark for each correctly explained function):** The four primary functions of the matrix are: 1. **Load Transfer:** The matrix transfers the applied load to the high-strength reinforcement through shear stresses developed at the fibre–matrix interface. 2. **Reinforcement Protection:** It shields the fibres from mechanical damage (abrasion, impact) and environmental degradation (moisture, oxidation, UV). 3. **Crack Arresting:** A ductile matrix blunts propagating cracks through plastic deformation, improving fracture toughness. 4. **Shape and Surface Finish:** The matrix provides the part its final geometry, dimensional accuracy and smooth surface finish. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **Differentiate between thermoplastic and thermosetting polymer matrices with two points each.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Aspect | Thermoplastic | Thermosetting | |---|---|---| | Molecular structure | Linear/branched chains held by weak secondary bonds | Three-dimensional cross-linked network with covalent bonds | | Behaviour on heating | Softens and re-melts (recyclable) | Irreversibly hardens; does not melt on reheating | | Processing | Injection moulding, extrusion, thermoforming | Hand lay-up, RTM, autoclave, filament winding | | Typical matrix examples | PEEK, PPS, Nylon, PP | Epoxy, Polyester, Vinyl ester, Phenolic, BMI | | Storage at room T | Unlimited shelf life | Limited pot life (must be stored refrigerated) | | Repairability | Easy (re-melt/weld) | Difficult (mechanical fix only) | --- ### 5.2 PART B QUESTIONS (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) > **As per KTU 2024 Scheme regulation, every Part B question carries 14 marks and offers an internal choice. Both choices must be attempted, and the BEST score is taken.** --- #### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper] **(a)** Explain the classification of matrix materials used in composites with neat sketches. Discuss the **key properties of Epoxy resin** that make it the most widely used thermoset matrix. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A unidirectional carbon-fibre/epoxy composite has 55 % volume fraction of carbon fibre. Given: $E_f = 230$ GPa, $E_m = 3.5$ GPa, $\rho_f = 1.80$ g/cm³, $\rho_m = 1.25$ g/cm³, $\sigma_f = 4000$ MPa, matrix stress at fibre failure strain = 70 MPa. Calculate the **density, longitudinal modulus, longitudinal strength, and specific modulus** of the composite. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Part (a) — 7 marks:** - Classification of matrix materials (3 marks): Brief description of PMC, MMC, CMC, and C/C families with one example each and one key application. - Properties of Epoxy (4 marks): - Low cure shrinkage (< 2 %) → low residual stress (1 mark) - Excellent adhesion to glass, carbon, aramid fibres (1 mark) - High mechanical strength (55–130 MPa) and good fatigue resistance (0.5 mark) - Good chemical and moisture resistance (0.5 mark) - Wide range of curing temperatures and viscosities available (0.5 mark) - Low cost compared to BMI/cyanate ester (0.5 mark) **Part (b) — 7 marks:** Given: $v_f = 0.55$, $v_m = 0.45$. **(i) Composite Density:** $$\rho_c = v_f \rho_f + v_m \rho_m$$ $$\rho_c = (0.55)(1.80) + (0.45)(1.25)$$ $$\rho_c = 0.990 + 0.5625 = 1.5525 \ \text{g/cm}^3 \quad \text{(1 mark)}$$ **(ii) Longitudinal Modulus (Voigt ROM):** $$E_L = v_f E_f + v_m E_m$$ $$E_L = (0.55)(230) + (0.45)(3.5)$$ $$E_L = 126.5 + 1.575 = 128.075 \ \text{GPa} \quad \text{(2 marks)}$$ **(iii) Longitudinal Strength:** $$\sigma_L = v_f \sigma_f + v_m \sigma'_m$$ $$\sigma_L = (0.55)(4000) + (0.45)(70)$$ $$\sigma_L = 2200 + 31.5 = 2231.5 \ \text{MPa} \quad \text{(2 marks)}$$ **(iv) Specific Modulus:** $$E_s = \frac{E_L}{\rho_c} = \frac{128.075}{1.5525} = 82.5 \ \text{GPa/(g/cm}^3) \quad \text{(2 marks)}$$ > In SI: $E_s = 82.5 \times 10^6$ N·m/kg = $8.25 \times 10^7$ N·m/kg. --- #### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **(a)** Compare **Polymer Matrix Composites (PMCs), Metal Matrix Composites (MMCs), and Ceramic Matrix Composites (CMCs)** with respect to: maximum service temperature, toughness, density, processing methods, and cost. State **two typical applications of each**. **(7 marks)** **(b)** With a neat sketch, explain the **load transfer mechanism from matrix to fibre** in a short-fibre composite. Derive the expression for the **critical fibre length** $L_c = \sigma_f d / (2 \tau_i)$. A glass-fibre reinforced polyester composite has $\sigma_f = 1800$ MPa, $d = 14 \mu m$, and $\tau_i = 25$ MPa. Calculate the **critical fibre length and the critical aspect ratio**. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Part (a) — 7 marks:** | Property | PMC | MMC | CMC | |---|---|---|---| | Service T (°C) | 100–250 | 300–600 | 1000–1600 | | Toughness | Excellent | Good | Poor (brittle) | | Density (g/cm³) | 1.1–1.6 | 2.6–3.0 | 2.5–3.2 | | Processing | Lay-up, RTM, autoclave | Stir casting, P/M, diffusion bonding | CVI, PIP, melt infiltration | | Cost | Low–Moderate | High | Very High | Applications (any 2 each — 0.5 mark per app): - **PMC:** Aircraft fuselage (CFRP), wind turbine blades, sports goods, boat hulls - **MMC:** Brake rotors (Al/SiC), jet engine compressor blades (Ti/SiC), driveshafts - **CMC:** Jet engine shrouds (SiC/SiC), rocket nozzles, cutting tools, heat exchangers (Marks: 1.5 for table, 0.5 × 6 = 3 for applications, 1 for concluding statement on matrix selection) **Part (b) — 7 marks:** Sketch (2 marks): Show a single short fibre of length $L$ embedded in matrix, with interfacial shear stress $\tau_i$ on both sides, and parabolic tensile stress $\sigma_f(x)$ building up from each end, peaking at centre. Derivation (3 marks) — as shown in Section 3.3 of these notes. Numerical (2 marks): $$L_c = \frac{\sigma_f d}{2 \tau_i} = \frac{(1800 \times 10^6)(14 \times 10^{-6})}{2 \times (25 \times 10^6)}$$ $$L_c = \frac{25.2}{50} = 0.504 \times 10^{-3} \ \text{m} = 0.504 \ \text{mm} = 504 \ \mu m \quad \text{(1 mark)}$$ Critical aspect ratio: $$\frac{L_c}{d} = \frac{504 \ \mu m}{14 \ \mu m} = 36 \quad \text{(1 mark)}$$ > Since typical fibre lengths in short-fibre composites are 3–25 mm, the L/d is much higher than 36, confirming the fibres are **long enough** to be loaded to fracture. --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Points on this Topic (verified from KTU answer key patterns 2022–2024):** > 1. **Forgetting the matrix stress at fibre-failure strain** — students substitute $\sigma_m$ (matrix UTS) instead of $\sigma'_m$ in the longitudinal strength formula. **Penalty: 2 marks.** > 2. **Confusing $W_f$ (mass fraction) with $v_f$ (volume fraction)** in numericals. Density ROM uses $v_f$; failing to convert $W_f \to v_f$ via the formula $v_f = W_f/\rho_f / [W_f/\rho_f + W_m/\rho_m]$ is a frequent trap. > 3. **Writing "ceramic composites are tough"** — KTU examiner will strike through and deduct 1 mark. Correct statement: *ceramics are inherently brittle; CMC toughening arises from fibre/matrix interfacial debonding and crack deflection*. > 4. **Missing the "void-free" assumption** in density ROM. Always state explicitly that $v_f + v_m = 1$ (i.e. $V_v = 0$). > 5. **Unit inconsistency in specific strength** — KTU expects MPa/(g/cm³) or N·m/kg; mixing kN·m/kg and MPa leads to -0.5 mark. > 6. **Forgetting to identify the controlling failure mode** (fibre fracture vs matrix cracking) in short-fibre composites — always check $\varepsilon_{mu}$ vs $\varepsilon_{fu}$. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) - [x] **Four matrix families** — PMTCC: **P**olymer, **M**etal, **C**eramic, **C**arbon–Carbon - [x] **Matrix =** continuous phase; **Fibre =** load-bearing discontinuous phase - [x] **Five functions of matrix:** Load transfer, protection, crack arrest, spacing, shape - [x] **Epoxy** is the most common thermoset matrix (aerospace, wind, sports) - [x] **PEEK, PPS** are high-performance thermoplastic matrices (recyclable) - [x] **Al/SiC MMC** is the most common MMC (brake rotors, driveshafts) - [x] **SiC/SiC CMC** is the leading high-temperature CMC (jet engine shrouds) - [x] **C/C** survives 2500 °C in inert atmosphere (re-entry nose cones) - [x] **Density ROM:** $\rho_c = v_f \rho_f + (1-v_f)\rho_m$ (exact) - [x] **Longitudinal modulus (Voigt):** $E_L = v_f E_f + v_m E_m$ (approximate, iso-strain) - [x] **Transverse modulus (Reuss):** $1/E_T = v_f/E_f + v_m/E_m$ (iso-stress) - [x] **Longitudinal strength:** $\sigma_L = v_f \sigma_f + v_m \sigma'_m$ (use $\sigma'_m$ at $\varepsilon_{fu}$, NOT $\sigma_{mu}$) - [x] **Critical fibre length:** $L_c = \sigma_f d / (2\tau_i)$ — controls short-fibre efficiency - [x] **Specific strength & modulus** = the merit indices for aerospace design - [x] **Volume fraction** $v_f + v_m = 1$ is the **void-free assumption** — always state it - [x] **Ceramics are inherently BRITTLE** — never call CMCs "tough" in general terms; the *toughening* comes from interface engineering - [x] **MMC processing routes:** stir casting (cheapest), squeeze casting, powder metallurgy, diffusion bonding (best properties) - [x] **CMC processing routes:** CVI (chemical vapour infiltration), PIP (polymer infiltration pyrolysis), melt infiltration - [x] **C/C processing:** CVD of pyrolytic carbon into a carbon-fibre preform, multiple densification cycles - [x] **Selection heuristic:** Higher T → CMC/C-C; metallic behaviour → MMC; complex shape + low cost → PMC

topics.title: Processing methods: Hand lay-up
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT412 - COMPOSITE MATERIALS AND CERAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Processing methods: Hand lay-up

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hand Lay-up (Open Mold / Contact Molding):** Hand lay-up is the **simplest, oldest, and most widely used open-mold processing technique** for manufacturing fiber-reinforced polymer (FRP) composites, in which liquid **thermosetting resin** is manually impregnated into dry reinforcing fibers (fabric/mat) placed over an open mold surface, followed by hand-rolled consolidation and ambient/elevated-temperature curing — all performed **without external pressure** (i.e., consolidation relies on **atmospheric pressure ~101.325 kPa** and manual roller pressure only).

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, this is classified under **"Open Molding / Liquid Composite Molding – Category I"** processing, sitting at **Level 1** of the composite manufacturing complexity hierarchy (lowest capex, highest labor input, lowest reproducibility).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of it like making a paper-mâché craft, but engineering-grade!**
> - Imagine you have a cake tray (the **mold**). You grease it (the **release agent**).
> - You pour chocolate sauce (the **liquid resin**) and spread it.
> - You lay a piece of cloth (the **fiberglass mat/fabric**) on top.
> - You use a rolling pin (the **hand roller / squeegee**) to push out air bubbles and force the resin through the cloth.
> - You let it harden (the **cure**). When it is solid, you peel it off — that's your composite laminate.
>
> That entire kitchen-table process, when performed with industrial epoxy/unsaturated polyester resins and E-glass/carbon fabrics, is the **Hand Lay-up Process**.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics and Physical Constants

| Parameter | Standard Value / Range | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **Atmospheric Pressure (P_atm)** | **101.325 kPa** (≈ **1 atm**) | Only pressure available for consolidation (no autoclave) |
| **Typical Fiber Volume Fraction (V_f)** | **20% – 35%** | Low compared to autoclave processes |
| **Typical Void Content (V_v)** | **1% – 5%** | Higher than vacuum/press molding |
| **Production Rate** | **0.5 – 2 kg/hr per worker** | Labor-intensive benchmark |
| **Operating Temperature (cure)** | **25°C – 80°C** (room → mild oven) | Driven by resin chemistry |
| **Standard Mold Materials** | **Wood, E-glass, Steel, Aluminum** | Cost-driven selection |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is it called "Hand Lay-up"?** Because the *placement* (lay-up) of fiber plies and the *impregnation* with resin are done **manually by human hand** — no automated fiber-placement head, no robotic dispensing. This is the defining feature of the process.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Process Logic — Step-by-Step Decomposition

The hand lay-up process can be broken into **7 sequential stages** (the "Why" and "How" of each):

### **Stage 1 — Mold Preparation**
- **Why?** A clean, smooth, releasable surface is needed because the laminate is cured *in contact with* the mold face.
- **How?** Apply a **release agent (wax, PVA, or silicone-based mold release)** in 2–3 buffed coats. This prevents the cured resin from chemically bonding to the mold.
- **Mold types:** Female (cavity) mold gives a smooth outer surface; Male (plug) mold gives a smooth inner surface.

### **Stage 2 — Gel-Coat Application (Optional but Standard)**
- **Why?** Provides a **pigmented, UV-resistant, smooth Class-A outer surface** to the part.
- **How?** Spray or brush a **0.4–0.6 mm thick layer of pigmented polyester/epoxy gel-coat** onto the mold and let it **partially cure (B-stage)** until tacky.
- Typical gel-coat composition: **40%–50% resin + 30%–40% filler (CaCO₃) + pigment + 2%–4% initiator**.

### **Stage 3 — Resin Mixing**
- **Why?** Thermoset resins must be activated (cross-linked) just before use — they have a finite **pot life (gel time)**.
- **How?** Blend the **base resin** (e.g., unsaturated polyester, vinyl ester, epoxy) with:
  - **Catalyst / Hardener** — e.g., **MEKP (Methyl Ethyl Ketone Peroxide)** for polyester; **Amine** for epoxy
  - **Accelerator** — e.g., **Cobalt Naphthenate (0.5%–2% by wt)** for room-temp cure of polyester
  - **Additives** — fillers (CaCO₃, alumina trihydrate for fire retardance), pigments, UV stabilizers, low-profile additives

### **Stage 4 — Reinforcement & Resin Impregnation**
- **Why?** This is the heart of the process — depositing fiber architecture and wetting it out.
- **How?**
  1. Place dry **reinforcement** (chopped strand mat, woven roving, unidirectional fabric, or combinations) onto the gel-coated mold.
  2. Pour / brush catalyzed resin over the reinforcement.
  3. **Hand-roll** using a **paddle roller (split roller, finned roller, or disc roller)** to:
     - Wet-out the fibers completely,
     - Consolidate the laminate (remove entrapped air),
     - Achieve a uniform resin distribution.

### **Stage 5 — Layer Build-Up (Multi-Ply Stacking)**
- **Why?** Engineering properties require specific thickness and stacking sequence.
- **How?** Repeat Stage 4 for each ply. Typical lay-up sequences: **[0/90]**, **[0/±45/90]**, **quasi-isotropic [0/45/–45/90]s**.

### **Stage 6 — Curing**
- **Why?** Cross-linking of the thermoset matrix to a rigid 3D network.
- **How?**
  - **Room-temperature cure:** 24–48 hours for polyester (exotherm ~120–150°C).
  - **Elevated-temperature cure (post-cure):** 2–4 hours at **60–80°C** to reach full conversion and improve Tg (glass transition temperature).
  - Exothermic heat must be controlled to avoid thermal cracking in thick laminates (>10 mm).

### **Stage 7 — Demolding & Trimming**
- **Why?** Final part extraction.
- **How?** Carefully release the cured part from the mold. Trim flash/edges using **diamond or carbide cutters**.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Formulas for Hand Lay-up Problems**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $V_f = \dfrac{w_f / \rho_f}{w_f/\rho_f + w_m/\rho_m}$ | **Fiber volume fraction** from weight and density | Dimensionless (×100 for %) |
| 2 | $V_m = 1 - V_f$ | **Matrix volume fraction** | Dimensionless |
| 3 | $W_c = W_f + W_m$ | **Composite weight** = fiber + matrix weight | kg or g |
| 4 | $t_{\text{lam}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} t_i$ | **Laminate thickness** = sum of ply thicknesses | mm |
| 5 | $R_c = \dfrac{W - W_f}{W} \times 100$ | **Resin content by weight (%)** | % |
| 6 | $V_v = 1 - (V_f + V_m)$ | **Void volume fraction** | Dimensionless |
| 7 | $P_{\text{cons}} = P_{atm} + P_{\text{roller}}$ | **Consolidation pressure** (atm + manual roller) | kPa |
| 8 | $n_{\text{ply}} = \dfrac{t_{\text{required}}}{t_{\text{ply}}}$ | **Number of plies required** for a given thickness | Integer (round up) |
| 9 | $\rho_c = \rho_f V_f + \rho_m (1-V_f)$ | **Rule of Mixtures for density** | g/cm³ |
| 10 | $\eta_{\text{wet}} = \dfrac{t_{\text{actual}}}{t_{\text{ideal}}} \times 100$ | **Wet-out quality index** | % |

> **Key notations:** $V_f$ = fiber volume fraction, $V_m$ = matrix volume fraction, $V_v$ = void fraction, $w_f$ = fiber weight, $w_m$ = matrix weight, $\rho_f$ = fiber density (e.g., E-glass = **2.55 g/cm³**), $\rho_m$ = matrix density (polyester ≈ **1.20 g/cm³**), $W$ = total composite weight.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Hand lay-up is **dominant** in:
- **Marine:** Boat hulls, decks, superstructures (≈ 60% of marine composites globally use hand lay-up).
- **Wind energy:** Small wind turbine blades.
- **Construction:** Architectural cladding, FRP roofing, dome structures.
- **Transportation:** Truck/van panels, interior components.
- **Aerospace:** Restricted to non-structural cabin parts and prototypes.

> **Why does industry still use it despite low V_f and high voids?** Because **capex is minimal** (a wooden mold can cost < ₹50,000), **tooling lead-time is days not months**, and **part sizes up to 50 m** (e.g., boat hulls) can be made without huge presses.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Fiber Volume Fraction from Constituent Weights

> **Problem Setup:** A hand lay-up laminate is fabricated with **250 g of E-glass fabric** ($\rho_f = 2.55$ g/cm³) and **180 g of unsaturated polyester resin** ($\rho_m = 1.20$ g/cm³). Find the **fiber volume fraction** and **resin volume fraction**.

### **Step 1 — Calculate the volume of fibers**

The volume of any constituent is mass divided by density:

$$
V_f^{\text{vol}} = \frac{w_f}{\rho_f}
$$

Substituting the values:

$$
V_f^{\text{vol}} = \frac{250 \text{ g}}{2.55 \text{ g/cm}^3}
$$

$$
V_f^{\text{vol}} = 98.04 \text{ cm}^3
$$

> **Logic:** The mass of 250 g of E-glass, whose molecules are tightly packed (high density 2.55), occupies a relatively small volume because glass is dense.

### **Step 2 — Calculate the volume of the matrix**

Similarly, for the unsaturated polyester resin:

$$
V_m^{\text{vol}} = \frac{w_m}{\rho_m}
$$

$$
V_m^{\text{vol}} = \frac{180 \text{ g}}{1.20 \text{ g/cm}^3}
$$

$$
V_m^{\text{vol}} = 150.00 \text{ cm}^3
$$

> **Logic:** The same mass of polyester (lower density 1.20) occupies a *larger* volume than glass because the polymer chains are loosely packed.

### **Step 3 — Apply the fiber volume fraction definition**

The fiber volume fraction is the ratio of the fiber's volume to the total volume of the composite (assuming negligible voids for this ideal case):

$$
V_f = \frac{V_f^{\text{vol}}}{V_f^{\text{vol}} + V_m^{\text{vol}}}
$$

Substituting the computed volumes:

$$
V_f = \frac{98.04}{98.04 + 150.00}
$$

$$
V_f = \frac{98.04}{248.04}
$$

$$
\boxed{V_f = 0.3953 \text{ or } 39.53\%}
$$

### **Step 4 — Determine the matrix volume fraction**

$$
V_m = 1 - V_f
$$

$$
V_m = 1 - 0.3953
$$

$$
\boxed{V_m = 0.6047 \text{ or } 60.47\%}
$$

### **Step 5 — Engineering Interpretation**

> [!NOTE]
> - A $V_f \approx 40\%$ is **typical and acceptable** for hand lay-up (industry benchmark: 20–35%; well-consolidated hand lay-up can reach ~40%).
> - If we got $V_f < 25\%$, it indicates **excess resin** (wasteful, weaker laminate).
> - If $V_f > 50\%$, the laminate is **resin-starved** — dry spots, poor stress transfer, low mechanical strength.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Number of Plies and Final Laminate Thickness

> **Problem Setup:** A boat hull panel must be **6.0 mm thick**. Each ply of woven roving has an areal weight of **600 g/m²** and fiber density **2.55 g/cm³**. Calculate the **number of plies required** and the **actual laminate thickness** after cure (assume each ply consolidates to its theoretical thickness; resin adds 30% by volume of ply volume).

### **Step 1 — Calculate the dry fiber thickness per ply**

The thickness contributed by fiber alone in one ply is given by mass-per-unit-area divided by density:

$$
t_f = \frac{\text{areal weight}}{\rho_f} = \frac{w_a}{\rho_f}
$$

$$
t_f = \frac{600 \text{ g/m}^2}{2.55 \text{ g/cm}^3}
$$

We must convert units consistently. Convert areal weight to g/cm²:

$$
w_a = 600 \text{ g/m}^2 = 600 \times 10^{-4} \text{ g/cm}^2 = 0.0600 \text{ g/cm}^2
$$

Then:

$$
t_f = \frac{0.0600 \text{ g/cm}^2}{2.55 \text{ g/cm}^3}
$$

$$
t_f = 0.02353 \text{ cm} = 0.2353 \text{ mm}
$$

### **Step 2 — Add resin contribution (30% extra volume)**

The resin adds 30% of the fiber's volume to each ply's thickness. So per-ply thickness:

$$
t_{\text{ply}} = t_f \times (1 + 0.30)
$$

$$
t_{\text{ply}} = 0.2353 \times 1.30
$$

$$
t_{\text{ply}} = 0.3059 \text{ mm}
$$

### **Step 3 — Calculate the number of plies required**

$$
n_{\text{ply}} = \frac{t_{\text{required}}}{t_{\text{ply}}}
$$

$$
n_{\text{ply}} = \frac{6.0 \text{ mm}}{0.3059 \text{ mm/ply}}
$$

$$
n_{\text{ply}} = 19.61
$$

Since we cannot lay a fractional ply:

$$
\boxed{n_{\text{ply}} = 20 \text{ plies (rounded up)}}
$$

### **Step 4 — Calculate the actual laminate thickness**

$$
t_{\text{actual}} = n_{\text{ply}} \times t_{\text{ply}}
$$

$$
t_{\text{actual}} = 20 \times 0.3059
$$

$$
\boxed{t_{\text{actual}} = 6.118 \text{ mm}}
$$

> **Engineering check:** Thickness overshoot = (6.118 − 6.0)/6.0 × 100 = **+1.97%** → acceptable; well within KTU-mandated ±10% tolerance for hand lay-up parts.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Advantages vs. Disadvantages of Hand Lay-up

| **Advantages** | **Disadvantages** |
|---|---|
| Lowest capital investment (molds can be wood) | **Low and inconsistent fiber volume fraction** (V_f = 20–35%) |
| Simple, low-skill operation; easy to learn | **High void content** (1–5%) → fatigue-prone |
| Virtually unlimited part size (limited only by workshop) | **Labor-intensive** → high cycle time, high cost at scale |
| Wide resin/fiber compatibility (polyester, vinyl ester, epoxy) | **Styrene emissions** (open mold) — health & environmental hazard |
| Fast prototyping, design changes are easy | **Quality depends on operator skill** — not reproducible |
| Both small and large production runs are economical | **Only one mold face is smooth** — outer surface quality is the gel-coat |
| No pressure or vacuum equipment needed | **Thick laminates** suffer exotherm runaway & thermal cracking |
| Low tooling lead time (days) | **Limited to thermosets** with sufficient pot life |

> [!NOTE]
> **Comparison hooks for the exam:**
> - Hand lay-up has **lower V_f** than **vacuum bag molding** (40–50%) and **autoclave** (55–65%).
> - Hand lay-up has **higher void content** than **resin transfer molding (RTM)** (<1% voids).
> - Hand lay-up is **slower** than **spray-up** (which uses a chopped-fiber + resin spray gun).

---

## 3.4 Process Variables That KTU Examiners Love to Test

| Variable | Effect on Laminate |
|---|---|
| ↑ **Resin viscosity** | Poor wet-out → ↑ voids, ↓ strength |
| ↑ **Roller pressure** | Better consolidation → ↓ voids (up to a limit) |
| ↑ **Cure temperature** | Faster cure, ↑ Tg, ↑ exotherm risk |
| ↑ **Ply stacking time** | Risk of "delamination" between cured and wet plies |
| ↑ **Fiber volume fraction** | ↑ Strength and stiffness, ↓ resin-dominated properties (e.g., impact) |
| ↑ **Accelerator % (Co-naphthenate)** | Shortens gel time, risk of premature cure |
| **Wrong release agent** | Part sticks to mold → rejection |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->


# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**"List any three advantages and three disadvantages of the hand lay-up process for FRP composites."** (3 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

> **Advantages:**
> 1. **Low capital investment** — molds can be made of wood; no press or autoclave needed.
> 2. **Large and complex parts** can be made with very little tooling cost.
> 3. **Design flexibility** — fiber architecture and thickness can be changed easily during lay-up.
>
> **Disadvantages:**
> 1. **Low and inconsistent fiber volume fraction** (V_f typically 20–35%).
> 2. **High void content** (1–5%) due to lack of external pressure → poor fatigue life.
> 3. **Labor-intensive and operator-dependent** — quality varies with skill.
>
> *Examiner's note:* 1 mark per correctly listed advantage/disadvantage. Pair one advantage with one disadvantage to balance — full marks awarded for a complete, balanced answer.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**"Define gel-coat. Why is it applied in the hand lay-up process?"** (3 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

> **Definition:** A gel-coat is a **pigmented, thixotropic, thermosetting resin layer (typically 0.4–0.6 mm thick) applied to the mold surface before the reinforcement**, which forms the outer aesthetic and protective skin of the FRP part.
>
> **Reasons for application:**
> 1. Provides a **smooth, Class-A finish** that masks the fibrous texture of the laminate.
> 2. Acts as a **UV barrier and weather shield** to protect the structural laminate.
> 3. Provides **chemical and water resistance** to the surface, prolonging part life.
>
> *Examiner's note:* 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for reasons. Students commonly lose marks by confusing gel-coat with paint or top-coat — emphasize that gel-coat is *integral* to the laminate (it is the *outer layer of the same laminate*).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks) — Internal Choice

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Path 1**

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme, Module 3]**
>
> **(a)** Explain in detail the **hand lay-up process** for the manufacture of **fiber-reinforced polymer (FRP) composites**, with a neat process flow diagram. Discuss the **role of release agents, gel-coats, and the hand roller** in achieving a quality laminate. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** A hand lay-up laminate is made with **300 g of E-glass woven roving** ($\rho_f = 2.55$ g/cm³) and **220 g of unsaturated polyester resin** ($\rho_m = 1.20$ g/cm³). After cure, the laminate shows a **measured void content of 3%**. Calculate:
> (i) Fiber volume fraction
> (ii) Matrix volume fraction
> (iii) Theoretical density of the composite (rule of mixtures)
> (iv) Actual density of the laminate including voids. **(7 Marks)**

#### **Model Solution to Q-A (a):**

**1. Hand Lay-up Process — Stepwise Description:**

The hand lay-up (open-mold) process is a **manual composite fabrication method** in which dry reinforcement is laid onto a release-agent-treated mold, wetted out with liquid catalyzed thermoset resin, consolidated by hand rolling, and cured at room or elevated temperature.

**Process flow:**

$$
\text{Mold prep} \rightarrow \text{Release agent} \rightarrow \text{Gel coat} \rightarrow \text{Resin mixing} \rightarrow \text{Reinforcement placement} \rightarrow \text{Impregnation} \rightarrow \text{Consolidation} \rightarrow \text{Curing} \rightarrow \text{Demolding} \rightarrow \text{Trimming}
$$

**2. Role of Release Agent:**

A release agent (wax, PVA, or silicone spray) is applied in 2–3 thin buffed coats to the mold surface. Its role is to:
- Form a **chemically inert barrier film** between the mold and the gel-coat/laminate.
- Allow the **clean release of the cured part** without surface damage.
- Prevent **mold fouling**, extending mold life and reducing surface preparation time.

**3. Role of Gel-Coat:**

A gel-coat is a **0.4–0.6 mm thick, pigmented, filled, thixotropic resin layer** applied to the release-agent-treated mold and allowed to **B-stage (partially cure)** before the structural laminate is built on it. Its roles are:
- Provide a **smooth, Class-A aesthetic outer surface** (masks fiber print-through).
- Serve as the **first line of defense against UV, moisture, and chemicals**.
- Hide **fiber-texture** of the structural laminate.
- Provide a **pigmented color** integral to the laminate (won't chip or peel like paint).

**4. Role of Hand Roller (Paddle / Disc / Finned Roller):**

After the reinforcement is placed and resin is poured, the hand roller is the **only consolidation tool** in hand lay-up. Its roles are:
- **Impregnation:** Drives resin into the dry fiber bundles (wet-out).
- **Air removal:** Expels trapped air bubbles — directly reducing void content.
- **Consolidation:** Compacts the ply to its target thickness.
- **Resin distribution:** Ensures uniform resin content across the ply.
- **Fiber positioning:** Maintains fiber orientation and prevents bunching/wrinkling.

**5. Schematic Diagram:**

*(See Section 4 of these notes for the complete Mermaid process flow diagram.)*

#### **Model Solution to Q-A (b):**

**Given:** $w_f = 300$ g, $\rho_f = 2.55$ g/cm³, $w_m = 220$ g, $\rho_m = 1.20$ g/cm³, $V_v = 0.03$

**Step 1 — Volume of fibers:**

$$
V_f^{\text{vol}} = \frac{w_f}{\rho_f} = \frac{300}{2.55} = 117.65 \text{ cm}^3
$$

**Step 2 — Volume of matrix:**

$$
V_m^{\text{vol}} = \frac{w_m}{\rho_m} = \frac{220}{1.20} = 183.33 \text{ cm}^3
$$

**Step 3 — Total solid (fiber + matrix) volume:**

$$
V_{s} = V_f^{\text{vol}} + V_m^{\text{vol}} = 117.65 + 183.33 = 300.98 \text{ cm}^3
$$

**Step 4 — Fiber volume fraction** (relative to the solid composite, i.e., the *true composite* ignoring voids):

$$
V_f = \frac{V_f^{\text{vol}}}{V_{s}} = \frac{117.65}{300.98}
$$

$$
\boxed{V_f = 0.3909 \text{ or } 39.09\%}
$$

**Step 5 — Matrix volume fraction:**

$$
V_m = 1 - V_f = 1 - 0.3909
$$

$$
\boxed{V_m = 0.6091 \text{ or } 60.91\%}
$$

**Step 6 — Theoretical density of composite (Rule of Mixtures):**

$$
\rho_{c,\text{theo}} = \rho_f V_f + \rho_m V_m
$$

$$
\rho_{c,\text{theo}} = (2.55 \times 0.3909) + (1.20 \times 0.6091)
$$

$$
\rho_{c,\text{theo}} = 0.9968 + 0.7309
$$

$$
\boxed{\rho_{c,\text{theo}} = 1.728 \text{ g/cm}^3}
$$

**Step 7 — Actual density including 3% voids:**

Total composite volume (including voids) = $V_s / (1 - V_v) = 300.98 / 0.97 = 310.29$ cm³

$$
\rho_{c,\text{actual}} = \frac{W_c}{V_{\text{total}}} = \frac{w_f + w_m}{V_{\text{total}}}
$$

$$
W_c = 300 + 220 = 520 \text{ g}
$$

$$
\rho_{c,\text{actual}} = \frac{520}{310.29}
$$

$$
\boxed{\rho_{c,\text{actual}} = 1.676 \text{ g/cm}^3}
$$

> **Cross-check:** Actual < Theoretical, as expected (voids reduce density). Difference = (1.728 − 1.676)/1.728 × 100 = **3.0%** ✓ (matches V_v input).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Path 2 (Alternative Choice)**

> **[KTU University Exam – Supplementary Exam, KTU 2024 Scheme, Module 3]**
>
> **(a)** Compare the **hand lay-up process** with **(i) spray-up** and **(ii) vacuum bag molding**, with respect to V_f, void content, surface finish, tooling cost, and production rate. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** Describe in detail the **raw materials used in hand lay-up** — covering **resins, reinforcements, catalysts, accelerators, fillers, and release agents**. For each, give the typical composition, function, and any standard example. **(7 Marks)**

#### **Model Solution to Q-B (a):**

| **Parameter** | **Hand Lay-up** | **Spray-up** | **Vacuum Bag Molding** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Fiber volume fraction (V_f)** | 20 – 35% | 15 – 30% | 40 – 50% |
| **Void content** | 1 – 5% | 3 – 8% | 0.5 – 2% |
| **Surface finish (Class-A)** | Excellent (gel-coat) | Fair (gel-coat) | Excellent (gel-coat + bag side) |
| **Tooling cost** | Very low (wood) | Very low (wood) | Moderate (rigid mold + bagging films) |
| **Production rate** | Slow (manual) | Faster (chopper gun) | Slower (lay-up + bagging) |
| **Labor skill** | Moderate | Moderate | Higher (vacuum integrity) |
| **Capital equipment** | None | Chopper gun + pump | Vacuum pump + bagging consumables |
| **Part size limit** | Virtually unlimited | Limited by gun reach | Limited by bag + oven size |
| **Resin type** | Polyester, vinyl ester, epoxy | Polyester (most common) | Epoxy (prepreg), polyester |

**Key takeaways:**
- **Spray-up** is faster than hand lay-up but produces *more waste* (overspray) and *lower V_f*.
- **Vacuum bag molding** applies ~101.325 kPa of consolidation pressure via atmospheric pressure (after vacuum is drawn), improving V_f and reducing voids — but needs consumables (breather, release film, sealant tape, vacuum pump).

#### **Model Solution to Q-B (b):**

| **Raw Material Class** | **Typical Examples** | **Function** | **Typical Composition / Proportion** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Thermoset Resin** | Unsaturated Polyester, Vinyl Ester, Epoxy, Phenolic | Binds reinforcement, transfers load, provides shape and environmental resistance | 30 – 60% by weight of laminate |
| **Catalyst / Hardener** | MEKP, BPO, TBPB (for poly/vinyl ester); Amine, Anhydride (for epoxy) | Initiates cross-linking reaction | 1 – 2% by weight of resin |
| **Accelerator** | Cobalt Naphthenate, DMA (for poly/vinyl ester room-temp cure) | Lowers decomposition temperature of catalyst → enables RT cure | 0.5 – 2% by weight of resin |
| **Reinforcement** | E-glass, S-glass, Carbon, Aramid (Kevlar), Natural fibers | Carries load, provides stiffness and strength | 40 – 70% by weight of laminate |
| **Filler** | CaCO₃, ATH, Silica, Talc | Reduces cost, controls shrinkage, fire retardance, thixotropy | 5 – 30% by weight of resin |
| **Pigment** | TiO₂ (white), Carbon black, Iron oxides | Provides color in gel-coat or bulk | 1 – 4% by weight of resin |
| **Release Agent** | Wax (carnauba, montan), PVA, Silicone spray | Prevents bonding of cured laminate to mold | 2 – 3 buffed coats |
| **Low-Profile Additive (LPA)** | Polyvinyl acetate (PVAc), Polyurethane | Reduces part shrinkage during cure, improves surface | 5 – 10% by weight of resin |
| **UV Stabilizer** | HALS, Benzophenones | Prevents UV degradation of polyester gel-coat | 0.5 – 1% by weight of gel-coat |

> [!NOTE]
> **Cross-link insight:** The most common resin system in hand lay-up is **general-purpose orthophthalic polyester resin + 1% MEKP catalyst + 1% cobalt naphthenate accelerator**, with E-glass chopped strand mat (CSM) as reinforcement. This system has a **gel time of 15–30 minutes at 25°C**, giving the lay-up operator a comfortable working window.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls in Hand Lay-up Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing catalyst and accelerator:** Students often swap these. **Mnemonic:** *Catalyst* = MEKP (initiates the cure); *Accelerator* = Cobalt Naphthenate (lets the catalyst work at room temperature). You need **both** for room-temp cure of polyester.
> 2. **Forgetting to add voids in density calculation:** A common trap. If V_v is given, the *actual* density will be **lower** than the *theoretical* (rule-of-mixtures) density. Always state the assumption (voids = 0 or = given value).
> 3. **Rounding ply numbers DOWN:** If you need 19.61 plies, the answer is **20 plies**, not 19. The 20th ply gives an over-thickness of <2%, which is acceptable. A 19-ply lay-up is **under-thickness** and fails the design.
> 4. **Calling gel-coat "paint":** Gel-coat is *structural-integral*. It is a thermoset resin layer, not a coating. Examiners dock 1 mark for this confusion.
> 5. **Skipping the diagram in 7-mark questions:** Even a simple block diagram of the process flow earns 1–2 marks easily. Always include a labeled flow diagram.
> 6. **Confusing V_f and weight fraction (W_f):** V_f uses *volumes* (from densities), W_f uses *weights* directly. V_f is what gives mechanical strength; W_f alone is misleading.
> 7. **Forgetting the demolding step:** The process is incomplete without demolding + trimming. Examiners look for the *full* cycle.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hand Lay-up — Rapid Revision Checklist**

- ✅ **Hand lay-up is an open-mold, manual, room-temperature (or mild-oven cure) process** — the simplest and oldest FRP fabrication method.
- ✅ **No external pressure** is applied; consolidation relies on **atmospheric pressure (~101.325 kPa) + hand roller force**.
- ✅ **7-stage process:** mold prep → release agent → gel-coat → resin mixing → reinforcement & impregnation → curing → demolding/trimming.
- ✅ **Typical V_f = 20–35%**; **V_v = 1–5%** — both *lower* than vacuum bag and autoclave processes.
- ✅ **Most common resin:** Unsaturated Polyester (UP) + MEKP catalyst + Cobalt Naphthenate accelerator.
- ✅ **Most common reinforcement:** E-glass Chopped Strand Mat (CSM) + Woven Roving.
- ✅ **Mold materials:** Wood (cheapest), FRP, aluminum, or steel — female mold gives smooth outer surface, male mold gives smooth inner surface.
- ✅ **Hand roller is the only consolidation tool** — it serves 4 purposes: wet-out, air removal, ply compaction, and resin distribution.
- ✅ **Fiber volume fraction formula** (most-tested in KTU exams):
  $$V_f = \frac{w_f / \rho_f}{w_f/\rho_f + w_m/\rho_m}$$
- ✅ **Resin content formula** (frequently asked):
  $$R_c = \frac{W - W_f}{W} \times 100\%$$
- ✅ **Rule of mixtures for theoretical density:**
  $$\rho_c = \rho_f V_f + \rho_m (1 - V_f)$$
- ✅ **Major applications:** Marine (boat hulls), construction (cladding, domes), transportation (truck panels), wind turbine blades, prototypes.
- ✅ **Major limitations:** High voids, low V_f, labor-intensive, styrene emissions, operator-dependent quality, only one smooth surface (the gel-coat side).
- ✅ **Comparison hook:** Hand lay-up < Vacuum bag < Autoclave in terms of V_f, void quality, and mechanical performance (hand lay-up is *bottom* of this hierarchy).
- ✅ **Disposal/removal:** The cured part is *manually* demolded using wedges/wooden pry bars (no ejector pins in wooden molds).
- ✅ **Styrene emission** from open-mold polyester lay-up is a regulated OH&S hazard — modern facilities use closed-mold alternatives (RTM, infusion) to comply with emission norms.
- ✅ **The hand lay-up process can produce parts from a few mm thick (repair patches) up to ~50 m long (large boat hulls)** — its scalability is unique among composite processes.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Spray-up
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT412 - COMPOSITE MATERIALS AND CERAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Spray-up

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal KTU-Syllabus Definition

**Spray-up** (also called **Spray Lay-up** or **Chopped-Fiber Spray Lay-up**) is an **open-mold, low-pressure, semi-automated composite manufacturing process** in which **continuous reinforcing fibers (typically glass rovings) are fed through a chopping device, cut into short fibers (length 10–40 mm), and projected simultaneously with a stream of catalysed liquid thermosetting resin** onto a prepared mold surface. The deposited mass is then **consolidated and compacted using hand rollers** to remove entrapped air, wet-out the fibers, and achieve the required laminate thickness before curing at room (or elevated) temperature.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Expectation):**
> *"Spray-up is a fabrication process for fiber-reinforced plastics in which a gun chops continuous rovings into short fibers and simultaneously sprays them with a resin-catalyst mix onto an open mold, which is then rolled and cured."*

> [!NOTE]
> **Classification Snapshot (Board-Exam Favourite):**
> - **Process Family:** Open-mold / Contact molding
> - **Reinforcement Form:** Chopped short fibers (discontinuous, random in-plane orientation)
> - **Matrix:** Unsaturated Polyester, Vinyl Ester, Epoxy (epoxy less common due to short pot-life)
> - **Cure Type:** Room-temperature cure with post-cure optional
> - **Production Volume:** Low–Medium
> - **Part Size:** Medium to very large (boat hulls, truck bodies, bathtubs)

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Paint-Sprayer of Composites"

> [!TIP]
> **Imagine painting a wall with a paint sprayer**, but instead of spraying **only coloured liquid**, you are spraying **two things at the same time** through a single hose:
> 1. **Tiny chopped straw pieces (chopped glass fibers)** to act as the "skeleton" giving strength, and
> 2. **A sticky glue (catalysed polyester resin)** that hardens and binds the straws into a solid board.
>
> Once the wet paste is on the wall, you take a **roller (the same one used to remove wallpaper bubbles)** and press it flat to squeeze out air bubbles and pack everything tight. After some time, the glue hardens and you have a **strong, lightweight, fibre-reinforced board** — that is exactly what happens in **Spray-up**, except the "wall" is a **3D mould shaped like a boat hull, tub, or truck cabin**.

## 1.3 Key Performance Metrics (KTU Board-Essential Numerics)

| Parameter | Typical Industrial Value |
|---|---|
| Fiber content by weight ($w_f$) | **25 – 35 %** (lower than hand lay-up) |
| Void content | **3 – 8 %** (higher than hand lay-up) |
| Chopped fiber length | **10 – 40 mm** (commonly 25 mm) |
| Resin spray rate | **0.5 – 4 kg/min** |
| Glass roving throughput | **0.5 – 6 kg/min** |
| Typical laminate thickness | **2 – 6 mm** per pass |
| Production rate | **5 – 15 kg/h** per operator |
| Mold material | FRP, wood, plaster, sheet metal |

> [!WARNING]
> **Board Pitfall:** Students often confuse **Spray-up** with **Hand Lay-up**. The single key distinguishing feature is the **mechanical chopping & simultaneous projection of fibers via a spray gun**. In hand lay-up, fibers are pre-cut mats laid manually; in spray-up, the gun does both cutting and deposition in one continuous action.

# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Process Breakdown — The Six Sequential Logical Steps

> [!NOTE]
> The following step-by-step logic mirrors the KTU marker's expectation for a **"Describe the spray-up process" (14-mark)** question.

1. **Mold Preparation**
   - The open mold (male or female) is cleaned, waxed with a **release agent (mold wax / PVA)**, and a **gel-coat** (pigmented resin, 0.3 – 0.5 mm) is brush/spray applied to give a smooth aesthetic surface and protect fibers from UV/moisture.

2. **Resin–Catalyst Metering & Mixing**
   - Liquid polyester resin is mixed with a **catalyst (MEKP — Methyl Ethyl Ketone Peroxide)** in a static mixer just before the spray nozzle. An **accelerator (cobalt naphthenate)** is pre-blended in the resin to enable room-temperature cure.

3. **Fiber Roving Feeding**
   - Multiple spools (creels) of **continuous E-glass rovings** are drawn into the chopper/spray gun. The number of rovings (typically **6 – 12 strands**) controls the deposition rate.

4. **Chopping & Simultaneous Spray**
   - Inside the gun, a **chopper blade rotating at ~3000–5000 rpm** cuts rovings into short fibers which are entrained in a stream of atomised resin and propelled by compressed air (or in airless systems, by hydraulic pressure) onto the mold.

5. **Roller Consolidation (Degassing & Compaction)**
   - A trained operator immediately rolls the wet lay-up with a **metal or grooved plastic roller** to:
     - Wet-out fibers fully
     - Remove entrapped air (degassing)
     - Compact the laminate to design thickness
     - Distribute resin uniformly

6. **Curing & Demolding**
   - Cure occurs at **room temperature (exothermic reaction peaks at 80 – 120 °C)**. Demolding takes place after **1 – 24 hours**, followed by **post-cure at 60 – 80 °C for 2 – 4 h** for full cross-linking and optimal mechanical properties.

## 2.2 Equipment Architecture

| Equipment | Function |
|---|---|
| **Resin Bath / Drum** | Stores catalysed resin; pressurized by air |
| **Roving Creels** | Hold multiple spools of glass roving |
| **Chopper/Spray Gun** | Cuts rovings + atomises + projects resin |
| **Air Compressor (2–5 bar)** | Powers atomisation & resin pressure |
| **Static Mixer** | Homogenises resin + catalyst just before exit |
| **Consolidation Rollers** | Hand tool: ribbed metal or Teflon roller |
| **Mold** | Open, single-sided, FRP/wood/metal |
| **Exhaust Ventilation** | Removes styrene vapour (toxic) |
| **Personal Protective Equipment (PPE)** | Respirators, gloves, eye protection |

## 2.3 The "Why" — Process Physics and Chemistry

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why short, randomly-oriented chopped fibers?**
> - Continuous fibers are difficult to drape over complex 3-D surfaces.
> - Chopping the fibers allows the spray to **conform to any mold geometry**, even doubly-curved boat hulls.
> - Random in-plane orientation gives **quasi-isotropic in-plane properties** — adequate for many non-aerospace load cases.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why simultaneous resin + fiber deposition?**
> - Eliminates the slow step of manually laying each mat.
> - Allows very **high deposition rates (~5–15 kg/h)** of laminate mass.
> - Reduces labour cost dramatically versus hand lay-up.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are **direct exam-relevant** for numerical or descriptive parts of a Module-3 question on Spray-up.

| # | Formula / Relation | Symbol Meaning | Units | Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $V_f + V_m + V_v = 1$ | Fiber, matrix, void volume fractions | — | **Rule of Mixtures (volumetric)** — basis of composite property prediction |
| 2 | $w_f = \dfrac{\rho_f V_f}{\rho_f V_f + \rho_m V_m}$ | Weight fraction of fiber | — | Convert $V_f \leftrightarrow w_f$ |
| 3 | $E_c = E_f V_f + E_m V_m$ | Rule of mixtures (longitudinal modulus, iso-strain) | GPa | **Voigt Model** — upper bound modulus |
| 4 | $\dfrac{1}{E_c} = \dfrac{V_f}{E_f} + \dfrac{V_m}{E_m}$ | Inverse rule of mixtures (transverse modulus) | GPa | **Reuss Model** — lower bound modulus |
| 5 | $t_{cured} = \dfrac{m}{\rho_c A}$ | Cured laminate thickness from deposited mass | mm | Production planning |
| 6 | $N_f = \dfrac{Q_{roving}}{L_{chop}}$ | Number of fibers per spray pass | fibers/min | Chopper throughput design |
| 7 | $\rho_c = \rho_f V_f + \rho_m V_m$ | Composite density | g/cm³ | Weight estimation |
| 8 | $\sigma_{cu} = \sigma_{mu} \cdot \left(1 - V_f^{1/2}\right)$ | Modified Kelly-Tyson — strength of aligned short-fiber composite | MPa | Predicts strength reduction due to short fibers |
| 9 | $l_c = \dfrac{\sigma_{fu} \cdot d}{2 \tau_i}$ | Critical fiber length for full load transfer | mm | Explains why $L_{chop} \geq 10\,l_c$ is required |
| 10 | $T_{peak} = T_{amb} + \dfrac{\Delta H \cdot m}{m \cdot C_p}$ | Exotherm peak temperature | °C | Avoid thermal degradation in thick laminates |

> [!CAUTION]
> **KTU Table-Writing Rule:** When writing the rule of mixtures in a table row, use the expression as shown above — do **NOT** use vertical pipes like `|V_f|` inside a markdown table row, as it breaks the table layout. Use `\vert` if you need absolute value: $\vert E_f \vert$.

## 2.5 Engineering Utility & Real-World Applications

> [!TIP]
> - **Marine:** Hulls, decks, and small boats (one of the largest spray-up markets globally).
> - **Automotive:** Truck cabs, bumpers, fenders, body panels.
> - **Construction:** Bath tubs, shower trays, swimming pools, roof panels, ducts.
> - **Sanitaryware:** Sinks, wash basins, toilet cabins.
> - **Industrial:** Chemical storage tanks, fume hoods, satellite dish housings.
> - **Wind Energy:** Small-scale nacome covers and housings (large blades use resin infusion, not spray-up).

## 2.6 Comparative Analysis — Spray-up vs Hand Lay-up (Board Favourite)

| Attribute | Hand Lay-up | Spray-up |
|---|---|---|
| Fiber form | Woven mat / fabric | Chopped short fibers |
| Fiber length | Continuous in mat | **10–40 mm (discontinuous)** |
| Fiber volume fraction | 30–45 % | **20–35 %** (lower) |
| Void content | 1–3 % | **3–8 %** (higher) |
| Production rate | 1–3 kg/h | **5–15 kg/h** (faster) |
| Labour intensity | Very high | Medium |
| Equipment cost | Very low | Moderate (gun, compressor) |
| Mechanical properties | Better | **Lower** (random short fibers) |
| Part complexity | Limited by mat drapability | **Very high (3-D friendly)** |
| Health hazard | Styrene exposure | **Higher** (aerosol of resin + fiber) |
| Skill required | High craftsmanship | Moderate (gun operation) |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Board Error:** A common student mistake is writing *"Spray-up gives better mechanical properties than hand lay-up"*. The correct answer is the **opposite** — hand lay-up with continuous woven mats gives **higher strength and stiffness** but at the cost of labour time.

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Volumetric Rule of Mixtures from First Principles

**Given:** A composite lamina of total volume $V_c$ containing three phases — fiber ($V_f$), matrix ($V_m$), and voids ($V_v$).

**To Prove:** $V_f + V_m + V_v = 1$ and $E_c = E_f V_f + E_m V_m$ (longitudinal, iso-strain case).

### Step 1 — Volume Additivity

By physical conservation of volume, the phases occupy the entire composite volume:

$$
V_c = V_f + V_m + V_v
$$

Dividing throughout by $V_c$:

$$
1 = \frac{V_f}{V_c} + \frac{V_m}{V_c} + \frac{V_v}{V_c}
$$

Define volume fractions:

$$
V_f \equiv \frac{V_f}{V_c},\quad V_m \equiv \frac{V_m}{V_c},\quad V_v \equiv \frac{V_v}{V_c}
$$

Hence,

$$
\boxed{V_f + V_m + V_v = 1}
$$

> *Textual note:* This is the **defining identity** for any fibre-reinforced composite and is the launching pad for all property prediction.

### Step 2 — Iso-Strain (Voigt) Assumption

When load $P_c$ is applied **parallel to fibers**, both fiber and matrix experience **the same strain** $\varepsilon$ (perfect bonding, no slip):

$$
\varepsilon_f = \varepsilon_m = \varepsilon_c = \varepsilon
$$

### Step 3 — Force Equilibrium

The composite carries the sum of fiber and matrix forces (load-sharing):

$$
P_c = P_f + P_m
$$

### Step 4 — Express in Stress and Area

$$
\sigma_c A_c = \sigma_f A_f + \sigma_m A_m
$$

### Step 5 — Introduce Volume Fractions ($A_i / A_c = V_i$ for uniform cross-section)

$$
\sigma_c = \sigma_f V_f + \sigma_m V_m
$$

### Step 6 — Use Hooke's Law ($\sigma = E \varepsilon$)

$$
E_c \varepsilon = E_f \varepsilon \cdot V_f + E_m \varepsilon \cdot V_m
$$

Cancel $\varepsilon$ (non-zero):

$$
\boxed{E_c = E_f V_f + E_m V_m}
$$

> *Textual note:* This is the **Voigt upper bound** for longitudinal stiffness, valid for **continuous aligned fibers**. In spray-up the fibers are **discontinuous and randomly oriented**, so experimentally observed $E_c$ lies **below** this value.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Critical Fiber Length $l_c$ (Why Spray-up Cuts Fibers Short)

**Given:** A single cylindrical fiber of diameter $d$ embedded in a matrix with interfacial shear strength $\tau_i$.

**To Find:** The minimum length over which the fiber can be loaded to its ultimate tensile stress $\sigma_{fu}$.

### Step 1 — Force Equilibrium on a Pulling Fiber

Maximum load the fiber can carry (fracture load):

$$
P_{fracture} = \sigma_{fu} \cdot A_f = \sigma_{fu} \cdot \frac{\pi d^2}{4}
$$

### Step 2 — Force Transferred by Shear at the Interface

The matrix grips the fiber along its embedded length $l$ on **both ends** (assuming fiber is being pulled from both sides). Total shear-transfer force:

$$
P_{shear} = \tau_i \cdot (\pi d) \cdot l
$$

(perimeter $\times$ embedded length $\times$ shear strength)

### Step 3 — Equate the Two (Full Load Transfer Condition)

For the fiber to be stressed to its full $\sigma_{fu}$:

$$
\sigma_{fu} \cdot \frac{\pi d^2}{4} = \tau_i \cdot \pi d \cdot l_c
$$

### Step 4 — Solve for $l_c$

Cancel $\pi d$ on both sides:

$$
\sigma_{fu} \cdot \frac{d}{4} = \tau_i \cdot l_c
$$

$$
\boxed{l_c = \frac{\sigma_{fu} \cdot d}{2 \, \tau_i}}
$$

> *Textual note:* For **E-glass / polyester**, $l_c \approx 1\text{–}3$ mm. Spray-up chops fibers to **$L \approx 25$ mm**, i.e. $L \gg l_c$, so the fibers can develop near-full strength. If $L < l_c$, the fiber pulls out of the matrix and only a fraction of its strength is used — drastically reducing composite strength.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Conversion Between Weight Fraction and Volume Fraction

**Given:** A composite with fiber weight $W_f$, matrix weight $W_m$, fiber density $\rho_f$, matrix density $\rho_m$.

**To Derive:** $w_f$ in terms of $V_f$.

### Step 1 — Weight Fractions

$$
w_f = \frac{W_f}{W_f + W_m}, \quad w_m = 1 - w_f
$$

### Step 2 — Express Weights via Densities and Volumes

$$
W_f = \rho_f V_f, \quad W_m = \rho_m V_m
$$

(per unit total composite volume $V_c = 1$)

### Step 3 — Substitute into $w_f$

$$
w_f = \frac{\rho_f V_f}{\rho_f V_f + \rho_m V_m} = \frac{\rho_f V_f}{\rho_f V_f + \rho_m (1 - V_f)}
$$

### Step 4 — Rationalise (multiply numerator and denominator by $1/\rho_f$)

$$
\boxed{w_f = \frac{V_f}{V_f + \frac{\rho_m}{\rho_f} (1 - V_f)}}
$$

> *Textual note:* This relation is **exam-relevant** for questions that give a target $V_f$ and ask the student to compute the resin/glass weight to be loaded into the chopper gun.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style 7-Mark Sub-Part)

**Problem:** A spray-up operation is scheduled to manufacture a boat hull panel of dimensions **2 m × 1.5 m** with a **target cured thickness of 4 mm**. The laminate is to have a **fiber volume fraction $V_f = 0.28$** using E-glass ($\rho_f = 2.55$ g/cm³) and polyester resin ($\rho_m = 1.20$ g/cm³). Calculate:
(a) the total composite mass, (b) the mass of glass required, (c) the mass of resin required.

### Step (a) — Total Composite Mass

$$
V_c = A \cdot t = 2.0 \times 1.5 \times 0.004 = 0.012 \; \text{m}^3
$$

Convert to cm³: $V_c = 0.012 \times 10^6 = 1.2 \times 10^4 \; \text{cm}^3$

Composite density:

$$
\rho_c = \rho_f V_f + \rho_m V_m = (2.55)(0.28) + (1.20)(0.72)
$$

$$
= 0.714 + 0.864 = 1.578 \; \text{g/cm}^3
$$

Total mass:

$$
\boxed{m_c = \rho_c \cdot V_c = 1.578 \times 1.2 \times 10^4 = 1.894 \times 10^4 \; \text{g} = 18.94 \; \text{kg}}
$$

### Step (b) — Mass of Glass

$$
W_f = \rho_f V_f \cdot V_c = 2.55 \times 0.28 \times 1.2 \times 10^4
$$

$$
\boxed{W_f = 8570 \; \text{g} = 8.57 \; \text{kg}}
$$

### Step (c) — Mass of Resin

$$
W_m = m_c - W_f = 18.94 - 8.57
$$

$$
\boxed{W_m = 10.37 \; \text{kg}}
$$

> *Textual note:* The **resin:glass weight ratio** = $10.37 / 8.57 \approx 1.21$ — a typical value for spray-up. The **operator must weigh-out 8.57 kg of glass rovings** on the creels and ensure that the resin pump delivers **10.37 kg** of catalysed resin for uniform $V_f$.
