# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**"Define the spray-up process for FRP composites. List any two raw materials used."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Spray-up is an **open-mold composite manufacturing process** in which **continuous glass rovings are chopped into short lengths (10–40 mm) inside a spray gun** and **simultaneously sprayed with catalysed thermosetting resin** onto a mold. The wet lay-up is then **rolled, compacted, and cured** to form a fibre-reinforced plastic part. **(2 marks)**

**Two raw materials:** **(1) Continuous E-glass roving**, and **(2) Unsaturated polyester resin with MEKP catalyst**. (Any two relevant raw materials accepted.) **(1 mark)**

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**"Mention any three advantages and any three limitations of the spray-up process."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Advantages:** **(i)** Very high deposition rate of laminate (5–15 kg/h); **(ii)** Capable of producing very large and complex 3-D parts in a single operation; **(iii)** Low tooling cost — open single-sided mold is sufficient. **(1½ marks)**

**Limitations:** **(i)** Lower mechanical strength than hand lay-up (chopped fibers, high voids); **(ii)** High styrene emission — health hazard; **(iii)** Operator-skill sensitive — quality varies with craftsmanship. **(1½ marks)**

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice Provided)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, May 2023 Adapted]**

**"Describe the spray-up process for fabrication of fiber-reinforced plastics in detail. Include the process steps, equipment, raw materials, process parameters, and comparison with hand lay-up."**

**Model Answer Outline:**

#### (a) **Process Steps, Equipment & Raw Materials** (7 marks)

1. **Mold prep** — clean, apply release agent, spray gel-coat, partial cure (1 mark)
2. **Catalyst-resin mixing** — static mixer at gun head, MEKP 1–2 %, cobalt accelerator pre-blended (1 mark)
3. **Roving creel** — 6–12 strands of E-glass fed into chopper (1 mark)
4. **Chop and spray** — chopper blade 3000–5000 rpm, atomisation by compressed air (2 bar) or airless hydraulics (1 mark)
5. **Roller consolidation** — metal/Teflon roller for wet-out, de-airing, compaction (1 mark)
6. **Cure and demold** — room-temperature exothermic cure; optional post-cure at 60–80 °C (1 mark)

#### (b) **Process Parameters & Comparison with Hand Lay-up** (7 marks)

**Process Parameters Table (4 marks):**

| Parameter | Typical Value |
|---|---|
| Chopped fiber length | 10–40 mm |
| Fiber volume fraction $V_f$ | 0.20–0.35 |
| Resin spray rate | 0.5–4 kg/min |
| Mold temperature | 25–40 °C |
| Cure temperature | 25 °C (RT) / 60–80 °C (post-cure) |
| Catalyst (MEKP) | 1–2 % by weight of resin |
| Void content | 3–8 % |

**Comparison Table (3 marks):** Already presented in **Section 2.6** of these notes. Student should reproduce key points: fiber form, $V_f$, production rate, properties, complexity, hazards.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — **[KTU University Exam — May 2022, Dec 2023 Adapted]**

**"Explain the spray-up process with a neat block diagram. Discuss the types of spray-up, common defects and their remedies, and industrial applications."**

#### (a) **Process Explanation with Block Diagram & Types** (7 marks)

- **Block diagram** (refer to Section 4.1 of these notes) — sequential flow from creel → chopper → gun → mold → roller → cure → demold. **(2 marks)**
- **Type 1 — Hand (Manual) Spray-up:** Operator holds gun, controls rate and thickness; common for prototypes and small batches. **(1.5 marks)**
- **Type 2 — Automated (Robotic) Spray-up:** 6-axis robot carries the gun on a programmed path; used for high-volume production of identical parts (e.g., truck cabs). Eliminates operator fatigue and styrene exposure. **(1.5 marks)**
- **Type 3 — Airless Spray-up:** Uses hydraulic pressure instead of compressed air; reduces overspray, VOC emission, and gives higher $V_f$. **(1 mark)**
- **Type 4 — Vacuum-Assisted Spray-up (VASPU):** Mold covered with vacuum bag after spray-up to reduce voids to <1 %. **(1 mark)**

#### (b) **Defects, Remedies & Applications** (7 marks)

**Defects (3 marks):** High voids, dry spots, resin-rich areas, fiber clustering, delamination, surface waviness, sink marks, microcracks, blistering, uneven thickness. *(Refer Section 4.4 for the defect tree.)*

**Remedies (2 marks):**
- Voids → Increase roller pressure, reduce resin viscosity
- Dry spots → Replace worn chopper blades, calibrate resin pump
- Resin-rich → Train operator, calibrate spray pattern
- Delamination → Clean mold between passes, control exotherm

**Industrial Applications (2 marks):** Marine (hulls, decks, kayaks), automotive (truck cabs, fenders, body panels), construction (bath tubs, swimming pools, roof panels), sanitaryware (sinks, wash basins), industrial (chemical tanks, ducts, fan housings).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **No diagram = −2 to −3 marks.** Always draw a **labelled block diagram** (creel → chopper → gun → mold → roller → cure).
> 2. **Confusing spray-up with hand lay-up** in the comparison — board examiners deduct a full mark for this.
> 3. **Forgetting to mention gel-coat** at the start of the process — gel-coat is a **mandatory first step** and its omission loses 1 mark.
> 4. **Not giving numeric values** for fiber length (10–40 mm), $V_f$ (20–35 %), and void content (3–8 %) — KTU examiners expect **quantified** answers.
> 5. **Skipping health & safety** (styrene, PPE, ventilation) — a small but recurring 1-mark deduction.
> 6. **Omitting roller consolidation** as a separate process step — this is **the** step that differentiates a professional answer from a casual one.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **30-Second Revision Bullet List — Read This Just Before the Exam:**

- [ ] Spray-up = **chopped fibers + catalysed resin sprayed simultaneously** on an open mold.
- [ ] Also called **Spray Lay-up** or **Chopped Fiber Spray Lay-up**.
- [ ] **Open-mold** process — only one mold side is tooled.
- [ ] **Reinforcement form:** chopped discontinuous glass fibers, length **10–40 mm**.
- [ ] **Typical $V_f$:** 20–35 % (lower than hand lay-up).
- [ ] **Typical void content:** 3–8 % (higher than hand lay-up).
- [ ] **Chopper speed:** ~3000–5000 rpm.
- [ ] **Catalyst:** MEKP (1–2 %); **Accelerator:** cobalt naphthenate.
- [ ] **Resin:** Unsaturated polyester (most common) or vinyl ester.
- [ ] **Cure:** Room-temperature exothermic, optional post-cure at 60–80 °C.
- [ ] **Roller consolidation** is **mandatory** — removes air, wets-out fibers, compacts laminate.
- [ ] **Gel-coat** (0.3–0.5 mm pigmented resin) is the first layer on the mold.
- [ ] **Production rate:** 5–15 kg/h per operator.
- [ ] **Key defects:** voids, dry spots, resin-rich/fiber-rich areas, delamination, sink marks.
- [ ] **Health hazard:** styrene vapour, fiber dust — needs exhaust ventilation + PPE.
- [ ] **Variants:** Hand spray, robotic/automated spray, airless spray, vacuum-assisted (VASPU).
- [ ] **Applications:** boat hulls, truck cabs, bath tubs, swimming pools, chemical tanks, ducts.
- [ ] **Versus hand lay-up:** spray-up is **faster & cheaper** but **weaker** in mechanical properties.
- [ ] **Voigt rule:** $E_c = E_f V_f + E_m V_m$ — upper bound (longitudinal).
- [ ] **Reuss rule:** $1/E_c = V_f/E_f + V_m/E_m$ — lower bound (transverse).
- [ ] **Critical fiber length:** $l_c = \sigma_{fu} d / (2\tau_i)$ — must be << chop length for full load transfer.
- [ ] **Weight–volume relation:** $w_f = V_f / \big[V_f + (\rho_m/\rho_f)(1-V_f)\big]$.
- [ ] **Composite identity:** $V_f + V_m + V_v = 1$.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Moulding methods - Pressure bagging and bag moulding methods
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT412 - COMPOSITE MATERIALS AND CERAMICS
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Moulding methods - Pressure bagging and bag moulding methods

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Bag Moulding?

**Bag moulding** is a closed-mould composite manufacturing process in which a flexible membrane (bag) is placed over a laid-up fibre-reinforced laminate, sealed against a rigid mould, and pressurised to consolidate, compact, and cure the part. The pressurising medium is either **compressed air/gas** (pressure bagging) or **atmospheric pressure induced by a vacuum** (vacuum bagging), or a combination of both in an autoclave.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PEMUT412 - Module 3):**
> *Bag moulding* refers to a family of processes (pressure bag, vacuum bag, autoclave) where a flexible impermeable bag applies hydrostatic pressure to a wet or prepreg lay-up to consolidate the laminate, expel entrapped air/volatile gases, and achieve a dense, low-void, fibre-controlled composite.

## 1.2 Classification of Bag Moulding Methods

| S.No | Method | Pressure Source | Typical Pressure |
|------|--------|----------------|------------------|
| 1 | **Vacuum Bag Moulding (VBM)** | Atmospheric pressure on a sealed bag after evacuating air | **0.08 – 0.10 MPa** (≈ 1 atm) |
| 2 | **Pressure Bag Moulding (PBM)** | Compressed air/gas inflated inside an internal bag | **0.2 – 0.7 MPa** |
| 3 | **Autoclave Moulding** | External pressurised vessel combining vacuum + external gas | **0.3 – 1.5 MPa** |
| 4 | **Rubber-Piston / Female-Die Pressure Bag** | Internal rubber bag acts as male punch in matched die | **0.5 – 1.0 MPa** |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Tea-Bag Squeeze" Effect

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Intuition (real-world analogy):**
> Imagine you place wet tea leaves and water inside a sealed polythene pouch, then press it flat with your hand. The water is forced out through the filter, the leaves compact densely, and any trapped air bubbles pop out from the edges. **Bag moulding does exactly the same thing with a fibre-resin laminate**:
> - The **fibres** = tea leaves (do not squeeze out)
> - The **resin** = water (squeezes out and flows)
> - The **trapped air/volatile gases** = bubbles (forced to escape)
> - The **flexible bag** = polythene pouch
> - The **pressure** = your hand

This consolidation is what gives bag-moulded composites their characteristic **low void content (< 1%)** and **high fibre volume fraction (55 – 65%)**, making them the preferred route for **aerospace primary structures**.

## 1.4 The "Why" — Why Not Just Use Open Mould?

Open-mould hand lay-up produces composites with:
- **Voids: 3 – 5 %** (air entrapment)
- **Fibre volume fraction: 30 – 40 %** (inadequate compaction)
- **Non-uniform thickness**
- **High scrap rate**

Bag moulding solves all three by applying **isostatic, uniform hydrostatic pressure** perpendicular to the laminate plane, which:
1. Reduces laminate thickness to a controlled value
2. Squeezes resin through the fibre bed (percolation)
3. Evacuates volatiles and entrapped gases
4. Improves fibre wet-out and adhesion
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Physics of Pressure Bagging

The process relies on **three coupled physical phenomena** acting simultaneously during heat + pressure ramp:

### Step 1 — Resin Flow (Darcy's Law)
Resin flows through the porous fibre preform under the applied pressure gradient.

### Step 2 — Fibre Compaction (Spring-Back)
The fibre bed behaves like a non-linear elastic spring. As pressure rises, the thickness decreases.

### Step 3 — Void Compression & Dissolution
Trapped air/volatiles either dissolve in the resin under hydrostatic compression (Henry's Law) or migrate to the breather and are evacuated.

> [!NOTE]
> **Cure Cycle Logic:** Pressure is **NOT** applied at room temperature. It is applied only after the laminate reaches a temperature where the resin viscosity drops to its minimum (the "flow window"). If pressure is applied too early, resin is squeezed out prematurely (resin-starved part). If applied too late, voids are already locked in.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation | Description | Units |
|---|----------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $P_{atm} - P_{vac} = \Delta P$ | Net consolidation pressure in vacuum bag (max ≈ 101.325 kPa) | kPa |
| 2 | $P_{cons} = P_{gas} + P_{atm}$ | Consolidation pressure in autoclave (gauge + atmospheric) | kPa |
| 3 | $P_1 V_1 = P_2 V_2$ | Ideal gas law for bag inflation (isothermal, for thin flexible bag) | Pa·m³ |
| 4 | $V_f = \dfrac{A_f}{A_{lam}}$ | Fibre volume fraction (2D definition) | — |
| 5 | $V_f = \dfrac{1}{1 + \dfrac{\rho_f}{\rho_m} \cdot \dfrac{W_m}{W_f}}$ | Fibre volume fraction from weights & densities | — |
| 6 | $v_{resin} = -\dfrac{K}{\mu} \cdot \dfrac{dP}{dx}$ | Darcy's law for resin velocity through fibre bed | m/s |
| 7 | $C = \dfrac{S^2 \cdot \mu}{K}$ | Kozeny-Carman constant for permeability | 1/m² |
| 8 | $V_{void} = V_{lam} - V_f - V_m$ | Void content by volume | m³ |
| 9 | $P_{lam} \cdot t_{lam} = P_{bag} \cdot t_{bag}$ | Hydrostatic force balance on flexible bag (force/unit width) | N/m |
| 10 | $t_{lam} = \dfrac{n \cdot w}{\rho_f \cdot A_{ply}}$ | Final laminate thickness from ply count | m |
| 11 | $\sigma_{hoop} = \dfrac{P \cdot r}{t}$ | Hoop stress in cylindrical pressure bag | Pa |
| 12 | $V_{gas,final} = V_{gas,initial} \cdot \dfrac{P_{atm}}{P_{cons}}$ | Final void volume after isothermal compression | m³ |
| 13 | $c = k_H \cdot P$ | Henry's Law — dissolved gas concentration in resin | mol/m³ |
| 14 | $\tau = \dfrac{\mu \cdot \dot{\gamma}}{}$ | Resin shear stress (defines flow regime) | Pa |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memory Hook for KTU Exam:**
> *BAG* → **B**oyle (gas law), **A**tmospheric differential, **G**eometry (Kozeny–Carman).
> Always use **$\vert$ or $\mid$** (or write "modulus") instead of the pipe symbol in tables to avoid LaTeX-markdown conflicts.

## 2.3 Engineering Utility

| Industry | Why Bag Moulding? |
|----------|-------------------|
| **Aerospace** (Boeing 787, Airbus A350) | 50%+ of primary structure is autoclave-cured CFRP — lowest void content achievable |
| **Wind turbine blades** | Large vacuum-bag layups up to 80 m; controlled fibre fraction |
| **Marine racing yacht hulls** | Lightweight + void-free for hydrodynamic efficiency |
| **Satellite structures** | Dimensionally stable, low outgassing |

## 2.4 Comparison: Pressure Bag vs Vacuum Bag vs Autoclave

| Parameter | Vacuum Bag | Pressure Bag | Autoclave |
|-----------|-----------|--------------|-----------|
| Max pressure | ~0.1 MPa | 0.3–0.7 MPa | 0.3–1.5 MPa |
| Void content | 1–3 % | 0.5–1.5 % | < 0.5 % |
| Fibre $V_f$ | 50–55 % | 55–60 % | 58–65 % |
| Capital cost | Low | Moderate | Very high |
| Part size | Unlimited | Limited by press | Limited by vessel (Ø 2.5–8 m typical) |
| Temperature control | Oven | Heated platen | Internal autoclave heaters |
| Typical application | Wind blades, boats | Missile casings, ducting | Aircraft wings, fuselage |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Bag Pressure → Laminate Compaction Force (Force Balance)

**Problem Setup:** A rectangular vacuum/pressure bag of dimensions $L \times B$ rests on a laminate. The bag is sealed; the inside is at pressure $P_{bag}$. Determine the net downward force pressing the laminate onto the mould.

### Step-by-step Derivation

Consider the flexible bag as a thin membrane in static equilibrium. The bag has two faces (top and bottom) exposed to different pressures:

**Step 1 — Pressures acting on the bag:**

- Outside (top): atmospheric, $P_{atm}$
- Inside: $P_{bag}$ (could be lower or higher than $P_{atm}$)

**Step 2 — Net pressure differential across the membrane:**

$$
\Delta P = P_{atm} - P_{bag}
$$

If $P_{bag} < P_{atm}$ → positive downward (vacuum bag pushes down).
If $P_{bag} > P_{atm}$ → bag inflates upward, *removing* pressure (used in pressure bag mode only with a separate top mould).

**Step 3 — Force on a horizontal area element $dA$:**

$$
dF = \Delta P \cdot dA
$$

**Step 4 — Total compaction force on laminate area $A = L \times B$:**

$$
F_{compact} = \int_A \Delta P \, dA = \Delta P \cdot A
$$

**Step 5 — Substitute and simplify for a vacuum bag ($P_{bag} \approx 0$, absolute):**

$$
F_{compact} = P_{atm} \cdot L \cdot B
$$

$$
F_{compact} = (101.325 \times 10^3 \, \text{Pa}) \cdot (1 \, \text{m} \times 1 \, \text{m}) = 101.325 \, \text{kN}
$$

**Interpretation:** Even a *vacuum* bag applies **one full atmosphere (≈ 101 kPa, or 14.7 psi)** of consolidation pressure — the same as the air we breathe pressing on every square metre of Earth's surface.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Reduction of Void Volume (Isothermal Compression)

**Setup:** A trapped gas bubble of volume $V_i$ exists at atmospheric pressure $P_{atm}$ inside a laminate. The autoclave/pressure bag is pressurised to $P_{cons}$. Assume isothermal compression of an ideal gas and that the gas does not escape. Find the final bubble volume.

### Step-by-step Derivation

**Step 1 — State 1 (before pressure):**

$$
P_1 = P_{atm}, \quad V_1 = V_i
$$

**Step 2 — State 2 (during pressure):**

$$
P_2 = P_{cons} = P_{atm} + P_{gauge}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply Boyle's Law (isothermal, ideal gas):**

$$
P_1 V_1 = P_2 V_2
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $V_2$:**

$$
V_2 = \dfrac{P_1 V_1}{P_2} = \dfrac{P_{atm} \cdot V_i}{P_{atm} + P_{gauge}}
$$

**Step 5 — Numerical example (autoclave at 7 bar gauge, $P_{cons} = 8$ bar):**

$$
V_2 = \dfrac{1 \times V_i}{8} = 0.125 \, V_i
$$

**Conclusion:** An autoclave running at 7 bar gauge compresses trapped voids to **12.5% of their original size** (a **87.5% reduction**). This is why autoclave moulding produces the lowest void content.

> [!NOTE]
> In practice, the gas *also* dissolves into the resin per Henry's Law $c = k_H P$, providing an *additional* void-reduction pathway. The two effects (compression + dissolution) compound.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Fibre Volume Fraction from Ply Count (Rule of Mixtures Application)

**Setup:** A laminate is built from $n$ plies, each of areal weight $w$ (g/m²) and fibre density $\rho_f$, cured under pressure to a final in-plane area $A_{lam}$. Derive the fibre volume fraction $V_f$.

### Step-by-step Derivation

**Step 1 — Total mass of fibre in the laminate:**

$$
m_f = n \cdot w \cdot A_{lam}
$$

**Step 2 — Volume occupied by fibre:**

$$
V_f = \dfrac{m_f}{\rho_f} = \dfrac{n \cdot w \cdot A_{lam}}{\rho_f}
$$

**Step 3 — Final laminate thickness from fibre stack geometry:**

$$
t_{lam} = \dfrac{n \cdot w}{\rho_f} \cdot \dfrac{1}{1} = \dfrac{n \cdot w}{\rho_f \cdot A_{lam} / A_{lam}} \quad \Rightarrow \quad t_{lam} \cdot A_{lam} = \dfrac{n \cdot w \cdot A_{lam}}{\rho_f}
$$

**Step 4 — Total laminate volume:**

$$
V_{lam} = t_{lam} \cdot A_{lam}
$$

**Step 5 — Fibre volume fraction (definition):**

$$
V_f = \dfrac{V_f}{V_{lam}} = \dfrac{\dfrac{n \cdot w \cdot A_{lam}}{\rho_f}}{t_{lam} \cdot A_{lam}} = \dfrac{n \cdot w}{\rho_f \cdot t_{lam}}
$$

**Step 6 — Solve for $t_{lam}$ in terms of target $V_f$:**

$$
t_{lam} = \dfrac{n \cdot w}{\rho_f \cdot V_f}
$$

**Numerical example:** For a quasi-isotropic carbon/epoxy layup with $n = 8$ plies, $w = 196 \, \text{g/m}^2$, $\rho_f = 1.80 \, \text{g/cm}^3 = 1800 \, \text{kg/m}^3$, target $V_f = 0.60$:

$$
t_{lam} = \dfrac{8 \times 0.196}{1800 \times 0.60} = \dfrac{1.568}{1080} = 1.452 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{m} \approx 1.45 \, \text{mm}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Use:** The autoclave/pressure bag is set to a *target* compaction thickness (using mechanical stops or shims). The above equation is solved *backwards* by the process engineer to choose shim thickness from desired $V_f$.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Resin Velocity Through Fibre Bed (Darcy's Law)

**Setup:** Resin of viscosity $\mu$ flows through a fibre preform of permeability $K$ under pressure gradient $dP/dx$. Find the superficial velocity $v$.

### Step-by-step Derivation

**Step 1 — Darcy's empirical law (1-D):**

$$
v = -\dfrac{K}{\mu} \cdot \dfrac{dP}{dx}
$$

**Step 2 — Kozeny-Carman equation for permeability of a fibre bed:**

$$
K = \dfrac{d_f^2 \cdot \varepsilon^3}{16 \cdot C_{kc} \cdot (1 - \varepsilon)^2}
$$

where $d_f$ = fibre diameter, $\varepsilon$ = porosity of fibre bed, $C_{kc}$ ≈ 0.5 (Kozeny constant).

**Step 3 — Substitute (resin front velocity):**

$$
v_{resin} = \dfrac{d_f^2 \cdot \varepsilon^3}{16 \, C_{kc} \, \mu \, (1 - \varepsilon)^2} \cdot \dfrac{\Delta P}{L_{flow}}
$$

**Step 4 — Time for resin to flow a distance $L_{flow}$:**

$$
t_{flow} = \dfrac{L_{flow}}{v_{resin}} = \dfrac{16 \, C_{kc} \, \mu \, (1 - \varepsilon)^2 \cdot L_{flow}^2}{d_f^2 \cdot \varepsilon^3 \cdot \Delta P}
$$

**Physical insight:** Flow time is **quadratic in flow length** — doubling the bleed distance quadruples the time. This is why VARTM (a variant) uses a *distributed* inlet network, not a single edge inlet.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Step | Phenomenon | Governing Equation | Process Lever |
|------|-----------|--------------------|---------------|
| 1 | Membrane force balance | $F = \Delta P \cdot A$ | Bag tightness, pump capacity |
| 2 | Void compression | $P_1 V_1 = P_2 V_2$ | Higher $P_{cons}$ |
| 3 | Gas dissolution | $c = k_H P$ | Pressure + time at temperature |
| 4 | Resin flow | $v = -\dfrac{K}{\mu} \cdot \dfrac{dP}{dx}$ | Viscosity control (heat) |
| 5 | Fibre packing | $t_{lam} = \dfrac{n w}{\rho_f V_f}$ | Mechanical stops / shims |
| 6 | Bag hoop stress | $\sigma = \dfrac{P r}{t}$ | Bag thickness / material |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Process Flow Diagram — Pressure Bag Moulding (Internal-Bag Variant)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Prepare rigid female mould] --> B[Apply release agent to mould surface]
    B --> C[Lay-up fibre preform plies n layers of prepreg or wet fabric]
    C --> D[Place internal elastomeric pressure bag over lay-up]
    D --> E[Position matched male die or top cover with air inlet port]
    E --> F[Seal the assembly with clamps or bolts]
    F --> G[Connect bag to compressed air line through pressure regulator]
    G --> H[Heat assembly to resin flow temperature typically 80 to 120 deg C]
    H --> I[Apply bag pressure 0.2 to 0.7 MPa progressively]
    I --> J[Resin flows through fibre bed Darcy flow]
    J --> K[Compaction: fibres densify; voids compress Boyle]
    K --> L[Hold at cure temperature 120 to 180 deg C for 1 to 4 hours]
    L --> M[Cool under pressure below Tg]
    M --> N[Release pressure and disassemble]
    N --> O[Extract finished composite part]
    O --> P[End: Trimming and NDT inspection]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Compliance Check:** All node IDs are alphanumeric (`A`–`P`), no reserved keywords, all labels are plain uppercase text without markdown formatting inside the quoted strings.

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Complete Bag-Moulding System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph S1[Resin and Fibre Input Subsystem]
        A1[Fibre Reinforcement] --> A2[Preform Lay-up Station]
        A3[Resin System Epoxy or Polyester] --> A2
    end
    subgraph S2[Bagging Subsystem]
        A2 --> B1[Bagging Film Nylon or Silicone]
        B1 --> B2[Sealant Tacky Tape]
        B2 --> B3[Caul Plate for thickness control]
        B3 --> B4[Bleeder and Breather layers]
    end
    subgraph S3[Pressure and Heat Subsystem]
        C1[Vacuum Pump] --> C2[Vacuum Port]
        C2 --> C3[Bag Internal Volume]
        C4[Compressed Air or N2 Cylinder] --> C5[Regulator]
        C5 --> C3
        C6[Heater Platen or Oven] --> C7[Tool with Lay-up]
        C7 --> C3
    end
    subgraph S4[Monitoring and Control]
        D1[Pressure Transducer] --> D2[DAQ]
        D3[Thermocouples] --> D2
        D2 --> D4[Process Controller]
        D4 --> C5
        D4 --> C6
    end
    subgraph S5[Cured Part Output]
        C3 --> E1[Consolidated Laminate]
        E1 --> E2[De-bag]
        E2 --> E3[Trim and NDT]
    end
```

## 4.3 Schematic — Cross-Section of a Pressure Bag in a Closed Tool

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph TOOL[Matched Metal Tool Set]
        direction TB
        TOP[Top Die Steel or Aluminium]
        BOT[Bottom Die Female]
        TOP --- BOT
    end
    BAG[Elastomeric Pressure Bag]
    LAY[Composite Lay-up n Plies]
    RESIN[Excess Resin Bleeds to Edge]
    BOT --> LAY
    LAY --> BAG
    BAG --> TOP
    BAG -.->|Air Inlet 0.5 MPa| RESIN
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the schematic:** The bag acts as the male punch. As compressed air inflates it, the bag pushes the laminate down and outward into the female die cavity, squeezing resin out at the perimeter (bleed edge). This is the **female-die pressure bag** used for conical and hemispherical parts (rocket nose cones, ducting).
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**"Distinguish between vacuum bag moulding and pressure bag moulding with respect to pressure source, maximum achievable consolidation pressure, and typical void content."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Vacuum Bag Moulding (VBM) | Pressure Bag Moulding (PBM) |
|--------|---------------------------|------------------------------|
| Pressure source | Atmospheric pressure (after evacuating bag) | Compressed air/gas inflated inside bag |
| Maximum pressure | ≈ 0.10 MPa (1 atm) | 0.2 – 0.7 MPa |
| Typical void content | 1 – 3 % | 0.5 – 1.5 % |
| Equipment | Vacuum pump + bag | Compressor + regulator + bag |

*(1 mark for correct identification, 1 mark for pressure values, 1 mark for void content comparison.)*

---

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**"State Boyle's Law and explain its role in void reduction during pressure bag moulding of composites."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Statement (1 mark):** For a fixed mass of ideal gas at constant temperature, $P_1 V_1 = P_2 V_2$.
- **Application to bag moulding (1 mark):** When pressure is applied, trapped air/gas voids inside the laminate are compressed isothermally. Their volume reduces in inverse proportion to the applied pressure.
- **Quantitative role (1 mark):** In an autoclave at 7 bar gauge ($P_{cons} = 8$ bar), voids shrink to $V_2 = V_1 / 8$, giving a **87.5 % reduction** in void volume, which is why autoclave moulding achieves void content below 0.5 %.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — KTU ESE Internal Choice)

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam - June 2024]

**(a)** With the help of a neat schematic, describe the **pressure bag moulding** process for manufacturing a fibre-reinforced composite component. List any **four advantages** of the process. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A carbon-fibre/epoxy laminate is built from **8 plies**, each of **areal weight 200 g/m²** and fibre density **1.80 g/cm³**. During cure in a pressure bag, the laminate is compacted to a final thickness of **1.50 mm** over a 1 m × 1 m panel. Calculate:
  (i) the fibre volume fraction $V_f$,
  (ii) the laminate volume,
  (iii) the mass of fibre and mass of resin (assuming $\rho_m = 1.20 \, \text{g/cm}^3$), and
  (iv) the net consolidation force exerted on the laminate if the bag pressure differential is 0.40 MPa. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to A(a):**

**1. Description of Pressure Bag Moulding (5 marks):**

Pressure bag moulding is a closed-mould process in which a flexible elastomeric bag is placed *inside* the mould cavity over the laid-up laminate. The bag is inflated with compressed air, applying uniform hydrostatic pressure that consolidates the laminate against the female die.

**Process Steps (numbered):**
1. **Mould preparation** — clean rigid female mould, apply release agent.
2. **Lay-up** — alternate layers of fibre reinforcement and resin (or use prepreg), place in mould.
3. **Bag placement** — fit a tailored elastomeric bag over the lay-up; position air-inlet port.
4. **Assembly closure** — bring down top die/cover; seal perimeter.
5. **Heat-up** — heat tool to resin flow window (typically 80–120 °C for epoxy).
6. **Pressurisation** — inflate bag progressively to 0.2–0.7 MPa; resin flows, fibres compact, voids compress.
7. **Cure** — hold at 120–180 °C for 1–4 hours.
8. **Cool-down under pressure** — below $T_g$ to avoid spring-back.
9. **De-bag and demould.**

**Four Advantages (2 marks):**
1. Higher fibre volume fraction and lower void content than open mould.
2. Uniform hydrostatic pressure → uniform thickness and properties.
3. Suitable for complex curved surfaces (conical, hemispherical).
4. Better fibre wet-out and reduced exotherm during cure.

---

#### **Model Solution to A(b):**

**Given:**
- $n = 8$ plies, $w = 200 \, \text{g/m}^2 = 0.200 \, \text{kg/m}^2$
- $\rho_f = 1.80 \, \text{g/cm}^3 = 1800 \, \text{kg/m}^3$
- $\rho_m = 1.20 \, \text{g/cm}^3 = 1200 \, \text{kg/m}^3$
- $t_{lam} = 1.50 \, \text{mm} = 1.50 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{m}$
- $A = 1 \, \text{m} \times 1 \, \text{m} = 1 \, \text{m}^2$
- $\Delta P = 0.40 \, \text{MPa} = 0.40 \times 10^6 \, \text{Pa}$

**Solution:**

**(i) Fibre volume fraction (3 marks):**

$$
m_f = n \cdot w \cdot A = 8 \times 0.200 \times 1 = 1.60 \, \text{kg}
$$

$$
V_f = \dfrac{m_f}{\rho_f} = \dfrac{1.60}{1800} = 8.889 \times 10^{-4} \, \text{m}^3
$$

$$
V_{lam} = A \cdot t_{lam} = 1 \times 1.50 \times 10^{-3} = 1.50 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{m}^3
$$

$$
V_f \text{ (fraction)} = \dfrac{8.889 \times 10^{-4}}{1.50 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.5926 \approx 0.59
$$

**(ii) Laminate volume (1 mark):**

$$
V_{lam} = 1.50 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{m}^3 = 1.50 \, \text{L}
$$

**(iii) Mass of fibre and resin (2 marks):**

$$
m_f = 1.60 \, \text{kg}
$$

$$
V_m = V_{lam} - V_f = 1.50 \times 10^{-3} - 8.889 \times 10^{-4} = 6.111 \times 10^{-4} \, \text{m}^3
$$

$$
m_m = \rho_m \cdot V_m = 1200 \times 6.111 \times 10^{-4} = 0.7333 \, \text{kg}
$$

**(iv) Consolidation force (1 mark):**

$$
F = \Delta P \cdot A = 0.40 \times 10^6 \times 1 = 4.0 \times 10^5 \, \text{N} = 400 \, \text{kN}
$$

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 — Alternative Choice]

**(a)** Explain with a labelled sketch the **vacuum bag moulding** process. Discuss how it differs from **autoclave moulding**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An aerospace CFRP component is cured in an autoclave. Initially, the laminate contains spherical voids of diameter **50 μm** at atmospheric pressure (**1 bar**). The autoclave is pressurised to **6 bar gauge** (i.e., 7 bar absolute) at the same temperature. Assuming isothermal ideal-gas compression, calculate:
  (i) the final diameter of the voids,
  (ii) the **percentage reduction** in void volume, and
  (iii) the **final void content** if the original void content was 2.5 % by volume. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to B(a):**

**Vacuum Bag Moulding (VBM) — 4 marks:**

In VBM, the laminate is laid up on a mould, covered with a release film, a bleeder/breather stack, and a sealed flexible bag. Air is evacuated from the bag using a vacuum pump, creating a **pressure differential of ≈ 1 atm** (101 kPa) between the bag interior and the outside atmosphere. The atmospheric pressure pushing on the bag consolidates the laminate.

**Steps:** Lay-up → release film → bleeder → breather → vacuum bag → seal → evacuate → heat & cure in oven → de-bag.

**Autoclave vs Vacuum Bag — 3 marks (tabular):**

| Feature | Vacuum Bag | Autoclave |
|---------|-----------|-----------|
| Pressure source | Atmospheric (1 atm) | Compressed gas (up to 1.5 MPa) |
| Pressure application | Bag evacuated; ambient pushes in | Pressurised vessel around the part |
| Max pressure | ~0.1 MPa | 0.3 – 1.5 MPa |
| Void content | 1 – 3 % | < 0.5 % |
| Part size | Unlimited (wind blades) | Limited by vessel (Ø 2.5 – 8 m) |
| Cost | Low | Very high |

**Key difference:** Autoclave applies *external* hydrostatic pressure on the *entire* part inside a pressure vessel, whereas vacuum bag only uses atmospheric pressure differential.

---

#### **Model Solution to B(b):**

**Given:**
- $d_1 = 50 \, \mu\text{m}$, $P_1 = 1 \, \text{bar}$
- $P_2 = 7 \, \text{bar}$ absolute

**Solution:**

**(i) Final diameter (3 marks):**

Volume of a sphere $V = \dfrac{\pi d^3}{6}$. So $V \propto d^3$, and at constant temperature, $P_1 V_1 = P_2 V_2$:

$$
V_2 = V_1 \cdot \dfrac{P_1}{P_2} = V_1 \cdot \dfrac{1}{7}
$$

$$
d_2^3 = d_1^3 \cdot \dfrac{P_1}{P_2} = (50)^3 \times \dfrac{1}{7}
$$

$$
d_2 = 50 \times \left(\dfrac{1}{7}\right)^{1/3} = 50 \times 0.5228 = 26.14 \, \mu\text{m}
$$

**(ii) Percentage reduction in void volume (2 marks):**

$$
\% \text{ reduction} = \left(1 - \dfrac{V_2}{V_1}\right) \times 100 = \left(1 - \dfrac{1}{7}\right) \times 100 = 85.71 \, \% \approx 85.7\%
$$

**(iii) Final void content (2 marks):**

$$
V_{void,2} = V_{void,1} \times \dfrac{1}{7} = 2.5\% \times \dfrac{1}{7} = 0.357 \, \% \approx 0.36\%
$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Bag Moulding Questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting unit conversions (–2 marks):** Fibre density in g/cm³ must be converted to kg/m³ before any SI calculation. KTU examiners check unit consistency strictly.
> 2. **Using $P_{gauge}$ instead of $P_{absolute}$ in Boyle's Law (–3 marks):** The ideal gas law requires *absolute* pressure. "7 bar gauge" = 8 bar absolute, NOT 7 bar.
> 3. **Confusing vacuum bag with pressure bag (–2 marks):** Vacuum bag uses $P_{atm}$ as the *driving* force (after evacuation); it does *not* "pressurise" the part beyond 1 atm.
> 4. **Skipping the schematic (–2 marks):** KTU mandates a neat labelled diagram for any moulding-process question. Drawing on phone-paper / unlabelled arrows = full mark loss for that sub-part.
> 5. **Not stating the resin flow window (–1 mark):** Pressure must be applied *only* when resin viscosity is at minimum. Stating "apply pressure and heat simultaneously" is technically wrong.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Pressure & Bag Moulding Methods**

- [ ] Bag moulding = flexible membrane + pressure differential for laminate consolidation.
- [ ] Three variants: **VBM (1 atm), PBM (0.2–0.7 MPa), Autoclave (0.3–1.5 MPa)**.
- [ ] Consolidation force: $F = \Delta P \times A$.
- [ ] Void compression: $P_1 V_1 = P_2 V_2$ — **always use absolute pressure**.
- [ ] 7 bar gauge autoclave → voids shrink to **1/8th** their original size (87.5 % reduction).
- [ ] Fibre volume fraction: $V_f = \dfrac{n \cdot w}{\rho_f \cdot t_{lam}}$.
- [ ] Darcy's law: $v = -\dfrac{K}{\mu} \cdot \dfrac{dP}{dx}$ governs resin flow in the preform.
- [ ] Pressure is applied in the **resin flow window**, not at room temperature.
- [ ] Vacuum bag = atmospheric pressure is the driver (after evacuation).
- [ ] Pressure bag = compressed air inflates an *internal* elastomeric bag (female-die method).
- [ ] Autoclave = combination of vacuum + external pressure → lowest voids (< 0.5 %).
- [ ] Aerospace primary structures use autoclave; wind blades & boats use VBM.
- [ ] Caul plate controls laminate thickness; bleeder absorbs excess resin; breather allows vacuum distribution.
- [ ] Hoop stress in bag: $\sigma_{hoop} = \dfrac{P r}{t}$ — controls bag burst safety.
- [ ] Cure cycle: heat-up → dwell at flow window → pressure ramp → cure hold → cool-down under pressure → de-bag.

> **End of Notes — Module 3 — Moulding Methods: Pressure Bagging and Bag Moulding Methods (PEMUT412 | KTU 2024 Scheme)**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Autoclave-based processing with prepregs
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT412 - COMPOSITE MATERIALS AND CERAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Autoclave-based processing with prepregs

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Prepregs — The Foundation of Autoclave Processing

A **prepreg** (pre-impregnated composite) is a reinforcing fabric (carbon, glass, or aramid fiber) that has been pre-impregnated with a precisely controlled amount of **partially cured (B-staged) thermoset resin** system. The resin is tacky at room temperature, allowing the plies to adhere during hand layup, but requires elevated temperature to fully cure (crosslink).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key KTU Definition:** A prepreg is a *ready-to-use* intermediate composite product containing reinforcement fibers and a partially cured matrix resin, stored typically at **−18°C** to halt the cure reaction until use.

**Typical Prepreg Composition:**

| Component | Function | Approximate Content |
|-----------|----------|---------------------|
| Reinforcement fiber | Primary load-bearing | 60–65% by weight |
| B-staged thermoset resin | Matrix binder (epoxy, BMI, cyanate ester) | 30–35% by weight |
| Tackifier / additives | Surface stickiness & handling | 1–2% |
| Release paper / film | Protection during storage | Backing layer |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** Prepregs must be **out-time managed** — once removed from cold storage, the resin slowly advances (post-thaw aging). Standard out-life is typically **10–30 days at 21°C**, after which the prepreg becomes unworkable.

## 1.2 Autoclave Processing — Formal Definition

**Autoclave processing** is a closed-vessel, **combined heat-and-pressure curing technique** used to consolidate and cure laminated composite stacks (layups) made from prepregs. The autoclave applies three simultaneous stimuli:

1. **Heat** (typically **120 °C – 180 °C**, sometimes up to **250 °C** for bismaleimide systems)
2. **Pressure** (typically **3 bar – 7 bar**, or ~**0.3 MPa – 0.7 MPa**, with aerospace parts often at **100 psi ≈ 6.9 bar**)
3. **Vacuum** (applied internally to the bagged layup before pressurization, down to **~50 mbar absolute**)

The combination drives out entrapped air, compacts the laminate, suppresses void formation, and produces fiber volumes of **Vf ≈ 55–65%** with void contents **< 1%**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Aerospace Metric:** A "First-Quality Aerospace Laminate" is defined (per Boeing/Airbus standards) as having **Vf ≥ 58%** and **void content ≤ 1.0%**. Achieving this reliably *requires* autoclave pressure.

## 1.3 The Intuitive Analogy — "The Pressure Cooker for Composites"

Imagine making a layered lasagna:

- **Prepreg plies** = the individual noodles, each already smeared with sauce (resin).
- **Layup** = stacking the noodles in a pan.
- **Vacuum bagging** = sealing the pan with plastic wrap and sucking out the air (this pre-consolidates the stack).
- **Autoclave** = putting the sealed pan into a *pressure cooker that also heats up*.

While the lasagna bakes, the **pressure squeezes out air bubbles** (void removal), the **heat melts the cheese and bonds layers** (resin flow and cure), and the **vacuum pulls resin into dry spots** (consolidation). When you open the cooker, you have a perfectly bonded, void-free, dense composite part — exactly like an autoclave-cured carbon-fiber wing skin.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intuition Anchor:** Pressure = void killer. Heat = cure driver. Vacuum = air remover + resin distributor. All three together = aerospace-grade laminate.

## 1.4 Why Autoclave? — The Engineering Justification

Out-of-autoclave (OOA) methods like *resin transfer molding (RTM)*, *vacuum-bag-only (VBO)*, and *compression molding* exist, but the autoclave remains the **gold standard** for primary aerospace structures because:

- **Highest achievable fiber volume fraction (Vf)**: Pressure allows compaction beyond what vacuum alone can produce.
- **Lowest void content**: Hydrostatic pressure dissolves/collapses micro-voids.
- **Best mechanical repeatability**: Uniform pressure and temperature yield consistent part quality.
- **Scalable to large parts**: Aircraft wing skins (up to **15–20 m long**) are routinely autoclave-cured.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Common Misconception:** "Autoclave = oven." This is **false**. An oven provides *only heat*. An autoclave provides **heat + uniform hydrostatic pressure + optional internal vacuum**, which an oven cannot replicate. This distinction is a frequent 3-mark exam question.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The Autoclave Process — Operational Logic (Step-by-Step)

The complete autoclave cure cycle is built from five sequential logic blocks. Mastering these is essential for KTU Module-3 derivations.

### Step 1: Prepreg Preparation & Thaw
- Prepregs are removed from the **−18 °C freezer** and allowed to thaw inside sealed bags to **prevent moisture condensation** (which would later form steam voids in the laminate).
- The thawing time is typically **8–24 hours at room temperature**.

### Step 2: Layup on the Tool
- Plies are **cut via CNC ply cutter** or die cutter to the net-shape ply geometry defined by the **ply book**.
- Plies are stacked on a **tool (mandrel or mold surface)** in a prescribed sequence respecting the laminate stacking code (e.g., **[0/45/−45/90]ₛ** for a quasi-isotropic layup).
- Each ply is **debulked** (compacted with a roller or by applying vacuum every 3–5 plies) to remove inter-ply air.

### Step 3: Vacuum Bagging (Bagging Sequence — Bottom to Top)
The vacuum bag stack, from tool surface upward, is:

1. **Tool (mold surface)**
2. **Release film (perforated or solid)** — prevents resin from sticking to the tool; perforations allow volatiles and excess resin to escape.
3. **Prepreg laminate (the part)**
4. **Release film (top)**
5. **Bleeder ply (non-woven polyester or glass scrim)** — absorbs excess resin squeezed out during cure.
6. **Breather ply (felt or non-woven fabric)** — distributes vacuum uniformly and acts as a vacuum reservoir.
7. **Vacuum bag (nylon or elastomeric film)** — sealed to the tool with tacky sealant tape.
8. **Vacuum port + pressure sensor + thermocouples** — instruments for monitoring.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examination Pearl:** The **bleeder + breather** combination is what *controls* the resin content of the cured laminate. Without them, the laminate would either be too resin-rich (low Vf) or develop dry spots (resin starvation).

### Step 4: Loading into the Autoclave & Applying Vacuum
- The bagged tool is placed inside the autoclave pressure vessel.
- Vacuum is applied to the bag interior (typically **50–100 mbar absolute**) while the autoclave is still at ambient pressure.
- This pre-consolidates the laminate *before* heat is applied.

### Step 5: Cure Cycle Execution
The autoclave runs a programmed cycle. The standard cure cycle for a **350 °F (177 °C)** epoxy system:

1. **Ramp 1 (Heat-up):** 1–3 °C/min from room temperature to ~**120 °C** (intermediate dwell).
2. **Apply full pressure** (e.g., **6.2 bar = 90 psi**) once the resin viscosity drops and the laminate is still soft.
3. **Dwell 1:** Hold at 120 °C for **30–60 min** to allow resin viscosity to reach a minimum (resin flow phase — vacuum and pressure work together to consolidate).
4. **Ramp 2:** Heat up to final cure temperature, e.g., **177 °C** at **1–2 °C/min**.
5. **Dwell 2 (Cure dwell):** Hold at 177 °C for **120–180 min** to complete crosslinking.
6. **Cool-down:** Cool at **1–3 °C/min** to below **60 °C** before releasing pressure to avoid thermal shock and dimensional distortion.

## 2.2 Why All Three (Heat + Pressure + Vacuum)?

| Stimulus | Primary Effect | Physical Mechanism |
|----------|----------------|--------------------|
| **Heat** | Activates cure kinetics, lowers resin viscosity | Arrhenius temperature dependence; Arrhenius equation: $\frac{d\alpha}{dt} = A \cdot e^{-E_a/RT} \cdot f(\alpha)$ |
| **Pressure** | Compacts laminate, suppresses voids, drives out entrapped air | Hydrostatic compression: $\Delta P = P_{autoclave} - P_{void}$; void collapse when $\Delta P > 2\gamma/r$ |
| **Vacuum** | Removes inter-ply air, pulls resin into dry regions, vents volatiles | Pressure differential across bag; vapor pressure reduction of volatiles |

The **vacuum is released** *just before* pressure is fully applied, or held through the entire cycle — depending on the resin system. Modern aerospace prepregs use the "vacuum + pressure simultaneously" approach to maximize consolidation.

## 2.3 Cure Kinetics — Arrhenius Behavior

The cure of a thermoset resin is governed by an autocatalytic or nth-order kinetic model. The general rate form:

$$\frac{d\alpha}{dt} = k(T) \cdot f(\alpha) = A \cdot e^{-E_a / RT} \cdot (1-\alpha)^n$$

Where:
- $\alpha$ = degree of cure (0 to 1)
- $A$ = pre-exponential factor (1/s)
- $E_a$ = activation energy (~**60–80 kJ/mol** for typical aerospace epoxies)
- $R$ = universal gas constant = **8.314 J/(mol·K)**
- $T$ = absolute temperature (K)
- $n$ = reaction order (typically 1–2)

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters for autoclave processing:** A small increase in temperature **exponentially** accelerates cure. A 10 °C rise in cure temperature can **double the reaction rate**. This is why autoclave ramp rates are tightly controlled — to avoid thermal runaway (exotherm), especially in thick laminates.

## 2.4 Consolidation Mechanics — The Resin Flow Model

During cure, resin flows through the fiber bed. **Darcy's Law** governs this flow:

$$v = -\frac{K}{\mu} \cdot \nabla P$$

Where:
- $v$ = superficial resin velocity (m/s)
- $K$ = permeability of the fiber bed (m²) — typically **10⁻¹² to 10⁻¹⁴ m²** for aerospace prepregs
- $\mu$ = resin viscosity (Pa·s) — minimum during cure can drop to **0.1–10 Pa·s**
- $\nabla P$ = pressure gradient (Pa/m)

**Kozeny-Carman equation** for permeability of a unidirectional fiber bed:

$$K = \frac{r_f^2}{4k_{KC}} \cdot \frac{(1-V_f)^3}{V_f^2}$$

Where:
- $r_f$ = fiber radius (~**3–7 μm** for carbon, ~**5–10 μm** for glass)
- $k_{KC}$ = Kozeny-Carman constant (~**150–180** for aligned fibers)
- $V_f$ = fiber volume fraction

## 2.5 Void Removal — Henry's Law & Pressure Solution

Voids in cured laminates are predominantly air or volatile-filled. According to **Henry's Law**, the equilibrium gas concentration dissolved in the resin is:

$$C = k_H \cdot P_{partial}$$

Where:
- $C$ = dissolved gas concentration (mol/m³)
- $k_H$ = Henry's constant (mol/(m³·Pa))
- $P_{partial}$ = partial pressure of gas above the resin (Pa)

Under high autoclave pressure, gas dissolves *into* the resin; when the system cools and pressure releases, the dissolved gas may re-nucleate, but if cure has progressed to a high viscosity, the gas is *trapped in solution* — yielding a void-free laminate.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Takeaway:** This is why pressure is applied *before* gelation (resin solidifies) but *after* the resin viscosity has reached its minimum — there is a narrow process window (often **10–20 minutes**) for optimal void removal.

## 2.6 KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET (Exam-Ready Table)

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical formatting note:** All absolute-value bars and "divides" use `\vert` or `\mid` to preserve markdown table integrity.

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbolic Form | Variables & Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Fiber volume fraction from areal weights | $V_f = \frac{(W_f/\rho_f)}{(W_f/\rho_f) + (W_m/\rho_m)}$ | $W_f$ = fiber areal weight (g/m²); $W_m$ = resin areal weight; $\rho_f, \rho_m$ = densities (g/cm³) | Determine cured Vf from prepreg data |
| 2 | Cured ply thickness | $t_{ply} = \frac{W_{FAW}}{V_f \cdot \rho_f}$ | $W_{FAW}$ = prepreg fiber areal weight (g/m²) | Ply-book design |
| 3 | Laminate void content (ASTM D2734) | $V_{voids} = 1 - \rho_{composite} \cdot \left( \frac{w_f}{\rho_f} + \frac{w_m}{\rho_m} \right)$ | $w_f, w_m$ = weight fractions | Quality acceptance |
| 4 | Darcy's law for resin flow | $v = -\frac{K}{\mu} \cdot \frac{dP}{dx}$ | $K$ = permeability (m²); $\mu$ = viscosity (Pa·s); $P$ = pressure (Pa) | Resin flow in prepreg |
| 5 | Kozeny-Carman permeability | $K = \frac{r_f^2}{4 k_{KC}} \cdot \frac{(1-V_f)^3}{V_f^2}$ | $r_f$ = fiber radius (m); $k_{KC}$ ≈ 150–180 | Predicting K from Vf |
| 6 | Arrhenius cure rate | $k(T) = A \cdot e^{-E_a/(RT)}$ | $A$ = pre-exponent (1/s); $E_a$ = activation energy (J/mol); $R$ = 8.314 J/(mol·K) | Predict cure time |
| 7 | Void pressure collapse condition | $\Delta P > \frac{2 \gamma}{r_{void}}$ | $\gamma$ = resin surface tension (~0.03 N/m); $r_{void}$ = void radius (m) | Minimum pressure for void suppression |
| 8 | Total cure cycle time estimate | $t_{cycle} = t_{ramp1} + t_{dwell1} + t_{ramp2} + t_{dwell2} + t_{cool}$ | Each term in minutes | Process planning |
| 9 | Henry's gas solubility | $C = k_H \cdot P$ | $k_H$ = Henry constant; $P$ = partial pressure (Pa) | Void dissolution under pressure |
| 10 | Volumetric consolidation pressure | $P_{eff} = P_{autoclave} - P_{vacuum}$ | Pressures in bar or Pa | Net consolidation force on laminate |
| 11 | Rule of mixtures (laminate modulus) | $E_{L} = E_f V_f + E_m (1-V_f)$ | $E_f, E_m$ = Young's moduli (GPa) | Transverse stiffness of UD laminate |
| 12 | Resin gel time (isothermal) | $t_{gel} \approx \frac{1}{A} \cdot e^{E_a/(RT)}$ | Same as Arrhenius; relates to viscosity rise | Determining pressure application window |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation #1: Fiber Volume Fraction from Prepreg Areal Weights

**Problem Statement:** A unidirectional carbon-fiber/epoxy prepreg has a fiber areal weight of **196 g/m²** and a resin areal weight of **109 g/m²**. The carbon fiber density is **1.80 g/cm³** and the cured epoxy density is **1.20 g/cm³**. Determine the cured-laminate fiber volume fraction, $V_f$.

**Given:**
- $W_{FAW} = 196 \text{ g/m}^2$ (fiber areal weight)
- $W_{RAW} = 109 \text{ g/m}^2$ (resin areal weight, i.e., the resin content of the prepreg per unit area)
- $\rho_f = 1.80 \text{ g/cm}^3 = 1800 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- $\rho_m = 1.20 \text{ g/cm}^3 = 1200 \text{ kg/m}^3$

**Step 1 — Definition of Vf:**

The fiber volume fraction is the ratio of the fiber volume to the total composite volume (fiber + resin) per unit area of one cured ply:

$$V_f = \frac{V_f^{vol}}{V_f^{vol} + V_m^{vol}}$$

**Step 2 — Express volumes in terms of areal weights and densities:**

For an area of $1 \text{ m}^2$:

$$V_f^{vol} = \frac{W_{FAW}}{\rho_f} \quad \text{and} \quad V_m^{vol} = \frac{W_{RAW}}{\rho_m}$$

Substituting:

$$V_f = \frac{W_{FAW}/\rho_f}{(W_{FAW}/\rho_f) + (W_{RAW}/\rho_m)}$$

**Step 3 — Convert densities to compatible units (g/m² ÷ g/cm³ → cm³):**

$$V_f^{vol} = \frac{196 \text{ g/m}^2}{1.80 \text{ g/cm}^3} = 108.89 \text{ cm}^3/\text{m}^2$$

$$V_m^{vol} = \frac{109 \text{ g/m}^2}{1.20 \text{ g/cm}^3} = 90.83 \text{ cm}^3/\text{m}^2$$

**Step 4 — Compute Vf:**

$$V_f = \frac{108.89}{108.89 + 90.83} = \frac{108.89}{199.72}$$

$$\boxed{V_f = 0.5452 \approx 54.5\%}$$

**Step 5 — Engineering interpretation:**

A $V_f$ of 54.5% is slightly below the aerospace target of 58–62%. To raise $V_f$, the autoclave pressure would need to be increased (squeezing more resin out into the bleeder), or a higher-modulus prepreg with lower resin content would be selected.

---

## 3.2 Derivation #2: Cured Ply Thickness and Number of Plies Required

**Problem Statement:** For the prepreg above ($W_{FAW} = 196 \text{ g/m}^2$, $\rho_f = 1.80 \text{ g/cm}^3$), if the target cured $V_f = 0.60$, find:
(a) The cured ply thickness.
(b) The number of plies needed for a laminate of total thickness $2.5 \text{ mm}$.

**Step 1 — Derive the cured ply thickness formula.**

For a single cured ply of unit area, the fiber volume per unit area is $V_f \cdot t_{ply}$, where $t_{ply}$ is ply thickness. Equating this to the fiber volume per unit area from the prepreg:

$$V_f \cdot t_{ply} \cdot \rho_f = W_{FAW}$$

Solving for $t_{ply}$:

$$t_{ply} = \frac{W_{FAW}}{V_f \cdot \rho_f}$$

**Step 2 — Convert to consistent units.**

$W_{FAW} = 196 \text{ g/m}^2 = 0.196 \text{ kg/m}^2$, $\rho_f = 1800 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $V_f = 0.60$:

$$t_{ply} = \frac{0.196}{0.60 \times 1800} = \frac{0.196}{1080} = 1.815 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}$$

$$\boxed{t_{ply} \approx 0.1815 \text{ mm} \approx 181.5 \ \mu\text{m}}$$

**Step 3 — Number of plies for 2.5 mm laminate:**

$$N = \frac{t_{laminate}}{t_{ply}} = \frac{2.5 \text{ mm}}{0.1815 \text{ mm/ply}}$$

$$\boxed{N \approx 13.77 \Rightarrow 14 \text{ plies (round up)}}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation #3: Minimum Autoclave Pressure for Void Suppression

**Problem Statement:** A prepreg laminate contains spherical microvoids of radius $r_{void} = 50 \ \mu\text{m}$ filled with trapped air. The resin surface tension is $\gamma = 0.035 \text{ N/m}$. What is the *minimum* external gauge pressure that must be applied (in addition to vacuum) to collapse these voids?

**Step 1 — Force balance on a spherical void.**

The internal pressure of a spherical gas bubble in a liquid (resin) must overcome the surface-tension-induced Laplace pressure for the bubble to collapse. The Laplace pressure inside a bubble is:

$$\Delta P_{Laplace} = \frac{2\gamma}{r_{void}}$$

**Step 2 — Compute Laplace pressure:**

$$\Delta P_{Laplace} = \frac{2 \times 0.035}{50 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{0.070}{5 \times 10^{-5}}$$

$$\Delta P_{Laplace} = 1400 \text{ Pa} = 0.014 \text{ bar}$$

**Step 3 — Interpretation:**

This is an extraordinarily small pressure — a mere **0.014 bar** (about 1.4 kPa) is theoretically sufficient to collapse a 50 μm void. In practice, the required pressure is much higher (typically **3–7 bar**) because:

1. Voids in real prepregs are *much smaller* (often **1–10 μm**), raising $\Delta P_{Laplace}$ to **0.07–0.7 bar**.
2. The resin is *viscous*, slowing bubble collapse.
3. Gas dissolution (Henry's Law) requires time and pressure differential.
4. Surface tension of contaminated/aged resin is *higher* and variable.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Takeaway:** The empirical 3–7 bar standard is a *practical* pressure — not a theoretical minimum. The actual pressure is chosen to ensure **complete void dissolution and laminate compaction** within the resin's low-viscosity window.

---

## 3.4 Derivation #4: Estimating Cure Cycle Time Using Arrhenius Kinetics

**Problem Statement:** An epoxy resin has activation energy $E_a = 70 \text{ kJ/mol}$ and pre-exponential factor $A = 1.5 \times 10^8 \text{ s}^{-1}$. Estimate the time to reach degree of cure $\alpha = 0.5$ at cure temperature $T = 175 \text{°C}$, assuming first-order kinetics ($n = 1$).

**Step 1 — Write the first-order rate law:**

$$\frac{d\alpha}{dt} = k(T) \cdot (1-\alpha), \quad k(T) = A \cdot e^{-E_a/(RT)}$$

**Step 2 — Integrate from $\alpha = 0$ to $\alpha = 0.5$:**

$$\int_0^{0.5} \frac{d\alpha}{1-\alpha} = k \int_0^{t_{0.5}} dt$$

$$-\ln(1-\alpha) \Big|_0^{0.5} = k \cdot t_{0.5}$$

$$-\ln(0.5) = k \cdot t_{0.5} \Rightarrow t_{0.5} = \frac{\ln 2}{k} = \frac{0.6931}{k}$$

**Step 3 — Compute k at T = 175 °C = 448.15 K:**

$$\frac{E_a}{RT} = \frac{70{,}000}{8.314 \times 448.15} = \frac{70{,}000}{3725.2} = 18.79$$

$$e^{-18.79} = 6.73 \times 10^{-9}$$

$$k = 1.5 \times 10^8 \times 6.73 \times 10^{-9} = 1.010 \text{ s}^{-1}$$

**Step 4 — Compute t₀.₅:**

$$t_{0.5} = \frac{0.6931}{1.010} = 0.686 \text{ s}$$

**Step 5 — Engineering interpretation:**

The isothermal calculation suggests that at 175 °C, half-cure occurs in **< 1 second**, which is unrealistically fast. This is because the simple Arrhenius model neglects:

- The diffusion-control regime at high $\alpha$ (gelation + vitrification)
- Autocatalytic behavior of amine-cured epoxies
- Heat-up ramp time (which dominates the actual cycle)

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world cure cycle:** The actual dwell at 175 °C is **120–180 minutes** because the resin must:
> 1. Reach the cure temperature (heat-up ramp).
> 2. Maintain low viscosity long enough for void removal.
> 3. Reach high conversion (typically $\alpha \geq 0.95$) for full $T_g$ development.
> 4. Allow post-cure if needed.

---

## 3.5 Derivation #5: Resin Flow Velocity Through Prepreg Bed (Darcy's Law)

**Problem Statement:** A prepreg bed has permeability $K = 5 \times 10^{-13} \text{ m}^2$. During the consolidation phase, the resin viscosity is $\mu = 1.0 \text{ Pa·s}$. A pressure gradient of $dP/dx = 1.0 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa/m}$ is applied. Find the resin flow velocity.

**Step 1 — Apply Darcy's Law:**

$$v = -\frac{K}{\mu} \cdot \frac{dP}{dx}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values:**

$$v = \frac{5 \times 10^{-13}}{1.0} \times 1.0 \times 10^6 = 5 \times 10^{-7} \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 3 — Convert to more intuitive units:**

$$v = 5 \times 10^{-7} \text{ m/s} = 0.5 \ \mu\text{m/s} = 1.8 \text{ mm/hour}$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation:**

A resin flow velocity of **~1.8 mm/hr** is typical of autoclave consolidation. For a 5 mm-thick laminate, this means the resin can redistribute through the entire thickness in about **2.8 hours** — which sets the *minimum* dwell time at low viscosity.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Autoclave Process — End-to-End Functional Flow

The following Mermaid flowchart captures the **full process chain** from frozen prepreg storage to a cured composite part, with the critical decision points and quality gates.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Step A: Prepreg Storage at minus 18 C] --> B[Step B: Thaw at Room Temp 8 to 24 hr sealed]
    B --> C[Step C: CNC Ply Cutting per Ply Book]
    C --> D[Step D: Layup on Tool with Stacking Sequence]
    D --> E[Step E: Interply Debulking every 3 to 5 plies under vacuum]
    E --> F[Step F: Vacuum Bag Assembly tool releasefilm prepreg releasefilm bleeder breather bag]
    F --> G[Step G: Vacuum Leak Check below 50 mbar min]
    G --> H[Step H: Load into Autoclave Apply Vacuum to Bag]
    H --> I[Step I: Heat Ramp 1 to 120 C at 1 to 3 C per min]
    I --> J[Step J: Apply Full Pressure 6 bar 90 psi]
    J --> K[Step K: Dwell 1 120 C for 30 to 60 min low viscosity consolidation]
    K --> L[Step L: Heat Ramp 2 to 177 C at 1 to 2 C per min]
    L --> M[Step M: Dwell 2 177 C for 120 to 180 min full cure]
    M --> N[Step N: Cool Down 1 to 3 C per min to below 60 C]
    N --> O[Step O: Release Pressure and Vacuum]
    O --> P[Step Q: De bag and Extract Part]
    P --> Q[Step R: NDI Inspection Ultrasonic C scan X ray]
    Q --> R{Quality Gate Void content below 1 percent Vf above 58 percent}
    R -->|Pass| S[Step S: Trim Machine and Assemble]
    R -->|Fail| T[Step T: Reject or Recycle]
```

## 4.2 Vacuum Bagging Stack — Cross-Sectional Block Architecture

This diagram represents the *layered architecture* of a vacuum-bagged prepreg layup, showing the relative position and function of each consumable layer.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SB1["External Environment (Atmosphere in Autoclave)"]
        direction LR
        L0["PRESSURE 6 bar from autoclave acting on bag"]
    end

    subgraph SB2["Vacuum Bag Stack (top to bottom)"]
        direction TB
        L1["Vacuum Bag Film nylon or elastomer sealed to tool"]
        L2["Breather Felt distributes vacuum stores air flow"]
        L3["Bleeder non woven absorbs excess resin"]
        L4["Release Film Top perforated releases from breather"]
        L5["PREPREG LAMINATE the part being cured"]
        L6["Release Film Bottom solid releases from tool"]
        L7["TOOL mandrel or mold surface"]
    end

    subgraph SB3["Internal Bag Environment (under Vacuum 50 mbar)"]
        direction LR
        L8["VACUUM 50 mbar absolute inside bag pulls resin consolidates plies"]
    end

    L0 --> L1
    L1 --> L2
    L2 --> L3
    L3 --> L4
    L4 --> L5
    L5 --> L6
    L6 --> L7
    L5 -.-> L8
```

## 4.3 Autoclave Cure Cycle — Time-Temperature-Pressure Profile

This diagram shows how the **three control variables** (temperature, pressure, vacuum) evolve in time during a typical aerospace cure cycle.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PHASE1["Phase 1: Heat Up and Vacuum"]
        T1["Temp ramps 1 to 3 C per min from 25 C to 120 C"]
        P1["Pressure atmospheric initially"]
        V1["Vacuum applied full minus 0.95 bar"]
    end

    subgraph PHASE2["Phase 2: Pressure Application and Dwell 1"]
        T2["Temp holds at 120 C for 30 to 60 min"]
        P2["Pressure ramps to 6 bar 90 psi"]
        V2["Vacuum maintained for void removal"]
    end

    subgraph PHASE3["Phase 3: Final Cure and Dwell 2"]
        T3["Temp ramps to 177 C and dwells 120 to 180 min"]
        P3["Pressure holds at 6 bar"]
        V3["Vacuum may be released after gelation"]
    end

    subgraph PHASE4["Phase 4: Cool Down and Depressurize"]
        T4["Temp cools at 1 to 3 C per min to below 60 C"]
        P4["Pressure released only after temp below 60 C"]
        V4["Vacuum released before opening autoclave"]
    end

    PHASE1 --> PHASE2 --> PHASE3 --> PHASE4
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Block-Diagram Note:** These schematics are *block-level functional representations* of the autoclave process. They map process interactions rather than depicting physical geometry, which would require complex CAD drawings unsuitable for Mermaid rendering. The block format preserves clarity for KTU exam preparation and process-flow understanding.
