# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> *Each Part-A question is mapped to a typical KTU Part-A slot (Module 3, 3 marks, ~50 words answer).*

---

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Model Question, May 2024]**
**Define a prepreg. Why is it stored at −18 °C, and what is meant by "out-time"?**

**Model Answer (≈55 words):**
A *prepreg* is a reinforcing fabric pre-impregnated with a *partially cured (B-staged) thermoset resin*, ready for layup and cure. It is stored at **−18 °C** to arrest the cure reaction and prevent premature crosslinking. *Out-time* is the cumulative time the prepreg spends at room temperature (typically **≤ 10–30 days**), beyond which the resin advances excessively and the prepreg becomes unusable.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Model Question, Dec 2023]**
**List the three stimuli applied inside an autoclave and state the primary physical effect of each.**

**Model Answer (≈60 words):**
The three stimuli are:
1. **Heat** (120–180 °C) → activates cure kinetics via Arrhenius temperature dependence and reduces resin viscosity to enable flow.
2. **Pressure** (3–7 bar) → compacts the laminate, suppresses void formation, and increases fiber volume fraction.
3. **Vacuum** (≤ 100 mbar) → removes entrapped air, distributes resin through the fiber bed, and vents volatiles.

Together they yield void-free, high-Vf aerospace laminates.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> *As per KTU 2024 ESE regulation: each Part-B question carries 14 marks split into (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks, with internal choice between two questions (OR option).*

---

### **QUESTION A (Choice 1) — [KTU University Exam — Model Question, May 2024]**

**(a)** With a neat schematic, describe the **vacuum bagging sequence** used in autoclave processing of a prepreg layup. Explain the function of each layer in the bagging stack. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A carbon-fiber/epoxy prepreg has a fiber areal weight of **200 g/m²** and a resin areal weight of **120 g/m²**. Given $\rho_f = 1.78 \text{ g/cm}^3$ and $\rho_m = 1.25 \text{ g/cm}^3$:
  (i) Calculate the **cured fiber volume fraction** $V_f$.
  (ii) If the target $V_f$ is **0.62**, what **cured ply thickness** is required?
  (iii) How many plies are needed for a **3.0 mm** thick laminate? **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q-A (a):

**Vacuum Bagging Stack (tool side → bag side):**

| Layer (from tool up) | Material | Function |
|----------------------|----------|----------|
| 1. Tool/Mold | Aluminum, steel, or CFRP | Defines part geometry, transmits pressure |
| 2. Release film (bottom) | Perforated or solid PTFE | Prevents adhesion to tool; controls resin bleed |
| 3. Prepreg laminate | Carbon/glass + B-staged resin | The part being cured |
| 4. Release film (top) | Perforated PTFE | Allows resin bleeding out, blocks breather contamination |
| 5. Bleeder ply | Non-woven polyester/glass | Absorbs excess resin; sets final resin content |
| 6. Breather ply | Felt or non-woven | Distributes vacuum uniformly; provides air flow path |
| 7. Vacuum bag | Nylon or elastomeric film | Seals the assembly; contains vacuum |
| 8. Sealant tape | Butyl rubber tacky tape | Seals bag to tool |

> Each layer is consumable; the bleeder and breather together *control* the final resin content and hence the $V_f$ of the cured laminate. (Full 7 marks for correctly naming all layers, stating functions, and indicating process significance.)

---

#### Model Solution for Q-A (b):

**(i) Cured $V_f$:**

$$V_f = \frac{W_{FAW}/\rho_f}{(W_{FAW}/\rho_f) + (W_{RAW}/\rho_m)}$$

Compute volumes per m²:

$$V_f^{vol} = \frac{200}{1.78} = 112.36 \text{ cm}^3/\text{m}^2$$

$$V_m^{vol} = \frac{120}{1.25} = 96.00 \text{ cm}^3/\text{m}^2$$

$$V_f = \frac{112.36}{112.36 + 96.00} = \frac{112.36}{208.36} = 0.5393$$

$$\boxed{V_f \approx 0.539 \text{ or } 53.9\%}$$

**(ii) Cured ply thickness at $V_f = 0.62$:**

$$t_{ply} = \frac{W_{FAW}}{V_f \cdot \rho_f} = \frac{200 \text{ g/m}^2}{0.62 \times 1.78 \text{ g/cm}^3}$$

Convert: $W_{FAW} = 0.200 \text{ kg/m}^2$, $\rho_f = 1780 \text{ kg/m}^3$:

$$t_{ply} = \frac{0.200}{0.62 \times 1780} = \frac{0.200}{1103.6} = 1.813 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}$$

$$\boxed{t_{ply} \approx 0.181 \text{ mm} = 181 \ \mu\text{m}}$$

**(iii) Number of plies for 3.0 mm laminate:**

$$N = \frac{3.0 \text{ mm}}{0.181 \text{ mm/ply}} = 16.57$$

$$\boxed{N = 17 \text{ plies (rounded up to integer)}}$$

---

### **QUESTION B (Choice 2) — [KTU University Exam — Model Question, Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Explain the **three simultaneous stimuli** (heat, pressure, vacuum) applied during autoclave curing of prepregs. Discuss the role of each in achieving low void content and high fiber volume fraction. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **cured ply thickness** expression $t_{ply} = W_{FAW}/(V_f \cdot \rho_f)$ starting from the definition of fiber volume fraction. An aerospace laminate must achieve $V_f = 0.60$ with a prepreg of fiber areal weight **195 g/m²** and fiber density **1.80 g/cm³**. If the autoclave pressure is reduced, what happens to the ply thickness and the resulting $V_f$? Justify with a one-sentence engineering explanation. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q-B (a):

| Stimulus | Magnitude (typical) | Physical Effect | Mechanism |
|----------|---------------------|-----------------|-----------|
| **Heat** | 120–180 °C | Activates resin cure; lowers viscosity | Arrhenius kinetics: $k = A e^{-E_a/RT}$; viscosity drops 100–1000× as T approaches $T_g$ |
| **Pressure** | 3–7 bar hydrostatic | Compacts plies; collapses voids; drives resin into bleeder | Hydrostatic compression: $\Delta P_{net} = P_{autoclave} - P_{vacuum}$ acts on the bag |
| **Vacuum** | ≤ 100 mbar absolute | Removes inter-ply air; pulls resin; vents volatiles | Pressure differential across bag; reduces boiling point of volatiles |

**Synthesis:** Before gelation, resin viscosity is at a minimum (typically **0.1–10 Pa·s**). The combined pressure differential and vacuum drive *resin flow* (Darcy) and *void dissolution* (Henry). After gelation, the resin vitrifies and locks in the consolidated, void-free state. (7 marks for stating all three stimuli, magnitudes, mechanisms, and synthesis.)

---

#### Model Solution for Q-B (b):

**Derivation of $t_{ply} = W_{FAW}/(V_f \cdot \rho_f)$:**

Consider a single cured ply of area $A$ and thickness $t_{ply}$. The fiber mass within the ply is:

$$m_f = W_{FAW} \cdot A$$

The fiber volume within the ply is:

$$V_f^{fiber} = \frac{m_f}{\rho_f} = \frac{W_{FAW} \cdot A}{\rho_f}$$

By definition, fiber volume fraction is the ratio of fiber volume to total ply volume:

$$V_f = \frac{V_f^{fiber}}{V_{ply}} = \frac{W_{FAW} \cdot A / \rho_f}{A \cdot t_{ply}} = \frac{W_{FAW}}{\rho_f \cdot t_{ply}}$$

Rearranging:

$$\boxed{t_{ply} = \frac{W_{FAW}}{V_f \cdot \rho_f}}$$

**Numerical evaluation** for $W_{FAW} = 195 \text{ g/m}^2$, $\rho_f = 1.80 \text{ g/cm}^3 = 1800 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $V_f = 0.60$:

$$t_{ply} = \frac{0.195 \text{ kg/m}^2}{0.60 \times 1800 \text{ kg/m}^3} = \frac{0.195}{1080} = 1.806 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}$$

$$\boxed{t_{ply} \approx 0.181 \text{ mm}}$$

**Effect of reduced autoclave pressure:** If pressure is reduced, **less resin is squeezed into the bleeder**, so the cured ply contains *more resin* (and *less fiber volume fraction*). The ply thickness *increases* (since $t_{ply} \propto 1/V_f$), and the resulting $V_f$ *decreases* — producing a resin-rich, lower-performance laminate. (Engineering justification in one sentence: *Lower pressure yields lower compaction, higher resin retention, and hence lower $V_f$ and thicker plies.*)

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Autoclave/Prepreg Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing prepreg storage temperature with cure temperature.** Storage is **−18 °C**; cure is **+120 to +180 °C**. Examiners deduct 1 mark for this mix-up.
> 2. **Forgetting to convert areal weights and densities to consistent SI units** (g/m² → kg/m²; g/cm³ → kg/m³) before computing $V_f$. Unit mismatch = 1-mark penalty.
> 3. **Stating "autoclave = oven."** An autoclave is *not* an oven — it is a **pressure vessel** that *also* heats. Ovens cannot apply hydrostatic pressure.
> 4. **Omitting one of the three stimuli.** A complete answer must reference **heat + pressure + vacuum** together. Listing only two is a 2-mark deduction.
> 5. **Confusing the *bleeder* and *breather*.** Bleeder absorbs resin; breather distributes vacuum. Reversing their functions costs 1 mark.
> 6. **Skipping the "rule-of-mixtures" style derivation of $V_f$.** Examiners expect the full derivation from areal weights + densities, not just the final number.
> 7. **Mixing up the role of Darcy's law (resin flow) with Henry's law (void dissolution).** These are *complementary but distinct* mechanisms — use them in the correct context.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Print/Save This Section**

- **Prepreg:** Reinforcement fabric pre-impregnated with B-staged thermoset resin; stored at **−18 °C**; has a finite **out-time** (10–30 days at 21 °C).
- **Autoclave** = *pressure vessel* that applies **heat + pressure + vacuum simultaneously** — not merely an oven.
- **Three stimuli magnitudes (typical aerospace):** $T = 120$–$180$ °C, $P = 3$–$7$ bar (≈ 45–100 psi), $P_{vacuum} \leq 100$ mbar.
- **Vacuum bag stack (bottom → top):** Tool → Release film → Prepreg → Release film → Bleeder → Breather → Vacuum bag → Sealant tape.
- **Bleeder absorbs resin; Breather distributes vacuum** — never reverse these.
- **Cure cycle phases:** Heat-up → Dwell 1 (low-viscosity consolidation) → Heat-up to final T → Dwell 2 (full cure) → Cool-down → Depressurize.
- **Pressure is applied *before* gelation** but *after* the resin viscosity reaches its minimum.
- **Key formulas (memorize for exam):**
  - $V_f = \dfrac{W_{FAW}/\rho_f}{W_{FAW}/\rho_f + W_{RAW}/\rho_m}$
  - $t_{ply} = \dfrac{W_{FAW}}{V_f \cdot \rho_f}$
  - $K = \dfrac{r_f^2}{4k_{KC}} \cdot \dfrac{(1-V_f)^3}{V_f^2}$ (Kozeny-Carman)
  - $v = -\dfrac{K}{\mu} \cdot \dfrac{dP}{dx}$ (Darcy)
  - $k(T) = A \cdot e^{-E_a/RT}$ (Arrhenius)
  - $\Delta P_{Laplace} = \dfrac{2\gamma}{r_{void}}$ (void collapse)
- **Aerospace quality targets:** $V_f \geq 58\%$, void content $\leq 1\%$.
- **Why all three stimuli?** Heat = cure; Pressure = compaction + void suppression; Vacuum = air removal + resin distribution.
- **Henry's Law governs void dissolution under pressure**; **Darcy's Law governs resin flow through fiber bed**.
- **Cool-down must be controlled (1–3 °C/min) and pressure released only below 60 °C** to avoid thermal shock and part distortion.
- **Permeability $K$** of prepreg bed is typically **10⁻¹² to 10⁻¹⁴ m²** — a *very* tight medium for resin flow.
- **Resin minimum viscosity window** (where consolidation must occur) is typically **0.1–10 Pa·s** — a narrow process window of **~10–20 minutes**.
- **Common defects avoided by autoclave processing:** voids, dry spots, porosity, delamination, fiber misalignment, and low $V_f$.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Pultrusion and filament winding
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT412 - COMPOSITE MATERIALS AND CERAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Pultrusion and filament winding

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Pultrusion — Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Pultrusion** is a continuous, automated, non-autoclave manufacturing process used to produce constant cross-section fibre-reinforced polymer (FRP) profiles by **pulling** (not pushing) reinforcing fibres through a resin impregnation bath and a heated steel die where curing (cross-linking) of the thermoset matrix takes place, followed by a cut-off saw that sections the cured profile to length.

The etymology of the word is **PULL + EXTRUSION** → **PULTRUSION**. Whereas extrusion pushes material through a die, pultrusion pulls the reinforcement package. The product is typically a long, straight, prismatic structural profile (rods, tubes, channels, I-beams, ladder rails, FRP gratings, electrical insulation rods).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Pultrusion belongs to the family of *continuous-length composite manufacturing processes* and is characterized by **high fibre volume fraction (V_f ≈ 0.40 to 0.60)**, **excellent fibre alignment along the pulling axis (0°)**, and **production rates** ranging from **0.5 to 4 m/min**.

### 1.2 Pultrusion — Intuitive Analogy

Think of **making noodles at home** 🍜. You take dough (resin) and you *push* it through a die using a hand press (extrusion). Now imagine instead you take dry spaghetti strands (fibres), dip them in sauce (resin bath), and **pull** the sauce-coated strands through a hot, shaped nozzle that hardens them into a rigid, structured stick. The act of pulling the strands through the hot die is exactly what pultrusion does — except at industrial scale, with thousands of glass or carbon fibres simultaneously.

## 1.3 Filament Winding — Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Filament Winding** is a closed-mold (rotating-mandrel) composite fabrication process in which continuous reinforcing fibre rovings, after passing through a resin bath, are wound under controlled tension onto a rotating mandrel at predetermined winding angles, after which the laminate is cured (typically in an oven or autoclave) and the mandrel is removed to yield a hollow, axisymmetric composite structure.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Filament winding is the **primary manufacturing route** for hollow, rotationally symmetric composite products such as **pressure vessels, pipes, rocket motor casings, CNG cylinders, storage tanks, and drive shafts**. The process enables precision **placement of fibres at any helical angle (typically 7° to 90°)** to optimize directional strength.

### 1.4 Filament Winding — Intuitive Analogy

Imagine you are wrapping a **balloon with a long sticky thread** 🎈. As the balloon rotates on a stick (mandrel), and you walk along its length while dispensing thread, the thread lays onto the balloon in a beautiful criss-cross (helical) pattern. By choosing how fast you walk versus how fast the balloon spins, you control the angle of the wrap. The sticky thread is the resin-impregnated fibre, the balloon is the mandrel, and the pattern you create is the structural skeleton of a pressure vessel.

## 1.5 Common Reinforcement & Resin Systems

| Component | Pultrusion | Filament Winding |
|-----------|-----------|------------------|
| **Fibres (Rovings/Tows)** | E-glass, S-glass, Carbon, Aramid | E-glass, S-glass, Carbon, Aramid, Boron |
| **Matrix Resins** | Polyester, Vinyl Ester, Epoxy, Phenolic | Epoxy, Polyester, Vinyl Ester, BMI (high temp) |
| **Surface Veil** | Polyester surface veil for UV/finish | Not always required |
| **Filler** | CaCO₃, Al(OH)₃ (fire retardant) | Usually none |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Reference:** The **ASTM D3917** and **ASTM D2996** standards govern pultruded and filament-wound FRP products respectively. Boiling-water absorption limits and Barcol hardness are typical acceptance criteria.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Pultrusion Process — Stage-by-Stage Logic

The pultrusion line is a *continuous* sequence of stations, each performing a specific function. Let us break it down.

### Stage 1: Fibre Creel & Roving Guides
- Continuous fibre rovings are mounted on a **creel rack** (typically several hundred to a few thousand ends).
- Number of rovings = **n_rovings**, each with a **yield** in **tex** (g/km) or **yield** in **yards/lb** (for E-glass: ~225 yield = 198 g/km ≈ 1980 dtex).
- Total fibre mass throughput: $\dot{m}_f = n \cdot Y_{tex} \cdot v_{line} \times 10^{-6}$ **(kg/min)**, where $v_{line}$ is line speed in m/min.

> The fibres are threaded through **card clothing / roving guides** to prevent entanglement and maintain tension uniformity.

### Stage 2: Resin Impregnation (Wet-Out Station)
- Rovings pass through a **resin bath** (open tank) or **resin injection chamber** (closed die impregnation for cleaner working).
- **Wet-out efficiency** depends on viscosity, surface tension, fibre–resin contact time, and resin temperature.
- Resin viscosity range: **200 – 1000 cP** at bath temperature (25–40 °C).
- For closed-injection pultrusion (CIP), resin pressure is **1–5 bar** to assist impregnation.

### Stage 3: Pre-Forming Guides
- After wet-out, the wet fibre bundle passes through **pre-forming dies** (shaped guides) that:
  1. Remove excess resin (with doctor blades or squeeze-out rollers).
  2. Shape the cross-section.
  3. Consolidate the laminate.
  4. Distribute and orient mat/cloth layers.

### Stage 4: Heated Curing Die
- The heart of pultrusion. Length **L_die = 0.5 to 1.5 m**, temperature **T_die = 120 °C to 180 °C** (polyester) or up to **200 °C** (epoxy).
- Three temperature zones:
  - **Pre-heat zone** (entry): resin viscosity drops, gelation starts.
  - **Gel & cure zone** (middle): exothermic peak; exotherm temperature can reach **180–220 °C** for thick sections.
  - **Cooling zone** (exit): profile is cooled below **T_g** before exiting to prevent cracking.

### Stage 5: Pulling Mechanism
- **Reciprocating grippers (caterpillar tracks)** or **continuous-belt pullers** apply a tensile load $F_{pull}$ to drag the laminate through the die.
- The pulling force is balanced against viscous drag at the die wall plus internal friction:

$$F_{pull} = \tau_{wall} \cdot A_{contact} + F_{cure\_shrinkage}$$

where $\tau_{wall}$ is the shear stress at the die–laminate interface (typically **5–30 kPa**).

### Stage 6: Cut-Off Saw
- A **diamond-tipped travelling saw** cuts the cured profile to the required length at a speed synchronized with the line speed.
- Cut length accuracy: **±3 mm** for industrial pultrusion.

## 2.2 The Filament Winding Process — Stage-by-Stage Logic

### Stage 1: Mandrel Preparation
- Mandrel is selected based on the part geometry: **metallic mandrels** (steel/aluminium for high-temp cure) or **plaster/composition/sand mandrels** (sacrificial, dissolved or broken out).
- Mandrel surface is coated with a **release agent** (silicone, wax, PVA).

### Stage 2: Fibre Delivery & Resin Impregnation
- Rovings from the **fibre creel** pass through a **resin bath** or **resin injection ring**.
- Each tow is metered for **tension** ($T_0 = 5$ to $30$ N) using individual **tensioning devices** (mechanical, pneumatic, or magnetic particle brakes).

### Stage 3: Winding Head & Carriage
- The **winding head** dispenses fibres; the **carriage** translates along the mandrel axis.
- Two motions:
  1. Mandrel rotation $\omega$ (rad/s).
  2. Carriage translation $v_c$ (m/s).
- The **winding angle** $\alpha$ is set by the ratio:

$$\tan \alpha = \frac{v_c}{\pi D_{avg} \, \omega}$$

where $D_{avg}$ is the **average winding diameter** (accounting for build-up).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Winding Classification:**
> - **Hoop winding (α ≈ 90°):** Purely circumferential, used for hoop-stress reinforcement.
> - **Helical winding (7° < α < 90°):** General-purpose structural winding.
> - **Polar winding:** Two end rotations per longitudinal pass (used for spherical/closed-end vessels).
> - **Geodesic winding:** Fibre path follows a geodesic (shortest path) on the surface — no inter-ply slip.
> - **Non-geodesic / planar winding:** Fibre path deviates from geodesic; requires **stability analysis** and slip considerations.

### Stage 4: Curing
- After winding, the mandrel is placed in an **oven** (80–150 °C) or **autoclave** (up to 1 MPa, 175 °C for epoxy).
- Typical cure cycle: **2–6 hours** depending on resin and wall thickness.

### Stage 5: Mandrel Extraction
- Mandrel is withdrawn mechanically, dissolved (plaster), or melted (low-melt alloy).
- For non-sacrificial mandrels, the product is pulled off using a hydraulic extractor.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The table below contains every equation a KTU 2024 examiner can plausibly ask for Module 3. Memorize units and boundary conditions.

| # | Equation | Symbol Meaning | Typical Units | Process |
|---|----------|----------------|---------------|---------|
| 1 | $\tan \alpha = \dfrac{v_c}{\pi D_{avg} \, \omega}$ | Winding angle geometry | rad | Filament Winding |
| 2 | $T = T_0 \, e^{\mu \theta}$ | Capstan (Euler) tension build-up | N | Filament Winding |
| 3 | $\sigma_h = \dfrac{p \, r}{t}$ | Hoop stress in a thin-walled pressure vessel | Pa | Filament Winding |
| 4 | $\sigma_L = \dfrac{p \, r}{2t}$ | Longitudinal (axial) stress in PV | Pa | Filament Winding |
| 5 | $\sigma_{f,\alpha} = \dfrac{\sigma_h}{\cos^2 \alpha}$ | Fibre-direction stress in helical winding | Pa | Filament Winding |
| 6 | $F_{pull} = \tau_{wall} \cdot A_{contact}$ | Pultrusion pulling force | N | Pultrusion |
| 7 | $v_{line} = \dfrac{L_{die}}{t_{resin}}$ | Maximum line speed from die residence time | m/s | Pultrusion |
| 8 | $V_f = \dfrac{A_f}{A_f + A_m}$ | Fibre volume fraction (cross-section basis) | dimensionless | Both |
| 9 | $d_{band} = \dfrac{w_{tow}}{\cos \alpha} \cdot \dfrac{1}{2}$ | Band width on a single circuit | m | Filament Winding |
| 10 | $n_{circuits} = \dfrac{L_{mandrel} \cdot \tan \alpha}{\pi D_{avg}}$ | Number of circuits per axial pass | dimensionless | Filament Winding |
| 11 | $\eta_{cure} = 1 - e^{-k t^n}$ | Autocatalytic cure kinetics (Kamal model) | dimensionless | Both |
| 12 | $P_{cure} = \rho \, c_p \, \dot{T}_{exo} \, V$ | Peak exotherm heat generation rate | W | Pultrusion (thick) |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Pultrusion** produces profiles for **walkway gratings** in chemical plants, **third-rail covers** for metros, **transmission poles** (replacing timber/steel), and **cooling-tower structural members**. The global pultruded-FRP market exceeds **USD 5 billion** as of 2024.
- **Filament winding** is the workhorse for **Type IV CNG cylinders** (carbon-fibre/epoxy, 700 bar service), **rocket motor cases** (ISRO's PSOM/PSLV solid boosters used carbon–epoxy wound casings), and **glass-fibre epoxy pipelines** for the oil & gas industry (GRE/GRP pipes, NPS 25 to 4000).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Industry Tip (Frequently asked in interviews):** Type IV CNG cylinders use a **fully wrapped aluminium liner** with a carbon-fibre/epoxy overwrap applied by **wet filament winding** at helical angles between **15° and 30°**, achieving mass savings of **> 40%** versus all-steel cylinders.

# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Winding Angle Equation (Filament Winding)

This is the **most heavily tested derivation** in Module 3. KTU examiners love it because it ties kinematics to laminate design.

### Setup
Consider a rotating mandrel of **average winding diameter** $D_{avg}$ and a carriage translating at axial speed $v_c$ while the mandrel rotates at angular velocity $\omega$ (rad/s). A single fibre tow is laid on the mandrel surface.

In **one complete revolution**, the mandrel advances the tow by an axial distance $\Delta x$ and wraps the fibre around the circumference.

**Step 1: Time for one full revolution.**

$$T_{rev} = \frac{2\pi}{\omega}$$

**Step 2: Axial distance covered by the carriage during one revolution.**

$$\Delta x = v_c \cdot T_{rev} = \frac{2\pi v_c}{\omega}$$

**Step 3: Unroll the cylindrical surface into a flat plane.** The fibre helix becomes a straight line on the rectangle (circumference × length). The helix is characterized by:
- One side = circumference = $\pi D_{avg}$.
- Other side = axial advance per revolution = $\Delta x = \dfrac{2\pi v_c}{\omega}$.

**Step 4: Define the winding angle** $\alpha$ as the angle between the fibre tow and the **mandrel axis** (not the hoop direction!). Then:

$$\tan \alpha = \frac{\text{circumferential advance per rev}}{\text{axial advance per rev}} = \frac{\pi D_{avg}}{\Delta x}$$

**Step 5: Substitute $\Delta x$ from Step 2.**

$$\tan \alpha = \frac{\pi D_{avg}}{\dfrac{2\pi v_c}{\omega}} = \frac{\pi D_{avg} \, \omega}{2\pi v_c} \cdot 2$$

Wait — let us re-examine the standard convention. In the **KTU 2024 reference materials**, the angle $\alpha$ is measured from the **hoop direction** for some textbooks and from the **axis** for others. The formulation above used the **axis-reference convention**, giving:

$$\tan \alpha_{axis} = \frac{\pi D_{avg} \, \omega}{2 v_c}$$

If instead $\alpha$ is measured from the **hoop (circumferential) direction**, the reciprocal applies:

$$\tan \alpha_{hoop} = \frac{v_c}{\pi D_{avg} \, \omega}$$

**Step 6 (Engineering shortcut):** The "reciprocity" identity is:

$$\tan \alpha_{hoop} = \cot \alpha_{axis} = \frac{1}{\tan \alpha_{axis}}$$

> Both forms are accepted in KTU solutions provided the convention is stated. We will use the **hoop-reference convention** (as is standard in ASTM standards) below:

$$\boxed{\tan \alpha = \frac{v_c}{\pi D_{avg} \, \omega}}$$

### 3.2 Derivation: Number of Circuits Per Axial Pass

**Step 1: Axial coverage of one circuit (one complete helix round-trip, considering forward + return).** In helical winding, the tow advances $\Delta x$ per revolution. The total mandrel length covered by a single circuit (one pass from one end to the other and back) is the **winding pitch**.

$$p = 2 \Delta x = \frac{4\pi v_c}{\omega}$$

**Step 2: Total axial length of the mandrel:**

$$L_{mandrel}$$

**Step 3: Number of circuits to cover the entire mandrel length once:**

$$n_{circuits} = \frac{L_{mandrel}}{p} = \frac{L_{mandrel} \, \omega}{4 \pi v_c}$$

Using the winding-angle relationship $\tan \alpha = \dfrac{v_c}{\pi D_{avg} \omega}$, we can write $v_c = \pi D_{avg} \omega \tan \alpha$:

$$n_{circuits} = \frac{L_{mandrel} \, \omega}{4 \pi \cdot \pi D_{avg} \omega \tan \alpha} = \frac{L_{mandrel}}{4 \pi^2 D_{avg} \tan \alpha}$$

For a **closed helical circuit** (single helix only, no return), the simpler **helical forward** count is:

$$\boxed{n_{circuits} = \frac{L_{mandrel} \cdot \tan \alpha}{\pi D_{avg}}}$$

> This is the version asked most often in KTU. Memorize it.

## 3.3 Derivation: Hoop-Stress in a Thin-Walled Filament-Wound Vessel

Consider a thin-walled cylindrical pressure vessel of **internal radius $r$**, **wall thickness $t$** (with $t \ll r$), subjected to **internal gauge pressure $p$**.

**Step 1: Free-body cut along a longitudinal diametrical plane.** A longitudinal stress $\sigma_L$ acts on the projected area $2 r \cdot L$ (per unit length) balancing the internal pressure force $p \cdot 2 r \cdot 1$ (per unit length):

$$\sigma_L \cdot 2 t = p \cdot 2 r \quad \Rightarrow \quad \sigma_L = \frac{p \, r}{2t}$$

**Step 2: Free-body cut along a transverse plane (perpendicular to axis).** A hoop stress $\sigma_h$ acts on the projected area $2 \cdot L \cdot t$ (per unit length) balancing the pressure force $p \cdot 2 r \cdot 1$ (per unit length). Wait, let us re-cut:

Cut a half-cylinder along an axial plane. The pressure force on the projected diameter is $p \cdot 2r$ (per unit length), and the resisting hoop stress acts over area $2 \cdot t \cdot 1 = 2t$ (per unit length):

$$\sigma_h \cdot 2 t = p \cdot 2 r \quad \Rightarrow \quad \sigma_h = \frac{p \, r}{t}$$

> **Hence: $\sigma_h = 2 \sigma_L$.** This is the famous **2:1 hoop-to-axial stress ratio** that motivates **angle-ply design** in filament-wound vessels.

## 3.4 Derivation: Optimal Winding Angle for a Pressure Vessel

For a closed-end cylindrical pressure vessel, $\sigma_h = 2 \sigma_L$. The laminate must be designed so that the **net fibre-direction stress** matches the pressure-vessel requirements.

**Step 1: For a helically wound laminate with fibres at $\pm \alpha$** (angle-ply) and hoop-ply at $90°$, classical lamination theory gives the **net axial** and **net hoop** forces:

$$N_x = \bar{Q}_{11} \epsilon_x^0 + \bar{Q}_{12} \epsilon_y^0$$
$$N_y = \bar{Q}_{12} \epsilon_x^0 + \bar{Q}_{22} \epsilon_y^0$$

**Step 2: For the optimal case** (no shear-extension coupling, balanced laminate), the optimal helical angle to resist a 2:1 stress ratio satisfies:

$$\tan^2 \alpha = 2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \alpha = 54.74^\circ$$

This famous angle **$\alpha = 54.74^\circ$** is called the **"magic angle" or "isoclinic angle"** in pressure-vessel design.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Exam Favourite:** Be prepared to derive **54.74°**. The derivation is a 5-line application of stress transformation and lamination theory.

## 3.5 Derivation: Pultrusion Line Speed (Residence-Time Limit)

In the heated die, the resin must achieve sufficient **degree of cure** $\alpha_{cure}$ before exit. The cure kinetics are described by the **Kamal autocatalytic model**:

$$\frac{d\alpha}{dt} = (k_1 + k_2 \, \alpha^m)(1-\alpha)^n$$

where $k_1$ and $k_2$ are Arrhenius rate constants ($k_i = A_i e^{-E_i / RT}$), and $m, n$ are reaction orders (typically $m = 1, n = 1$ for polyester).

**Step 1: Define required residence time** $t_{resin}$ as the time needed to reach $\alpha_{cure} \geq 0.85$ (the "no-tack" threshold).

**Step 2: Maximum line speed** is then limited by:

$$v_{line, max} = \frac{L_{die}}{t_{resin}}$$

**Step 3 (Engineering estimate for polyester):** Empirical rule — for a die temperature of **135 °C** and wall thickness of **6 mm**, $t_{resin} \approx 30$ s, giving $v_{line} \approx 0.5$ m/min. Doubling die temperature can increase line speed by **3–5×**.

## 3.6 Derivation: Capstan (Euler) Equation for Fibre Tension

When a fibre tow passes over a friction contact (e.g., a guide roller or a slip ring), the **tension increases exponentially** with wrap angle and friction coefficient:

$$T_{out} = T_{in} \cdot e^{\mu \theta}$$

**Step 1: Differential force balance on an arc element** $ds = r \, d\theta$:

$$dT = \mu \, N = \mu \, T \, d\theta$$

**Step 2: Separate variables and integrate** from $\theta_1$ to $\theta_2$:

$$\int_{T_{in}}^{T_{out}} \frac{dT}{T} = \mu \int_{\theta_1}^{\theta_2} d\theta$$

**Step 3: Result:**

$$\boxed{T_{out} = T_{in} \, e^{\mu (\theta_2 - \theta_1)}}$$

This is the **Euler-Eytelwein capstan equation**, critical for tension control in both pultrusion (where fibres pass over multiple guides) and filament winding (where the tow bends around the payout eye).

## 3.7 Worked Numerical Example (Typical KTU 14-Mark Style)

**Problem:** A filament-wound pressure vessel has an internal diameter of **300 mm** and a wall thickness of **6 mm**. The mandrel rotates at **30 rpm** and the carriage translates at **12 mm/s**. Determine: (a) the winding angle, (b) the hoop and axial stresses at an internal pressure of **1.5 MPa**, and (c) the fibre-direction stress in the helical ply.

**Given:**
- $D_{avg} = 0.300$ m (assume negligible build-up),
- $\omega = 30 \text{ rpm} = \pi \text{ rad/s}$,
- $v_c = 0.012$ m/s,
- $p = 1.5 \text{ MPa}$,
- $t = 0.006$ m.

**Step (a): Winding angle.**

$$\tan \alpha = \frac{v_c}{\pi D_{avg} \omega} = \frac{0.012}{\pi \times 0.300 \times \pi} = \frac{0.012}{2.961} = 0.004053$$

$$\alpha = \arctan(0.004053) = 0.232^\circ$$

This is essentially **pure hoop winding** ($\alpha \approx 0^\circ$ from hoop). To get a more meaningful helical angle, increase $v_c$ or reduce $\omega$.

**Step (b): Hoop and axial stresses.**

$$\sigma_h = \frac{p \, r}{t} = \frac{1.5 \times 10^6 \times 0.150}{0.006} = 37.5 \text{ MPa}$$

$$\sigma_L = \frac{p \, r}{2t} = \frac{37.5}{2} = 18.75 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step (c): Fibre-direction stress.**

$$\sigma_{f,\alpha} = \frac{\sigma_h}{\cos^2 \alpha} = \frac{37.5}{\cos^2(0.232^\circ)} \approx 37.5 \text{ MPa}$$

($\cos(0.232^\circ) \approx 1.000$, so no significant change — as expected for nearly pure hoop winding.)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For a richer engineering example, the examiner may give $v_c = 50$ mm/s, yielding $\alpha \approx 0.97^\circ$ — still nearly hoop. The instructive case is $v_c = 0.5$ m/s, which gives $\alpha \approx 9.65^\circ$ — a meaningful helical angle.

## 3.8 Comparative Analytical Matrix: Pultrusion vs. Filament Winding

| Criterion | Pultrusion | Filament Winding |
|-----------|-----------|------------------|
| Geometry produced | Prismatic, constant cross-section | Axisymmetric (cylinders, spheres, cones) |
| Fibre orientation control | Mostly unidirectional (0°) along pull axis | Full ±α control, hoop, helical, polar |
| Typical $V_f$ | 0.40 – 0.65 | 0.50 – 0.75 |
| Production rate | 0.5 – 4 m/min (continuous) | 5 – 30 min per part (semi-continuous) |
| Capital cost | Medium (USD 0.5 – 3 M) | High (USD 1 – 10 M for 4-axis machines) |
| Part length | Unlimited (cut to size) | Limited by mandrel length (typically ≤ 12 m) |
| Curing | In-line in heated die | Off-line in oven/autoclave |
| Waste | Low (flash negligible) | Moderate (mandrel, end caps) |
| Key products | Beams, rods, gratings, ladder rails | Pressure vessels, pipes, rocket casings |

# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Pultrusion Process Line

The diagram below shows the in-line, continuous architecture of a pultrusion manufacturing line, with material/energy flow indicators.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[FIBRE CREEL RACK] --> A2[ROVING GUIDES AND CARD CLOTHING]
    A2 --> A3[RESIN IMPREGNATION BATH OR RESIN INJECTION CHAMBER]
    A3 --> A4[PRE FORMING DIES WITH SQUEEZE OUT ROLLERS]
    A4 --> A5[HEATED CURING DIE 120 TO 180 C WITH THREE ZONES]
    A5 --> A6[PULLING UNIT CATERPILLAR OR BELT GRIPPERS]
    A6 --> A7[DIAMOND CUT OFF SAW SYNCHRONIZED]
    A7 --> A8[STACKING AND PACKAGING STATION]
    H1[HEAT SOURCE DIE HEATER] --> A5
    A6 --> A2
    R1[RESIN SUPPLY] --> A3
    F1[FIBRE ROVINGS] --> A1
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **solid arrows** (A1 → A2 → A3 ...) represent the **physical motion of the fibre-resin bundle** as it is pulled through the line.
- The **dashed arrows** represent **auxiliary material/energy flows** (resin supply, heat input, feedback for tension control).
- The pulling unit (A6) is the *only* driver; every upstream station is passive. This is why pultrusion is called a **"pull-driven"** process.

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Filament Winding Machine Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    B1[FIBRE CREEL WITH INDIVIDUAL TENSIONERS] --> B2[RESIN BATH OR RESIN INJECTION RING]
    B2 --> B3[FIBRE DELIVERY EYELET OR COMB]
    B3 --> B4[WINDING HEAD MOUNTED ON CARRIAGE]
    B4 --> B5[ROTATING MANDREL DRIVEN BY SPINDLE]
    B5 --> B6[PART LAY UP ON MANDREL SURFACE]
    B6 --> B7[POST WINDING TRANSFER TO OVEN OR AUTOCLAVE]
    B7 --> B8[CURE CYCLE 2 TO 6 HOURS]
    B8 --> B9[MANDREL EXTRACTION]
    B9 --> B10[FINAL MACHINING AND NDT]
    C1[CARRIAGE DRIVE LINEAR ACTUATOR] --> B4
    C2[SPINDLE MOTOR ROTATION] --> B5
    C3[CONTROL SYSTEM PLC] --> C1
    C3 --> C2
    C3 --> B1
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **PLC controller** synchronizes the **mandrel rotation** and the **carriage translation** to maintain a precise winding angle $\alpha$ as per the formula $\tan \alpha = v_c / (\pi D_{avg} \omega)$.
- Tension control is **closed-loop** — each roving has an individual dancer/tensioner.
- The **mandrel remains the mould** — there is no open die, which is why filament winding is classified as a *closed-mould* process.

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Winding Angle Visualization on an Unrolled Cylinder

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    D1[UNROLLED MANDREL SURFACE] --> D2[RECTANGLE OF WIDTH PI TIMES D]
    D2 --> D3[HELIX APPEARS AS A STRAIGHT LINE]
    D3 --> D4[ANGLE ALPHA BETWEEN HELIX AND MANDREL AXIS]
    D4 --> D5[GEODESIC PATHS PRESERVE ANGLE OVER SURFACE]
    D5 --> D6[NON GEODESIC PATHS REQUIRE SLIP OR BRIDGING]
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- This unrolling technique is the conceptual basis for the **winding-angle derivation** in Section 3.1.
- The geodesic path is the *natural* path on a cylinder (and is the path a thread follows if wound without external force).
- For closed-end vessels, **non-geodesic (planar) winding** is often required near the polar openings — this is what makes **polar winding** (used for spherical end caps) a separate design discipline.

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — Process Selection Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[NEW COMPOSITE PART] --> S2{IS THE CROSS SECTION CONSTANT ALONG LENGTH?}
    S2 -- YES --> S3{PROFILE LENGTH OVER 3 METRES?}
    S3 -- YES --> S4[CHOOSE PULTRUSION]
    S3 -- NO --> S5[CONSIDER EXTRUSION WITH FIBRE OR PULTRUSION WITH SHORT LENGTH]
    S2 -- NO --> S6{IS THE PART HOLLOW AND AXISYMMETRIC?}
    S6 -- YES --> S7{IS IT A PRESSURE VESSEL OR PIPE?}
    S7 -- YES --> S8[CHOOSE FILAMENT WINDING]
    S7 -- NO --> S9[CONSIDER CENTRIFUGAL CASTING OR FW]
    S6 -- NO --> S10[CHOOSE LAYUP RTM OR AUTOCLAVE]
```

**Reading the diagram:** This decision tree is a high-yield exam tool. Many KTU Part A questions ask: *"Which process would you use for…?"* — the answer is a direct application of this logic.

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Marking Convention for Part A:** 3 marks are awarded for a precise, well-structured answer. Word limit: 80–100 words. Use bullet points, a diagram, or a labelled figure wherever possible. Definition + example + 1 distinguishing feature is the safe formula.

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

> **Q1.** *Distinguish between pultrusion and extrusion in the context of composite manufacturing. Why is pultrusion preferred for fibre-reinforced polymer (FRP) profiles?*

**Model Answer:**

- **Extrusion** *pushes* a homogeneous thermoplastic melt through a die, while **pultrusion** *pulls* a bundle of continuous reinforcing fibres (pre-impregnated with a thermoset resin) through a heated die.
- In extrusion, the reinforcement (if any) is discontinuous and randomly distributed; in pultrusion, **continuous fibres are aligned along the load-bearing direction (0°)**, yielding a higher specific stiffness and strength.
- Pultrusion is preferred for FRP profiles because it produces **high fibre volume fraction (V_f up to 0.65)**, **excellent axial strength** (along the pull axis), and supports **continuous, automated production** of long structural profiles with minimal labour and low scrap.

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

> **Q2.** *What is filament winding? List any three industrial products manufactured by filament winding.*

**Model Answer:**

- **Filament winding** is a closed-mould composite manufacturing process in which continuous fibre rovings, after passing through a resin bath, are wound under controlled tension at predetermined winding angles onto a rotating mandrel. The laminate is then cured (oven or autoclave), and the mandrel is removed to yield a hollow, axisymmetric composite structure.
- **Three industrial products:**
  1. **CNG / hydrogen storage cylinders (Type IV)** — carbon-fibre/epoxy overwrap on aluminium liner.
  2. **Glass-fibre epoxy pipes (GRE/GRP pipes)** — for oil & gas and chemical transport.
  3. **Rocket motor casings** — filament-wound carbon-epoxy used in ISRO solid rocket boosters.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern for PEMUT412:** Part B has 14-mark questions with **internal choice**. Two fully-worked alternative questions (Question A and Question B) are provided below. Each is divided into two 7-mark sub-parts as per the KTU question-paper template.

### ⭐ QUESTION A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024]

> **Q(A).** *(a) Describe the pultrusion process with the help of a neat labelled line diagram. Discuss the role of the heated die, including its three temperature zones.* **[7 marks]**
>
> *(b) A pultrusion line uses **1200 E-glass rovings**, each of yield **2400 tex (g/km)**, running at a line speed of **1.2 m/min**. The fibre volume fraction of the cured profile is **0.55** and the cross-sectional area of the profile is **1200 mm²**. Determine: (i) the mass throughput of fibres per hour, (ii) the cross-sectional area occupied by the fibres, and (iii) the cross-sectional area occupied by the matrix. Take fibre density $\rho_f = 2.55$ g/cm³.* **[7 marks]**

#### Model Solution for Q(A)(a)

**Pultrusion Process Description:**

Pultrusion is a continuous composite manufacturing process in which dry fibre rovings and/or mat reinforcements are **pulled** through a resin bath (impregnation stage), then through a heated die (curing stage), and finally through a cut-off saw that sections the cured profile.

**The major stations in order are:**

1. **Fibre Creel** — Multiple rovings (glass, carbon, aramid) are mounted on a creel rack under controlled tension.
2. **Roving Guides** — Card-clothing plates align rovings to prevent tangling.
3. **Resin Bath** — Open tank or closed resin-injection chamber where wet-out occurs (resin viscosity 200–1000 cP).
4. **Pre-Forming Dies** — Shape the wet bundle and squeeze out excess resin.
5. **Heated Curing Die** — The heart of the process, with three zones:
   - **Pre-heat zone (entry):** Resin viscosity drops, flows evenly, gelation begins (~80–100 °C).
   - **Gel & cure zone (middle):** Major exothermic cross-linking reaction; peak exotherm can reach 180–220 °C (controlled by zone temperature of 130–170 °C).
   - **Cooling zone (exit):** Profile cools below the glass transition temperature $T_g$ to prevent thermal cracking on exit.
6. **Pulling Unit** — Reciprocating caterpillar or continuous-belt grippers supply the pulling force $F_{pull}$.
7. **Cut-Off Saw** — Diamond-tipped travelling saw cuts the cured profile to the required length.

**Role of the Heated Die:**
The heated die performs **two simultaneous functions**: (i) it **shapes** the cross-section of the profile, and (ii) it **cures** the thermoset resin by maintaining the precise temperature profile required for gelation, vitrification, and full cure. The die wall shear also creates a consolidated, low-porosity laminate.

**Line Diagram:** (Refer to Section 4.1 Mermaid block diagram.)

#### Model Solution for Q(A)(b)

**Given:**
- $n = 1200$ rovings
- $Y = 2400 \text{ tex} = 2400 \text{ g/km} = 2.4 \text{ g/m}$
- $v_{line} = 1.2 \text{ m/min}$
- $V_f = 0.55$
- $A_{total} = 1200 \text{ mm}^2 = 1.2 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$
- $\rho_f = 2.55 \text{ g/cm}^3 = 2550 \text{ kg/m}^3$

**Part (i): Mass throughput of fibres per hour.**

Total linear density of fibres:
$$\rho_{L,total} = n \cdot Y = 1200 \times 2.4 \text{ g/m} = 2880 \text{ g/m}$$

Mass per minute:
$$\dot{m}_f = 2880 \text{ g/m} \times 1.2 \text{ m/min} = 3456 \text{ g/min}$$

Mass per hour:
$$\dot{m}_f = 3456 \times 60 = 207,360 \text{ g/h} = 207.36 \text{ kg/h}$$

$$\boxed{\dot{m}_f = 207.36 \text{ kg/h}}$$

**Part (ii): Cross-sectional area occupied by fibres.**

Volume of fibre in 1 hour:
$$V_f^{vol} = \frac{\dot{m}_f}{\rho_f} = \frac{207.36 \text{ kg/h}}{2550 \text{ kg/m}^3} = 0.0813 \text{ m}^3/\text{h}$$

Length processed in 1 hour:
$$L_h = 1.2 \text{ m/min} \times 60 \text{ min} = 72 \text{ m/h}$$

Cross-sectional area of fibre:
$$A_f = \frac{V_f^{vol}}{L_h} = \frac{0.0813}{72} = 1.129 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2 = 1129 \text{ mm}^2$$

$$\boxed{A_f = 1129 \text{ mm}^2}$$

**Part (iii): Cross-sectional area of the matrix.**

$$A_m = A_{total} - A_f = 1200 - 1129 = 71 \text{ mm}^2$$

**Verification of $V_f$:**
$$V_f = \frac{A_f}{A_{total}} = \frac{1129}{1200} = 0.941$$

> **Note for the student:** The computed $V_f \approx 0.94$ is *much higher* than the given value of 0.55, which suggests the data in this problem is internally inconsistent for a real pultrusion. However, the *calculation method* is correct and matches what the examiner expects. In a real exam, present your computed answer and note the discrepancy; do not change the given $V_f$.

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠ Examiner's Pitfall (Part B Numerical):**
> - Do **not confuse tex units**: 1 tex = 1 g/km = 0.001 g/m.
> - The fibre cross-section should be derived from the **mass throughput divided by length** — many students mistakenly compute the *total* roving area using tex directly without converting to mass.
> - Always state the **glass transition temperature** context when discussing pultrusion die temperature zones. Examiners penalize answers that confuse $T_{die}$ with $T_g$.

---

### ⭐ QUESTION B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

> **Q(B).** *(a) Describe the filament winding process with its classification. Explain the term 'geodesic winding' and its significance.* **[7 marks]**
>
> *(b) A filament-wound pressure vessel has an internal diameter of **500 mm** and a wall thickness of **8 mm**. The mandrel rotation is **20 rpm** and the carriage translation speed is **15 mm/s**. The internal pressure is **2.0 MPa**. Determine: (i) the winding angle, (ii) the hoop and axial stresses, (iii) the fibre-direction stress in the helical ply. Verify whether the laminate is near the optimal 'magic angle'.* **[7 marks]**

#### Model Solution for Q(B)(a)

**Filament Winding Process Description:**

Filament winding is a closed-mould composite manufacturing process in which continuous fibre tows (rovings), after passing through a resin bath under controlled tension, are wound on a rotating mandrel at a precisely controlled winding angle. The wound part is then cured (oven or autoclave), and the mandrel is removed.

**Stages:**
1. Mandrel preparation and release-agent application.
2. Fibre creel and tensioning.
3. Resin bath (or resin injection).
4. Winding on rotating mandrel (spindle + carriage synchronized by PLC).
5. Curing (room-temp, oven, or autoclave).
6. Mandrel extraction and post-processing.

**Classification of Winding Patterns:**

- **Hoop Winding (α ≈ 90°):** Tow is wrapped almost circumferentially; provides hoop-direction reinforcement.
- **Helical Winding (7° < α < 90°):** General-purpose winding at any intermediate angle.
- **Polar Winding:** Fibre path passes through both poles (used for spherical/closed-end vessels).
- **Geodesic Winding:** The tow follows a *geodesic path* (shortest path) on the mandrel surface.
- **Non-Geodesic / Planar Winding:** The tow deviates from a geodesic; stability must be ensured by friction or by over-winding with a hoop layer.

**Geodesic Winding — Significance:**

A **geodesic path** on a doubly-curved surface is the curve of shortest distance between two points (analogous to a great-circle route on a sphere). On a cylinder, the geodesic is a helix at a specific angle that depends on the cylinder diameter.

The significance of geodesic winding:
- The fibre is in a state of **pure tension** (no out-of-plane forces, no slip required).
- The path is **stable** — friction between plies is sufficient to maintain position.
- The fibre does not migrate during cure, so the **as-wound geometry equals the as-cured geometry**.
- For closed-end vessels with spherical or torispherical heads, geodesic paths transition smoothly from the cylindrical body to the dome, avoiding fibre wrinkling or bridging.

#### Model Solution for Q(B)(b)

**Given:**
- $D_{avg} = 0.500$ m
- $\omega = 20 \text{ rpm} = \dfrac{20 \times 2\pi}{60} = 2.094 \text{ rad/s}$
- $v_c = 0.015$ m/s
- $p = 2.0 \text{ MPa} = 2 \times 10^6$ Pa
- $t = 0.008$ m
- $r = 0.250$ m

**Part (i): Winding angle.**

$$\tan \alpha = \frac{v_c}{\pi D_{avg} \omega} = \frac{0.015}{\pi \times 0.500 \times 2.094}$$

Compute denominator:
$$\pi \times 0.500 \times 2.094 = 1.5708 \times 2.094 = 3.290 \text{ m/s}$$

So:
$$\tan \alpha = \frac{0.015}{3.290} = 0.004559$$

$$\alpha = \arctan(0.004559) = 0.261^\circ$$

> **Interpretation:** The winding is essentially **pure hoop** (very small helical component). To achieve a meaningful helical angle, $v_c$ must be increased substantially (e.g., to 0.5 m/s would give $\alpha \approx 8.7^\circ$).

**Part (ii): Hoop and axial stresses.**

$$\sigma_h = \frac{p \, r}{t} = \frac{2 \times 10^6 \times 0.250}{0.008} = \frac{500{,}000}{0.008} = 62.5 \text{ MPa}$$

$$\sigma_L = \frac{\sigma_h}{2} = \frac{62.5}{2} = 31.25 \text{ MPa}$$

**Part (iii): Fibre-direction stress in the helical ply.**

$$\sigma_{f,\alpha} = \frac{\sigma_h}{\cos^2 \alpha} = \frac{62.5}{\cos^2(0.261^\circ)} \approx \frac{62.5}{0.99999} \approx 62.5 \text{ MPa}$$

**Verification against the magic angle (54.74°):**

The **optimal winding angle for closed-end pressure vessels** is:
$$\alpha_{opt} = 54.74^\circ$$

In this problem, $\alpha = 0.261^\circ$, which is **far from the optimal 54.74°**. This means:
- The laminate is essentially a **pure hoop-ply design**.
- It will resist **hoop stress** extremely well but will provide **little axial reinforcement**, requiring a **separate axial-ply strategy** (e.g., a hoop + helical combination, or a filament-wound laminate with alternating hoop and helical layers).
- The **fibre-direction stress** in the helical ply is essentially the full hoop stress (since $\cos^2 \alpha \to 1$).

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠ Examiner's Pitfall (Part B Filament Winding):**
> - **Most common mistake:** Computing the winding angle in the **wrong convention** (axis-vs-hoop reference). State your convention explicitly. The **KTU reference** uses the hoop convention $\tan \alpha = v_c / (\pi D_{avg} \omega)$.
> - **Second common mistake:** Forgetting to convert **rpm to rad/s** ($1 \text{ rpm} = 2\pi/60 \text{ rad/s}$). This is the #1 source of 2-mark deductions.
> - **Third pitfall:** Failing to verify the **magic-angle criterion** (54.74°). Examiners specifically look for this comment because it tests engineering judgement, not just computation.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Mile Revision — Memorize These Before the Exam:**

- [ ] **Pultrusion = PULL + EXTRUSION.** Pulling force, not pushing. Reinforcement is the load-bearing backbone.
- [ ] **Pultrusion stations (in order):** Creel → Guides → Resin Bath → Pre-Former → Heated Die → Puller → Saw. Know at least 5 of these by heart.
- [ ] **Pultrusion die has THREE temperature zones:** Pre-heat, Gel-Cure (peak exotherm 180–220 °C), and Cooling.
- [ ] **Typical Pultrusion V_f = 0.40 to 0.65.** Typical line speed = **0.5 to 4 m/min**.
- [ ] **Filament Winding = closed-mould process** for hollow, axisymmetric parts. Used for pressure vessels, pipes, rocket casings, CNG cylinders.
- [ ] **Winding Angle Formula (hoop convention):** $\tan \alpha = v_c / (\pi D_{avg} \omega)$. **Memorize this verbatim.**
- [ ] **Capstan (Euler) Equation:** $T_{out} = T_{in} \, e^{\mu \theta}$. Used for tension build-up at guides.
- [ ] **Hoop Stress:** $\sigma_h = p r / t$. **Axial Stress:** $\sigma_L = p r / (2t)$. **Ratio is 2:1.**
- [ ] **Magic Angle for Pressure Vessels: 54.74°.** This is the optimal helical angle for $\pm\alpha$ angle-ply laminates.
- [ ] **Geodesic Winding** = shortest path on surface; pure tension state; no slip needed; **stable.**
- [ ] **Polar Winding** = required for spherical/closed-end vessels; 2 rotations per axial pass.
- [ ] **Hoop Winding (α ≈ 90°)** gives maximum hoop reinforcement; **Helical Winding (7° < α < 90°)** gives both hoop and axial reinforcement.
- [ ] **Tex definition:** 1 tex = 1 gram per kilometre. Useful for roving mass throughput calculations.
- [ ] **Pull Force in pultrusion:** $F_{pull} = \tau_{wall} \cdot A_{contact}$. Typical $\tau_{wall}$ = 5–30 kPa.
- [ ] **Cure Kinetics:** Kamal model — $\frac{d\alpha}{dt} = (k_1 + k_2 \alpha^m)(1-\alpha)^n$.
- [ ] **Process selection shortcut:** Constant cross-section + long length → **Pultrusion.** Hollow + axisymmetric + pressure → **Filament Winding.** Open mould + complex shape → **Hand Layup / RTM.**
- [ ] **Industry context:** Type IV CNG cylinders use filament-wound carbon/epoxy at α ≈ 15–30°; rocket casings use α ≈ 54.74° for balanced performance.

> **End of Module 3 Notes — Pultrusion & Filament Winding** | PEMUT412 | KTU 2024 Scheme
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Metal matrix composites (MMC): Classification of metals
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT412 - COMPOSITE MATERIALS AND CERAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Metal matrix composites (MMC): Classification of metals

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition of Metal Matrix Composites (MMC)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Definition (Mandatory for 2-mark and 3-mark questions):**
> A **Metal Matrix Composite (MMC)** is a heterogeneous engineered material composed of a *metal or metal alloy matrix* (the continuous phase) reinforced with a *ceramic, metallic, or organic second phase* (the discontinuous/reinforcement phase), wherein the reinforcement is deliberately incorporated to enhance specific properties such as stiffness, strength, wear resistance, thermal stability, or dimensional stability beyond those achievable by the base metal alone.

The **matrix** in an MMC is the metal that binds the reinforcement together, transfers load to the reinforcement through the matrix–reinforcement interface, and protects the reinforcement from environmental degradation. The **reinforcement** carries the major portion of the applied load.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 – Module 3):**
> The classification of metals that can serve as matrices in MMCs is a foundational concept. Not every metal qualifies as a good matrix; selection is governed by density, melting point, wettability with reinforcement, interfacial bonding, thermal expansion compatibility, and intended service environment.

### 1.2 Classification of Metals Used as Matrices in MMCs

Metals used as matrices in MMCs are broadly classified into **five categories** based on their density, melting behavior, and engineering utility:

| S.No. | Class of Matrix Metal | Typical Examples | Distinguishing Trait |
|-------|----------------------|------------------|----------------------|
| 1 | **Light / Low-Density Metals** | Aluminum (Al), Magnesium (Mg), Titanium (Ti), Beryllium (Be) | Density < **5 g/cm³**; used in aerospace & automotive |
| 2 | **Refractory / High-Melting Metals** | Tungsten (W), Molybdenum (Mo), Niobium (Nb), Tantalum (Ta) | Melting point > **2000 °C**; used in high-temperature applications |
| 3 | **Precious / Noble Metals** | Platinum (Pt), Palladium (Pd), Silver (Ag), Gold (Au) | Chemical inertness; used in catalysis, biomedical implants |
| 4 | **Transition / Ferrous & Non-Ferrous Structural Metals** | Copper (Cu), Nickel (Ni), Cobalt (Co), Iron (Fe) | High strength, magnetic/electrical properties |
| 5 | **Low-Melting / Soft Metals** | Lead (Pb), Zinc (Zn), Tin (Sn) | Used in bearings, solders, and specialized functional composites |
| 6 | **Intermetallic Compounds** | NiAl, Ni₃Al, TiAl, Ti₃Al, FeAl, MoSi₂ | Ordered crystal structure; bridge metals and ceramics |

> [!NOTE]
> **Aluminum (Al)** is the **most widely used matrix** in commercial MMCs, accounting for approximately **65–70%** of MMC production globally, followed by **Magnesium (Mg)** and **Titanium (Ti)**.

### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — "The Concrete & Steel Rebar Model"

Think of a metal matrix composite exactly like **reinforced concrete**:
- The **metal matrix** (e.g., aluminum) plays the role of **concrete** — it is ductile, holds everything together, and is the bulk continuous phase.
- The **reinforcement** (e.g., SiC particles or Al₂O₃ fibers) plays the role of **steel rebar** — it is stiff, strong, and carries the structural load.
- The **matrix–reinforcement interface** is the **bond zone** — just as good adhesion between rebar and concrete is critical, the wettability and chemical bonding between the molten metal and the ceramic reinforcement decides whether the MMC will succeed or fail.

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for remembering matrix classes — "LRPTL I":**
> **L**ight metals → **R**efractory metals → **P**recious metals → **T**ransition metals → **L**ow-melting metals → **I**ntermetallics

### 1.4 Reinforcement Types Used with Metal Matrices

The classification of MMCs is incomplete without the reinforcement counterpart:

| Reinforcement Geometry | Aspect Ratio (l/d) | Typical Examples |
|------------------------|-------------------|------------------|
| **Continuous fibers** | > 1000 | Boron (B), Carbon (C), Al₂O₃, SiC fibers |
| **Short (discontinuous) fibers** | 10 – 1000 | Al₂O₃, SiC short fibers |
| **Whiskers** | > 1000 (single crystal) | SiC, Al₂O₃ whiskers |
| **Particulates** | 1 – 10 | SiC, Al₂O₃, TiC, TiB₂, B₄C, WC |
| **Woven fabrics / Laminates** | Variable | SiC/SiC, C/C preforms infiltrated with Al |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why Certain Metals Are Preferred as Matrices — The Selection Criteria

The selection of a metal as a matrix is **not arbitrary**. It must satisfy a stringent set of engineering criteria:

1. **Low Density** — Required for weight-critical applications (aerospace, automotive). Density of pure **Al is 2.70 g/cm³**, **Mg is 1.74 g/cm³**, **Ti is 4.51 g/cm³**.
2. **Adequate Ductility & Toughness** — The matrix must be able to undergo plastic deformation to transfer load to the brittle reinforcement via shear lag.
3. **Compatibility with Reinforcement** — Good **wettability** (contact angle < 90°) is essential. Wettability can be improved by surface coatings (e.g., Ni-coated carbon fibers in Al matrix).
4. **Thermal Expansion Compatibility (CTE matching)** — Minimizes residual thermal stresses at the interface. For example, **CTE of Al ≈ 23 × 10⁻⁶ /°C**; **CTE of SiC ≈ 4.5 × 10⁻⁶ /°C**.
5. **Chemical Stability** — The matrix must not degrade the reinforcement through unwanted interfacial reactions (e.g., Al reacts with SiC to form Al₄C₃, which is brittle and hygroscopic).
6. **Processability** — Capable of being processed via casting, powder metallurgy, squeeze casting, or in-situ routes.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interfacial Reaction (KTU 2024 Hot Topic):**
> The unwanted reaction **4Al + 3SiC → Al₄C₃ + 3Si** is the most cited degradation pathway in **Al–SiC MMCs**. The Al₄C₃ phase is needle-like, brittle, and hydrolyzes in humid environments, severely degrading mechanical properties. **Silicon (Si) additions (typically 5–10 wt%)** suppress this reaction.

### 2.2 Detailed Classification of Metals for MMC Matrices

#### 2.2.1 Light Metals (Density < 5 g/cm³)

**Aluminum (Al) and its alloys** (e.g., Al 6061, Al 2024, Al 7075, Al-Si casting alloys, Al-Cu)
- Most widely used matrix family
- Reinforced with SiC, Al₂O₃, B₄C, C, TiB₂
- Applications: brake rotors, pistons, cylinder liners, drive shafts, bicycle frames

**Magnesium (Mg) and its alloys** (e.g., AZ91, AM60, WE43)
- Lowest structural density (1.74 g/cm³)
- Used in automotive steering wheels, laptop housings
- Reinforcements: SiC, Al₂O₃, B₄C

**Titanium (Ti) and its alloys** (e.g., Ti-6Al-4V, Ti-6Al-2Sn-4Zr)
- Excellent high-temperature strength, corrosion resistance
- Reinforcements: SiC, TiB, TiC, Al₂O₃
- Applications: jet engine compressor blades, biomedical implants

**Beryllium (Be)**
- Low density (1.85 g/cm³) and very high stiffness (E = 287 GPa)
- Toxicity limits wide usage; used in space structures and X-ray windows

#### 2.2.2 Refractory Metals (Melting Point > 2000 °C)

- **Tungsten (W)**: Tₘ = 3422 °C; used in rocket nozzle inserts reinforced with ThO₂
- **Molybdenum (Mo)**: Tₘ = 2623 °C; reinforced with ZrO₂
- **Niobium (Nb)**: Tₘ = 2477 °C
- **Tantalum (Ta)**: Tₘ = 3017 °C
- Used in nuclear, space, and high-temperature structural applications

#### 2.2.3 Precious / Noble Metals

- **Platinum (Pt), Palladium (Pd), Silver (Ag), Gold (Au)**
- Used as matrices in functional composites for catalysis, sensors, and electrical contacts
- Reinforced with oxide dispersoids (e.g., Al₂O₃ in Ag for electrical contacts — the classic **Ag–CdO / Ag–SnO₂** contact composites)

#### 2.2.4 Transition / Structural Metals

- **Copper (Cu)**: reinforced with W, Mo, SiC, Al₂O₃, graphite — used in resistance welding electrodes, heat sinks
- **Nickel (Ni)**: reinforced with ThO₂, Al₂O₃, SiC — high-temperature turbine applications (ODS Ni-superalloys)
- **Iron (Fe) and Steels**: cermets, cutting tools
- **Cobalt (Co)**: WC-Co cemented carbides ("hard metals" / "Widia")

#### 2.2.5 Intermetallic Compounds

These are **ordered alloys** with stoichiometric compositions, e.g., NiAl, Ni₃Al, TiAl, FeAl, MoSi₂. They bridge the gap between metals and ceramics — combining metallic bonding at low temperatures with ceramic-like behavior at high temperatures.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rule of Mixtures (RoM) is the single most important formula for MMC property prediction in KTU exams.** Memorize all four forms below.

| # | Property | Rule of Mixtures Formula | Boundary Conditions |
|---|----------|--------------------------|---------------------|
| 1 | **Density** | $\rho_{c} = V_{m}\rho_{m} + V_{r}\rho_{r}$ | $V_{m} + V_{r} = 1$ |
| 2 | **Young's Modulus (Parallel — iso-strain)** | $E_{c} = V_{m}E_{m} + V_{r}E_{r}$ | Valid for **continuous unidirectional fibers** loaded along fiber axis |
| 3 | **Young's Modulus (Series — iso-stress)** | $\dfrac{1}{E_{c}} = \dfrac{V_{m}}{E_{m}} + \dfrac{V_{r}}{E_{r}}$ | Valid for **laminates loaded transverse to layers** |
| 4 | **Ultimate Tensile Strength (UTS)** | $\sigma_{c} = V_{m}\sigma_{m} + V_{r}\sigma_{r}$ | Applies to parallel loading; *in practice, modified Halpin-Tsai is used* |
| 5 | **Thermal Conductivity (Parallel)** | $k_{c} = V_{m}k_{m} + V_{r}k_{r}$ | **Wiener upper bound** |
| 6 | **Thermal Conductivity (Series)** | $\dfrac{1}{k_{c}} = \dfrac{V_{m}}{k_{m}} + \dfrac{V_{r}}{k_{r}}$ | **Wiener lower bound** |
| 7 | **Coefficient of Thermal Expansion (CTE)** | $\alpha_{c} = \dfrac{V_{m}\alpha_{m}E_{m} + V_{r}\alpha_{r}E_{r}}{V_{m}E_{m} + V_{r}E_{r}}$ | Turner model — assumes uniform strain |
| 8 | **Volume Fraction ↔ Weight Fraction** | $V_{r} = \dfrac{w_{r}/\rho_{r}}{w_{r}/\rho_{r} + w_{m}/\rho_{m}}$ | Use when problem gives weight % |
| 9 | **Specific Modulus** | $E/\rho$ | Used in aerospace selection |
| 10 | **Specific Strength** | $\sigma_{UTS}/\rho$ | Used in aerospace selection |

> [!NOTE]
> **Notation Convention Used in All Formulas:**
> Subscript *c* = composite, *m* = matrix, *r* = reinforcement
> *V* = volume fraction, *w* = weight (mass) fraction, *ρ* = density, *E* = Young's modulus, *σ* = strength, *k* = thermal conductivity, *α* = CTE

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility of MMC Classification

| Application Sector | Typical MMC System | Reason for Choice |
|--------------------|--------------------|-------------------|
| Aerospace (Boeing 787, Airbus A350) | Al–SiC, Ti–SiC, Al–Al₂O₃ | High specific stiffness |
| Automotive (Toyota piston, Ford driveshaft) | Al–SiCp, Al–Al₂O₃ | Wear resistance, low weight |
| Defense | W–Cu, W–Ni–Fe (heavy alloys) | Kinetic energy penetrators |
| Electronics | Cu–W, Cu–Mo, Al–SiC | CTE-matched heat sinks |
| Sports | Carbon–Al, SiC–Al | Stiffness and damping |
| Cutting Tools | WC–Co, TiC–Ni | Hardness and toughness |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Density of a Composite (Volume Rule of Mixtures)

**Statement:** The density of a composite material equals the volume-weighted average of the densities of its constituent phases.

**Step 1 — Start with the definition of density:**

$$\rho = \dfrac{m}{V}$$

**Step 2 — Express the total mass of the composite as the sum of masses of each phase:**

$$m_{c} = m_{m} + m_{r}$$

**Step 3 — Write the mass of each phase as density × volume:**

$$m_{c} = \rho_{m} V_{m} + \rho_{r} V_{r}$$

**Step 4 — Express the total volume of the composite:**

$$V_{c} = V_{m} + V_{r}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute into the density formula:**

$$\rho_{c} = \dfrac{\rho_{m} V_{m} + \rho_{r} V_{r}}{V_{c}}$$

**Step 6 — Apply the normalization $V_c = 1$ (or equivalently, divide by $V_c$ and use volume fractions $V_m + V_r = 1$):**

$$\boxed{\rho_{c} = V_{m}\,\rho_{m} + V_{r}\,\rho_{r}}$$

This is the **linear Rule of Mixtures (RoM)** for density.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Young's Modulus for Continuous Unidirectional Fiber MMC (Voigt / Iso-Strain Model)

**Assumption:** Both matrix and fibers experience the **same strain** (iso-strain) when load is applied parallel to the fibers.

**Step 1 — Compatibility (strain) condition:**

$$\varepsilon_{c} = \varepsilon_{m} = \varepsilon_{r} = \varepsilon$$

**Step 2 — Force equilibrium (load sharing):**

$$F_{c} = F_{m} + F_{r}$$

**Step 3 — Express force as stress × area:**

$$\sigma_{c}\,A_{c} = \sigma_{m}\,A_{m} + \sigma_{r}\,A_{r}$$

**Step 4 — Divide by total area $A_c$ and use $A_i / A_c = V_i$:**

$$\sigma_{c} = \sigma_{m}\,V_{m} + \sigma_{r}\,V_{r}$$

**Step 5 — Apply Hooke's law, $\sigma_i = E_i \varepsilon$:**

$$\sigma_{c} = E_{c}\,\varepsilon, \quad \sigma_{m} = E_{m}\,\varepsilon, \quad \sigma_{r} = E_{r}\,\varepsilon$$

**Step 6 — Substitute back:**

$$E_{c}\,\varepsilon = (E_{m}\,V_{m} + E_{r}\,V_{r})\,\varepsilon$$

**Step 7 — Cancel the common strain $\varepsilon$ (non-zero):**

$$\boxed{E_{c} = V_{m}\,E_{m} + V_{r}\,E_{r}}$$

This is the **Voigt upper bound** — a theoretical maximum for continuous aligned reinforcement.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Young's Modulus Transverse to Fibers (Reuss / Iso-Stress Model)

**Assumption:** Both phases experience the **same stress** (iso-stress) when load is applied perpendicular to the fibers.

**Step 1 — Equilibrium of stresses:**

$$\sigma_{c} = \sigma_{m} = \sigma_{r} = \sigma$$

**Step 2 — Compatibility of displacements (total deformation = sum):**

$$\delta_{c} = \delta_{m} + \delta_{r}$$

**Step 3 — Express deformation as $\delta = (\sigma/E) \cdot L$:**

$$\dfrac{\sigma \cdot L_{c}}{E_{c}} = \dfrac{\sigma \cdot L_{m}}{E_{m}} + \dfrac{\sigma \cdot L_{r}}{E_{r}}$$

**Step 4 — Divide by total length $L_c$ and use $L_i / L_c = V_i$:**

$$\dfrac{1}{E_{c}} = \dfrac{V_{m}}{E_{m}} + \dfrac{V_{r}}{E_{r}}$$

**Step 5 — Invert to obtain:**

$$\boxed{E_{c} = \left(\dfrac{V_{m}}{E_{m}} + \dfrac{V_{r}}{E_{r}}\right)^{-1}}$$

This is the **Reuss lower bound**.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Conversion from Weight Fraction to Volume Fraction

**Given:** Mass fraction of reinforcement $w_r$ and of matrix $w_m$, with $w_r + w_m = 1$.

**Step 1 — For a basis of 1 kg of composite:**

$$m_{r} = w_{r}\quad \text{and} \quad m_{m} = w_{m} = 1 - w_{r}$$

**Step 2 — Volume of each phase is mass / density:**

$$V_{r} = \dfrac{w_{r}}{\rho_{r}} \quad \text{and} \quad V_{m} = \dfrac{1 - w_{r}}{\rho_{m}}$$

**Step 3 — Total volume of composite:**

$$V_{c} = \dfrac{w_{r}}{\rho_{r}} + \dfrac{1 - w_{r}}{\rho_{m}}$$

**Step 4 — Volume fraction of reinforcement:**

$$\boxed{V_{r} = \dfrac{w_{r}/\rho_{r}}{w_{r}/\rho_{r} + (1 - w_{r})/\rho_{m}}}$$

---

### 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (Full KTU-Style Calculation)

> **Problem (KTU-pattern):**
> An aluminum matrix is reinforced with **30 vol% SiC particles**.
> Given: $\rho_{Al} = 2.70 \text{ g/cm}^3$, $\rho_{SiC} = 3.20 \text{ g/cm}^3$, $E_{Al} = 70 \text{ GPa}$, $E_{SiC} = 410 \text{ GPa}$.
> Calculate: **(a)** Density, **(b)** Longitudinal Young's modulus (Voigt), **(c)** Transverse Young's modulus (Reuss).

**Part (a) — Density:**

$$\rho_{c} = V_{m}\rho_{m} + V_{r}\rho_{r} = (0.70)(2.70) + (0.30)(3.20)$$

$$\rho_{c} = 1.890 + 0.960 = 2.850 \text{ g/cm}^3$$

**Part (b) — Longitudinal modulus (Voigt):**

$$E_{c} = (0.70)(70) + (0.30)(410) = 49.0 + 123.0 = 172.0 \text{ GPa}$$

**Part (c) — Transverse modulus (Reuss):**

$$\dfrac{1}{E_{c}} = \dfrac{0.70}{70} + \dfrac{0.30}{410} = 0.01000 + 0.000732 = 0.010732$$

$$E_{c} = \dfrac{1}{0.010732} \approx 93.18 \text{ GPa}$$

**Engineering Interpretation:** The longitudinal modulus (172 GPa) is **2.46×** that of unreinforced Al, but the transverse modulus (93.18 GPa) is only marginally higher than pure Al. This anisotropy is a defining feature of fiber-reinforced MMCs.

### 3.6 Halpin-Tsai Equation (Improved Modulus for Particulate MMCs)

For **particulate** MMCs (not continuous fibers), the Halpin-Tsai equation is preferred:

$$E_{c} = E_{m}\,\dfrac{1 + 2q\,\eta\,V_{r}}{1 - q\,\eta\,V_{r}}$$

where the constant $q$ is the **aspect ratio** of the reinforcement (for spheres, $q = 1$ for modulus) and $\eta$ is given by:

$$\eta = \dfrac{(E_{r}/E_{m}) - 1}{(E_{r}/E_{m}) + 2q}$$

For the same Al–SiC example with spherical particles ($q = 1$):

$$\eta = \dfrac{(410/70) - 1}{(410/70) + 2} = \dfrac{4.857}{7.857} = 0.6182$$

$$E_{c} = 70 \times \dfrac{1 + 2(1)(0.6182)(0.30)}{1 - (1)(0.6182)(0.30)} = 70 \times \dfrac{1.3709}{0.8145} = 70 \times 1.683 \approx 117.8 \text{ GPa}$$

This value (**117.8 GPa**) lies between the Voigt bound (172 GPa) and Reuss bound (93.18 GPa), as physically expected.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Classification of Metals Used as MMC Matrices (Mermaid Tree Diagram)

```mermaid
graph TD

    A --> C[Refractory Metals Melting point above 2000 C]

    A --> E[Transition Metals Structural]
    A --> F[Low Melting Metals Soft]
    A --> G[Intermetallic Compounds Ordered Alloys]

    B --> B1[Al and Alloys 6061, 2024, 7075, Al-Si]
    B --> B2[Mg and Alloys AZ91, AM60, WE43]
    B --> B3[Ti and Alloys Ti-6Al-4V]
    B --> B4[Be Aerospace only]

    C --> C1[Tungsten W, Tm 3422 C]

    C --> C4[Tantalum Ta, Tm 3017 C]

    D --> D1[Platinum Pt]
    D --> D2[Palladium Pd]

    E --> E1[Copper Cu, Reinforced with W or Mo]
    E --> E2[Nickel Ni, ODS Superalloys]
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — MMC Selection Logic Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    start[Start: Define Application Requirements] --> q1{Density Critical?}
    q1 -- Yes --> q2{Max Service Temperature?}
    q1 -- No --> q3{High Temperature Resistance Needed?}

    q2 -- Below 450 C --> matA[Select Al or Mg Matrix]
    q2 -- 450 to 600 C --> matB[Select Ti Matrix]
    q2 -- Above 600 C --> matC[Select Ni Matrix or Intermetallic]

    q3 -- Yes --> matC
    q3 -- No --> q4{Electrical or Thermal Conductor?}

    q4 -- Yes --> matD[Select Cu or Ag Matrix]
    q4 -- No --> q5{Wear or Cutting Application?}

    q5 -- Yes --> matE[Select Co or Fe Matrix with Carbide]
    q5 -- No --> matF[Default: Al Matrix with SiC]

    matA --> proc1[Processing: Stir Casting or Squeeze Casting]
    matB --> proc2[Processing: Powder Metallurgy or In-Situ]
    matC --> proc3[Processing: Powder Metallurgy]
    matD --> proc4[Processing: Infiltration]
    matE --> proc5[Processing: Liquid Phase Sintering]
    matF --> proc1

    proc1 --> final[Final MMC Component]
    proc2 --> final
    proc3 --> final
    proc4 --> final
    proc5 --> final
```

### 4.3 MMC Constituent Schematic Block

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[MMC Component] --> B[Matrix Phase - Continuous]
    A --> C[Reinforcement Phase - Discontinuous]
    A --> D[Matrix-Reinforcement Interface]

    B --> B1[Provides Ductility]
    B --> B2[Transfers Load]
    B --> B3[Protects Reinforcement]

    C --> C1[Carries Applied Load]
    C --> C2[Provides Stiffness]
    C --> C3[Resists Wear]

    D --> D1[Mechanical Bonding]
    D --> D2[Chemical Bonding]
    D --> D3[Residual Thermal Stress Zone]
```

### 4.4 Schematic: Comparison of Microstructure Across Matrix Classes

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Matrix Class] --> B[Light Metal Al, Mg, Ti]
    A --> C[Refractory Metal W, Mo]
    A --> D[Intermetallic NiAl, TiAl]
    A --> E[Precious Metal Ag, Pt]

    B --> B1[Reinforced with SiC, Al2O3, B4C]
    B --> B2[Microstructure: Particles or Short Fibers in Ductile Matrix]
    B --> B3[Failure: Ductile Matrix Cracking plus Particle Pull-Out]

    C --> C1[Reinforced with ThO2, ZrO2]
    C --> C2[Microstructure: ODS Alloys with Nano Dispersoids]
    C --> C3[Failure: Creep Rupture]

    D --> D1[Reinforced with TiB2, Al2O3]
    D --> D2[Microstructure: Ordered Lattice plus Ceramic Phase]
    D --> D3[Failure: Brittle Cleavage]

    E --> E1[Reinforced with Oxide or Carbide]
    E --> E2[Microstructure: Functional Composite]
    E --> E3[Failure: Contact Erosion]
```
