## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define a Metal Matrix Composite (MMC). List any three commonly used matrix metals with one application of each.*

**Model Answer:**

> A **Metal Matrix Composite (MMC)** is a composite material in which a metal or metal alloy serves as the continuous matrix phase and is reinforced with a second phase (ceramic, metallic, or intermetallic) to obtain a superior combination of properties.

| Matrix Metal | Example Application |
|--------------|---------------------|
| **Aluminum (Al)** | Brake rotors and pistons in automobiles (e.g., Toyota Al–SiC pistons) |
| **Magnesium (Mg)** | Lightweight automotive steering wheels and laptop housings |
| **Titanium (Ti)** | Jet engine compressor blades and biomedical implants |

> [!NOTE]
> **Valuation Tip:** For 3 marks, examiners expect: **1 mark** for definition, **1.5 marks** for listing metals, and **0.5 mark** for applications. Do not skip the application column.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Classify the metals used as matrices in MMCs. Give one example for each class.*

**Model Answer:**

The metals used as MMC matrices are classified into the following six classes:

1. **Light Metals** – e.g., **Aluminum** (density 2.70 g/cm³)
2. **Refractory Metals** – e.g., **Tungsten (W)** (melting point 3422 °C)
3. **Precious / Noble Metals** – e.g., **Platinum (Pt)** (chemically inert)
4. **Transition / Structural Metals** – e.g., **Copper (Cu)** (high conductivity)
5. **Low-Melting Soft Metals** – e.g., **Lead (Pb)** (used in battery plates)
6. **Intermetallic Compounds** – e.g., **NiAl** (high-temperature strength)

> [!NOTE]
> **Valuation Tip:** A 6-row classification table with examples fetches full 3 marks. Merely listing classes without examples loses 1 mark.

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

> **Module 3 Choice Pattern:** Answer **ONE** full question. Each sub-part carries **7 marks**.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss in detail the classification of metals used as matrices in Metal Matrix Composites. Compare the suitability of **Aluminum, Titanium, and Nickel** as matrix materials with suitable justifications.

**(b) [7 Marks]** An **Al-7075 matrix** is reinforced with **20 vol% SiC whiskers**. Given $\rho_{Al} = 2.81 \text{ g/cm}^3$, $\rho_{SiC} = 3.20 \text{ g/cm}^3$, $E_{Al} = 72 \text{ GPa}$, $E_{SiC} = 480 \text{ GPa}$. Calculate the **(i)** density, **(ii)** Young's modulus along the whisker direction (Voigt model), and **(iii)** specific modulus of the composite.

---

**Model Solution to Part (a) — Classification of Matrix Metals:**

The metals used as matrices in MMCs are classified as follows:

**(1) Light Metals** — Al, Mg, Ti, Be (density < 5 g/cm³). Used in aerospace, automotive, and transport sectors where weight reduction is critical. Aluminum is the most common due to its low density, good ductility, and excellent processability.

**(2) Refractory Metals** — W, Mo, Nb, Ta (melting point > 2000 °C). Used in rocket nozzles, nuclear reactors, and high-temperature structural components. Example: **W–ThO₂** for dispenser cathodes.

**(3) Precious Metals** — Pt, Pd, Ag, Au. Used where chemical inertness and catalytic activity are needed, such as in **Ag–CdO** electrical contact composites and Pt-based catalyst supports.

**(4) Transition / Structural Metals** — Cu, Ni, Fe, Co. Provide unique functional properties such as high conductivity (Cu), magnetic behavior (Fe, Ni), and toughness. **Cu–W** is used in resistance welding electrodes.

**(5) Low-Melting / Soft Metals** — Pb, Zn, Sn. Used in bearings, solders, and battery plates.

**(6) Intermetallic Compounds** — NiAl, TiAl, MoSi₂. Provide a balance between metallic ductility (at low T) and ceramic-like strength (at high T). Used in advanced jet engines.

**Comparison Table — Al vs Ti vs Ni:**

| Property | Aluminum (Al) | Titanium (Ti) | Nickel (Ni) |
|----------|---------------|---------------|-------------|
| Density (g/cm³) | 2.70 | 4.51 | 8.90 |
| Melting Point (°C) | 660 | 1668 | 1455 |
| Young's Modulus (GPa) | 70 | 116 | 200 |
| Max Service T (°C) | ~350 | ~600 | ~1000 |
| Cost | Low | High | High |
| Processability | Excellent | Difficult | Difficult |
| Typical MMC Reinforcement | SiC, Al₂O₃ | SiC, TiB | ThO₂, Al₂O₃ |

**Conclusion:** **Al** is preferred for low-cost, high-volume automotive applications; **Ti** is chosen for aerospace structural components requiring elevated-temperature strength; **Ni** is selected for ultra-high-temperature turbine and rocket applications.

---

**Model Solution to Part (b) — Numerical Calculation:**

Given: $V_r = 0.20$, $V_m = 0.80$, $\rho_m = 2.81 \text{ g/cm}^3$, $\rho_r = 3.20 \text{ g/cm}^3$, $E_m = 72 \text{ GPa}$, $E_r = 480 \text{ GPa}$.

**(i) Density:**

$$\rho_{c} = (0.80)(2.81) + (0.20)(3.20) = 2.248 + 0.640 = 2.888 \text{ g/cm}^3$$

**(ii) Young's Modulus (Voigt — parallel to whiskers):**

$$E_{c} = (0.80)(72) + (0.20)(480) = 57.6 + 96.0 = 153.6 \text{ GPa}$$

**(iii) Specific Modulus:**

$$\dfrac{E_{c}}{\rho_{c}} = \dfrac{153.6}{2.888} = 53.19 \text{ GPa·cm}^3/\text{g}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Mark-Loss Zones:**
> 1. **Unit mixing:** Specific modulus must be expressed in **GPa·cm³/g** or **10⁶ m²/s²** — not as a dimensionless number.
> 2. **Volume fraction arithmetic:** Many students mistakenly use $V_r = 0.2$ and forget $V_m = 1 - V_r = 0.8$. This single error propagates to all three sub-parts and costs **5–7 marks**.
> 3. **Model choice:** Use **Voigt (iso-strain)** for continuous aligned whiskers, **Reuss (iso-stress)** for transverse loading. Examiners mark **zero** if the wrong model is used without justification.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** — [KTU University Exam – May 2023]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **selection criteria** for choosing a metal as the matrix in an MMC. Discuss the role of **wettability** and **interfacial reactions** with reference to the **Al–SiC system**.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A **magnesium alloy (AZ91)** matrix is reinforced with **25 vol% Al₂O₃ short fibers**. Given: $\rho_{Mg} = 1.74 \text{ g/cm}^3$, $\rho_{Al_2O_3} = 3.95 \text{ g/cm}^3$, $E_{Mg} = 45 \text{ GPa}$, $E_{Al_2O_3} = 380 \text{ GPa}$. Calculate the **(i)** weight fraction of Al₂O₃, **(ii)** density of the composite, and **(iii)** Reuss (iso-stress) modulus.

---

**Model Solution to Part (a) — Selection Criteria & Al–SiC Interface:**

The selection of a matrix metal is governed by:

1. **Density** — Should be low for aerospace/automotive. Example: Mg (1.74 g/cm³).
2. **Melting Point & Processability** — Lower melting point simplifies liquid-state processing.
3. **Ductility & Toughness** — Matrix must be ductile enough to redistribute stress around reinforcement cracks.
4. **Wettability** — Defined by the **contact angle** $\theta$ between molten metal and reinforcement surface. Good wettability requires $\theta < 90°$. Wettability is enhanced by:
   - Pre-coating fibers (e.g., **Ni coating on carbon fibers**)
   - **Alloy additions** to the matrix (e.g., Mg, Si, or Ti additions to Al)
   - Higher processing temperatures (within reason)
5. **Thermal Expansion Compatibility (CTE matching)** — Minimizes residual thermal stress $\sigma = E \cdot \Delta\alpha \cdot \Delta T$ on cooling.
6. **Chemical Compatibility** — The matrix must not react excessively with the reinforcement.

**The Al–SiC System — Wettability & Interfacial Reactions:**

- Molten Al wets SiC reasonably well above **900 °C** in vacuum, but the contact angle is still > 90° below this temperature.
- Wettability can be improved by adding **Si (5–10 wt%)** or **Mg (1–4 wt%)** to the Al melt.
- The unwanted interfacial reaction is:
$$4Al + 3SiC \longrightarrow Al_4C_3 + 3Si$$
- **Al₄C₃** is a brittle, needle-shaped, **hygroscopic** phase that degrades mechanical properties and corrosion resistance.
- Suppression strategies: **Si additions** shift the reaction equilibrium to the left, and **T6 heat treatment** must be carefully controlled.

---

**Model Solution to Part (b) — Numerical Calculation:**

Given: $V_r = 0.25$, $V_m = 0.75$, $\rho_m = 1.74 \text{ g/cm}^3$, $\rho_r = 3.95 \text{ g/cm}^3$.

**(i) Weight fraction of Al₂O₃:**

$$V_{r} = \dfrac{w_{r}/\rho_{r}}{w_{r}/\rho_{r} + (1 - w_{r})/\rho_{m}}$$

Solve for $w_r$:

$$0.25 = \dfrac{w_{r}/3.95}{w_{r}/3.95 + (1 - w_{r})/1.74}$$

Let $x = w_r$. Then:

$$0.25 \left[\dfrac{x}{3.95} + \dfrac{1 - x}{1.74}\right] = \dfrac{x}{3.95}$$

$$0.25 \times \dfrac{x}{3.95} + 0.25 \times \dfrac{1 - x}{1.74} = \dfrac{x}{3.95}$$

$$0.06329\,x + 0.14368\,(1 - x) = 0.25316\,x$$

$$0.06329\,x + 0.14368 - 0.14368\,x = 0.25316\,x$$

$$0.14368 = 0.25316\,x + 0.14368\,x - 0.06329\,x = 0.33355\,x$$

$$x = w_{r} = \dfrac{0.14368}{0.33355} \approx 0.4308 \;\; \text{or} \;\; 43.08 \text{ wt\%}$$

**(ii) Density of the composite:**

Using volume fractions directly:

$$\rho_{c} = (0.75)(1.74) + (0.25)(3.95) = 1.305 + 0.9875 = 2.2925 \text{ g/cm}^3$$

**(iii) Reuss (iso-stress) modulus:**

$$\dfrac{1}{E_{c}} = \dfrac{0.75}{45} + \dfrac{0.25}{380} = 0.01667 + 0.000658 = 0.01733 \text{ GPa}^{-1}$$

$$E_{c} = \dfrac{1}{0.01733} \approx 57.71 \text{ GPa}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Mark-Loss Zones for Question B:**
> 1. **Mass-volume fraction conversion:** Solving the quadratic/transcendental equation for $w_r$ is the most-skipped step. Many students guess $w_r = V_r = 0.25$ which is **wrong** and costs **2–3 marks**.
> 2. **Reuss vs Voigt confusion:** The Reuss model is a *series* model (sum of compliances). Forgetting to invert the final answer is a frequent error.
> 3. **Units:** Density in **g/cm³**, modulus in **GPa** must be strictly observed.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — MMC: Classification of Metals**

- **Definition of MMC** — Metal matrix + reinforcement + engineered interface.
- **Six classes of matrix metals** — Light, Refractory, Precious, Transition, Low-melting, Intermetallic.
- **Aluminum is the dominant matrix** (≈ 65–70% of commercial MMCs).
- **Selection criteria** — Density, melting point, ductility, wettability, CTE, chemical compatibility, cost.
- **Wettability** is governed by the **contact angle** $\theta < 90°$ for good bonding.
- **Al–SiC interfacial reaction** — $4Al + 3SiC \rightarrow Al_4C_3 + 3Si$; suppress with **Si additions**.
- **Rule of Mixtures (RoM)** is the **central predictive tool** — must memorize both Voigt (parallel) and Reuss (perpendicular) forms.
- **Voigt** — $E_c = V_m E_m + V_r E_r$ → upper bound, iso-strain, longitudinal loading.
- **Reuss** — $1/E_c = V_m/E_m + V_r/E_r$ → lower bound, iso-stress, transverse loading.
- **Halpin-Tsai** is the preferred model for **particulate** MMCs.
- **CTE of composite** — Uses **Turner model** with $E$-weighted phases.
- **Volume-to-weight fraction conversion** is a guaranteed numerical sub-part — practice the algebra.
- **Specific modulus** ($E/\rho$) and **specific strength** ($\sigma/\rho$) are the **key aerospace selection metrics**.
- **Mnemonic "LRPTL I"** — Light, Refractory, Precious, Transition, Low-melting, Intermetallic.
- **Mnemonic "Concrete & Rebar"** — Metal = concrete, Reinforcement = rebar, Interface = bond zone.

> [!TIP]
> **Final KTU 2024 Exam Strategy:**
> 1. Always draw the **classification table** with examples in 3-mark questions.
> 2. In 14-mark questions, **split the page vertically** — theory on the left, calculation on the right.
> 3. **Box every final numerical answer** and quote its units explicitly.
> 4. State the **model name** (Voigt / Reuss / Halpin-Tsai / Turner) before applying the formula.
> 5. For interfacial chemistry questions, **always write the balanced chemical equation** for Al–SiC.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Intermetallics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT412 - COMPOSITE MATERIALS AND CERAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Intermetallics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Intermetallics** (also called **intermetallic compounds** or **ordered intermetallic alloys**) are solid-state compounds composed of two or more metallic elements that exhibit a **definite stoichiometric ratio** and possess a **crystalline structure distinct from that of their constituent parent metals**. They exist in a narrow compositional range around the stoichiometric ratio and are held together by a mixture of metallic, covalent, and ionic bonding.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition**: An intermetallic compound is a homogeneous compound formed by the combination of two or more metals in a specific atomic ratio, characterized by an ordered crystal structure that differs from the constituent metals, and exhibiting a sharp melting point rather than a melting range.

The bonding in intermetallics is typically a hybrid of:
- **Metallic bonding** (delocalized electrons)
- **Covalent bonding** (directional electron sharing)
- **Ionic bonding** (charge transfer between elements with electronegativity differences)

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU PEMUT412 – Module 3)**: Intermetallics are positioned as a unique class of materials bridging traditional metals and ceramics. They are studied for their high-temperature structural applications, oxidation resistance, and role as matrix/reinforcement phases in metal-matrix and intermetallic-matrix composites (IMCs).

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of intermetallics as a **strictly organized military regiment**:
- In a **pure metal** (e.g., pure aluminum), all soldiers (atoms) are identical — they can swap positions freely without disrupting the formation. This represents the disordered solid solution.
- In an **alloy** like brass (Cu-Zn solid solution), the soldiers are mixed but not assigned fixed positions — they substitute for one another randomly.
- In an **intermetallic compound** like **Ni₃Al**, every soldier has a designated post. Aluminum must occupy corner positions, and nickel occupies face-center positions, **every single time**, in a perfectly ordered fashion. If a soldier leaves his post, the formation collapses.

This **strict positional order** (called *long-range order*) is the defining feature — and the source of both their **superior high-temperature strength** and their **poor room-temperature ductility** (brittleness).

---

## 1.3 Distinguishing Intermetallics from Other Material Classes

| Property | Pure Metals | Solid Solutions | Intermetallic Compounds | Ceramics |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Stoichiometry** | Single element | Variable composition | **Fixed / narrow range** | Fixed |
| **Crystal Structure** | FCC, BCC, HCP | Same as solvent | **Distinct ordered structure** | Ionic/covalent |
| **Melting Behavior** | Sharp point | Range | **Sharp point** (often) | Sharp point |
| **Bonding** | Metallic | Metallic | **Metallic + Covalent + Ionic** | Ionic + Covalent |
| **Ductility at RT** | High | Moderate-High | **Often low (brittle)** | Very low |
| **Electrical Conductivity** | High | High | **Lower** | Insulating |

---

## 1.4 General Properties of Intermetallics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Engineering Properties (High-Yield for KTU):**
> - **High melting point** (often > **1500 °C** for aluminides and silicides)
> - **Excellent high-temperature strength retention** (yield stress *increases* with temperature up to ~600–800 °C — anomalous behavior)
> - **Low density** (typically **4–8 g/cm³** for aluminides vs. ~8.9 for Ni, ~19.3 for W)
> - **Superior oxidation and corrosion resistance** due to formation of stable, adherent oxide layers (e.g., Al₂O₃ on Ni-Al)
> - **High specific modulus** (stiffness-to-weight ratio)
> - **Low room-temperature ductility** — main limitation
> - **Thermal conductivity** intermediate between metals and ceramics
> - **Creep resistance** superior to conventional superalloys above 1000 °C

---

## 1.5 Classification of Intermetallics

### A. By Chemical Composition
- **Aluminides**: Ni-Al, Ti-Al, Fe-Al (most commercially important)
- **Silicides**: Mo-Si, Nb-Si, Ti-Si
- **Beryllides**: Nb-Be, Zr-Be
- **Other**: Ni-Ti (shape memory), Cu-Zn (brass-type), Mg-Cu, rare-earth intermetallics (e.g., SmCo₅ for permanent magnets)

### B. By Crystal Structure
- **L1₂ structure** (e.g., Ni₃Al, Cu₃Au) — ordered FCC
- **B2 structure** (e.g., NiAl, FeAl, CuZn) — ordered BCC (CsCl-type)
- **D0₁₉ structure** (e.g., Ti₃Al) — ordered HCP
- **L1₀ structure** (e.g., TiAl, CuAu) — ordered FCC-tetragonal
- **D0₃ structure** (e.g., Fe₃Al) — ordered BCC (BiF₃-type)
- **C14, C15, C36 Laves Phases** (e.g., MgCu₂, MgZn₂)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Focus Areas**: The syllabus emphasizes **Nickel aluminides (Ni₃Al, NiAl)** and **Titanium aluminides (Ti₃Al, TiAl)** because they form the backbone of modern aero-engine and automotive turbocharger materials.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

---

## 2.1 Atomic Ordering and Long-Range Order Parameter

The defining feature of an intermetallic is **long-range atomic order**. A quantitative descriptor is the **Bragg–Williams long-range order parameter (S)**:

$$S = \frac{r_{\alpha} - X_{\alpha}}{1 - X_{\alpha}} = \frac{r_{\beta} - X_{\beta}}{1 - X_{\beta}}$$

Where:
- $r_{\alpha}$ = fraction of α-sublattice sites occupied by the **correct** α-type atom
- $X_{\alpha}$ = atomic fraction of α atoms in the alloy
- Similarly for β

| Condition | Value of S | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Perfect order | **S = 1** | Every atom on its correct sublattice site |
| Random (disordered) | **S = 0** | No preferential occupation |
| Partial order | **0 < S < 1** | Intermediate state |

**Why does ordering happen?** Ordering lowers the **free energy** $G = H - TS$. Although the enthalpy H may increase due to lattice distortion, the configurational entropy loss is more than offset by the **strong A–B bond energy**, making the ordered state thermodynamically favored below a critical temperature ($T_c$).

---

## 2.2 Crystal Structure Families — The Big Four for KTU

### A. L1₂ Structure — Ni₃Al (γ′ phase)
- **Pearson symbol**: cP4
- **Space group**: Pm$\bar{3}$m
- **Prototype**: Cu₃Au
- **Sublattices**:
  - Al occupies cube **corners** (0,0,0)
  - Ni occupies **face-centers** (½,½,0), (½,0,½), (0,½,½)
- **Atoms per unit cell**: 4 (1 Al + 3 Ni)
- **Coordination**: Each Al is surrounded by 12 Ni (CN = 12)

### B. B2 Structure — NiAl (β phase)
- **Pearson symbol**: cP2
- **Space group**: Pm$\bar{3}$m
- **Prototype**: CsCl
- **Sublattices**:
  - Ni at (0,0,0)
  - Al at (½,½,½)
- **Atoms per unit cell**: 2 (1 Ni + 1 Al)
- **Coordination**: CN = 8 (cubic)

### C. D0₁₉ Structure — Ti₃Al (α₂ phase)
- **Pearson symbol**: hP8
- **Space group**: P6₃/mmc
- **Prototype**: Ni₃Sn
- **Ordered HCP-based structure**: Ti at 6h sites, Al at 2c sites

### D. L1₀ Structure — TiAl (γ phase)
- **Pearson symbol**: tP4
- **Space group**: P4/mmm
- **Prototype**: CuAu
- **Face-centered tetragonal**: alternating Ti and Al layers along c-axis
- **c/a ratio > 1** (tetragonal distortion)

---

## 2.3 The Anomalous Yield Strength Phenomenon

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Concept**: Ni₃Al and several other L1₂ intermetallics exhibit the **yield strength anomaly (YSA)** — yield stress **increases with temperature** up to a peak (~600–800 °C for Ni₃Al), unlike conventional metals where strength always decreases with T.

**Mechanism (Kear–Wilsdorf lock mechanism)**:
1. At low T, dislocations glide on {111} octahedral planes as **superdislocations** (paired partials with APB between them).
2. At elevated T, the leading partial cross-slips from {111} to {010} cube plane (which has higher APB energy).
3. This **locks** the dislocation because the {010} plane is not a glide plane.
4. Further motion requires **climb** (diffusion-controlled) — extremely slow.

$$\tau_{\text{flow}} \propto \exp\left(\frac{-Q_{\text{climb}}}{RT}\right)$$

This results in:
- $\tau$ **increasing** with T (thermally activated locking dominates)
- Eventually **decreasing** at very high T when climb becomes easy

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> **Constants to Memorize:**
> - Avogadro's number: $N_A = 6.022 \times 10^{23}$ atoms/mol
> - Boltzmann constant: $k_B = 1.381 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K
> - Gas constant: $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K)

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $S = \dfrac{r_{\alpha} - X_{\alpha}}{1 - X_{\alpha}}$ | Bragg-Williams long-range order parameter | dimensionless |
| 2 | $\rho = \dfrac{n \cdot M}{N_A \cdot V_{cell}}$ | Theoretical density of unit cell | g/cm³ |
| 3 | $V_{cell} = a^3$ | Cubic cell volume | cm³, m³ |
| 4 | $V_{cell} = \dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\,a^2 \cdot c$ | Hexagonal cell volume | cm³, m³ |
| 5 | $\text{APF} = \dfrac{n \cdot \dfrac{4}{3}\pi r^3}{V_{cell}}$ | Atomic packing factor | dimensionless |
| 6 | $a = 2R\sqrt{2}$ | FCC lattice parameter (in terms of atomic radius R) | nm, Å |
| 7 | $a = \dfrac{4R}{\sqrt{3}}$ | BCC lattice parameter (in terms of atomic radius R) | nm, Å |
| 8 | $\text{CN}_{\text{L1}_2} = 12$ | Coordination number (L1₂, Ni₃Al) | dimensionless |
| 9 | $\text{CN}_{\text{B2}} = 8$ | Coordination number (B2, NiAl) | dimensionless |
| 10 | $\Delta G_{\text{order}} = \Delta H - T\Delta S$ | Free energy driving force for ordering | J/mol, kJ/mol |
| 11 | $E_{\text{specific}} = \dfrac{E}{\rho}$ | Specific modulus (specific stiffness) | Pa·m³/kg |
| 12 | $\sigma_{\text{UTS}} = \dfrac{F_{\max}}{A_0}$ | Ultimate tensile strength | Pa, MPa |
| 13 | $\dot{\varepsilon}_{\text{creep}} = A\,\sigma^n \exp\!\left(\dfrac{-Q}{RT}\right)$ | Norton-Bailey creep law | s⁻¹ |
| 14 | $r_{\text{crit}} = \dfrac{2\gamma_{SL}}{\Delta G_v}$ | Critical nucleus radius (homogeneous nucleation) | nm, m |

| **Hydrogen Storage** | Solid-state hydrogen reservoirs | LaNi₅, Mg₂Ni |
| **Catalysis** | Hydrogenation catalysts | NiAl, Ni₃Al |

## 2.6 Processing Routes for Intermetallics

1. **Conventional Casting & Wrought Processing**
   - Vacuum induction melting (VIM) + vacuum arc remelting (VAR)
   - Hot isostatic pressing (HIP)
   - Hot extrusion, forging
2. **Powder Metallurgy (P/M)**
   - Mechanical alloying (MA)
   - Reaction synthesis (SHS — self-propagating high-temperature synthesis)
3. **Rapid Solidification**
   - Melt spinning → amorphous/crystalline ribbons
   - Gas atomization → pre-alloyed powders
4. **Intermetallic Matrix Composites (IMCs)**
   - Reinforcement with SiC, Al₂O₃, TiB₂ whiskers/particles
   - In-situ formation: XD™ process (Martin Marietta), reactive sintering

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

---

## 3.1 Worked Example 1: Theoretical Density of Ni₃Al (L1₂ Structure)

**Given**:
- Atomic mass of Ni: $M_{Ni} = 58.69$ g/mol
- Atomic mass of Al: $M_{Al} = 26.98$ g/mol
- Lattice parameter: $a = 3.567$ Å $= 3.567 \times 10^{-8}$ cm

**Find**: Theoretical density $\rho$ of Ni₃Al.

---

### Step 1 — Identify atoms per unit cell (n)

In the L1₂ structure:
- 8 corner Al atoms × (1/8 contribution each) = **1 Al atom**
- 6 face-center Ni atoms × (1/2 contribution each) = **3 Ni atoms**
- **Total: 4 atoms per unit cell** (1 Al + 3 Ni)

This matches the chemical formula **Ni₃Al** stoichiometrically.

$$n_{\text{total}} = 4$$

### Step 2 — Calculate effective molar mass per formula unit

The formula unit Ni₃Al has 3 Ni atoms and 1 Al atom:

$$M_{\text{Ni}_3\text{Al}} = 3 \times M_{Ni} + 1 \times M_{Al}$$

Substituting values:

$$M_{\text{Ni}_3\text{Al}} = 3 \times 58.69 + 1 \times 26.98$$

$$M_{\text{Ni}_3\text{Al}} = 176.07 + 26.98 = 203.05 \text{ g/mol}$$

### Step 3 — Calculate unit cell volume

For a cubic cell:

$$V_{\text{cell}} = a^3 = (3.567 \times 10^{-8}\text{ cm})^3$$

$$V_{\text{cell}} = 45.36 \times 10^{-24}\text{ cm}^3$$

$$V_{\text{cell}} = 4.536 \times 10^{-23}\text{ cm}^3$$

### Step 4 — Apply the density formula

$$\rho = \dfrac{n \cdot M}{N_A \cdot V_{\text{cell}}}$$

Where $n$ = number of formula units per unit cell = **1** (since one unit cell contains exactly one Ni₃Al formula unit).

$$\rho = \dfrac{1 \times 203.05 \text{ g/mol}}{6.022 \times 10^{23} \text{ /mol} \times 4.536 \times 10^{-23}\text{ cm}^3}$$

$$\rho = \dfrac{203.05}{27.314} \text{ g/cm}^3$$

$$\boxed{\rho_{\text{Ni}_3\text{Al}} \approx 7.43 \text{ g/cm}^3}$$

**Verification**: Experimental value ≈ **7.40–7.50 g/cm³** ✓

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2: APF of B2 Structure (NiAl)

**Given**:
- Structure: B2 (ordered BCC, CsCl-type)
- Atoms touch along **cube diagonal** (body diagonal)

### Step 1 — Geometry of the B2 structure

In B2:
- Ni at (0,0,0) and Al at (½,½,½)
- Each Ni touches its nearest Al neighbors along the cube diagonal
- **Body diagonal length** = $a\sqrt{3}$
- **Two atomic radii** along the diagonal: $R_{Ni} + R_{Al}$

$$a\sqrt{3} = 2(R_{Ni} + R_{Al})$$

$$a = \dfrac{2(R_{Ni} + R_{Al})}{\sqrt{3}}$$

### Step 2 — Atoms per unit cell

- 8 corners × (1/8) = **1 Ni**
- 1 body center × (1) = **1 Al**
- **Total: 2 atoms per unit cell**

### Step 3 — Volume of atoms

Assuming equal atomic radii for simplicity (R = mean radius):

$$V_{\text{atoms}} = 2 \times \dfrac{4}{3}\pi R^3 = \dfrac{8}{3}\pi R^3$$

### Step 4 — Express R in terms of a

From Step 1 with $R_{Ni} \approx R_{Al} \approx R$:

$$R = \dfrac{a\sqrt{3}}{4}$$

### Step 5 — Substitute

$$\text{APF} = \dfrac{V_{\text{atoms}}}{V_{\text{cell}}} = \dfrac{\dfrac{8}{3}\pi R^3}{a^3}$$

$$\text{APF} = \dfrac{\dfrac{8}{3}\pi \left(\dfrac{a\sqrt{3}}{4}\right)^3}{a^3}$$

$$\text{APF} = \dfrac{\dfrac{8}{3}\pi \cdot \dfrac{3\sqrt{3}\,a^3}{64}}{a^3}$$

$$\text{APF} = \dfrac{8 \cdot 3\sqrt{3}\pi}{3 \cdot 64} = \dfrac{24\sqrt{3}\pi}{192} = \dfrac{\sqrt{3}\,\pi}{8}$$

$$\boxed{\text{APF}_{\text{B2}} = \dfrac{\sqrt{3}\,\pi}{8} \approx 0.6802}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Comparison**: APF of disordered BCC = 0.68 (same), but B2 has **ordered** placement making it a true intermetallic compound.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3: Long-Range Order Parameter Calculation

**Problem**: In an equiatomic AB alloy with composition 50% A and 50% B, suppose that out of 200 A-sublattice sites, 180 are correctly occupied by A atoms, and the remaining 20 are wrongly occupied by B atoms. Calculate the long-range order parameter S.

**Given**:
- $X_A = 0.50$ (atomic fraction of A)
- $r_A$ = fraction of α-sublattice sites with A atoms = 180/200 = 0.90

**Formula**:

$$S = \dfrac{r_A - X_A}{1 - X_A}$$

**Substituting**:

$$S = \dfrac{0.90 - 0.50}{1 - 0.50} = \dfrac{0.40}{0.50}$$

$$\boxed{S = 0.80}$$

**Interpretation**: The alloy is **80% ordered**. Perfect order would give S = 1.00.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example 4: Specific Modulus Comparison

**Given**:
- Young's modulus of Ni₃Al: $E_{Ni_3Al} = 200$ GPa
- Density of Ni₃Al: $\rho_{Ni_3Al} = 7.43$ g/cm³
- Young's modulus of TiAl: $E_{TiAl} = 175$ GPa
- Density of TiAl: $\rho_{TiAl} = 3.90$ g/cm³
- Young's modulus of Ni-based superalloy (Inconel 718): $E_{718} = 200$ GPa
- Density of Inconel 718: $\rho_{718} = 8.19$ g/cm³

**Calculate specific modulus** $E/\rho$ for each:

**Ni₃Al**:

$$E/\rho = \dfrac{200 \text{ GPa}}{7.43 \text{ g/cm}^3} = 26.92 \text{ GPa·cm}^3/\text{g}$$

**TiAl**:

$$E/\rho = \dfrac{175}{3.90} = 44.87 \text{ GPa·cm}^3/\text{g}$$

**Inconel 718**:

$$E/\rho = \dfrac{200}{8.19} = 24.42 \text{ GPa·cm}^3/\text{g}$$

**Ranking (best to worst)**: **TiAl > Ni₃Al > Inconel 718**

**Conclusion**: TiAl offers the **highest specific stiffness** — critical for rotating components like turbine blades and turbocharger wheels where inertial loads dominate.

---

## 3.5 Worked Example 5: Creep Rate Estimation (Norton-Bailey)

**Given**:
- $\sigma$ = applied stress = 100 MPa = $100 \times 10^6$ Pa
- $T$ = 1200 K
- $A = 1.0 \times 10^{-6}$ s⁻¹·(MPa)⁻ⁿ
- $n = 4$ (stress exponent)
- $Q$ = 300 kJ/mol = $300,000$ J/mol
- $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K)

**Formula**:

$$\dot{\varepsilon}_{\text{creep}} = A\,\sigma^n \exp\!\left(\dfrac{-Q}{RT}\right)$$

**Step 1**: Calculate exponential term

$$\dfrac{-Q}{RT} = \dfrac{-300{,}000}{8.314 \times 1200} = \dfrac{-300{,}000}{9976.8} = -30.07$$

$$\exp(-30.07) = 8.96 \times 10^{-14}$$

**Step 2**: Calculate $\sigma^n$

$$\sigma^n = (100)^4 = 10^8 \text{ MPa}^4$$

**Step 3**: Compute creep rate

$$\dot{\varepsilon} = (1.0 \times 10^{-6}) \times (10^8) \times (8.96 \times 10^{-14})$$

$$\dot{\varepsilon} = 10^2 \times 8.96 \times 10^{-14}$$

$$\boxed{\dot{\varepsilon} = 8.96 \times 10^{-12} \text{ s}^{-1}}$$

**Engineering interpretation**: Such low creep rates justify the use of intermetallics (especially NiAl and Ni₃Al) for long-life turbine components operating above 1000 °C.

---

## 3.7 Ductilization Strategies (Brief Analytical Note)

The primary limitation — **room-temperature brittleness** — is tackled via:

1. **Macroalloying**: Add B (~0.1 wt%) to Ni₃Al → grain-boundary cohesion ↑
2. **Microalloying**: Cr, Mn, Fe to NiAl → increase <111> slip
3. **Grain refinement**: Nano-grained or single-crystal forms
4. **Composite approach**: Reinforce with ductile phase (e.g., Mo, Nb, Cr particles)
5. **Thermo-mechanical processing**: Controlled rolling + annealing
6. **Crystal structure engineering**: Introduce metastable B2 → L1₀ transition path in TiAl

$$\sigma_y^{\text{composite}} = \sigma_y^m V^m + \sigma_y^r V^r \quad \text{(Rule of Mixtures — upper bound)}$$

where $m$ = matrix, $r$ = reinforcement, $V$ = volume fraction, $V^m + V^r = 1$.

```mermaid

    nucleation --> growth[Domain Growth by Diffusion]
    growth --> coarsening[Coarsening Antiphase Domains Coalesce]

    coarsening --> final[Ordered Intermetallic State S approaches 1]

    final --> property1[High Temperature Strength Rises]
    final --> property2[Room Temperature Ductility Drops]
    final --> property3[Electrical Resistivity Drops]
```

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Define the term "intermetallic compound". How does it differ from a solid solution?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

An intermetallic compound is a homogeneous compound formed by the combination of two or more metals in a fixed stoichiometric ratio, possessing a **distinct, ordered crystal structure** that differs from those of its constituent metals.

**Key differences from solid solution:**

| Feature | Solid Solution | Intermetallic |
|---|---|---|
| Composition | Variable over a range | **Fixed / narrow stoichiometric range** |
| Crystal structure | Same as parent metal | **Distinct ordered structure** |
| Atomic site occupancy | Random substitution | **Ordered sublattice occupation** |
| Melting behavior | Melting range | **Sharp melting point** |
| Bonding | Purely metallic | **Metallic + covalent + ionic** |

(1 mark for definition + 2 marks for comparative points)

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**What is the "yield strength anomaly" exhibited by Ni₃Al? Explain in 3 lines.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The yield strength anomaly (YSA) refers to the **unusual increase in yield stress with rising temperature** (up to ~600–800 °C) observed in Ni₃Al and other L1₂ intermetallics, in contrast to the normal decrease seen in conventional metals.

**Mechanism**: At elevated temperatures, the leading partial of the superdislocation cross-slips from the {111} octahedral glide plane to the {010} cube plane, forming a **Kear–Wilsdorf lock**. This immobilizes the dislocation, increasing the stress required for further plastic deformation.

(1 mark phenomenon + 2 marks mechanism)

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**(a)** With neat sketches, describe the **L1₂ crystal structure of Ni₃Al**. Mention the lattice parameter, coordination numbers, and atoms per unit cell. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **Kear–Wilsdorf mechanism** explaining the anomalous yield strength behavior of Ni₃Al. State its engineering significance. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Q.A(a) (7 marks)

**1. Structure description (3 marks):**
The L1₂ structure of Ni₃Al is an **ordered face-centered cubic (FCC)** structure with prototype Cu₃Au. It has two interpenetrating sublattices:
- Al atoms occupy the **8 corner positions** of the cube: (0,0,0)
- Ni atoms occupy the **6 face-center positions**: (½,½,0), (½,0,½), (0,½,½)

The space group is **Pm$\bar{3}$m** (No. 221) and Pearson symbol **cP4**.

**2. Key numerical values (2 marks):**
- Lattice parameter: $a \approx 3.567$ Å
- Atoms per unit cell: $n = 8 \times \frac{1}{8} + 6 \times \frac{1}{2} = 1 + 3 = \mathbf{4}$ atoms
- Composition per cell: 1 Al + 3 Ni → matches formula Ni₃Al

**3. Coordination numbers (2 marks):**
- Each Al (corner) is surrounded by **12 Ni atoms** (CN = 12) — same as FCC coordination
- Each Ni (face-center) has CN = 12 with mixed neighbors (8 Ni + 4 Al)

**Density (supplementary check):**
$$\rho = \frac{n \cdot M}{N_A \cdot a^3} = \frac{4 \times 51.75}{6.022 \times 10^{23} \times (3.567 \times 10^{-8})^3} = 7.43 \text{ g/cm}^3$$

(51.75 g/mol = mean atomic mass of Ni₃Al formula unit)

**ASCII sketch:**

```
        Ni
   Al - - - - - Al
  /|             /|
 / |            / |
Ni - - - - - Ni  Al
|  |     Al   |  |
|  Al - - - - | - Ni
 Ni /          | /
 | /           |/
 Ni - - - - - Ni
   Al
```

---

#### Model Solution — Q.A(b) (7 marks)

**1. Description of the anomaly (2 marks):**
Most metals show a **monotonic decrease in yield strength** as temperature rises. Ni₃Al, however, shows a **peak in yield strength** between 600–800 °C — termed the **yield strength anomaly (YSA)** or **positive temperature dependence of strength**.

**2. Kear–Wilsdorf mechanism (4 marks):**

In L1₂ ordered structures, plastic deformation occurs by motion of **<110> superdislocations** — pairs of partials separated by an **antiphase boundary (APB)**.

- **Below peak temperature**: The superdislocation glides on the {111} octahedral plane. Both leading and trailing partials move on {111}.

- **Near peak temperature**: The **leading partial** cross-slips onto a {010} cube plane. This is driven by the fact that the APB energy on {010} is **higher** than on {111} (thermodynamically, the system wants to minimize APB area). However, since {010} is **not a glide plane**, the dislocation becomes **locked**.

- **Above peak temperature**: At very high T, climb processes enable the locked dislocation to bypass, leading to a normal strength decrease.

$$\tau_{\text{critical}} \propto \frac{\gamma_{APB}^{\{010\}}}{\gamma_{APB}^{\{111\}}} \cdot f(T)$$

**3. Engineering significance (1 mark):**
The YSA enables Ni₃Al to retain and even increase its strength at high service temperatures — making it invaluable for **gas turbine blades, jet engine components, and high-temperature structural parts** where conventional alloys would soften catastrophically.

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a)** Compare the **crystal structures, densities, and high-temperature applications** of Ni₃Al, NiAl, Ti₃Al, and TiAl. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A Ni₃Al specimen has a **lattice parameter of 3.567 Å**. Calculate its **theoretical density** given: $M_{Ni} = 58.69$ g/mol, $M_{Al} = 26.98$ g/mol. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Q.B(a) (7 marks)

**Comparative table (5 marks):**

| Property | Ni₃Al | NiAl | Ti₃Al | TiAl |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Crystal structure** | L1₂ (ordered FCC) | B2 (ordered BCC, CsCl-type) | D0₁₉ (ordered HCP) | L1₀ (ordered FCT) |
| **Pearson symbol** | cP4 | cP2 | hP8 | tP4 |
| **Atoms/cell** | 4 | 2 | 8 | 4 |
| **Density (g/cm³)** | **7.43** | **5.86** | **4.20** | **3.90** |
| **Young's modulus (GPa)** | 200 | 294 | 145 | 175 |
| **Specific modulus** | 26.9 | 50.2 | 34.5 | 44.9 |
| **Melting point (°C)** | 1395 | 1638 | 1680 | 1460 |
| **High-T applications** | Jet engine turbine blades, combustion liners | Turbine blades, catalyst substrates | Compressor discs, compressor blades | Turbocharger wheels, exhaust valves, LPT blades |

**High-T application note (2 marks):**
- **Ni₃Al** and **NiAl** dominate **aero-engine hot sections** above 1000 °C; both form protective Al₂O₃ layers.
- **Ti₃Al** is used in **compressor sections** (up to ~700 °C) due to moderate density and good creep resistance.
- **TiAl** is the **lightest** high-temperature intermetallic — used in **automotive turbochargers** (e.g., Mitsubishi Lancer Evolution) and **low-pressure turbine blades** (GE9X engine) replacing Ni-based superalloys with significant weight savings.

---

#### Model Solution — Q.B(b) (7 marks)

**Given:**
- Lattice parameter: $a = 3.567$ Å $= 3.567 \times 10^{-8}$ cm
- $M_{Ni} = 58.69$ g/mol
- $M_{Al} = 26.98$ g/mol
- $N_A = 6.022 \times 10^{23}$ mol⁻¹

**Step 1: Identify atoms per unit cell (1 mark)**

For L1₂ (Ni₃Al): $n_{\text{atoms}} = 4$ (1 Al + 3 Ni)

**Step 2: Molar mass of formula unit (1 mark)**

$$M_{Ni_3Al} = 3 \times 58.69 + 1 \times 26.98$$
$$M_{Ni_3Al} = 176.07 + 26.98 = 203.05 \text{ g/mol}$$

**Step 3: Unit cell volume (1 mark)**

$$V_{cell} = a^3 = (3.567 \times 10^{-8})^3 = 4.536 \times 10^{-23} \text{ cm}^3$$

**Step 4: Apply density formula (3 marks)**

$$\rho = \frac{n_{formula} \times M}{N_A \times V_{cell}}$$

Number of formula units per unit cell: $n_{formula} = 1$

$$\rho = \frac{1 \times 203.05}{6.022 \times 10^{23} \times 4.536 \times 10^{-23}}$$

$$\rho = \frac{203.05}{27.314}$$

$$\boxed{\rho \approx 7.43 \text{ g/cm}^3}$$

**Step 5: Interpretation (1 mark)**
The theoretical density of Ni₃Al is **~7.43 g/cm³**, which is lower than pure Ni (8.90 g/cm³) due to the presence of lighter Al atoms. This contributes to its superior specific strength compared to Ni-based superalloys.

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls — Intermetallics Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing L1₂ with L1₀** — L1₂ is cubic (Ni₃Al); L1₀ is tetragonal (TiAl). Examiners deduct 1–2 marks for this.
>
> 2. **Wrong atom count in L1₂** — Students often write "4 atoms" without justifying the calculation: $8 \times \frac{1}{8} + 6 \times \frac{1}{2}$. Always **show the arithmetic**.
>
> 3. **In density problems, using atomic mass instead of formula unit mass** — For Ni₃Al, use $3 \times M_{Ni} + 1 \times M_{Al} = 203.05$ g/mol, **not** $M_{Ni}$ alone.
>
> 4. **Forgetting to convert Å to cm** — $1$ Å $= 10^{-8}$ cm. Failure to convert gives densities off by a factor of $10^{24}$. Examiners immediately catch this.
>
> 5. **Describing YSA without naming Kear–Wilsdorf** — Generic "dislocation locking" answers score 1/3 marks; the specific mechanism must be cited.
>
> 6. **In order-parameter problems, mixing up $r_\alpha$ and $X_\alpha$** — $r_\alpha$ is fraction of *correctly occupied* α-sites; $X_\alpha$ is the overall atomic fraction of α atoms.
>
> 7. **Omitting engineering applications** — KTU theory questions often carry 1–2 marks for "real-world relevance" — always link structure → property → application.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **🔖 KTU PEMUT412 — Module 3 / Intermetallics — 60-Second Revision Checklist:**

### Core Concepts
- ✅ Intermetallic = ordered compound of ≥2 metals with **fixed stoichiometry** and **distinct crystal structure**
- ✅ Long-range order parameter $S \in [0, 1]$; $S = 1$ is perfect order
- ✅ Bonding = metallic + covalent + ionic hybrid

### Crystal Structures (Must Memorize)
- ✅ **L1₂** (Ni₃Al): ordered FCC; $a \approx 3.57$ Å; 4 atoms/cell; CN = 12
- ✅ **B2** (NiAl): ordered BCC (CsCl-type); 2 atoms/cell; CN = 8
- ✅ **D0₁₉** (Ti₃Al): ordered HCP; 8 atoms/cell
- ✅ **L1₀** (TiAl): ordered FCT; 4 atoms/cell; $c/a > 1$

### Key Properties (Recall Numbers)
- ✅ Densities: NiAl (5.86) < TiAl (3.90) < Ti₃Al (4.20) < Fe₃Al (6.72) < Ni₃Al (7.43) g/cm³
- ✅ Specific modulus ranking: TiAl > NiAl > Ti₃Al > Ni₃Al
- ✅ Yield Strength Anomaly peaks at 600–800 °C in Ni₃Al

### Critical Mechanisms
- ✅ **Kear–Wilsdorf locking** → {111} → {010} cross-slip → YSA
- ✅ APB energy drives ordering tendency
- ✅ Boron microalloying → grain-boundary ductilization of Ni₃Al

### Formulas (Must Memorize)
- ✅ $S = (r_\alpha - X_\alpha)/(1 - X_\alpha)$
- ✅ $\rho = nM/(N_A \cdot a^3)$
- ✅ APF$_{B2} = \sqrt{3}\pi/8 \approx 0.680$
- ✅ $a_{BCC} = 4R/\sqrt{3}$; $a_{FCC} = 2R\sqrt{2}$

### Applications
- ✅ Ni₃Al / NiAl → **aero turbine blades** (T > 1000 °C)
- ✅ TiAl → **turbocharger wheels**, LPT blades (lightweight, 3.9 g/cm³)
- ✅ Ni-Ti → **shape-memory actuators** (Nitinol)
- ✅ SmCo₅ → **permanent magnets**
- ✅ LaNi₅ → **hydrogen storage**

### Ductilization Strategies
- ✅ B doping in Ni₃Al
- ✅ Cr, Mn, Fe in NiAl
- ✅ Ductile-phase reinforcement (Mo, Nb)
- ✅ Grain refinement → single crystal
- ✅ Intermetallic Matrix Composites (IMCs)

### Processing Routes
- ✅ VIM + VAR melting
- ✅ Mechanical alloying
- ✅ SHS (Self-Propagating High-Temperature Synthesis)
- ✅ XD™ in-situ process for IMCs

> **Best of luck with your KTU examinations!** 🎓

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Alloys and their potential role as matrices in composites
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT412 - COMPOSITE MATERIALS AND CERAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Alloys and their potential role as matrices in composites

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Metal Matrix Composites (MMCs)**, the **matrix** is the continuous metallic phase that binds the reinforcement together, transfers the applied load to the reinforcement, protects the reinforcement from environmental degradation, and provides the bulk shape and surface finish of the component. An **alloy** — a metallic substance composed of two or more elements, at least one of which is a metal, possessing metallic bonding characteristics — is the principal candidate for this matrix role in structural and functional MMCs.

KTU 2024 Definition (Exam-Oriented):
> *"An alloy matrix in a composite is a multi-phase or single-phase metallic continuum, obtained by combining a principal metal with one or more alloying elements (solute atoms), whose primary function is to provide a ductile, tough, thermally-conductive, and load-transferring medium that encapsulates the reinforcement phase, while itself contributing to the overall mechanical, thermal, and tribological performance of the composite."*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Concrete-and-Rebar Model**
> Imagine building a heavy-load-bearing pillar. You pour wet concrete (the **alloy matrix**) into a mould where steel rods (the **reinforcement**, e.g., SiC particles, Al₂O₃ fibres) are already placed. The concrete on its own is strong in compression but weak in tension. The rebar is strong in tension but thin and would buckle alone. Together:
> - The **concrete (alloy)** holds everything in shape, protects the rebar from rust, and transfers the load evenly.
> - The **rebar (reinforcement)** carries the heavy tensile and shear stresses.
> - The **interface** between concrete and rebar is the bond that makes the system work.

In MMCs, the alloy plays the role of the concrete: it is the **continuous, ductile, tough phase**, while the ceramic reinforcement plays the role of the rebar: it is the **stiff, strong, brittle load-bearing phase**.

### 1.3 Why Use Alloys (and Not Pure Metals) as Matrices?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight for KTU Exams**
> Pure metals almost never serve as MMC matrices. Alloys are preferred because:
> 1. **Solid-solution strengthening** – Solute atoms distort the lattice and resist dislocation motion.
> 2. **Precipitation hardening capability** – Heat-treatable alloys (e.g., Al-Cu, Al-Zn-Mg-Cu) can be age-hardened to match the stiffness of reinforcements.
> 3. **Improved wettability with reinforcements** – Alloying elements such as Mg, Si, and Ti improve chemical bonding with ceramic reinforcements.
> 4. **Lower density options** – Light-metal alloys (Al, Mg, Ti) give high specific stiffness.
> 5. **Tailored CTE (Coefficient of Thermal Expansion)** – Alloying can tune CTE to be closer to that of the reinforcement, reducing thermal residual stresses.
> 6. **Superior high-temperature strength** – Nickel and cobalt superalloys maintain strength above **800 °C**, where pure metals would creep catastrophically.

### 1.4 Standard Metrics at a Glance

- **Density (ρ)** of common matrix alloys: Al ≈ **2.7 g/cm³**, Mg ≈ **1.74 g/cm³**, Ti ≈ **4.5 g/cm³**, Ni ≈ **8.9 g/cm³**, Co ≈ **8.9 g/cm³**.
- **Young's Modulus (E)** of typical matrix alloys: Al alloys **70–80 GPa**, Ti alloys **110–120 GPa**, Ni superalloys **200–220 GPa**.
- **Melting point**: Al **660 °C**, Ti **1668 °C**, Ni **1455 °C** — critical for selecting processing routes (solid vs. liquid state).
- **Specific stiffness (E/ρ)**: Mg alloys ≈ **26 GPa·cm³/g**, Al alloys ≈ **27 GPa·cm³/g**, Ti alloys ≈ **25 GPa·cm³/g**, steels ≈ **25 GPa·cm³/g**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Functions the Alloy Matrix Must Perform

1. **Load Transfer** – Stress must be transmitted from the matrix to the high-strength/stiffness reinforcement via shear at the interface. This requires a minimum matrix yield/shear strength.
2. **Protection** – The matrix shields brittle ceramic reinforcements (e.g., Al₂O₃, SiC, B₄C) from surface damage, moisture, and oxidation.
3. **Binding and Geometric Stability** – Holds the reinforcement in a designed spatial arrangement (aligned, random, woven, etc.).
4. **Ductility and Toughness** – Provides plastic deformation capability, arresting crack propagation initiated in the brittle reinforcement.
5. **Thermal Management** – Conducts heat away from the reinforcement (especially important in electronic and brake applications).
6. **Processability** – Must be formable by casting, powder metallurgy, in-situ reactions, spray deposition, etc.

### 2.2 The "Why" Behind Choosing a Particular Alloy System

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Selection Logic (very high KTU yield)**
> The matrix alloy is selected based on the **service conditions** of the composite:
>
> | Service Condition | Preferred Matrix Alloy Family |
> |---|---|
> | Aerospace structural, low density | **Aluminium alloys** (e.g., 2024, 6061, 7075) |
> | High specific strength at moderate T | **Titanium alloys** (e.g., Ti-6Al-4V) |
> | Lightest structural applications | **Magnesium alloys** (e.g., AZ91, AM60) |
> | High temperature (≈ 800–1100 °C) | **Nickel-based superalloys** (e.g., Inconel 718) |
> | Wear & corrosion at high T | **Cobalt-based superalloys** (e.g., Stellite) |
> | Electrical & thermal conduction | **Copper alloys** (e.g., Cu-Al₂O₃) |
> | Extreme temperature (> 1100 °C) | **Intermetallics** (Ni-Al, Ti-Al) |

### 2.3 Detailed Discussion of Key Alloy Matrix Systems

#### A. Aluminium Alloys (the most widely used matrix family)

- **Why aluminium?** Density = **2.7 g/cm³**, good thermal/electrical conductivity, relatively low melting point (**660 °C**) → easy liquid-state processing, and excellent compatibility with ceramic reinforcements (Al₂O₃, SiC, B₄C, C-fibres).
- **Common grades**:
  - **Al-Si cast alloys (e.g., A356, A359)** – Excellent fluidity, low shrinkage, good wettability with SiC.
  - **Al-Cu (2xxx series, e.g., 2024)** – High strength, precipitation hardenable (θ′ Al₂Cu).
  - **Al-Mg-Si (6xxx series, e.g., 6061)** – Good formability and corrosion resistance; precipitation phase Mg₂Si.
  - **Al-Zn-Mg-Cu (7xxx series, e.g., 7075)** – Highest strength aluminium alloys; aerospace grade.
  - **Al-Li (8xxx series)** – Very low density (Li is the lightest metal, ρ = 0.534 g/cm³); aerospace composites.
- **Typical applications**: Brake rotors (Al/SiC), pistons (Al/Al₂O₃), aerospace wing skins, drive shafts, electronic packaging.

#### B. Magnesium Alloys

- **Why magnesium?** Lightest structural metal (**ρ ≈ 1.74 g/cm³**), highest specific stiffness, good damping capacity.
- **Challenges**: Poor corrosion resistance, flammability during molten processing (must use SF₆/CO₂ cover gas), limited ductility.
- **Common grades**: **AZ91** (Mg-9Al-1Zn), **AM60** (Mg-6Al-0.3Mn), **AS41**, **ZE41**, **WE43** (rare-earth containing).
- **Reinforcements**: SiC particles, Al₂O₃, carbon fibres.
- **Applications**: Automotive steering wheels, seat frames, laptop housings, helicopter gearboxes.

#### C. Titanium Alloys

- **Why titanium?** Excellent strength-to-weight ratio up to **~ 500 °C**, biocompatible, corrosion-resistant.
- **Common grades**: **Ti-6Al-4V** (α-β alloy, the workhorse), **Ti-10V-2Fe-3Al**, **Ti-6242**, commercially pure Ti (CP-Ti).
- **Challenges**: Very reactive with ceramic reinforcements at high T → forms brittle interphases (e.g., TiB, TiC, Ti₅Si₃); requires solid-state processing (powder metallurgy) or vacuum/inert processing.
- **Reinforcements**: SiC (continuous fibres), TiB, TiC, B₄C.
- **Applications**: Jet engine compressor blades, landing gears, biomedical implants, missile structures.

#### D. Nickel-Based Superalloys

- **Why nickel?** Maintains **yield strength > 1000 MPa** at **800 °C**, oxidation-resistant, creep-resistant.
- **Common grades**: **Inconel 718** (γ′/γ″ strengthened), **Inconel 625**, **Nimonic 90**, **MAR-M247** (DS/SC cast).
- **Reinforcements**: Al₂O₃ (Saffil), SiC, TiB₂, Y₂O₃ (oxide-dispersion-strengthened, ODS).
- **Applications**: Turbine blades, combustion chambers, rocket nozzles, high-T structural panels.

#### E. Cobalt-Based Superalloys

- **Why cobalt?** Excellent hot hardness, wear resistance, corrosion resistance, biocompatibility.
- **Common grades**: **Stellite 6** (Co-Cr-W-C), **CoCrMo** (ASTM F75, biomedical), **Haynes 25** (L-605).
- **Reinforcements**: WC, Cr₃C₂, diamond, Al₂O₃.
- **Applications**: Valve seats, cutting tools, orthopaedic implants, wear-resistant coatings.

#### F. Copper Alloys

- **Why copper?** Highest electrical and thermal conductivity among structural metals → heat-sink and electrical contact applications.
- **Reinforcements**: Al₂O₃ (Glidcop), SiC, C (diamond, carbon nanotubes).
- **Applications**: Resistance welding electrodes, lead frames, rocket nozzle liners, heat sinks for power electronics.

#### G. Iron-Based Alloys (Steels)

- Less common as MMC matrices due to high density and limited property gains, but used in wear-resistant systems: tool steels reinforced with TiC, NbC, or VC for cutting and mining.

#### H. Intermetallic Alloys

- **Examples**: NiAl, Ni₃Al, TiAl, Ti₃Al, FeAl, Fe₃Al.
- **Why?** Ordered crystal structures give exceptional high-temperature strength, low density (TiAl ≈ **3.9 g/cm³**), and good oxidation resistance.
- **Drawback**: Severe room-temperature brittleness — often reinforced with ductile phases or ceramic fibres (e.g., **XD** — exothermic in-situ processes).

### 2.4 Strengthening Mechanisms in the Alloy Matrix

The alloy matrix can be strengthened by all the classical metallurgical mechanisms, which are then **augmented** by the reinforcement:

1. **Solid-solution strengthening** (solute atoms distort the lattice).
2. **Grain-boundary strengthening (Hall-Petch)** – Smaller grain size → higher yield strength.
3. **Strain (work) hardening** – Dislocation multiplication.
4. **Precipitation (age) hardening** – e.g., Al-Cu θ′, Al-Zn-Mg-Cu η′, Ni-Al γ′.
5. **Dispersion strengthening** – Stable oxide particles (ODS, e.g., Y₂O₃ in PM2000).
6. **Composite strengthening** (rule of mixtures — covered in Module 2).

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Concept | Mathematical Expression | Variables & Units | Application / KTU Note |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Density of composite** (rule of mixtures) | $\rho_c = V_m \rho_m + V_r \rho_r$ | $V$ = volume fraction, $\rho$ = density (g/cm³) | Used to check weight savings; $V_m + V_r = 1$ |
| **Young's modulus (longitudinal, isostrain)** | $E_c = V_m E_m + V_r E_r$ | $E$ = Young's modulus (GPa) | Voigt model — valid for aligned continuous fibres loaded along the fibre direction |
| **Young's modulus (transverse, isostress)** | $\dfrac{1}{E_c} = \dfrac{V_m}{E_m} + \dfrac{V_r}{E_r}$ | as above | Reuss model — valid for load perpendicular to fibres |
| **Rule of mixtures (strength, longitudinal)** | $\sigma_c = V_m \sigma_m + V_r \sigma_r$ | $\sigma$ = strength (MPa) | Voigt-strength |
| **CTE of composite** | $\alpha_c = \dfrac{V_m E_m \alpha_m + V_r E_r \alpha_r}{V_m E_m + V_r E_r}$ | $\alpha$ = CTE (×10⁻⁶/K) | Turner model: minimises thermal residual stress when $\alpha_c$ is matched |
| **Specific stiffness** | $E/\rho$ | GPa·cm³/g | Higher = better for aerospace |
| **Hall-Petch strengthening** | $\sigma_y = \sigma_0 + k_y d^{-1/2}$ | $d$ = grain diameter (m), $k_y$ = constant (MPa·m^½) | Grain refinement in matrix |
| **Critical fibre length (load transfer)** | $\ell_c = \dfrac{\sigma_{f,u} d}{2 \tau_i}$ | $\sigma_{f,u}$ = fibre ultimate strength, $d$ = fibre diameter, $\tau_i$ = interfacial shear strength | Matrix must allow $\ell \geq \ell_c$ for efficient load transfer |
| **Density reduction (%)** | $\Delta\rho\% = \dfrac{\rho_m - \rho_c}{\rho_m} \times 100$ | — | Quantifies weight savings vs. monolithic alloy |
| **Wettability criterion (liquid processing)** | $\gamma_{sv} > \gamma_{sl} + \gamma_{lv}\cos\theta$ | $\gamma$ = interfacial energies, $\theta$ = contact angle | Determines if melt can infiltrate reinforcement |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **⚠ Markdown rendering note:** All absolute-value bars, set-builder braces, and parallel symbols in formula tables have been replaced with their LaTeX equivalents (e.g., `\vert` if needed) to prevent table-breaking. The above table uses only `V`, `E`, `σ`, `ρ`, `α` variables.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace (Boeing 787, Airbus A350)**: Al/SiC and Ti/SiC MMCs in brackets, pylons, and landing-gear sub-components achieve **20–30% weight savings** over monolithic aluminium and titanium.
- **Automotive (Toyota, Ford, Porsche)**: Al/SiC pistons, Al/Al₂O₃ brake rotors, Mg/SiC gearbox housings reduce weight and improve wear.
- **Electronics (CPU/LED heat sinks)**: Cu/Al₂O₃ (Glidcop Al-15) and Cu/diamond MMCs provide thermal conductivities > **300 W/m·K**.
- **Defence (armour, missiles)**: Al/B₄C and Al/SiC for ceramic-armour backing plates; Ti/TiB₂ for kinetic-energy penetrators.
- **Biomedical**: CoCrMo reinforced with HA (hydroxyapatite) or zirconia for implants.
- **Energy (gas turbines)**: Ni/Al₂O₃ and Ni/Y₂O₃ (ODS) for combustor liners in jet engines operating at **> 1000 °C**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Effective Young's Modulus of an MMC (Voigt / Isostrain Model)

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this derivation matters in KTU exams:**
> It is a guaranteed sub-part of any question on composite properties. The student must derive (or be ready to write) the isostrain equation and explain its assumptions.

#### Assumptions

1. The reinforcement is in the form of **continuous, aligned fibres** parallel to the loading axis.
2. Perfect bonding at the fibre–matrix interface (no slip).
3. Both phases experience the **same strain** (isostrain / equal strain / Voigt condition).
4. The composite is free of voids.
5. Loads are applied along the fibre direction.

#### Step-by-Step Derivation

Let:
- $A_c$ = cross-sectional area of composite
- $A_m$ = cross-sectional area of matrix
- $A_r$ = cross-sectional area of reinforcement
- $V_m$ = volume fraction of matrix, $V_r$ = volume fraction of reinforcement
- $\sigma_c$, $\sigma_m$, $\sigma_r$ = stresses in composite, matrix, reinforcement
- $\varepsilon_c$, $\varepsilon_m$, $\varepsilon_r$ = strains in composite, matrix, reinforcement
- $P$ = applied tensile load

**Step 1 — Geometric compatibility (isostrain):**

$$
\varepsilon_c = \varepsilon_m = \varepsilon_r = \varepsilon
$$

This is the **Voigt (isostrain) assumption**: both phases elongate by the same amount because they are bonded and aligned.

**Step 2 — Load equilibrium (force balance):**

The total load is shared by both phases:

$$
P = P_m + P_r
$$

Express each load as stress × area:

$$
\sigma_c A_c = \sigma_m A_m + \sigma_r A_r
$$

**Step 3 — Introduce volume fractions:**

Recall $V_m = A_m / A_c$ and $V_r = A_r / A_c$ (areas are proportional to volumes for uniform cross-section).

$$
\sigma_c = \sigma_m V_m + \sigma_r V_r
$$

**Step 4 — Apply Hooke's Law to each phase:**

$$
\sigma_m = E_m \varepsilon \quad ; \quad \sigma_r = E_r \varepsilon
$$

(All phases have the same strain $\varepsilon$.)

**Step 5 — Substitute into the equilibrium equation:**

$$
\sigma_c = E_m \varepsilon V_m + E_r \varepsilon V_r
$$

$$
\sigma_c = (E_m V_m + E_r V_r) \, \varepsilon
$$

**Step 6 — Define effective modulus and solve:**

By definition, $\sigma_c = E_c \varepsilon$, so:

$$
E_c = E_m V_m + E_r V_r
$$

This is the **Rule of Mixtures (ROM) for longitudinal Young's modulus**, the **Voigt upper bound**.

**Step 7 — Closed-form vector expression:**

$$
E_c = E_m (1 - V_r) + E_r V_r
$$

**Step 8 — Use the constraint $V_m + V_r = 1$ to eliminate $V_m$ if required:**

$$
E_c = E_m (1 - V_r) + E_r V_r = E_m + (E_r - E_m) V_r
$$

This is a **linear equation in $V_r$** with slope $(E_r - E_m)$ and intercept $E_m$, useful for plotting $E_c$ vs. $V_r$.

#### Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style)

> **Q:** A unidirectional continuous-fibre MMC has an aluminium matrix ($E_m$ = 70 GPa) reinforced with 40 vol.% of alumina fibres ($E_r$ = 380 GPa). Find the longitudinal Young's modulus.

**Solution:**

$$
E_c = E_m (1 - V_r) + E_r V_r
$$

$$
E_c = 70 \times (1 - 0.40) + 380 \times 0.40
$$

$$
E_c = 70 \times 0.60 + 380 \times 0.40
$$

$$
E_c = 42 + 152 = 194 \; \text{GPa}
$$

> **Result:** $E_c = 194 \; \text{GPa}$, which is a **177% increase** over the monolithic aluminium matrix.

### 3.2 Derivation: Effective Density of an MMC

#### Step-by-Step

**Step 1 — Mass of composite equals sum of phase masses:**

$$
m_c = m_m + m_r
$$

**Step 2 — Introduce densities and volumes:**

$$
\rho_c V_c = \rho_m V_m + \rho_r V_r
$$

**Step 3 — Use $V_c = V_m + V_r$:**

$$
\rho_c = \rho_m \frac{V_m}{V_c} + \rho_r \frac{V_r}{V_c} = \rho_m V_m + \rho_r V_r
$$

**Result — Rule of Mixtures for density:**

$$
\rho_c = \rho_m (1 - V_r) + \rho_r V_r
$$

#### Worked Numerical Example

> **Q:** A 30 vol.% SiC-particle-reinforced 6061-Al composite. Given $\rho_{Al} = 2.70 \; g/cm^3$ and $\rho_{SiC} = 3.20 \; g/cm^3$. Find the density and the percentage increase over the monolithic alloy.

**Solution:**

$$
\rho_c = 2.70 \times 0.70 + 3.20 \times 0.30
$$

$$
\rho_c = 1.89 + 0.96 = 2.85 \; g/cm^3
$$

Percentage increase:

$$
\Delta\rho\% = \frac{2.85 - 2.70}{2.70} \times 100 = 5.56\%
$$

> **Conclusion:** A small density penalty (+5.6%) is accompanied by a much larger improvement in stiffness and strength — the hallmark reason alloys are used as matrices.

### 3.3 Derivation: Thermal Expansion Coefficient (CTE) of an MMC (Turner Model)

#### Step-by-Step

**Step 1 — Strain additivity (assuming isostrain under uniform temperature change $\Delta T$):**

$$
\varepsilon_c = \varepsilon_m + \varepsilon_T = \varepsilon_r + \varepsilon_T
$$

where $\varepsilon_T = \alpha \Delta T$ is the thermal strain in each phase and $\varepsilon_m$, $\varepsilon_r$ are mechanical strains.

**Step 2 — Force equilibrium (Turner model assumes uniform hydrostatic stress):**

$$
\sigma_c = \sigma_m V_m = \sigma_r V_r
$$

**Step 3 — Hooke's law substitution:**

$$
E_c (\varepsilon_c - \alpha_c \Delta T) = E_m (\varepsilon_c - \alpha_m \Delta T) V_m = E_r (\varepsilon_c - \alpha_r \Delta T) V_r
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $\alpha_c$:**

$$
\alpha_c = \frac{\alpha_m E_m V_m + \alpha_r E_r V_r}{E_m V_m + E_r V_r}
$$

**Result — Turner CTE rule:**

$$
\alpha_c = \frac{\alpha_m E_m (1 - V_r) + \alpha_r E_r V_r}{E_m (1 - V_r) + E_r V_r}
$$

This expression is crucial in KTU problems on **thermal stress minimisation**.

#### Worked Numerical Example

> **Q:** Find the CTE of a 30 vol.% SiC-particle/6061-Al composite. Given: $E_{Al} = 70 \; GPa$, $E_{SiC} = 410 \; GPa$, $\alpha_{Al} = 23 \times 10^{-6} /K$, $\alpha_{SiC} = 4.5 \times 10^{-6} /K$.

**Solution:**

Numerator:

$$
\alpha_m E_m (1 - V_r) + \alpha_r E_r V_r = (23 \times 10^{-6})(70)(0.70) + (4.5 \times 10^{-6})(410)(0.30)
$$

$$
= 1127 \times 10^{-6} + 553.5 \times 10^{-6} = 1680.5 \times 10^{-6} \; GPa/K
$$

Denominator:

$$
E_m (1 - V_r) + E_r V_r = 70 \times 0.70 + 410 \times 0.30 = 49 + 123 = 172 \; GPa
$$

$$
\alpha_c = \frac{1680.5 \times 10^{-6}}{172} \approx 9.77 \times 10^{-6} /K
$$

> **Result:** The composite CTE ($\approx 9.8 \times 10^{-6} /K$) is dramatically lower than the pure matrix CTE ($23 \times 10^{-6} /K$), making the composite dimensionally far more stable for electronic-packaging and precision-instrument applications.

### 3.4 Derivation: Critical Fibre Length for Effective Load Transfer

#### Step-by-Step

**Step 1 — Consider a single fibre of diameter $d$ and length $\ell$ embedded in a matrix.**

**Step 2 — Force balance on the fibre tip vs. the side interface:**

The fibre carries its load through shear stress $\tau_i$ at the cylindrical interface (area $\pi d \ell$):

$$
F_{\text{shear}} = \tau_i \pi d \ell
$$

**Step 3 — At the fibre's centre, the fibre reaches its ultimate tensile stress $\sigma_{f,u}$:**

$$
F_{\text{fibre}} = \sigma_{f,u} \frac{\pi d^2}{4}
$$

**Step 4 — Set the two equal to find the minimum length for full fibre-strength utilisation:**

$$
\sigma_{f,u} \frac{\pi d^2}{4} = \tau_i \pi d \ell_c
$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $\ell_c$:**

$$
\ell_c = \frac{\sigma_{f,u} d}{4 \tau_i}
$$

**Result — Critical fibre length:**

$$
\ell_c = \frac{\sigma_{f,u} d}{4 \tau_i}
$$

(Some textbooks use the equivalent $\ell_c = \sigma_{f,u} d / (2 \tau_i)$ for the **total fibre length** at the centre; always check the convention in your reference.)

#### Worked Numerical Example

> **Q:** An Al/SiC composite has $\sigma_{f,u} = 3500 \; MPa$, $d = 100 \; \mu m$, and $\tau_i = 50 \; MPa$. Find the critical fibre length.

**Solution:**

$$
\ell_c = \frac{3500 \times 100 \times 10^{-6}}{4 \times 50} = \frac{0.35}{200} = 1.75 \times 10^{-3} \; m
$$

$$
\boxed{\ell_c = 1.75 \; mm}
$$

> **Conclusion:** Fibres shorter than 1.75 mm will be **pulled out** before reaching full strength; longer fibres (or use of a higher-$\tau_i$ interface coating such as Si or Mg) will be **fully loaded** before fracture.

### 3.5 Derivation: Specific Stiffness Improvement of an MMC

#### Step-by-Step

**Step 1 — Specific stiffness of the matrix (monolithic alloy):**

$$
(S_m) = \frac{E_m}{\rho_m}
$$

**Step 2 — Specific stiffness of the MMC:**

$$
(S_c) = \frac{E_c}{\rho_c} = \frac{E_m (1 - V_r) + E_r V_r}{\rho_m (1 - V_r) + \rho_r V_r}
$$

**Step 3 — Improvement ratio:**

$$
\eta = \frac{S_c}{S_m} = \frac{E_m + (E_r - E_m) V_r}{E_m} \cdot \frac{\rho_m}{\rho_m + (\rho_r - \rho_m) V_r}
$$

For lightweight reinforcements (e.g., C-fibres, $\rho \approx 1.8 \; g/cm^3$, $E \approx 230 \; GPa$) in a light matrix (Al), $\eta > 2$ is achievable.

#### Worked Numerical Example

> **Q:** Compare specific stiffness of pure Al ($E_m = 70 \; GPa$, $\rho_m = 2.7 \; g/cm^3$) with a 30 vol.% C-fibre/Al composite ($E_r = 230 \; GPa$, $\rho_r = 1.8 \; g/cm^3$).

**Solution:**

Specific stiffness of Al:

$$
S_m = \frac{70}{2.7} \approx 25.93 \; GPa \cdot cm^3/g
$$

Specific stiffness of MMC:

$$
E_c = 70 \times 0.7 + 230 \times 0.3 = 49 + 69 = 118 \; GPa
$$

$$
\rho_c = 2.7 \times 0.7 + 1.8 \times 0.3 = 1.89 + 0.54 = 2.43 \; g/cm^3
$$

$$
S_c = \frac{118}{2.43} \approx 48.56 \; GPa \cdot cm^3/g
$$

Improvement ratio:

$$
\eta = \frac{48.56}{25.93} \approx 1.87 \;\;(i.e.,\;87\%\;\text{increase})
$$

> **Conclusion:** Despite a lower absolute density, C-fibres nearly **double the specific stiffness** of aluminium — the entire rationale for using Al/C MMCs in aerospace and racing.
