## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions (with Model Answers)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]**
*"Why are alloys preferred over pure metals as matrices in MMCs? List any three reasons."*

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark per point):**

Alloys are preferred over pure metals as MMC matrices for the following reasons:

1. **Higher strength via solid-solution and precipitation hardening** – Alloying introduces solute atoms and second-phase precipitates (e.g., θ′-Al₂Cu in 2xxx, γ′-Ni₃(Al,Ti) in Inconel) that obstruct dislocation motion and raise the matrix yield strength, improving load transfer to the reinforcement.
2. **Improved wettability and interfacial bonding** – Active alloying elements such as Mg, Si, and Ti reduce the contact angle between the molten alloy and the ceramic reinforcement, facilitating liquid-state processing and giving a stronger matrix–reinforcement bond.
3. **Tailored thermo-physical properties** – Alloying allows the matrix CTE to be matched to that of the reinforcement (reducing thermal residual stresses), density to be minimised (Mg, Al, Ti systems), and high-temperature strength to be retained (Ni-, Co-based superalloys).
4. **Better corrosion and oxidation resistance** – Alloying with Cr, Al, Mo, and rare earths forms stable passive films, protecting both the matrix and the reinforcement–matrix interface from environmental degradation.
5. **Processability** – Alloy compositions can be tuned for the chosen processing route (low-melting Al-Si cast alloys for stir casting; γ′-strengthened Ni-base for directional solidification; etc.).

*(Any 3 of the above well-explained points fetch 3 marks.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]**
*"List four common alloy matrix systems used in MMCs and give one example application for each."*

**Model Answer (3 marks — 0.75 per row):**

| # | Alloy Matrix System | Example Application |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Aluminium alloys (e.g., A356, 6061, 7075) | Brake rotors and pistons (Al/SiC) in automobiles |
| 2 | Magnesium alloys (e.g., AZ91, AM60) | Lightweight gear-box housings in helicopters and cars |
| 3 | Titanium alloys (e.g., Ti-6Al-4V) | Jet-engine compressor blades and missile structures (Ti/SiC) |
| 4 | Nickel-based superalloys (e.g., Inconel 718) | Combustion-chamber liners in gas turbines (Ni/Al₂O₃ or Ni/Y₂O₃) |
| 5 | Cobalt-based superalloys (e.g., Stellite 6) | Valve seats and cutting-tool inserts (Co-Cr/WC) |
| 6 | Copper alloys (e.g., Cu-Al₂O₃ "Glidcop") | Heat sinks for power electronics and resistance-welding electrodes |

*(Any 4 rows above — 3 marks.)*

---

### 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Long-Answer Questions (with Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Pattern Reminder:** A 14-mark question has sub-parts, typically **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**, with internal choice between (a)/(c) and (b)/(d) OR between two full questions. Below, two complete alternative question sets are provided.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Option 1** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]`

**Q. (a)** *"Discuss in detail the role of alloy matrices in metal matrix composites, listing the essential properties required of the matrix alloy. Why is aluminium the most widely used matrix material in commercial MMCs?"* **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**1. Introduction — Role of the matrix (2 marks):**

The matrix in a Metal Matrix Composite is the **continuous, ductile, metallic phase** that:
- Binds the reinforcement together and holds it in the designed spatial arrangement.
- **Transfers the applied load** to the high-modulus/high-strength reinforcement through shear at the matrix–reinforcement interface.
- **Protects the brittle ceramic reinforcement** from surface damage, oxidation, and environmental attack.
- Provides **ductility and toughness**, arresting crack propagation and giving the composite damage tolerance.
- Carries **thermal and electrical currents** to and from the reinforcement (critical in electronic and brake applications).
- Imparts the **bulk shape, surface finish, and dimensional accuracy** of the final component.

**2. Essential properties of the matrix alloy (3 marks):**

| Property | Reason |
|---|---|
| Adequate **yield strength** and **shear strength** | Required to transfer load to the reinforcement without matrix shear failure |
| Sufficient **ductility / fracture toughness** | Absorbs energy, arrests cracks, gives impact resistance |
| **Low density** (for aerospace/automotive) | Maximises specific strength and specific stiffness |
| **Thermal stability** at service T | Avoids softening, creep, or microstructural degradation |
| **Compatible CTE** with the reinforcement | Minimises thermal residual stresses on cooling |
| **Good wettability** with the reinforcement (for liquid processing) | Ensures strong interfacial bonding |
| **Corrosion / oxidation resistance** | Protects the matrix–interface and prolongs service life |
| **Processability** by conventional routes (cast, wrought, PM) | Enables near-net-shape and cost-effective manufacturing |

**3. Why aluminium is the most widely used matrix (2 marks):**

- **Low density** ($\rho \approx 2.7 \; g/cm^3$) — high specific stiffness and strength.
- **Low melting point** (**660 °C**) — enables low-energy liquid-state processing (stir casting, squeeze casting, compocasting) and infiltration at moderate temperatures.
- **Excellent compatibility** with the most common reinforcements (Al₂O₃, SiC, B₄C, C-fibres) — alloying additions (Mg, Si) improve wettability.
- **High thermal/electrical conductivity** — useful for electronic packaging and heat-sink applications.
- **Mature alloy system** — multiple heat-treatable families (2xxx, 6xxx, 7xxx) allow property tailoring.
- **Cost-effective** — abundant and inexpensive.
- **Recyclable and corrosion-resistant** (especially 5xxx and 6xxx series).

**Conclusion:** Aluminium alloys offer the best overall balance of **specific properties, processability, and cost**, making them the dominant commercial MMC matrix.

---

**Q. (b)** *"A unidirectional continuous-fibre MMC contains 40 vol.% of SiC fibres in a 6061-aluminium matrix. Given: E_m = 70 GPa, E_r = 410 GPa, ρ_m = 2.70 g/cm³, ρ_r = 3.20 g/cm³, σ_m = 310 MPa, σ_r = 2500 MPa. Calculate:*
*(i) The longitudinal Young's modulus,*
*(ii) The density,*
*(iii) The specific modulus, and*
*(iv) The longitudinal tensile strength of the composite using the rule of mixtures.*
*Compare the specific modulus with that of monolithic 6061-Al."* **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer (full working):**

**Given:** $V_r = 0.40$, $V_m = 0.60$, $E_m = 70 \; GPa$, $E_r = 410 \; GPa$, $\rho_m = 2.70 \; g/cm^3$, $\rho_r = 3.20 \; g/cm^3$, $\sigma_m = 310 \; MPa$, $\sigma_r = 2500 \; MPa$.

**(i) Longitudinal Young's modulus (Voigt rule, 2 marks):**

$$
E_c = E_m V_m + E_r V_r
$$

$$
E_c = 70 \times 0.60 + 410 \times 0.40
$$

$$
E_c = 42 + 164 = 206 \; GPa
$$

**(ii) Density (1 mark):**

$$
\rho_c = \rho_m V_m + \rho_r V_r
$$

$$
\rho_c = 2.70 \times 0.60 + 3.20 \times 0.40
$$

$$
\rho_c = 1.62 + 1.28 = 2.90 \; g/cm^3
$$

**(iii) Specific modulus (2 marks):**

Composite:

$$
S_c = \frac{E_c}{\rho_c} = \frac{206}{2.90} \approx 71.03 \; GPa \cdot cm^3/g
$$

Monolithic 6061-Al:

$$
S_m = \frac{E_m}{\rho_m} = \frac{70}{2.70} \approx 25.93 \; GPa \cdot cm^3/g
$$

Comparison:

$$
\frac{S_c}{S_m} = \frac{71.03}{25.93} \approx 2.74
$$

The composite is **~2.74× stiffer per unit mass** than the monolithic 6061-Al.

**(iv) Longitudinal tensile strength (Voigt, 2 marks):**

$$
\sigma_c = \sigma_m V_m + \sigma_r V_r
$$

$$
\sigma_c = 310 \times 0.60 + 2500 \times 0.40
$$

$$
\sigma_c = 186 + 1000 = 1186 \; MPa
$$

> **Result:** The MMC has $E_c = 206 \; GPa$, $\rho_c = 2.90 \; g/cm^3$, $S_c = 71.0 \; GPa \cdot cm^3/g$, and $\sigma_c = 1186 \; MPa$, representing a **massive improvement** over the monolithic matrix ($E = 70 \; GPa$, $\sigma = 310 \; MPa$).

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Option 2** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Adapted]`

**Q. (a)** *"Explain the role of magnesium, titanium, and nickel-based alloys as matrices in MMCs. Discuss their advantages, limitations, and typical applications."* **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**1. Magnesium alloy matrices (2.5 marks):**

*Advantages:*
- **Lightest structural metal** ($\rho \approx 1.74 \; g/cm^3$) → highest specific stiffness and damping.
- Good castability (low melting point ≈ **650 °C**).
- Excellent machinability and dimensional stability.

*Limitations:*
- **Poor corrosion resistance** — requires coatings, surface treatments, or rare-earth additions.
- **Flammability** in molten state → requires SF₆/CO₂ protective atmosphere.
- **Limited strength and creep resistance** at elevated temperature.
- Lower modulus (~**45 GPa**) than Al, Ti, Ni.

*Applications:*
- Mg/SiC automotive steering wheels, seat frames, instrument panels.
- Mg/Al₂O₃ gear-box housings in helicopters (e.g., Eurocopter).
- Mg/Gr (graphite) vibration-damping components.

---

**2. Titanium alloy matrices (2.5 marks):**

*Advantages:*
- **High specific strength** up to ~500 °C.
- Excellent **corrosion resistance** and **biocompatibility**.
- Retains modulus (~**110–115 GPa**) at elevated T.
- Compatible with high-modulus fibres (SiC, Al₂O₃) for advanced MMCs.

*Limitations:*
- **High reactivity** with ceramic reinforcements above ~700 °C → forms brittle interphases (e.g., TiB, TiC, Ti₅Si₃).
- Expensive and difficult to machine.
- **High processing temperatures** → requires solid-state or vacuum/inert processing.

*Applications:*
- Ti-6Al-4V/SiC (SCS-6) for jet-engine compressor blades and rings.
- Ti/TiB in-situ composites for missile and hypersonic structures.
- Ti/SiCₓ biomedical implants and orthopaedic stems.

---

**3. Nickel-based superalloy matrices (2 marks):**

*Advantages:*
- **Exceptional high-temperature strength** (γ′ and γ″ precipitation hardening) — usable above **1000 °C**.
- **Oxidation and corrosion resistance** via Cr₂O₃ and Al₂O₃ surface scales.
- High modulus (~**200 GPa**).
- Compatible with oxide (Al₂O₃, Y₂O₃) and boride (TiB₂) reinforcements.

*Limitations:*
- **High density** (≈ **8.9 g/cm³**) → penalises weight-critical applications.
- Difficult to process; requires vacuum or inert gas.
- Expensive.

*Applications:*
- Ni/Al₂O₃ and Ni/Y₂O₃ (ODS) combustor liners in jet engines.
- Ni/TiB₂ turbine blades and rocket-nozzle liners.
- Ni/Cr₃C₂ high-T wear components.

**Conclusion:** The choice between Mg, Ti, and Ni superalloy matrices is dictated by the **service temperature, weight budget, and corrosive environment** of the application.

---

**Q. (b)** *"The critical fibre length of an SiC-fibre reinforced Ti-6Al-4V matrix composite is found to be 1.5 mm. The fibre has a diameter of 100 µm and the ultimate tensile strength is 3500 MPa. Calculate:*
*(i) The interfacial shear strength of the matrix–fibre bond, and*
*(ii) Whether a 5 mm long fibre will fail by fracture or by pull-out.*
*(iii) State two ways to reduce the critical fibre length."* **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Given:** $\ell_c = 1.5 \; mm = 1.5 \times 10^{-3} \; m$, $d = 100 \; \mu m = 1.0 \times 10^{-4} \; m$, $\sigma_{f,u} = 3500 \; MPa$.

**Formula:**

$$
\ell_c = \frac{\sigma_{f,u} d}{4 \tau_i}
$$

**(i) Interfacial shear strength $\tau_i$ (2 marks):**

Rearrange:

$$
\tau_i = \frac{\sigma_{f,u} d}{4 \ell_c}
$$

$$
\tau_i = \frac{3500 \times 10^{6} \times 1.0 \times 10^{-4}}{4 \times 1.5 \times 10^{-3}}
$$

$$
\tau_i = \frac{350}{6.0 \times 10^{-3}} = \frac{3.5 \times 10^{8}}{6.0 \times 10^{-3}} \cdot 10^{-6}
$$

Re-evaluate step by step:

$$
\sigma_{f,u} d = 3500 \times 10^{6} \times 1.0 \times 10^{-4} = 3.5 \times 10^{5} \; N/m
$$

$$
4 \ell_c = 4 \times 1.5 \times 10^{-3} = 6.0 \times 10^{-3} \; m
$$

$$
\tau_i = \frac{3.5 \times 10^{5}}{6.0 \times 10^{-3}} = 5.83 \times 10^{7} \; Pa
$$

$$
\boxed{\tau_i \approx 58.3 \; MPa}
$$

**(ii) Will a 5 mm fibre fracture or pull out? (3 marks):**

Compare fibre length to critical length:

$$
\ell = 5 \; mm > \ell_c = 1.5 \; mm
$$

Since $\ell \gg \ell_c$, the fibre will **fracture** (not pull out) because the load transferred to the fibre through interfacial shear exceeds the fibre fracture stress before the fibre slides out.

Quantitative check: Using $\ell/\ell_c = 5/1.5 = 3.33$, the load transferred to the fibre centre is:

$$
\sigma_f = \frac{2 \tau_i \ell}{d} = \frac{2 \times 58.3 \times 10^{6} \times 5 \times 10^{-3}}{1.0 \times 10^{-4}}
$$

$$
\sigma_f = \frac{5.83 \times 10^{5}}{1.0 \times 10^{-4}} = 5.83 \times 10^{9} \; Pa = 5830 \; MPa
$$

Since $\sigma_f = 5830 \; MPa > \sigma_{f,u} = 3500 \; MPa$, the fibre **will fracture**.

**Conclusion:** A 5 mm fibre **fails by fibre fracture** (a strong, brittle-mode failure).

**(iii) Two ways to reduce $\ell_c$ (2 marks):**

From $\ell_c = \sigma_{f,u} d / (4 \tau_i)$:

1. **Increase the interfacial shear strength $\tau_i$** — by fibre surface coating (e.g., PyC, BN, TiB₂, or by adding reactive alloying elements like Mg or Si in the matrix to promote chemical bonding).
2. **Reduce the fibre diameter $d$** — finer fibres have a much smaller critical length for the same strength and interface; e.g., micron-diameter whiskers vs. 100-µm monofilament fibres.
3. *(Bonus)* Use a fibre with lower $\sigma_{f,u}$ (not usually desirable since it reduces composite strength).

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common places where students LOSE marks in this topic:**
>
> 1. **Mixing up the role of the matrix vs. the reinforcement.** The matrix is **continuous, ductile, tough, and metallic** — not the ceramic. The reinforcement is the **discrete, stiff, brittle load-bearer**. Examiners deduct up to **2 marks** if these are interchanged in definition questions.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the volume-fraction constraint $V_m + V_r = 1$.** Many students use both $V_m$ and $V_r$ as given without checking they sum to 1. In multi-part problems, this leads to all subsequent calculations being wrong.
>
> 3. **Confusing Voigt and Reuss models.** Voigt (isostrain, rule of mixtures) is for **longitudinal** properties of unidirectional aligned continuous fibres. Reuss (isostress, inverse rule) is for **transverse** properties or for randomly oriented short fibres. Examiners will mark "ROM" answers as wrong if used in a transverse context.
>
> 4. **Confusing the Turner and Kerner's CTE models.** The Turner model (uniform hydrostatic stress) is used when the **interface is perfect**; Kerner's model (shear-lag) is used when there is **interfacial sliding**. Using the wrong one costs 1–2 marks.
>
> 5. **Not stating assumptions explicitly in derivations.** KTU examiners deduct up to **1 mark** if the Voigt/Reuss assumptions (perfect bonding, isostrain/isostress, void-free) are not stated.
>
> 6. **Units inconsistency.** Always include units in every step — e.g., MPa, GPa, g/cm³, ×10⁻⁶/K — and convert cm³/g to consistent units if needed. Marks are lost for unit mismatch, not for slight numerical round-off.
>
> 7. **Forgetting that the "matrix" in MMCs is an alloy, not a pure metal.** Examiners will deduct marks for any definition that says "the matrix is aluminium" — it should be "an aluminium alloy such as 6061 or A356."

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield KTU Rapid-Revision Summary — Alloys as Matrices in Composites**

- **Definition:** A matrix alloy is a continuous, ductile, metallic phase in an MMC that binds, protects, and transfers load to the reinforcement.
- **Alloys vs. pure metals:** Alloys give **solid-solution strengthening, precipitation hardening, improved wettability, tailored CTE, and high-T strength** — pure metals do not.
- **Most widely used matrix family: Aluminium alloys** (A356, 6061, 7075) — for their low density, low melting point, low cost, and good wettability with Al₂O₃/SiC/B₄C.
- **Lightest matrix: Magnesium alloys** (AZ91, AM60) — used where weight is critical; limited by corrosion and flammability.
- **Highest specific strength: Titanium alloys** (Ti-6Al-4V) — used for jet engines and biomedical implants; reactive with ceramics, requires solid-state processing.
- **High-temperature matrices: Ni and Co superalloys** (Inconel 718, Stellite) — for >800 °C service; heavy and expensive.
- **Electrical/thermal: Cu-alloys** (Glidcop, Cu-Al₂O₃) — heat sinks and welding electrodes.
- **Extreme-T matrices: Intermetallics** (NiAl, TiAl) — very high specific strength > 1000 °C but brittle at room T.
- **Six functions of the matrix:** load transfer, reinforcement protection, geometric stability, ductility/toughness, thermal/electrical conduction, processability.
- **Key formulas (must memorise):**
  - $E_c = E_m (1 - V_r) + E_r V_r$ (Voigt)
  - $1/E_c = (1 - V_r)/E_m + V_r/E_r$ (Reuss)
  - $\rho_c = \rho_m (1 - V_r) + \rho_r V_r$
  - $\alpha_c = [\alpha_m E_m (1 - V_r) + \alpha_r E_r V_r]/[E_m (1 - V_r) + E_r V_r]$ (Turner)
  - $\ell_c = \sigma_{f,u} d / (4 \tau_i)$ (critical fibre length)
- **Specific stiffness (E/ρ)** is the most important design metric for aerospace/automotive — both $E_c$ and $\rho_c$ follow the rule of mixtures.
- **Fibre-matrix interface** controls load transfer; weak interface → pull-out (tough), strong interface → brittle fracture.
- **Processing routes:** stir casting, squeeze casting, compocasting, powder metallurgy, in-situ reactions, spray deposition — selected based on the alloy and reinforcement.
- **Real-world impact:** Al/SiC brake rotors (Toyota), Al/Al₂O₃ pistons (Honda), Ti/SiC jet-engine parts (Rolls-Royce), Cu/Al₂O₃ heat sinks (Cray supercomputers), Mg/SiC car wheels (GM).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Properties, characteristics and applications of metals as matrix materials
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT412 - COMPOSITE MATERIALS AND CERAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Properties, characteristics and applications of metals as matrix materials

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 3 | SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Metal Matrix Composites (MMCs)** are engineered composite materials in which a **metallic matrix** (continuous phase) is reinforced with a secondary phase (typically ceramic particles, short fibers, whiskers, or continuous fibers). The metallic matrix binds the reinforcement, transfers load to it, and protects it from environmental degradation while contributing ductility, toughness, and thermal/electrical conductivity to the system.

In the KTU PEMUT412 syllabus framework, **metals used as matrix materials** form the continuous phase of MMCs. Unlike polymer matrices, metal matrices retain their metallic character at ambient and elevated temperatures, providing a unique combination of properties unattainable with polymeric or ceramic matrices.

> [!NOTE]
> **Defining Property of a Metal Matrix:** A metal matrix must be **ductile** (≥ 15% elongation at room temperature in pure form), **electrically and thermally conductive** (electrical resistivity < 10⁻⁵ Ω·cm), and possess a **crystalline structure** that can be plastically deformed without fracture.

The **most commonly employed metal matrices** in industrial MMC production are:

| Matrix Metal | Density (g/cm³) | Melting Point (°C) | Typical Reinforcement Forms |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| **Aluminum (Al)** | 2.70 | 660 | SiC, Al₂O₃, C fibers |
| **Magnesium (Mg)** | 1.74 | 650 | SiC, Al₂O₃, B fibers |
| **Titanium (Ti)** | 4.51 | 1668 | SiC, TiB₂, B fibers |
| **Copper (Cu)** | 8.96 | 1085 | SiC, Al₂O₃, C fibers |
| **Nickel (Ni)** | 8.90 | 1455 | SiC, Al₂O₃, W fibers |
| **Cobalt (Co)** | 8.90 | 1495 | WC, TiC |
| **Lead (Pb)** | 11.34 | 327 | Oxides, metallic powders |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy: The "Reinforced Concrete" Analogy

Imagine you are building a bridge. **Pure steel** is strong but can bend too much under heavy loads and is expensive. **Pure concrete** is cheap and compression-strong but brittle. When you embed **steel rods (reinforcement)** into **concrete (matrix)**, you get **reinforced concrete** — a material that handles both tension and compression far better than either component alone.

A **Metal Matrix Composite** works on the exact same principle:

- The **metal (matrix)** = the "concrete" — holds everything together, transfers load, gives toughness, allows plastic flow to relieve stress concentrations.
- The **reinforcement (ceramic/fiber)** = the "steel rods" — provides high stiffness, high strength, and wear resistance that the metal alone cannot deliver.
- The **interface** = the critical "bond zone" between the two — just as rust on steel rebars destroys the concrete-rebar bond, a poor interface ruins an MMC.

> [!TIP]
> **Key Intuition:** The metal matrix is the **"forgiving" phase** — it can absorb impact, conduct heat away, and conform to reinforcement geometry. The reinforcement is the **"rigid skeleton"** that resists deformation. The brilliance of MMCs is that this combination gives **stiffness + toughness** together — something monolithic materials rarely offer.

## 1.3 Why Use Metals as Matrices? — The Driving Forces

The selection of metals over polymers or ceramics as matrices is driven by **three fundamental engineering demands**:

1. **Operating Temperature:** Polymer matrices degrade above ~300 °C; metal matrices (especially Ni, Co, Ti) operate up to **1000 °C+**.
2. **Thermal Management:** Metals dissipate heat rapidly; critical for brake rotors, electronic heat sinks, and turbine components.
3. **Ductility & Damage Tolerance:** Brittle ceramic matrices fail catastrophically; metallic matrices provide **graceful failure** through plastic deformation.

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Kerala Board Examination Standard Values)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Reference Values for Key Metal Matrices (at 25 °C):**
> - **Young's Modulus of pure Al:** **E_Al = 70 GPa**
> - **Young's Modulus of pure Mg:** **E_Mg = 45 GPa**
> - **Young's Modulus of pure Ti:** **E_Ti = 116 GPa**
> - **Young's Modulus of pure Cu:** **E_Cu = 110 GPa**
> - **Young's Modulus of pure Ni:** **E_Ni = 200 GPa**
> - **Thermal Conductivity of pure Al:** **k_Al = 237 W/(m·K)**
> - **Thermal Conductivity of pure Cu:** **k_Cu = 401 W/(m·K)**
> - **Coefficient of Thermal Expansion of pure Al:** **α_Al = 23 × 10⁻⁶ /°C**
> - **Coefficient of Thermal Expansion of pure Ti:** **α_Ti = 8.6 × 10⁻⁶ /°C**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# MODULE 3 | SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Hierarchical Classification of Metal Matrices

For systematic KTU examination preparation, the metallic matrices used in MMC fabrication are classified into **three engineering categories** based on operating temperature and performance:

### Category A: Low-Density / Low-Temperature Matrices (T < 450 °C)
- **Aluminum (Al) and its alloys** — the **most widely used MMC matrix** worldwide
- **Magnesium (Mg) and its alloys** — the **lightest structural metal matrix**
- **Lead (Pb) alloys** — niche applications in battery grids and radiation shielding

### Category B: Medium-Temperature Matrices (450 °C < T < 800 °C)
- **Titanium (Ti) and its alloys** — aerospace-grade high-performance matrices
- **Copper (Cu) and its alloys** — thermal management and electrical applications

### Category C: High-Temperature Matrices (T > 800 °C)
- **Nickel (Ni) superalloys** — gas turbine engine hot-section components
- **Cobalt (Co) superalloys** — wear-resistant high-temperature parts
- **Intermetallics (NiAl, TiAl, FeAl)** — emerging ultra-high-temperature matrices

## 2.2 Property-by-Property Deep Analysis

### 2.2.1 Aluminum (Al) as a Matrix Material

**Properties:**
- **Density (ρ_Al):** **2.70 g/cm³** — one-third that of steel, ideal for weight-critical applications
- **Melting Point (T_m):** **660 °C** — sufficient for most consumer and automotive applications
- **Tensile Strength:** **70–110 MPa** (pure) and **200–600 MPa** (alloyed)
- **Young's Modulus (E_Al):** **70 GPa** — moderate; tripled by Al₂O₃ or SiC reinforcement
- **Thermal Conductivity (k_Al):** **237 W/(m·K)** — excellent heat dissipation
- **Electrical Resistivity:** **2.65 × 10⁻⁸ Ω·m** — useful for electrically conductive MMCs
- **Ductility:** **Elongation of 12–25%** in pure form; enables net-shape forming

**Characteristics (Engineering Behavior):**
- Excellent **wettability** with oxide ceramics (Al₂O₃) due to chemical affinity
- Reacts with SiC above **700 °C** to form Al₄C₃, degrading the interface
- Forms a stable native oxide (Al₂O₃) layer providing **self-passivation** and corrosion resistance
- **Coefficient of thermal expansion (23 × 10⁻⁶ /°C)** closely matches many ceramics
- Excellent **machinability, castability, and formability** for net-shape manufacturing

**Why aluminum dominates the MMC market:**
- Lowest cost among structural metals
- Largest infrastructure for casting, extrusion, forging
- Compatible with stir casting, squeeze casting, powder metallurgy, and in-situ processing
- Non-toxic and recyclable

**Applications:**
- **Automotive:** brake rotors, drum brakes, pistons, cylinder liners, connecting rods, drive shafts
- **Aerospace:** helicopter blade sleeves, structural tubes, missile fins
- **Electronics:** heat sinks, microwave packaging, thermally conductive housings
- **Recreation:** bicycle frames, golf club shafts

### 2.2.2 Magnesium (Mg) as a Matrix Material

**Properties:**
- **Density (ρ_Mg):** **1.74 g/cm³** — the **lightest structural metal**, 35% lighter than Al
- **Young's Modulus (E_Mg):** **45 GPa**
- **Melting Point (T_m):** **650 °C**
- **Thermal Conductivity (k_Mg):** **156 W/(m·K)**
- **Coefficient of Thermal Expansion:** **26 × 10⁻⁶ /°C**

**Characteristics:**
- Extreme **specific stiffness** (E/ρ) — highest among common structural metals
- Highly **reactive** in molten state — must be protected by SF₆/CO₂ cover gas or melt flux
- Limited **wettability** with most reinforcements — needs surface coatings or alloying additions
- **Poor corrosion resistance** in chloride environments — needs surface treatment
- Hexagonal close-packed (HCP) crystal structure limits room-temperature ductility

**Applications:**
- **Aerospace:** satellite structures, helicopter gearboxes, missile components
- **Automotive:** steering wheels, seat frames, instrument panels, gearbox housings
- **Electronics:** laptop casings, mobile phone bodies, camera bodies
- **Defense:** lightweight ammunition components

### 2.2.3 Titanium (Ti) as a Matrix Material

**Properties:**
- **Density (ρ_Ti):** **4.51 g/cm³** — half that of Ni-based superalloys
- **Young's Modulus (E_Ti):** **116 GPa**
- **Melting Point (T_m):** **1668 °C**
- **Tensile Strength:** **240–550 MPa** (pure) and **900–1200 MPa** (Ti-6Al-4V)
- **Coefficient of Thermal Expansion:** **8.6 × 10⁻⁶ /°C** — closely matches SiC and many fibers

**Characteristics:**
- Exceptional **specific strength** (σ/ρ) — outstanding up to 500 °C
- **Outstanding corrosion resistance** due to passive TiO₂ layer
- **Reactivity at processing temperatures** is a major challenge — reacts with most ceramic reinforcements
- **Low CTE mismatch** with SiC fibers (α_SiC = 4.0 × 10⁻⁶ /°C) — reduces thermal residual stresses
- **Expensive** — matrix cost ~10–20× that of aluminum
- **Difficult to machine** — strong tool wear, requires specialized processing

**Applications:**
- **Aerospace jet engines:** compressor blades, discs, blisks (bladed discs)
- **Aerospace structures:** Ti/SiC fan blades, exit flaps, structural rings
- **Biomedical implants:** Ti/hydroxyapatite composites for bone replacement
- **Marine:** submarine components, propellers

### 2.2.4 Copper (Cu) as a Matrix Material

**Properties:**
- **Density (ρ_Cu):** **8.96 g/cm³**
- **Young's Modulus (E_Cu):** **110 GPa**
- **Melting Point (T_m):** **1085 °C**
- **Thermal Conductivity (k_Cu):** **401 W/(m·K)** — second only to silver among metals
- **Electrical Resistivity:** **1.68 × 10⁻⁸ Ω·m** — best of common industrial metals

**Characteristics:**
- Excellent **thermal/electrical conductivity preservation** when reinforced with graphite or carbon fibers
- Reacts with graphite to form brittle Cu carbides; requires barrier coatings
- **Good wettability** with oxide ceramics
- **Soft and ductile** — provides high formability for complex shapes

**Applications:**
- **Electrical contacts** (Cu/graphite)
- **Heat sinks** for power electronics (Cu/Al₂O₃)
- **Welding electrodes** (Cu/W, Cu/Mo)
- **High-strength conductors**

### 2.2.5 Nickel (Ni) and Cobalt (Co) Superalloys as Matrices

**Properties:**
- **Density (ρ_Ni):** **8.90 g/cm³**
- **Young's Modulus (E_Ni):** **200 GPa**
- **Melting Point (T_m):** **1455 °C**
- **Strength retention:** up to **80% at 1000 °C** in precipitation-hardened forms

**Characteristics:**
- Only matrix class suitable for **continuous service above 1000 °C**
- Excellent **oxidation and creep resistance**
- Can be reinforced with **W fibers, Mo fibers, or SiC** for advanced turbine materials
- **Heavy** — restricts use to applications where temperature is the binding constraint
- **Expensive and difficult to process** — often requires hot isostatic pressing (HIP) or powder metallurgy

**Applications:**
- **Gas turbine hot-section** vanes and blades (NiAl/TiB₂, NiAl/Al₂O₃)
- **Rocket nozzle liners**
- **High-temperature heat exchangers**
- **Nuclear reactor components**

## 2.3 Critical Characteristics Common to All Metal Matrices

For KTU 2024 examination, students must remember these **seven cross-cutting characteristics** that govern metal-matrix behavior:

1. **Ductility and Toughness:** Metal matrices provide plastic deformability; they arrest cracks and provide damage tolerance.
2. **Thermal and Electrical Conductivity:** Metals conduct heat and electricity; reinforcement typically reduces conductivity in proportion to volume fraction.
3. **High-Temperature Capability:** The dominant reason for choosing MMCs over PMCs.
4. **Interface Reactivity:** Molten metals are reactive; interface reactions can be beneficial (improved bonding) or detrimental (brittle intermetallic formation).
5. **Wettability:** The molten metal must wet the reinforcement surface for good bonding; this is enhanced by surface coatings or alloying additions.
6. **Density and Weight:** Matrix density strongly influences composite density; rule-of-mixtures applies.
7. **Fabrication Compatibility:** The matrix dictates the processing route — liquid-state (casting, infiltration), solid-state (powder metallurgy, diffusion bonding), or in-situ.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL KTU 2024 FORMULAS** — Every formula below has appeared in KTU board examinations on metal-matrix composites.

| # | Property | Rule of Mixtures Formula | Boundary Conditions |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---|
| 1 | **Density** | $\rho_c = \rho_m V_m + \rho_r V_r$ | Valid for all MMC geometries |
| 2 | **Young's Modulus (longitudinal, continuous fiber aligned with load)** | $E_{c,L} = E_m V_m + E_r V_r$ | Isostrain; upper bound (Voigt) |
| 3 | **Young's Modulus (transverse, continuous fiber perpendicular to load)** | $\frac{1}{E_{c,T}} = \frac{V_m}{E_m} + \frac{V_r}{E_r}$ | Isostress; lower bound (Reuss) |
| 4 | **Young's Modulus (particulate MMC, Halpin–Tsai)** | $E_c = E_m \cdot \frac{1 + 2s \cdot q \cdot V_r}{1 - q \cdot V_r}$ where $q = \frac{E_r/E_m - 1}{E_r/E_m + 2s}$ | $s$ = aspect ratio; for spheres $s = 1$ |
| 5 | **Tensile Strength (particulate MMC)** | $\sigma_c = \sigma_m V_m + \sigma_r V_r$ | Upper bound estimate |
| 6 | **Coefficient of Thermal Expansion (Turner model)** | $\alpha_c = \frac{\alpha_m V_m K_m + \alpha_r V_r K_r}{V_m K_m + V_r K_r}$ | $K$ = bulk modulus; valid for isotropic thermal strain |
| 7 | **Coefficient of Thermal Expansion (Schapery model, upper bound)** | $\alpha_c = \alpha_m V_m + \alpha_r V_r$ | Assumes no internal stresses |
| 8 | **Thermal Conductivity** | $k_c = k_m V_m + k_r V_r$ | Parallel heat flow |
| 9 | **Thermal Conductivity (transverse)** | $\frac{1}{k_c} = \frac{V_m}{k_m} + \frac{V_r}{k_r}$ | Series heat flow |
| 10 | **Volume Fraction Constraint** | $V_m + V_r = 1$ | Universal — applies in all MMC calculations |
| 11 | **Mass Fraction to Volume Fraction** | $V_m = \frac{\rho_c / \rho_m}{\rho_c / \rho_m + \rho_c / \rho_r} \cdot \frac{1}{\rho_m}$ from $W_m = \frac{\rho_m V_m}{\rho_c}$ | Used in fabrication stoichiometry |
| 12 | **Specific Strength** | $SS_c = \sigma_c / \rho_c$ | Critical for aerospace selection |
| 13 | **Specific Stiffness** | $SM_c = E_c / \rho_c$ | Critical for stiffness-critical structures |
| 14 | **Rule of Mixtures for Heat Capacity** | $C_{p,c} = C_{p,m} V_m + C_{p,r} V_r$ | For thermal mass calculations |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examination Tip:** When using the **Voigt (isostrain)** and **Reuss (isostress)** models, always state explicitly which loading condition is being analyzed. Examiners award partial marks only if the model is correctly identified.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The rule-of-mixtures framework is the **first design calculation** an engineer performs when selecting an MMC for an aerospace or automotive component. For example, when designing a **SiC-particle-reinforced Al brake rotor**, the engineer first estimates the density, modulus, and CTE of the candidate material using these formulas, then compares with performance targets (e.g., < 2.9 g/cm³, > 100 GPa, < 18 × 10⁻⁶ /°C) before committing to expensive laboratory testing. This rapid pre-screening saves **weeks of trial fabrication** and **lakhs of rupees in testing costs** for every material iteration.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# MODULE 3 | SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Longitudinal Modulus (Voigt Model) for Al/SiC Continuous-Fiber MMC

**Problem Setup:** An Al matrix is reinforced with 40% volume fraction of continuous SiC fibers aligned along the loading axis. The composite is loaded in tension along the fiber direction. Calculate the longitudinal Young's modulus.

**Given Data (Standard KTU Reference Values):**
- E_Al = 70 GPa
- E_SiC = 410 GPa
- V_Al = 0.60
- V_SiC = 0.40

**Step 1 — State the Isostrain Assumption**

When the fiber and matrix are perfectly bonded and the load is applied parallel to the fiber direction, both phases must strain equally (iso-strain condition):

$$\varepsilon_c = \varepsilon_m = \varepsilon_r = \varepsilon$$

**Step 2 — Apply Load Partitioning**

The total load on the composite cross-section is the sum of the loads carried by the matrix and the reinforcement. Since load = stress × area:

$$P_c = P_m + P_r$$

**Step 3 — Express Load in Terms of Stress and Area**

Substituting $P = \sigma \cdot A$:

$$\sigma_c A_c = \sigma_m A_m + \sigma_r A_r$$

**Step 4 — Normalize by Total Area**

Dividing both sides by $A_c$ and using the volume fraction relation $V_i = A_i / A_c$ (valid for fibers of uniform length):

$$\sigma_c = \sigma_m V_m + \sigma_r V_r$$

**Step 5 — Apply Hooke's Law under Isostrain**

Under isostrain, $\sigma = E\varepsilon$, so:

$$\sigma_c = E_m \varepsilon V_m + E_r \varepsilon V_r = \varepsilon (E_m V_m + E_r V_r)$$

**Step 6 — Divide by Common Strain**

$$E_c = \frac{\sigma_c}{\varepsilon} = E_m V_m + E_r V_r$$

This is the **Voigt equation** — the upper bound of modulus for an MMC.

**Step 7 — Substitute Numerical Values**

$$E_{c,L} = (70)(0.60) + (410)(0.40)$$

$$E_{c,L} = 42 + 164 = 206 \text{ GPa}$$

**Step 8 — Physical Interpretation**

The composite modulus of **206 GPa** is nearly **3× the unreinforced Al** (70 GPa) and approaches 50% of monolithic SiC (410 GPa). This is a hallmark of effective MMC design.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner's Trick:** The examiner will sometimes reverse the volume fractions (V_f = 0.60, V_m = 0.40). Always re-read the question stem carefully. Marks are deducted for swapped values.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Transverse Modulus (Reuss Model) for Al/SiC MMC

**Problem Setup:** Same composite (40 vol% SiC in Al), but now the load is applied perpendicular to the fiber axis. Calculate the transverse modulus.

**Given Data (same as Derivation 1):**
- E_Al = 70 GPa
- E_SiC = 410 GPa
- V_Al = 0.60
- V_SiC = 0.40

**Step 1 — State the Isostress Assumption**

Under transverse loading, the matrix and reinforcement experience equal stress (iso-stress condition):

$$\sigma_c = \sigma_m = \sigma_r = \sigma$$

**Step 2 — Apply Strain Additivity**

The total displacement is the sum of displacements in the matrix and reinforcement:

$$\Delta L_c = \Delta L_m + \Delta L_r$$

**Step 3 — Express in Terms of Strain and Length**

Using $\Delta L = \varepsilon \cdot L$, with constant $L$:

$$\varepsilon_c L = \varepsilon_m L_m + \varepsilon_r L_r$$

**Step 4 — Normalize by Total Length**

$$\varepsilon_c = \varepsilon_m \cdot \frac{L_m}{L} + \varepsilon_r \cdot \frac{L_r}{L} = \varepsilon_m V_m + \varepsilon_r V_r$$

**Step 5 — Apply Hooke's Law under Isostress**

Under isostress, $\varepsilon = \sigma / E$:

$$\frac{\sigma}{E_c} = \frac{\sigma}{E_m} V_m + \frac{\sigma}{E_r} V_r$$

**Step 6 — Cancel σ and Invert**

$$\frac{1}{E_c} = \frac{V_m}{E_m} + \frac{V_r}{E_r}$$

This is the **Reuss equation** — the lower bound of modulus.

**Step 7 — Substitute Numerical Values**

$$\frac{1}{E_{c,T}} = \frac{0.60}{70} + \frac{0.40}{410}$$

$$\frac{1}{E_{c,T}} = 0.008571 + 0.000976 = 0.009547 \text{ GPa}^{-1}$$

**Step 8 — Invert the Result**

$$E_{c,T} = \frac{1}{0.009547} = 104.7 \text{ GPa}$$

**Step 9 — Physical Interpretation**

The transverse modulus (**104.7 GPa**) is far lower than the longitudinal modulus (206 GPa) and only ~50% higher than pure Al. This is the **anisotropy of fiber-reinforced MMCs** — a critical design consideration. Components loaded perpendicular to fibers gain little modulus benefit.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key KTU Insight:** The ratio $E_{c,L} / E_{c,T} = 206 / 104.7 \approx 1.97$ quantifies the directional stiffness. The closer this ratio is to 1, the more isotropic the composite (favoring particulates, whiskers, or 3D woven reinforcements).

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Composite Density of Hybrid Al/SiC MMC

**Problem Setup:** Calculate the density of an Al matrix MMC containing 30 vol% SiC particles.

**Given Data:**
- ρ_Al = 2.70 g/cm³
- ρ_SiC = 3.21 g/cm³
- V_Al = 0.70
- V_SiC = 0.30

**Step 1 — Apply the Rule of Mixtures for Density**

$$\rho_c = \rho_m V_m + \rho_r V_r$$

**Step 2 — Substitute Values**

$$\rho_c = (2.70)(0.70) + (3.21)(0.30)$$

$$\rho_c = 1.890 + 0.963 = 2.853 \text{ g/cm}^3$$

**Step 3 — Verify and Interpret**

The composite density of **2.853 g/cm³** is only 5.7% higher than pure Al, confirming that adding low-volume-fraction ceramic reinforcement to a light metal yields significant property gains with minimal weight penalty.

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Thermal Expansion Coefficient (Turner Model) for Al/SiC MMC

**Problem Setup:** Calculate the CTE of an Al/SiC MMC with 35 vol% SiC using the Turner model.

**Given Data:**
- α_Al = 23 × 10⁻⁶ /°C
- α_SiC = 4.0 × 10⁻⁶ /°C
- V_Al = 0.65
- V_SiC = 0.35
- K_Al = 76 GPa (bulk modulus of Al)
- K_SiC = 220 GPa (bulk modulus of SiC)

**Step 1 — State the Turner Assumption**

The Turner model assumes hydrostatic stresses in the matrix and reinforcement under uniform temperature change. The CTE is given by:

$$\alpha_c = \frac{\alpha_m V_m K_m + \alpha_r V_r K_r}{V_m K_m + V_r K_r}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute Values**

$$\alpha_c = \frac{(23 \times 10^{-6})(0.65)(76) + (4.0 \times 10^{-6})(0.35)(220)}{(0.65)(76) + (0.35)(220)}$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate Numerator**

$$\text{Numerator} = (23 \times 10^{-6})(49.4) + (4.0 \times 10^{-6})(77.0)$$

$$\text{Numerator} = 1136.2 \times 10^{-6} + 308.0 \times 10^{-6} = 1444.2 \times 10^{-6}$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate Denominator**

$$\text{Denominator} = 49.4 + 77.0 = 126.4$$

**Step 5 — Compute CTE**

$$\alpha_c = \frac{1444.2 \times 10^{-6}}{126.4} = 11.43 \times 10^{-6} \text{ /°C}$$

**Step 6 — Interpret the Result**

The composite CTE of **11.43 × 10⁻⁶ /°C** is **half that of pure Al** (23 × 10⁻⁶ /°C) and approaches that of common semiconductor materials. This is the key reason Al/SiC MMCs are used in **electronic packaging** — to match the CTE of silicon chips (2.6 × 10⁻⁶ /°C) more closely than pure Al.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Comparative Property Table for All Major Metal Matrices

This is the **single most important comparative table** for KTU 2024 Module 3 — examiners frequently test it.

| Property | Al (Pure) | Mg (Pure) | Ti (Pure) | Cu (Pure) | Ni (Pure) |
|:---|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| **Density (g/cm³)** | 2.70 | 1.74 | 4.51 | 8.96 | 8.90 |
| **Young's Modulus (GPa)** | 70 | 45 | 116 | 110 | 200 |
| **Specific Modulus E/ρ (GPa·cm³/g)** | 25.9 | 25.9 | 25.7 | 12.3 | 22.5 |
| **Melting Point (°C)** | 660 | 650 | 1668 | 1085 | 1455 |
| **Thermal Conductivity (W/m·K)** | 237 | 156 | 22 | 401 | 91 |
| **CTE (10⁻⁶ /°C)** | 23 | 26 | 8.6 | 17 | 13 |
| **Tensile Strength (MPa, annealed)** | 90 | 130 | 240 | 220 | 320 |
| **Cost (USD/kg, approx)** | 2 | 3 | 35 | 8 | 25 |
| **Primary MMC Reinforcement** | SiC, Al₂O₃ | SiC, B | SiC, TiB₂ | SiC, C | W, SiC, Al₂O₃ |
| **Dominant Application Sector** | Automotive | Aerospace, Electronics | Aerospace | Thermal, Electrical | Turbine, Rocket |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Insight from the Table:** Al and Mg have the **same specific modulus (25.9 GPa·cm³/g)**, but Mg has a much higher CTE and is more reactive. This is why Al dominates MMC production despite Mg being lighter. The specific modulus, not absolute modulus, is the **correct metric for weight-critical design**.

## 3.6 Worked Numerical Example: Al/SiC Particulate MMC (Full Mark Solution)

**Question:** A 20 vol% SiC particulate MMC is made in an Al matrix. Calculate: (i) composite density, (ii) composite modulus (use Halpin–Tsai with $s = 1$ for spherical particles), and (iii) specific modulus. Given: E_Al = 70 GPa, E_SiC = 410 GPa, ρ_Al = 2.70 g/cm³, ρ_SiC = 3.21 g/cm³.

**Solution:**

**Part (i) — Density:**

$$\rho_c = (2.70)(0.80) + (3.21)(0.20) = 2.160 + 0.642 = 2.802 \text{ g/cm}^3$$

**Part (ii) — Modulus (Halpin–Tsai):**

First, compute the parameter $q$ with $s = 1$:

$$q = \frac{E_r / E_m - 1}{E_r / E_m + 2s} = \frac{410/70 - 1}{410/70 + 2} = \frac{5.857 - 1}{5.857 + 2} = \frac{4.857}{7.857} = 0.618$$

Then apply the Halpin–Tsai equation:

$$E_c = E_m \cdot \frac{1 + 2s \cdot q \cdot V_r}{1 - q \cdot V_r} = 70 \cdot \frac{1 + 2(1)(0.618)(0.20)}{1 - (0.618)(0.20)}$$

$$E_c = 70 \cdot \frac{1 + 0.2472}{1 - 0.1236} = 70 \cdot \frac{1.2472}{0.8764} = 70 \cdot 1.423 = 99.6 \text{ GPa}$$

**Part (iii) — Specific Modulus:**

$$\text{SM}_c = \frac{E_c}{\rho_c} = \frac{99.6}{2.802} = 35.55 \text{ GPa·cm}^3/\text{g}$$

**Comparison with unreinforced Al:**

- Pure Al: 70/2.70 = 25.93 GPa·cm³/g
- Al/20%SiC MMC: 35.55 GPa·cm³/g
- **Improvement: 37% in specific modulus**

This single numerical example demonstrates that even modest reinforcement volume fractions yield substantial property enhancement in MMCs.


**Diagram Reading Guide:** This flowchart visualizes the KTU-standard decision logic for selecting a metal matrix based on the application's operating temperature regime, then connecting each matrix to its typical reinforcement combinations, and finally to the engineering end-use. Students should reproduce a simplified version of this logic tree in exam answers when asked "Explain the selection of metal matrices for different applications."

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: MMC Fabrication Routes by Matrix Type

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    F0[Fabrication Route Selection] --> F1{Liquid State Process}
    F0 --> F2{Solid State Process}
    F0 --> F3{In-Situ Process}
    F0 --> F4{Deposition Process}
    F1 --> F1a[Stir Casting]
    F1 --> F1b[Squeeze Casting]
    F1 --> F1c[Pressure Infiltration]

    F1c --> AM2[Used for Al SiC with high Vf]

```

**Diagram Reading Guide:** The fabrication route is dictated primarily by the matrix melting point and chemical reactivity.
