# MODULE 3 | SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 PART A: 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions (Direct Conceptual Recall)

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Q: List any three important properties of aluminum that make it the most widely used metal matrix in composite materials.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — one mark per valid point):**

**Point 1 (1 mark):** **Low density (2.70 g/cm³)** combined with reasonable Young's modulus (70 GPa) gives aluminum a high specific stiffness, making it suitable for weight-critical applications such as aerospace and automotive components.

**Point 2 (1 mark):** **Low melting point (660 °C)** allows processing by economical liquid-state methods such as stir casting, squeeze casting, and melt infiltration, reducing manufacturing cost significantly compared to titanium or nickel.

**Point 3 (1 mark):** **Excellent wettability with oxide ceramics** (especially Al₂O₃), good corrosion resistance due to a passive oxide film, and high ductility (12–25% elongation) allow it to form strong, tough interfaces with reinforcements like SiC and Al₂O₃ in aluminum matrix composites.

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**Q: Why is titanium preferred as a matrix material in aerospace applications even though it is more expensive than aluminum?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Reason 1 (1 mark):** **Superior specific strength** — titanium has a tensile strength of 240–550 MPa (pure) and 900–1200 MPa (Ti-6Al-4V alloy) at densities of 4.51 g/cm³, giving a specific strength roughly **3× that of aluminum** alloys, critical for jet engine components.

**Reason 2 (1 mark):** **High-temperature capability** — titanium retains significant strength up to ~500 °C, whereas aluminum alloys rapidly soften above 300 °C. This is essential for compressor blades and discs in jet engines.

**Reason 3 (1 mark):** **Low coefficient of thermal expansion (8.6 × 10⁻⁶ /°C)** closely matches that of SiC fibers (4.0 × 10⁻⁶ /°C), minimizing thermal residual stresses in Ti/SiC composites used in aerospace structures such as fan blades and exit flaps.

## 5.2 PART B: 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice — Two Alternatives)

### QUESTION A (14 MARKS) [KTU University Exam - Model Question]

**Q: (a)** Discuss in detail the **properties, characteristics, and applications of aluminum and magnesium as metal matrix materials** in composite systems. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A metal matrix composite consists of **40 vol% SiC particles in an aluminum matrix**. Calculate the **(i) composite density**, **(ii) composite Young's modulus** using the Halpin–Tsai equation for spherical particles, and **(iii) the specific modulus** of the composite. Given: ρ_Al = 2.70 g/cm³, ρ_SiC = 3.21 g/cm³, E_Al = 70 GPa, E_SiC = 410 GPa. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution for Question A**

#### Part (a) — Properties, Characteristics, and Applications of Al and Mg Matrices

**1. Aluminum (Al) as a Matrix Material**

**Properties (2 marks):**
- **Density:** 2.70 g/cm³ (one-third of steel)
- **Young's Modulus:** 70 GPa (pure); 200–600 MPa tensile strength
- **Melting Point:** 660 °C
- **Thermal Conductivity:** 237 W/(m·K)
- **CTE:** 23 × 10⁻⁶ /°C
- **Ductility:** 12–25% elongation (pure)
- **Electrical Resistivity:** 2.65 × 10⁻⁸ Ω·m

**Characteristics (1 mark):**
- Excellent wettability with oxide ceramic reinforcements (Al₂O₃)
- Good corrosion resistance due to passive Al₂O₃ layer
- Reacts with SiC above 700 °C forming brittle Al₄C₃
- Compatible with stir casting, squeeze casting, powder metallurgy
- Self-passivating and recyclable

**Applications (1 mark):**
- Automotive: brake rotors, pistons, cylinder liners
- Aerospace: helicopter rotor blade sleeves, missile fins
- Electronics: heat sinks, microwave packaging
- Recreation: bicycle frames, golf club shafts

**2. Magnesium (Mg) as a Matrix Material**

**Properties (1 mark):**
- **Density:** 1.74 g/cm³ (lightest structural metal)
- **Young's Modulus:** 45 GPa
- **Melting Point:** 650 °C
- **Thermal Conductivity:** 156 W/(m·K)
- **CTE:** 26 × 10⁻⁶ /°C
- **Specific Stiffness:** Equal to aluminum (25.9 GPa·cm³/g)

**Characteristics (1 mark):**
- Extreme specific stiffness — highest among common structural metals
- High chemical reactivity in molten state — requires protective atmosphere
- Limited wettability with reinforcements — needs surface coatings
- Hexagonal close-packed structure limits room-temperature ductility
- Poor corrosion resistance in chloride environments

**Applications (1 mark):**
- Aerospace: satellite structures, helicopter gearboxes
- Automotive: steering wheels, seat frames, gearbox housings
- Electronics: laptop casings, mobile phone bodies
- Defense: lightweight ammunition components

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical Computation

**Given:**
- V_SiC = 0.40, V_Al = 0.60
- ρ_Al = 2.70 g/cm³, ρ_SiC = 3.21 g/cm³
- E_Al = 70 GPa, E_SiC = 410 GPa, s = 1 (spherical particles)

**Step 1 — Density (1 mark):**

$$\rho_c = \rho_{Al} V_{Al} + \rho_{SiC} V_{SiC} = (2.70)(0.60) + (3.21)(0.40)$$

$$\rho_c = 1.620 + 1.284 = 2.904 \text{ g/cm}^3$$

**Step 2 — Halpin–Tsai Modulus (4 marks):**

Compute the parameter $q$ for $s = 1$:

$$q = \frac{E_{SiC}/E_{Al} - 1}{E_{SiC}/E_{Al} + 2s} = \frac{410/70 - 1}{410/70 + 2} = \frac{5.857 - 1}{5.857 + 2} = \frac{4.857}{7.857} = 0.618$$

Apply Halpin–Tsai equation:

$$E_c = E_{Al} \cdot \frac{1 + 2s \cdot q \cdot V_{SiC}}{1 - q \cdot V_{SiC}}$$

$$E_c = 70 \cdot \frac{1 + 2(1)(0.618)(0.40)}{1 - (0.618)(0.40)}$$

$$E_c = 70 \cdot \frac{1 + 0.4944}{1 - 0.2472} = 70 \cdot \frac{1.4944}{0.7528}$$

$$E_c = 70 \cdot 1.985 = 138.96 \approx 139.0 \text{ GPa}$$

**Step 3 — Specific Modulus (2 marks):**

$$\text{SM}_c = \frac{E_c}{\rho_c} = \frac{139.0}{2.904} = 47.86 \text{ GPa·cm}^3/\text{g}$$

**Interpretation:** The specific modulus of the composite (47.86) is **85% higher than pure Al** (25.93), confirming the high efficiency of SiC reinforcement for stiffness-critical applications.

---

### QUESTION B (14 MARKS) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam - Model Question]

**Q: (a)** Compare the **properties and applications of titanium, copper, and nickel as matrix materials** in metal matrix composites. Highlight the temperature regimes for which each is most suitable. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A continuous-fiber Ti/SiC MMC has **30 vol% SiC fibers aligned along the loading axis**. Calculate the **(i) longitudinal modulus** and **(ii) transverse modulus** of the composite. Comment on the anisotropy. Given: E_Ti = 116 GPa, E_SiC = 410 GPa. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution for Question B**

#### Part (a) — Comparative Analysis of Ti, Cu, and Ni Matrices

**Comparison Table (4 marks):**

| Property / Matrix | Titanium (Ti) | Copper (Cu) | Nickel (Ni) |
|:---|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| **Density (g/cm³)** | 4.51 | 8.96 | 8.90 |
| **Young's Modulus (GPa)** | 116 | 110 | 200 |
| **Melting Point (°C)** | 1668 | 1085 | 1455 |
| **Thermal Conductivity (W/m·K)** | 22 | 401 | 91 |
| **CTE (10⁻⁶ /°C)** | 8.6 | 17 | 13 |
| **Maximum Service T (°C)** | ~500 | ~600 | >1000 |
| **Cost** | High | Medium | High |
| **Primary Reinforcement** | SiC fibers | C, Al₂O₃ | W, SiC |
| **Dominant Application** | Jet engines, biomedical | Electrical contacts, heat sinks | Turbine blades, rocket nozzles |

**Temperature Regime Discussion (3 marks):**

**Titanium (T < 500 °C aerospace regime):** Titanium matrices are optimal for **medium-temperature aerospace applications** such as jet engine compressor blades and discs, where their **low CTE (8.6 × 10⁻⁶ /°C)** matches SiC fibers and minimizes thermal stress. The high specific strength and corrosion resistance make Ti/SiC the leading MMC for advanced fan blade systems.

**Copper (T < 600 °C thermal/electrical regime):** Copper matrices are unmatched for **thermal management and electrical conduction applications**. With the highest thermal conductivity (401 W/m·K) of any metal except silver, Cu/SiC and Cu/graphite composites are used as **heat sinks in power electronics** and as **high-strength electrical contacts**. Its role is to **preserve the conductivity** of the matrix while improving strength and reducing thermal expansion.

**Nickel (T > 800 °C high-temperature regime):** Nickel superalloys are the **only commercially viable matrices for sustained operation above 1000 °C**. Reinforced with tungsten fibers or SiC, they form components for **gas turbine hot-section vanes, rocket nozzle liners, and heat exchangers**. The high modulus (200 GPa), high melting point (1455 °C), and oxidation resistance justify their cost in extreme environments.

---

#### Part (b) — Longitudinal and Transverse Modulus Calculations

**Given:** V_Ti = 0.70, V_SiC = 0.30, E_Ti = 116 GPa, E_SiC = 410 GPa

**Step 1 — Longitudinal Modulus (Voigt) (3 marks):**

$$E_{c,L} = E_{Ti} V_{Ti} + E_{SiC} V_{SiC} = (116)(0.70) + (410)(0.30)$$

$$E_{c,L} = 81.2 + 123.0 = 204.2 \text{ GPa}$$

**Step 2 — Transverse Modulus (Reuss) (3 marks):**

$$\frac{1}{E_{c,T}} = \frac{V_{Ti}}{E_{Ti}} + \frac{V_{SiC}}{E_{SiC}} = \frac{0.70}{116} + \frac{0.30}{410}$$

$$\frac{1}{E_{c,T}} = 0.006034 + 0.000732 = 0.006766 \text{ GPa}^{-1}$$

$$E_{c,T} = \frac{1}{0.006766} = 147.8 \text{ GPa}$$

**Step 3 — Anisotropy Comment (1 mark):**

The **anisotropy ratio** is:

$$\frac{E_{c,L}}{E_{c,T}} = \frac{204.2}{147.8} = 1.38$$

This 38% stiffness anisotropy is typical of unidirectional continuous-fiber MMCs. **Design implication:** A Ti/SiC compressor blade loaded along the fiber direction gains a 76% modulus increase over monolithic Ti (204.2 vs 116 GPa), but a blade loaded transversely would gain only 27%. This is why fiber orientation must be **specified and controlled** during composite lay-up — a critical design constraint absent in monolithic metals.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Commonly Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing Voigt and Reuss models:** Students frequently apply the longitudinal (isostrain) formula when the load is transverse, losing 3–4 marks. Always identify the loading direction **first**.
> 2. **Forgetting to invert** the Reuss formula: The transverse modulus is computed as $1/E_c$, not $E_c$ directly. A common error is to sum $(V_m/E_m) + (V_r/E_r)$ and call it the modulus — wrong.
> 3. **Ignoring the $s$ parameter in Halpin–Tsai:** For non-spherical reinforcements, $s$ is the aspect ratio. Setting $s = 1$ for whiskers or short fibers is incorrect.
> 4. **Mixing mass fraction and volume fraction:** All rule-of-mixtures equations in MMC design use **volume fraction**, not mass fraction. The exam may give mass fractions to test this knowledge.
> 5. **Not stating the model used:** Examiners award 0.5–1 mark just for naming the model (Voigt/Reuss/Halpin–Tsai) before applying it. Always state: "Using the Voigt (isostrain) model..." in the answer.
> 6. **Forgetting the physical interpretation:** The numerical answer alone is worth only ~50% of the marks. The remaining marks are for explaining what the number means in engineering terms.

## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Re-read before the KTU board exam:**

### Core Properties to Memorize
- **Al:** ρ = 2.70 g/cm³, E = 70 GPa, T_m = 660 °C, k = 237 W/m·K, α = 23 × 10⁻⁶ /°C
- **Mg:** ρ = 1.74 g/cm³, E = 45 GPa, T_m = 650 °C, k = 156 W/m·K, α = 26 × 10⁻⁶ /°C
- **Ti:** ρ = 4.51 g/cm³, E = 116 GPa, T_m = 1668 °C, k = 22 W/m·K, α = 8.6 × 10⁻⁶ /°C
- **Cu:** ρ = 8.96 g/cm³, E = 110 GPa, T_m = 1085 °C, k = 401 W/m·K, α = 17 × 10⁻⁶ /°C
- **Ni:** ρ = 8.90 g/cm³, E = 200 GPa, T_m = 1455 °C, k = 91 W/m·K, α = 13 × 10⁻⁶ /°C

### Critical Application-Matrix Pairings
- **Brake rotors & pistons → Al matrix + SiC/Al₂O₃**
- **Jet engine compressor blades → Ti matrix + SiC fibers**
- **Satellite structures → Mg matrix + SiC particles**
- **Power electronics heat sinks → Cu matrix + Al₂O₃/SiC**
- **Turbine hot vanes → Ni superalloy + W fibers**
- **Biomedical implants → Ti matrix + hydroxyapatite**

### Equations to Memorize (top 5)
- **Density (Rule of Mixtures):** $\rho_c = \rho_m V_m + \rho_r V_r$
- **Longitudinal Modulus (Voigt):** $E_{c,L} = E_m V_m + E_r V_r$
- **Transverse Modulus (Reuss):** $1/E_{c,T} = V_m/E_m + V_r/E_r$
- **Halpin–Tsai Modulus (Particulate MMC):** $E_c = E_m \cdot (1 + 2sqV_r)/(1 - qV_r)$ with $q = (E_r/E_m - 1)/(E_r/E_m + 2s)$
- **Turner CTE Model:** $\alpha_c = (\alpha_m V_m K_m + \alpha_r V_r K_r)/(V_m K_m + V_r K_r)$

### Key Conceptual Takeaways
- **Aluminum** is the most widely used matrix due to its low cost, low density, excellent fabricability, and good wettability with oxide ceramics.
- **Magnesium** is the lightest structural metal matrix, chosen when weight is the absolute priority, but limited by chemical reactivity and poor corrosion resistance.
- **Titanium** dominates medium-to-high-temperature aerospace MMCs because of its high specific strength, low CTE matching SiC, and corrosion resistance.
- **Copper** is the matrix of choice for thermal and electrical conduction MMCs, where it must preserve high conductivity.
- **Nickel superalloys** are the only matrices viable for continuous service above 1000 °C in gas turbines and rocket engines.
- **Specific modulus (E/ρ)** is the correct design metric for weight-critical applications, not absolute modulus.
- **The rule of mixtures** governs density always, modulus in the longitudinal direction, and provides an upper bound for most other properties.
- **Anisotropy in fiber MMCs** is quantified by the ratio $E_{c,L}/E_{c,T}$, which can approach 2.0 in unidirectional composites.
- **Fabrication route** is dictated by matrix melting point: low-melting matrices (Al, Mg) use casting; high-melting matrices (Ti, Ni) use powder metallurgy.
- **Interface reactivity** is a double-edged sword: limited reaction gives poor bonding, excessive reaction gives brittle intermetallic phases — optimal processing requires balancing these.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **FINAL EXAM-DAY REMINDER:** The KTU PEMUT412 board examiner expects you to **(1) state the matrix material and its key property, (2) name the reinforcement, (3) identify the application, and (4) explain the property advantage in one sentence.** Master this 4-step answer pattern and you will score consistently above 80% in this module.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Production techniques: Powder metallurgy
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEMUT412 - COMPOSITE MATERIALS AND CERAMICS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Production techniques: Powder metallurgy ## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Powder Metallurgy (P/M)** is a metal-forming process in which fine, uniformly sized metal powders are compacted under high pressure in a precisely shaped die, and then heated (sintered) in a controlled atmosphere furnace to bond the particles metallurgically into a coherent, near-net-shape engineering component. According to KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus (PEMUT412, Module 3), powder metallurgy encompasses the entire scientific and engineering discipline dealing with: - Production of metal/ceramic/composite powders - Characterization of these powders - Consolidation into bulk engineering shapes - Sintering theory and mechanisms > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Definition to Memorize:** *"Powder metallurgy is an art and science of producing metal powders and utilizing them to manufacture precision components by compaction and sintering, without melting the base material."* ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Snowball to Ice" Metaphor Imagine you are trying to make a solid object from snow: 1. **Powder Production** → Like crushing ice into tiny snowflakes (raw material preparation). 2. **Blending** → Mixing different sized snowflakes to pack tightly. 3. **Compaction** → Pressing snow in your hands to form a snowball (mechanical bonding). 4. **Sintering** → Leaving the snowball in a freezer — the snowflakes fuse at their contact points (without melting) into a strong, cohesive ice ball. Just as you never melted the snow to make the ice ball, **P/M never melts the metal** — it only fuses particles. This is why P/M is classified as a **solid-state fabrication route**. ### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics - **Solid-state Sintering Temperature:** Typically **0.7 to 0.9 × T_m** (where T_m is melting point in Kelvin). - **Compaction Pressure Range:** **150 MPa to 1000 MPa** (uniaxial die compaction). - **Typical Powder Particle Size:** **1 μm to 200 μm**. - **Standard Apparent Density (Hall Flow):** Measured using **ASTM B213** standard. - **Green Density:** Typically **60–80%** of theoretical density. - **Sintered Density:** Typically **85–95%** of theoretical density. > [!NOTE] > **Green Compact** = the compacted but un-sintered part. **Sintered Part** = the final product after furnace treatment. ### 1.4 Why Powder Metallurgy? (Engineering Motivation) | Advantage | Real-World Example | |-----------|-------------------| | Near-net-shape manufacturing | Self-lubricating bronze bearings | | Minimal material wastage (≥ 97% utilization) | Tungsten carbide cutting inserts | | Controlled porosity | Filter elements, oil-impregnated bushings | | Composite material fabrication | W-Cu electrical contacts, diamond tools | | Refractory metal processing | Tungsten filaments, molybdenum parts | | High dimensional accuracy | Gears, sprockets for automobiles | ## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Four Pillars of Powder Metallurgy #### **Pillar 1: Powder Production Techniques** **A. Mechanical Methods** - **Atomization:** A molten metal stream is disintegrated by high-velocity gas (gas atomization), water (water atomization), or centrifugal force (centrifugal atomization). - **Machining / Milling:** Turnings, chips, or coarse particles are reduced by ball milling, attrition milling, or hammer milling. - **Mechanical Alloying (MA):** Repeated welding, fracturing, and re-welding of powder particles in a high-energy ball mill to produce **oxide-dispersion-strengthened (ODS)** superalloys. **B. Chemical Methods** - **Reduction:** Metal oxides (e.g., Fe₂O₃, CuO) are reduced by hydrogen or carbon monoxide. - **Electrolytic Deposition:** Powder is deposited at the cathode from a salt solution. - **Precipitation from Solution:** Chemical reduction from aqueous or organic solutions. - **Thermal Decomposition:** Carbonyl process: **Fe(CO)₅ → Fe + 5CO** at high temperatures. **C. Atomization (Most Industrial Method)** - **Gas Atomization:** Yields spherical, high-purity powders (used for aerospace superalloys). - **Water Atomization:** Yields irregular-shaped powders (excellent for cold compaction). - **Centrifugal Atomization (Rotating Electrode Process, REP):** Produces ultra-pure, spherical powders. #### **Pillar 2: Powder Characterization** | Property | Test Method (ASTM Standard) | |----------|----------------------------| | Particle size distribution | ASTM B214 (Sieve analysis), ASTM B822 (Laser diffraction) | | Apparent density | ASTM B212 (Hall funnel) | | Flow rate | ASTM B213 (Hall flow meter) | | Compressibility | ASTM B331 | | Green strength | ASTM B312 | #### **Pillar 3: Compaction (Forming)** **Uniaxial Die Compaction:** - Powder is loaded into a rigid die. - A punch compresses powder at **150–1000 MPa**. - Friction between powder and die walls causes **density gradient**. **Cold Isostatic Pressing (CIP):** - Powder sealed in a flexible elastomeric mold. - Pressure (typically **100–400 MPa**) applied via hydraulic fluid. - Yields uniform green density (used for large parts). **Hot Isostatic Pressing (HIP):** - Combines high temperature (0.5–0.9 T_m) with isostatic pressure. - Produces near-100% dense parts (used for aerospace turbine discs). **Powder Injection Molding (PIM):** - Powder mixed with thermoplastic binder → feedstock. - Injection molded → debound → sintered. - Used for small, complex shapes. #### **Pillar 4: Sintering Mechanisms** **Three Stages of Solid-State Sintering:** 1. **Initial Stage (Neck Formation):** - Contact points between particles develop into **necks**. - Neck growth driven by surface energy minimization. - No significant densification; porosity remains interconnected. 2. **Intermediate Stage:** - Pores form continuous, cylindrical channels along grain edges. - Grain boundaries form; densification becomes measurable. 3. **Final Stage:** - Pores become isolated, spherical, and located at grain corners. - Significant shrinkage and densification. ### 2.2 Driving Force for Sintering The fundamental thermodynamic driver is the **reduction in total surface free energy (γ)**. $$\Delta G_{\text{sintering}} < 0 \quad \text{(spontaneous process)}$$ ### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | Formula | Meaning / Use | Units | |---------|---------------|-------| | $\gamma_{sv} - \gamma_{gb} = 2\gamma_{ss}\cos(\psi/2)$ | Sintering dihedral angle equilibrium (γ = surface energy) | J/m² | | $\dfrac{x}{r} = \left(\dfrac{3\gamma_{sv} \cdot t}{r \cdot \eta}\right)^{1/3}$ | Neck growth (Frenkel viscous flow model); x = neck radius, r = particle radius | dimensionless ratio | | $\rho_{green} = \dfrac{m_{powder}}{V_{die}}$ | Green density = mass of powder / die cavity volume | g/cm³ | | $\% \text{ Theoretical Density} = \dfrac{\rho_{measured}}{\rho_{theoretical}} \times 100$ | Compares actual to theoretical (X-ray) density | % | | $\sigma_{axial} = \dfrac{F}{A_{punch}}$ | Applied compaction pressure | Pa (MPa) | | $\dot{\varepsilon} = \dfrac{3\gamma_{sv}}{4\eta \cdot r}$ | Strain rate in viscous flow sintering (Frenkel) | s⁻¹ | | $D = D_0 \exp\left(-\dfrac{Q}{RT}\right)$ | Arrhenius diffusion (vacancy, grain boundary, surface diffusion) | m²/s | | $\Delta V/V = 3\Delta L/L$ | Volumetric shrinkage ↔ linear shrinkage | dimensionless | > [!NOTE] > **Critical KTU Concept:** Dihedral angle ψ determines pore geometry. If ψ < 60°, pores are unstable (closed); if ψ > 120°, pores remain open at grain boundaries. ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Automotive:** Sintered gears (powder forged steel) — used in transmission systems. - **Aerospace:** ODS nickel superalloys via **mechanical alloying** for turbine blades. - **Electrical:** W-Cu pseudo-alloy contacts (infiltrated) for high-voltage switches. - **Biomedical:** Porous titanium implants made by P/M for bone ingrowth. - **Diamond Tools:** Metal-bonded diamond grinding wheels. ## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Frenkel's Viscous Flow Neck-Growth Model **Problem Setup:** Two identical spherical particles of radius $r$ in contact. A neck of radius $x$ forms between them, driven by surface energy $\gamma_{sv}$. **Step 1 — Energy Balance:** The driving force for sintering is the reduction in surface area. For two coalescing spheres: $$A_{\text{initial}} = 2 \times (4\pi r^2) = 8\pi r^2$$ $$A_{\text{final}} \approx 8\pi r^2 - 2\pi x^2 + 2\pi x^2 \cdot \left(\dfrac{x}{2r}\right) \approx 8\pi r^2 - \Delta A$$ The driving stress (Laplace pressure) acting at the neck is: $$\sigma = -\dfrac{\gamma_{sv}}{r_{neck}}$$ **Step 2 — Viscous Flow Assumption:** Material flows from the bulk (high-curvature region) toward the neck (low-curvature region) as if it were a viscous fluid of viscosity $\eta$. The volumetric flow rate into the neck (Frenkel, 1945): $$\dot{V} = \dfrac{3\pi x^4}{32\eta} \cdot \dfrac{\gamma_{sv}}{r}$$ **Step 3 — Geometric Relation:** Volume of the neck (approx. as a section of a sphere): $$V_{neck} = \dfrac{\pi x^4}}{2r}$$ **Step 4 — Rate of Neck Growth:** Setting $\dot{V}_{neck} = \dot{V}_{flow}$: $$\dfrac{d}{dt}\left(\dfrac{\pi x^4}}{2r}\right) = \dfrac{3\pi x^4}}{32\eta} \cdot \dfrac{\gamma_{sv}}{r}$$ Wait — let me re-derive carefully using the proper Frenkel result. The well-known Frenkel equation is: $$\left(\dfrac{x}{r}\right)^2 = \dfrac{3\gamma_{sv} \cdot t}{2\eta \cdot r}$$ **Clean Derivation (Final Form):** We model two spheres under surface tension as a viscous liquid. The curvature difference produces a stress that drives flow. $$\sigma_{effective} = \dfrac{\gamma_{sv}}{r}\left(\dfrac{r}{x} - 1\right) \approx \dfrac{\gamma_{sv}}{x}$$ Poisson's equation for a viscous sphere under uniaxial stress gives a strain rate: $$\dot{\varepsilon} = \dfrac{\sigma}{3\eta}$$ Geometrically, the rate of linear neck growth relates to bulk strain: $$\dfrac{d}{dt}\left(\dfrac{x}{r}\right) = \dot{\varepsilon} = \dfrac{\gamma_{sv}}{3\eta x}$$ Separating variables: $$3\eta x \, dx = \gamma_{sv} \, dt$$ Integrating from 0 to t: $$\dfrac{3\eta x^2}}{2} = \gamma_{sv} t$$ $$\boxed{\dfrac{x^2}}{r^2} = \dfrac{2\gamma_{sv} t}{3\eta r}}$$ Or equivalently: $$\dfrac{x}{r} = \left(\dfrac{2\gamma_{sv} t}{3\eta r}\right)^{1/2}$$ **Step 5 — Sanity Check:** - As $t \to 0$: $x/r \to 0$ ✓ (no initial neck). - Larger $\gamma_{sv}$ → faster neck growth ✓. - Larger $\eta$ → slower growth ✓ (higher viscosity resists flow). - Larger $r$ → slower growth ✓ (bigger particles harder to weld). --- ### 3.2 Derivation: Compaction Pressure-Density Relationship (Heckel Equation) **Step 1 — Empirical Observation:** For many metal powders, log(1/(1-D)) is approximately linear in compaction pressure, where D is the fractional density. **Step 2 — The Heckel Equation:** $$\ln\left(\dfrac{1}{1-D}\right) = K \cdot P + A$$ where: - $D$ = fractional green density = $\rho_{green} / \rho_{theoretical}$ - $P$ = applied compaction pressure (MPa) - $K$ = slope (related to powder yield strength) - $A$ = constant related to initial packing density **Step 3 — Physical Interpretation:** $1/K$ is often used as an indicator of powder compressibility: - High $1/K$ → soft, easily compressible powder (e.g., sponge iron). - Low $1/K$ → hard, incompressible powder (e.g., atomized tool steel). **Step 4 — Worked Numerical Example:** A copper powder is compacted at $P_1 = 200$ MPa giving $D_1 = 0.78$, and at $P_2 = 400$ MPa giving $D_2 = 0.86$. Find $K$ and $A$: $$\ln\left(\dfrac{1}{1-0.78}\right) = \ln(4.545) = 1.514$$ $$\ln\left(\dfrac{1}{1-0.86}\right) = \ln(7.143) = 1.966$$ $$K = \dfrac{1.966 - 1.514}{400 - 200} = \dfrac{0.452}{200} = 2.26 \times 10^{-3} \text{ MPa}^{-1}$$ $$A = 1.514 - (2.26 \times 10^{-3})(200) = 1.514 - 0.452 = 1.062$$ $$\boxed{\ln\left(\dfrac{1}{1-D}\right) = (2.26 \times 10^{-3})P + 1.062}$$ --- ### 3.3 Derivation: Sintered Density vs. Sintering Time (Exponential Approach) For isothermal sintering, density typically approaches the theoretical limit asymptotically: $$\rho(t) = \rho_{green} + (\rho_{th} - \rho_{green})\left[1 - \exp(-kT)\right]$$ where $k = k_0 \exp(-Q/RT)$ is the rate constant. For short times ($kt \ll 1$): $$\rho(t) \approx \rho_{green} + (\rho_{th} - \rho_{green}) \cdot k t$$ This linear early-stage behavior is what makes the initial sintering stage experimentally useful for extracting activation energy $Q$. --- ### 3.4 Summary Table — Process Variables vs. Outcomes | Process Variable | Effect on Green Density | Effect on Sintered Density | |-----------------|-------------------------|----------------------------| | ↑ Compaction Pressure | ↑ Linear increase | ↑ Initial density → ↑ sintered density | | ↑ Sintering Temperature | No effect | ↑ Faster densification (Arrhenius) | | ↑ Sintering Time | No effect | ↑ (up to plateau) | | ↑ Particle Size (large) | ↓ Lower packing | ↓ Lower sintered density | | ↑ Powder Angularity | ↑ Better interlock | ↑ Higher green strength | | ↑ Lubricant (e.g., Zn stearate) | ↓ Slightly lower | ↑ Better sintered density (no friction) | ## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block-Level Flow Diagram: Complete Powder Metallurgy Process ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Raw Ore / Melt] --> B[Powder Production] B --> B1[Mechanical Methods
Machining / Milling] B --> B2[Atomization
Gas / Water / Centrifugal] B --> B3[Chemical Methods
Reduction / Electrolytic] B1 --> C[Powder Characterization] B2 --> C B3 --> C C --> C1[Particle Size Analysis] C --> C2[Apparent Density] C --> C3[Flow Rate Test] C --> D[Blending / Mixing] D --> D1[Add Lubricant] D --> D2[Add Alloying Elements] D1 --> E[Compaction] D2 --> E E --> E1[Uniaxial Die Press] E --> E2[Cold Isostatic Press CIP] E --> E3[Hot Isostatic Press HIP] E --> E4[Powder Injection Molding PIM] E1 --> F[Green Compact] E2 --> F E3 --> F E4 --> F F --> G[Sintering Furnace] G --> G1[Solid State Sintering] G --> G2[Liquid Phase Sintering] G1 --> H[Sintered Part] G2 --> H H --> I[Secondary Operations] I --> I1[Sizing / Coining] I --> I2[Infiltration] I --> I3[Heat Treatment] I1 --> J[Finished Component] I2 --> J I3 --> J ``` ### 4.2 Block Diagram: Three Stages of Solid-State Sintering ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Initial Stage
Neck Formation] --> B[Intermediate Stage
Pore Channels] B --> C[Final Stage
Isolated Pores] A --> A1[Driving Force: Surface Energy] A --> A2[No Densification] A --> A3[Interconnected Porosity] B --> B1[Grain Boundary Diffusion] B --> B2[15-20% Linear Shrinkage] C --> C1[Pore Pinning at Grain Corners] C --> C2[95-99% Theoretical Density] ``` ### 4.3 Block Diagram: Powder Production Method Classification ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Powder Production Methods] --> B[Mechanical] A --> C[Atomization] A --> D[Chemical] A --> E[Electrochemical] B --> B1[Machining] B --> B2[Ball Milling] B --> B3[Mechanical Alloying] C --> C1[Gas Atomization - Spherical] C --> C2[Water Atomization - Irregular] C --> C3[Centrifugal - REP Process] D --> D1[Reduction of Oxides] D --> D2[Carbonyl Process] D --> D3[Precipitation from Solution] E --> E1[Electrolytic Deposition] ``` ### 4.4 Schematic Block: Compaction Methods Comparison ```mermaid flowchart TB A[Compaction Techniques] --> B[Uniaxial Die] A --> C[CIP - Cold Isostatic] A --> D[HIP - Hot Isostatic] A --> E[PIM - Powder Injection Molding] B --> B1[Pressure: 150-1000 MPa] B --> B2[Simple Shapes Only] B --> B3[Density Gradient Present] C --> C1[Pressure: 100-400 MPa] C --> C2[Uniform Density] C --> C3[Large Parts Possible] D --> D1[Temp: 0.5-0.9 T_m] D --> C1 D --> D2[Near 100% Density] E --> E1[Powder + Binder] E --> E2[Complex Shapes] E --> E3[Small Parts] ``` ## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** **Define powder metallurgy. List any four methods of producing metal powders.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** *Definition (1 Mark):* Powder metallurgy is a manufacturing process in which fine metal powders are compacted under high pressure in a die and then heated (sintered) in a controlled atmosphere to form a solid, near-net-shape engineering component without melting the base material. *Four Methods (½ Mark Each):* 1. **Atomization** (gas, water, centrifugal) 2. **Mechanical methods** (machining, ball milling, mechanical alloying) 3. **Chemical reduction** of metal oxides (e.g., Fe₂O₃ + H₂ → Fe + H₂O) 4. **Electrolytic deposition** (powder deposited at cathode) 5. **Carbonyl process** (thermal decomposition of metal carbonyls) 6. **Precipitation from solution** (chemical reduction from aqueous solution) --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam - June 2024]** **What is sintering? Explain briefly the three stages of solid-state sintering.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** *Definition (1 Mark):* Sintering is a thermal process in which compacted metal powder particles are bonded together by diffusion at elevated temperatures (typically 0.7–0.9 × T_m) without complete melting, driven by the reduction of surface free energy. *Three Stages (2 Marks):* 1. **Initial Stage:** Neck formation at particle contact points; negligible densification; pores remain interconnected. 2. **Intermediate Stage:** Pores form continuous channels along grain edges; densification begins; grain boundary diffusion dominates. 3. **Final Stage:** Pores become isolated and spherical at grain corners; significant shrinkage; near-theoretical density achieved. --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — KTU Module Internal Choice --- **Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** **(a)** Explain in detail the various methods of producing metal powders. Discuss the advantages and disadvantages of atomization. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** What is sintering? Describe the mechanism of solid-state sintering with a neat sketch showing the three stages. **(7 Marks)** **Model Answer:** **(a) Powder Production Methods (7 Marks):** The major industrial methods for producing metal powders are classified into three broad categories: **1. Mechanical Methods (2 Marks):** - **Machining:** Produces coarse chips or turnings, which are subsequently milled to fine powders. - **Ball Milling / Attrition Milling:** Particles are reduced in size by impact and abrasion in a rotating cylindrical mill. - **Mechanical Alloying (MA):** High-energy ball milling produces composite powders through repeated welding, fracturing, and re-welding of particles. Used for ODS superalloys, Al alloys. **2. Atomization (3 Marks):** A molten metal stream is broken up into droplets by a high-energy fluid jet, which then solidify as spherical powders. - **Gas Atomization:** Uses inert gas (argon, nitrogen); produces high-purity spherical powders for aerospace. - **Water Atomization:** High cooling rate; produces irregular-shaped powders ideal for cold compaction (sintered steels). - **Centrifugal Atomization / REP:** Rotating electrode produces ultra-pure spherical powders for superalloys. **Advantages of Atomization:** - High production rate (industrial scale). - Wide range of metals and alloys. - Spherical particles (good flow). - Tight control of particle size distribution. **Disadvantages of Atomization:** - High capital cost for atomization chambers. - Wide particle size distribution requires classification. - Reactive metals require inert atmosphere (costly). **3. Chemical & Electrochemical Methods (2 Marks):** - **Reduction:** Fe₂O₃ + 3H₂ → 2Fe + 3H₂O (sponge iron powder). - **Carbonyl Process:** Fe(CO)₅ → Fe + 5CO (very pure Fe powder). - **Electrolytic Deposition:** Powder deposited at cathode from salt solution; high purity, dendritic morphology. --- **(b) Solid-State Sintering Mechanism (7 Marks):** **Definition (1 Mark):** Sintering is a heat treatment process that bonds powder particles into a coherent mass by atomic diffusion at temperatures below the melting point. **Three Stages (5 Marks):** **Stage 1 — Initial Stage (Neck Formation):** At the contact points between adjacent particles, atomic diffusion occurs, forming necks. Surface area decreases; total surface energy decreases. No measurable shrinkage. Porosity remains highly interconnected. **Stage 2 — Intermediate Stage:** Grain boundaries form; pores become cylindrical channels along grain edges. Material transport via grain boundary diffusion and volume diffusion leads to significant densification. Approximately 15–20% linear shrinkage observed. **Stage 3 — Final Stage:** Pores become isolated and tend toward spherical shape at grain corners (driven by minimum surface energy). Dihedral angle ψ controls equilibrium. Densification reaches 90–98% of theoretical. **Sketch (1 Mark):** Particle pairs showing neck growth, with pore geometry evolving from interconnected to isolated. **Driving Force:** Reduction in total surface free energy $\Delta G_{sv} < 0$. --- **Question B (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam - June 2024]** **(a)** What are the different compaction methods used in powder metallurgy? Explain cold isostatic pressing with a sketch. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Discuss the characterization of metal powders in terms of (i) particle size distribution (ii) flow rate (iii) apparent density. Mention the relevant ASTM standards. **(7 Marks)** **Model Answer:** **(a) Compaction Methods (7 Marks):** **Classification of Compaction Methods (2 Marks):** 1. Uniaxial Die Compaction 2. Cold Isostatic Pressing (CIP) 3. Hot Isostatic Pressing (HIP) 4. Powder Injection Molding (PIM) 5. Hot Pressing 6. Extrusion / Rolling of green compacts **Cold Isostatic Pressing (CIP) — Detailed (5 Marks):** **Principle:** Powder is enclosed in a flexible, elastomeric mold (silicone rubber, polyurethane). The mold is submerged in a hydraulic fluid inside a pressure vessel. Hydrostatic pressure (100–400 MPa) is applied uniformly on all sides. **Process Steps:** 1. Powder is filled into a flexible mold. 2. The mold is sealed (vacuum-degassed). 3. Mold is placed inside the pressure chamber. 4. High-pressure fluid is pumped in. 5. Pressure is held for a few seconds to minutes. 6. Pressure is released; green compact is removed. **Sketch Description (2 Marks):** A cylindrical pressure vessel containing the elastomeric mold submerged in hydraulic fluid, with pressure inlet and pressure gauge. **Advantages:** - Uniform green density (no density gradient). - Suitable for large parts and complex shapes. - Lower die wear (no rigid die contact). - High green strength. **Disadvantages:** - Lower dimensional accuracy compared to die compaction. - Higher cycle time. - More expensive than uniaxial pressing. --- **(b) Powder Characterization (7 Marks):** **Characterization is essential to ensure consistent quality of sintered products.** **(i) Particle Size Distribution (2.5 Marks):** - Sieve analysis (ASTM B214) for 45 μm–1000 μm particles. - Laser diffraction (ASTM B822) for 1 μm–500 μm particles. - Sedimentation, microscopy for sub-micron powders. - Sieves are stacked from coarse (top) to fine (bottom); powder is shaken, and the weight retained on each sieve is measured. - Data plotted as cumulative weight % vs. sieve size. **(ii) Flow Rate (2 Marks):** - Measured using Hall Flow Meter (ASTM B213). - 50 g of powder flows through a calibrated funnel of 2.54 mm orifice. - Time recorded; flow rate = mass / time (typically **20–40 s/50 g** for good flow). - Important for die filling consistency. **(iii) Apparent Density (2.5 Marks):** - Powder loosely fills a Hall funnel (ASTM B212) and flows into a cup of known volume (25 cm³). - Mass measured; apparent density = mass / volume. - Typical values: iron powder ~2.5 g/cm³; copper ~2.0 g/cm³. - High apparent density → better die filling → higher green density. --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes Students Make (Lose 1–2 Marks Each):** > > 1. **Confusing green density with sintered density.** Green = before sintering; Sintered = after sintering. Many students use them interchangeably — **this is a fatal error.** > 2. **Forgetting to mention the sintering atmosphere** (e.g., hydrogen for tungsten, vacuum for stainless steel). Examiners specifically check atmosphere control. > 3. **Skipping the role of surface energy** as the driving force. The phrase "driven by reduction in surface free energy" carries 1 mark by itself. > 4. **Not specifying ASTM standards** in characterization answers. KTU examiners reward ASTM code mentions. > 5. **Confusing CIP and HIP.** CIP = cold (room temperature); HIP = hot (high temperature + pressure simultaneously). Many students mix these up. > 6. **Omitting units** in numerical problems (e.g., MPa, g/cm³, μm). > 7. **Forgetting the "no melting" feature** of P/M — Examiners want students to emphasize that the process is below the melting point. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember #### **Rapid Revision Checklist:** - **Definition:** Powder metallurgy = powder production + compaction + sintering (no melting). - **Key Powder Production Methods:** Atomization (most industrial), mechanical (milling), chemical (reduction, carbonyl). - **Atomization Types:** Gas (spherical, pure), Water (irregular, cheap), Centrifugal (ultra-pure). - **Characterization:** Particle size, flow rate, apparent density (use ASTM standards). - **ASTM Standards to Remember:** B212 (apparent density), B213 (flow rate), B214 (sieve), B822 (laser diffraction). - **Compaction Methods:** Uniaxial die, CIP (cold isostatic), HIP (hot isostatic), PIM (injection molding). - **CIP vs HIP:** CIP = cold + isostatic; HIP = hot + isostatic; HIP gives near-100% density. - **Three Stages of Sintering:** Initial (neck), Intermediate (channels), Final (isolated pores). - **Sintering Temperature:** 0.7–0.9 × T_m. - **Driving Force:** Reduction in surface free energy. - **Dihedral Angle ψ:** Determines pore stability; ψ < 60° → closed pores; ψ > 120° → open at GB. - **Frenkel Equation:** $(x/r)^2 = 2\gamma_{sv} t / (3\eta r)$. - **Heckel Equation:** $\ln[1/(1-D)] = KP + A$. - **Green Density:** 60–80% theoretical. - **Sintered Density:** 85–95% theoretical (can be higher with HIP). - **Key Advantages:** Near-net shape, low waste, controlled porosity, refractory metals. - **Key Disadvantages:** High tooling cost, size limitations, residual porosity. - **Applications:** Sintered gears (auto), W-Cu contacts (electrical), diamond tools (cutting), porous Ti implants (biomedical). - **Infiltration:** Filling residual pores with a lower-melting metal (e.g., Cu into W). - **Sintering Atmosphere:** H₂ (for refractory metals), N₂ (for steels), vacuum (for reactive metals). - **Mechanical Alloying:** Produces ODS alloys (e.g., MA 956, Inconel MA 758).

topics.title: Diffusion bonding
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT412 - COMPOSITE MATERIALS AND CERAMICS
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Diffusion bonding

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Diffusion Bonding (DB)** is a solid-state joining process in which two closely matched surfaces are united at an elevated temperature (typically **0.5 – 0.8 T<sub>m</sub>**) under a controlled, low-to-moderate compressive pressure (**1 – 15 MPa**), held for a sufficient duration, in a protective atmosphere (vacuum or inert gas), to produce a metallurgical bond **without macroscopic plastic deformation, melting, or filler material addition**. The bond is achieved through a combination of localized plastic flow, creep, and atomic diffusion across the interface.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examiner's Definition (Memorise This):**
> *“Diffusion bonding is a solid-state joining technique that uses simultaneous application of heat and pressure to promote atomic diffusion across the mating interface, producing a joint whose microstructure and properties are essentially identical to the parent material, with no fusion zone (FZ), heat-affected zone (HAZ), or filler-induced dissimilarity.”*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Imagine pressing two slices of butter together on a warm day. The slices don't melt, but their surfaces gradually merge because the molecules have enough thermal energy to "creep" into one another. Now replace butter with titanium sheets and butter molecules with metal atoms, and instead of a warm day, you heat the assembly inside a vacuum furnace under a slow hydraulic press. The atoms on each surface gradually migrate across the boundary until the boundary itself disappears. **That is diffusion bonding.**

**Everyday analogue table:**

| Real-World Phenomenon | Diffusion Bonding Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Two cold panes of glass fusing when heated | Two metal surfaces fusing under heat + pressure |
| Sand grains sticking together when damp & pressed | Surface asperities bonding under compaction |
| Glacier plastic flow under its own weight | Creep-driven contact growth at joint interface |
| Sn–Pb solder wetting a Cu pad | Surface diffusion + interfacial reaction (in DB, more controlled) |

## 1.3 Why Diffusion Bonding Matters in Composites & Ceramics

> [!NOTE]
> **Why ceramics/composites cannot be welded conventionally:**
> - **Brittleness:** Cannot tolerate the thermal shock or high HAZ stresses of arc/laser welding.
> - **Dissimilar CTE (Coefficient of Thermal Expansion):** Fusion welding creates residual stresses → cracking.
> - **Reaction sensitivity:** Molten pools react aggressively with fibres/reinforcements.
> - **No melting acceptable:** Reinforcements (SiC, Al<sub>2</sub>O<sub>3</sub>, C-fibres) may decompose above T<sub>m</sub> of matrix.

Diffusion bonding solves all these because the maximum temperature stays **below** the matrix melting point and the macroscopic strain stays **below** the elastic/plastic limit, preserving fibre architecture in **MMC (Metal Matrix Composites)** and **CMC (Ceramic Matrix Composites)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Metrics to Remember:**
> - Bonding Temperature: **0.5 – 0.8 T<sub>m</sub>** (K)
> - Bonding Pressure: **1 – 15 MPa**
> - Holding Time: **15 min – 4 h**
> - Vacuum level: **≤ 10<sup>-3</sup> Pa** (high vacuum)
> - Resultant bond strength: **≥ 90% of parent metal UTS** (for titanium DB)

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Sequential Mechanisms of Bond Formation

Every diffusion bond forms by the **sequential, overlapping** action of three mechanisms:

### Mechanism 1 — Plastic Deformation (Initial Asperity Collapse)
- Surfaces, however polished, are **rough at the micron scale** (R<sub>a</sub> = 0.4 – 1.6 µm).
- On application of pressure, only the **asperity tips** touch first.
- These tips yield plastically → true contact area grows from ~1% to ~15–20% of nominal area.
- Dominant in the **first 60 s** of the bonding cycle.

### Mechanism 2 — Power-Law Creep (Contact Growth)
- At elevated temperature, asperities continue to deform by **dislocation climb-controlled creep**.
- The contact area grows **linearly with time** at constant T, P.
- Described by **King's equation** (covered in §3).

### Mechanism 3 — Diffusion-Controlled Pore Elimination (Final Closure)
- The remaining isolated pores at the interface shrink by:
  - **Surface diffusion** (along pore walls)
  - **Grain-boundary diffusion** (along grain boundaries meeting the pore)
  - **Volume (lattice) diffusion** (through bulk matrix)
- Pore elimination gives the **final, void-free bond**.

## 2.2 The Four Bonding Stages (Stage Model)

| Stage | Description | Dominant Mechanism | Duration |
|---|---|---|---|
| **I – Initial Contact** | Asperity tips touch; localised plastic deformation | Plastic flow | 0 – 5 % of t |
| **II – Contact Growth** | Contact area expands; interface becomes continuous | Power-law creep | 5 – 60 % |
| **III – Pore Closure** | Isolated lenticular pores shrink | Diffusion (gb + vol) | 60 – 95 % |
| **IV – Diffusion Weld** | Bonds convert to grain boundaries; interface vanishes | GB migration | 95 – 100 % |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Print this table — it covers ~70% of the numerical problems asked in KTU ESE on Diffusion Bonding.**

| # | Formula (LaTeX) | Meaning / Variables | Units | Validity / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $D = D_0 \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{Q}{RT}\right)$ | Arrhenius diffusion; D<sub>0</sub> = pre-exponential, Q = activation energy | D in m²/s, Q in J/mol | Foundation equation |
| 2 | $\dot{\varepsilon} = \dfrac{ADGb}{kT}\left(\dfrac{\sigma}{G}\right)^{n}$ | Power-law creep (Bird–Dorn–Mukherjee) | s<sup>-1</sup> | Stage II kinetics |
| 3 | $\eta = \dfrac{\sigma}{3\dot{\varepsilon}} = \dfrac{G}{3A}\left(\dfrac{\sigma}{G}\right)^{1-n}\exp\!\left(\dfrac{Q}{RT}\right)$ | Effective viscosity under creep | Pa·s | King’s formulation input |
| 4 | $\left(\dfrac{x}{a}\right)^{n} = \dfrac{nP}{3\eta}\,t + C$ | King’s contact-growth equation (simplified) | dimensionless | Stage II dominant |
| 5 | $\dfrac{x}{a} = \left(\dfrac{2\,D_{gb}\,\delta\,\Omega\,P\,t}{a^{2}kT}\right)^{1/4}$ | Coble – Grain-boundary diffusion bonding | dimensionless | Stage III, fine grain |
| 6 | $\dfrac{x}{a} = \left(\dfrac{4\,D_{s}\,\Omega\,\gamma_{s}\,P\,t}{a^{2}kT^{2}}\right)^{1/3}$ | Surface-diffusion controlled bonding | dimensionless | Low-P, high-T |
| 7 | $\sigma_{b} = \sigma_{0}\left[1 - \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{t}{\tau}\right)\right]$ | Bond strength build-up (1st-order) | MPa | Engineering approximation |
| 8 | $t_{b} = \dfrac{kT\,a^{2}}{D_{gb}\,\delta\,\Omega\,P}$ | Time for full bond by GB-diffusion | seconds | Order-of-magnitude |
| 9 | $\Delta L = \dfrac{P\,L}{E}$ | Elastic closure (instantaneous) | m | First instant of pressure |
| 10 | $T_{bond} = (0.5\text{ to }0.8)\,T_{m}$ | Empirical bonding temperature window | K | Design rule of thumb |

**Symbol legend (used across all formulas):**

$D_0$ — pre-exponential diffusion coefficient (m²/s)
$Q$ — activation energy for diffusion (J/mol or kJ/mol)
$R$ — universal gas constant = **8.314 J·mol<sup>-1</sup>·K<sup>-1</sup>**
$T$ — absolute bonding temperature (K)
$k$ — Boltzmann constant = **1.381 × 10<sup>-23</sup> J·K<sup>-1</sup>**
$G$ — shear modulus (Pa)
$b$ — Burgers vector (m)
$A$ — Dorn constant (dimensionless, ≈ 1 – 10)
$n$ — creep exponent (typically 3 – 7 for metals, 1 – 2 for ceramics)
$a$ — asperity half-length / pitch (m)
$x$ — instantaneous contact half-length (m)
$\delta$ — grain-boundary width ≈ **0.5 nm**
$\Omega$ — atomic volume (m³/mol)
$\gamma_s$ — surface energy (J/m²)
$P$ — applied bonding pressure (Pa)
$\eta$ — effective viscosity (Pa·s)
$\sigma, \sigma_b, \sigma_0$ — stress, bond strength, parent UTS (Pa, MPa)
$\tau$ — characteristic bonding time (s)

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application Sector | Use of Diffusion Bonding |
|---|---|
| **Aerospace (Ti-alloys)** | Joining Ti-6Al-4V fan blades to discs, sandwich panels for engine nacelles |
| **Nuclear** | Zircaloy–stainless steel transition joints, fuel-pin end-caps |
| **Electronics** | Cu–Cu thermocompression bonding, MEMS wafer bonding |
| **MMC / CMC fabrication** | Consolidating layered Al/SiC, Ti/SiC fibre laminates |
| **Medical implants** | Joining Ti hip-stem necks to femoral heads |
| **Optics & Cryogenics** | Stainless-steel vacuum chambers where welds must be leak-tight and non-magnetic |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Fact:** The famous **“hot isostatic pressing (HIP)”** is *the same* mechanism as diffusion bonding, but applied to a powder compact or a part containing internal voids — the *can* acts as the “interface.”

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation I — Arrhenius Diffusion Coefficient

**Starting point:** Fick's first law for steady-state flux through area *A* over a gradient ∇c:

$$J = -D\,\nabla c$$

For thermally activated jumps across a barrier of height *Q*, transition-state theory gives the jump frequency as:

$$\nu = \nu_0 \exp\!\left(-\frac{Q}{RT}\right)$$

Combining the jump frequency with the geometric factor (mean free path λ and partition function Z), the diffusion coefficient emerges as:

$$D = \frac{1}{6}\,\lambda^{2}\,\nu_0 \exp\!\left(-\frac{Q}{RT}\right)$$

By defining the constant term as $D_0$:

$$\boxed{D = D_0 \exp\!\left(-\frac{Q}{RT}\right)}$$

**Conversion logic:**
- $D_0$ is a material property ≈ $\lambda^{2}\nu_0/6$ — typically **10<sup>-5</sup> to 10<sup>-3</sup> m²/s**.
- $Q$ for **self-diffusion in α-Fe** ≈ 280 kJ/mol; **for Ni** ≈ 285 kJ/mol; **for Cu grain boundary** ≈ 105 kJ/mol.
- Doubling the absolute temperature (within the 0.5–0.8 T<sub>m</sub> window) increases D by **≈ 10² – 10³**.

## 3.2 Derivation II — Power-Law Creep (B–D–M Equation)

**Starting point:** Orowan's relation — plastic strain rate equals mobile dislocation density ρ<sub>m</sub> times dislocation velocity v:

$$\dot{\varepsilon} = \rho_{m}\,b\,v$$

For climb-controlled creep, the climb velocity is given by:

$$v_{climb} = \frac{D_{sd}\,\sigma\,b^{3}}{l\,k\,T}$$

where $D_{sd}$ is the self-diffusion coefficient, $l$ is the dislocation link length, and σ is the resolved shear stress. Substituting and using the Taylor relation $\rho_{m} \propto (\sigma/G)^2$ gives:

$$\dot{\varepsilon} \propto \frac{D\,G\,b}{k\,T}\left(\frac{\sigma}{G}\right)^{n}$$

Adding the Dorn constant *A* (encapsulating dislocation core width, climb distance, etc.):

$$\boxed{\dot{\varepsilon} = A\,\frac{D\,G\,b}{k\,T}\left(\frac{\sigma}{G}\right)^{n}}$$

**Conversion logic:**
- $n = 3$ → dislocation climb in pure metals.
- $n = 5$ → Harper–Dorn creep.
- $n = 1$ → Nabarro–Herring / Coble diffusional creep (very fine grain).
- For ceramics (Al<sub>2</sub>O<sub>3</sub>, SiC), $n$ typically = 1.5 – 2.

## 3.3 Derivation III — Effective Viscosity η

Inverting the B–D–M equation for σ and dividing by 3 (because $\sigma \to 3\eta\dot{\varepsilon}$ in uniaxial analogy):

$$\eta = \frac{\sigma}{3\dot{\varepsilon}} = \frac{\sigma}{3\,A\,\dfrac{D\,G\,b}{kT}\left(\dfrac{\sigma}{G}\right)^{n}}$$

After cancelling one power of σ:

$$\eta = \frac{kT}{3\,A\,D\,G\,b}\left(\frac{G}{\sigma}\right)^{n-1}\,G$$

Substituting $D = D_0\exp(-Q/RT)$:

$$\boxed{\eta = \frac{G}{3\,A}\left(\frac{\sigma}{G}\right)^{1-n}\exp\!\left(\frac{Q}{RT}\right)}$$

**Conversion logic:** At low stress and high T, η becomes very small → asperities deform easily. At low T or high stress, η is huge → asperities rigid.

## 3.4 Derivation IV — King's Contact-Growth Equation

**Geometry:** Each asperity is modelled as a half-cylinder of radius *a*, height *h*, contacting a flat surface. After deformation, the asperity has flattened into a circular contact of half-width *x*, with the contact length ratio being the **bonding fraction**:

$$R_b = \left(\frac{x}{a}\right)^{2}$$

**Force balance on a single asperity:**
The applied pressure *P* supports the contact force through the deformed asperity cross-section. For power-law creep with effective viscosity η:

$$P\,\pi a^{2} = 3\eta\,\dot{\varepsilon}\,V_{asp}$$

Expressing strain rate as the rate of contact-area growth divided by instantaneous contact area:

$$\dot{\varepsilon} = \frac{2}{x}\frac{dx}{dt}\cdot\frac{1}{\text{geometric factor}}$$

After substituting and integrating from 0 → t (with initial contact ratio $R_0$):

$$\int_{R_0}^{R_b}\frac{dR}{R(1-R)\left[1 - K(1-R)\right]} = \frac{P}{\eta}\,t$$

For typical engineering conditions, the term $K(1-R) \ll 1$, which simplifies the integral to:

$$\boxed{\left(\frac{x}{a}\right)^{n} = \frac{n\,P}{3\eta}\,t + C}$$

where *C* is the initial-condition constant.

**Conversion logic:**
- $n = 3$ (metals): $(x/a)^3 \propto t$ → cube-root growth of contact area.
- Higher *P* or lower *η* (high T) → faster bonding.
- Doubling *T* reduces *η* exponentially → bonding time reduced by **orders of magnitude**.

## 3.5 Worked Example (KTU-Type Numerical)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem (typical 7-mark KTU module question):**
> *Two Ti-6Al-4V plates are diffusion bonded at 1173 K and 5 MPa. Calculate the time required to achieve a contact ratio (x/a) = 0.6. Given:*
> $A = 1$, $D_0 = 1.7\times10^{-5}\,\text{m}^{2}\text{s}^{-1}$, $Q = 169\,\text{kJ/mol}$, $G = 44\,\text{GPa}$, $b = 2.95\times10^{-10}\,\text{m}$, $n = 3$, $R = 8.314\,\text{J/mol·K}$, $T = 1173\,\text{K}$.

**Step 1 — Compute the diffusion coefficient:**

$$D = D_0 \exp\!\left(-\frac{Q}{RT}\right) = 1.7\times10^{-5}\exp\!\left(-\frac{169000}{8.314\times1173}\right)$$

Compute the exponent:

$$-\frac{169000}{8.314\times1173} = -\frac{169000}{9752.32} = -17.330$$

So:

$$D = 1.7\times10^{-5} \times e^{-17.330} = 1.7\times10^{-5}\times 3.02\times10^{-8}$$

$$D \approx 5.13\times10^{-13}\,\text{m}^{2}\text{s}^{-1}$$

**Step 2 — Compute effective viscosity η:**

$$\eta = \frac{G}{3A}\left(\frac{\sigma}{G}\right)^{1-n}\exp\!\left(\frac{Q}{RT}\right) = \frac{44\times10^{9}}{3\times1}\left(\frac{5\times10^{6}}{44\times10^{9}}\right)^{1-3}\exp(17.330)$$

Compute the stress ratio:

$$\frac{\sigma}{G} = \frac{5\times10^{6}}{44\times10^{9}} = 1.136\times10^{-4}$$

$$\left(\frac{\sigma}{G}\right)^{-2} = \frac{1}{(1.136\times10^{-4})^{2}} = 7.745\times10^{7}$$

Compute the exponential factor:

$$\exp(17.330) = 3.31\times10^{7}$$

Multiplying:

$$\eta = \frac{44\times10^{9}}{3}\times7.745\times10^{7}\times3.31\times10^{7}$$

$$\eta = 1.467\times10^{10}\times7.745\times10^{7}\times3.31\times10^{7}$$

$$\eta \approx 3.76\times10^{25}\,\text{Pa·s}$$

**Step 3 — Apply King's equation with n = 3:**

$$\left(\frac{x}{a}\right)^{n} = \frac{n\,P}{3\eta}\,t \;\Rightarrow\; (0.6)^{3} = \frac{3\times5\times10^{6}}{3\times3.76\times10^{25}}\,t$$

$$0.216 = \frac{5\times10^{6}}{3.76\times10^{25}}\,t$$

$$0.216 = 1.33\times10^{-19}\,t$$

$$t = \frac{0.216}{1.33\times10^{-19}} \approx 1.62\times10^{18}\,\text{s}$$

**Step 4 — Realistic engineering interpretation:**

This astronomical number tells us that **at 5 MPa and 1173 K, full contact by creep alone is impractical**. In practice, one must raise T to ≥ 1223 K (≈ 0.6 T<sub>m</sub> for Ti) — which exponentially reduces *η* — to obtain realistic bonding times of 30–60 min. **This is exactly why the empirical rule 0.5–0.8 T<sub>m</sub> exists.**

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Trap:** Many students compute η incorrectly because they use shear stress *τ* instead of normal stress *σ* in the B-D-M equation. Always use **the applied bonding pressure directly as σ** for King’s model in 1-D simplification. Also, $n$ in King’s equation is the **creep exponent**, NOT a fitting parameter.

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Mechanism Map

| Regime | Dominant Mechanism | Driving Force | Rate Law | Microstructural Signature |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Low T (< 0.4 T<sub>m</sub>) | Plastic flow only | Applied P | $\dot\varepsilon\propto\sigma^{n}$ | Asperity peaks flattened, no pore rounding |
| Med T (0.4–0.6 T<sub>m</sub>) | Plastic + creep | P + T | King's $(x/a)^n$ law | Bonded area with lenticular pores |
| High T (0.6–0.8 T<sub>m</sub>) | GB + volume diffusion | T, ∇µ | $D_{gb}$ (Coble) | Pores rounded → eliminated |
| Very high T (> 0.8 T<sub>m</sub>) | Recrystallisation | T | Arrhenius × n=1 | New grains across interface → bond invisible |
