# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 Marks)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define diffusion bonding. List any four process parameters that influence the bond strength.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Diffusion bonding is a solid-state joining process in which two mating surfaces are united under simultaneous application of elevated temperature (typically **0.5–0.8 T<sub>m</sub>**) and low-to-moderate pressure (**1–15 MPa**), without macroscopic deformation, melting, or filler material, to produce a metallurgical bond via atomic diffusion.

Four key process parameters influencing bond strength are:
1. **Bonding temperature (T)** — exponential influence via $D = D_0\exp(-Q/RT)$.
2. **Bonding pressure (P)** — drives initial asperity collapse and contact growth.
3. **Holding time (t)** — allows diffusion-driven void elimination.
4. **Surface preparation** — roughness R<sub>a</sub>, cleanliness, oxide removal.

*(Additional valid parameters: atmosphere/vacuum, heating/cooling rates, material compatibility.)*

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Why is diffusion bonding preferred over fusion welding for ceramic-matrix composites (CMCs)?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Diffusion bonding is preferred over fusion welding for CMCs for the following reasons:

1. **No melting required:** The reinforcement phase (e.g., SiC fibres, Al<sub>2</sub>O<sub>3</sub>) decomposes or reacts aggressively at the matrix melting point; diffusion bonding operates at **T < T<sub>m</sub>**.
2. **Preservation of fibre architecture:** Absence of a molten pool means **no fibre displacement, dissolution, or interfacial reactions** that would degrade composite strength.
3. **Minimal residual stress:** The temperature window (0.5–0.8 T<sub>m</sub>) limits thermal gradients → low CTE-mismatch stresses that would otherwise crack the brittle ceramic.
4. **Bond-line identical to parent material:** No FZ/HAZ, no filler → uniform microstructure.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (ESE Module Choice: 14 Marks)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]*

**(a)** With the help of a suitable schematic, explain the **four sequential stages** of bond formation during diffusion bonding. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **King's contact-growth equation** for diffusion bonding controlled by power-law creep, starting from the Bird-Dorn-Mukherjee creep law. State clearly the assumptions. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution (a):

| Stage | Description | Microstructural Signature | Dominant Mechanism |
|---|---|---|---|
| **I — Initial Contact** | First asperity tips touch when pressure applied; localised plastic flow flattens them. | Random isolated contact points; R<sub>a</sub> visible elsewhere. | Plastic deformation |
| **II — Contact Growth** | Asperity height reduces, contact patches enlarge; interface becomes continuous. | Continuous bonded area with **lenticular voids** trapped. | Power-law creep + GB diffusion |
| **III — Pore Closure** | Lenticular voids shrink and spheroidize by surface, grain-boundary and volume diffusion. | Spherical pores at triple points; porosity drops to <1 %. | Coble + Herring–Nabarro diffusion |
| **IV — Complete Bond** | Pores eliminated; new grains grow across the original interface. | Microstructure indistinguishable from parent; bond invisible metallographically. | Grain-boundary migration + recrystallisation |

#### Model Solution (b):

**Step 1 — Start from Bird–Dorn–Mukherjee creep law:**

$$\dot{\varepsilon} = A\,\frac{D\,G\,b}{kT}\left(\frac{\sigma}{G}\right)^{n}$$

**Step 2 — Invert to define effective viscosity η:**

$$\eta = \frac{\sigma}{3\dot{\varepsilon}} \;\Rightarrow\; \eta = \frac{G}{3A}\left(\frac{\sigma}{G}\right)^{1-n}\exp\!\left(\frac{Q}{RT}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Geometry of a single asperity** (modelled as a half-cylinder of radius *a*, half-length *x* after creep):

The strain rate of asperity flattening is:

$$\dot{\varepsilon}_{asp} = \frac{1}{x}\frac{dx}{dt}$$

**Step 4 — Force balance** on the asperity under applied pressure P:

$$P\,\pi a^{2} = 3\eta\,\dot{\varepsilon}_{asp}\,\pi x^{2}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute and simplify:**

$$P\,a^{2} = 3\eta\,\frac{x}{dt}\,x^{2}\;\Rightarrow\; P\,a^{2}\,dt = 3\eta\,x^{2}\,dx$$

**Step 6 — Integrate** from initial contact $x_0$ to $x$, over time $0 \to t$:

$$\int_{0}^{t}\frac{P\,a^{2}}{3\eta}\,dt = \int_{x_{0}}^{x} x^{2}\,dx$$

$$\frac{P\,a^{2}}{3\eta}\,t = \frac{1}{3}\left[x^{3}-x_{0}^{3}\right]$$

**Step 7 — Neglect $x_0$** for practical bonding times ($x \gg x_0$):

$$x^{3} = \frac{P\,a^{2}}{\eta}\,t$$

Dividing both sides by $a^{3}$:

$$\boxed{\left(\frac{x}{a}\right)^{n} = \frac{nP}{3\eta}\,t + C \quad \text{(generalised King’s equation)}}$$

**Assumptions stated:**
- Asperities are modelled as regular cylinders/half-cylinders of identical geometry.
- Creep is the rate-controlling mechanism (Stage II).
- Material is homogeneous and isotropic.
- Constant T, P during the bonding interval (isothermal, isobaric).
- No phase transformations or chemical reactions at the interface.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice

**(a)** What are the **mechanisms** by which diffusion bonding occurs? Describe the **King's model** for power-law creep-controlled bonding with its governing equation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A diffusion-bonding operation on aluminium alloy 2024 is to be carried out at 773 K under 10 MPa pressure. Using King's equation, determine the time required to achieve a contact ratio of 0.5. Given: A = 1.5, D<sub>0</sub> = 1.7 × 10<sup>-5</sup> m²/s, Q = 142 kJ/mol, G = 26 GPa, n = 3, b = 2.86 × 10<sup>-10</sup> m. R = 8.314 J/mol·K. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution (a):

The three primary mechanisms of diffusion bonding are:

1. **Plastic deformation** of asperities (early stage, pressure-controlled).
2. **Power-law creep** of asperities (intermediate stage, T + P controlled).
3. **Diffusion-controlled pore elimination** via surface, grain-boundary and volume diffusion (final stage, T + time controlled).

**King's Model:**

King (1967) modelled the bonded interface as a periodic array of cylindrical asperities of radius *a* contacting a flat counter-face. The strain rate of asperity collapse by power-law creep is set equal to the applied pressure via an effective viscosity $\eta$:

$$\left(\frac{x}{a}\right)^{n} = \frac{nP}{3\eta}\,t + C$$

where *x* is the instantaneous contact half-width, *P* is bonding pressure, *t* is time, *n* is creep exponent, and $\eta$ is the effective viscosity given by:

$$\eta = \frac{G}{3A}\left(\frac{\sigma}{G}\right)^{1-n}\exp\!\left(\frac{Q}{RT}\right)$$

#### Model Solution (b):

**Given:** T = 773 K, P = 10 MPa = 10 × 10<sup>6</sup> Pa, target (x/a) = 0.5, A = 1.5, D<sub>0</sub> = 1.7 × 10<sup>-5</sup> m²/s, Q = 142 000 J/mol, G = 26 × 10<sup>9</sup> Pa, n = 3, b = 2.86 × 10<sup>-10</sup> m.

**Step 1 — Compute D:**

$$D = 1.7\times10^{-5}\exp\!\left(-\frac{142000}{8.314\times773}\right)$$

$$\frac{142000}{8.314\times773} = \frac{142000}{6426.7} = 22.10$$

$$D = 1.7\times10^{-5}\times e^{-22.10} = 1.7\times10^{-5}\times 2.51\times10^{-10}$$

$$D \approx 4.27\times10^{-15}\,\text{m}^{2}\text{s}^{-1}$$

**Step 2 — Compute η:**

$$\eta = \frac{G}{3A}\left(\frac{\sigma}{G}\right)^{1-n}\exp\!\left(\frac{Q}{RT}\right) = \frac{26\times10^{9}}{3\times1.5}\left(\frac{10\times10^{6}}{26\times10^{9}}\right)^{-2}\exp(22.10)$$

Stress ratio:

$$\frac{\sigma}{G} = \frac{10^{7}}{2.6\times10^{10}} = 3.846\times10^{-4}$$

$$\left(\frac{\sigma}{G}\right)^{-2} = 6.76\times10^{6}$$

Exponential:

$$\exp(22.10) = 3.55\times10^{9}$$

Multiplying:

$$\eta = \frac{2.6\times10^{10}}{4.5}\times6.76\times10^{6}\times3.55\times10^{9}$$

$$\eta = 5.778\times10^{9}\times6.76\times10^{6}\times3.55\times10^{9}$$

$$\eta \approx 1.387\times10^{26}\,\text{Pa·s}$$

**Step 3 — Apply King's equation (n = 3, C = 0):**

$$(0.5)^{3} = \frac{3\times10\times10^{6}}{3\times1.387\times10^{26}}\,t$$

$$0.125 = \frac{10^{7}}{1.387\times10^{26}}\,t = 7.21\times10^{-20}\,t$$

$$t = \frac{0.125}{7.21\times10^{-20}} \approx 1.73\times10^{18}\,\text{s}$$

**Engineering interpretation:** This shows the operation is not feasible by creep alone at 773 K. The temperature must be raised toward **0.7 T<sub>m</sub>** (≈ 773 K already → acceptable for Al but bonding time must be increased) — hence 30–60 min bonding times are observed experimentally, but full contact requires surface preparation & diffusion to assist.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU students most commonly LOSE MARKS on Diffusion Bonding questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing *bonding temperature* with *melting temperature*.** Always state as a *fraction of T<sub>m</sub>* (e.g., 0.6 T<sub>m</sub>), not a fixed value like "1000 °C".
>
> 2. **Forgetting the order of magnitude of the pre-exponential D<sub>0</sub> and Q.** Wrong units (eV instead of J/mol) instantly give absurd D values.
>
> 3. **Mis-identifying the four stages.** Examiners want Stage I (plastic) → II (creep) → III (pore closure) → IV (recrystallisation). Saying "welding → cooling → testing" loses full marks.
>
> 4. **Drawing King's schematic without labelling the asperity geometry** (a, x, h). Examiners mark 1–2 marks for the *labelled diagram*.
>
> 5. **Omitting assumptions in derivations.** King's model assumes isothermal, isobaric, single-mechanism-dominated conditions. State them explicitly.
>
> 6. **Numerical errors in exponents** (e.g., −17.33 vs −17.33 × 10<sup>3</sup>) cause 5–7 orders of magnitude errors. Always double-check the **sign and magnitude of the exponent** of the Arrhenius term.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### 🔁 Rapid Revision Checklist

- [x] **Definition:** Solid-state joining at 0.5–0.8 T<sub>m</sub>, 1–15 MPa, in vacuum/inert atmosphere; **no melting, no filler, no macroscopic deformation**.
- [x] **Four stages:** Plastic contact → Creep growth → Diffusion pore closure → Recrystallisation.
- [x] **Three mechanisms:** Plastic deformation + Power-law creep + Diffusional flow.
- [x] **Master equation — Arrhenius:** $D = D_0 \exp(-Q/RT)$.
- [x] **Master equation — King's contact growth:** $(x/a)^n = (nP/3\eta)\,t + C$.
- [x] **Master equation — Effective viscosity:** $\eta = (G/3A)\,(\sigma/G)^{1-n}\exp(Q/RT)$.
- [x] **Master equation — Coble GB-diffusion bonding:** $x/a = (2D_{gb}\delta\Omega Pt/a^2 kT)^{1/4}$.
- [x] **Order of parameter influence:** T (exponential, strongest) > P (linear, moderate) > t (square-root or cube-root) > surface prep (qualitative) > atmosphere.
- [x] **Standard metrics:** 0.5–0.8 T<sub>m</sub>, 1–15 MPa, 15 min–4 h, vacuum ≤ 10<sup>-3</sup> Pa.
- [x] **Why DB for composites/ceramics:** No fibre damage, no HAZ, no FZ, low residual stress, parent-microstructure-equivalent bond.
- [x] **Process map:** Pump + Furnace + Vacuum + Workpiece stack + DAQ (refer to schematic in §4.1).
- [x] **Practical applications:** Ti fan blades (aerospace), Zircaloy-SS joints (nuclear), Cu-Cu wafer bonding (electronics), MMC/CMC consolidation, medical Ti implants.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Memorise Point for KTU ESE:**
> *"Diffusion bonding is to ceramics & composites what arc welding is to steels — the *only* practical joining route. Master King's equation and Arrhenius diffusion, and you master the topic."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Melt stirring
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT412 - COMPOSITE MATERIALS AND CERAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Melt stirring

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Melt Stirring** (also known as **Stir Casting** or **Melt Agitation**) is a **liquid-state fabrication route** used in the processing of Metal Matrix Composites (MMCs) and certain discontinuously reinforced composites. In this technique, a reinforcement phase (ceramic particles, short fibers, whiskers) is introduced into a **molten metallic matrix** (e.g., Al, Mg, Cu, Ti alloys) and dispersed homogeneously through **controlled mechanical agitation** prior to solidification.

The process relies on the formation of a **forced vortex** at the free surface of the melt, which acts as a particle-acceptance window. Particles poured over the vortex are entrained, wetted, and transported into the bulk liquid by turbulent fluid flow, shear forces, and centrifugal action.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Cue:** Melt stirring is classified under the **Liquid-State Processing** family of composite fabrication techniques. The other siblings in this family are **squeeze casting, compocasting (rheocasting), spray casting, and in-situ processing**. Examiners frequently ask for comparative distinctions.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Washing Machine" Model

Imagine a top-loading **washing machine** filled with water (the molten metal). You drop in colored sand particles (ceramic reinforcement) while the agitator spins. Three things happen:

1. The spinning action creates a **funnel-shaped dip (vortex)** at the surface — this is your *particle-entry window*.
2. The turbulent eddies break up clumps of sand into individual grains — this is *de-agglomeration*.
3. The swirling motion keeps the grains suspended long enough for them to be carried deep into the liquid — this is *homogeneous distribution*.

When the machine stops, the heaviest sand settles, but ideally, a uniformly speckled suspension remains until solidification locks everything in place. That speckled, frozen suspension is your **composite ingot**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Analogy Anchors:**
> - The **vortex** ↔ a funnel-shaped surface depression acting as a particle-ingestion gate.
> - **Stirrer (impeller)** ↔ the washing-machine agitator transferring momentum.
> - **Molten metal pool** ↔ the wash water (viscous fluid medium).
> - **Ceramic particles** ↔ the suspended sand grains.

## 1.3 Process Variables (KTU High-Yield)

| Process Parameter | Typical Range (Al-alloy MMC) | Physical Effect |
|---|---|---|
| Stirring Speed (N) | **300 – 1000 rpm** | Determines vortex depth and turbulence intensity |
| Melt Temperature | **600 – 800 °C** (above liquidus + 50–100 °C) | Controls viscosity and wettability |
| Stirring Time | **5 – 30 minutes** | Influences distribution uniformity |
| Particle Size | **5 – 100 µm** | Smaller → harder to incorporate; Larger → settle faster |
| Particle Pre-heating | **200 – 400 °C** | Reduces thermal shock and aids wetting |
| Crucible/Blade Material | **Graphite, Stainless steel coated** | Avoids Fe contamination in Al melts |

> [!NOTE]
> The **reinforcement volume fraction** achievable by melt stirring is typically limited to **15 – 30 vol%** in conventional setups. Beyond ~30%, the viscosity becomes impractically high and the melt becomes non-Newtonian.

## 1.4 Categorization of Melt Stirring Routes

Melt stirring is not a single technique — it is a family differentiated by the **energy input mechanism**:

1. **Mechanical Stir Casting** — Direct impeller rotation (most common).
2. **Electromagnetic (Induction) Stirring** — Lorentz force drives convection (no moving parts).
3. **Gas Bubble Stirring (Melt Bubbling / Purging)** — Inert gas injection drives recirculation.
4. **Centrifugal Stirring** — Rotating crucible produces high-g dispersion.
5. **Ultrasonic-assisted Stirring** — Acoustic cavitation breaks clusters.

> [!TIP]
> KTU examiners love asking: *"Differentiate mechanical stir casting from electromagnetic stirring."* Keep the energy-input distinction crisp: **mechanical = kinetic via impeller**, **electromagnetic = kinetic via Lorentz force (J × B)**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Physics of Particle Incorporation — A Structured Walk-through

The entire science of melt stirring can be unpacked into **five sequential physical phenomena**. Each phenomenon corresponds to one or more measurable process variables.

### Step 1 — Vortex Formation at the Free Surface
A rotating impeller (paddle, turbine, or propeller) imparts angular momentum to the melt. The centripetal acceleration pushes fluid outward along the blades, and the resulting pressure drop at the impeller axis pulls the free surface **downward**, producing a **forced vortex** with characteristic shape:

- **Vortex Depth (h_v):** Increases with stirrer speed, blade pitch, and radius; decreases with melt viscosity.
- **Vortex Stability Criterion:** The **Froude Number (Fr)** of the impeller must exceed a critical value to maintain a stable, symmetric vortex (Fr > 0.1 typically).

### Step 2 — Particle Introduction Across the Free Surface
Pre-heated particles are sprinkled onto the vortex. For incorporation to occur, the **buoyancy-corrected weight** of the particle must be overcome by the **downward drag force** generated by the descending vortex flow. This is captured by the **Critical Velocity Model** (Sahin, 2002; Rohatgi et al.).

### Step 3 — Wetting at the Particle–Melt Interface
Wetting is governed by **Young's Equation** at the three-phase contact line:

$$\gamma_{sv} = \gamma_{sl} + \gamma_{lv}\cos\theta$$

where $\theta$ is the **contact angle**. For spontaneous incorporation, $\theta < 90°$. Most ceramic reinforcements (Al₂O₃, SiC, TiB₂) in molten Al have $\theta > 90°$ in their as-received state — this is the **fundamental wetting problem** of melt stirring.

### Step 4 — Dispersion and De-agglomeration
Once wetted, the particle must be separated from its neighbors. The turbulent kinetic energy of the impeller region must exceed the **inter-particle adhesion energy**. The relevant dimensionless group is the **Reynolds Number**:

$$Re = \frac{\rho_l N D^2}{\mu}$$

- $Re < 10$ → laminar (poor dispersion)
- $10 < Re < 10^4$ → transitional
- $Re > 10^4$ → fully turbulent (good dispersion)

### Step 5 — Prevention of Re-agglomeration and Settling
After the stirrer stops (during solidification), particles are subject to **Stokes settling**. To keep them suspended until the alloy solidifies, the **settling velocity** must be smaller than the **solidification front velocity** in the direction opposing settling. Alternatively, the melt can be poured into a mold where solidification locks the distribution rapidly.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why melt stirring is "cheap but messy":** It uses inexpensive foundry equipment (no vacuum, no autoclave), but suffers from three inherent defects — (1) gas porosity from entrained air, (2) interfacial reactions (e.g., SiC + Al → Al₄C₃), (3) non-uniform particle distribution due to settling and clustering.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> **CRITICAL FORMATTING RULE:** All absolute values and conditionals inside the table use `\vert` and `\mid` (never the bare `|` symbol) to preserve markdown table integrity.

| # | Equation / Quantity | Symbolic Form | Meaning / Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Contact angle (Young) | $\gamma_{sv} = \gamma_{sl} + \gamma_{lv}\cos\theta$ | Determines whether a particle is spontaneously wet by melt ($\theta < 90°$). |
| 2 | Critical entry velocity | $V_c = \left[ \dfrac{4\,\gamma_{lv}\,d_p}{3\,C_D\,\rho_l} \right]^{1/2}$ | Minimum downward fluid velocity at the vortex surface required to drag a particle of diameter $d_p$ into the melt against surface tension. |
| 3 | Particle settling velocity (Stokes) | $V_s = \dfrac{d_p^{\,2}\,g\,\vert \rho_p - \rho_l \vert}{18\,\mu}$ | Terminal velocity of a single sphere in a viscous fluid; sets the time window before settling ruins distribution. |
| 4 | Impeller Reynolds number | $Re = \dfrac{\rho_l\,N\,D_i^{\,2}}{\mu}$ | $\rho_l$ = melt density, $N$ = stirrer speed, $D_i$ = impeller diameter. $Re > 10^4$ ⇒ turbulent mixing. |
| 5 | Froude number of impeller | $Fr = \dfrac{N^{\,2}\,D_i}{g}$ | $Fr > 0.1$ typically required for stable vortex formation. |
| 6 | Power number (Newton) | $P = N_p\,\rho_l\,N^{\,3}\,D_i^{\,5}$ | Power draw of the impeller; $N_p$ depends on impeller geometry (≈5–6 for a 4-blade paddle). |
| 7 | Bond number | $Bo = \dfrac{\rho_l\,g\,L^{\,2}}{\gamma_{lv}}$ | Ratio of gravitational to surface-tension forces at the free surface. |
| 8 | Capillary velocity | $V_{cap} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2\,\gamma_{lv}}{\rho_l\,d_p}}$ | Velocity scale above which surface-tension effects become negligible compared to inertial drag. |
| 9 | Incorporation criterion | $V_{down} \geq V_c \;\;\text{AND}\;\; \theta < 90°$ | Combined condition for successful particle entry into melt. |
| 10 | Maximum volume fraction (empirical) | $V_f^{\max} \approx 0.3$ | Practical upper bound for stir-cast MMCs in conventional setups. |

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Melt stirring is the **workhorse of low-cost MMC production**, especially in:

- **Automotive:** Al–SiC brake rotors, Al–Al₂O₃ pistons, Mg–SiC gearbox housings.
- **Aerospace (secondary):** Al–TiB₂ grain-refined billets (in-situ route, related family).
- **Electronics:** Al–SiC electronic packaging substrates (matched CTE with chips).
- **Tribology:** Al–SiC wear-resistant liners for slurry pipelines.

The key engineering advantage is **scalability** — a 500-kg foundry furnace can be adapted with a stirrer, whereas methods like squeeze casting or spray deposition require capital-intensive custom rigs.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Critical Particle Entry Velocity (Force-Balance Model)

**Statement:** A ceramic particle resting at the free surface of the melt is subjected to gravity, buoyancy, surface tension, and hydrodynamic drag. The minimum downward fluid velocity (at the vortex surface) required to overcome these resisting forces and drag the particle into the melt is called the **Critical Entry Velocity, $V_c$**.

**Setting up the force balance (at the threshold of engulfment):**

Forces tending to keep the particle at the surface:
- Effective weight (gravity minus buoyancy): $F_g = (\pi/6)\,d_p^{\,3}\,(\rho_p - \rho_l)\,g$
- Surface-tension force at the three-phase line: $F_\sigma = \pi\,d_p\,\gamma_{lv}\,\sin\theta$
  (The $\sin\theta$ projection accounts for the contact angle; at $\theta = 90°$ this is maximum.)

Forces tending to drag the particle down:
- Hydrodynamic drag: $F_D = (1/2)\,C_D\,\rho_l\,V^{\,2}\,(\pi/4)\,d_p^{\,2}$

> At the **critical condition**, drag exactly balances the resistance:

$$\begin{aligned}
F_D &= F_g + F_\sigma \\[6pt]
\tfrac{1}{2}\,C_D\,\rho_l\,V_c^{\,2}\,\tfrac{\pi}{4}\,d_p^{\,2} \;=\; \tfrac{\pi}{6}\,d_p^{\,3}\,(\rho_p - \rho_l)\,g \;+\; \pi\,d_p\,\gamma_{lv}\,\sin\theta
\end{aligned}$$

> **Step 1 — Multiply both sides by $4/(\pi d_p^{\,2})$:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\tfrac{1}{2}\,C_D\,\rho_l\,V_c^{\,2} \;=\; \tfrac{2}{3}\,d_p\,(\rho_p - \rho_l)\,g \;+\; \tfrac{4\,\gamma_{lv}\,\sin\theta}{d_p}
\end{aligned}$$

> **Step 2 — In the dominant case (small dense particles, $\theta \to 180°$ when not wetted), the gravity term can be dropped (it is an order of magnitude smaller than the surface-tension term for $d_p \sim 10\;\mu m$).** The expression simplifies to:

$$\begin{aligned}
\tfrac{1}{2}\,C_D\,\rho_l\,V_c^{\,2} &\approx \tfrac{4\,\gamma_{lv}}{d_p} \\[6pt]
V_c^{\,2} &= \tfrac{8\,\gamma_{lv}}{C_D\,\rho_l\,d_p} \\[6pt]
V_c &= \sqrt{\dfrac{8\,\gamma_{lv}}{C_D\,\rho_l\,d_p}}
\end{aligned}$$

> **Step 3 — Some authors absorb the numerical prefactor into a modified drag coefficient and report the form:**

$$\boxed{\;V_c \;\approx\; \left[\dfrac{4\,\gamma_{lv}\,d_p}{C_D\,\rho_l}\right]^{1/2}\;}$$

These two forms differ by a factor of $\sqrt{2\,d_p^{\,2}}$ vs $d_p^{\,1}$ scaling — they are valid in different limits (gravity-dominated vs surface-tension-dominated). KTU examiners usually accept either if the limiting assumptions are stated.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Stokes Settling Velocity (Stability of Suspension)

**Statement:** A particle dispersed in a melt will sink (if $\rho_p > \rho_l$) or float (if $\rho_p < \rho_l$) under gravity at a terminal velocity given by Stokes' law, provided the flow around the particle remains laminar (low Reynolds number, $Re_p < 0.1$).

**Starting point:** At terminal velocity, drag = buoyancy-corrected weight.

$$\begin{aligned}
F_D &= F_g \\[6pt]
3\pi\mu d_p V_s &= \tfrac{\pi}{6}\,d_p^{\,3}\,\vert \rho_p - \rho_l \vert\,g
\end{aligned}$$

> **Step 1 — Solve for $V_s$:**

$$\begin{aligned}
V_s &= \dfrac{d_p^{\,3}\,\vert \rho_p - \rho_l \vert\,g}{18\,\mu\,d_p} \\[6pt]
V_s &= \dfrac{d_p^{\,2}\,\vert \rho_p - \rho_l \vert\,g}{18\,\mu}
\end{aligned}$$

> **Step 2 — Validity check (Stokes regime):**

$$Re_p = \dfrac{\rho_l V_s d_p}{\mu} = \dfrac{\rho_l\,d_p^{\,3}\,\vert \rho_p - \rho_l \vert\,g}{18\,\mu^{\,2}} < 0.1$$

> For Al-melt at $700\,°C$ ($\mu \approx 1.1 \times 10^{-3}\;\text{Pa·s}$, $\rho_l \approx 2400\;\text{kg/m}^3$) and SiC particles ($\rho_p \approx 3210\;\text{kg/m}^3$, $d_p = 30\;\mu m$):

$$\begin{aligned}
V_s &= \dfrac{(30\times10^{-6})^{2}\,\vert 3210 - 2400 \vert\,(9.81)}{18\,(1.1\times10^{-3})} \\[6pt]
&= \dfrac{(9\times10^{-10})\,(810)\,(9.81)}{1.98\times10^{-2}} \\[6pt]
&= \dfrac{7.15\times10^{-6}}{1.98\times10^{-2}} \\[6pt]
&\approx 3.6\times10^{-4}\;\text{m/s}
\end{aligned}$$

> This settling rate corresponds to ~1.3 m per hour — meaning a 30-cm deep melt loses most of its SiC within ~15–20 minutes if stirring stops. **This is why solidification must be rapid post-stirring.**

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Critical Stirrer Speed for Vortex Formation

**Statement:** For a given impeller and crucible geometry, there is a minimum stirrer speed $N_{cr}$ below which the vortex is too shallow to accept particles. This is derived by equating the **centrifugal head** to the **hydrostatic head**.

The pressure drop at the vortex core (radius $r$) due to forced vortex flow is:

$$\Delta P = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho_l\,\omega^{\,2}\,r^{\,2} = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho_l\,(2\pi N)^{\,2}\,r^{\,2}$$

This pressure drop depresses the free surface by a depth $h_v$ such that $\Delta P = \rho_l\,g\,h_v$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\rho_l\,g\,h_v &= \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho_l\,(2\pi N)^{\,2}\,r^{\,2} \\[6pt]
h_v &= \dfrac{2\pi^{\,2}\,N^{\,2}\,r^{\,2}}{g}
\end{aligned}$$

For the vortex to reach the impeller radius $r \approx R_i/2$ and remain stable:

$$\boxed{\;N_{cr} \;\approx\; \dfrac{1}{2\pi\,R_i}\sqrt{2\,g\,h_{desired}}\;}$$

> **Practical rule-of-thumb:** Most stir-casting operations run at $N = (1.5 - 2.5)\times N_{cr}$ to ensure a vigorous but non-splashing vortex.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Effect of Process Variables

| Variable ↑ (Increase) | Effect on $V_c$ | Effect on $V_s$ | Effect on $h_v$ | Net Quality Outcome |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Particle size $d_p$ ↑ | $\propto d_p^{1/2}$ ↑ | $\propto d_p^{2}$ ↑↑ | No direct effect | Larger particles settle faster; medium size (~20–40 µm) is optimum. |
| Stirrer speed $N$ ↑ | No direct effect | No direct effect | $\propto N^{2}$ ↑↑ | Deeper vortex ⇒ easier particle entry, BUT excessive $N$ entrains gas. |
| Melt viscosity $\mu$ ↓ (hotter melt) | No direct effect | $\propto 1/\mu$ ↑ | No direct effect (indirect via Re) | Lower viscosity helps dispersion, but reactivity with reinforcement also rises. |
| Pre-heating particles ↑ | $\theta$ ↓ (better wetting) | No direct effect | No direct effect | Reduces thermal shock and improves wetting kinetics. |
| Contact angle $\theta$ ↓ (better wetting) | $\propto \sin\theta$ ↓ | No direct effect | No direct effect | Particles enter more easily; use Mg-addition or K₂TiF₆ flux to improve wetting of Al/SiC. |
| Volume fraction $V_f$ ↑ | No direct effect | No direct effect | No direct effect | Effective viscosity $\mu_{eff} \uparrow$, so required $N$ rises; clustering tendency ↑. |

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Practice Standard)

**Problem:** SiC particles ($d_p = 20\;\mu m$, $\rho_p = 3210\;\text{kg/m}^3$, $\theta = 130°$) are to be incorporated into molten Al ($\rho_l = 2385\;\text{kg/m}^3$, $\gamma_{lv} = 0.86\;\text{N/m}$, $g = 9.81\;\text{m/s}^2$). Take $C_D = 0.4$ (typical for a small particle entering a turbulent free surface).

**Find:** (a) The critical entry velocity $V_c$. (b) The settling velocity $V_s$ at $700\,°C$ ($\mu = 1.1\times10^{-3}\;\text{Pa·s}$). (c) Comment on whether stir-casting at $N = 600\;\text{rpm}$ with a 50-mm-radius impeller is feasible.

**Solution:**

**(a)** Using the surface-tension-dominated form:

$$\begin{aligned}
V_c &= \sqrt{\dfrac{8\,\gamma_{lv}}{C_D\,\rho_l\,d_p}} \\[6pt]
&= \sqrt{\dfrac{8 \times 0.86}{0.4 \times 2385 \times 20\times10^{-6}}} \\[6pt]
&= \sqrt{\dfrac{6.88}{0.01908}} \\[6pt]
&= \sqrt{360.6} \\[6pt]
&\approx 18.99\;\text{m/s}
\end{aligned}$$

> This is the **minimum surface-fluid velocity** at the vortex. Actual values achieved by impeller-driven flows typically exceed this easily.

**(b)** Using Stokes' law:

$$\begin{aligned}
V_s &= \dfrac{d_p^{\,2}\,\vert \rho_p - \rho_l \vert\,g}{18\,\mu} \\[6pt]
&= \dfrac{(20\times10^{-6})^{2}\,\vert 3210 - 2385 \vert\,(9.81)}{18\,(1.1\times10^{-3})} \\[6pt]
&= \dfrac{(4\times10^{-10})\,(825)\,(9.81)}{1.98\times10^{-2}} \\[6pt]
&\approx 1.64\times10^{-4}\;\text{m/s} \;\; \approx \; 0.59\;\text{m/hr}
\end{aligned}$$

**(c)** Critical stirrer speed (with desired vortex depth $h = 25\;\text{mm}$):

$$\begin{aligned}
N_{cr} &= \dfrac{1}{2\pi\,(0.05)}\sqrt{2 \times 9.81 \times 0.025} \\[6pt]
&= \dfrac{1}{0.3142}\sqrt{0.4905} \\[6pt]
&\approx \dfrac{0.7004}{0.3142} \;\approx\; 2.23\;\text{rev/s} \;\approx\; 134\;\text{rpm}
\end{aligned}$$

> The operating speed of **600 rpm** is roughly **4.5× the critical**, which falls in the recommended $(1.5-2.5)\times N_{cr}$ window only if vortex depth is monitored — at 4.5× the depth, gas entrainment becomes likely. **Recommended: 250–350 rpm for this geometry.**
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Apparatus Schematic — Mechanical Stir Casting Rig

The figure below maps the **functional architecture** of a typical bottom-pour or top-pour stir-casting rig. Because a true physical cross-section would require complex drawing tools, the Mermaid block presents a **block-level functional flow** of all interacting sub-systems, including the **feedback control loop** between sensors and the variable-frequency drive (VFD).

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    node1["Crucible Furnace (Resistance Heated, Graphite Lined)"] --> node2["Molten Metal Pool (Temp 600 to 800 C)"]
    node2 --> node3["Impeller Shaft (Graphite or Coated Steel)"]
    node3 --> node4["Two or Four Blade Paddle (Tilted 30 to 45 deg)"]
    node4 --> node5["Forced Vortex at Free Surface"]
    node5 --> node6["Particle Feeder (Vibratory Hopper)"]
    node6 --> node7["Pre Heated Ceramic Particles (200 to 400 C)"]
    node7 --> node5
    node2 --> node8["Thermocouple Type K (Melt Temperature Sensor)"]
    node2 --> node9["Inert Gas Blanket (Argon Purging)"]
    node8 --> node10["PID Temperature Controller"]
    node10 --> node1
    node4 --> node11["Variable Frequency Drive (VFD)"]
    node11 --> node12["Stirrer Speed Display (rpm)"]
    node3 --> node13["Tachometer Feedback"]
    node13 --> node11
    node2 --> node14["Bottom Pour Nozzle or Ladle Skim"]
    node14 --> node15["Pre Heated Metallic Mold"]
    node15 --> node16["Composite Ingot or Casting"]
```

## 4.2 Process Flow Diagram — Sequential Operations

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    sA["Stage A: Charge Melting"] --> sB["Stage B: Degassing with Argon"]
    sB --> sC["Stage C: Deslagging of Oxide Layer"]
    sC --> sD["Stage D: Pre heating of Ceramic Particles"]
    sD --> sE["Stage E: Lower Impeller and Initiate Stirring"]
    sE --> sF["Stage F: Particle Addition via Vibratory Feeder"]
    sF --> sG["Stage G: Continuous Stirring for 5 to 30 min"]
    sG --> sH["Stage H: Withdrawal of Stirrer and Skimming"]
    sH --> sI["Stage I: Pouring into Pre heated Mold"]
    sI --> sJ["Stage J: Solidification under Controlled Cooling"]
    sJ --> sK["Stage K: Demolding and Post Process Machining"]
```

## 4.3 Comparison Block — Mechanical vs Electromagnetic Stirring

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    comp["Melt Stirring Family"] --> mech["Mechanical Stir Casting"]
    comp --> emag["Electromagnetic Stirring"]
    mech --> mechA["Energy Input: Impeller Rotation"]
    mech --> mechB["Pros: Simple, Low Cost, High Local Turbulence"]
    mech --> mechC["Cons: Blade Erosion, Fe Contamination, Gas Entrapment"]
    emag --> emagA["Energy Input: Lorentz Force J x B"]
    emag --> emagB["Pros: No Moving Parts, Contamination Free"]
    emag --> emagC["Cons: Lower Local Shear, Expensive Coils, Heat from Coils"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams for KTU Exams:** Examiners often ask students to *redraw a labeled schematic of a stir-casting setup*. Memorize the **nine essential labels**: (1) Crucible, (2) Furnace shell, (3) Heating element, (4) Impeller, (5) Shaft, (6) Motor with VFD, (7) Thermocouple, (8) Particle feeder, (9) Inert-gas inlet. Each label is worth approximately 0.5–1 mark.

## 4.4 Particle Incorporation Micro-Mechanism (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    p1["Particle Approaches Vortex Surface"] --> p2{"Wettability Condition theta less than 90?"}
    p2 -- No --> p3["Use Flux Addition Mg or K2TiF6 to Modify theta"]
    p3 --> p2
    p2 -- Yes --> p4{"Vortex Velocity exceeds Vc?"}
    p4 -- No --> p5["Increase Stirrer Speed N"]
    p5 --> p4
    p4 -- Yes --> p6["Particle Engulfed into Bulk Melt"]
    p6 --> p7["De agglomeration via Turbulent Shear Re greater than 10 to the 4"]
    p7 --> p8["Homogeneous Suspension Achieved"]
    p8 --> p9["Rapid Solidification to Lock Distribution"]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Q: Define melt stirring and name two reinforcement materials commonly incorporated by this route.**

**Model Answer:**
Melt stirring is a liquid-state composite fabrication technique in which pre-heated ceramic particles are mechanically dispersed into a molten metal alloy through forced convection created by a rotating impeller. The two reinforcements most commonly incorporated via melt stirring are:
1. **Silicon Carbide (SiC)** — particulates for Al-matrix MMCs.
2. **Alumina (Al₂O₃)** — particulates for wear-resistant Al-matrix MMCs.

Other acceptable answers: TiB₂, B₄C, fly ash, graphite.

**Mark Distribution (Examiner Pattern):**
- Definition with mechanism: **2 marks**
- Two correct reinforcements: **1 mark**

---

### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Q: List any three process parameters that critically affect the quality of a stir-cast MMC.**

**Model Answer:**
The three most critical process parameters are:
1. **Stirring speed (N)** — controls vortex depth and turbulence (typical: 300–1000 rpm).
2. **Melt temperature** — controls viscosity and interfacial reactivity (typically 50–100 °C above liquidus).
3. **Reinforcement pre-heating temperature** — affects wetting and prevents thermal shock (typically 200–400 °C).

**Acceptable alternatives:** stirring time, particle size, blade design, holding time, volume fraction.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the **stages of melt stirring** for fabricating an Al–SiC particulate MMC with a neat labeled diagram. Discuss the role of **vortex formation** and **wettability**. (7 marks)

**(b)** Derive the expression for the **critical particle entry velocity** at the melt free surface, starting from a force-balance on the particle. (7 marks)

---

### Model Solution — Question A(a)

**Stages of Melt Stirring (for Al–SiC MMC):**

1. **Stage 1 — Melting and Degassing:** The Al alloy (e.g., A356 or AA6061) is melted in a graphite-lined crucible at 720–760 °C. Hexachloroethane (C₂Cl₆) or argon is bubbled to remove dissolved hydrogen.

2. **Stage 2 — Pre-heating of Reinforcement:** SiC particles (typically 10–40 µm) are pre-heated to 300–400 °C to drive off adsorbed moisture and to reduce the thermal shock upon contact with the melt. This step is critical because moisture otherwise reacts explosively with molten Al.

3. **Stage 3 — Vortex Generation:** The impeller (coated steel or graphite) is lowered into the melt and rotated at 300–600 rpm. The forced vortex depresses the free surface into a funnel shape. The depth of this vortex must be controlled: too shallow and particles float; too deep and air is entrained.

4. **Stage 4 — Particle Addition:** Pre-heated SiC particles are sprinkled through a vibratory feeder onto the surface of the vortex at a controlled rate (typically 10–30 g/min) to avoid cluster formation.

5. **Stage 5 — Continuous Stirring:** Stirring continues for 10–25 minutes to achieve homogeneous distribution. Magnesium (1–2 wt%) may be added to improve wetting by lowering the contact angle.

6. **Stage 6 — Skimming and Pouring:** The stirrer is withdrawn, oxide slag is skimmed off, and the composite melt is poured into a pre-heated metallic or sand mold.

7. **Stage 7 — Solidification:** Controlled solidification locks the particle distribution. Cooling rate must be high enough to prevent SiC settling, but not so high as to introduce thermal stresses.

**Role of Vortex:**
- Acts as a **particle-entry window**, dragging particles below the free surface.
- Generates **turbulent kinetic energy** that breaks particle clusters.
- Promotes **uniform distribution** through convective mixing.

**Role of Wettability:**
- Wetting is governed by Young's equation; $\theta < 90°$ is needed for spontaneous entry.
- Poor wetting causes particles to float on top ("dross" formation).
- Wettability is enhanced by **(i) Mg addition**, **(ii) K₂TiF₆ flux coating**, **(iii) pre-heating particles**, and **(iv) using Al₂O₃ instead of bare SiC in some cases**.

**Mark Allocation:**
- Six stages described: **3 marks**
- Vortex role explained: **2 marks**
- Wettability role + enhancement methods: **2 marks**

---

### Model Solution — Question A(b)

The derivation follows the same path as **Section 3.1** above. Key steps to be presented:

1. Draw a free-body diagram of the particle at the free surface.
2. Identify three forces: gravity–buoyancy $F_g$, surface-tension $F_\sigma$, hydrodynamic drag $F_D$.
3. Write force equilibrium at the threshold of engulfment.
4. Substitute expressions for each force (using sphere geometry).
5. Simplify by considering the dominant regime (surface-tension dominated for $\mu m$-scale particles).
6. Arrive at the final boxed expression $V_c = \left[\dfrac{4\,\gamma_{lv}\,d_p}{C_D\,\rho_l}\right]^{1/2}$.
7. Discuss physical meaning: $V_c$ increases with $\gamma_{lv}$ and $d_p$, decreases with $\rho_l$ and $C_D$.

**Mark Allocation:**
- Free-body diagram: **2 marks**
- Force expressions: **2 marks**
- Algebraic manipulation: **2 marks**
- Final boxed formula + interpretation: **1 mark**

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` *(Alternative Choice)*

**(a)** Discuss the **various defects** encountered in stir-cast MMCs and the **remedial measures** adopted to overcome them. (7 marks)

**(b)** A batch of molten aluminum at **720 °C** is to be reinforced with **20 µm SiC particles**. Given: $\rho_{Al} = 2385\;\text{kg/m}^3$, $\rho_{SiC} = 3210\;\text{kg/m}^3$, $\mu_{Al}(720\,°C) = 1.05\times10^{-3}\;\text{Pa·s}$, $g = 9.81\;\text{m/s}^2$. Calculate the **settling velocity** of the SiC particles. Comment on the **maximum holding time** for a 30-cm-deep crucible. (7 marks)

---

### Model Solution — Question B(a)

**Defects in Stir-Cast MMCs:**

| # | Defect | Cause | Remedial Measure |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Gas porosity** | Air entrained by vortex; dissolved H₂ from moisture | Argon degassing; vacuum-assisted stirring; controlled vortex depth ($N \le 2.5\,N_{cr}$) |
| 2 | **Particle clustering** | Inadequate wetting; high feeding rate; insufficient stirring | Pre-heating particles; Mg addition; slower feeding rate; longer stirring time |
| 3 | **Settling / segregation** | Density mismatch ($\rho_{SiC} > \rho_{Al}$); long solidification time | Rapid solidification (chill mold); high cooling rate; reduce holding time |
| 4 | **Interfacial reactions** (e.g., SiC + Al → Al₄C₃ at >750 °C) | Excessive melt temperature; long contact time | Use SiO₂-coated SiC; keep melt T below 720 °C; minimize holding time |
| 5 | **Slag / dross inclusions** | Oxide films folded into melt | Skim carefully; use flux cover; argon blanket |
| 6 | **Particle fracture** | High impeller shear at high N | Lower N; use larger-diameter, lower-speed impellers; softer paddles |
| 7 | **Impurity pickup (Fe in Al)** | Steel impeller dissolution | Use graphite impellers or coated (aluminized) steel |

**Mark Allocation:**
- Any 5 defects with causes + remedies: **5 marks** (1 mark each)
- Logical presentation and additional defects (e.g., hot tearing): **2 marks**

---

### Model Solution — Question B(b)

Using Stokes' law:

$$V_s = \dfrac{d_p^{\,2}\,\vert \rho_p - \rho_l \vert\,g}{18\,\mu}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
V_s &= \dfrac{(20\times10^{-6})^{2}\,\vert 3210 - 2385 \vert\,(9.81)}{18\,(1.05\times10^{-3})} \\[6pt]
&= \dfrac{(4\times10^{-10})\,(825)\,(9.81)}{1.89\times10^{-2}} \\[6pt]
&= \dfrac{3.237\times10^{-6}}{1.89\times10^{-2}} \\[6pt]
&\approx 1.71\times10^{-4}\;\text{m/s}
\end{aligned}$$

**Maximum Holding Time for 30-cm crucible (assuming particles start from top and travel entire depth):**

$$\begin{aligned}
t_{max} &= \dfrac{0.30}{1.71\times10^{-4}} \;\approx\; 1754\;\text{s} \;\approx\; 29.2\;\text{minutes}
\end{aligned}$$

**Engineering Comment:**
- This is an **idealized** upper bound assuming single-particle Stokes settling.
- In reality, the **effective viscosity of a 15–20 vol% SiC suspension is 3–5× higher** than pure Al melt, reducing $V_s$ by a similar factor.
- However, **clustered particles settle much faster** (hindered settling exponent).
- **Practical safe holding time: 10–15 minutes** after stirring stops, before pouring.

**Mark Allocation:**
- Correct formula selection: **2 marks**
- Numerical substitution: **2 marks**
- Correct answer $V_s \approx 1.7\times10^{-4}\;\text{m/s}$: **1 mark**
- Maximum holding time calculation: **1 mark**
- Engineering comment / critical discussion: **1 mark**

---

## 5.3 Examiner's Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Commonly Lose Marks on Melt-Stirring Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing "wettability" with "wetting agent":** Wettability is the *property* (characterized by $\theta$). Wetting agents (Mg, K₂TiF₆) are the *means to improve it*. Examiners deduct marks if these are used interchangeably.
> 2. **Forgetting the condition for Stokes' law:** Many students apply $V_s = d_p^2 \Delta\rho\, g/(18\mu)$ for **millimeter-sized** particles where the formula is invalid. Always state $Re_p < 0.1$ and laminar flow.
> 3. **Ignoring the gas-porosity defect:** When asked "what are the limitations of stir casting?", students list only wetting and clustering. **Gas porosity is the single biggest defect** in production — examiners reward this awareness.
> 4. **Mixing up $V_c$ and $V_s$:** Critical velocity $V_c$ is a *surface-entry* condition; settling velocity $V_s$ is a *bulk-suspension* condition. They use different force balances.
> 5. **Drawing impeller in the wrong orientation:** A flat-blade paddle drawn *horizontally* at the surface only skims. It must be *submerged* and angled 30–45° for downward pumping.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Bullet-Point Density):**

- ☐ **Melt stirring = liquid-state MMC fabrication by impeller-driven particle incorporation.**
- ☐ **Reinforcements used:** SiC, Al₂O₃, TiB₂, B₄C; matrices: Al, Mg, Cu alloys.
- ☐ **Maximum $V_f$ achievable:** ~15–30% (beyond this, melt becomes unworkable).
- ☐ **Five physical phenomena:** vortex formation → particle introduction → wetting → de-agglomeration → prevention of settling.
- ☐ **Wetting criterion:** $\theta < 90°$ for spontaneous entry; use Mg or K₂TiF₆ to modify.
- ☐ **Critical entry velocity:** $V_c = \left[4\gamma_{lv}d_p/(C_D\rho_l)\right]^{1/2}$ — surface-tension-dominated.
- ☐ **Stokes settling velocity:** $V_s = d_p^{\,2}\,\vert\rho_p - \rho_l\vert\,g/(18\mu)$ — applicable when $Re_p < 0.1$.
- ☐ **Critical stirrer speed:** $N_{cr} \approx (2\pi R_i)^{-1}\sqrt{2gh_{desired}}$; practical operating $N = 1.5-2.5\times N_{cr}$.
- ☐ **Vortex quality indicator:** $Fr > 0.1$ (Froude number); $Re > 10^4$ (Reynolds number) for turbulent mixing.
- ☐ **Major defects:** gas porosity, clustering, settling, interfacial reactions (Al₄C₃ formation in Al/SiC), Fe contamination, dross.
- ☐ **Standard process parameters (Al/SiC):** 700–760 °C melt T, 300–600 rpm, 10–25 min stirring, 200–400 °C particle pre-heat.
- ☐ **Family cousins:** compocasting (semi-solid), squeeze casting (pressurized infiltration), spray casting (atomization) — keep distinct.
- ☐ **Cost advantage:** melt stirring reuses foundry infrastructure — cheapest MMC route (~₹200–500/kg vs ₹2000+ for spray cast).
- ☐ **Industry applications:** brake rotors, pistons, cylinder liners, electronic packaging, wear plates.
- ☐ **Master Equation Pair:** $\boxed{\;V_c\;}$ (entry) and $\boxed{\;V_s\;}$ (settling) — examiners love to ask them side-by-side.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Memory Mnemonic — "W-V-D-S-S":**
> **W**etting → **V**ortex → **D**ispersion → **S**tabilization → **S**olidification.
> These are the five stages in order; remember the chain and you can answer almost any process-flow question.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Squeeze casting
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT412 - COMPOSITE MATERIALS AND CERAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Squeeze casting

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Squeeze Casting — Core Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Squeeze casting** (also called *liquid metal forging* or *pressure crystallization*) is a hybrid near-net-shape manufacturing process that combines the features of **casting** and **die forging** in a single operation. Molten or semi-solid metal is poured into a preheated, reusable hardened-steel die, and **high hydrostatic pressure (typically 50–150 MPa)** is applied and maintained throughout solidification. The pressure eliminates gas porosity, suppresses shrinkage cavities, and—critically for composites—drives the liquid matrix to infiltrate a porous preform of fibers, whiskers, or particulates.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Focus (Module 3):** Squeeze casting is studied under *Processing of Metal Matrix Composites (MMCs)* because it is one of the **two most industrially important liquid-state routes** (the other being stir casting). The examiner expects you to know the process, its variants, process parameters, and its role in MMC fabrication.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Squeezed Orange" Model

Imagine cutting an orange in half and squeezing it while the juice is still inside. The juice has nowhere to go, so it fills every microscopic pocket of pulp completely. Now replace the orange pulp with a bundle of **glass fibers** and the juice with **molten aluminium**. When you "squeeze" the liquid aluminium at high pressure:

- The aluminium is **forced into every void** between the fibers (perfect infiltration).
- The fibres are **mechanically locked** in place once the aluminium freezes.
- The resulting composite has **virtually zero porosity**.

That, in essence, is squeeze casting of an MMC.

## 1.3 Key Process Metrics (Bolded for Memory)

| Parameter | Typical Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Applied pressure | **50 – 150** | **MPa** |
| Die (tool) temperature | **250 – 350** | **°C** |
| Pour temperature (above liquidus) | **50 – 100** | **°C** superheat |
| Dwell (holding) time | **30 – 120** | **seconds** |
| Volume fraction of reinforcement (V_f) | **0.10 – 0.40** | — |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Reference Pressure (KPI):** The most commonly cited benchmark in KTU textbooks for MMC squeeze casting is **~100 MPa**, applied for **~60 s**, with a die temperature of **~300 °C**.

## 1.4 Position in the MMC Processing Family

Squeeze casting is a **liquid-state processing route** for MMCs, sitting between **stir casting** (low pressure, particulate MMC) and **squeeze infiltration** of preforms (high pressure, fiber-reinforced MMC). The classification is:

- **Solid-state processing:** Diffusion bonding, powder metallurgy, roll bonding.
- **Liquid-state processing:** Stir casting, **squeeze casting**, spray forming, compocasting.
- **In-situ processing:** Reactive synthesis (e.g., Lanxide™ process).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Sequence (Step-by-Step)

The squeeze casting process executes in **seven ordered steps**:

1. **Preform preparation** — A porous preform (fibers/short fibers/whiskers/particulates bonded with a small amount of organic or inorganic binder) is made separately by pressing, sintering, or paper-making techniques.
2. **Die preheating** — The die is heated to **250–350 °C** to prevent thermal shock, premature solidification ("chill zone"), and to maintain laminar flow of melt.
3. **Preform placement** — The preform is loaded into the lower die half and located precisely.
4. **Pouring** — Molten metal (degassed, deoxidised) is poured directly onto/around the preform at the required superheat.
5. **Press closure & pressurization** — The upper punch descends and applies pressure (50–150 MPa) within **1–2 seconds** of pouring.
6. **Dwell under pressure** — Pressure is held until **complete solidification** is achieved. This is the heart of the process.
7. **Ejection & trimming** — The punch retracts, the part is ejected, and any flash is trimmed.

> [!TIP]
> **"Why pressurize during solidification?"** Two reasons dominate the KTU marking scheme: (i) it compensates for the **volumetric shrinkage** of the metal (~3–6 % for Al alloys) and (ii) it forces the liquid to **infiltrate sub-micron pores** in the preform against the capillary and viscous resistance.

## 2.2 The Two Principal Variants

### 2.2.1 Direct Squeeze Casting
- Molten metal is poured into the **lower die**; the **upper punch directly contacts** the melt.
- Pressure transmission is **near-instantaneous and uniform**.
- Used for **simple shapes** (cylindrical bearings, pistons, brake drums).
- **Higher mechanical properties** due to direct forging action on solidifying shell.

### 2.2.2 Indirect Squeeze Casting
- Molten metal enters a **shot sleeve**, then is pushed through a **gate/runner** into a closed die cavity containing the preform.
- Pressure is transmitted through the **gating system**, so there is some pressure loss.
- Used for **complex shapes** with multiple cavities (e.g., automotive suspension arms).
- Lower punch load required; tooling is more expensive.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Frequently Asked:** "Differentiate direct and indirect squeeze casting." Memorise the table below:

| Feature | Direct | Indirect |
|---|---|---|
| Melt location | Lower die cavity | Shot sleeve |
| Pressure application | Direct punch on melt | Through runner system |
| Pressure loss | Negligible | 10–30 % |
| Shape complexity | Low | High |
| Tooling cost | Lower | Higher |
| Typical product | Bearing, piston | Connecting rod, knuckle |

## 2.3 Process Parameters — The "Five Knobs"

A KTU examiner will always expect you to list and explain the **five governing parameters** that control the quality of a squeeze-cast MMC:

1. **Applied pressure (P)** — Higher P → lower porosity, deeper preform infiltration, refined grain.
2. **Die temperature (T_d)** — Too low → chill, incomplete infiltration. Too high → die softening, longer cycle.
3. **Melt superheat (ΔT)** — Higher superheat → longer solidification time, more reaction between melt and reinforcement.
4. **Dwell time (t_d)** — Must exceed total solidification time; otherwise porosity returns.
5. **Preform temperature (T_p)** — Often preheated close to melt temperature to minimise thermal mismatch cracking of fibers (e.g., Al₂O₃).

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these equations — they account for > 60 % of marks in any numerical/long-answer question on squeeze casting.**

| # | Formula | Meaning of Symbols | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $V_f = \dfrac{V_r}{V_c}$ | $V_f$ = volume fraction of reinforcement; $V_r$ = volume of reinforcement; $V_c$ = total composite volume | Composition control |
| 2 | $W_f = \dfrac{\rho_r V_r}{\rho_r V_r + \rho_m V_m}$ | $W_f$ = weight fraction; $\rho_r, \rho_m$ = densities of reinforcement and matrix | Composition control |
| 3 | $t_s = B \left( \dfrac{V}{A} \right)^n$ (Chvorinov's rule) | $t_s$ = solidification time; $B, n$ = constants (n ≈ 2); $V/A$ = modulus | Casting cycle design |
| 4 | $P_c = \dfrac{2 \sigma_{lv} \cos\theta}{r}$ | $P_c$ = capillary pressure; $\sigma_{lv}$ = liquid–vapour surface tension; $\theta$ = contact angle; $r$ = pore radius | Threshold pressure for infiltration |
| 5 | $\dfrac{dP}{dz} = \dfrac{\mu}{K} v$ (Darcy's law) | $\mu$ = viscosity; $K$ = permeability; $v$ = velocity of melt in preform | Preform infiltration analysis |
| 6 | $P_{app} \geq P_c + P_{visc} + P_{th}$ | $P_{app}$ = applied pressure; $P_{visc}$ = viscous pressure drop; $P_{th}$ = metallostatic head | Minimum pressure check |
| 7 | $L_c = \dfrac{\sigma_f \, d}{2 \tau_i}$ | $L_c$ = critical fiber length; $\sigma_f$ = fiber strength; $d$ = fiber diameter; $\tau_i$ = interfacial shear stress | Fiber pull-out / load transfer |
| 8 | $\sigma_c = \sigma_f V_f (1 - \dfrac{L_c}{2L}) + \sigma_m (1 - V_f)$ (Kelly-Tyson) | $L$ = actual fiber length; $\sigma_m$ = matrix stress at composite failure | Composite strength prediction |

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Saver:** In markdown tables, never use the literal pipe `|` for absolute value. We use `\vert` or `\mid` instead. Example: writing $\vert x \vert$ as $\lvert x \rvert$ prevents the table from breaking.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive pistons** — Toyota and Honda have used squeeze-cast Al-Si/Al₂O₃ short-fiber pistons since the 1980s. Result: **higher scuff resistance** and **lower thermal expansion** than unreinforced Al pistons.
- **Brake rotors & drums** — Squeeze-cast Al/SiC particulate MMC rotors (e.g., Duralcan™) are 40–60 % lighter than cast iron with comparable wear life.
- **Aerospace brackets** — Indirect squeeze casting is used for Al/Al₂O₃ continuously-reinforced brackets in helicopter transmission housings.
- **Substrate plates** — Al/SiC electronic substrates for power modules are squeeze-cast for matched coefficient of thermal expansion (CTE) to silicon chips.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — From Chvorinov's Rule to Pressure-Aided Solidification Time

Chvorinov's rule states that the solidification time of a casting is proportional to the square of its (volume / surface area) ratio, called the **modulus (M)**.

$$t_s = B \left( \frac{V}{A} \right)^2 = B \, M^2$$

**Step 1.** Under normal gravity casting, the only driving force for feeding the volumetric shrinkage is the metallostatic head $h \rho_m g$ and the small atmospheric pressure on the riser.

**Step 2.** When external pressure $P_{app}$ is applied, the feeding pressure driving the liquid metal to compensate shrinkage becomes:

$$\Delta P_{feed} = P_{app} + P_{atm} + h \rho_m g - P_{gas}$$

**Step 3.** A higher feeding pressure **suppresses the nucleation of shrinkage pores** and forces interdendritic liquid to flow where it is needed. The empirical relationship (used in KTU textbook treatments) modifies Chvorinov's constant:

$$B_{squeeze} = B_0 \left( \frac{P_{atm}}{P_{app} + P_{atm}} \right)^{0.5}$$

**Step 4.** Substituting back, the **solidification time under squeeze casting** is:

$$t_{s,squeeze} = B_0 \left( \frac{P_{atm}}{P_{app} + P_{atm}} \right)^{0.5} \left( \frac{V}{A} \right)^2$$

**Step 5.** Therefore, increasing $P_{app}$ **reduces the effective solidification time** by a factor of $\left( \frac{P_{atm}}{P_{app} + P_{atm}} \right)^{0.5}$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** For $P_{app} = 100 \, \text{MPa}$ and $P_{atm} = 0.101 \, \text{MPa}$:
>
> $$\frac{t_{s,squeeze}}{t_{s,atm}} = \left( \frac{0.101}{100.101} \right)^{0.5} \approx 0.032$$
>
> So solidification time under squeeze casting is about **3 %** of the gravity-cast value — this is why squeeze-cast parts have **very fine grains**.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Minimum Applied Pressure to Infiltrate a Fibrous Preform

Consider a cylindrical fiber of radius $r_f$ arranged in a hexagonal array inside a preform of porosity $\phi$. The **maximum pore throat radius** between fibers is approximately:

$$r = r_f \left( \sqrt{\frac{2\pi}{3\sqrt{3} \, \phi}} - 1 \right)$$

For typical fiber preforms ($\phi \approx 0.4$ to $0.5$), $r \approx 0.4 \, r_f$.

**Step 1.** The melt will not spontaneously enter the preform if the contact angle $\theta > 90°$ (non-wetting system — true for Al/Al₂O₃ and Al/SiC). The **capillary resistance pressure** that opposes infiltration is:

$$P_c = \frac{2 \sigma_{lv} \cos\theta}{r}$$

**Step 2.** As the melt flows through a preform of height $h$, the **viscous pressure drop** follows Darcy's law. For a fully-saturated preform of permeability $K$:

$$P_{visc} = \frac{\mu \, h \, v}{K}$$

where $v$ is the superficial velocity and $K$ is given by the **Kozeny-Carman equation**:

$$K = \frac{\phi^3}{C \, S^2 (1 - \phi)^2}$$

with $C \approx 5$ (Kozeny constant) and $S$ the specific surface area of the porous medium.

**Step 3.** For the melt to fully infiltrate the preform against these resistances, the applied pressure must satisfy the **gating balance**:

$$\boxed{P_{app} \geq P_c + P_{visc} + \rho_m g \, h}$$

**Step 4.** Numerical demonstration (a KTU-favourite calculation):
- $r = 20 \, \mu\text{m} = 20 \times 10^{-6} \, \text{m}$
- $\sigma_{lv} = 0.9 \, \text{N/m}$ (molten Al at 700 °C)
- $\theta = 140°$ → $\cos\theta = -0.766$
- $h = 0.05 \, \text{m}$ (50 mm preform)
- $\mu = 1.4 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{Pa \cdot s}$
- $v = 0.05 \, \text{m/s}$ (typical)
- $K = 1 \times 10^{-10} \, \text{m}^2$

$$P_c = \frac{2 \times 0.9 \times (-0.766)}{20 \times 10^{-6}} = -6.89 \times 10^{4} \, \text{Pa} = -0.069 \, \text{MPa}$$

Wait — $\cos\theta$ is **negative** (non-wetting), so the capillary term is **negative**, meaning it actually *assists* infiltration slightly when the pressure is applied to the melt from outside. The net external pressure that must be supplied is:

$$P_{ext} = P_{visc} - P_c + \rho_m g h$$

$$P_{visc} = \frac{(1.4 \times 10^{-3})(0.05)(0.05)}{1 \times 10^{-10}} = 3.5 \times 10^{7} \, \text{Pa} = 35 \, \text{MPa}$$

$$\rho_m g h = 2400 \times 9.81 \times 0.05 = 1177 \, \text{Pa} \approx 0.001 \, \text{MPa}$$

$$P_{ext} = 35 - (-0.069) + 0.001 \approx 35.07 \, \text{MPa}$$

**Step 5.** Hence the **minimum applied pressure ≈ 35 MPa**, comfortably below the typical 100 MPa operational range — explaining why squeeze casting reliably infiltrates Al₂O₃ preforms.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Rule of Mixtures Modified for Squeeze-Cast MMC

For a squeeze-cast MMC with discontinuous aligned fibers, the **longitudinal tensile strength** (rule of mixtures, Kelly-Tyson) is:

$$\sigma_{c,L} = \sigma_f \, V_f \left( 1 - \frac{L_c}{2L} \right) + \sigma_m' \, (1 - V_f)$$

The factor $\left( 1 - \dfrac{L_c}{2L} \right)$ accounts for the fact that fibers shorter than $L_c$ cannot be loaded to their fracture stress.

**Numerical example for KTU board-level problem:**
- Al₂O₃ short fibers: $\sigma_f = 2000 \, \text{MPa}$, $d = 10 \, \mu\text{m}$, $L = 3 \, \text{mm}$
- Interfacial shear strength: $\tau_i = 25 \, \text{MPa}$
- $V_f = 0.30$, $\sigma_m' = 180 \, \text{MPa}$ (matrix stress at fiber failure strain)

**Step 1.** Compute critical length:

$$L_c = \frac{\sigma_f \, d}{2 \tau_i} = \frac{2000 \times 10 \times 10^{-6}}{2 \times 25} = 4 \times 10^{-4} \, \text{m} = 0.4 \, \text{mm}$$

**Step 2.** Compute fiber-efficiency factor:

$$1 - \frac{L_c}{2L} = 1 - \frac{0.4}{2 \times 3.0} = 1 - 0.0667 = 0.9333$$

**Step 3.** Composite strength:

$$\sigma_{c,L} = 2000 \times 0.30 \times 0.9333 + 180 \times (1 - 0.30)$$

$$\sigma_{c,L} = 560.0 + 126.0 = 686 \, \text{MPa}$$

> [!TIP]
> This 686 MPa is in the right ballpark for squeeze-cast Al/Al₂O₃ short-fiber MMCs reported in literature (typically 500–750 MPa longitudinal strength).

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Process Parameter → Microstructure → Property

| Process Parameter | Microstructural Effect | Resulting Property Change |
|---|---|---|
| ↑ Applied pressure | Pore closure, grain refinement | ↑ Strength, ↑ ductility, ↑ fatigue life |
| ↑ Die temperature | Slower solidification, more interdiffusion | ↑ Bonding, ↓ chill, may ↑ interfacial reaction |
| ↑ Melt superheat | More time above liquidus | Better infiltration, ↑ risk of fiber degradation |
| ↑ Dwell time | More complete solidification | ↓ shrinkage, ↓ porosity |
| ↑ Preform temperature | Less thermal gradient | ↓ fiber cracking, ↑ interface integrity |
| ↑ $V_f$ | More load-bearing fibers | ↑ stiffness, ↑ strength (only up to limit) |
| ↑ Interfacial reaction | Formation of brittle phases (e.g., Al₄C₃ in Al/SiC) | ↓ strength, ↓ corrosion resistance |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Process Flow — Mermaid Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Prepare Reinforcement Preform] --> B[Heat Die to 250-350 C]
    B --> C[Load Preform into Lower Die Cavity]
    C --> D[Pour Degassed Molten Metal at Required Superheat]
    D --> E[Close Die and Apply Punch Pressure 50-150 MPa within 1-2 s]
    E --> F[Hold Pressure During Solidification - Dwell 30-120 s]
    F --> G[Retract Punch and Open Die]
    G --> H[Eject Squeeze Cast MMC Component]
    H --> I[Trim Flash and Perform NDT]
    I --> J[Final Machining if Required]
    J --> K[End: MMC Part Ready for Heat Treatment and Service]

    style A fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333
    style K fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333
    style E fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333
    style F fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333
```

## 4.2 Direct vs Indirect — Block Comparison Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph DIRECT["DIRECT SQUEEZE CASTING"]
        A1[Pour Melt into Lower Die] --> B1[Preform in Cavity]
        B1 --> C1[Upper Punch Contacts Melt Directly]
        C1 --> D1[Pressure Acts on Solidifying Shell]
        D1 --> E1[Simple Shape, High Pressure Fidelity]
    end

    subgraph INDIRECT["INDIRECT SQUEEZE CASTING"]
        A2[Melt in Shot Sleeve] --> B2[Plunger Pushes Melt]
        B2 --> C2[Runner and Gate System]
        C2 --> D2[Pressure Transmitted Through Gates]
        D2 --> E2[Complex Shape, Some Pressure Loss]
    end

    style DIRECT fill:#E0F2FE,stroke:#0369A1
    style INDIRECT fill:#FEF3C7,stroke:#B45309
```

## 4.3 Infiltration Mechanics — Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Applied External Pressure P_app] --> SUM{Pressure Balance at Melt Front}
    CAP[Capillary Resistance P_c equals 2 sigma cos theta over r] --> SUM
    VIS[Viscous Pressure Drop P_visc equals mu h v over K] --> SUM
    GRAV[Metallostatic Head rho g h] --> SUM

    SUM --> Q1{Is P_app greater than P_c plus P_visc plus rho g h?}
    Q1 -- YES --> OK[Complete Infiltration - Sound MMC]
    Q1 -- NO --> FAIL[Incomplete Infiltration - Porous MMC with Defects]

    style OK fill:#86EFAC,stroke:#16A34A
    style FAIL fill:#FCA5A5,stroke:#DC2626
    style SUM fill:#FDE68A,stroke:#B45309
```

```mermaid

    S0[Stage 0: Molten Matrix with Cold Preform] --> S1[Stage 1: Skin Formation on Preform Fibers]
    S1 --> S2[Stage 2: Pressure-Driven Infiltration of Pore Network]
    S2 --> S3[Stage 3: Dendritic Solidification Under Pressure]
    S3 --> S4[Stage 4: Final Solid State - Densified MMC with Refined Grain]

    style S0 fill:#FECACA,stroke:#7F1D1D
    style S1 fill:#FED7AA,stroke:#9A3412
```
