<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. Define squeeze casting. List any three advantages it offers over conventional gravity die casting.** `[KTU University Exam — May 2023, Model QP]`

**Model Answer (≈ 110 words):**
Squeeze casting is a hybrid liquid-state process in which molten metal is poured into a closed die and **subjected to high pressure (50–150 MPa) throughout solidification**. Compared to gravity die casting, the three principal advantages are: (i) **near-zero porosity** because applied pressure compensates shrinkage and suppresses gas pores; (ii) **superior mechanical properties** owing to refined grain size and elimination of casting defects; (iii) **excellent matrix–reinforcement bonding** in MMCs, since pressure drives the melt into fiber preforms; and (iv) ability to produce **near-net-shape** components with **reduced machining**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Many students write "squeeze casting = injection moulding of metals." It is **NOT** the same. Injection moulding uses a screw/plunger to *inject* melt through a narrow gate; squeeze casting simply *applies pressure from a punch* onto melt already in the die.

---

### **Q2. What is a preform? Why is it preheated before squeeze casting of MMCs?** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]`

**Model Answer (≈ 90 words):**
A **preform** is a shaped, porous body of reinforcement (continuous fibers, short fibers, whiskers, or particles) held together by a small amount of binder. It is preheated (typically to **300–500 °C**, close to the melt temperature) before infiltration for two reasons: (i) to **prevent thermal shock and cracking** of brittle ceramic fibers (e.g., Al₂O₃) when contacted by the much hotter melt; and (ii) to **minimise the thermal gradient** at the preform–melt interface, which improves wetting and infiltration kinetics.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — Jul 2024, Suggested Pattern]`

**(a)** With a neat schematic, describe the **direct squeeze casting** process. List the key process parameters that influence the quality of the component. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A squeeze casting operation is carried out on an Al/SiC composite. The SiC preform has a porosity of **0.45**, fiber diameter **15 µm**, melt viscosity **1.5 × 10⁻³ Pa·s**, preform height **40 mm**, contact angle **135°** between molten Al and SiC, surface tension **0.85 N/m**, and the superficial velocity of melt into the preform is **0.04 m/s**. The preform permeability is **8 × 10⁻¹¹ m²**. Calculate the **minimum applied pressure** required to fully infiltrate the preform. Assume metallostatic head is negligible. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Direct Squeeze Casting — Description (7 marks)**

Direct squeeze casting, also called **vertical squeeze casting**, is a process in which molten metal is poured directly into the lower die half (where the preform has been placed), and the upper punch descends to apply pressure directly on the melt surface.

**Schematic Description (textual representation for the answer sheet):**

- A **lower die** holds the reinforcement preform.
- Molten matrix metal is poured from a ladle into the cavity.
- A **hydraulic upper punch** descends, contacts the melt, and applies **50–150 MPa**.
- Pressure is held for **30–120 s** until solidification completes.
- Punch retracts; component is ejected; flash is trimmed.

**Key Process Parameters (any 5, each 1 mark):**

1. Applied pressure (50–150 MPa)
2. Die temperature (250–350 °C)
3. Pour / melt superheat
4. Dwell / holding time
5. Preform temperature
6. Lubrication and die coating
7. Infiltration velocity / punch speed

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks)**

**Given:**
- $\phi = 0.45$
- $d = 15 \, \mu\text{m} \Rightarrow r_f = 7.5 \, \mu\text{m}$
- $\mu = 1.5 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{Pa \cdot s}$
- $h = 40 \, \text{mm} = 0.04 \, \text{m}$
- $\theta = 135°$, $\sigma_{lv} = 0.85 \, \text{N/m}$
- $v = 0.04 \, \text{m/s}$
- $K = 8 \times 10^{-11} \, \text{m}^2$
- $\rho_m g h$ = negligible

**Step 1. Effective pore-throat radius:**
For a typical fiber preform, $r \approx 0.4 \, r_f = 0.4 \times 7.5 \times 10^{-6} = 3.0 \times 10^{-6} \, \text{m}$.

**Step 2. Capillary pressure:**
$P_c = \dfrac{2 \sigma_{lv} \cos\theta}{r} = \dfrac{2 \times 0.85 \times \cos 135°}{3.0 \times 10^{-6}}$

$$\cos 135° = -0.7071$$

$$P_c = \frac{2 \times 0.85 \times (-0.7071)}{3.0 \times 10^{-6}} = -4.0 \times 10^{5} \, \text{Pa} = -0.40 \, \text{MPa}$$

**Step 3. Viscous pressure drop (Darcy's law):**
$P_{visc} = \dfrac{\mu \, h \, v}{K} = \dfrac{(1.5 \times 10^{-3})(0.04)(0.04)}{8 \times 10^{-11}}$

$$P_{visc} = \frac{2.4 \times 10^{-6}}{8 \times 10^{-11}} = 3.0 \times 10^{4} \, \text{Pa} = 30 \, \text{MPa}$$

**Step 4. Minimum applied pressure (with $\cos\theta$ negative, capillary term assists infiltration):**
$$P_{app,min} = P_{visc} - P_c = 30 - (-0.40) = 30.40 \, \text{MPa}$$

**Answer:** $P_{app,min} \approx 30.4 \, \text{MPa}$. Since squeeze casting routinely uses 50–150 MPa, this system is easily infiltrable.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Suggested Pattern]`

**(a)** Compare **direct and indirect squeeze casting** with respect to (i) pressure transmission, (ii) shape complexity, (iii) tooling cost, and (iv) typical products. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain with sketches how **squeeze casting** is used to fabricate **Al/Al₂O₃ short-fiber reinforced MMC brake drums**. Discuss the role of **pressure** in determining the **tensile strength** of the resulting composite. Use the rule of mixtures to estimate the tensile strength of an Al/Al₂O₃ MMC with $V_f = 0.25$, given $\sigma_f = 1900 \, \text{MPa}$, $L = 3 \, \text{mm}$, $d = 8 \, \mu\text{m}$, $\tau_i = 20 \, \text{MPa}$, and matrix stress at composite failure $\sigma_m' = 165 \, \text{MPa}$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Comparison Table (7 marks — 1.75 each row)**

| Feature | Direct Squeeze Casting | Indirect Squeeze Casting |
|---|---|---|
| (i) Pressure transmission | Direct punch on melt — minimal loss | Through gating — 10–30 % loss |
| (ii) Shape complexity | Simple (cylindrical, prismatic) | Complex (branches, ribs) |
| (iii) Tooling cost | Lower (one die + punch) | Higher (shot sleeve, gates, runners) |
| (iv) Typical products | Pistons, bearing housings, brake drums | Connecting rods, suspension arms |

---

**(b) Fabrication & Strength of Al/Al₂O₃ Brake Drum (7 marks)**

**Fabrication Sketch (textual):**

- Al₂O₃ short fibers are mixed with a small amount of silica binder and **cold-pressed** into a drum-shaped preform.
- The preform is **sintered at 1100 °C** for porosity control (target $\phi = 0.40$).
- The preform is **preheated to 500 °C** and placed in the lower die.
- Molten Al-7 % Si alloy at **720 °C** is poured.
- Upper punch applies **100 MPa** within 2 s.
- Held for **90 s**; ejected; machined.

**Role of Pressure in Strength:**
- Pressure eliminates gas and shrinkage porosity, giving near-theoretical density.
- Pressure drives the melt into fiber bundles, ensuring **full infiltration** of micro-pores.
- Better infiltration → **lower defect population** → higher **load-bearing efficiency** of fibers.
- Pressure also causes **grain refinement**, raising matrix contribution to strength.

**Rule of Mixtures Calculation:**

**Step 1. Critical fiber length:**
$$L_c = \frac{\sigma_f \, d}{2 \tau_i} = \frac{1900 \times 8 \times 10^{-6}}{2 \times 20} = 3.8 \times 10^{-4} \, \text{m} = 0.38 \, \text{mm}$$

**Step 2. Fiber efficiency factor:**
$$1 - \frac{L_c}{2L} = 1 - \frac{0.38}{2 \times 3.0} = 1 - 0.0633 = 0.9367$$

**Step 3. Composite longitudinal tensile strength (Kelly-Tyson):**
$$\sigma_{c,L} = \sigma_f V_f \left( 1 - \frac{L_c}{2L} \right) + \sigma_m' (1 - V_f)$$

$$\sigma_{c,L} = 1900 \times 0.25 \times 0.9367 + 165 \times 0.75$$

$$\sigma_{c,L} = 444.9 + 123.75 = 568.7 \, \text{MPa}$$

**Answer:** $\sigma_{c,L} \approx 569 \, \text{MPa}$, which is a realistic value for squeeze-cast Al/Al₂O₃ short-fiber MMCs.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Units!** Every calculation must carry units. The most common mark-deduction is forgetting to convert $\mu m$ to $m$ in the critical-length formula.
> 2. **Sign convention on $\cos\theta$:** A wetting system ($\theta < 90°$) has a *positive* capillary pressure that *resists* infiltration, while a non-wetting system (Al on Al₂O₃, $\theta > 90°$) has a *negative* capillary pressure that *assists* infiltration. Confusing the two gives an inflated pressure requirement.
> 3. **Don't confuse $V_f$ and $W_f$:** Volume fraction and weight fraction are related by densities. KTU questions often give weight fraction and ask for volume — students blindly apply the rule of mixtures with $W_f$, which is wrong.
> 4. **Squeeze casting ≠ Die casting:** The pressure in squeeze casting is **maintained throughout solidification**, whereas in pressure die casting it is applied only to fill the cavity.
> 5. **Chvorinov's constant B is not universal** — it depends on alloy, mould material, and pressure. Always state the modified form when discussing squeeze casting.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision Checklist (read this the night before the exam):**

- ✅ **Definition:** Squeeze casting = liquid-state hybrid process applying **50–150 MPa** during solidification.
- ✅ **Two variants:** **Direct** (simple shapes, direct punch) and **Indirect** (complex shapes, gated).
- ✅ **Five process parameters:** Pressure, die temperature, superheat, dwell time, preform temperature.
- ✅ **Five process steps:** Preform prep → die preheat → pour → pressurise → dwell → eject.
- ✅ **Capillary pressure:** $P_c = \dfrac{2 \sigma_{lv} \cos\theta}{r}$ — sign of $\cos\theta$ decides whether capillary force helps or resists infiltration.
- ✅ **Darcy's law for preform:** $P_{visc} = \dfrac{\mu h v}{K}$.
- ✅ **Pressure balance for infiltration:** $P_{app} \geq P_{visc} - P_c + \rho_m g h$ (for non-wetting systems, $P_c < 0$).
- ✅ **Kelly-Tyson rule of mixtures** (for discontinuous aligned fibers): $\sigma_{c,L} = \sigma_f V_f \left( 1 - \dfrac{L_c}{2L} \right) + \sigma_m' (1 - V_f)$.
- ✅ **Critical fiber length:** $L_c = \dfrac{\sigma_f d}{2 \tau_i}$.
- ✅ **Chvorinov's rule modified for pressure:** solidification time under squeeze is a small fraction of gravity-cast value (≈ 3 % at 100 MPa).
- ✅ **Key industrial products:** pistons, brake rotors/drums, connecting rods, electronic substrates.
- ✅ **Common defects avoided:** shrinkage porosity, gas porosity, incomplete infiltration.
- ✅ **Differentiate from:** stir casting (no external pressure), die casting (pressure only for filling, not during solidification), forging (no melt involved).
- ✅ **Always cite the fibre-matrix system** (e.g., Al/Al₂O₃, Al/SiC, Mg/Al₂O₃) — KTU marks are awarded for correct pairing.
- ✅ **Typical $V_f$ range in squeeze-cast MMCs:** 0.10 – 0.40. Above 0.40, infiltration becomes very difficult even at 150 MPa.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Liquid infiltration under pressure
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT412 - COMPOSITE MATERIALS AND CERAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Liquid infiltration under pressure

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Liquid Infiltration under Pressure** (also known as **Pressure Infiltration Casting** or **Squeeze Casting**) is a non-conventional composite manufacturing process in which a liquid matrix phase (typically a molten metal, polymer melt, or ceramic slurry) is forced under an externally applied pressure into the interstitial spaces of a porous, rigid reinforcement preform. The applied pressure overcomes the capillary resistance (non-wetting threshold) and drives the fluid flow according to **Darcy's Law of flow through porous media**, thereby producing a near-net-shape, low-porosity composite.

The preform is usually made of:
- **Ceramic particulates** (e.g., SiC, Al₂O₃)
- **Short fibers / whiskers** (e.g., Saffil Al₂O₃)
- **Continuous fiber tows** (e.g., carbon, alumina)
- **Interconnected porous ceramic skeletons** for interpenetrating phase composites (IPCs)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Under pressure infiltration is classified as a *Liquid State Fabrication* route for Metal Matrix Composites (MMCs) and Ceramic Matrix Composites (CMCs), sitting alongside stir casting, compocasting, and spray deposition in Module 3 of PEMUT412.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Sponge & Honey" Model

Imagine dipping a dry kitchen sponge into a beaker of thick honey. If the sponge's pores are larger than the honey's surface tension can handle, the honey seeps in slowly by gravity alone. But honey is *viscous*, so it just sits on top. Now, place the sponge in a sealed chamber, pour the honey on top, and pressurize the chamber with a piston — suddenly the honey is **squeezed** through the sponge uniformly, filling even the deepest, narrowest pores.

In engineering terms:
- The **sponge** = the porous ceramic reinforcement preform
- The **honey** = the molten metal matrix (e.g., molten Al at **660 °C**)
- The **pressurized piston** = the applied external gas or mechanical pressure
- The **escape route** = vented air being pushed out

This is exactly how squeeze casting infiltrates molten aluminum into a SiC preform to make a brake disc or piston — except at industrial scale, with pressures of **50–100 MPa** and dwell times of seconds.

### 1.3 Why Apply Pressure? — The Wetting Problem

Most molten metals (Al, Mg, Cu) **do not wet** ceramic reinforcements like SiC or Al₂O₃. The **contact angle θ > 90°**, meaning the liquid surface resists entry into the pore (think of mercury on glass). Without pressure, infiltration is thermodynamically forbidden. Applied pressure **forces** the non-wetting liquid into the preform, making the process viable.

> [!NOTE]
> The applied pressure must exceed the **threshold (capillary) pressure** for infiltration to begin. This threshold is mathematically expressed in Section 2.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Underlying Physical Phenomena

The pressure infiltration process is governed by **four coupled physical phenomena**:

1. **Thermodynamics of Wetting** — determined by the contact angle θ between liquid and reinforcement.
2. **Capillary Pressure (Young–Laplace Equation)** — opposes infiltration in non-wetting systems.
3. **Fluid Flow Through Porous Media** — described by Darcy's Law.
4. **Heat Transfer During Solidification** — the metal must freeze under pressure to prevent porosity.

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic Flow

**Step 1: Wetting Assessment**
- Measure the **contact angle θ** between the molten matrix and the reinforcement at processing temperature.
- If θ < 90° → system is **wetting** → spontaneous infiltration is possible (e.g., Cu on graphite).
- If θ > 90° → system is **non-wetting** → external pressure is **mandatory** (e.g., Al on SiC).

**Step 2: Threshold Pressure Calculation**
- For a cylindrical pore of radius *r*, the pressure resisting entry is:

$$P_{\text{threshold}} = -\frac{2 \gamma_{LV} \cos\theta}{r}$$

- When θ > 90°, cos θ is negative, making **−cos θ positive**, hence P_threshold > 0.
- Pore radius *r* is the **effective hydraulic radius** of the porous preform.

**Step 3: Applied Pressure Selection**
- The applied pressure **P_applied** must satisfy:

$$P_{\text{applied}} \; > \; P_{\text{threshold}} \; = \; -\frac{2 \gamma_{LV} \cos\theta}{r}$$

- Typical industrial pressures: **5 MPa – 100 MPa**.

**Step 4: Fluid Flow Rate (Darcy's Law)**
- Once pressure is applied, the volumetric flow velocity through the preform is:

$$v \; = \; -\frac{K}{\mu} \, \nabla P$$

where:
- *K* = permeability of the preform (m²)
- *μ* = dynamic viscosity of the molten metal (Pa·s)
- ∇P = pressure gradient across the preform

**Step 5: Permeability Estimation (Kozeny–Carman Equation)**
- For a packed bed of particles, permeability is:

$$K \; = \; \frac{\varepsilon^{3}}{k \, S^{2} (1-\varepsilon)^{2}}$$

where:
- ε = porosity of the preform (volume fraction of pores)
- *S* = specific surface area of the reinforcement per unit volume
- *k* = Kozeny constant (≈ **5** for most packed beds)

**Step 6: Infiltration Time (1-D Slug Flow Model)**
- Time required to infiltrate a preform of length *L* under a constant pressure drop ΔP:

$$t \; = \; \frac{\mu \, L^{2}}{2 \, K \, \Delta P}$$

**Step 7: Solidification Under Pressure**
- Once infiltration completes, pressure is maintained until the metal solidifies. This minimizes shrinkage porosity (Shrinkage ≈ **3–6 %** for Al alloys gets compressed out by the applied load).

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Equation / Concept** | **Formula** | **Typical Units** | **Physical Meaning** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Threshold (capillary) pressure | $P_{\text{th}} = -\dfrac{2 \gamma_{LV} \cos\theta}{r}$ | Pa | Minimum pressure to force non-wetting liquid into pore |
| Darcy's law (flow velocity) | $v = -\dfrac{K}{\mu} \nabla P$ | m/s | Fluid velocity through porous medium |
| Kozeny–Carman permeability | $K = \dfrac{\varepsilon^{3}}{k S^{2} (1-\varepsilon)^{2}}$ | m² | Permeability of packed particle bed |
| Infiltration time (1-D slug) | $t = \dfrac{\mu L^{2}}{2 K \Delta P}$ | s | Time for complete infiltration |
| Capillary number | $\text{Ca} = \dfrac{\mu v}{\gamma_{LV}}$ | dimensionless | Ratio of viscous to surface tension forces |
| Volume fraction of reinforcement | $V_f = 1 - \varepsilon$ | dimensionless | Solid loading in preform |
| Effective pore radius (packed bed) | $r_{\text{eff}} = \dfrac{\varepsilon}{S(1-\varepsilon)}$ | m | Hydraulic radius for irregular pores |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive Industry**: Squeeze-cast **Al-SiC** pistons, brake rotors, and cylinder liners — Toyota and Honda use pressure-infiltrated MMC components.
- **Aerospace**: Carbon-fiber reinforced aluminum (Cf/Al) for satellite structural parts.
- **Electronics**: **AlSiC** (Al-infiltrated SiC) heat spreaders for high-power IGBT modules — infiltrated with controlled CTE matching silicon dies.
- **Defense**: Tungsten-infiltrated copper kinetic energy penetrators.
- **Ceramics Processing**: Preceramic polymer infiltration + pyrolysis cycles for C/SiC turbine components (e.g., SNECMA/SPACE C/SiC nozzles).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Pressure infiltration is the **only viable route** when the reinforcement is in the form of a rigid, pre-shaped perform that cannot be deformed — making it indispensable for **net-shape MMC component manufacturing**.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Threshold (Capillary) Pressure — Young–Laplace for a Cylindrical Pore

We consider a molten metal in contact with a cylindrical pore of radius *r* inside the preform. We perform a **mechanical force balance** on the liquid–vapor meniscus at the pore mouth.

**Step 1: Identify the forces acting on the meniscus**

The liquid–vapor interface is curved with two principal radii of curvature:
- $R_1 = r$ (in the plane of the cross-section)
- $R_2 = \infty$ (along the axis of the cylinder — the pore is long, so curvature is zero along the axis)

**Step 2: Apply the Young–Laplace equation**

The pressure jump across a curved interface is:

$$\Delta P \; = \; \gamma_{LV} \left( \frac{1}{R_1} + \frac{1}{R_2} \right)$$

Substituting $R_1 = r$ and $R_2 \to \infty$:

$$\Delta P \; = \; \frac{\gamma_{LV}}{r}$$

This is the pressure difference *across* the curved meniscus due to surface tension alone. For a wetting fluid (θ < 90°), the meniscus is concave (curves inward), making ΔP favorable to infiltration.

**Step 3: Introduce the contact angle correction**

The liquid surface meets the solid pore wall at the **contact angle θ** (measured inside the liquid). The vertical component of the surface tension force that opposes entry is $\gamma_{LV} \cos\theta$. Geometrically, this reduces the driving pressure:

$$P_{\text{threshold}} \; = \; -\frac{2 \gamma_{LV} \cos\theta}{r}$$

The factor **2** arises because the meniscus has two surface tension vectors (one on each side of the pore circumference) pulling outward against the applied pressure.

**Step 4: Verify the sign convention**

- For θ < 90° (wetting): cos θ > 0 → $P_{\text{threshold}}$ is **negative** → spontaneous infiltration (no external pressure needed).
- For θ > 90° (non-wetting): cos θ < 0 → $-2\gamma_{LV}\cos\theta > 0$ → $P_{\text{threshold}}$ is **positive** → external pressure must be applied.

> **Physical interpretation:** This equation tells the engineer exactly how much pressure is needed to push a non-wetting metal into a ceramic pore — a direct design input for hydraulic press sizing.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of the 1-D Infiltration Time

Consider infiltration along a single Cartesian axis *x*, into a preform of length *L*, with constant applied pressure $P_{\text{applied}}$ at *x = 0* and atmospheric pressure at the advancing front *x = ℓ(t)*.

**Step 1: Darcy's law along the infiltrated length**

$$v(\text{front}) \; = \; \frac{d\ell}{dt} \; = \; \frac{K}{\mu} \cdot \frac{\Delta P}{\ell} \; = \; \frac{K \, (P_{\text{applied}} - P_{\text{atm}})}{\mu \, \ell}$$

**Step 2: Rearrange as a separable ODE**

$$\ell \, d\ell \; = \; \frac{K \, \Delta P}{\mu} \, dt$$

**Step 3: Integrate from 0 to *L* (infiltration length) and 0 to *t***

$$\int_{0}^{L} \ell \, d\ell \; = \; \frac{K \Delta P}{\mu} \int_{0}^{t} dt$$

$$\frac{L^{2}}{2} \; = \; \frac{K \Delta P}{\mu} \cdot t$$

**Step 4: Solve for infiltration time**

$$t \; = \; \frac{\mu L^{2}}{2 K \Delta P}$$

> **Engineering takeaway:** Infiltration time scales **quadratically** with preform thickness. Doubling the preform thickness quadruples the required infiltration time at a given pressure.

---

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam Style)

**Problem:** A preform of **Al₂O₃ short fibers** (volume fraction $V_f = 0.40$, pore radius $r = 20 \, \mu m$) is to be infiltrated with **molten aluminum** at **750 °C**.
Given:
- $\gamma_{LV}$ of molten Al at 750 °C = **0.85 N/m**
- Contact angle θ = **130°** (non-wetting)
- Preform thickness $L = 50 \, mm$
- Applied pressure $\Delta P = 5 \, MPa$
- Permeability $K = 1.5 \times 10^{-11} \, m^{2}$
- Dynamic viscosity μ = **$1.2 \times 10^{-3} \, Pa \cdot s$**

**Find:** (a) Threshold pressure (b) Time for full infiltration.

**Solution:**

**Part (a): Threshold pressure**

$$\cos(130^{\circ}) \; = \; -0.6428$$

$$P_{\text{threshold}} \; = \; -\frac{2 \times 0.85 \times (-0.6428)}{20 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$P_{\text{threshold}} \; = \; \frac{1.0928}{20 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$P_{\text{threshold}} \; = \; 54{,}640 \; Pa \; \approx \; 0.055 \; MPa$$

Since applied pressure (**5 MPa**) ≫ threshold pressure (**0.055 MPa**), infiltration will proceed easily. ∎

**Part (b): Infiltration time**

$$t \; = \; \frac{(1.2 \times 10^{-3}) \times (0.050)^{2}}{2 \times (1.5 \times 10^{-11}) \times (5 \times 10^{6})}$$

$$t \; = \; \frac{(1.2 \times 10^{-3}) \times (2.5 \times 10^{-3})}{1.5 \times 10^{-4}}$$

$$t \; = \; \frac{3.0 \times 10^{-6}}{1.5 \times 10^{-4}}$$

$$t \; = \; 0.02 \; s \; = \; 20 \; ms$$

> **Remark:** At 5 MPa applied pressure, the 50 mm Al₂O₃ preform is fully infiltrated in just **20 milliseconds** — pressure infiltration is extraordinarily fast compared to vacuum infiltration or melt stirring.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Pressure vs. Other Liquid-State Methods

| **Parameter** | **Stir Casting** | **Squeeze Casting / Pressure Infiltration** | **Vacuum Infiltration** |
|---|---|---|---|
| External pressure | None (gravity + stir) | **5–100 MPa** | < 1 atm vacuum |
| Wetting requirement | Yes (must be wetting) | **No (pressure overrides wetting)** | Yes |
| Porosity in product | 5–10 % | **< 1 %** | 2–5 % |
| Reinforcement types | Particles only | **Particles, whiskers, fibers** | Particles, fibers |
| Fiber damage | Moderate | **Possible (fiber breakage at high P)** | Low |
| Net-shape capability | Limited | **Excellent** | Moderate |
| Cycle time | Minutes | **Seconds** | Minutes |
| Equipment cost | Low | **High** | Moderate |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Process Flow of Pressure Infiltration (Squeeze Casting)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[START: Reinforcement Selection] --> B[Preform Preparation: Fiber or Particle Packing]
    B --> C[Preform Preheating: 400 to 800 deg C]
    C --> D[Melting of Matrix Metal: e.g., Al at 750 deg C]
    D --> E[Pour Molten Metal into Die Cavity with Preform]
    E --> F[Apply High Pressure via Hydraulic Punch: 50 to 100 MPa]
    F --> G{Infiltration Complete?}
    G -- No --> F
    G -- Yes --> H[Solidification Under Pressure: Maintain Load]
    H --> I[Ejection of Composite Component]
    I --> J[Secondary Machining if Required]
    J --> K[END: MMC Component Ready for Inspection]
    F -.Heat Flow.-> L[Heat Transfer Through Preform]
    L -.Latent Heat.-> H
```

### 4.2 Functional Architecture: Pressure Infiltration System Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PRESSURE_SYSTEM[PRESSURE GENERATION SUBSYSTEM]
        P1[Hydraulic Cylinder] --> P2[High Pressure Intensifier]
        P2 --> P3[Pressure Control Valve: 0 to 100 MPa]
    end

    subgraph DIE_ASSEMBLY[DIE AND PREFORM ASSEMBLY]
        D1[Lower Die Platen] --> D2[Porous Ceramic Preform]
        D2 --> D3[Upper Punch or Ram]
        D3 --> D4[Vent Channels for Air Escape]
    end

    subgraph MELT_DELIVERY[MOLTEN METAL DELIVERY]
        M1[Resistance Furnace: 750 to 900 deg C] --> M2[Transfer Ladle]
        M2 --> M3[Pouring Port into Die]
    end

    P3 --> D3
    M3 --> D2
    D4 --> V1[Atmosphere or Vacuum Pump]

    D2 --> O1[Solidified MMC Billet]
    O1 --> E1[Ejection System]
```

### 4.3 Microstructural Evolution Under Pressure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Stage 1: Dry Preform with Interconnected Porosity] --> S2[Stage 2: Molten Metal Contact and Wetting Resistance]
    S2 --> S3[Stage 3: Pressure Exceeds Threshold: Liquid Front Advances]
    S3 --> S4[Stage 4: Complete Pore Filling and Solidification Onset]
    S4 --> S5[Stage 5: Densified MMC with < 1 percent Porosity]
    S1 -.V_f 30 to 60 percent.-> S2
    S3 -.Darcy Flow.-> S4
    S4 -.Under Pressure.-> S5
```

### 4.4 Force Balance on a Pore (Visual Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[External Applied Pressure: P_applied] --> B[Pore Entrance]
    B --> C[Liquid Front at Position x equals L of t]
    C --> D[Capillary Resistance: minus 2 gamma LV cos theta over r]
    D --> E[Net Driving Pressure: P_applied minus P_threshold]
    E --> F[Darcy Flow Through Porous Network]
    F --> G[Infiltration Velocity: v equals K over mu times Delta P]
```

---

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
*Define pressure infiltration in composite manufacturing. Why is external pressure mandatory when infiltrating aluminum into a silicon-carbide preform?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Pressure infiltration is a liquid-state composite fabrication process in which a molten metal is forced under an externally applied pressure (typically 5–100 MPa) into the pores of a porous ceramic preform. The applied pressure overcomes the capillary resistance arising from the non-wetting nature of the system. For molten Al on SiC, the contact angle θ exceeds 90° (typically ~130°), making cos θ negative and yielding a positive threshold pressure of ~0.05–0.5 MPa. Hence, spontaneous infiltration is impossible, and external pressure is mandatory to achieve complete densification.

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]**
*State and explain Darcy's Law as applied to pressure infiltration. Define the term "permeability" of a preform.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Darcy's Law describes the flow of a fluid through a porous medium:

$$v \; = \; -\frac{K}{\mu} \nabla P$$

where *v* is the superficial flow velocity, *K* is the permeability of the preform (m²), *μ* is the dynamic viscosity of the molten metal, and ∇P is the applied pressure gradient. **Permeability** is a material property of the preform that quantifies how easily the fluid can flow through its pore network — high permeability means large, interconnected pores; low permeability indicates fine, tortuous pore channels. For ceramic particle preforms, permeability is estimated by the Kozeny–Carman equation.

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **As per KTU ESE pattern**, students answer ONE full question from a choice of TWO per module. Each 14-mark question carries two sub-parts of 7 marks each.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks): Pressure Infiltration Process & Theory** [KTU 2024 ESE Pattern]

**(a)** With the aid of a neat schematic, describe the **squeeze casting / pressure infiltration process** for manufacturing a metal matrix composite. List its advantages over stir casting. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the expression for the **threshold pressure** required to infiltrate a non-wetting liquid into a cylindrical pore. A SiC preform with an average pore radius of **15 µm** is infiltrated by molten aluminum at 750 °C. Given $\gamma_{LV} = 0.85 \, N/m$ and θ = 140°, calculate the threshold pressure. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Process Description (7 marks):**

The squeeze casting process for MMC fabrication involves the following steps:

1. **Preform preparation**: A porous preform of the reinforcement (e.g., SiC particles or Al₂O₃ fibers) is prepared, often with a small amount of inorganic binder, and preheated to **400–800 °C** to avoid thermal shock upon contact with molten metal.
2. **Melt preparation**: The matrix alloy (e.g., Al-12 % Si) is melted in a separate furnace and brought to the desired superheat (typically 50–100 °C above its liquidus).
3. **Die assembly**: The preheated preform is placed in a hardened steel die, and the molten metal is poured over it.
4. **Pressurized infiltration**: A hydraulic punch applies a pressure of **50–100 MPa** directly on the molten metal, forcing it through the preform. Air is vented through escape channels.
5. **Solidification under pressure**: The pressure is maintained throughout solidification, compressing any shrinkage porosity.
6. **Ejection**: Once fully solidified, the composite billet is ejected from the die.

**Advantages over stir casting** (write any 4):
- ✔ Produces **near-net-shape** components with **< 1 % porosity**
- ✔ Suitable for **fibers, whiskers, and particles** — not limited to particles
- ✔ Independent of wettability — can process **non-wetting systems**
- ✔ Shorter cycle time (seconds) — suitable for mass production
- ✔ Better **fiber-matrix bonding** due to compressive infiltration
- ✔ Improved mechanical properties (higher strength, stiffness)

**(b) Threshold Pressure Derivation & Calculation (7 marks):**

**Derivation** (refer to Section 3.1 above for full derivation):

The mechanical force balance on a hemispherical meniscus at the mouth of a cylindrical pore yields:

$$P_{\text{threshold}} \; = \; -\frac{2 \gamma_{LV} \cos\theta}{r}$$

**Calculation:**

$$\cos(140^{\circ}) \; = \; -0.7660$$

$$P_{\text{threshold}} \; = \; -\frac{2 \times 0.85 \times (-0.7660)}{15 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$P_{\text{threshold}} \; = \; \frac{1.3022}{15 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$P_{\text{threshold}} \; = \; 86{,}813 \; Pa \; \approx \; 0.087 \; MPa \; \approx \; 87 \; kPa$$

Therefore, an applied pressure of at least **87 kPa** (and ideally several times higher to ensure rapid infiltration) is required to initiate and sustain infiltration.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks): Infiltration Kinetics & Permeability** [KTU 2024 ESE Pattern]

**(a)** State and explain **Darcy's Law** for flow through porous media. Derive the expression for the **time required to infiltrate a preform of length L** under a constant applied pressure ΔP. Discuss how doubling the preform thickness affects infiltration time. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A preform with permeability $K = 2.5 \times 10^{-11} \, m^{2}$ and thickness **30 mm** is to be infiltrated by a molten metal of viscosity $\mu = 1.8 \times 10^{-3} \, Pa \cdot s$ under an applied pressure of **8 MPa**. Calculate the **infiltration time**. If the applied pressure is increased to **20 MPa**, what is the new infiltration time? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Darcy's Law & Time Derivation (7 marks):**

Darcy's Law:

$$v \; = \; \frac{K \, \Delta P}{\mu \, \ell(t)}$$

Integration (refer to Section 3.2 for full derivation):

$$t \; = \; \frac{\mu \, L^{2}}{2 \, K \, \Delta P}$$

**Effect of doubling thickness**: Since $t \propto L^2$, doubling the preform thickness **quadruples (4×)** the infiltration time. This is a critical design constraint — thick components require either higher pressures or longer processing windows.

**(b) Numerical Calculation (7 marks):**

**Case 1: ΔP = 8 MPa**

$$t_1 \; = \; \frac{(1.8 \times 10^{-3}) \times (0.030)^{2}}{2 \times (2.5 \times 10^{-11}) \times (8 \times 10^{6})}$$

$$t_1 \; = \; \frac{(1.8 \times 10^{-3}) \times (9.0 \times 10^{-4})}{4.0 \times 10^{-4}}$$

$$t_1 \; = \; \frac{1.62 \times 10^{-6}}{4.0 \times 10^{-4}} \; = \; 4.05 \times 10^{-3} \; s \; \approx \; 4.05 \; ms$$

**Case 2: ΔP = 20 MPa**

Since $t \propto 1/\Delta P$:

$$t_2 \; = \; t_1 \times \frac{8}{20} \; = \; 4.05 \; ms \times 0.4 \; = \; 1.62 \; ms$$

**Verification by direct substitution:**

$$t_2 \; = \; \frac{(1.8 \times 10^{-3}) \times (9.0 \times 10^{-4})}{2 \times (2.5 \times 10^{-11}) \times (20 \times 10^{6})} \; = \; \frac{1.62 \times 10^{-6}}{1.0 \times 10^{-3}} \; = \; 1.62 \; ms \; \checkmark$$

---

### ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes students make — and where you lose marks:**
>
> 1. **Sign error in threshold pressure equation**: Forgetting the negative sign before $2\gamma_{LV}\cos\theta$. If cos θ is negative, the **double negative** makes the threshold pressure **positive**. Mark loss: **1–2 marks**.
> 2. **Mixing up contact angle sign convention**: Some textbooks define θ from the solid side; the KTU convention measures it from the **liquid side**. Always draw the meniscus first.
> 3. **Unit conversion errors**: Pore radius given in µm must be converted to m before plugging in (e.g., 20 µm = 20 × 10⁻⁶ m). A single missed factor of 10⁶ will swing the answer by six orders of magnitude.
> 4. **Forgetting the "²" in time equation**: Infiltration time scales with L², not L. If you write $t = \mu L / (K \Delta P)$, you will be marked wrong.
> 5. **Ignoring permeability in Kozeny–Carman equation**: Many students write $K = \varepsilon^3 / (1-\varepsilon)^2$ — forgetting the constant *k* and specific surface area *S²*.
> 6. **Not stating assumptions**: In derivations, students forget to mention "1-D flow, constant permeability, no chemical reaction at interface, isothermal liquid." Examiners reward explicit assumptions.

---

### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Pressure infiltration** is a **liquid-state MMC fabrication route** where external pressure overcomes non-wetting capillary resistance.
- ✅ **Threshold pressure formula** (must memorize): $P_{\text{th}} = -2\gamma_{LV}\cos\theta / r$.
- ✅ **Non-wetting systems (θ > 90°)** require external pressure; **wetting systems (θ < 90°)** can infiltrate spontaneously.
- ✅ **Darcy's Law** governs flow velocity: $v = (K/\mu)\nabla P$.
- ✅ **Kozeny–Carman** gives permeability of packed particle beds: $K = \varepsilon^3 / [k S^2 (1-\varepsilon)^2]$.
- ✅ **Infiltration time** scales as $L^2 / \Delta P$ — quadratic in thickness, inverse in pressure.
- ✅ **Typical industrial pressures**: 5–100 MPa; **typical preform porosities**: 30–70 %.
- ✅ **Squeeze casting** is the most common industrial embodiment of pressure infiltration.
- ✅ **Advantages**: low porosity (< 1 %), net-shape, fast, non-wetting capability.
- ✅ **Disadvantages**: high equipment cost, possible fiber damage, limited preform complexity.
- ✅ **Real-world examples**: Al-SiC brake discs, AlSiC electronic heat sinks, Cf/Al aerospace parts.
- ✅ **Module 3 connection**: Sits alongside stir casting, compocasting, and spray deposition as a member of the **liquid-phase fabrication family**.
- ✅ **Key design knobs**: applied pressure, preform preheat, melt superheat, permeability, viscosity, contact angle.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: In-situ process
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT412 - COMPOSITE MATERIALS AND CERAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** In-situ process

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **In-situ process** (also called *in-situ reaction synthesis* or *reactive processing*) is a composite fabrication technique in which the **reinforcement phase is synthesized directly within the matrix** through controlled chemical reactions occurring during the manufacturing cycle, rather than being introduced as a pre-fabricated external constituent.

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Spirit):**  
> *"In-situ composites are those in which the reinforcing phase is formed within the matrix by a chemical reaction during the fabrication of the composite itself — the reinforcement is generated in the place where it is finally required."*

In KTU terminology, this belongs to the family of **liquid-state processing routes** of Metal Matrix Composites (MMCs) and Ceramic Matrix Composites (CMCs), where **thermodynamic driving force** (negative Gibbs free energy) compels the constituent reactants to convert *in place* into the desired ceramic or intermetallic reinforcement.

### 1.2 Types of In-situ Reactions (Classification Table)

| S.No | Sub-Class | Reaction Medium | Typical Reinforcement Formed |
|------|-----------|-----------------|------------------------------|
| 1 | Gas–Liquid In-situ Reaction | Molten metal + reactive gas | Oxides, nitrides (e.g., Al₂O₃, AlN) |
| 2 | Solid–Liquid In-situ Reaction | Molten metal + solid reactant | Carbides, borides (e.g., TiC, TiB₂) |
| 3 | Liquid–Liquid In-situ Reaction | Two molten metallic phases | Intermetallics (e.g., Ni₃Al) |
| 4 | Self-Propagating High-Temp. Synthesis (SHS) | Powder compact ignited locally | Carbides, silicides, borides |
| 5 | In-situ Eutectic Growth | Directional solidification | Aligned fibers/layers |
| 6 | In-situ Polymerization | Monomer + catalyst | Polymer matrix composites |
| 7 | Sol–Gel In-situ | Solution chemistry | Nano-oxides in ceramics |

---

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition 🍰

> [!NOTE]  
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Self-Baking Cake"**  
> Imagine you are baking a marble cake. In the **ex-situ method**, you would buy chocolate chips from a shop and sprinkle them into the batter — the chips were made *elsewhere* and added later.  
> In the **in-situ method**, however, you add cocoa powder + a tiny amount of baking soda + sugar into the batter. During baking, the heat triggers a **Maillard-type chemical reaction** that *creates* chocolate-flavored caramelized pockets *right inside* the cake matrix. The reinforcement is born at the very location where you need it.  
> 
> ✅ *Advantage:* The chocolate pockets form a **perfect chemical bond** with the surrounding batter — no gaps, no peeling off. This is exactly the superior **matrix–reinforcement interfacial bonding** that makes in-situ composites scientifically superior to ex-situ composites.

---

## 1.4 Physical Constants and Reference Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> - **Standard reaction temperature range:** **800 K – 2500 K** (527 °C – 2227 °C)  
> - **Typical reinforcement size in in-situ process:** **0.1 µm – 5 µm** (sub-micron to fine micron range)  
> - **Standard free energy of formation of Al₂O₃ at 1000 K:** $\Delta G_f^0 \approx -1320 \ \text{kJ/mol}$  
> - **Standard enthalpy of formation of TiC:** $\Delta H_f^0 \approx -184 \ \text{kJ/mol}$

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📗 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Breakdown — The Five Logical Steps

The in-situ process, irrespective of the sub-class, follows a universal five-step logical chain:

- **Step 1 — Reactant Selection:** Choose precursor elements/compounds whose free energy of reaction is *strongly negative* at the processing temperature. The two elements should have a wide **mutual solubility gap** at the processing temperature so that one precipitates out as the reinforcement.

- **Step 2 — Melting / Activation:** Heat the matrix alloy above its liquidus temperature. This provides the kinetic energy necessary to overcome the activation energy barrier ($E_a$) of the desired reaction.

- **Step 3 — Chemical Reaction (Nucleation):** The dissolved reactant atoms (e.g., dissolved carbon in molten aluminum) react with the alloying element (e.g., Ti) to form the ceramic nucleus. The thermodynamic feasibility is governed by the **Gibbs free energy criterion**: $\Delta G < 0$.

- **Step 4 — Growth & Coarsening:** The nuclei grow into fine particles by atomic diffusion. Particle size is controlled by the **competition between nucleation rate and growth rate** — rapid cooling favors many small particles (Ostwald ripening suppression).

- **Step 5 — Solidification & Entrapment:** The matrix solidifies around the freshly formed reinforcement, locking it in a thermodynamically stable, contamination-free interface.

---

## 2.2 Why Is In-situ Preferred? — The "Why & How" Logic

| Mechanism | Why it Matters | How it Helps |
|-----------|----------------|--------------|
| **Clean Interface** | No adsorbed gases, no surface oxide films | Strong chemical bond; efficient load transfer |
| **Thermodynamic Stability** | Reinforcement is in equilibrium with matrix | Stable at high temperatures |
| **Fine Particle Size** | Nucleation dominates over growth | High strength per Hall–Petch relation |
| **Uniform Distribution** | Particles form everywhere uniformly | Isotropic properties |
| **Cost-Effectiveness** | No external ceramic powder purchase | Lower raw-material cost |

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Table 2.3 — Master Equation Reference for In-situ Process**

| # | Equation | Meaning / Use |
|---|----------|----------------|
| 1 | $\Delta G = \Delta H - T\Delta S$ | Thermodynamic feasibility of reaction |
| 2 | $\Delta G < 0$ | Reaction proceeds spontaneously |
| 3 | $\Delta G^0 = -RT \ln K$ | Equilibrium constant link |
| 4 | $\Delta H_{rxn} = \sum \Delta H_f^0(\text{products}) - \sum \Delta H_f^0(\text{reactants})$ | Enthalpy of formation |
| 5 | $J = J_0 \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{\Delta G^*}{k_B T}\right)$ | Nucleation rate (Arrhenius-type) |
| 6 | $\Delta G^* = \dfrac{16 \pi \sigma^3}{3 \Delta G_v^2}$ | Critical free energy barrier for nucleation |
| 7 | $r^* = \dfrac{-2\sigma}{\Delta G_v}$ | Critical nucleus radius |
| 8 | $\sigma_{y} = \sigma_0 + k \cdot d^{-1/2}$ | Hall–Petch strengthening from fine particles |
| 9 | $\Delta V_{rxn} = V_{products} - V_{reactants}$ | Volume change; negative $\Delta V$ = shrinkage (good for bonding) |
| 10 | $K_{sp} = [A^{n+}]^a [B^{m-}]^b$ | Solubility product for precipitation control |

> ⚠️ **Note on Table Symbols:** Vertical bar delimiters (used in chemistry to denote concentration) have been replaced with square brackets `[ ]` to comply with markdown table syntax rules. Use `\vert` in inline LaTeX if vertical bars are absolutely required.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace:** Al/TiC in-situ pistons, engine blocks; in-situ Al₂O₃ reinforced Al for **aircraft skin ribs**.
- **Automotive:** In-situ Mg₂Si reinforced Al alloys for **brake rotors** (Toyota, Honda patents).
- **Defense:** In-situ AlN reinforced Al for **armor plates** (high hardness).
- **Electronics:** In-situ AgSn alloys for **lead-free solder** composites with Cu₆Sn₅ in-situ fibers.
- **Wear-resistant tools:** Ni–Cr / Cr₃C₂ in-situ cermets for cutting tools and dies.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📙 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Worked Example — Formation of TiC in Molten Aluminum

We will derive the **reaction feasibility, weight fraction, and theoretical density** of an Al–TiC in-situ MMC produced by the addition of titanium and carbon to molten aluminum.

### Problem Statement
Pure aluminum is melted at **1000 K (727 °C)**. Titanium powder and graphite powder are added. Carbon dissolves in aluminum and reacts with dissolved Ti to form TiC particles in-situ:

$$\text{Ti (dissolved)} + \text{C (dissolved)} \rightarrow \text{TiC (solid)}$$

**Given data at 1000 K:**
- $\Delta G_f^0(\text{TiC}) = -172 \ \text{kJ/mol}$  
- $\Delta G_f^0(\text{Al}) = 0$ (reference element)  
- $\rho_{Al} = 2.70 \ \text{g/cm}^3$  
- $\rho_{TiC} = 4.93 \ \text{g/cm}^3$  
- Molar mass $M_{Al} = 26.98 \ \text{g/mol}$  
- Molar mass $M_{Ti} = 47.87 \ \text{g/mol}$  
- Molar mass $M_{C} = 12.01 \ \text{g/mol}$  
- Molar mass $M_{TiC} = 59.88 \ \text{g/mol}$

---

### 3.1.1 Step 1 — Verify Thermodynamic Feasibility

The reaction is:

$$\text{Ti} + \text{C} \rightarrow \text{TiC}$$

By Hess's law:

$$\Delta G_{rxn}^0 = \Delta G_f^0(\text{TiC}) - \left[\Delta G_f^0(\text{Ti}) + \Delta G_f^0(\text{C})\right]$$

Since pure Ti and pure graphite in their standard states have $\Delta G_f^0 = 0$:

$$\Delta G_{rxn}^0 = (-172) - (0 + 0)$$

$$\boxed{\Delta G_{rxn}^0 = -172 \ \text{kJ/mol}}$$

> **Interpretation:** The value is **strongly negative**, confirming that the reaction is **thermodynamically spontaneous at 1000 K**. This is the foundation for KTU problems asking "justify whether the in-situ reaction will occur."

---

### 3.1.2 Step 2 — Theoretical Weight Fraction Calculation

Suppose we add 5 wt% of Ti powder and 1.5 wt% of C powder to molten Al (basis: 100 g total charge).

**Mass of Ti** = $5.0 \ \text{g}$  
**Mass of C** = $1.5 \ \text{g}$  
**Mass of Al** = $93.5 \ \text{g}$

**Step (a) — Convert to moles:**

$$n_{Ti} = \frac{5.0}{47.87} = 0.1045 \ \text{mol}$$

$$n_{C} = \frac{1.5}{12.01} = 0.1249 \ \text{mol}$$

**Step (b) — Identify limiting reactant:**  
Stoichiometry is 1 mol Ti : 1 mol C. Since $n_{Ti} < n_{C}$, **Ti is the limiting reactant**.

**Step (c) — Moles of TiC formed:**

$$n_{TiC} = 0.1045 \ \text{mol}$$

**Step (d) — Mass of TiC formed:**

$$m_{TiC} = 0.1045 \times 59.88 = 6.257 \ \text{g}$$

**Step (e) — Weight percentage of TiC in final MMC:**

$$W_{TiC} = \frac{6.257}{100} \times 100\%$$

$$\boxed{W_{TiC} \approx 6.26 \ \text{wt\%}}$$

**Step (f) — Unreacted carbon remaining:**

$$m_{C, \text{left}} = 1.5 - (0.1045 \times 12.01) = 1.5 - 1.255 = 0.245 \ \text{g}$$

This small residue may dissolve in Al or float as slag; in practice, excess carbon is added deliberately to scavenge all Ti.

---

### 3.1.3 Step 3 — Theoretical Density of the Composite (Rule of Mixtures)

For an MMC with $V_f$ as the volume fraction of reinforcement:

$$\rho_{c} = \rho_m (1 - V_f) + \rho_r \cdot V_f$$

**Step (a) — Compute volume fraction of TiC:**

$$V_{TiC} = \frac{m_{TiC} / \rho_{TiC}}{m_{Al}/\rho_{Al} + m_{TiC}/\rho_{TiC}}$$

$$V_{TiC} = \frac{6.257 / 4.93}{93.5/2.70 + 6.257/4.93}$$

$$V_{TiC} = \frac{1.2692}{34.63 + 1.2692} = \frac{1.2692}{35.899}$$

$$V_{TiC} = 0.03536 \quad \text{or} \quad 3.54 \ \text{vol\%}$$

**Step (b) — Compute composite density:**

$$\rho_c = 2.70 \times (1 - 0.0354) + 4.93 \times 0.0354$$

$$\rho_c = 2.70 \times 0.9646 + 4.93 \times 0.0354$$

$$\rho_c = 2.6044 + 0.1745$$

$$\boxed{\rho_c \approx 2.78 \ \text{g/cm}^3}$$

> **Engineering Insight:** The composite is only **2.8% denser** than pure Al (2.70 g/cm³) but has dramatically higher strength and stiffness due to the **fine, uniformly distributed TiC**.

---

## 3.2 General Analytical Matrix for KTU Problems

> [!NOTE]  
> **Use this decision matrix in KTU Part B numerical questions:**

| Question Pattern | Equations to Apply | Common Mistake to Avoid |
|-------------------|---------------------|--------------------------|
| "Justify if reaction is feasible" | $\Delta G = \Delta H - T\Delta S$ | Forgetting $T\Delta S$ term at high T |
| "Find weight % of reinforcement" | Moles of limiting reactant × Molar mass | Picking wrong limiting reactant |
| "Find theoretical density" | Rule of mixtures in **volume** | Using weight fractions directly |
| "Compare in-situ vs ex-situ" | Interface energy, contamination | Forgetting thermodynamic stability point |
| "Calculate nucleation barrier" | $\Delta G^* = 16\pi\sigma^3 / 3\Delta G_v^2$ | Using surface energy in wrong units |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 📕 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Process Flow Diagram — Generic In-situ MMC Fabrication

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Matrix Metal e.g. Al] --> B[Melting above Liquidus T]
    B --> C[Addition of Reactant 1 e.g. Ti powder]
    C --> D[Addition of Reactant 2 e.g. C powder]
    D --> E[Dissolution and Diffusion in Melt]
    E --> F{Is delta G negative?}
    F -- Yes --> G[Chemical Reaction forms Ceramic Nuclei]
    F -- No --> H[Reaction does not proceed - Process Fails]
    G --> I[Nucleation and Growth of Reinforcement]
    I --> J[Stirring for Homogeneous Distribution]
    J --> K[Controlled Solidification]
    K --> L[Final In-situ MMC with Fine Uniform Reinforcement]
    H --> M[Adjust T or Change Reactants]
    M --> C
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Comparative Architecture: In-situ vs Ex-situ

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph EX["EX-SITU PROCESS"]
        E1[External Ceramic Powder Factory] --> E2[Surface Treatment and Cleaning]
        E2 --> E3[Mixing with Molten Matrix]
        E3 --> E4[Possible Interface Contamination and Porosity]
    end

    subgraph IN["IN-SITU PROCESS"]
        I1[Matrix Metal Melt] --> I2[Add Reactant Elements]
        I2 --> I3[In-situ Chemical Reaction]
        I3 --> I4[Clean Atomic Scale Interface]
    end

    EX --> COMP[Final Composite]
    IN --> COMP
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Classification Tree — In-situ Process Sub-Types

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    root[In-situ Composite Processing]
    root --> g1[Gas Liquid Reaction]
    root --> g2[Solid Liquid Reaction]
    root --> g3[Combustion Synthesis SHS]
    root --> g4[Directional Solidification Eutectic]
    root --> g5[In-situ Polymerization]
    root --> g6[Sol Gel Derived]

    g1 --> eg1[Example Al Al2O3 from O2 gas]
    g2 --> eg2[Example Al TiC from Ti and C]
    g3 --> eg3[Example NiAl Al2O3 self propagating]
    g4 --> eg4[Example Al Al3Ni aligned fibers]
    g5 --> eg5[Example Epoxy with nano SiO2]
    g6 --> eg6[Example Glass ceramic with ZrO2]
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📒 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]**
**"Differentiate between in-situ and ex-situ composite fabrication routes with two suitable examples each."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | In-situ | Ex-situ |
|-----------|---------|---------|
| Reinforcement origin | Formed *inside* the matrix by chemical reaction | Pre-made and added externally |
| Interface | Atomically clean, strong bond | Possible surface contamination |
| Particle size | Sub-micron (0.1–5 µm) | Coarser (10–100 µm) |
| Cost | Lower (no external ceramic) | Higher |
| Example 1 | Al–TiC (Ti + C → TiC in Al melt) | Al–SiC (SiC powder stirred into Al melt) |
| Example 2 | Al–Al₂O₃ (Al + CuO → Al₂O₃) | Ni–Al₂O₃ (Al₂O₃ powder added to Ni melt) |

*(1 mark — definition; 1 mark — interface/size distinction; 1 mark — examples)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]**
**"State the thermodynamic criterion for an in-situ reaction to proceed spontaneously. Show its application for the formation of Al₂O₃ from Al and CuO."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- Thermodynamic criterion: **$\Delta G < 0$** at processing temperature, where $\Delta G = \Delta H - T\Delta S$. *(1 mark)*
- Reaction: $2\text{Al} + 3\text{CuO} \rightarrow \text{Al}_2\text{O}_3 + 3\text{Cu}$
- Using $\Delta G_f^0(\text{Al}_2\text{O}_3) \approx -1582 \ \text{kJ/mol}$ and $\Delta G_f^0(\text{CuO}) \approx -130 \ \text{kJ/mol}$:

$$\Delta G_{rxn}^0 = \Delta G_f^0(\text{Al}_2\text{O}_3) - 3\Delta G_f^0(\text{CuO})$$

$$\Delta G_{rxn}^0 = -1582 - 3 \times (-130) = -1192 \ \text{kJ/mol}$$

- Since $\Delta G_{rxn}^0 = -1192 \ \text{kJ/mol} \ll 0$, the reaction is **highly spontaneous** at standard conditions. *(1 mark for calculation + 1 mark for conclusion)*

---

## 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Note:** As per KTU 2024 scheme End Semester Examination pattern, each Part B question has an **internal choice** between two alternative questions. Both are provided below.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model]**

**(a)** Explain the **in-situ process** for fabrication of metal matrix composites with a neat schematic. Discuss the role of **thermodynamic free energy** in selecting the reinforcement phase. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For the in-situ formation of **Al₂O₃ in an aluminum melt via the reaction** $2\text{Al} + 3\text{CuO} \rightarrow \text{Al}_2\text{O}_3 + 3\text{Cu}$:
- Calculate the **standard Gibbs free energy change** at 1000 K using the data: $\Delta G_f^0(\text{Al}_2\text{O}_3) = -1320$ kJ/mol, $\Delta G_f^0(\text{CuO}) = -95$ kJ/mol.
- If 10 g of CuO is added to excess molten Al, calculate the **mass of Al₂O₃ formed**.
- State **two advantages** of in-situ composites over ex-situ. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution for (a) — 7 marks:**

1. **Definition of in-situ process** *(1 mark)*: A composite fabrication route where the reinforcement is chemically synthesized inside the matrix during processing, not added externally.

2. **Schematic description (refer to Section 4.1 Mermaid)** *(2 marks)*: Sequence — melt matrix → add reactants → dissolution → reaction (only if $\Delta G < 0$) → nucleation → growth → solidification → in-situ MMC.

3. **Role of thermodynamic free energy** *(2 marks)*:
   - Reaction must be spontaneous: $\Delta G < 0$.
   - Determines feasibility; strong negative $\Delta G$ ensures complete conversion of reactants.
   - Phase selection: only those reinforcements whose $\Delta G_f^0$ is highly negative (oxides > nitrides > carbides > borides in general stability) will form.

4. **Example of TiC in Al** *(1 mark)*: $\Delta G_{rxn} \approx -172$ kJ/mol; reaction proceeds, fine TiC particles form.

5. **Conclusion** *(1 mark)*: Thermodynamic screening is the *first filter* before attempting any in-situ fabrication.

#### **Model Solution for (b) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1 — Calculate $\Delta G_{rxn}^0$** *(2 marks)*:

$$\Delta G_{rxn}^0 = \Delta G_f^0(\text{Al}_2\text{O}_3) - 3 \Delta G_f^0(\text{CuO})$$

$$\Delta G_{rxn}^0 = -1320 - 3 \times (-95)$$

$$\Delta G_{rxn}^0 = -1320 + 285$$

$$\boxed{\Delta G_{rxn}^0 = -1035 \ \text{kJ/mol}}$$

This is **strongly negative**, confirming feasibility.

**Step 2 — Mass of Al₂O₃ formed** *(3 marks)*:

Molar mass: $M_{CuO} = 63.55 + 16.00 = 79.55 \ \text{g/mol}$  
Molar mass: $M_{Al_2O_3} = 2(26.98) + 3(16.00) = 101.96 \ \text{g/mol}$

$$n_{CuO} = \frac{10}{79.55} = 0.1257 \ \text{mol}$$

From stoichiometry: 3 mol CuO → 1 mol Al₂O₃

$$n_{Al_2O_3} = \frac{0.1257}{3} = 0.0419 \ \text{mol}$$

$$m_{Al_2O_3} = 0.0419 \times 101.96$$

$$\boxed{m_{Al_2O_3} \approx 4.27 \ \text{g}}$$

**Step 3 — Two advantages** *(2 marks)*:
1. **Atomically clean interface** → superior load transfer between matrix and reinforcement.
2. **Thermodynamically stable** reinforcement → composite retains properties at elevated temperatures.
3. *(Optional third point)* Uniform distribution of fine particles → isotropic mechanical properties.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]**

**(a)** Describe the **Self-Propagating High-Temperature Synthesis (SHS)** method for in-situ composite fabrication. State **two merits** and **two demerits** of the SHS route. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** In an Al–SiC in-situ composite, the desired volume fraction of SiC is **20%**. Calculate the **theoretical density** of the composite.  
Given: $\rho_{Al} = 2.70 \ \text{g/cm}^3$, $\rho_{SiC} = 3.20 \ \text{g/cm}^3$.  
Compare this value with pure aluminum and comment on the engineering significance. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution for (a) — 7 marks:**

1. **Definition of SHS** *(2 marks)*: Self-Propagating High-Temperature Synthesis is an in-situ process where a compact mixture of reactant powders is locally ignited at one end; the highly exothermic reaction ($\Delta H \ll 0$) self-sustains a combustion wave that sweeps through the entire compact, converting reactants into the desired ceramic product.

2. **Process steps** *(2 marks)*:
   - Mix reactant powders (e.g., Ti + C for TiC; Mo + Si for MoSi₂).
   - Cold-press into a pellet.
   - Ignite one end using a tungsten coil or laser.
   - Combustion front propagates at 1–25 cm/s; temperatures reach 2000–4000 K.
   - Product is a porous or dense ceramic/MMC ingot.

3. **Two merits** *(1.5 marks)*:
   - Very rapid processing (seconds to minutes).
   - High purity of product (volatile impurities burn off).
   - Energy-efficient (once ignited, no external heating needed).

4. **Two demerits** *(1.5 marks)*:
   - Difficult to control porosity in the final product.
   - Requires highly exothermic reactions; not all systems are SHS-suitable.
   - Brittle products may form due to thermal gradients.

#### **Model Solution for (b) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1 — Apply the rule of mixtures for density** *(3 marks)*:

$$\rho_c = \rho_m (1 - V_f) + \rho_r \cdot V_f$$

$$\rho_c = 2.70 \times (1 - 0.20) + 3.20 \times 0.20$$

$$\rho_c = 2.70 \times 0.80 + 3.20 \times 0.20$$

$$\rho_c = 2.16 + 0.64$$

$$\boxed{\rho_c = 2.80 \ \text{g/cm}^3}$$

**Step 2 — Comparison with pure Al** *(2 marks)*:

$$\Delta \rho = 2.80 - 2.70 = 0.10 \ \text{g/cm}^3$$

$$\text{Percentage increase} = \frac{0.10}{2.70} \times 100\% \approx 3.7\%$$

**Step 3 — Engineering significance** *(2 marks)*:
- Only a **3.7% weight penalty** is incurred for adding 20 vol% of stiff SiC reinforcement.
- The composite will have significantly higher **specific stiffness** ($E/\rho$) and **specific strength** ($\sigma_y/\rho$), making it ideal for **aerospace structural components** where mass is critical.
- The composite will also exhibit **superior wear resistance** and **thermal stability** compared to monolithic aluminum.

---

> [!WARNING]  
> **🚨 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**  
> 1. **Wrong limiting reactant:** Students often pick the reactant with the lower mass instead of the lower *moles* — always convert to moles first.  
> 2. **Confusion between weight fraction and volume fraction:** The rule of mixtures for density uses **volume fraction**, not weight fraction. A 5 wt% ceramic is often only 2–3 vol%.  
> 3. **Forgetting to multiply stoichiometric coefficients:** In $\Delta G_{rxn}$ calculations, multiply each $\Delta G_f^0$ by its stoichiometric coefficient (e.g., 3 × CuO, not 1 × CuO).  
> 4. **Missing units:** Always state $\Delta G$ in **kJ/mol** consistently; mixing kJ and J will lose 0.5 marks.  
> 5. **Hand-waving advantages:** KTU examiners want *specific* technical advantages (clean interface, thermodynamic stability, fine size), not generic statements like "it is better."

---

## 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — In-situ Process (Module 3)**

- ✅ **Definition:** Reinforcement formed *in place* via chemical reaction during composite processing.
- ✅ **Master criterion:** $\Delta G = \Delta H - T\Delta S < 0$ → reaction is spontaneous.
- ✅ **Five sub-classes:** Gas–Liquid, Solid–Liquid, Liquid–Liquid, SHS, Directional Eutectic, Polymerization, Sol–Gel.
- ✅ **Key advantages:** clean atomic interface, thermodynamic stability, fine particle size (0.1–5 µm), uniform distribution, low cost.
- ✅ **Key disadvantages:** limited to thermodynamically favorable systems; control of morphology is challenging; some reactions are violently exothermic.
- ✅ **Must-know examples:** Al/TiC (Ti + C), Al/Al₂O₃ (2Al + 3CuO), Al/AlN (Al + N₂), Ni/Al₂O₃, Fe/TiC.
- ✅ **SHS:** Self-Propagating High-Temperature Synthesis — ignition at one end, combustion wave sweeps through, no external heat needed after ignition.
- ✅ **Density rule:** $\rho_c = \rho_m(1-V_f) + \rho_r V_f$ — uses **volume** fractions.
- ✅ **Nucleation theory:** $\Delta G^* = 16\pi\sigma^3 / 3\Delta G_v^2$ — controls reinforcement particle size.
- ✅ **Hall–Petch link:** Finer reinforcement particles → higher yield strength via $\sigma_y = \sigma_0 + k d^{-1/2}$.
- ✅ **Engineering applications:** aerospace (Al/TiC pistons), automotive (Mg₂Si/Al brake rotors), defense (AlN/Al armor), electronics (lead-free solder in-situ fibers), cutting tools (Cr₃C₂ cermets).
- ✅ **Critical Pitfall:** Always convert mass → moles → identify limiting reactant → compute reinforcement mass.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Introduction, properties, characteristics and applications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT412 - COMPOSITE MATERIALS AND CERAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Introduction, properties, characteristics and applications

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Ceramics (KTU 2024 Definition):** *Ceramics are inorganic, non-metallic solids composed of metallic and non-metallic elements bonded primarily by ionic and/or covalent bonds, generally processed at high temperatures, and characterized by high hardness, high melting points, brittleness, and excellent chemical stability.*

According to the **KTU 2024 PEMUT412 syllabus**, ceramics are broadly classified under three major heads:

$$
\text{Ceramics} \;\Rightarrow\; \begin{cases} \text{Traditional Ceramics} \\ \text{Advanced (Technical) Ceramics} \\ \text{Glasses} \end{cases}
$$

Where:
- **Traditional Ceramics** → made from natural raw materials (clay, silica, feldspar).
- **Advanced Ceramics** → made from synthetic, high-purity powders (oxides, carbides, nitrides, borides).
- **Glasses** → amorphous (non-crystalline) ceramics with no long-range atomic order.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of ceramics like "the bones of engineering":**
> Just as human bones are hard, brittle, strong in compression, and made of calcium phosphate (a ceramic), engineering ceramics such as alumina (Al₂O₃) and zirconia (ZrO₂) provide rigid structural support but will fracture (not deform) when overloaded. Unlike metals, which "bend before they break," ceramics "break before they bend."

**Real-world analogies:**
- A **clay pot fired in a kiln** → traditional ceramic (low-cost, porous).
- A **silicon nitride (Si₃N₄) turbine blade** → advanced ceramic (high-temperature structural use).
- A **window pane** → glassy ceramic (transparent, amorphous).

### 1.3 Standard Metrics and Bonding

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Typical Bond Strength (Ionic/Covalent):** **500 – 1000 kJ/mol**
> - **Young's Modulus Range:** **70 – 500 GPa** (compared to steel ≈ 200 GPa)
> - **Operating Temperature:** up to **1600 °C – 2000 °C** without degradation
> - **Electrical Resistivity (Al₂O₃):** **> 10¹⁴ Ω·cm** (excellent insulator)

The strong directional **covalent/ionic bonding** is the *root cause* of every characteristic property discussed in this module.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Ceramics (Structured Logic)

Ceramics are classified based on **chemistry, structure, and application**:

**Step 1 — By Chemical Composition:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Ceramics} &= \text{Oxide ceramics} \;\cup\; \text{Non-oxide ceramics} \\[4pt]
\text{Oxide} &\Rightarrow \text{Al}_2\text{O}_3,\; \text{ZrO}_2,\; \text{MgO},\; \text{SiO}_2,\; \text{TiO}_2 \\[4pt]
\text{Non-oxide} &\Rightarrow \text{SiC},\; \text{Si}_3\text{N}_4,\; \text{B}_4\text{C},\; \text{AlN},\; \text{BN}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — By Application Category:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Functional Use} &= \begin{cases}
\text{Structural} & : \text{Al}_2\text{O}_3,\; \text{SiC},\; \text{Si}_3\text{N}_4 \\
\text{Electrical} & : \text{BaTiO}_3,\; \text{PZT (lead zirconate titanate)} \\
\text{Magnetic} & : \text{Ferrites (MnZn, NiZn)} \\
\text{Optical} & : \text{Transparent Al}_2\text{O}_3,\; \text{Glass-ceramics} \\
\text{Biological} & : \text{Hydroxyapatite, Bioglass, Zirconia} \\
\text{Refractory} & : \text{MgO, SiC, fireclay bricks}
\end{cases}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — By Microstructure:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Microstructure} &= \begin{cases}
\text{Crystalline} & : \text{Polycrystalline (Al}_2\text{O}_3) \\
\text{Amorphous} & : \text{Glass (SiO}_2\text{)} \\
\text{Composite} & : \text{Cermets (Ceramic + Metal)} \\
\text{Porous} & : \text{Foam ceramics for insulation}
\end{cases}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Why this matters (KTU exam context):**
A 14-mark question will often ask you to *correlate* structure → property → application. Memorize the **Bonding ↔ Property ↔ Application** chain.

---

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Mandatory formulas and metrics for KTU board examinations on ceramics.**

| # | Property / Equation | Mathematical Form | Standard Units | Engineering Significance |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Density (theoretical) | $\rho = \dfrac{n \cdot M}{N_A \cdot V_c}$ | $\text{g/cm}^3$ | Predicts weight of final component |
| 2 | Young's Modulus (rule of mixtures) | $E_c = E_f V_f + E_m V_m$ | $\text{GPa}$ | For cermet/ceramic composites |
| 3 | Thermal Conductivity (Fourier) | $q = -k \, \nabla T$ | $\text{W/m·K}$ | Heat-shield design |
| 4 | Linear Thermal Expansion | $\Delta L = \alpha L_0 \Delta T$ | $\text{K}^{-1}$ | Thermal-shock resistance |
| 5 | Thermal Shock Parameter | $R = \dfrac{\sigma_f (1-\nu)}{E \alpha}$ | $\text{K}$ | Predicts fracture on rapid cooling |
| 6 | Weibull Modulus (strength distribution) | $P_f = 1 - \exp\!\left[-\left(\dfrac{\sigma}{\sigma_0}\right)^m\right]$ | dimensionless | Reliability of brittle ceramics |
| 7 | Dielectric Constant | $\varepsilon_r = \dfrac{C}{C_0}$ | dimensionless | Capacitor design (BaTiO₃) |
| 8 | Curie Temperature | $T_C$ (ferroelectric transition) | $\text{K}$ | Limits piezo operating range |
| 9 | Flexural Strength (3-point bend) | $\sigma_f = \dfrac{3PL}{2bd^2}$ | $\text{MPa}$ | Standard KTU lab measurement |
| 10 | Fracture Toughness | $K_{IC} = Y \sigma \sqrt{\pi a}$ | $\text{MPa}\sqrt{m}$ | Resistance to crack propagation |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Exam Tip:** Use `\vert` or `\mid` (not `|`) in all answers to avoid markdown rendering errors in the question paper.

---

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Ceramics dominate three high-stakes engineering domains:

- **Aerospace:** SiC/Si₃N₄ turbine blades operate at **> 1400 °C** without cooling, increasing jet engine efficiency.
- **Electronics:** BaTiO₃ capacitors form the heart of every smartphone (ε_r ≈ **1000 – 10 000**).
- **Biomedical:** ZrO₂ and hydroxyapatite (HA) are used in hip implants and dental crowns due to **bio-inertness** and **osseointegration**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Weibull Modulus for Brittle Ceramic Failure

Ceramics fail by crack propagation from the *weakest flaw*. The probability of failure $P_f$ under an applied stress $\sigma$ follows a **Weibull distribution**, which the KTU board frequently tests in **Part B (7-mark sub-question)**.

**Step 1 — Define the incremental failure probability:**
For a body of volume $V$ subjected to a uniform stress $\sigma$:

$$
\frac{dP_f}{dV} = n(\sigma)
$$

where $n(\sigma)$ is the *crack-density function* — number of dangerous flaws per unit volume.

**Step 2 — Empirically model $n(\sigma)$ as a power law:**

$$
n(\sigma) = n_0 \left(\frac{\sigma}{\sigma_0}\right)^m
$$

where:
- $n_0$ = normalizing constant,
- $\sigma_0$ = characteristic strength (63.2 % failure probability),
- $m$ = **Weibull modulus** (higher $m$ → narrower strength distribution → more reliable ceramic).

**Step 3 — Integrate over the body volume $V$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_f &= 1 - \exp\!\left[-\int_V n(\sigma)\,dV\right] \\[6pt]
    &= 1 - \exp\!\left[-V\, n_0 \left(\frac{\sigma}{\sigma_0}\right)^m\right] \\[6pt]
    &= 1 - \exp\!\left[-\left(\frac{\sigma}{\sigma_0}\right)^m\right] \quad \text{(normalized form)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation:**

- $P_f(\sigma = 0) = 0$ (no stress → no failure).
- $P_f(\sigma = \sigma_0) = 1 - e^{-1} \approx 0.632$ (definition of characteristic strength).
- $P_f(\sigma \to \infty) = 1$ (certain failure).

**Step 5 — Solve a KTU-style numerical:**

> **Problem:** A Si₃N₄ ceramic has $m = 12$ and $\sigma_0 = 850$ MPa. Find the stress at which $P_f = 0.10$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
0.10 &= 1 - \exp\!\left[-\left(\frac{\sigma}{850}\right)^{12}\right] \\[4pt]
\exp\!\left[-\left(\frac{\sigma}{850}\right)^{12}\right] &= 0.90 \\[4pt]
-\left(\frac{\sigma}{850}\right)^{12} &= \ln(0.90) = -0.10536 \\[4pt]
\left(\frac{\sigma}{850}\right)^{12} &= 0.10536 \\[4pt]
\frac{\sigma}{850} &= (0.10536)^{1/12} = 0.819 \\[4pt]
\sigma &= 0.819 \times 850 \approx \mathbf{696 \; \text{MPa}}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### 3.2 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Ceramics vs Metals vs Polymers

> [!NOTE]
> **This is the single most-asked comparison in KTU Part A (3-mark) questions.**

| Property | Ceramics (Al₂O₃) | Metals (Steel) | Polymers (Nylon) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Stiffness (E) | **High (≈ 380 GPa)** | Moderate (≈ 200 GPa) | Low (≈ 2 GPa) |
| Strength (σ) | High (compressive) | High (tensile) | Low |
| Ductility | **≈ 0 % (brittle)** | 10 – 40 % | 5 – 300 % |
| Toughness (K_IC) | Low (≈ 4 MPa√m) | High (≈ 50 MPa√m) | High |
| Density | Low (≈ 3.9 g/cm³) | High (≈ 7.8 g/cm³) | Very Low (≈ 1.1) |
| Melting Point | **> 2000 °C** | ≈ 1500 °C | < 300 °C |
| Electrical | Insulator (10¹⁴ Ω·cm) | Conductor | Insulator |
| Thermal Shock | Poor (Al₂O₃) / Good (SiC) | Good | Poor |
| Cost | High (machining) | Moderate | Very Low |

**Key Insight:** Ceramics trade *ductility* for *high-temperature stability, hardness, and corrosion resistance*.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Thermal Shock Resistance Parameter

When a ceramic is rapidly cooled ($\Delta T$), the surface contracts faster than the core → tensile stress builds up:

$$
\sigma_{\text{thermal}} = \frac{E \alpha \Delta T}{1 - \nu}
$$

**Step-by-step substitution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_{\text{thermal}} &= E \, \alpha \, \Delta T \, (1 - \nu)^{-1} \\[4pt]
\text{Define } R &= \frac{\sigma_f (1-\nu)}{E \alpha} \\[4pt]
\text{If } \sigma_{\text{thermal}} &\geq \sigma_f \Rightarrow \text{Fracture} \\[4pt]
\Rightarrow \Delta T_{\text{critical}} &= \frac{\sigma_f (1-\nu)}{E \alpha} = R
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Numerical Example:** Al₂O₃ has $E = 380$ GPa, $\alpha = 8 \times 10^{-6}$ /K, $\sigma_f = 300$ MPa, $\nu = 0.22$.

$$
\Delta T_c = \frac{300 \times 10^6 \times (1-0.22)}{380 \times 10^9 \times 8 \times 10^{-6}} \approx \mathbf{77 \; \text{K}}
$$

**This is why Al₂O₃ furnace tubes are pre-heated slowly** — to avoid thermal-shock fracture.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
