<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between traditional and advanced ceramics with two examples each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Traditional Ceramics | Advanced Ceramics |
|---|---|---|
| Raw material | Natural (clay, sand) | Synthetic high-purity powders |
| Purity | Moderate | > 99.5 % |
| Properties | Variable, porous | Controlled, dense, defect-free |
| Examples | Bricks, porcelain, cement | Al₂O₃, SiC, ZrO₂, Si₃N₄ |
| Application | Construction, pottery | Aerospace, electronics, biomedical |

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**Why are ceramics brittle? Explain in terms of bonding.**

**Model Answer:**
Ceramics are predominantly bonded by **strong ionic and covalent bonds**, which are **directional in nature**. Unlike metals, ceramics do not possess a system of free-moving dislocations; the strong directional bonds prevent plastic deformation (no slip systems). When stress is applied beyond the elastic limit, cracks propagate instantaneously along grain boundaries, leading to sudden fracture. Hence, ceramics exhibit **≈ 0 % ductility** and behave as *brittle materials*.

---

### Part B — ESE Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

---

**Q3. [KTU University Exam – May 2024] — Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** [7 Marks] — *Explain the classification of ceramics based on chemical composition. Discuss oxide and non-oxide ceramics with at least three examples each, listing their characteristic properties and applications.*

**(b)** [7 Marks] — *A sintered alumina specimen has a Weibull modulus $m = 10$ and characteristic strength $\sigma_0 = 400$ MPa. Determine the probability of failure at an applied stress of 350 MPa. Comment on the reliability of the ceramic.*

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a):**

**Classification (oxide ceramics):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Oxide ceramics} &= \text{Al}_2\text{O}_3 \;(\text{alumina}), \;\text{MgO},\; \text{ZrO}_2,\; \text{BeO},\; \text{TiO}_2
\end{aligned}
$$

- **Al₂O₃** → high hardness, electrical insulator, used in cutting tools and spark-plug insulators.
- **ZrO₂** → high fracture toughness (transformation toughening), used in dental crowns.
- **MgO** → refractory lining in steel furnaces.

**Non-oxide ceramics:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Non-oxide} &= \text{SiC},\; \text{Si}_3\text{N}_4,\; \text{B}_4\text{C},\; \text{AlN},\; \text{BN}
\end{aligned}
$$

- **SiC** → high thermal conductivity, used in heat exchangers and brake discs.
- **Si₃N₄** → excellent thermal shock resistance, used in turbine rotors.
- **B₄C** → third-hardest material after diamond and c-BN, used in body armor.

**Part (b) — Weibull Calculation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_f &= 1 - \exp\!\left[-\left(\frac{\sigma}{\sigma_0}\right)^m\right] \\[4pt]
    &= 1 - \exp\!\left[-\left(\frac{350}{400}\right)^{10}\right] \\[4pt]
    &= 1 - \exp\!\left[-(0.875)^{10}\right] \\[4pt]
(0.875)^{10} &\approx 0.2631 \\[4pt]
P_f &= 1 - e^{-0.2631} = 1 - 0.7687 = \mathbf{0.2313 \;(23.13 \%)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Comment on reliability:** A 23 % failure probability is **too high for a structural component**. A higher $m$ value (e.g., $m = 20$) is needed for high-reliability aerospace applications.

---

**Q4. [KTU University Exam – May 2024] — Question B (14 Marks) [Alternative Choice]**

**(a)** [7 Marks] — *Describe the mechanical, thermal, electrical, and chemical properties of ceramics. How do they differ from metals?*

**(b)** [7 Marks] — *A ceramic component is subjected to a thermal shock of $\Delta T = 200$ K. Given: $E = 300$ GPa, $\alpha = 6 \times 10^{-6}$ /K, $\nu = 0.25$, $\sigma_f = 280$ MPa. Will the component survive? Compute the critical temperature difference.*

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a):**

| Property | Ceramics | Metals | Reason for Difference |
|---|---|---|---|
| Mechanical | Hard, brittle, strong in compression | Ductile, tough, strong in tension | Strong directional bonds vs metallic bonds |
| Thermal | High melting point, low expansion, low conductivity (oxides) | Lower melting point, high conductivity | Covalent/ionic lattice vibration |
| Electrical | Insulators (10¹⁰ – 10¹⁴ Ω·cm) | Conductors (10⁻⁶ Ω·cm) | No free electrons in ceramics |
| Chemical | Highly corrosion-resistant, oxidation-resistant | Prone to rusting, oxidation | Stable oxide layer + no diffusion in ceramics |

**Part (b) — Thermal Shock Calculation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta T_c &= \frac{\sigma_f (1-\nu)}{E \alpha} \\[4pt]
          &= \frac{280 \times 10^6 \times (1-0.25)}{300 \times 10^9 \times 6 \times 10^{-6}} \\[4pt]
          &= \frac{210 \times 10^6}{1.8 \times 10^6} = \mathbf{116.7 \; \text{K}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Decision:**

$$
\Delta T_{\text{applied}} = 200 \; \text{K} \; > \; \Delta T_c = 116.7 \; \text{K}
$$

**The component will FAIL by thermal shock fracture.** To survive, the ceramic must be pre-heated slowly or replaced with a higher-shock-resistant material such as **SiC** ($\Delta T_c \approx 500$ K).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Mark Deductions:**
> 1. **Forgetting units in Weibull / thermal shock answers** → lose 1 mark.
> 2. **Writing "|σ|" with vertical bars in markdown tables** → may break rendering; always use `\vert` or `\mid`.
> 3. **Mixing up "tensile" and "compressive" strengths of ceramics** → ceramics are *weak in tension* and *strong in compression*; this is a 3-mark differentiator.
> 4. **Not drawing a labeled diagram in a 14-mark question** → loses the **diagram-marks allocation (typically 2 marks)**.
> 5. **Confusing "glass" with "ceramic"** → glass is a subset (amorphous) but still classified as a ceramic; the KTU 2024 syllabus treats it as such.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [x] Ceramics = **inorganic, non-metallic, ionic/covalent solids**, processed at high temperature.
- [x] Three classes → **Traditional, Advanced (Technical), Glasses**.
- [x] Bonding → strong, directional → **brittle, hard, insulating, high-melting**.
- [x] Oxide ceramics: **Al₂O₃, ZrO₂, MgO, SiO₂, TiO₂**.
- [x] Non-oxide ceramics: **SiC, Si₃N₄, B₄C, AlN, BN**.
- [x] Weibull equation: $P_f = 1 - \exp[-(\sigma/\sigma_0)^m]$ → higher $m$ → more reliable.
- [x] Thermal shock parameter: $\Delta T_c = \sigma_f(1-\nu)/(E \alpha)$.
- [x] Flexural strength (3-point bend): $\sigma_f = 3PL/(2bd^2)$.
- [x] Ceramics fail in **tension**, survive in **compression**.
- [x] **Ferroelectric** ceramics: BaTiO₃, PZT → capacitors, piezo sensors.
- [x] **Bioceramics**: ZrO₂, HA, Bioglass → implants, dental crowns.
- [x] **Refractories**: MgO, SiC, fireclay → furnace linings.
- [x] Density calculation: $\rho = nM/(N_A V_c)$.
- [x] Always **cite at least one industrial application** in a 14-mark answer.
- [x] **Draw a labeled Mermaid-style or hand-drawn block diagram** to score diagram marks.
- [x] **Use `\vert` instead of `|`** in all markdown tables during digital exams.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fabrication methods: Cold pressing and sintering, Hot pressing, Reaction bonding, Lanxide process, In-situ chemical technique, Sol-gel technique
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT412 - COMPOSITE MATERIALS AND CERAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Fabrication methods: Cold pressing and sintering, Hot pressing, Reaction bonding, Lanxide process, In-situ chemical technique, Sol-gel technique

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Overview of Ceramic & Composite Fabrication

Ceramic and metal-matrix composite (MMC) components are rarely produced by simple casting like metals. Because ceramics possess **strong covalent/ionic bonds** and **very high melting points** (often above **2000 °C**), they cannot be melted and poured conveniently. Instead, fabricators use a family of **powder-based, chemical, or reactive techniques** to convert raw precursors (powders, melts, sols, or slurries) into dense, shaped engineering components.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 4 Focus:** Six industrially vital fabrication routes:
> 1. **Cold Pressing and Sintering** (Powder Metallurgy Route)
> 2. **Hot Pressing** (Pressure-Assisted Densification)
> 3. **Reaction Bonding** (In-situ Reactive Forming)
> 4. **Lanxide / DIMOX Process** (Directed Metal Oxidation)
> 5. **In-Situ Chemical Technique** (Reactive MMC synthesis)
> 6. **Sol-Gel Technique** (Wet-Chemical Ceramic Synthesis)

---

## 1.2 Cold Pressing and Sintering

**Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology):**
*Cold pressing and sintering* is a two-stage powder metallurgy route in which ceramic or composite powders are first compacted at **room temperature** inside a rigid steel die under uniaxial pressure (typically **50 – 200 MPa**) to form a "green body," which is then heated in a furnace (at **0.6 – 0.9 × T_melting**, in Kelvin) to achieve densification by atomic diffusion across particle boundaries — a process termed **sintering**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — "Making a Snowball into Ice":**
> Imagine packing loose snow tightly into a ball (cold pressing → green body). The ball is fragile, but if you hold it in your palms for some time, the heat and slight pressure from your hands fuse the ice crystals into a hard, dense ice ball (sintering). The final ice ball is smaller, denser, and mechanically stronger than the original snow. Cold pressing gives *shape*; sintering gives *strength*.

**Key Metrics in Bold:**
- **Compaction Pressure:** 50 – 200 MPa
- **Sintering Temperature:** 0.6 – 0.9 × T_m (K)
- **Achievable Density:** 90 – 95 % of theoretical
- **Holding Time:** 30 min – 4 h

---

## 1.3 Hot Pressing

**Formal Definition:**
*Hot pressing* is a densification technique in which the ceramic/composite powder is simultaneously subjected to **uniaxial pressure (10 – 40 MPa)** and **elevated temperature (0.5 – 0.7 × T_m)** inside a graphite or refractory die, resulting in near-theoretical density (> **99 %**) in a single step.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — "Ironing a Wrinkled Shirt":**
> Imagine your shirt is wrinkled (porous powder). Cold pressing alone (just hands) removes only big wrinkles. But combining heat (iron) with pressure (ironing motion) flattens the fabric completely. Hot pressing does the same to a powder compact: heat softens the diffusion paths; pressure collapses the residual pores simultaneously.

**Key Metrics in Bold:**
- **Applied Pressure:** 10 – 40 MPa
- **Temperature:** 0.5 – 0.7 × T_m (K)
- **Final Density:** > **99 %** theoretical
- **Cycle Time:** 30 min – 2 h

---

## 1.4 Reaction Bonding

**Formal Definition:**
*Reaction bonding* is an in-situ reactive fabrication process in which a porous preform of one reactant is infiltrated by a liquid/gaseous second reactant, and a **chemical reaction** between them forms the desired ceramic phase with negligible net volume change.

The classical example is *Reaction Bonded Silicon Carbide* (RBSC / SiSiC):

$$\text{Si(l)} + \text{C(s)} \rightarrow \text{SiC(s)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — "Concrete Setting":**
> When you mix cement powder with water, the water penetrates the cement grains and reacts chemically, setting into hard concrete without much shrinkage. Reaction bonding behaves identically: molten Si infiltrates a porous carbon preform and reacts in-situ to form SiC — the part grows strong *while it is being made*, with almost zero dimensional change (a huge advantage for net-shape manufacturing).

**Key Metrics in Bold:**
- **Infiltration Temperature:** 1400 – 1800 °C
- **Net Shrinkage:** < **1 %** (near net-shape)
- **Density Achieved:** 80 – 90 % theoretical
- **Residual Free Si:** 5 – 15 % (typical)

---

## 1.5 Lanxide Process (DIMOX — Directed Metal Oxidation)

**Formal Definition:**
The *Lanxide* process (developed by Lanxide Corporation, USA in the 1980s) — also called **DIMOX™** (Directed Metal OXidation) — is a proprietary reactive fabrication route in which a **molten metal** (typically **aluminium**) is allowed to grow a ceramic matrix (**Al₂O₃** or **AlN**) outward by controlled oxidation in air, with the molten metal being continuously wicked through the oxide product to sustain reaction at the growth front.

$$\text{4 Al(l)} + \text{3 O}_2\text{(g)} \rightarrow \text{2 Al}_2\text{O}_3\text{(s)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — "Growing an Icicle Upside Down":**
> Picture an icicle forming in slow motion: water continuously rises to the tip and freezes, making the icicle *grow longer on its own*. Similarly, in the Lanxide process, molten Al continuously wicks upward through the growing Al₂O₃ layer, reacts with oxygen at the top, and the ceramic composite *spontaneously grows* into a preform placed above the melt. No external pressure is needed.

**Key Metrics in Bold:**
- **Operating Temperature:** 900 – 1400 °C
- **Growth Rate:** ~ **1 – 3 cm/day**
- **Additives:** Mg (**3 – 5 %**), Si (**3 – 5 %**)
- **Residual Metal:** 5 – 30 vol. %

---

## 1.6 In-Situ Chemical Technique

**Formal Definition:**
The *in-situ chemical technique* (also called reactive synthesis) for MMCs is a process in which the **reinforcement phase is chemically formed within the molten or solid metal matrix** through a controlled chemical reaction between added reactants, eliminating the need to add pre-formed ceramic particles.

A classic example: formation of Al₂O₃ particles in an aluminium melt via the thermite-type reaction:

$$\text{3 SiO}_2\text{(s)} + \text{4 Al(l)} \rightarrow \text{2 Al}_2\text{O}_3\text{(s)} + \text{3 Si(l)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — "Curd Forming in Milk":**
> When you add a few drops of lemon to warm milk, you don't add any solid particles — yet tiny white curd particles *form inside* the milk due to a chemical reaction. Similarly, in-situ MMC fabrication lets reinforcements (Al₂O₃, TiB₂, SiC) precipitate directly *inside* a metal bath — giving extremely clean, wetted interfaces without any external powder addition.

**Key Metrics in Bold:**
- **Reinforcement Size:** 0.1 – 10 µm
- **Interface:** atomically clean (no oxidation layer)
- **Reaction Type:** thermite, displacement, gas–liquid
- **Volume Fraction of Reinforcement:** 5 – 25 %

---

## 1.7 Sol-Gel Technique

**Formal Definition:**
The *sol-gel technique* is a **wet-chemical synthesis** route in which a colloidal suspension (**sol**) of ceramic precursors (typically metal alkoxides or salt solutions) undergoes **hydrolysis and polycondensation** reactions at low temperatures to form a continuous three-dimensional **gel** network, which is then dried and calcined to yield a nanocrystalline ceramic.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — "Making Jelly from Fruit Juice":**
> Take fruit juice (sol — liquid with suspended particles), add a gelling agent (initiator) — the juice slowly sets into a wobbly jelly (gel — solid network trapping liquid). Dry the jelly, and it shrinks into a crisp dry sheet (xerogel). Heat the sheet, and you get fine ceramic powder. The sol-gel route follows exactly these four steps — sol → gel → xerogel → ceramic.

**Key Metrics in Bold:**
- **Reaction Temperature:** **25 – 80 °C** (room-T chemistry!)
- **Final Ceramic Sintering T:** 500 – 1200 °C
- **Particle Size of Product:** **1 – 100 nm** (nanoceramic)
- **Purity:** exceptionally high (**99.99 %+**)

---

## 1.8 Comparative Snapshot

> [!TIP]
> **Quick Comparative Cue for KTU Examinations:**
> - *Cold + Sinter:* cheapest, simplest, porous (5–10 % porosity remains).
> - *Hot Press:* densest, expensive, limited to simple shapes.
> - *Reaction Bonding:* near-net-shape, residual Si limits high-T use.
> - *Lanxide/DIMOX:* grows the part itself; near-net-shape & high toughness.
> - *In-Situ MMC:* reinforcement is born inside; best interfacial bonding.
> - *Sol-Gel:* only method for nano-ceramics & thin films; high purity.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Cold Pressing and Sintering — Theory

### 2.1.1 Stages in Cold Pressing

When a powder mass is compacted in a die, three sequential stages occur:

- **Stage I — Particle Rearrangement:** At low pressure (< **40 MPa**), particles slide past each other and fill voids. The compact undergoes rapid densification.
- **Stage II — Plastic Deformation:** At intermediate pressure (**40 – 100 MPa**), the soft ceramic particles deform plastically, creating new contact points and increasing green density.
- **Stage III — Work Hardening / Brittle Fracture:** For brittle ceramics at high pressure (> **100 MPa**), particles fracture, fragment, and re-pack. Beyond this stage, density saturates.

> **Why does porosity remain?**
> Even at 200 MPa, a few percent of *closed pores* remain trapped between particles. They cannot be eliminated by mechanical pressing alone. **Sintering** is therefore indispensable.

### 2.1.2 Stages in Sintering (Solid-State)

After the green body is heated, sintering proceeds through:

- **Initial Stage (< 5 % shrinkage):** Neck growth between particles; surface diffusion dominates. Pore shape becomes smoother, but density barely changes.
- **Intermediate Stage (5 – 15 % shrinkage):** Cylindrical pores form along grain edges; grain boundaries act as vacancy sinks. Both **grain boundary diffusion** and **lattice diffusion** are active.
- **Final Stage (> 90 % theoretical density):** Isolated spherical pores shrink; pore removal governed by **lattice diffusion** from the pore surface to the grain boundary.

---

## 2.2 Hot Pressing — Theory

Hot pressing combines two driving forces:

- **Chemical driving force:** Surface energy reduction (curvature of pores)
- **Mechanical driving force:** External pressure (typically 10–40 MPa, supplied by hydraulic ram)

These two add together, lowering the **sintering activation energy** by an amount proportional to the applied stress.

The dominant mass-transport mechanism in hot pressing is **dislocation creep / power-law creep**, controlled by the Weertman-type equation discussed in Section 3.

**Advantages:**
- Lower temperatures than cold pressing + sintering
- Near-full density (> 99 %)
- Suppresses grain growth → finer microstructure → better mechanical properties

**Limitations:**
- Expensive graphite dies (consumable)
- Slow production rate
- Limited to simple geometries (discs, blocks, cylinders)

---

## 2.3 Reaction Bonding — Theory

### 2.3.1 Mechanism of RBSC
1. A porous **carbon preform** is shaped (green-body of carbon + binder).
2. The preform is heated to **> 1414 °C** (Si melting point).
3. **Molten silicon** is introduced (by capillary wicking or contact).
4. Silicon reacts exothermically with carbon to form **SiC**:
   $$\text{Si(l)} + \text{C(s)} \rightarrow \text{SiC(s)} \quad \Delta H \approx -73 \text{ kJ/mol}$$
5. Any unreacted Si remains as **free Si** in the matrix (5 – 15 vol. %), which limits the maximum use temperature to **~1350 °C** (Si melting point).

### 2.3.2 Why Near Net-Shape?
Because Si fills the *exact same volume* previously occupied by carbon (atomic packing consideration), there is **almost zero net dimensional change**. This is the single biggest advantage of reaction bonding.

---

## 2.4 Lanxide / DIMOX Process — Theory

The Lanxide process depends on a subtle balance:
- A thin, **passive oxide film** (Al₂O₃) forms naturally on molten Al in air and would normally *block* further oxidation.
- Additives like **Mg (3 – 5 %)** and **Si (3 – 5 %)** disrupt the continuity of this film, allowing **molten Al to wick through microchannels** in the oxide.
- The Al reaches the top surface, reacts with O₂, and the new Al₂O₃ grows on top of the old oxide.
- This growth proceeds **continuously**, at 1–3 cm/day, into a *preform* placed above the melt.
- The final composite consists of **α-Al₂O₃ matrix + 5–30 vol. % residual Al metal** (and/or Si, MgAl₂O₄ spinel).

The growth is *directed* (upward, by capillary wicking), hence the name *Directed Metal OXidation*.

---

## 2.5 In-Situ Chemical Technique — Theory

There are three principal in-situ MMC routes:

1. **Displacement / Thermite Reactions** (highly exothermic)
   $$\text{Fe}_2\text{O}_3 + 2\text{Al} \rightarrow \text{Al}_2\text{O}_3 + 2\text{Fe} \quad \text{(thermite)}$$
2. **Gas–Liquid Reactions** (e.g., bubbling CH₄ into Al melt → Al₄C₃)
3. **Precipitation from Supersaturated Melt** (slow cooling → TiB₂ crystallises)

The *thermodynamic feasibility* is decided by the standard free-energy change **ΔG°**, and the *kinetic viability* by the Arrhenius rate constant **k** (see Section 3).

**Why is the interface clean?**
Since the reinforcement is *born* inside the matrix, no atmospheric contamination occurs at the interface. The reinforcement is *automatically wetted* by the matrix → exceptional load transfer → high strength.

---

## 2.6 Sol-Gel Technique — Theory

The sol-gel process involves **three** chemical reactions in sequence:

### 2.6.1 Hydrolysis
A metal alkoxide M(OR)ₙ reacts with water:
$$\text{M(OR)}_n + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{M(OH)(OR)}_{n-1} + \text{ROH}$$

Example for tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS, precursor of SiO₂):
$$\text{Si(OC}_2\text{H}_5)_4 + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{Si(OC}_2\text{H}_5)_3\text{OH} + \text{C}_2\text{H}_5\text{OH}$$

### 2.6.2 Condensation (Polycondensation)
Two hydroxylated species link, releasing water or alcohol:
$$2 \text{ M(OR)}_{n-1}\text{OH} \rightarrow (\text{RO})_{n-1}\text{M–O–M(OR)}_{n-1} + \text{H}_2\text{O}$$

This builds up **Si–O–Si bridges** and forms a 3-D polymer-like network → the **gel**.

### 2.6.3 Drying and Calcination
- **Drying** (25–150 °C) removes solvent → **xerogel** (shrinks by 50–80 %)
- **Calcination** (500–800 °C) burns organics → **amorphous ceramic**
- Optional high-T treatment (1000–1200 °C) → **crystalline ceramic**

---

> **All Key Formulas, Conditions, Units — KTU Module-4 Important Table**

| # | Formula / Equation | Description | Typical Units / Values | Applicable Process |
|---|--------------------|-------------|------------------------|--------------------|
| 1 | $\rho_{g} = \dfrac{m_{powder}}{V_{compact}}$ | Green density of pressed compact | g/cm³ | Cold Pressing |
| 2 | $\rho_{rel} = \dfrac{\rho_{g}}{\rho_{th}} \times 100\%$ | Relative green density | % (typical 50–65 %) | Cold Pressing |
| 3 | $\rho_{th} = \sum_i \rho_i \, V_i$ | Rule of mixtures (theoretical) | g/cm³ | All composites |
| 4 | $T_{sint} \approx (0.6 \text{ to } 0.9) \times T_{m}$ | Sintering temperature range (K) | K | Sintering |
| 5 | $\dfrac{\Delta L}{L_0} = 1 - \left(\dfrac{\rho_0}{\rho_f}\right)^{1/3}$ | Linear shrinkage (isotropic) | dimensionless | Sintering |
| 6 | $\dfrac{x}{r} = \left(\dfrac{k \, t}{r^2}\right)^{1/3}$ | Neck-growth parameter (Kingery) | dimensionless | Sintering (initial) |
| 7 | $\dot{\varepsilon} = A \cdot \sigma^{n} \cdot \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{Q}{RT}\right)$ | Power-law creep (hot pressing) | s⁻¹ | Hot Pressing |
| 8 | $k = A \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{E_a}{RT}\right)$ | Arrhenius rate constant | s⁻¹ | In-situ reaction |
| 9 | $J = -D \dfrac{\partial C}{\partial x}$ | Fick's first law (diffusion flux) | mol·m⁻²·s⁻¹ | Sintering / infiltration |
| 10 | $V_{SiC} = V_C \times \dfrac{\rho_C \cdot M_{SiC}}{\rho_{SiC} \cdot M_C}$ | Stoichiometric volume conversion | cm³ | Reaction Bonding |
| 11 | $G_{rate} = \dfrac{1.5 \times 10^{-3} \, C_{Mg}}{\eta_{Al}}$ | Empirical growth-rate (DIMOX) | cm/day | Lanxide |
| 12 | $\dfrac{\partial [Si-OH]}{\partial t} = k_h [Si-OR][H_2O]$ | Hydrolysis kinetics (1st order) | mol/L·s | Sol-gel |
| 13 | $t_{gel} \propto [H_2O]^{-1}$ | Gelation time (TEOS) | s | Sol-gel |
| 14 | $V_{reinforcement} = \dfrac{n \cdot M_{reinforcement}}{\rho_{reinforcement}}$ | In-situ vol. fraction | cm³ | In-situ MMC |
| 15 | $Pore \; fraction \; P = 1 - \rho_{rel}$ | Porosity (cold-pressed green) | dimensionless | All powder routes |

> **Boundary Conditions to Remember:**
> - Sintering temperature is **always quoted in Kelvin** for the 0.6–0.9 T_m rule.
> - In Fick's law, the negative sign denotes flux *opposite* to concentration gradient.
> - In the Arrhenius equation, **R = 8.314 J·mol⁻¹·K⁻¹** is universal.
> - For Si/SiC, **ρ_SiC ≈ 3.21 g/cm³**, **ρ_Si ≈ 2.33 g/cm³**, **ρ_C ≈ 2.26 g/cm³**.

## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Fabrication Method | Major Industrial Application | Why Chosen? |
|--------------------|------------------------------|-------------|
| Cold Pressing + Sintering | Spark plug insulators, cutting tool inserts (WC-Co), Al₂O₃ substrates | Low cost, mass production |
| Hot Pressing | Transparent AlON windows, SiC armour tiles, Bi-Te thermoelectrics | High density, fine grain |
| Reaction Bonding | RBSC kiln furniture, RBSC heat exchangers, RBSC rocket nozzles | Near-net-shape, low shrinkage |
| Lanxide / DIMOX | Al₂O₃/Al pistons, tank treads, brake rotors | High toughness, low cost |
| In-situ MMC | Al-SiC pistons, automotive brake drums, aerospace MMCs | Clean interface, high strength |
| Sol-Gel | Anti-reflection coatings, silica aerogels, ceramic fibres, bioactive glass | Nano-purity, thin films |

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Linear Shrinkage in Sintering (Densification Geometry)

**Statement:** During sintering, an isotropic porous body shrinks uniformly in all three dimensions. Derive the relationship between linear shrinkage and the initial & final densities.

**Step 1 — Set up the conservation of mass.**

The mass of the powder compact is conserved during sintering. Let:
- $\rho_0$ = green (initial) density
- $\rho_f$ = final (sintered) density
- $V_0$ = green volume
- $V_f$ = final volume

Mass conservation gives:
$$m = \rho_0 \cdot V_0 = \rho_f \cdot V_f$$

**Step 2 — Relate volume change to linear dimensions.**

For an isotropic body, the volume scales as the cube of a characteristic linear dimension $L$:
$$V = k \cdot L^3 \quad (k = \text{geometric constant, same initially and finally})$$

Therefore:
$$\dfrac{V_f}{V_0} = \left(\dfrac{L_f}{L_0}\right)^3$$

**Step 3 — Combine the two relations.**

From mass conservation:
$$\dfrac{V_f}{V_0} = \dfrac{\rho_0}{\rho_f}$$

Equating both expressions for the volume ratio:
$$\left(\dfrac{L_f}{L_0}\right)^3 = \dfrac{\rho_0}{\rho_f}$$

**Step 4 — Take the cube root.**

$$\dfrac{L_f}{L_0} = \left(\dfrac{\rho_0}{\rho_f}\right)^{1/3}$$

**Step 5 — Solve for linear shrinkage.**

Linear shrinkage is defined as:
$$\dfrac{\Delta L}{L_0} = \dfrac{L_0 - L_f}{L_0} = 1 - \dfrac{L_f}{L_0}$$

Substituting Step 4:
$$\dfrac{\Delta L}{L_0} = 1 - \left(\dfrac{\rho_0}{\rho_f}\right)^{1/3}$$

$$\boxed{\;\dfrac{\Delta L}{L_0} = 1 - \left(\dfrac{\rho_0}{\rho_f}\right)^{1/3}\;}$$

> **Numerical Example (for KTU 5-mark type problems):**
> A green Al₂O₃ compact has $\rho_0 = 2.10$ g/cm³ and is sintered to $\rho_f = 3.85$ g/cm³. Find the linear shrinkage.
>
> Substitute:
> $$\dfrac{\Delta L}{L_0} = 1 - \left(\dfrac{2.10}{3.85}\right)^{1/3} = 1 - (0.5454)^{1/3} = 1 - 0.8158 = 0.1842$$
>
> Hence linear shrinkage $\approx$ **18.4 %**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Arrhenius Kinetics for In-Situ Reaction

**Statement:** For the in-situ reaction $\text{3 SiO}_2 + \text{4 Al} \rightarrow \text{2 Al}_2\text{O}_3 + \text{3 Si}$ inside an aluminium melt, derive the rate constant at 1000 K given $A = 1.2 \times 10^4$ s⁻¹ and $E_a = 180$ kJ/mol.

**Step 1 — State the Arrhenius equation.**

$$k = A \cdot \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{E_a}{RT}\right)$$

where:
- $A = 1.2 \times 10^4$ s⁻¹ (frequency factor)
- $E_a = 180{,}000$ J/mol
- $R = 8.314$ J·mol⁻¹·K⁻¹
- $T = 1000$ K

**Step 2 — Evaluate the exponent.**

$$\dfrac{E_a}{RT} = \dfrac{180{,}000}{8.314 \times 1000} = \dfrac{180{,}000}{8314} \approx 21.65$$

**Step 3 — Compute the exponential term.**

$$\exp(-21.65) = 4.02 \times 10^{-10}$$

(Use natural-log table or calculator: $\ln(2.49 \times 10^9) \approx 21.65$, so $\exp(-21.65) \approx 4.02 \times 10^{-10}$.)

**Step 4 — Multiply by the pre-exponential factor.**

$$k = (1.2 \times 10^4) \times (4.02 \times 10^{-10}) = 4.82 \times 10^{-6} \; \text{s}^{-1}$$

$$\boxed{\;k(1000\,\text{K}) \approx 4.82 \times 10^{-6} \;\text{s}^{-1}\;}$$

> **Interpretation for KTU Answers:**
> Such a small $k$ at 1000 K means the reaction is *sluggish* and would need a *catalyst* (e.g., Mg addition) or a higher temperature (e.g., 1200 K) to proceed at a useful rate. The in-situ technique is therefore typically used with the melt at 700–900 °C (≈ 1000–1170 K) above the Al melting point, and reaction is driven by *stirring* and *thermodynamic favourability* (large negative $\Delta G$).

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Volume of SiC Formed in Reaction Bonding

**Statement:** A RBSC preform contains $m_C = 100$ g of carbon (graphite). When fully infiltrated by molten Si, what mass of SiC is produced and what is the resulting SiC volume? (Given: M_C = 12 g/mol, M_Si = 28 g/mol, M_SiC = 40 g/mol, ρ_SiC = 3.21 g/cm³.)

**Step 1 — Moles of carbon available.**

$$n_C = \dfrac{m_C}{M_C} = \dfrac{100}{12} = 8.333 \text{ mol}$$

**Step 2 — Stoichiometry of the reaction Si + C → SiC.**

1 mol C produces 1 mol SiC. So:
$$n_{SiC} = n_C = 8.333 \text{ mol}$$

**Step 3 — Mass of SiC produced.**

$$m_{SiC} = n_{SiC} \times M_{SiC} = 8.333 \times 40 = 333.33 \text{ g}$$

**Step 4 — Mass of Si consumed (to react with the carbon).**

Since 1 mol Si reacts with 1 mol C:
$$n_{Si} = n_C = 8.333 \text{ mol}$$
$$m_{Si, \text{reacted}} = 8.333 \times 28 = 233.33 \text{ g}$$

**Step 5 — Volume of SiC produced.**

$$V_{SiC} = \dfrac{m_{SiC}}{\rho_{SiC}} = \dfrac{333.33}{3.21} = 103.84 \text{ cm}^3$$

**Step 6 — Volume of carbon consumed (to compare).**

$$V_C = \dfrac{m_C}{\rho_C} = \dfrac{100}{2.26} = 44.25 \text{ cm}^3$$

**Step 7 — Compute the net volumetric change.**

$$\Delta V = V_{SiC} - V_C = 103.84 - 44.25 = 59.59 \text{ cm}^3 \; (\text{expansion})$$

But in practice, this expansion **fills the pre-existing porosity** of the green body, leaving the *external* dimensions nearly unchanged. This is the origin of the *near-net-shape* claim of reaction bonding.

> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often write that the *external* part expands by 59.6 cm³. In reality, the porosity of the preform (typically 30–50 vol. %) absorbs this expansion. Hence **net external dimensional change is < 1 %**.

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Neck-Growth in Initial-Stage Sintering

**Statement:** In the initial stage of sintering, two spherical particles of radius $r$ form a "neck" of radius $x$ between them. Derive the relationship between $x/r$ and time $t$ for surface-diffusion-controlled sintering.

**Step 1 — Geometry of the neck.**

Two spheres of radius $r$, with a neck of radius $x$ and a concave neck surface of radius $\rho$ (the negative curvature). The driving force is the *gradient of surface curvature* which causes surface atoms to migrate.

**Step 2 — Geometric relations.**

From the geometry of two intersecting spheres:
$$x^2 = 2 r \rho \quad \Rightarrow \quad \rho = \dfrac{x^2}{2r}$$

**Step 3 — Surface diffusion flux (Nabarro–Herring / Kingery).**

Surface diffusion flux is given by:
$$J_s = - \dfrac{D_s \cdot \gamma_s \cdot \delta_s}{k_B T} \cdot \dfrac{d\kappa}{ds}$$

where $D_s$ = surface diffusivity, $\gamma_s$ = surface energy, $\delta_s$ = surface-layer thickness, $\kappa$ = surface curvature.

**Step 4 — Volume of material flowing into the neck.**

The volume of the neck region is approximately:
$$V_{neck} = \pi x^2 \cdot \rho / 2 \approx \dfrac{\pi x^4}{4 r}$$

(Using $\rho = x^2/(2r)$ from Step 2.)

**Step 5 — Time-rate of neck growth.**

The rate of change of neck volume equals the flux into the neck region:
$$\dfrac{dV_{neck}}{dt} = J_s \cdot A_{neck-surface}$$

After simplifying (substituting the curvature gradient and integrating), we obtain:
$$\dfrac{x^6}{r^9} = \dfrac{k \cdot t}{r^4}$$

where $k$ is a constant that absorbs $D_s$, $\gamma_s$, $\delta_s$, $k_B$, $T$.

**Step 6 — Take the sixth root.**

$$\dfrac{x}{r} = \left(\dfrac{k \, t}{r^2}\right)^{1/6}$$

But Kingery's classical result (which KTU expects) is more commonly written as:
$$\boxed{\;\dfrac{x}{r} = \left(\dfrac{k \, t}{r^2}\right)^{1/3}\;}$$

(where $k$ absorbs different combinations depending on the dominant diffusion mechanism: surface, grain-boundary, or volume diffusion. For *volume diffusion*, the exponent is **1/5**; for *grain-boundary diffusion*, the exponent is **1/4**; for *surface diffusion*, **1/6**.)

> **KTU Pitfall (Critical!):** Do **not** mix up the exponents. The KTU 2024 syllabus emphasises the **volume-diffusion-dominated** initial sintering model with exponent **1/5**, while Kingery's classical textbook uses 1/3 for the "geometry-only" derivation. Always state the *diffusion mechanism* before quoting the exponent.

---

## 3.5 Derivation: Power-Law Creep in Hot Pressing

**Statement:** Show how the densification rate in hot pressing depends on applied stress, temperature, and grain size.

**Step 1 — State the steady-state creep rate (Weertman-type power-law creep).**

$$\dot{\varepsilon} = A_s \cdot \dfrac{\sigma^n}{d^p} \cdot \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{Q}{RT}\right)$$

where:
- $\dot{\varepsilon}$ = strain rate (s⁻¹)
- $A_s$ = material constant
- $\sigma$ = applied stress
- $n$ = stress exponent (typically 1–5 for hot pressing of ceramics)
- $d$ = grain size
- $p$ = grain-size exponent (typically 2–3)
- $Q$ = activation energy for creep
- $R$ = 8.314 J/mol·K
- $T$ = absolute temperature

**Step 2 — Density change rate.**

For a uniaxially hot-pressed body, the densification rate is:
$$\dfrac{d\rho}{dt} = 3 \rho \, (1 - \rho) \, \dot{\varepsilon}$$

(For density $\rho$ expressed as a fraction of theoretical density.)

**Step 3 — Final compact density after time $t$ at constant $\sigma$, $T$.**

Integrating (assuming constant $T$, $\sigma$, $d$):
$$\int_{\rho_0}^{\rho_f} \dfrac{d\rho}{3 \rho (1 - \rho)} = \dot{\varepsilon} \cdot t$$

The left-hand side integrates to:
$$\ln\!\left(\dfrac{1 - \rho_0}{\rho_0} \cdot \dfrac{\rho_f}{1 - \rho_f}\right) = \dot{\varepsilon} \cdot t$$

**Step 4 — Solve for the time required to reach a target density $\rho_f$.**

$$t = \dfrac{1}{\dot{\varepsilon}} \ln\!\left[\dfrac{\rho_f (1 - \rho_0)}{\rho_0 (1 - \rho_f)}\right]$$

Substituting $\dot{\varepsilon}$:
$$\boxed{\;t = \dfrac{d^p}{A_s \, \sigma^n} \exp\!\left(\dfrac{Q}{RT}\right) \cdot \ln\!\left[\dfrac{\rho_f (1 - \rho_0)}{\rho_0 (1 - \rho_f)}\right]\;}$$

> **Interpretation:** To halve the time, you can either:
> - Double $\sigma$ (if $n = 1$) → halves the time, OR
> - Increase $T$ so that $\exp(Q/RT)$ decreases by half.
> - Halve the grain size (if $p = 2$) → time becomes 1/4 (finer grains = faster densification).
>
> This is why hot pressing of fine-grained powders at moderate temperature + moderate pressure is so efficient.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix: Comparing All Six Methods (KTU 14-Mark Ready)

| Parameter | Cold Press + Sinter | Hot Pressing | Reaction Bonding | Lanxide / DIMOX | In-Situ MMC | Sol-Gel |
|-----------|---------------------|--------------|------------------|------------------|-------------|---------|
| **State of starting material** | Powder (solid) | Powder (solid) | Porous preform + liquid | Molten metal + air | Molten metal + reactant | Liquid sol |
| **External pressure required** | Yes (50–200 MPa, room T) | Yes (10–40 MPa, high T) | No (capillary only) | No (capillary-driven) | No | No |
| **Operating temperature** | 0.6–0.9 T_m (K) | 0.5–0.7 T_m (K) | 1400–1800 °C | 900–1400 °C | 700–900 °C | 25–80 °C (gelation) |
| **Density achievable** | 90–95 % | > 99 % | 80–90 % | 95–99 % | 95–99 % | 95–99 % (after calcining) |
| **Net-shape capability** | Moderate (15–20 % shrinkage) | Poor (limited shape) | Excellent (<1 % shrinkage) | Excellent | Good (machining needed) | Good (depends on drying) |
| **Residual porosity** | 5–10 % | < 1 % | 10–20 % | < 5 % | < 1 % | < 5 % |
| **Residual impurity phase** | Sintering aid (MgO) | Graphite die residue | Free Si (5–15 %) | Free Al (5–30 %) | Reaction by-product | Solvent, organic residue |
| **Reinforcement type** | External (pre-mixed) | External (pre-mixed) | Formed in-situ (SiC) | Formed in-situ (Al₂O₃) | Formed in-situ (Al₂O₃, TiB₂) | Formed chemically (SiO₂, TiO₂) |
| **Typical product** | WC-Co inserts, Al₂O₃ substrates | SiC armour, AlON windows | RBSC kiln furniture, RBSC nozzles | Al₂O₃/Al pistons, brakes | Al/SiC, Al/Al₂O₃ pistons | Silica aerogel, fibres, coatings |
| **Cost** | Low | High | Medium | Medium | Medium | High (chemicals) |
| **Key advantage** | Simple & cheap | Highest density | No shrinkage | No external pressure | Clean interface | Nano-purity |
| **Key limitation** | Residual porosity | Die cost, shape | Free Si limits T | Mg additive required | Reaction control | Long gel-dry time |

> **KTU 2024 Examination Tip:** For a 14-mark descriptive answer on "comparison of fabrication routes," reproduce this entire table, and add 2–3 sentences of explanation for each row. That alone typically scores 9–10 marks; the remaining 4–5 marks come from a clear concluding statement on selection criteria.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 / Model Paper Style]**

**Q: Define "sintering" in the context of ceramic fabrication. List any three mechanisms of material transport that drive sintering.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** *Sintering* is a thermally activated solid-state process in which a porous powder compact (green body) is heated to a temperature typically between **0.6 T_m and 0.9 T_m** (in Kelvin) so that atoms migrate across particle boundaries, causing **neck growth, grain growth, and pore elimination** — leading to densification and strengthening of the compact without the application of external pressure.

**Three Material-Transport Mechanisms (1.5 marks, 0.5 each):**
1. **Surface diffusion** — atoms migrate along particle surfaces toward the neck region; dominant in the *initial stage*; causes neck growth without much densification.
2. **Grain-boundary diffusion** — atoms travel along the grain boundary from the boundary to the neck; dominant in the *intermediate stage*; contributes significantly to densification.
3. **Lattice (volume) diffusion** — atoms move through the crystal lattice from the bulk of the particle to the neck; dominant in the *final stage*; responsible for elimination of isolated pores.

> [!NOTE]
> *Vapour transport and viscous flow* are additional mechanisms (not required but earn bonus credit if mentioned).

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024 / Model Paper Style]**

**Q: What is the "Sol-Gel" technique? Name the two principal chemical reactions that govern sol-to-gel transformation.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** The *Sol-Gel technique* is a **wet-chemical synthesis** route for producing ceramics and glasses in which a colloidal suspension (**sol**) of metal-alkoxide or salt-solution precursors undergoes chemical reactions at low temperatures (25–80 °C) to form a continuous three-dimensional **gel** network, which is subsequently dried and calcined to yield a nanocrystalline ceramic powder, fibre, or thin film.

**Two Principal Reactions (1.5 marks, 0.75 each):**
1. **Hydrolysis** — the metal alkoxide M(OR)ₙ reacts with water to form hydroxylated species M(OH)(OR)ₙ₋₁ and releases alcohol ROH.
2. **Polycondensation** — two hydroxylated species link together with the elimination of water (or alcohol), forming **M–O–M oxo-bridges** that build the 3D gel network.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Option 1

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022 Module 4 Style]**

**Q(A): (a)** Describe the **Hot Pressing** technique for fabrication of ceramic composites with a neat schematic. Discuss its **advantages and limitations** in detail. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **Lanxide (DIMOX) process** with its underlying mechanism, important operating parameters, and industrial applications. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q(A) Part (a) — Hot Pressing (7 marks)

**Introduction (1 mark):**
Hot pressing is a pressure-assisted densification technique in which ceramic or composite powders are simultaneously subjected to **uniaxial pressure (10–40 MPa)** and **elevated temperature (0.5–0.7 × T_m, in Kelvin)** inside a graphite or refractory die, achieving **> 99 %** of theoretical density in a single step.

**Schematic (2 marks):** [Refer to the Hot-Pressing Block Diagram in Section 4.2]

- A hydraulic ram supplies uniaxial pressure.
- Induction heater raises the die temperature.
- Powder is confined between two graphite punches.
- Vacuum / inert atmosphere prevents oxidation.
- Water-cooled copper coils allow controlled cooling.

**Process Steps (1.5 marks):**
1. Powder is poured into a pre-heated graphite die.
2. Top and bottom punches are inserted.
3. A pressure of 10–40 MPa is applied *gradually*.
4. Temperature is raised to 0.5–0.7 T_m and held for 30 min – 2 h.
5. The system is cooled under pressure.
6. The dense compact is ejected.

**Advantages (1.5 marks):**
- Densities > 99 % theoretical are routinely achieved.
- Lower temperatures (0.5–0.7 T_m) than cold pressing + sintering, suppressing grain growth.
- Finer microstructure → higher strength and toughness.
- Suitable for non-oxide ceramics (SiC, Si₃N₄, B₄C) that are hard to densify by other means.

**Limitations (1 mark):**
- Graphite dies are expensive and consumable.
- Limited to simple geometries (cylinders, plates, blocks).
- Slow batch production → low throughput.
- Reaction between powder and die at high T can contaminate the product.

---

#### Model Solution for Q(A) Part (b) — Lanxide / DIMOX (7 marks)

**Introduction (1 mark):**
The Lanxide process (commercial name **DIMOX — Directed Metal OXidation**, developed by Lanxide Corporation, USA, in 1980s) is a proprietary reactive technique in which a molten metal (typically **aluminium**) reacts with a gaseous oxidant (air/oxygen) to form a ceramic matrix that grows outward into a pre-placed preform.

**Mechanism (2.5 marks):**
1. A molten Al bath is alloyed with **3–5 % Mg** and **3–5 % Si** at **900–1400 °C**.
2. A thin Al₂O₃ film forms on the melt surface, but Mg disrupts the film's continuity.
3. Molten Al wicks *upward* through microchannels in the discontinuous oxide film.
4. At the top growth front, Al reacts with atmospheric O₂:
   $$\text{4 Al(l)} + \text{3 O}_2\text{(g)} \rightarrow \text{2 Al}_2\text{O}_3\text{(s)}$$
5. New Al₂O₃ forms on top of the old oxide, and the growth front advances into the preform.
6. The process continues at **1–3 cm/day** until the preform is fully consumed.

**Operating Parameters (1.5 marks):**
- Temperature: **900–1400 °C**
- Atmosphere: air or controlled O₂ partial pressure
- Additives: Mg (**3–5 %**), Si (**3–5 %**)
- Preform materials: Al₂O₃ platelets, SiC particles, Al₂O₃ fibres
- Final composition: α-Al₂O₃ + 5–30 vol % residual Al

**Industrial Applications (2 marks):**
- Al₂O₃/Al **pistons** for diesel engines.
- **Brake rotors** and **tank treads** in military vehicles.
- **Heat sinks** for electronics.
- **Cutting tool inserts** with high toughness.
- **Armor tiles** (Al₂O₃/Al composites).

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Option 2

> **[KTU University Exam — June 2023 Module 4 Style]**

**Q(B): (a)** With the help of a suitable reaction, describe the **Reaction Bonding process** for fabrication of Silicon Carbide (SiC). What are its **main advantages and limitations**? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **In-Situ Chemical Technique** for the fabrication of Metal Matrix Composites. Give at least **two examples** of in-situ reactions. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q(B) Part (a) — Reaction Bonding of SiC (7 marks)

**Introduction (1 mark):**
Reaction bonding is a near-net-shape fabrication process in which a porous preform of one reactant is infiltrated by a liquid/gaseous second reactant; the in-situ chemical reaction between them forms the desired ceramic phase, accompanied by negligible net dimensional change.

**Reaction for RBSC (1.5 marks):**
The classical reaction is:
$$\text{Si(l)} + \text{C(s)} \xrightarrow{1400 \text{ to } 1800\,°\text{C}} \text{SiC(s)} \quad \Delta H \approx -73 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

**Process Steps (2 marks):**
1. Carbon powder is mixed with a binder and shaped into a **porous preform** (30–50 vol % porosity).
2. The preform is heated in a furnace to above the **Si melting point (1414 °C)**.
3. Molten Si is introduced — it wicks into the preform by **capillary action**.
4. Si reacts exothermically with C, forming **SiC** in situ.
5. Unreacted Si (5–15 vol %) remains as a *free phase* trapped in the matrix.

**Main Advantages (1.5 marks):**
- **Near-net-shape:** Net dimensional change is < 1 %, eliminating expensive machining.
- **Low processing temperature** (1400–1800 °C) compared to sintering of pure SiC (> 2100 °C).
- **Lower cost** than hot-pressed or CVD-SiC.
- **Complex shapes** can be produced (RBSC heat exchangers, tubes, nozzles).

**Limitations (1 mark):**
- Residual **free Si (5–15 %)** limits maximum use temperature to **~1350 °C** (Si melting point).
- Density is limited to **80–90 %** of theoretical.
- Lower mechanical strength and thermal conductivity than hot-pressed or sintered SiC.

---

#### Model Solution for Q(B) Part (b) — In-Situ Chemical Technique (7 marks)

**Introduction (1 mark):**
The *In-situ chemical technique* (also called **reactive synthesis**) is a route for fabricating Metal Matrix Composites (MMCs) in which the **reinforcement phase is chemically formed within the metal matrix** by a controlled reaction between added reactants, eliminating the need for externally added ceramic particles.

**Key Advantages Over Ex-Situ MMCs (1.5 marks):**
- **Atomically clean** matrix–reinforcement interface (no oxide films, no contamination).
- **Stronger interfacial bonding** → better load transfer → higher strength.
- **Finer reinforcement size** (0.1–10 µm) → improved strength and wear resistance.
- **Lower cost** of ceramic reactant powders compared to pre-formed nano-ceramics.

**Example 1 — Al₂O₃ Reinforcement via Displacement Reaction (2 marks):**
$$\text{3 SiO}_2\text{(s)} + \text{4 Al(l)} \rightarrow \text{2 Al}_2\text{O}_3\text{(s)} + \text{3 Si(l)}$$
- SiO₂ powder is added to a stirred Al melt at 700–900 °C.
- SiO₂ is reduced by Al, forming fine Al₂O₃ particles *in situ* and releasing Si which dissolves in the Al matrix (improving strength further).
- Result: **Al/SiC-Al₂O₃** hybrid MMC with high wear resistance.

**Example 2 — TiB₂ Reinforcement via Halide Salt Route (2 marks):**
$$\text{3 K}_2\text{TiF}_6\text{(s)} + \text{6 KBF}_4\text{(s)} + \text{10 Al(l)} \rightarrow \text{3 TiB}_2\text{(s)} + \text{9 KAlF}_4\text{(s)} + \text{K}_3\text{AlF}_6\text{(s)}$$
- Salts are added to molten Al at 800 °C.
- **TiB₂ particles (0.5–5 µm)** precipitate homogeneously throughout the melt.
- The gaseous/slag by-products (KAlF₄, K₃AlF₆) float to the top and are skimmed off.
- Result: **Al/TiB₂ MMC** with excellent stiffness, used in automotive pistons and brake drums.

**Conclusion (0.5 mark):**
In-situ MMCs offer superior interfacial cleanliness and mechanical performance, but require **careful temperature and stirring control** to prevent unwanted side reactions (e.g., Al₄C₃ formation in Al–CH₄ systems, which is hygroscopic and degrades the MMC).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Traps in KTU 2024 Module 4 Answers — How to Avoid Them:**
>
> 1. **Cold Pressing vs Hot Pressing Confusion:** Many students interchange the temperature/pressure ranges. Remember: **Cold Pressing = Room T + 50–200 MPa**, but **Sintering = High T (0.6–0.9 T_m) + No pressure**. **Hot Pressing = High T (0.5–0.7 T_m) + 10–40 MPa — simultaneously**.
> 2. **Lanxide vs Reaction Bonding:** Students often write "molten metal reacts with oxygen to form ceramic" for *both*. The distinguishing feature of Lanxide is that the **ceramic *grows* outward** into a preform over hours/days, while in reaction bonding the ceramic is formed *in situ* within a *static* porous preform.
> 3. **Sol-Gel Stoichiometry:** Forgetting the *condensation* step and mentioning only *hydrolysis* will cost 0.5–1 mark. Both reactions must be stated.
> 4. **Units of Sintering Temperature:** The rule "0.6–0.9 T_m" requires **T_m in Kelvin**. Students who write °C will be marked down.
> 5. **In-Situ MMC Interface:** Do not say "the interface is *good*"; say "the interface is *atomically clean and free of oxide films*." This specific phrasing scores full marks.
> 6. **Mermaid Diagram Reproduction:** In long answers, draw a *labelled* process flow — not just a sequence of boxes. Each box should have the operating condition (T, P, time) written next to it.
> 7. **Free Silicon in RBSC:** Always mention the 5–15 vol % residual free Si and the resulting 1350 °C use-temperature limit. Skipping this loses 1 mark.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **30-Second Revision Punch-List for KTU Module 4 — Fabrication Methods**
>
> **COLD PRESSING + SINTERING**
> - Powder → die → 50–200 MPa at room T → green body → sinter at 0.6–0.9 T_m (K) → 90–95 % density.
> - Three stages: (i) particle rearrangement, (ii) plastic deformation, (iii) work-hardening/fracture.
> - Sintering stages: initial (neck growth), intermediate (pore-channel closure), final (isolated pore removal).
> - Linear shrinkage $\Delta L / L_0 = 1 - (\rho_0 / \rho_f)^{1/3}$.
>
> **HOT PRESSING**
> - Simultaneous T (0.5–0.7 T_m) + P (10–40 MPa) in graphite die → > 99 % density.
> - Power-law creep: $\dot{\varepsilon} = A \sigma^n / d^p \cdot \exp(-Q/RT)$.
> - Advantages: highest density, finer grain, lower T. Limitations: expensive die, simple shapes only.
>
> **REACTION BONDING**
> - Si(l) + C(s) → SiC(s) at 1400–1800 °C; near-net-shape (<1 % shrinkage).
> - Residual free Si = 5–15 vol %; max use T ≈ 1350 °C; final density 80–90 %.
>
> **LANXIDE / DIMOX**
> - Molten Al + O₂ → Al₂O₃; grows 1–3 cm/day at 900–1400 °C.
> - Additives: Mg (3–5 %), Si (3–5 %); residual Al = 5–30 vol %.
> - Products: pistons, brake rotors, armour tiles.
>
> **IN-SITU CHEMICAL TECHNIQUE (MMC)**
> - Reinforcement born *inside* the melt; clean interface; size 0.1–10 µm.
> - Examples: 3SiO₂ + 4Al → 2Al₂O₃ + 3Si; K₂TiF₆ + KBF₄ + Al → TiB₂.
> - Volume fraction of reinforcement: 5–25 vol %.
>
> **SOL-GEL TECHNIQUE**
> - Steps: hydrolysis → condensation → gelation → aging → drying (xerogel) → calcination → ceramic.
> - Hydrolysis: M(OR)ₙ + H₂O → M(OH)(OR)ₙ₋₁ + ROH.
> - Condensation: 2 M(OH)(OR)ₙ₋₁ → (RO)ₙ₋₁M–O–M(OR)ₙ₋₁ + H₂O.
> - Operating T 25–80 °C; product size 1–100 nm; purity 99.99 %+.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Carbon fiber/Carbon matrix composites: Processing of Carbon/Carbon Composites, Oxidation protection of Carbon/Carbon Composites, Properties of Carbon/Carbon Composites, and Application of Carbon/Carbon Composites
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT412 - COMPOSITE MATERIALS AND CERAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Carbon fiber/Carbon matrix composites: Processing of Carbon/Carbon Composites, Oxidation protection of Carbon/Carbon Composites, Properties of Carbon/Carbon Composites, and Application of Carbon/Carbon Composites

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Definition of Carbon/Carbon (C/C) Composites

A **Carbon/Carbon (C/C) composite** is a specialized class of composite material in which **both the reinforcement (fibers) and the matrix are composed of carbon**. Carbon fibers (typically PAN-based, pitch-based, or rayon-based) reinforce a carbon matrix derived from pitch, resin, or chemical vapor deposition. This unique all-carbon architecture enables C/C composites to retain — and in many cases *increase* — their mechanical strength at temperatures where conventional materials (superalloys, ceramics, refractory metals) fail.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (PEMUT412 - Module 4):**
> *"Carbon/Carbon composites are carbon-matrix composites reinforced with carbon fibers. They are the only engineering material that combines high specific strength, high specific modulus, and excellent thermomechanical properties at ultra-high temperatures (>2000°C) in inert or vacuum environments."*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Charcoal Brick That Never Burns"

Imagine a **charcoal briquette** used in BBQ grills. It is essentially porous carbon. Now, take that brittle, weak charcoal and embed thousands of microscopic carbon "threads" (carbon fibers) inside it before turning it into a dense solid carbon block. The fibers prevent catastrophic crack propagation, while the carbon matrix binds them. The resulting "carbon-reinforced carbon" is **light like plastic, strong like steel, and can survive re-entry temperatures of the Space Shuttle** (~1650°C). However, just like the BBQ charcoal, if you expose it to *air* at high temperature, it burns — that is why **oxidation protection** is the central engineering challenge for C/C composites.

### 1.3 Why C/C Composites Exist — The Driving Problem

Conventional high-temperature alloys (Ni-superalloys) soften above ~1100°C. Refractory metals (W, Mo) oxidize catastrophically. Ceramics (SiC, Si₃N₄) are brittle and prone to thermal shock. **C/C composites uniquely solve all three problems simultaneously** in non-oxidizing atmospheres.

| Metric | C/C Composite | Ni-Superalloy | SiC Ceramic |
|---|---|---|---|
| Max Use Temp (inert) | **>2500 °C** | ~1100 °C | ~1400 °C |
| Density | **1.6–2.0 g/cm³** | 8.5 g/cm³ | 3.2 g/cm³ |
| Thermal Shock Resistance | Excellent | Good | Poor |
| Oxidation Susceptibility | **High (major drawback)** | Moderate | Low |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Insight:** C/C composites are the **only structural material** that gains strength with temperature (up to ~2500°C). This counter-intuitive behavior arises because graphitization of the matrix improves crystallinity and reduces flaw density at high temperature.

### 1.4 Classification by Reinforcement Architecture

1. **Unidirectional (1D) C/C** — fibers aligned along one axis; maximum directional strength.
2. **2D Laminated C/C** — woven cloth plies stacked; balanced in-plane properties.
3. **2.5D Woven C/C** — through-thickness fibers added; resists delamination.
4. **3D Woven/Needled C/C** — fully 3D fiber architecture; used in rocket nozzles.
5. **C/C with chopped fibers** — lower performance, used for tribological (brake) applications.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Processing of Carbon/Carbon Composites

The single greatest challenge in C/C manufacture is **densification** — fully filling the porous preform with carbon matrix. Since carbon does not melt under normal pressure (it sublimates at ~3650°C), conventional liquid-metal casting is impossible. Instead, three families of processing routes dominate:

### 2.1.1 Route A — Liquid Phase Impregnation (Pitch/Resin Process)

**Operating Principle:** A liquid carbon precursor is forced into the porous fiber preform, then pyrolyzed (carbonized) at high temperature to leave behind solid carbon.

**Step-by-step logic:**
1. **Preform fabrication** — Carbon fibers arranged in desired 2D/3D architecture.
2. **Vacuum impregnation** — Preform submerged in **coal-tar pitch** (preferred) or **petroleum pitch** at 250–350°C under vacuum (≈1 kPa) to drive liquid pitch into pores.
3. **Curing / Soaking** — Pressure (1–10 MPa) applied; pitch held at 200–250°C to thermoset.
4. **Carbonization** — Slow pyrolysis at **800–1100°C in inert atmosphere (N₂/Ar)**. Volatiles escape, leaving a porous carbon char. Shrinkage (~20%) opens new microcracks.
5. **Re-impregnation + re-carbonization** — Cycle repeated **4–8 times** to close porosity. Each cycle adds 5–15% density.
6. **Graphitization** — Final heat treatment at **2400–3000°C** converts amorphous carbon to graphitic structure (improves thermal conductivity, modulus).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Carbon Yield:** Pitch yields **~50–70% carbon** (high); phenolic resin yields only **~30–40%** (low). Therefore, **pitch is preferred for C/C brakes and structural parts**, while resin is used where low impurity is critical.

### 2.1.2 Route B — Chemical Vapor Infiltration (CVI)

**Operating Principle:** A hydrocarbon gas (CH₄, C₃H₈, C₂H₂) infiltrates a porous preform and **thermally decomposes** (cracks) at 900–1200°C, depositing pyrolytic carbon on the fiber surfaces.

$$C_nH_m \xrightarrow{1000°C,\ \text{Inert Gas}} nC_{(s)} + \tfrac{m}{2}H_2$$

**CVI Variants (High-yield for KTU):**

| CVI Type | Temperature | Pressure | Cycle Time | Quality |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **ICVI** (Isothermal) | 1000–1100 °C | 1–10 kPa | 300–1000 h | Highest density |
| **GCVI** (Pressure Gradient) | 900–1100 °C | 10–100 kPa | 100–300 h | Medium |
| **FCVI** (Forced Flow + Thermal Gradient) | 900–1200 °C | 100 kPa+ | 20–50 h | Fastest, complex furnace |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Favourite:** ICVI is the industry standard for aircraft brake discs. FCVI was developed by Oak Ridge National Lab to densify thick rocket nozzle preforms in <50 hours.

### 2.1.3 Route C — Hybrid / Combined Routes

Modern industry combines routes:
- **Pitch CVI + CVI** for nose tips.
- **Resin pyrolysis + pitch CVI** for cost reduction.
- **CVI + HIP** (Hot Isostatic Pressing) for premium aerospace parts.

### 2.2 Oxidation Protection of C/C Composites

**The Core Problem:** Carbon oxidizes in air as:
$$C_{(s)} + O_2 \rightarrow CO_2 \quad \Delta G^{\circ} = -394\ \text{kJ/mol (at 25°C)}$$

Above ~**400°C**, oxidation rate becomes catastrophic. Solution strategies fall into three classes:

#### 2.2.1 External Barrier Coatings

- **SiC coatings** (CVD-deposited, 50–200 μm thick) — primary oxygen barrier up to ~1700°C.
- **Si₃N₄ coatings** — better oxidation resistance below 1400°C but cracks above.
- **Multi-layer coatings:** SiC/Si₃N₄/SiC — seals through-thickness cracks.
- **Glass-ceramic top coats** (e.g., BaO-Al₂O₃-SiO₂) — self-healing of microcracks above 800°C.
- **Thermal barrier coatings (TBCs):** Y₂O₃-stabilized ZrO₂ — applied over SiC for >2000°C service.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CTE Mismatch Problem:** Pure SiC has CTE ≈ 4.5 × 10⁻⁶/K; C/C composite ≈ 1–2 × 10⁻⁶/K. Mismatch causes coating spallation. Solution: **functionally graded coatings (FGCs)** where composition gradually transitions from SiC to Si₃N₄ to mullite to glass-ceramic.

#### 2.2.2 Internal (Bulk) Protection

- **Boron additives** (~3–5 wt%) blended into matrix precursor. At ~580°C, B forms **B₂O₃ glass** which flows and seals microcracks → "self-healing" behaviour.
- **SiC particulate fillers** in matrix — internal oxidation barrier.
- **ZrB₂ / HfB₂ ultra-high-temperature ceramics (UHTCs)** — used in hypersonic leading edges (>2000°C).

#### 2.2.3 Combined Systems

Modern C/C brake discs use **dual protection:** internal B-doping + external SiC coating. Aerospace nose tips use **B-doping + SiC + glass-ceramic + TBC stack** (4-layer system).

### 2.3 Properties of C/C Composites

| Property | Typical Value | Engineering Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **Density** | **1.6 – 2.0 g/cm³** | Among lightest structural materials |
| Tensile Strength | 200 – 1000 MPa | Higher than aluminum alloys |
| Young's Modulus | 70 – 200 GPa | Comparable to steel at 1/4 weight |
| Flexural Strength | 300 – 1200 MPa | Excellent for nozzle liners |
| CTE (in-plane) | **0 – 2 × 10⁻⁶ /K** | Near-zero thermal expansion |
| Thermal Conductivity | 50 – 250 W/m·K | Directional heat spreader |
| Specific Heat | 700 – 1900 J/kg·K | High thermal inertia |
| Max Service Temp (inert) | **>2500 °C** | Highest of any structural material |
| Coefficient of Friction | 0.15 – 0.45 | Ideal for braking systems |
| Oxidation Onset (in air) | ~400 °C | **Major drawback** |
| Electrical Resistivity | 3 – 50 μΩ·m | Excellent conductor |

> [!NOTE]
> **Anomalous Strength-Temperature Behaviour:** Unlike metals (strength drops with T) or ceramics (strength drops sharply at T > 0.5 Tm), **C/C composites gain 20–50% strength** between 1000°C and 2500°C in inert atmosphere due to **stress-induced graphitization** and closure of microcracks.

### 2.4 Applications of C/C Composites

#### 2.4.1 Aerospace & Re-entry Systems
- **Rocket nozzle throats & exit cones** — Space Shuttle, Ariane 5, Falcon 9.
- **Spacecraft nose tips & leading edges** — Apollo, Space Shuttle Orbiter (C/C tiles on wing leading edges).
- **Thermal protection system (TPS)** components.

#### 2.4.2 Aircraft Brakes (Largest Commercial Market — ~70% of C/C production)
- Boeing 777, 787, Airbus A330, A340, A380.
- Military aircraft: F-15, F-16, F-22, Rafale, Eurofighter.
- Formula-1 and high-performance racing car brake discs.

#### 2.4.3 Nuclear Energy
- Tokamak fusion reactor first-wall components (ITER).
- Control rod cladding in advanced gas-cooled reactors.

#### 2.4.4 Industrial & Mechanical
- Hot-pressing dies for diamond synthesis.
- Heat treatment fixtures, sintering trays.
- Glass industry hot-end components.

#### 2.4.5 Biomedical
- Bone plates, dental implants, prosthetic ligaments (biocompatible carbon).

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet (Mandatory Reference)

$$
\rho_c = V_f \rho_f + V_m \rho_m
$$

$$
\sigma_c = V_f \sigma_f + V_m \sigma_m \quad \text{(Rule of Mixtures, Voigt)}
$$

$$
E_c = V_f E_f + V_m E_m \quad \text{(Rule of Mixtures, parallel loading)}
$$

$$
\alpha_c = V_f \alpha_f + V_m \alpha_m \quad \text{(Linear CTE, Voigt)}
$$

$$
k_c = V_f k_f + V_m k_m \quad \text{(Thermal Conductivity, parallel)}
$$

$$
P_{\text{oxidation}} \propto e^{-E_a / RT} \quad \text{(Arrhenius Oxidation Rate)}
$$

$$
\Delta L_{\text{carbonization}} = \alpha_{pyro} \cdot \Delta T \cdot L_0
$$

$$
\eta_{\text{densification}} = \frac{\rho_{\text{final}} - \rho_{\text{preform}}}{\rho_{\text{theoretical}} - \rho_{\text{preform}}} \times 100\%
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Units Check:** All densities in **g/cm³**, conductivities in **W/m·K**, moduli in **GPa**, CTEs in **10⁻⁶/K**.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Density of C/C Composite

**Given:** A 3D-woven C/C composite has fiber volume fraction $V_f = 0.45$, matrix volume fraction $V_m = 0.45$, and residual porosity $V_p = 0.10$. Carbon fiber density $\rho_f = 1.80$ g/cm³, matrix density $\rho_m = 1.95$ g/cm³ (pitch-derived pyrolytic carbon after graphitization).

**Find:** Bulk density of the composite.

**Step 1 — Establish volume constraint:**
$$
V_f + V_m + V_p = 1
$$
$$
\Rightarrow 0.45 + 0.45 + 0.10 = 1.00 \quad \checkmark
$$

**Step 2 — Apply rule of mixtures (closed porosity treated as zero mass):**
$$
\rho_c = V_f \rho_f + V_m \rho_m + V_p \cdot \rho_p
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute (pores contribute 0 mass):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\rho_c &= (0.45)(1.80) + (0.45)(1.95) + (0.10)(0.00) \\
&= 0.810 + 0.8775 + 0 \\
&= 1.6875 \ \text{g/cm}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Comparison with theoretical maximum (no porosity):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\rho_{\text{theoretical}} &= (0.45)(1.80) + (0.55)(1.95) \\
&= 0.810 + 1.0725 = 1.8825 \ \text{g/cm}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Relative density:**
$$
\rho_{\text{rel}} = \frac{1.6875}{1.8825} = 0.8964 = 89.64\%
$$

> **Interpretation:** Residual porosity of 10% drops bulk density by ~10% but also creates oxidation entry pathways. This is why **densification cycles** (pitch impregnation) are critical — each cycle closes pores and adds carbon mass.

---

## 3.2 Derivation — Effective CTE of Laminated C/C

**Given:** 2D C/C laminate, $V_f = 0.40$, $\alpha_f = -0.5 \times 10^{-6}/K$ (pitch-based carbon fiber can be *negative* along fiber axis!), $\alpha_m = 2.5 \times 10^{-6}/K$ (graphitic matrix), $E_f = 380$ GPa, $E_m = 15$ GPa.

**Find:** Effective in-plane CTE using the **Schapery bound** (analytical CTE for laminated systems):

**Step 1 — Voigt (iso-strain) CTE formula:**
$$
\alpha_c = \frac{V_f E_f \alpha_f + V_m E_m \alpha_m}{V_f E_f + V_m E_m}
$$

**Step 2 — Numerator:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
N &= (0.40)(380)(-0.5 \times 10^{-6}) + (0.60)(15)(2.5 \times 10^{-6}) \\
&= (152)(-0.5 \times 10^{-6}) + (9)(2.5 \times 10^{-6}) \\
&= -76.0 \times 10^{-6} + 22.5 \times 10^{-6} \\
&= -53.5 \times 10^{-6}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Denominator:**
$$
D = (0.40)(380) + (0.60)(15) = 152 + 9 = 161\ \text{GPa}
$$

**Step 4 — Effective CTE:**
$$
\alpha_c = \frac{-53.5 \times 10^{-6}}{161} = -0.332 \times 10^{-6}\ /\text{K}
$$

> **Interpretation:** The negative fiber CTE dominates → **net negative thermal expansion** along fiber axis! This is why C/C is used in **space-stable structures** (telescope mirrors, optical benches) — dimensionally invariant over wide temperature ranges.

---

## 3.3 Derivation — Oxidation Mass Loss (Arrhenius Model)

**Given:** A C/C brake disc operates in air at $T = 800°C = 1073$ K. Activation energy for carbon oxidation $E_a = 170$ kJ/mol. Pre-exponential factor $k_0 = 1.2 \times 10^5$ g/(m²·s). Initial mass $m_0 = 5.0$ kg, surface area $A = 0.8$ m².

**Find:** Mass loss after $t = 1$ hour of operation.

**Step 1 — Arrhenius rate equation:**
$$
k(T) = k_0 \cdot e^{-E_a / (R T)}
$$

**Step 2 — Plug in numbers:**
$$
\frac{E_a}{RT} = \frac{170{,}000}{(8.314)(1073)} = \frac{170{,}000}{8921} = 19.06
$$

**Step 3 — Compute rate:**
$$
k(1073) = 1.2 \times 10^5 \cdot e^{-19.06} = 1.2 \times 10^5 \cdot 5.0 \times 10^{-9} = 6.0 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{g/(m}^2 \cdot \text{s)}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert time:**
$$
t = 1\ \text{hour} = 3600\ \text{s}
$$

**Step 5 — Mass loss:**
$$
\Delta m = k(T) \cdot A \cdot t = (6.0 \times 10^{-4})(0.8)(3600) = 1.728\ \text{g}
$$

**Step 6 — Percentage:**
$$
\%\text{ loss} = \frac{1.728}{5000} \times 100 = 0.0346\%
$$

> **Conclusion:** At 800°C in air, mass loss is small in 1 hour, but **doubling temperature** to 1600°C → exponent becomes 38.12 → rate increases by factor $e^{19} \approx 1.8 \times 10^8$. This explosive sensitivity is why oxidation protection is **non-negotiable** above 600°C.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Processing Routes

| Decision Parameter | Pitch Impregnation | Resin Impregnation | ICVI | FCVI |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Carbon Yield | **High (50–70%)** | Low (30–40%) | N/A (gas) | N/A (gas) |
| Cycle Time | 100–300 h | 200–400 h | **500–1000 h** | 20–50 h |
| Final Density | 1.7–1.85 g/cm³ | 1.5–1.7 g/cm³ | **1.85–2.0 g/cm³** | 1.7–1.85 g/cm³ |
| Purity | Medium | High | **Highest** | High |
| Cost | **Low** | Medium | **High** | High |
| Microcrack Density | Low | Medium | **Very Low** | Low |
| Best Application | Brakes, structural | Lab-scale parts | **Rocket nozzles** | Large preforms |

---
